gender_filter.note: Filter diseases by gender before symptom scoring

diseases.name: Malnutrition
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Anthropometric Measurements (Physical Measurements)

Body Mass Index (BMI): Calculated as weight in kg divided by height in meters squared. For adults, a BMI < 18.5 is considered underweight/malnourished.

Mid-Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC): A critical screening tool in India for children (6–59 months) and adults to measure muscle wasting. A MUAC < 115 mm in children indicates severe acute malnutrition (SAM).

Weight-for-Age (WFA): Used to identify underweight children.

Height-for-Age (HFA): Used to identify chronic malnutrition (stunting).

Weight-for-Height/Length (WFH): Used to identify acute malnutrition (wasting).

Skinfold Thickness: Measures subcutaneous fat stores.

2. Routine Biochemical Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): To check for anemia (low hemoglobin), which is common in malnutrition.

Serum Albumin: A primary marker for protein deficiency (hypoalbuminemia).

Serum Prealbumin (Transthyretin): A more sensitive, short-term indicator of protein status than albumin, used for monitoring recovery.

Blood Glucose (RBS): To detect hypoglycemia, often present in severe malnutrition.

Liver Function Test (LFT) & Kidney Function Test (KFT): To assess organ function, which can be impaired in severe cases.

Serum Electrolytes: Specifically testing for Potassium, Calcium, Magnesium, and Phosphate, which are often depleted.

Total Lymphocyte Count (TLC): Evaluates immune system function (levels < 1500/mm³ indicate malnutrition, < 900/mm³ severe malnutrition).

3. Micronutrient Deficiency Tests

Iron Studies: Serum Iron, Ferritin, and Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC).

Vitamin B12 and Folate Test.

Vitamin D (25-hydroxyvitamin D) test.

Zinc Levels: Particularly important for children with chronic diarrhea.

Vitamin A and E levels.

Specialized Diagnostic Tools (If Needed)

Stool Examination: To rule out parasites and worm infestations causing malabsorption.

Bioelectrical Impedance Analysis (BIA): Measures body composition (fat vs. muscle mass).

Thyroid Function Test: To check for hyperthyroidism (which increases metabolic demands).

Key Indicators in Children (SAM):

According to Indian and WHO guidelines, a child is classified with Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) if they have a MUAC < 115 mm, a Weight-for-Height Z-score < -3, or presence of bilateral pitting edema.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Foods (RUTF) - Nutritional Treatment

Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food (RUTF) / Therapeutic Paste: Provides 500 kcal per 92g sachet.

Local RUTF Brands/Producers in India: NutriFEEDO, Provika-RUTF, and other local, peanut-based paste formulations.

Ready-to-Use Supplementary Food (RUSF): Used for Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM), such as Nutrifeedo Lite.

2. Routine Medications for Malnutrition (Generic)

Amoxicillin: Oral, used as a broad-spectrum antibiotic for children with uncomplicated SAM, typically 40 mg/kg twice daily.

Vitamin A: Given to treat deficiencies and support immune function.

Albendazole: Used for deworming in the second week of treatment.

Iron and Folic Acid (IFA) Syrup/Drops: Used to treat anemia, although some RUTF products are already fortified with iron.

Measles Vaccine: Administered if the child is over 9 months and not previously vaccinated.

Zinc Supplementation: Used in cases of continuing diarrhoea and to aid growth.

3. Appetite Stimulants (Appetite Management)

Cyproheptadine Syrup/Tablets: Often combined with Tricholine Citrate and Sorbitol (e.g., generic names: Cypon, Aptimust, Cyaptin) to improve appetite and aid weight gain.

Megestrol Acetate: A high-level appetite stimulant (e.g., Megesta 160).

Buclizine Syrup/Tablets: Another appetite stimulant, such as Longifene.

4. Therapeutic Diets (Hospital-Based)

For severely malnourished children with medical complications, hospitalized children are provided with specific formulas:

F-75: Therapeutic milk diet (75 kcal/100 ml) for the stabilization phase.

F-100: Therapeutic milk diet (100 kcal/100 ml) for the rehabilitation phase.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Jan Aushadhi Poshan (Malt-based food): A fortified malt-based food designed to boost daily nutritional intake and address malnutrition in children and adults. It is available in 500gm jars (plain and with Cocoa) and 30g sachets.

Jan Aushadhi Protein Powder: Available in various flavors (Chocolate, Vanilla, Kesar Pista) in 250g tins, designed to provide high protein, vitamins, and minerals.

Jan Aushadhi Janani: A protein-rich supplement formulated for females, enriched with DHA, folic acid, calcium, and iron.

Protein Bars: A convenient, fortified snack containing protein, vitamins, and minerals.

Immunity Bars: Nutritional bars designed for children and adults.

Iron & Folic Acid Supplements: Specific formulations such as Ferrous Ascorbate + Folic Acid Tablets are available to treat iron-deficiency anemia.

Jan Aushadhi Poshan (500g): ₹175

Jan Aushadhi Poshan with Cocoa (500g): ₹180

Protein Powder (All variants, 250g): ₹200

Jan Aushadhi Janani (250g): ₹225

Protein Bar (35g): ₹40

Jan Aushadhi Immunity Bar (10g): ₹10
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Ashwagandhadi Avaleha/Churna: Widely used to increase weight, BMI, and muscle mass.

Chyawanprash: A powerful Rasayana (rejuvenator) that boosts immunity and promotes energy.

Shatavari Kalp/Churna: Supports growth, enhances strength, and nourishes tissues.

Amritprasha Ghrita: Effective in improving weight, reducing general weakness (Dourbalya), and increasing appetite.

Punarnava Mandura: Frequently used for managing anemia, which is often associated with malnutrition.

Kushmanda Kalpa: Used for increasing weight in children.

Vidarikandadi Churna: Indicated for improving body mass and nourishing tissues.

Rajanyadi Churna: Often used to improve digestive health and appetite.

Brahmi Ghrita: Sometimes recommended for malnourished children to improve overall health.

Sandu Shishuvin Syrup: Designed to support nutrition in infants and children.

Balamritam/Balamritam Syrup (Sitaram Ayurveda): Specifically for children's nourishment, treating indigestion, and loss of appetite.

Kerala Ayurveda Balakalpam Syrup: Helps improve digestion, appetite, and immunity.

Himalaya Bonnisan Syrup: Aids in healthy growth and appetite in children.

Proyurveda Zimvit Syrup: Contains ashwagandha and other herbs to help with general weakness and anemia.

Revinto Wormicid Plus Syrup: Used to address the underlying cause of malnutrition, such as worm infestations.

Abhyanga (Massage): The use of oils like Chandana bala lakshadi taila or Bala ashwagandhadi taila is recommended to improve circulation and strength.

Medicated Milk: Ashwagandha ksheerapaka (milk decoction) is a traditional remedy to boost weight.
diseases.india_specific: Specific Nutritional Deficiencies in India Anemia (Iron Deficiency): Pale skin, dizziness, and fatigue. Vitamin A Deficiency: Night blindness or increased sensitivity to light. Mineral/Vitamin Deficiencies: Bleeding gums and cracked, swollen tongues.
diseases.important_notes:
Consultation Required: These medicines should be taken under the guidance of a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner to ensure correct dosage based on the child or adult’s specific condition.

Holistic Approach: Ayurvedic treatment for malnutrition is most effective when combined with a balanced, high-nutrition diet (Ahara) and lifestyle adjustments (Vihara), such as ensuring adequate sleep and consistent, nutritious, and freshly prepared meals.

Nutritional Rehab: These therapies work best alongside standard nutritional rehabilitation, particularly for severe, acute malnutrition

diseases.name: Tuberculosis
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Clinical Assessment & Screening

Symptom Screening: Any person with cough >2 weeks, fever >2 weeks, significant weight loss, or hemoptysis should be evaluated for TB. NTEP uses a 4-symptom screen (cough, fever, weight loss, night sweats) for active case finding.

Chest X-ray (Posteroanterior View): First-line radiological investigation showing upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, fibrosis, or miliary pattern. Used as initial screening tool at PHC/CHC level under NTEP.

Contact Tracing Assessment: All household contacts of confirmed TB patients must be screened, per NTEP guidelines. Children <6 years and immunocompromised contacts require prophylactic treatment.

2. Microbiological & Molecular Diagnostic Tests

Sputum Smear Microscopy (ZN Staining): Two sputum samples examined under microscope for Acid-Fast Bacilli (AFB). Sensitivity ~60-70% for pulmonary TB. Available at all Designated Microscopy Centres (DMCs) under NTEP.

CBNAAT/GeneXpert MTB/RIF: Cartridge-Based Nucleic Acid Amplification Test that simultaneously detects Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Rifampicin resistance within 2 hours. Sensitivity ~95% for smear-positive and ~70% for smear-negative TB. India has 7,767+ rapid molecular testing facilities under NTEP.

TrueNat MTB & MTB-RIF Dx: Indian-made (Molbio Diagnostics, Goa) portable, battery-operated molecular test for TB detection and Rifampicin resistance. Designed for peripheral laboratories with minimal infrastructure. Endorsed by WHO and widely deployed across India.

Sputum Culture (MGIT/LJ Media): Gold standard for TB diagnosis. Mycobacterial Growth Indicator Tube (MGIT) liquid culture gives results in 1-3 weeks; Löwenstein-Jensen (LJ) solid culture takes 4-8 weeks. Available at Culture & Drug Susceptibility Testing (C&DST) labs (87 labs across India under NTEP).

Line Probe Assay (LPA): FL-LPA detects Isoniazid and Rifampicin resistance; SL-LPA detects Fluoroquinolone and Injectable agent resistance. Used for rapid Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST) at intermediate reference labs.

3. Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST)

Universal DST (UDST): NTEP mandates every diagnosed TB patient must be tested for at least Rifampicin resistance before or at treatment initiation. This is a key policy distinguishing India's TB programme.

Phenotypic DST (Culture-Based): Tests bacterial growth against first-line drugs (HRZE) and second-line drugs (Fluoroquinolones, Aminoglycosides, Bedaquiline, Linezolid). Takes 4-12 weeks but remains the reference standard.

Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS): Emerging technology for comprehensive resistance profiling. Being piloted at select ICMR/NTEP reference labs for detecting resistance to newer drugs like Bedaquiline and Pretomanid.

4. Immunological & Serological Tests

Tuberculin Skin Test (TST/Mantoux Test): Intradermal injection of 5 TU PPD. Induration ≥10mm is positive in immunocompetent individuals; ≥5mm in HIV-positive/immunocompromised. Indicates TB infection (latent or active), not disease alone.

Interferon-Gamma Release Assay (IGRA): Blood-based test (QuantiFERON-TB Gold / T-SPOT.TB) measuring immune response to TB-specific antigens. More specific than TST as it is not affected by prior BCG vaccination. Used selectively in India due to cost.

5. Routine Blood & Biochemical Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): May show normocytic normochromic anemia, raised ESR, and lymphocytopenia. Leukocytosis may indicate secondary bacterial infection.

Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR): Non-specific inflammatory marker, typically elevated (>20 mm/hr) in active TB. Useful for monitoring treatment response.

Liver Function Tests (LFT): Baseline LFT mandatory before starting ATT as Isoniazid, Rifampicin, and Pyrazinamide are hepatotoxic. Monitor ALT/AST during treatment.

Renal Function Tests (KFT): Baseline creatinine and BUN required especially before prescribing Streptomycin, Kanamycin, or Amikacin (nephrotoxic/ototoxic aminoglycosides).

HIV Testing: NTEP mandates Provider-Initiated Testing & Counselling (PITC) for HIV in all diagnosed TB patients. TB-HIV co-management is critical.

Blood Glucose (Fasting/Random/HbA1c): Screen for diabetes as TB-DM comorbidity worsens outcomes. NTEP recommends bidirectional screening of all TB patients for diabetes and vice versa.

6. Special Investigations for Extra-Pulmonary TB

Pleural Fluid Analysis (ADA): Adenosine Deaminase Activity (ADA) >40 IU/L in pleural fluid is highly suggestive of tubercular pleural effusion. Combined with lymphocytic predominance and high protein.

CSF Analysis (TB Meningitis): CSF shows raised protein (100-500 mg/dL), low glucose (<45 mg/dL), lymphocytic pleocytosis (10-500 cells). ADA >10 IU/L supports TB meningitis diagnosis.

FNAC/Biopsy with Histopathology: Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology or tissue biopsy showing caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells is diagnostic. Used for lymph node TB, intestinal TB, bone TB.

Urine LAM Test (TB-LAM): Point-of-care lateral flow assay detecting Lipoarabinomannan antigen in urine. WHO-recommended for HIV-positive patients with advanced immunosuppression (CD4 <200).

7. Key Diagnostic Criteria (NTEP & WHO Guidelines)

A patient is classified as Bacteriologically Confirmed TB if positive on sputum smear microscopy, culture, or WHO-approved rapid molecular test (CBNAAT/TrueNat). Clinically Diagnosed TB is diagnosed by a clinician based on X-ray, histology, or strong clinical evidence without bacteriological confirmation. NTEP classifies TB as Drug-Sensitive TB (DS-TB) or Drug-Resistant TB (DR-TB: MDR-TB, Pre-XDR-TB, XDR-TB) based on DST results.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Anti-TB Drugs (DS-TB: NTEP Standard Regimen)

Isoniazid (H): Bactericidal, oral. Intensive Phase (IP): 5 mg/kg/day for 8 weeks (56 doses). Continuation Phase (CP): 5 mg/kg/day for 16 weeks (112 doses). Most potent early bactericidal activity. Always co-prescribe Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) to prevent peripheral neuropathy.

Rifampicin (R): Bactericidal, oral. 10 mg/kg/day (max 600mg). Given throughout IP and CP. Most important sterilizing drug. Turns urine/tears orange-red (counsel patients). Key drug for determining MDR-TB.

Pyrazinamide (Z): Bactericidal/sterilizing, oral. 25 mg/kg/day. Given during IP only (8 weeks). Effective against semi-dormant bacilli in acidic intracellular environment. Hepatotoxic — monitor LFTs.

Ethambutol (E): Bacteriostatic, oral. 15 mg/kg/day. Given during both IP and CP (total 24 weeks under NTEP). Can cause optic neuritis — advise patients to report visual changes immediately.

Fixed-Dose Combinations (FDC): NTEP provides daily FDCs in weight-band dosing: HRZE (4-FDC) for IP, HRE (3-FDC) for CP. Weight bands: 25-39 kg (2 tabs), 40-54 kg (3 tabs), 55-69 kg (4 tabs), ≥70 kg (5 tabs).

2. Second-Line & MDR-TB Drugs

Bedaquiline (Bdq): Diarylquinoline, oral. 400mg daily for 2 weeks then 200mg thrice weekly for 22 weeks. First new TB drug in 40+ years. Core drug for MDR-TB regimens. Requires ECG monitoring for QTc prolongation.

Pretomanid (Pa): Nitroimidazole, oral. 200mg daily. Used in the BPaLM regimen (Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid ± Moxifloxacin) approved by India's MoHFW for MDR-TB. Shortens treatment to 6-9 months.

Linezolid (Lzd): Oxazolidinone, oral. 600mg daily. Key drug in BPaLM and all-oral MDR-TB regimens. Monitor for myelosuppression (CBC monthly) and peripheral neuropathy.

Moxifloxacin (Mfx): Fluoroquinolone, oral. 400mg daily. Used in both shorter MDR-TB regimens and BPaLM. Also used when Isoniazid resistance is detected.

Levofloxacin (Lfx): Fluoroquinolone, oral. 750-1000mg daily. Alternative to Moxifloxacin in MDR-TB regimens. Preferred in pediatric DR-TB due to better safety data.

Clofazimine (Cfz): Riminophenazine, oral. 100mg daily. Used in shorter MDR-TB regimen. Causes reversible skin discoloration (brownish-black). Originally an anti-leprosy drug.

Cycloserine/Terizidone (Cs/Trd): 250-750mg daily based on weight. Used in longer MDR-TB regimens. Monitor for CNS side effects (seizures, psychosis, depression).

Delamanid (Dlm): Nitroimidazole, oral. 100mg twice daily for 24 weeks. Alternative Group C drug for MDR-TB. WHO-recommended for children ≥3 years. ECG monitoring required.

3. Supportive & Adjunct Medications

Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6): 10-25mg daily. Co-prescribed with Isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy. Essential for malnourished patients and those with diabetes or HIV co-infection.

Corticosteroids (Prednisolone/Dexamethasone): Used in TB meningitis (reduces mortality by ~25%), pericardial TB, severe pleural effusion, and adrenal TB. Prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg tapered over 6-8 weeks.

Iron + Folic Acid Tablets: For concurrent anemia management. NTEP recommends addressing nutritional deficiencies alongside ATT.

Antiemetics (Ondansetron/Domperidone): For managing nausea/vomiting, common side effects of ATT, especially during the Intensive Phase. Helps ensure treatment adherence.

4. Government Programme Support (NTEP)

All first-line and second-line TB drugs are provided FREE under NTEP through DOTS centres. Nikshay Poshan Yojana provides ₹500/month DBT to all notified TB patients for nutritional support throughout treatment. NTEP has disbursed ₹2,781 crores to ~1 crore beneficiaries cumulatively.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Rifampicin + Isoniazid Tablets IP (450mg + 300mg): Fixed-dose combination anti-TB tablet. Core first-line ATT drug available at Jan Aushadhi Kendras at significantly reduced cost compared to branded equivalents. Pack of 10 tablets.

Ethambutol Tablets IP 800mg: First-line anti-TB drug used in both Intensive and Continuation phases. Available as affordable generic at PMBJP outlets. Pack of 10 tablets.

Linezolid Tablets IP 600mg: Critical second-line drug used in BPaLM regimen for MDR-TB. One of the most expensive TB drugs — Jan Aushadhi pricing makes it significantly more affordable. Pack of 10 tablets.

Levofloxacin Tablets IP 500mg: Fluoroquinolone antibiotic used in MDR-TB regimens and as empirical therapy for respiratory infections. Available in affordable generic form. Pack of 10 tablets.

Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) Tablets: Essential co-prescription with Isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy. Low-cost supplement critical for TB treatment compliance.

Iron + Folic Acid Tablets: For managing TB-associated anemia. Ferrous Sulphate + Folic Acid combination available at Jan Aushadhi stores. Essential supportive care during ATT.

Jan Aushadhi Protein Powder: High-protein nutritional supplement (available in Chocolate, Vanilla, Kesar Pista flavors, 250g tins) to address the severe protein-calorie malnutrition commonly seen in TB patients.

Jan Aushadhi Poshan (Malt-based Food): Fortified malt-based nutritional supplement (500g jars, plain and Cocoa variants) to boost daily caloric intake during TB recovery.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For symptomatic management of fever and body aches during TB treatment. Affordable OTC analgesic/antipyretic. Pack of 10 tablets.

Multivitamin Tablets: General nutritional supplementation to support immune recovery during TB treatment. Contains essential vitamins and minerals.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Rifampicin + Isoniazid (450mg+300mg), 10's: ₹35.03

Ethambutol Tablets 800mg, 10's: ₹22.50

Linezolid Tablets 600mg, 10's: ₹176.16

Levofloxacin Tablets 500mg, 10's: ₹18.50

Protein Powder (All variants, 250g): ₹200

Jan Aushadhi Poshan (500g): ₹175

Jan Aushadhi Poshan with Cocoa (500g): ₹180

Paracetamol 500mg, 10's: ₹3.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Sitopaladi Churna: Classical Ayurvedic formulation for Kasa (cough) and Shwasa (respiratory distress). Contains Mishri, Vamshalochana, Pippali, Ela, and Twak. Given 3g thrice daily with honey. Helps relieve persistent cough and clears mucus in TB patients as adjunct therapy.

Chyawanprash (Rasayana): Powerful Ayurvedic rejuvenator and immunomodulator. Contains Amla (Vitamin C-rich), Ashwagandha, Guduchi, and 40+ herbs. 10-20g daily with warm milk. Boosts Ojas (vital immunity) depleted in Rajayakshma.

Vasavaleha / Vasa Avaleha: Prepared from Adhatoda vasica (Vasa), the primary Ayurvedic herb for Raktapitta (hemoptysis) and chronic cough. Effective in managing blood-streaked sputum and bronchial inflammation in TB.

Draksharishta (Drakshasava): Fermented Ayurvedic preparation of Draksha (grapes) with Dhataki, Ela, Twak, and Madhu. Used as Balya (strength-promoting) and Raktavardhaka (blood-enriching) Rasayana in wasting diseases including Rajayakshma.

Ashwagandha Churna/Capsules (Withania somnifera): Adaptogenic Rasayana that enhances physical strength, supports immune resilience, and combats fatigue. Research shows immunomodulatory effects that complement ATT. 3-6g daily with milk.

Swarna Malini Vasant Ras: Classical Herbo-mineral preparation containing Swarna Bhasma (gold calx) and Mauktik Bhasma (pearl calx). Traditionally used for chronic fever, cough, and Rajayakshma. Must be administered under strict Ayurvedic practitioner supervision.

Vasant Kusumakar Ras: Contains bhasmas of Suvarna, Raupya, Vanga, Naga, Abhraka, and Mauktika with herbal decoctions. Classically indicated for Kshaya (consumption/TB), Prameha (diabetes), and Vata disorders.

Mahalaxmi Vilas Ras: Classical formulation with Abhrak Bhasma, Dhattura, and herbal ingredients. Used in chronic respiratory conditions including Rajayakshma for its Ojo-vardhaka (immunity-enhancing) and Deepana (digestive stimulant) properties.

Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia): Powerful immunomodulator and Rasayana. Acts as Aamapachana (toxin-digestive) and Jwaraghna (antipyretic). Available as Guduchi Satva, Guduchi Ghana Vati, or fresh juice. 500mg-1g twice daily.

Pippali Rasayana (Piper longum): Enhances respiratory function and bioavailability of other medicines (Yogavahi property). Classical Rasayana specifically indicated for Kshaya Roga. Graduated dosing protocol (Vardhamana Pippali).

Bhringarajasava: Fermented Ayurvedic preparation. Published clinical study (Ayu 2012) demonstrated its efficacy as Naimittika Rasayana in Rajayakshma patients, showing improvement in weight, appetite, and ESR levels when used alongside ATT.

Chitraka-Haritaki Avaleha: Combination of Chitraka (Plumbago zeylanica) and Haritaki (Terminalia chebula). Used for Kasa, Shwasa, and digestive disorders. Improves appetite and digestion compromised in TB patients.

Eladi Churna: Classical formulation containing Ela (cardamom), Twak (cinnamon), and Patra (bay leaf). Used for Kasa and Kshaya. Helps manage respiratory symptoms and improve palatability of food.

Dhanvantara Gutika: Classical preparation indicated for Rajayakshma, Kasa, and Jwara. Contains Dashamoola and other herbs. Used for managing chronic fever, cough, and debility associated with TB.

Himalaya Septilin Tablets/Syrup: Proprietary Ayurvedic immunomodulator containing Guduchi, Amalaki, and Maharasnadi Quath. Supports immune function during TB treatment. Available OTC across India.

Dabur Chyawanprash: Most widely available branded Chyawanprash in India. Contains 40+ Ayurvedic ingredients. Recommended as daily immunomodulatory supplement during and after TB treatment for recovery.

Kottakkal Indukantham Kashayam/Ghritam: From Kottakkal Arya Vaidya Sala. Classical formulation for Kshaya, Shosha (wasting), and Daurbalya (weakness). Nourishes depleted Dhatus and promotes tissue regeneration.

Abhyanga (Oil Massage) with Bala Ashwagandhadi Taila: Therapeutic oil massage to improve circulation, reduce muscle wasting, and promote strength in debilitated TB patients. Bala and Ashwagandha base provides Balya and Brimhana (nourishing) properties.
diseases.india_specific:
TB-HIV Co-infection: India has one of the highest TB-HIV co-infection burdens globally. HIV-positive individuals are 15-22 times more likely to develop active TB, and co-infection dramatically increases mortality.

TB-Diabetes Comorbidity: India's rising diabetes epidemic (77+ million cases) significantly increases TB risk. Diabetic patients have 2-3 times higher risk of developing active TB and poorer treatment outcomes.

Malnutrition & Undernutrition: India's high malnutrition rates (especially in tribal/rural populations) are a major driver of TB. The Government provides ₹500/month under Nikshay Poshan Yojana to address this.

Drug-Resistant TB (MDR/XDR-TB): India has the highest burden of MDR-TB globally. Approximately 1.5 lakh new MDR-TB cases emerge annually, driven by incomplete treatment, poor drug quality, and unregulated private sector prescribing.

Overcrowding & Poor Ventilation: Dense urban slums, overcrowded housing, poorly ventilated workplaces, and packed public transport in Indian cities create ideal conditions for airborne Mycobacterium tuberculosis transmission.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Ayurveda as Adjunct Only: Ayurvedic treatments must NEVER replace standard anti-TB drugs (ATT). TB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis and requires bactericidal antibiotics for cure. Ayurvedic formulations are supportive adjunct therapy for managing side effects, boosting immunity, and aiding recovery. Incomplete ATT leads to drug-resistant TB which is far harder and costlier to treat.

Mandatory DOTS Compliance: All TB patients must complete the full course of ATT under NTEP's Directly Observed Treatment (DOT). Treatment interruption is the single biggest cause of MDR-TB emergence. Rural health workers must emphasize that feeling better does NOT mean stopping medicines. The full DS-TB regimen is 6 months (IP: 2 months HRZE + CP: 4 months HRE).

Hepatotoxicity Monitoring: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, and Pyrazinamide are all hepatotoxic. Baseline LFTs must be done before starting ATT. If ALT rises >3x upper normal with symptoms or >5x without symptoms, stop ATT and refer to higher centre. Ayurvedic hepatoprotectives (Guduchi, Bhumyamalaki) may help but do NOT replace medical monitoring.

Drug Interactions — Rifampicin Warning: Rifampicin is a potent CYP450 inducer that reduces the effectiveness of many drugs including oral contraceptives, antiretrovirals (ARVs), antidiabetics, and anticoagulants. All co-medications must be reviewed. HIV-TB patients need ARV dose adjustments. Inform patients on oral contraceptives to use additional barrier methods.

Holistic Nutritional Support: TB treatment is most effective when combined with adequate nutrition (high-protein, high-calorie diet). Leverage Nikshay Poshan Yojana (₹500/month), Ayurvedic Rasayana supplements, and community support through Nikshay Mitra initiative. Address malnutrition, diabetes, and substance abuse (smoking, alcohol) concurrently as these worsen TB outcomes.

diseases.name: Dengue Fever
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Clinical Assessment & Triage

WHO/NCVBDC Clinical Classification: Dengue Fever (DF) — fever + 2 of: headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, hemorrhagic manifestations, leukopenia. Dengue Hemorrhagic Fever (DHF) — DF + plasma leakage + thrombocytopenia (<100,000/mm³). Dengue Shock Syndrome (DSS) — DHF + circulatory failure.

Tourniquet Test (Rumpel-Leede Test): Inflate BP cuff midway between SBP and DBP for 5 minutes. Positive if ≥20 petechiae per 1 square inch. Part of WHO dengue case definition. Simple bedside test for capillary fragility feasible at PHC level.

Warning Signs Assessment: Abdominal pain, persistent vomiting (≥3/24hrs), clinical fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, lethargy/restlessness, hepatomegaly >2cm, rising hematocrit with falling platelets. ANY warning sign = hospitalize immediately.

2. Serological & Antigen-Based Tests

NS1 Antigen Test (ELISA/Rapid): Detects dengue Non-Structural protein 1 in blood. Positive from Day 1 to Day 5-7 of fever. Sensitivity 80-97% in first 3 days. Most useful EARLY diagnostic test. Available as rapid card test at PHC/CHC level.

Dengue IgM Antibody (MAC-ELISA): Detectable from Day 5 onwards, peaks at Day 10-14. Indicates current or recent primary infection. Positive IgM = Probable Dengue. Used by NCVBDC sentinel surveillance labs across India.

Dengue IgG Antibody (ELISA): Rises slowly in primary infection (after 2 weeks). In SECONDARY infection, IgG rises rapidly (by Day 1-2) with high titers — indicates previous dengue exposure and higher risk for DHF/DSS. IgM:IgG ratio helps distinguish primary vs secondary infection.

NS1 + IgM/IgG Combo Rapid Test: Combined rapid diagnostic kit testing all three markers simultaneously. Ideal for point-of-care diagnosis at PHC/CHC. Provides comprehensive picture regardless of day of illness. Widely available in India (₹200-400 per test).

3. Molecular Diagnostic Tests

RT-PCR (Reverse Transcriptase-PCR): Gold standard for dengue confirmation and serotype identification (DENV-1 to DENV-4). Positive in first 5 days (viremic phase). Available at NCVBDC Apex Referral Labs and NIV Pune network.

Viral Culture: Isolation of dengue virus from blood sample during first 5 days. Research/reference lab tool, not used for routine diagnosis. Takes 7-10 days.

4. Routine Hematological Monitoring (CRITICAL for management)

Complete Blood Count (CBC) with Hematocrit: MOST IMPORTANT monitoring test. Check every 6-12 hours in warning sign patients. Rising hematocrit (>20% baseline) = plasma leakage. Falling hematocrit with persistent shock = internal hemorrhage. Leukopenia (<5000/mm³) is typical in dengue.

Platelet Count (Serial Monitoring): Progressive thrombocytopenia is hallmark of DHF. Platelet <100,000/mm³ = meet DHF criteria. Platelet <20,000/mm³ with bleeding = consider platelet transfusion. Monitor every 6-12 hours during critical phase (Day 3-7).

Hematocrit (Packed Cell Volume - PCV): Single most important parameter for fluid management. Rising PCV = give IV fluids. Falling PCV without clinical improvement = suspect internal bleeding. Target: maintain PCV within 20% of baseline.

5. Biochemical & Organ Function Tests

Liver Function Tests (LFT): AST/ALT elevation common in dengue (AST typically rises more than ALT). AST/ALT >1000 IU/L indicates severe hepatic involvement. Monitor for hepatic encephalopathy.

Serum Albumin: Hypoalbuminemia (<3.5 g/dL) indicates plasma leakage. Correlates with severity of DHF.

Coagulation Profile (PT/INR, APTT): Assess for coagulopathy in severe dengue. Prolonged PT/APTT with bleeding suggests DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation).

Serum Electrolytes & Renal Function: Monitor Na+, K+, creatinine for fluid/electrolyte imbalances, especially during aggressive IV fluid therapy. Hyponatremia is common.

Blood Glucose: Hypoglycemia can occur in severe dengue, especially in children. Monitor regularly.

6. Imaging (If Clinically Indicated)

Chest X-ray (Right Lateral Decubitus): Detects right-sided pleural effusion — earliest imaging sign of plasma leakage. Should be done in all DHF-suspected cases.

Abdominal Ultrasound: Detects ascites, gallbladder wall thickening (>3mm — pericholecystic edema), pleural effusion, hepatomegaly. Non-invasive evidence of plasma leakage.

7. Key Diagnostic Criteria (NCVBDC/WHO 2023)

Probable Dengue: Fever + NS1 positive OR IgM positive. Confirmed Dengue: RT-PCR positive or viral culture positive. DHF requires ALL four criteria: fever, hemorrhagic tendencies, thrombocytopenia (<100,000/mm³), AND evidence of plasma leakage (rising HCT >20%, pleural effusion, ascites, hypoalbuminemia).
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Fluid Management (CORNERSTONE of Dengue Treatment)

Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS): First-line rehydration for Group A (DF without warning signs) patients managed at home. Encourage 2-3 liters/day of ORS, coconut water, rice water, dal water, fresh fruit juice. Monitor urine output (should be ≥0.5 ml/kg/hr).

Intravenous Normal Saline (0.9% NS): First-line IV crystalloid for Group B (warning signs) and Group C (severe dengue) patients. Initial bolus 5-10 ml/kg/hr for 1-2 hours, then titrate based on hematocrit, urine output, and vitals.

Ringer's Lactate (RL): Alternative IV crystalloid. Preferred by some clinicians due to physiological electrolyte composition. Use interchangeably with NS based on availability.

Colloids (Dextran 40 / Gelatin-based): Reserved for patients NOT responding to crystalloid boluses, or with refractory shock. Use only when hematocrit remains elevated despite 2-3 crystalloid boluses. Monitor for anaphylaxis.

2. Antipyretic & Symptomatic Treatment

Paracetamol (Acetaminophen): ONLY recommended antipyretic for dengue. 500-1000mg every 4-6 hours (max 4g/day adults, weight-based in children). Reduces fever and relieves headache/myalgia.

AVOID NSAIDs/Aspirin: Ibuprofen, Aspirin, Diclofenac, Nimesulide are STRICTLY CONTRAINDICATED in dengue. They impair platelet function, increase bleeding risk, and can cause gastric hemorrhage. This is the single most important prescribing warning.

Antiemetics (Ondansetron/Domperidone): For persistent nausea/vomiting. Ondansetron 4-8mg IV/oral preferred. Helps maintain oral intake and prevent dehydration.

H2 Blockers/PPIs (Ranitidine/Pantoprazole): Gastroprotective agents for patients with abdominal pain, nausea, or GI hemorrhage risk. Pantoprazole 40mg IV/oral once daily.

3. Blood Product Transfusion (ONLY when indicated)

Platelet Transfusion: ONLY indicated when: (a) Platelet <10,000/mm³ without bleeding, (b) Platelet <20,000/mm³ with active mucosal bleeding, (c) Planned invasive procedure with platelet <50,000/mm³. Do NOT transfuse platelets for counts alone — it does not prevent progression.

Packed Red Blood Cells (PRBC): For significant hemorrhage with falling hematocrit and hemodynamic instability. Cross-match and prepare blood products early in severe dengue.

Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP): For coagulopathy with active bleeding (prolonged PT/INR, APTT). Give 10-15 ml/kg.

4. Severe Dengue / DSS Emergency Management

IV Fluid Resuscitation Protocol (DSS): Immediate NS/RL bolus 10-20 ml/kg over 15-30 minutes. If no improvement, repeat bolus. If still no response, switch to colloids. Monitor hematocrit every 1-2 hours. Target: urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr, stable BP, declining hematocrit.

Vasopressors (Noradrenaline/Dopamine): For refractory shock not responding to fluids. Use only in ICU setting with invasive monitoring. Indicates severe DSS requiring tertiary care referral.

Oxygen Therapy: For patients with SpO2 <95%, respiratory distress, pleural effusion with dyspnea. High-flow nasal cannula or NIV as needed.

5. NCVBDC Classification-Based Management

Group A (Home): DF without warning signs. Oral fluids, paracetamol, daily platelet monitoring, advise return if warning signs appear. Group B (Hospital): DF with warning signs OR DHF without shock. IV fluids, serial CBC/PCV every 6-12 hours. Group C (ICU/Referral): DSS, severe hemorrhage, organ impairment. Aggressive IV fluids, blood products, ICU care.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: ONLY safe antipyretic for dengue fever. Must be used instead of Ibuprofen/Aspirin (which are contraindicated). Available at all PMBJP Kendras. Pack of 10 tablets.

Paracetamol Syrup 250mg/5ml: Pediatric formulation for children with dengue fever. Weight-based dosing essential. Available at Jan Aushadhi stores.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Papaya Leaf Juice/Extract (Carica papaya — Erandakarkati): Most widely studied Ayurvedic/natural remedy for dengue. Multiple clinical studies show significant platelet count increase (15-20% within 48-72 hours). Commercially available as Caripill tablets (Micro Labs). 30-50ml fresh juice twice daily or 1100mg tablet thrice daily. Endorsed by AYUSH practitioners across India.

Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia — Amrita): Powerful Jwaraghna (antipyretic) and immunomodulator. Guduchi Ghana Vati is specifically studied for dengue fever management. Published research (Ayu 2014) confirms anti-inflammatory activity. 500mg-1g twice daily as decoction or tablet.

Sudarshan Ghana Vati/Churna: Classical Sarva Jwarahara (antipyretic) formulation. Used in published Ayurvedic case studies for dengue management alongside conventional treatment. Amapachana (toxin-clearing) and Tridoshahara. 2 tablets thrice daily.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum — Holy Basil): Traditional Jwaraghna and Krimighna (antimicrobial). Tulsi tea with ginger (5-7 leaves + 1-inch ginger) 2-3 cups daily. Helps reduce fever, boost immunity, and relieve body ache. Widely available in every Indian household.

Neem (Azadirachta indica — Nimba): Antiviral, blood-purifying (Raktashodhaka), and Pittahara. Neem leaf decoction (handful of leaves boiled in 250ml water) 50-60ml twice daily. Supports immune defense and reduces viral load. Tamil Nadu government has promoted Neem for dengue.

Sitopaladi Churna: Classical formulation indicated for Kasa, Jwara, and Kaphapradhana diseases. Used in published dengue case reports alongside Guduchi and Pippali. 3g with honey at 3-hour intervals during acute fever phase.

Chirayita / Kirata Tikta (Swertia chirata): Bitter herb classically used for Sannipata Jwara (mixed-dosha fever, correlating to dengue). Powerful Jwarahara and Deepana. Decoction 20-30ml twice daily.

Amla/Amalaki (Emblica officinalis): Richest natural source of Vitamin C. Powerful Rasayana and Raktapittahara (controls hemorrhage). Supports platelet recovery and immune function. Fresh juice or capsule 500mg-1g twice daily.

Tribhuvan Kirti Ras: Classical Rasa preparation specifically indicated for Vishamajwara (intermittent/irregular fever). Contains Shuddha Hingula, Shuddha Vatsanabha, Pippali, Tankana. 125-250mg twice daily with honey. Strict practitioner supervision required.

Sanjivani Vati: Classical formulation from Sharangdhara Samhita. Contains Vidanga, Guduchi, Shunthi, Pippali, Haritaki, Vibhitaki, Amalaki, Vacha, Bhallataka Shuddha. Jwaraghna and Vishaghna (antipyretic and antitoxic). 250-500mg twice daily.

Mahasudarshan Churna/Kadha: Extended version of Sudarshan Churna with 40+ herbs. Potent Jwarahara for all types of fever. Used widely across India during dengue season. 3-5g twice daily with warm water.

Coconut Water (Narikela Jala): Ayurvedic Pittashamaka (Pitta-pacifying) and natural electrolyte replenisher. Rich in potassium and magnesium. 200-300ml every 4-6 hours. Prevents dehydration — the primary cause of dengue mortality.

Pomegranate Juice (Dadima Rasa): Raktapittahara and Balya. Rich in iron, antioxidants, and Vitamin C. Combats dengue-related anemia and fatigue. 100-200ml twice daily.

Dabur Giloy Neem Tulsi Juice: Widely available proprietary formulation combining three key anti-dengue herbs. Supports immunity during viral fever season. 15-30ml twice daily with water.

Himalaya Guduchi Tablets: Standardized Guduchi extract (Tinospora cordifolia) 250mg tablets. Convenient dosage form for immunomodulatory and antipyretic support. 1-2 tablets twice daily.

Baidyanath Sudarshan Ghana Vati: Well-known branded formulation of the classical Sudarshan preparation. 2 tablets thrice daily for fever management. Available at most Ayurvedic pharmacies across India.

Patanjali Giloy Ghanvati: Affordable and widely distributed Guduchi extract tablets from Patanjali. 1-2 tablets twice daily. Available at Patanjali stores and through their distribution network across rural India.

Wheatgrass Juice (Godhuma Ankura Rasa): Emerging evidence for platelet-boosting properties. Rich in chlorophyll, vitamins, and minerals. 30-50ml fresh juice twice daily. Often used alongside papaya leaf juice.
diseases.india_specific:
Monsoon Epidemic Pattern: India reports 100,000-200,000+ dengue cases annually with peak transmission during July-November monsoon season. All four DENV serotypes (DENV-1 to DENV-4) circulate in India. Post-monsoon stagnant water creates Aedes aegypti breeding grounds.

Urban Slum Vulnerability: Dense urban populations with poor sanitation, open water storage containers (matkas, drums, coolers), and limited municipal vector control are highest-risk zones. Delhi, Kerala, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, and West Bengal report highest caseloads.

Concurrent Infections (Dual/Triple Fever): During monsoon, patients may have co-infections of dengue + malaria, dengue + chikungunya, or dengue + typhoid (all transmitted in same season/geography). This complicates diagnosis and treatment — always test for all three in febrile patients during monsoon.

Thrombocytopenia Panic & Unnecessary Platelet Transfusions: A major India-specific problem — patients and families panic over falling platelet counts, leading to unnecessary and expensive platelet transfusions. NCVBDC guidelines clearly state: platelet transfusion is needed ONLY when platelet count <10,000/mm³ WITHOUT bleeding OR <20,000/mm³ WITH active bleeding. Death in dengue is from dehydration/shock, NOT low platelets alone.

Pediatric & Pregnancy Vulnerability: Children under 15 are most affected in endemic areas. Dengue in pregnancy carries risk of vertical transmission, preterm delivery, and neonatal thrombocytopenia. Pregnant women with dengue require close hospital monitoring.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — No Antiviral Exists for Dengue: There is NO specific antiviral drug for dengue. Treatment is ENTIRELY supportive — fluid management is the cornerstone. Ayurvedic remedies support symptom relief and immune recovery but cannot kill the dengue virus. Death in dengue is preventable with timely fluid resuscitation.

NEVER Give Aspirin/NSAIDs: Aspirin, Ibuprofen, Diclofenac, and Nimesulide are STRICTLY CONTRAINDICATED in dengue. They impair platelet function and increase hemorrhage risk. ONLY Paracetamol is safe for fever. This is the single most dangerous prescribing error in dengue and must be communicated to every health worker.

Platelet Panic — Educate Against Unnecessary Transfusion: Falling platelet counts cause extreme anxiety in Indian families, often leading to demands for expensive platelet transfusions. Educate that: (a) platelets recover naturally in 7-10 days, (b) transfusion is needed ONLY below 10,000/mm³ without bleeding or below 20,000/mm³ with active bleeding, (c) death occurs from DEHYDRATION/SHOCK, not low platelets alone.

Critical Phase Monitoring (Day 3-7): The most dangerous period in dengue is the DEFERVESCENCE phase (when fever breaks, Day 3-7). This is when plasma leakage, hemorrhage, and shock occur. Patients must NOT be sent home when fever drops — they need closest monitoring during this period. Serial CBC/PCV every 6-12 hours.

Holistic Ayurvedic Support — Timing Matters: Papaya leaf juice, Guduchi, and Tulsi are most effective when started EARLY (Day 1-3 of fever) alongside proper hydration. During the critical phase (Day 3-7), medical fluid management takes absolute priority. Post-recovery (Day 7+), Rasayana therapy (Chyawanprash, Ashwagandha) helps restore strength. Ayurvedic herbs should complement, never delay, hospital care.

diseases.name: Diabetes (Type 1 & 2)
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Screening Tests

Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG): Blood glucose after 8-hour overnight fast. Normal <100 mg/dL, Prediabetes (IFG) 100-125 mg/dL, Diabetes ≥126 mg/dL. Requires two readings on separate days for diagnosis. Available at all PHC/CHC levels.

Random Plasma Glucose (RPG): Non-fasting blood glucose. Diabetes diagnosed if ≥200 mg/dL WITH classic symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, unexplained weight loss). Useful for opportunistic screening.

Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT): 75g glucose load, measure at 0 and 2 hours. 2-hr value: Normal <140, Prediabetes (IGT) 140-199, Diabetes ≥200 mg/dL. Gold standard for prediabetes detection. ICMR recommends for high-risk individuals.

2. Diagnostic & Monitoring Tests

HbA1c (Glycated Hemoglobin): Reflects average blood glucose over 2-3 months. Normal <5.7%, Prediabetes 5.7-6.4%, Diabetes ≥6.5%. ICMR/RSSDI target for treatment: <7.0% for most adults. Not affected by fasting state.

Postprandial Blood Glucose (PPBG): Blood glucose 2 hours after meal. Target <180 mg/dL (ICMR). Important in Indian context as postprandial spikes are a major issue with carbohydrate-heavy Indian diets.

Self-Monitoring of Blood Glucose (SMBG): Glucometer-based home monitoring. Essential for insulin users. Only 16.7% of Indian diabetics practice SMBG (ICMR-INDIAB study). Cost and education are barriers.

Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM): Emerging technology — sensor-based continuous glucose tracking. Limited to urban/tertiary centres in India due to cost (₹3,000-6,000/sensor).

3. Type 1 Diabetes-Specific Tests

C-Peptide Level: Measures endogenous insulin production. Low/undetectable C-peptide (<0.6 ng/mL) confirms Type 1 DM / absolute insulin deficiency. Helps distinguish Type 1 from Type 2.

Autoantibodies Panel: GAD65 antibodies, IA-2 antibodies, Insulin autoantibodies (IAA), ZnT8 antibodies. Positive in 85-90% of Type 1 DM at diagnosis. Confirms autoimmune etiology.

Urine/Blood Ketones: Urine ketone strips (nitroprusside test) or blood beta-hydroxybutyrate. Positive ketones + hyperglycemia = DKA risk. Essential for Type 1 DM monitoring. Blood ketones >3 mmol/L = severe DKA.

4. Complication Screening Tests (Mandatory Annual)

Fundoscopy / Retinal Screening: Dilated eye exam or digital fundus photography annually. Detects diabetic retinopathy — leading cause of preventable blindness in working-age Indians. NPCDCS programme provides free screening.

Urine Albumin-to-Creatinine Ratio (UACR): Spot urine test for microalbuminuria (30-300 mg/g). Earliest marker of diabetic nephropathy. Annual screening recommended by ICMR. Microalbuminuria = start ACE inhibitor/ARB.

Serum Creatinine & eGFR: Assess kidney function. eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² = CKD Stage 3+. Diabetic nephropathy is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease in India.

Lipid Profile (Fasting): Total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, triglycerides. Diabetic dyslipidemia (high TG, low HDL) is common. LDL target <100 mg/dL (or <70 if CVD present). ICMR recommends annual testing.

ECG & Cardiovascular Risk Assessment: Annual 12-lead ECG. Diabetes doubles CVD risk. Screen for silent myocardial ischemia, especially in Type 2 DM >40 years.

Foot Examination (Monofilament + Pulse Check): 10g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament for neuropathy + pedal pulse palpation for peripheral artery disease. Annual exam prevents diabetic foot amputations.

Thyroid Function Test (TST/Free T4): Type 1 DM has 15-30% association with autoimmune thyroid disease. Annual thyroid screening recommended for all Type 1 patients.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria (ICMR/WHO/ADA)

Type 1 DM: Absolute insulin deficiency, positive autoantibodies, low C-peptide, prone to DKA. Usually presents <30 years but can occur at any age. Requires lifelong insulin. Type 2 DM: Insulin resistance ± relative deficiency. FPG ≥126 or OGTT 2hr ≥200 or HbA1c ≥6.5% or RPG ≥200 with symptoms. Any ONE criterion on TWO separate occasions confirms diagnosis.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Type 2 DM — First-Line Oral Medications

Metformin (Biguanide): First-line drug per ICMR/RSSDI/ADA. Start 500mg once daily with meals, titrate to 1000mg twice daily. Reduces hepatic glucose output. Weight-neutral/slight loss. Contraindicated if eGFR <30. Monitor Vitamin B12 levels annually.

Glimepiride / Gliclazide (Sulfonylureas): Second-line if Metformin alone insufficient. Glimepiride 1-4mg daily, Gliclazide 40-320mg daily. Stimulates insulin secretion. Risk of hypoglycemia and weight gain. Gliclazide MR preferred for lower hypoglycemia risk.

Sitagliptin / Vildagliptin / Teneligliptin (DPP-4 Inhibitors): Add-on therapy. Weight-neutral, low hypoglycemia risk. Teneligliptin widely prescribed in India (most affordable DPP-4i). Not recommended as sole add-on per ACP 2024 guidelines.

Empagliflozin / Dapagliflozin (SGLT2 Inhibitors): Recommended add-on per ICMR/ADA when HbA1c above target despite Metformin. Proven cardiovascular and renal protection. Causes glycosuria — counsel about genital infections. Monitor for euglycemic DKA.

2. Type 2 DM — Injectable Therapies

Insulin Glargine / Insulin Degludec (Basal Insulin): Long-acting insulin for Type 2 DM when oral agents fail. Start 10 IU or 0.2 IU/kg at bedtime. Titrate by 2-4 IU every 3-7 days targeting FPG 80-130 mg/dL. Available as pens and vials.

Premix Insulin (30/70, 50/50): Mix of rapid + intermediate insulin. Commonly prescribed in Indian primary care for Type 2 DM. Given before breakfast and dinner. Brands: Mixtard, Humalog Mix, NovoMix.

Semaglutide / Liraglutide (GLP-1 Receptor Agonists): Weekly (Semaglutide) or daily (Liraglutide) injection. Proven mortality reduction (ADA/ACP 2024). Significant weight loss (4-6 kg). Expensive in India but increasingly used.

3. Type 1 DM — Insulin Regimens

Basal-Bolus Regimen: Basal insulin (Glargine/Degludec/Detemir) once daily + Rapid-acting insulin (Aspart/Lispro/Glulisine) before each meal. Gold standard for Type 1 DM. Requires carbohydrate counting and SMBG.

Insulin Pump Therapy (CSII): Continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion via pump. Most physiological delivery. Limited to urban tertiary centres in India due to cost (₹2-5 lakh device + ₹5,000/month consumables).

Human Insulin (Regular + NPH): Older, cheaper regimen. Regular insulin before meals + NPH at bedtime. Still widely used in rural India due to cost advantage. Available under NPCDCS free drug scheme.

4. Supportive & Complication Management

ACE Inhibitors (Enalapril/Ramipril): For diabetic nephropathy (microalbuminuria/proteinuria) AND hypertension. Renoprotective independent of BP. First-line antihypertensive in diabetics.

Statins (Atorvastatin/Rosuvastatin): For diabetic dyslipidemia. All Type 2 DM patients >40 years should be on statin therapy per ICMR guidelines. Atorvastatin 10-20mg daily.

Aspirin (Low-Dose 75-150mg): Secondary prevention in diabetics with established CVD. Not routinely for primary prevention per current guidelines.

5. Government Programme Support

NPCDCS (National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, CVD, and Stroke) provides free screening, diagnosis, and medicines at NCD clinics in CHCs/District Hospitals across India. Metformin, Glimepiride, and Human Insulin available free under the programme.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Gudmar / Meshashringi (Gymnema sylvestre): Called 'sugar destroyer' in Hindi. Classical Ayurvedic antidiabetic (Madhumehaghna). Published research shows reduction in HbA1c and fasting glucose. Suppresses sweet taste perception and may support beta-cell regeneration. 400-600mg extract daily.

Vijaysar / Indian Kino (Pterocarpus marsupium): Classical Pramehaghna herb. The heartwood is used — water kept overnight in Vijaysar tumbler (widely sold across India) is consumed on empty stomach. Research shows hypoglycemic and beta-cell protective effects.

Karela / Bitter Gourd (Momordica charantia): Contains charantin and polypeptide-p with insulin-like activity. Fresh juice 30-50ml daily on empty stomach. Extensively studied; moderate evidence for blood sugar reduction. Widely used across rural India.

Methi / Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum): Seeds soaked overnight and consumed on empty stomach. Contains galactomannan fiber that slows glucose absorption. ICMR-recognized antidiabetic herb. 10-25g seed powder daily with water.

Jamun / Indian Blackberry (Syzygium cumini): Seeds dried and powdered — traditional Madhumeha remedy. Contains jamboline with hypoglycemic properties. 3-5g seed powder twice daily. Fruit pulp also beneficial.

Neem (Azadirachta indica): Nimbidin and nimbin compounds show antidiabetic activity. Neem leaf powder or extract 2-4g daily. Also supports wound healing relevant to diabetic foot management.

Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia): Rasayana with hypoglycemic and immunomodulatory properties. Published studies show significant FPG and PPBG reduction. 500mg-1g extract twice daily.

Amla/Amalaki (Emblica officinalis): Richest natural Vitamin C source. Antioxidant protection against diabetic complications (retinopathy, nephropathy). Triphala (Amla + Haritaki + Bibhitaki) also used for diabetes management.

Turmeric / Haridra (Curcuma longa): Curcumin shows insulin-sensitizing and anti-inflammatory properties. 500mg-1g curcumin extract daily. May help prevent prediabetes to diabetes progression (published RCT evidence).

Cinnamon / Twak (Cinnamomum verum): Improves insulin sensitivity and reduces fasting glucose. 1-6g daily. Include in cooking or as supplement. Widely available and affordable.

Himalaya Diabecon Tablets: Proprietary formulation with Meshashringi, Pitasara, Shilajeet. Most widely sold Ayurvedic antidiabetic in India. 2 tablets twice daily. Available OTC across all pharmacies.

Dabur Madhuvaani: Sugar-free Ayurvedic health supplement for diabetics. Contains Jamun, Karela, Methi, and Shilajeet. Available at Dabur retail outlets.

BGR-34 (CSIR Formulation): Developed by CSIR-NBRI and CSIR-CIMAP. Contains Daruharidra, Giloy, Vijaysar, Majeeth. Clinically tested herbo-mineral antidiabetic. Available at pharmacies. 2 tablets twice daily before meals.

Panchakarma — Vamana & Virechana: Classical detoxification therapies recommended in Ayurvedic texts for Prameha (diabetes). Vamana (therapeutic emesis) and Virechana (therapeutic purgation) to clear Kapha and Medas accumulation. Only under expert Ayurvedic physician supervision.

Yoga & Pranayama (Complementary Practice): Surya Namaskar, Mandukasana (frog pose), Ardha Matsyendrasana (spinal twist), Kapalbhati, and Bhramari pranayama have published evidence for improving insulin sensitivity and glycemic control.
diseases.india_specific:
Diabetes Capital of the World: India has 101+ million people with diabetes (IDF 2024) and 136 million with prediabetes — the highest numbers globally. ICMR-INDIAB study shows prevalence rising fastest in rural areas and among lower socioeconomic groups.

Young-Onset Type 2 DM: Unlike Western populations, Indian Type 2 DM commonly presents in the 25-40 age group (vs 50+ in West). The 'thin-fat Indian' phenotype — normal BMI but high visceral fat and insulin resistance — is a unique risk factor.

Dietary Transition & Urbanization: Shift from traditional high-fiber millets/dal diet to refined carbohydrates (white rice, maida, sugar), processed foods, and sedentary lifestyle is the primary driver. South India has highest prevalence due to rice-heavy diets.

TB-Diabetes Bidirectional Risk: Diabetics have 2-3x higher TB risk; TB worsens glycemic control. NTEP mandates bidirectional screening. This dual burden is India-specific at massive scale.

Under-diagnosis & Late Presentation: Nearly 50% of Indian diabetics are undiagnosed. Many present first with complications — retinopathy, nephropathy, diabetic foot, or cardiovascular events — rather than early symptoms.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Type 1 DM Requires Lifelong Insulin: Type 1 Diabetes is an autoimmune condition where the pancreas produces NO insulin. Ayurvedic medicines, dietary changes, or herbal remedies CANNOT replace insulin in Type 1 DM. Stopping insulin in Type 1 = Diabetic Ketoacidosis = DEATH. This must be communicated firmly to patients and families.

Ayurveda as Complementary for Type 2: Ayurvedic herbs (Gudmar, Vijaysar, Karela, Methi) have genuine evidence for blood sugar reduction in Type 2 DM, but should complement — not replace — prescribed oral medications or insulin. Never stop Metformin/Sulfonylureas based on Ayurvedic advice alone. Monitor HbA1c every 3 months.

Hypoglycemia Awareness: Rural health workers must educate patients on recognizing low blood sugar (sweating, tremors, palpitations, confusion, hunger) and immediate management — eat 15g fast-acting sugar (glucose tablets, 3-4 tsp sugar in water, fruit juice). Carry glucose tablets always. Severe hypoglycemia can cause unconsciousness and death.

Diabetic Foot — Prevention Saves Limbs: India has the highest number of diabetic foot amputations globally. Educate patients on: daily foot inspection, proper footwear (never walk barefoot), immediate attention to cuts/blisters, annual monofilament screening. Early detection prevents 85% of amputations.

Government Support — NPCDCS: Free diabetes screening, diagnosis, and medicines (Metformin, Glimepiride, Insulin) available at NCD clinics under NPCDCS. Ayushman Bharat-HWCs provide population-based NCD screening for all adults >30 years. Leverage these free services.

diseases.name: Typhoid Fever
diseases.tests:
1. Microbiological Culture Tests (Gold Standard)

Blood Culture (Aerobic): Gold standard for typhoid diagnosis. Positive in 40-80% of cases in first week, declining thereafter. Collect 10-15ml blood (adults) before antibiotic administration. Takes 48-72 hours. Available at district hospital level.

Stool Culture: Positive from second week onwards (in 30% of cases). Used for carrier detection. Culture on SS/MacConkey/Bismuth sulfite agar. Salmonella Typhi or Paratyphi isolation is confirmatory.

Bone Marrow Culture: Highest sensitivity (80-95%) even after antibiotics started. Reserved for diagnostic dilemma cases. Aspirate from iliac crest. Painful but definitive when blood culture is negative.

Urine Culture: Positive in 25% of cases from third week. Less sensitive but useful adjunct.

2. Serological & Rapid Tests

Widal Test: Measures agglutinating antibodies against O (somatic) and H (flagellar) antigens. Positive: O titer ≥1:160, H titer ≥1:160 (or 4-fold rise in paired sera). LIMITATIONS: High false-positive rate in endemic areas like India due to background immunity and cross-reactions. Single Widal NOT diagnostic — requires paired sera.

Typhidot (IgM/IgG Rapid Test): Detects IgM and IgG antibodies against S. Typhi outer membrane protein. More specific than Widal. IgM-positive = acute infection. Results in hours. Available as point-of-care kit.

Tubex TF Test: Detects IgM antibodies to S. Typhi O9 LPS antigen. Semi-quantitative colored particle agglutination. Sensitivity 78%, Specificity 89%. Rapid (10 minutes). More accurate than single Widal.

3. Molecular Diagnostic Tests

Blood PCR for S. Typhi: Detects Salmonella Typhi DNA in blood. Higher sensitivity than blood culture (>90%). Expensive, available at reference labs. Useful in culture-negative cases or post-antibiotic initiation.

4. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): Leukopenia (low WBC) is typical in typhoid (unlike other bacterial infections). Relative lymphocytosis, mild anemia, and thrombocytopenia may be present.

Liver Function Tests (LFT): Mild to moderate elevation of AST/ALT and alkaline phosphatase common. Helps differentiate from hepatitis.

C-Reactive Protein (CRP) & ESR: Elevated inflammatory markers. Non-specific but help monitor treatment response.

Blood Culture Sensitivity (ABST): CRITICAL for guiding antibiotic therapy given high MDR rates in India. Tests Ciprofloxacin, Azithromycin, Ceftriaxone, Ampicillin, Chloramphenicol, Cotrimoxazole sensitivity.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Confirmed Typhoid: Isolation of S. Typhi from blood/stool/bone marrow culture. Probable Typhoid: Compatible clinical picture + positive serology (Typhidot IgM or 4-fold Widal rise) in endemic setting. In India's endemic context, clinical suspicion + any positive test warrants treatment initiation pending culture.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Antibiotics (Empirical — pending culture)

Ceftriaxone (IV/IM): Current empirical drug of choice in India due to fluoroquinolone resistance. 2g IV daily (adults), 75 mg/kg/day (children) for 10-14 days. Available at PHC level for IM injection.

Azithromycin (Oral): Preferred oral option for uncomplicated typhoid. 500mg-1g daily for 5-7 days (adults), 10-20 mg/kg/day (children). Well tolerated, good compliance due to short course.

Cefixime (Oral): Oral cephalosporin for outpatient management. 200mg twice daily (adults) for 14 days. Alternative when IV Ceftriaxone not feasible.

2. Second-Line / MDR Typhoid Antibiotics

Ciprofloxacin / Ofloxacin (Fluoroquinolones): Previously first-line, now resistance rates >80% in many Indian regions. Use ONLY if culture shows sensitivity. 500mg twice daily for 7-14 days.

Chloramphenicol: Historical first-line drug. 500mg four times daily for 14 days. Still effective in some regions but bone marrow toxicity risk. Monitor CBC during treatment.

Ampicillin / Amoxicillin: Alternative if culture-sensitive. Amoxicillin 1g thrice daily for 14 days. Rising resistance limits empirical use.

Cotrimoxazole (Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole): Alternative for sensitive strains. 960mg twice daily for 14 days. Resistance common in India.

3. Severe / Complicated Typhoid

Meropenem (IV): For XDR typhoid (resistant to Ceftriaxone AND Azithromycin). 1g IV every 8 hours. Reserved for ICU-level management. Emerging need with XDR strains.

Dexamethasone (IV): For typhoid with altered consciousness/shock (typhoid encephalopathy). 3mg/kg initial dose, then 1mg/kg every 6 hours for 48 hours. Reduces mortality by 50% in severe cases (Cochrane evidence).

4. Supportive Management & Prevention

Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS): For mild dehydration and maintenance. Encourage 2-3 liters/day fluids — ORS, coconut water, dal water, rice water.

Typhoid Vaccines: Vi capsular polysaccharide vaccine (Typbar/Typhim Vi) — single IM dose, 55-72% efficacy for 3 years. Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (Typbar-TCV by Bharat Biotech) — WHO-prequalified, single dose, superior efficacy, approved for children >6 months. India included TCV in Universal Immunization Programme (UIP).

Carrier Treatment: Chronic carriers — Ciprofloxacin 750mg twice daily for 28 days OR Ampicillin + Probenecid for 6 weeks. Cholecystectomy if gallstones present.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia): Premier Jwaraghna (antipyretic) in Ayurveda. Published research confirms immunomodulatory and antipyretic activity. Fresh stem juice or Guduchi Satva 500mg-1g twice daily. Widely recommended by AYUSH practitioners for enteric fevers.

Sudarshan Ghana Vati/Churna: Classical Sarva Jwarahara formulation containing Chirayita, Guduchi, Devadaru and 40+ herbs. Specifically indicated for Sannipata Jwara and Vishama Jwara. 2 tablets thrice daily.

Chirayita / Kirata Tikta (Swertia chirata): Potent Jwarahara and Deepana herb. Classical use in Jwara (fever) and Krimi (infections). Bitter decoction 20-30ml twice daily. Contains amarogentin with antimicrobial properties.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum): Krimighna and Jwaraghna. Tulsi decoction or tea for fever management and immune support. 5-7 fresh leaves boiled with ginger, 2-3 times daily.

Pravala Pishti (Coral Calcium): Classical preparation for Jwara with Pitta predominance (high fever, burning). Cooling, Pittashamaka. 250-500mg twice daily with honey/mishri. Used alongside Guduchi for synergistic antipyretic effect.

Maha Sudarshan Kadha/Churna: Extended formulation of Sudarshan with 40+ herbs. Potent broad-spectrum Jwarahara. 15-30ml decoction or 3-5g churna twice daily with warm water.

Kutaj Ghana Vati (Holarrhena antidysenterica): Specifically for intestinal symptoms — diarrhea associated with typhoid. Kutaj is classical Atisaraghna (anti-diarrheal) and Krimighna. 2 tablets thrice daily.

Sanjeevani Vati: Classical formulation for Jwara and Visha (toxins). Contains Vidanga, Shunthi, Pippali, Guduchi. Deepana and Jwarahara. 250-500mg twice daily.

Baidyanath Sudarshan Ghana Vati: Widely available branded classical formulation. 2 tablets thrice daily.

Patanjali Giloy Ghanvati: Affordable standardized Guduchi extract. 1-2 tablets twice daily.

Himalaya Septilin: Proprietary immunomodulator for supporting recovery from infections. 2 tablets twice daily.

Dabur Giloy Ki Ghanvati: Branded Guduchi tablets available at most pharmacies across India. 1-2 tablets twice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Waterborne Transmission & Sanitation: Typhoid is primarily spread through contaminated water and food. India's incomplete sanitation coverage (open defecation, untreated sewage contaminating water sources) makes it a persistent endemic threat. Monsoon flooding worsens outbreaks.

MDR & XDR Typhoid Emergence: Drug-resistant Salmonella Typhi is a major Indian concern. Resistance to Chloramphenicol, Ampicillin, and Cotrimoxazole is widespread. Fluoroquinolone-resistant strains now predominate. Azithromycin-resistant strains emerging.

Street Food & Unhygienic Preparation: Roadside food stalls serving raw salads, cut fruits, unboiled water, and chutney are common vehicles. 'Typhoid Mary' carriers among food handlers are a significant public health issue in India.

Chronic Carriers & Gallbladder Reservoir: 2-5% of typhoid patients become chronic carriers, harboring Salmonella in the gallbladder. They shed bacteria in stool for >1 year and serve as ongoing community infection source.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Antibiotics Are Essential: Untreated typhoid has 10-30% mortality from intestinal perforation and hemorrhage. Ayurvedic antipyretics support symptom management but CANNOT kill Salmonella Typhi. Antibiotics must be started promptly on clinical suspicion — do not wait for culture results.

Complete Full Antibiotic Course: Incomplete antibiotic course is the primary driver of drug-resistant typhoid AND chronic carrier state. Even if fever resolves in 3-4 days, the full 7-14 day course must be completed. Educate patients that stopping early creates MDR bacteria.

Water Safety & Hygiene: Typhoid is a waterborne disease. Boil drinking water, wash hands before eating/after toilet, avoid raw food from street vendors during monsoon. Community-level interventions (clean water supply, sanitation) are the ultimate solution.

Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV): Bharat Biotech's Typbar-TCV is now part of India's Universal Immunization Programme. Single-dose, WHO-prequalified, approved for children >6 months. Recommend vaccination for all children in endemic areas.

Beware Intestinal Complications: During weeks 2-3, watch for sudden severe abdominal pain (perforation) or bloody/black stools (hemorrhage). These are SURGICAL EMERGENCIES requiring immediate hospital transfer. Avoid solid food, vigorous palpation during this period.

diseases.name: Urinary Tract Infection
diseases.tests:
1. Urine Tests (Primary Diagnostic)

Urine Routine & Microscopy: First diagnostic test. Pyuria (WBC >5/HPF), bacteriuria, RBCs, epithelial cells. Positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites on dipstick. Mid-stream clean-catch sample essential to avoid contamination.

Urine Culture & Sensitivity (C&S): Gold standard. Significant bacteriuria: ≥10^5 CFU/ml (symptomatic women ≥10^3 sufficient). Identifies pathogen (E. coli 75-90%, Klebsiella, Proteus, Enterococcus) and antibiotic sensitivity. Takes 48-72 hours.

Urine Dipstick (Point-of-Care): Rapid bedside test. Positive leukocyte esterase (sensitivity 75-96%) and/or nitrites (specificity 94-98%). Negative nitrites do NOT rule out UTI (Enterococcus, Staphylococcus don't produce nitrites). Feasible at PHC level.

2. Blood Tests (For Complicated UTI/Pyelonephritis)

Complete Blood Count (CBC): Leukocytosis with neutrophilia in pyelonephritis. Normal in uncomplicated lower UTI.

Blood Culture: For suspected urosepsis (fever + UTI + hemodynamic instability). Positive in 20-30% of pyelonephritis cases.

Serum Creatinine & BUN: Assess renal function, especially in recurrent UTI, pyelonephritis, or obstructive uropathy.

C-Reactive Protein (CRP) / Procalcitonin: Elevated in upper UTI/pyelonephritis. Procalcitonin >0.5 ng/ml suggests systemic bacterial infection. Helps distinguish upper from lower UTI.

3. Imaging (If Indicated)

Ultrasound KUB (Kidney-Ureter-Bladder): For complicated UTI, recurrent UTI, male UTI, poor treatment response. Detects hydronephrosis, renal abscess, stones, post-void residual volume. Non-invasive, available at CHC/district level.

CT Scan Abdomen with Contrast: For suspected renal/perinephric abscess, emphysematous pyelonephritis, or complicated UTI not responding to antibiotics. District/tertiary hospital level.

4. Special Situations

Urine Culture in Pregnancy: Mandatory screening in first trimester for asymptomatic bacteriuria. Treat even if asymptomatic to prevent pyelonephritis and preterm delivery.

Voiding Cystourethrogram (VCUG): In children with recurrent UTI to detect vesicoureteral reflux (VUR). Referral to pediatric urologist.

5. Diagnostic Criteria

Uncomplicated UTI (Cystitis): Dysuria + frequency + urgency in healthy non-pregnant woman + positive urine dipstick. Empirical treatment without culture is acceptable. Complicated UTI: UTI in males, pregnant women, children, catheterized patients, structural abnormalities, immunocompromised, or pyelonephritis — always requires urine culture and targeted therapy.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Uncomplicated Lower UTI (Cystitis) — Empirical Antibiotics

Nitrofurantoin (Macrocrystals): First-line per current guidelines. 100mg twice daily for 5 days. Low resistance rates in India (~10%). Achieves high urinary concentration. NOT effective for pyelonephritis (poor tissue penetration). Avoid if eGFR <30.

Fosfomycin (Trometamol): Single 3g oral dose — excellent compliance. Rising use in India. Effective against ESBL-producing E. coli. Reserve for when Nitrofurantoin not suitable.

Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX): 960mg twice daily for 3 days. Use only if local resistance <20%. High resistance in many Indian regions (>60%) limits empirical use — use only if culture-sensitive.

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate: 625mg thrice daily for 5-7 days. Alternative for resistant organisms. Safe in pregnancy.

2. Complicated UTI / Pyelonephritis

Ceftriaxone (IV): 1-2g IV daily for 10-14 days. First-line parenteral for pyelonephritis requiring hospitalization. De-escalate to oral therapy based on culture after clinical improvement.

Ciprofloxacin / Levofloxacin (Oral): 500mg twice daily (Cipro) or 750mg daily (Levo) for 7-14 days for pyelonephritis. Use only if culture-sensitive due to high fluoroquinolone resistance in India.

Meropenem / Ertapenem (IV): For ESBL-producing or MDR gram-negative UTI. Ertapenem 1g IV daily. Reserve carbapenems to prevent resistance escalation.

Amikacin (IV/IM): Aminoglycoside for severe UTI. 15 mg/kg/day. Monitor renal function and hearing. Useful against MDR organisms.

3. Supportive & Preventive Measures

Increased Fluid Intake: Encourage 2-3 liters water daily. Adequate hydration is the simplest and most effective UTI prevention strategy.

Cranberry Extract Supplements: Some evidence for preventing recurrent UTI by preventing bacterial adhesion to bladder wall. Capsules available commercially.

Probiotics (Lactobacillus): Vaginal and oral probiotics may reduce recurrent UTI in women by maintaining healthy vaginal flora.

Low-Dose Prophylaxis: For recurrent UTI (≥3/year): Nitrofurantoin 50-100mg at bedtime for 6-12 months. Post-coital prophylaxis also effective.
