diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Gokshura / Tribulus (Tribulus terrestris): Classical Mutravirechaniya (urinary tract cleanser) and Mutrala (diuretic). First-choice Ayurvedic herb for Mutrakricchra (dysuria/UTI). 3-5g powder twice daily with water or as decoction.

Punarnava (Boerhaavia diffusa): Potent Shothaghna (anti-inflammatory) and Mutrala (diuretic). Classical use in Mutraroga (urinary diseases). Supports kidney function. Punarnava Mandura for associated anemia. 3-5g powder or 15-30ml juice twice daily.

Varuna / Crataeva (Crataeva nurvala): Classical Ashmari Bhedana (stone-breaking) and Mutrala. Used in UTI, urinary stones, and BPH. Bark decoction 30-50ml twice daily.

Chandraprabha Vati: Classical multi-herb formulation specifically for Prameha and Mutraroga. Contains Shilajeet, Guggulu, Loha Bhasma, and 37 ingredients. 2 tablets twice daily with milk/water. Most widely prescribed Ayurvedic UTI formulation.

Gokshuradi Guggulu: Classical formulation combining Gokshura with Guggulu. For Mutrakricchra (painful urination), Mutrashmari (urinary stones), and inflammatory urinary conditions. 2 tablets twice daily.

Shilajeet (Asphaltum/Mineral Pitch): Powerful Rasayana and Mutravaha Srotoshodhaka. Contains fulvic acid with antimicrobial properties. 250-500mg twice daily with warm milk. Published evidence for antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.

Pashanbheda (Bergenia ligulata): Name means 'stone breaker'. Classical herb for Ashmari (stones) and Mutrakrichhra. Useful in UTI with calculi. 3-5g powder twice daily.

Coconut Water (Narikela Jala): Natural Mutrala (diuretic). Alkalinizes urine, soothes burning sensation. 200-300ml 3-4 times daily. Most accessible and affordable supportive remedy in India.

Himalaya Cystone Tablets: Proprietary formulation with Pashanabheda, Shilapushpa, and Hajrul Yahood Bhasma. Most widely sold herbal UTI/kidney stone product in India. 2 tablets twice daily.

Dabur Chandraprabha Vati: Branded classical formulation. Available at all Dabur retail outlets. 2 tablets twice daily.

Baidyanath Gokshuradi Guggulu: Well-known branded formulation for urinary disorders. Available at most Ayurvedic pharmacies.
diseases.india_specific:
Female Predominance & Hygiene Practices: 50-60% of Indian women experience at least one UTI in their lifetime. Short female urethra, proximity to anus, and improper toileting practices (back-to-front wiping) increase risk. Lack of clean toilet facilities in rural India compounds the problem.

Water Scarcity & Voluntary Dehydration: In many rural and semi-urban areas, women deliberately restrict water intake and urination frequency due to lack of access to clean toilets. This voluntary dehydration and urine retention significantly increases UTI risk.

Antibiotic Resistance Crisis: E. coli (most common UTI pathogen) resistance rates in India: Cotrimoxazole >60%, Fluoroquinolones >50%, Ampicillin >70%. ESBL-producing organisms rising. Empirical antibiotic choice must consider local resistance patterns.

Pregnancy-Related UTI: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy (2-10%) can progress to pyelonephritis if untreated, risking preterm delivery and low birth weight. Routine urine culture screening recommended in first trimester.

Catheter-Associated UTI (CAUTI): Most common healthcare-associated infection in Indian hospitals. Prolonged catheterization, non-sterile technique, and delayed removal are risk factors.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Antibiotics for Confirmed UTI: Bacterial UTI requires antibiotic treatment. Ayurvedic herbs (Gokshura, Punarnava, Chandraprabha Vati) are excellent for supportive care, urinary symptom relief, and recurrence prevention, but cannot replace antibiotics for acute bacterial infection. Untreated pyelonephritis can lead to sepsis and death.

Urine Culture Before Antibiotics (Complicated UTI): For all complicated UTI (males, children, pregnant women, recurrent, catheter-related), always obtain urine culture BEFORE starting antibiotics. Empirical therapy can begin, but must be adjusted based on sensitivity report given India's high antibiotic resistance.

Hydration Is the Best Prevention: Adequate water intake (2-3 liters/day) is the single most effective UTI prevention measure. In rural India, women must be educated that restricting water intake due to lack of toilet facilities worsens UTI risk. Advocate for Swachh Bharat toilet access.

Pregnancy UTI — Screen and Treat Promptly: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy MUST be screened (first trimester urine culture) and treated, as 20-40% progress to pyelonephritis, risking preterm delivery. Use safe antibiotics: Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), Amoxicillin-Clavulanate, Cephalosporins.

Recurrent UTI in Women — Holistic Approach: For women with ≥3 UTIs/year, combine: adequate hydration, post-coital voiding, front-to-back wiping, Cranberry supplements, probiotics, Gokshura/Chandraprabha Vati for long-term support, and consider low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis if needed.

diseases.name: Pneumonia
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment & Severity Scoring

Respiratory Rate Assessment: IMNCI criteria — Pneumonia if fast breathing: <2 months ≥60/min, 2-12 months ≥50/min, 1-5 years ≥40/min, adults ≥30/min. Chest indrawing = Severe pneumonia. This simple assessment can be done by ASHA workers.

Pulse Oximetry (SpO2): Essential point-of-care test. SpO2 <94% = supplemental oxygen needed. SpO2 <90% = severe pneumonia. Pulse oximeters should be available at all PHC/CHC levels.

CURB-65 Severity Score (Adults): Confusion, Urea >7 mmol/L, RR ≥30, BP <90/60, Age ≥65. Score 0-1: outpatient. Score 2: hospital. Score 3-5: ICU consideration. Simple bedside scoring tool.

2. Imaging

Chest X-ray (PA View): First-line imaging. Shows lobar consolidation, bronchopneumonia pattern, interstitial infiltrates, or pleural effusion. Available at CHC/district level. Confirms clinical diagnosis and assesses extent.

CT Chest (HRCT): For complicated pneumonia, non-resolving pneumonia, immunocompromised patients. Detects early changes, abscess, empyema. Used extensively during COVID-19 for scoring severity.

Point-of-Care Lung Ultrasound: Emerging bedside tool. Detects consolidation, pleural effusion, interstitial syndrome. Increasingly available at secondary level facilities in India.

3. Microbiological Tests

Sputum Gram Stain & Culture: Collect deep-cough sputum before antibiotics. Gram stain gives rapid clue: gram-positive diplococci (Pneumococcus), gram-negative coccobacilli (H. influenzae). Culture identifies pathogen + sensitivity.

Blood Culture (Aerobic): Positive in 5-14% of community-acquired pneumonia. Always collect before antibiotics in hospitalized patients. Essential for severe pneumonia.

4. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): Leukocytosis with neutrophilia (bacterial), lymphocytosis (viral), eosinophilia (parasitic). Leukopenia in severe sepsis.

C-Reactive Protein (CRP) / Procalcitonin: CRP >100 mg/L suggests bacterial pneumonia. Procalcitonin >0.25 ng/ml supports bacterial etiology. Helps guide antibiotic initiation and duration.

Arterial Blood Gas (ABG): For severe pneumonia with respiratory distress. Assesses oxygenation (PaO2), ventilation (PaCO2), and acid-base status.

5. Special Tests (If Indicated)

Urinary Pneumococcal Antigen: Rapid detection of S. pneumoniae. Sensitivity 70-80%. Remains positive despite antibiotics.

Urinary Legionella Antigen: For Legionella pneumonia. Important in outbreak settings.

TB Evaluation: In India, always consider TB in non-resolving pneumonia, chronic cough with fever. Sputum for AFB, CBNAAT/TrueNat.

6. Diagnostic Criteria

Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP): New respiratory symptoms (cough, sputum, dyspnea) + fever + new infiltrate on chest X-ray. Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP): Pneumonia developing >48 hours after hospital admission. Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP): Pneumonia developing >48 hours after intubation.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) — Outpatient

Amoxicillin (Oral): First-line for mild CAP. 500mg-1g thrice daily for 5-7 days. Covers S. pneumoniae. WHO and IMNCI recommended first-line for childhood pneumonia.

Azithromycin (Oral): For atypical coverage or Penicillin allergy. 500mg day 1, then 250mg days 2-5. Covers Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella.

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Oral): 625mg thrice daily for 5-7 days. Broader coverage including H. influenzae and Moraxella. Alternative first-line in India.

2. CAP — Hospitalized (Non-ICU)

Ceftriaxone (IV) + Azithromycin (Oral/IV): Standard combination. Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + Azithromycin 500mg daily for 5-7 days. Covers typical and atypical pathogens.

Levofloxacin (IV/Oral): Respiratory fluoroquinolone. 750mg daily for 5-7 days. Alternative monotherapy. Covers both typical and atypical organisms.

3. Severe CAP (ICU)

Piperacillin-Tazobactam (IV) + Azithromycin: For severe CAP. Pip-Taz 4.5g IV every 6 hours + Azithromycin. Covers Pseudomonas if risk factors present.

Meropenem (IV): For MDR organisms or hospital-acquired pneumonia. 1g IV every 8 hours.

Vancomycin / Linezolid (IV): Add if MRSA pneumonia suspected (post-influenza, cavitary lesions, empyema). Linezolid 600mg IV twice daily preferred for MRSA pneumonia.

4. Supportive Care

Oxygen Therapy: Maintain SpO2 >94% (>90% in COPD). Nasal cannula 1-6 L/min, face mask 6-10 L/min, NIV or mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure.

Nebulization (Salbutamol + Ipratropium): For bronchospasm associated with pneumonia. Salbutamol 2.5mg + Ipratropium 0.5mg nebulized every 4-6 hours.

IV Fluid Management: Judicious fluids — avoid overhydration (worsens pulmonary edema). Target euvolemia.

5. Vaccination (Prevention)

Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV13): Part of India's Universal Immunization Programme since 2017. 3 doses at 6, 14 weeks, and 9 months. Prevents invasive pneumococcal disease in children.

Hib Vaccine (Pentavalent): Included in UIP. Protects against Haemophilus influenzae type b pneumonia.

Influenza Vaccine (Annual): Recommended for high-risk groups — elderly, diabetics, COPD, immunocompromised.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Sitopaladi Churna: Classical formulation for Kasa (cough) and Shwasa (respiratory distress). Contains Vamshalochana, Pippali, Ela, Twak, Mishri. 3g with honey thrice daily. Clears respiratory congestion and reduces cough intensity.

Vasavaleha / Vasa Avaleha: Adhatoda vasica-based preparation for persistent cough, hemoptysis, and bronchial inflammation. Classical Shwasahara and Kasahara. 10g twice daily with warm water.

Talisadi Churna: Extended formulation of Sitopaladi with Talispatra. Superior action on Kasa and Shwasa. 3g with honey twice daily.

Kanakasava: Classical Asava for Shwasa (respiratory distress) and Kasa. Contains Dhattura, Pippali, Bharangi. Specifically for bronchial congestion and wheezing. 15-30ml with equal water twice daily.

Bharangi (Clerodendrum serratum): Potent Shwasahara and Kasahara herb. Published anti-inflammatory and bronchodilatory activity. Used in classical formulations for respiratory diseases.

Yashtimadhu / Mulethi (Glycyrrhiza glabra): Demulcent and expectorant. Soothes inflamed respiratory mucosa. Boosts immunity. 3-5g powder with honey or as decoction.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum): Antimicrobial and immunomodulatory. Tulsi decoction with ginger and black pepper (Trikatu) for respiratory infections. 2-3 cups daily.

Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia): Immunomodulatory Rasayana. Supports immune recovery from pneumonia. Guduchi Satva or Ghana Vati 500mg-1g twice daily.

Chyawanprash: Comprehensive Rasayana for post-pneumonia recovery. Boosts lung function and immunity. 10-20g daily with warm milk.

Dabur Honitus Cough Syrup: Herbal cough syrup with Tulsi, Mulethi, Banaphsha. Symptom relief for productive cough. 10ml thrice daily.

Himalaya Bresol Tablets: Proprietary herbal formulation for respiratory health with Haridra and Tulsi. 1 tablet twice daily.

Kottakkal Dasamoolarishtam: Classical formulation for Jwara and Shwasa. Antipyretic and respiratory supportive. 15-30ml with equal water twice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Leading Cause of Child Mortality in India: Pneumonia kills approximately 1.27 lakh children under 5 years annually in India — more than diarrhea, malaria, and measles combined. Malnutrition, indoor air pollution, and incomplete vaccination are key drivers.

Indoor Air Pollution (Chulha/Biomass Fuel): 70% of rural Indian households use biomass fuel (wood, cow dung, crop residue) for cooking. Chronic smoke exposure damages airways and significantly increases pneumonia risk, especially in women and children.

IMNCI Protocol at Primary Level: India's Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness (IMNCI) classifies childhood pneumonia by respiratory rate and chest indrawing at PHC/subcenter level — enabling ASHA workers to identify and refer severe cases.

COVID-19 & Post-COVID Pneumonia: Since 2020, COVID-19 pneumonia (ground-glass opacities on CT) and secondary bacterial/fungal pneumonia (Mucormycosis) have become significant. Post-COVID fibrosis affects long-term lung function.

Antibiotic Resistance: S. pneumoniae Penicillin resistance (~10-15%), H. influenzae Ampicillin resistance (~30%), rising MRSA and ESBL-producing gram-negative pneumonia in hospitalized patients.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Pneumonia Kills: Pneumonia is the leading infectious cause of death in children under 5 worldwide, and India accounts for the largest share. Any child with fast breathing, chest indrawing, or inability to drink must be referred to hospital IMMEDIATELY. Do not delay for Ayurvedic treatment.

Antibiotics Are Non-Negotiable for Bacterial Pneumonia: Ayurvedic formulations (Sitopaladi, Vasavaleha, Tulsi) provide excellent respiratory symptom support but CANNOT treat bacterial pneumonia. Amoxicillin/Ceftriaxone must be started promptly. Viral pneumonia is supportive care only.

Oxygen Saves Lives: SpO2 monitoring should be routine for all pneumonia patients. SpO2 <94% requires oxygen. Pulse oximeters at PHC/CHC level are essential. India's Oxygen Concentrator programme under PM-CARES has improved oxygen access in rural areas.

Vaccination Is Best Prevention: PCV13 (pneumococcal), Pentavalent (Hib), and annual Influenza vaccines prevent the most common causes of pneumonia. Ensure all children are up to date with UIP schedule. Educate against vaccine hesitancy.

Indoor Air Pollution — Eliminate Chulha Smoke: Transition from biomass chulha to LPG (Ujjwala Yojana) significantly reduces pneumonia risk in women and children. This is both a health and development intervention.

diseases.name: Hypertension
diseases.tests:
1. Blood Pressure Measurement

Office BP Measurement: Using validated sphygmomanometer or digital BP monitor. Patient seated, relaxed for 5 minutes, arm supported at heart level. Take 2-3 readings 1-2 minutes apart, average the last 2. Use appropriate cuff size (standard adult, large adult, pediatric).

Classification (AHA/ESC/ISH): Normal <120/80. Elevated 120-129/<80. Stage 1 HTN 130-139/80-89. Stage 2 HTN ≥140/≥90. Hypertensive Crisis >180/120. Indian guidelines (ISH/API) generally use ≥140/90 threshold for diagnosis.

Ambulatory Blood Pressure Monitoring (ABPM): 24-hour automated BP recording. Gold standard for confirming diagnosis, detecting white-coat hypertension and masked hypertension. Average daytime >135/85 or 24-hr >130/80 = hypertension.

Home Blood Pressure Monitoring (HBPM): Patient self-monitoring with validated digital device. Average ≥135/85 mmHg confirms hypertension. Improves treatment adherence and control.

2. Target Organ Damage Assessment

ECG (12-lead): Screen for Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH), arrhythmias, ischemic changes. LVH criteria: Sokolow-Lyon SV1+RV5 >35mm. Annual ECG recommended.

Echocardiography: For confirmed LVH on ECG, suspected heart failure, or valvular disease. Measures LV mass index, ejection fraction, diastolic function.

Fundoscopy / Retinal Examination: Detects hypertensive retinopathy — Keith-Wagener-Barker grading (Grade I-IV). Grade III-IV = accelerated/malignant hypertension.

Serum Creatinine & eGFR: Assess renal function. Hypertensive nephropathy is leading cause of CKD in India. Annual monitoring.

Urine Albumin-to-Creatinine Ratio (UACR): Detects microalbuminuria — earliest sign of hypertensive kidney damage.

3. Routine Blood Tests

Lipid Profile (Fasting): Assess cardiovascular risk. Total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, triglycerides. Treat dyslipidemia concurrently.

Fasting Blood Glucose / HbA1c: Screen for diabetes (frequently coexists). ICMR recommends bidirectional screening.

Serum Electrolytes (Na+, K+): Baseline before starting diuretics/ACE inhibitors. Hypokalemia may indicate secondary cause (hyperaldosteronism).

Complete Blood Count & Thyroid Function: Baseline assessment. Hypothyroidism is a reversible cause of hypertension.

4. Secondary Hypertension Workup (If Suspected)

Renal Artery Doppler: For suspected renovascular hypertension (refractory HTN, abdominal bruit, renal asymmetry).

Plasma Aldosterone/Renin Ratio: Screen for primary aldosteronism in resistant hypertension.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Hypertension is diagnosed when: Office BP ≥140/90 mmHg on 2-3 separate occasions, OR ABPM daytime average ≥135/85, OR HBPM average ≥135/85. Single elevated reading is NOT diagnostic. Classification guides treatment urgency and approach.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Antihypertensives

Amlodipine (CCB): 5-10mg once daily. Most prescribed antihypertensive in India. Effective, well-tolerated, long-acting. Ankle edema is main side effect. Excellent for Indian patients — affordable and widely available.

Telmisartan / Losartan (ARBs): Telmisartan 40-80mg daily, Losartan 50-100mg daily. Preferred for diabetic patients (renoprotective). Do NOT use in pregnancy.

Enalapril / Ramipril (ACE Inhibitors): Enalapril 5-20mg daily, Ramipril 2.5-10mg daily. Renoprotective. Dry cough side effect in 10-15%. Alternative to ARBs.

Hydrochlorothiazide / Chlorthalidone (Thiazide Diuretics): HCTZ 12.5-25mg daily, Chlorthalidone 6.25-25mg daily. Effective and cheap. Monitor electrolytes (hypokalemia, hyponatremia).

2. Second-Line & Combination Therapy

Amlodipine + Telmisartan Fixed-Dose Combination: Most commonly prescribed combination in India. Available as single tablet (5/40, 5/80). Improves compliance and control.

Metoprolol / Atenolol (Beta-Blockers): Metoprolol 25-100mg daily. For hypertension with tachycardia, angina, post-MI. Not first-line monotherapy per current guidelines.

Spironolactone: 25-50mg daily. Add-on for resistant hypertension (4th drug). Also useful in primary aldosteronism. Monitor potassium.

Prazosin / Doxazosin (Alpha-Blockers): For resistant hypertension or BPH with hypertension. Prazosin 1-5mg twice daily.

3. Hypertensive Emergency Management

IV Labetalol: 20mg IV bolus, repeat every 10-20 minutes. First-line for most hypertensive emergencies. Max 300mg. Oral switch once BP controlled.

IV Nitroglycerin: For hypertensive emergency with acute coronary syndrome or pulmonary edema. 5-100 mcg/min titrated.

Sodium Nitroprusside (IV): Potent vasodilator for refractory hypertensive emergency. ICU-only. Requires arterial line monitoring.

4. Lifestyle Modification (Foundation of Treatment)

DASH Diet + Salt Restriction: Reduce salt to <5g/day. DASH diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat dairy. Can lower BP by 8-14 mmHg. Indian adaptations: reduce pickle, papad, processed snacks.

Regular Exercise: 30 minutes moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walking, cycling) 5 days/week. Lowers BP by 4-9 mmHg. Yoga also effective.

Weight Management: 1 kg weight loss = approximately 1 mmHg BP reduction. Target BMI <25 (or <23 for Asian Indians).

Alcohol & Tobacco Cessation: Limit alcohol, stop smoking/tobacco completely. Both are independent cardiovascular risk factors.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina): Classical Ayurvedic antihypertensive — the plant from which Reserpine was originally derived. Root powder 200-600mg daily. Known in Ayurveda as Nidrajnana (sleep-inducing) and effective for Uccha Raktachapa (high BP). Use under practitioner supervision.

Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna): Premier Hridya (cardiac tonic) in Ayurveda. Bark powder reduces BP and improves cardiac function. Published RCT evidence for modest BP reduction. 500mg-1g bark powder twice daily with milk or as decoction.

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi): Classical sedative and antihypertensive herb. Calms Vata, reduces anxiety-related hypertension. 250-500mg root powder at bedtime. Also used for insomnia.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera): Adaptogenic Rasayana that reduces cortisol and stress-related hypertension. Published evidence for modest BP reduction. 300-600mg root extract twice daily.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri): Medhya Rasayana with anxiolytic and mild antihypertensive properties. Reduces stress and improves cognitive function. 300-500mg extract daily.

Garlic / Lahsun (Allium sativum): Evidence-based antihypertensive food/herb. Meta-analyses show 3-5 mmHg systolic BP reduction. 2-4 fresh cloves daily or aged garlic extract.

Triphala (Amla + Haritaki + Bibhitaki): Classical Rasayana with antioxidant and mild antihypertensive properties. Supports digestive health and metabolic balance. 3-5g powder at bedtime with warm water.

Mukta Vati (Divya Pharmacy / Patanjali): Widely used proprietary Ayurvedic antihypertensive. Contains Sarpagandha, Jatamansi, Brahmi, Shankhpushpi, Mukta Pishti. 2 tablets twice daily. Most popular Ayurvedic BP medicine in India.

Himalaya Abana Tablets: Proprietary cardioprotective with Arjuna and Ashwagandha. For mild hypertension support. 2 tablets twice daily.

Yoga & Pranayama: Shavasana (corpse pose), Sukhasana meditation, Anulom-Vilom (alternate nostril breathing), and Bhramari pranayama have published evidence for 5-10 mmHg BP reduction. Daily 30-minute practice recommended.
diseases.india_specific:
Massive Undiagnosed Burden: India has an estimated 220+ million adults with hypertension (ICMR-INDIAB), but only 45% are aware, 37% treated, and merely 12% have controlled BP. Rural awareness is even lower.

Salt-Heavy Indian Diet: Indian cuisine relies heavily on salt (average intake 9-11 g/day vs WHO recommended <5 g/day), pickles, papad, and processed foods. Reducing salt intake alone can lower BP by 5-10 mmHg.

Young-Onset Hypertension: Unlike Western populations, Indian hypertension often presents in the 30-40 age group. Stress, sedentary lifestyle, and dietary changes drive this earlier onset.

Hypertension-Diabetes-CVD Triad: Hypertension, diabetes, and dyslipidemia frequently coexist in Indian patients. This metabolic syndrome triad dramatically increases heart attack and stroke risk. All three must be managed simultaneously.

Traditional Medicine Non-Compliance: Many Indian patients discontinue antihypertensives once BP normalizes, believing they are 'cured'. This start-stop pattern is dangerous and must be addressed through education.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Lifelong Medication: Hypertension is a CHRONIC condition requiring lifelong management. Patients must NEVER stop antihypertensive medication when BP normalizes — this is the most common and dangerous mistake in India. Normal BP means the medicine is WORKING, not that the disease is cured.

Ayurveda as Complementary: Sarpagandha, Arjuna, and Mukta Vati have genuine antihypertensive activity, but their effect is modest (3-10 mmHg). They can complement prescribed medications but should NOT replace Amlodipine/Telmisartan in moderate-severe hypertension (≥160/100). Monitor BP regularly when using Ayurvedic adjuncts.

Salt Reduction Is Medicine: Reducing salt intake from Indian average 10g to <5g/day can lower BP by 5-10 mmHg — equivalent to one antihypertensive drug. Educate about hidden salt in pickles, papad, packaged foods, and namkeen. This is the most impactful lifestyle intervention.

Screen All Adults >30 Years: NPCDCS/Ayushman Bharat Health & Wellness Centres offer free BP screening for all adults >30 years. Given that 55% of Indian hypertensives are undiagnosed, opportunistic screening at every healthcare contact is essential.

Hypertensive Emergency — Immediate Referral: BP >180/120 with symptoms (headache, chest pain, visual changes, confusion) is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Do not attempt oral treatment alone — refer immediately for IV antihypertensives. Uncontrolled hypertensive emergency causes stroke, heart attack, and kidney failure.

diseases.name: Asthma
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment & Lung Function

Spirometry (PFT — Pulmonary Function Test): Gold standard for asthma diagnosis. Demonstrates reversible airflow obstruction: FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 AND FEV1 improvement ≥12% and ≥200ml after bronchodilator (Salbutamol 400mcg). Available at district hospital level.

Peak Expiratory Flow Rate (PEFR): Simple handheld device measuring maximum expiration speed. PEF variability >10% (adults) or >13% (children) over 2 weeks supports asthma diagnosis. Useful for home monitoring and assessing severity of exacerbations.

Pulse Oximetry (SpO2): Essential during exacerbations. SpO2 <92% indicates severe attack requiring oxygen and hospitalization.

2. Allergy & Trigger Assessment

Skin Prick Testing: Identifies specific allergen triggers (dust mites, pollen, mold, pet dander, cockroach). Helps guide allergen avoidance. Available at allergy clinics in tertiary centres.

Serum Total IgE: Elevated in allergic/atopic asthma. Non-specific but supports allergic phenotype.

Blood Eosinophil Count: Eosinophils >300 cells/μL suggest eosinophilic asthma phenotype. Guides biologic therapy decisions.

3. Exhaled Biomarkers

Fractional Exhaled Nitric Oxide (FeNO): FeNO >40 ppb in adults (>35 in children) indicates eosinophilic airway inflammation. Helps diagnose asthma and monitor steroid response. Emerging availability in Indian cities.

4. Severity & Control Assessment

GINA Assessment of Asthma Control: Well-controlled (daytime symptoms ≤2/week, no night symptoms, no activity limitation, reliever ≤2/week), Partly controlled (any 1-2 above), Uncontrolled (≥3 above). Guides step-up/step-down of treatment.

Asthma Control Test (ACT): 5-question patient-reported score (max 25). Score ≥20 = well-controlled, 16-19 = partly controlled, ≤15 = uncontrolled. Simple tool for PHC/CHC use.

5. Differential Diagnosis Tests

Chest X-ray: Usually normal in asthma (helps exclude pneumonia, TB, foreign body, pneumothorax). Hyperinflation may be seen in severe chronic asthma.

HRCT Chest: For atypical presentations, non-responsive asthma, to exclude bronchiectasis or other structural disease.

6. Key Diagnostic Criteria (GINA 2024)

Asthma is diagnosed by: (1) History of variable respiratory symptoms (wheeze, cough, dyspnea, chest tightness) that vary in intensity and over time, AND (2) Confirmed variable expiratory airflow limitation on spirometry (positive bronchodilator reversibility or PEF variability). Clinical diagnosis in children <6 years is based on symptom pattern, family history, and response to treatment trial.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Quick-Relief / Rescue Medications

Salbutamol Inhaler (MDI 100mcg/puff): MOST IMPORTANT rescue medication. 2-4 puffs via spacer for acute symptoms. Can repeat every 20 minutes for 3 doses in severe exacerbation. MUST be carried by every asthma patient at all times.

Ipratropium Bromide (MDI/Nebulization): Anticholinergic bronchodilator. Added to Salbutamol in severe exacerbations. Nebulized 0.5mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses in emergency.

2. Controller / Preventer Medications (Daily Use)

Low-Dose Inhaled Corticosteroid (ICS): Budesonide 200-400mcg/day or Beclomethasone 200-400mcg/day via MDI + spacer. CORNERSTONE of asthma control. Step 2 therapy. Prevents exacerbations, reduces airway inflammation.

ICS + LABA Combination: Budesonide/Formoterol (Budecort/Foracort) 200/6mcg or Fluticasone/Salmeterol (Seroflo) 250/50mcg. Step 3-4 therapy. Most prescribed controller in moderate-severe asthma. Can be used as both maintenance and reliever (MART strategy).

Montelukast (LTRA): 10mg once daily at bedtime (adults), 4-5mg (children). Add-on controller. Useful for exercise-induced and allergic asthma. Oral tablet — good for patients who refuse inhalers.

3. Severe Asthma Management

Systemic Corticosteroids: Prednisolone 40-50mg/day (adults) for 5-7 days for acute exacerbations. IV Hydrocortisone 100mg or Methylprednisolone for severe attacks requiring hospitalization.

Magnesium Sulfate (IV): 2g IV over 20 minutes for life-threatening or near-fatal asthma in emergency. Bronchodilatory effect via smooth muscle relaxation.

Oxygen Therapy: Target SpO2 94-98%. Nasal cannula or face mask during exacerbations. Humidified oxygen preferred.

Nebulization Protocol (Emergency): Salbutamol 2.5-5mg + Ipratropium 0.5mg nebulized every 20 minutes for first hour, then hourly.

4. Inhaler Technique Education (CRITICAL)

MDI + Spacer Technique: MUST be taught and demonstrated at every visit. Steps: shake inhaler, attach to spacer, exhale fully, seal lips around mouthpiece, press canister, breathe in slowly and deeply, hold breath 10 seconds. >80% of patients use inhalers incorrectly — this is the #1 cause of treatment failure.

Budesonide + Formoterol Inhaler (200/6mcg): Combination controller. Pack of 1.

Montelukast Tablets 10mg: Oral controller for add-on therapy. Pack of 10.

Deriphyllin Tablets (Theophylline + Etofylline): Bronchodilator. Pack of 10.

Prednisolone Tablets IP 5mg: Oral steroid for exacerbations. Pack of 10.

Spacer Device: Essential for proper MDI use. Available at Jan Aushadhi stores.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vasa / Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica): Premier Shwasahara herb in Ayurveda. Bronchodilatory and mucolytic. Vasicine alkaloid is the precursor of Bromhexine. Vasa Avaleha 10g twice daily or leaf juice 10-20ml twice daily.

Haridra / Turmeric (Curcuma longa): Anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory. Curcumin reduces airway inflammation in asthma. 500mg-1g curcumin extract daily or golden milk (Haldi doodh) at bedtime.

Pippali (Piper longum): Classical Shwasahara and Kasahara. Pippali Rasayana (graduated dosing) for chronic respiratory conditions. Enhances bioavailability of other herbs (Yogavahi).

Bharangi (Clerodendrum serratum): Potent bronchodilator in Ayurvedic texts. Published anti-asthmatic activity. Used in Bharangyadi Kwatha for Shwasa and Kasa.

Kanakasava: Classical Asava containing Dhattura (Datura metel) seed extract. Specifically indicated for Tamaka Shwasa (bronchial asthma). 15-30ml with equal water twice daily. Practitioner supervision essential.

Agastya Haritaki Rasayana: Classical formulation for chronic respiratory conditions. Contains Haritaki, Dashamoola, Bharangi, Pushkaramoola. 10g twice daily with warm water. Widely used for Shwasa and Kasa.

Sitopaladi Churna: For cough associated with asthma. 3g with honey thrice daily.

Chyawanprash: Rasayana for strengthening lungs and immunity. 10-20g daily. Especially recommended during winter months.

Himalaya Bresol Tablets/Syrup: Proprietary formulation with Haridra, Tulsi, Vasaka. Respiratory support. 2 tablets twice daily.

Dabur Vasavaleha: Branded Vasa preparation. 10g twice daily with warm water.

Yoga & Pranayama: Anulom-Vilom, Kapalbhati (with caution during exacerbation), Bhastrika (with caution), and Bhramari pranayama improve lung function. Buteyko breathing technique reduces hyperventilation. Published RCT evidence for improved asthma control.
diseases.india_specific:
Rising Prevalence in Urban India: Asthma affects approximately 3-5% of Indian adults and 5-10% of children. Urban prevalence is 2-3 times higher than rural due to air pollution, construction dust, and indoor allergens.

Air Pollution Crisis: India has 14 of the world's 20 most polluted cities. PM2.5 and PM10 levels regularly exceed WHO limits by 10-20x. Stubble burning (North India), vehicular emissions, and industrial pollution are major asthma triggers.

Biomass Fuel Smoke (Rural): Chulha smoke exposure in rural women and children causes chronic airway inflammation. This occupational asthma is preventable with LPG transition (Ujjwala Yojana).

Under-diagnosis & Inhaler Stigma: Asthma is significantly under-diagnosed in India. Many patients use oral medications (theophylline, steroids) instead of inhalers due to stigma — 'inhaler means serious disease' misconception. Inhaler education is critical.

Seasonal & Allergen Triggers: Monsoon mold, Diwali crackers (PM spikes), winter fog/smog, pollen (spring), and house dust mites are India-specific triggers. Occupational asthma from cotton dust (byssinosis), stone dust, and chemical exposure in Indian industries.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Inhalers Are Safe and Essential: The biggest barrier to asthma control in India is inhaler stigma. Educate: (1) Inhalers deliver medicine DIRECTLY to lungs — faster, more effective, fewer side effects than oral medicines. (2) Inhaled steroids at prescribed doses are SAFE for long-term use — they are NOT the same as oral steroids. (3) Using an inhaler does not mean disease is 'severe' or 'permanent'.

Ayurveda for Long-Term Support: Vasa, Bharangi, Kanakasava, and Agastya Haritaki have genuine bronchodilatory and anti-inflammatory effects for chronic management. They complement but CANNOT replace rescue inhalers (Salbutamol) during acute attacks. Always carry rescue inhaler even if using Ayurvedic maintenance therapy.

Asthma Action Plan — Traffic Light System: Green zone (well-controlled): take daily controller. Yellow zone (worsening): increase controller, use reliever. Red zone (severe): use reliever every 20 minutes, take oral steroids, GO TO HOSPITAL. Every patient needs a written action plan.

Avoid Triggers — India-Specific: Diwali crackers, mosquito coils, dhoop/agarbatti smoke, chulha fumes, construction dust, and burning garbage are common Indian asthma triggers. Indoor air quality matters as much as outdoor pollution.

Air Pollution Days — Stay Indoors: During AQI >200 days (common in Delhi NCR, Indo-Gangetic plain in winter), asthma patients should stay indoors, keep windows closed, use air purifier if possible, and may need temporary controller step-up.

diseases.name: Severe Dehydration
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment (WHO/IMNCI Framework)

WHO Dehydration Assessment: No dehydration: <5% body weight loss. Some dehydration: 5-10% loss (restless, sunken eyes, drinks eagerly, skin pinch slow). Severe dehydration: >10% loss (lethargic/unconscious, sunken eyes, unable to drink, skin pinch VERY slow). This clinical assessment requires NO laboratory tests.

IMNCI Classification (Children): Ask: duration of diarrhea, blood in stool. Look/Feel: general condition (alert/restless/lethargic), sunken eyes, skin pinch, ability to drink. Classify: No dehydration → Plan A (home ORS). Some dehydration → Plan B (ORS at health facility). Severe → Plan C (IV fluids).

Capillary Refill Time (CRT): Press nail bed for 5 seconds, release, measure color return. Normal <2 seconds. 2-3 seconds = some dehydration. >3 seconds = severe dehydration/shock.

2. Laboratory Tests (When Available)

Serum Electrolytes (Na+, K+, Cl-, HCO3-): Critical for guiding fluid replacement. Hyponatremic (<135), Isonatremic, or Hypernatremic (>145) dehydration have different fluid replacement strategies.

Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) & Creatinine: Elevated BUN:Creatinine ratio (>20:1) indicates pre-renal azotemia from dehydration. Monitor for acute kidney injury.

Blood Gas (ABG/VBG): Metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3-) common in severe dehydration. Guides bicarbonate replacement if pH <7.1.

Serum Lactate: Elevated lactate (>2 mmol/L) indicates tissue hypoperfusion from hypovolemia. Guides fluid resuscitation adequacy.

Complete Blood Count: Elevated hematocrit (hemoconcentration) from plasma volume loss. Helps quantify dehydration severity.

Urine Specific Gravity: >1.025 indicates concentrated urine/dehydration. Simple dipstick test.

3. Stool Tests (If Diarrheal Dehydration)

Stool Routine & Microscopy: RBCs, WBCs, parasites. Bloody/mucoid stool indicates dysentery (Shigella, Entamoeba).

Stool Culture: For persistent diarrhea, bloody diarrhea, or suspected cholera outbreak. Identifies Vibrio cholerae, Salmonella, Shigella.

Rapid Cholera Test (Crystal VC Dipstick): Point-of-care test for Vibrio cholerae O1/O139. Results in 15 minutes. Critical during monsoon outbreaks.

4. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Severe Dehydration is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS: Lethargic/unconscious + sunken eyes + unable/barely able to drink + skin pinch goes back VERY slowly. Laboratory tests support management but are NOT required for diagnosis. Treatment must begin IMMEDIATELY based on clinical assessment.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. WHO Plan C — Severe Dehydration (IV Fluids IMMEDIATELY)

Ringer's Lactate (IV): First-line IV fluid. Infuse 100 ml/kg over 3 hours (infants <12 months: 30 ml/kg in first hour, then 70 ml/kg in next 5 hours; children/adults: 30 ml/kg in 30 minutes, then 70 ml/kg in 2.5 hours). If RL not available, use Normal Saline.

Normal Saline 0.9% (IV): Alternative to RL. Same volume protocol. Preferred if hyperkalemia suspected.

Intraosseous Access: If IV access impossible in severely dehydrated/shocked child, use intraosseous (IO) needle in proximal tibia for emergency fluid administration. Saves lives when veins are collapsed.

2. WHO Plan B — Some Dehydration (ORS at Health Facility)

ORS Solution (WHO Low-Osmolarity): Give 75 ml/kg of ORS over 4 hours at health facility under observation. Reassess after 4 hours and reclassify. ORS composition: Na+ 75 mmol/L, Glucose 75 mmol/L, K+ 20 mmol/L, Citrate 10 mmol/L, Cl- 65 mmol/L.

3. WHO Plan A — No Dehydration (Home Treatment)

ORS (Home): Give ORS after every loose stool: <2 years: 50-100ml, 2-10 years: 100-200ml, >10 years: as much as wanted. Continue breastfeeding. Continue normal feeding.

Zinc Supplementation: 20mg/day for children >6 months (10mg for <6 months) for 10-14 days. Reduces diarrhea duration by 25% and recurrence for 2-3 months. WHO/UNICEF recommended. Included in India's IMNCI protocol.

4. Electrolyte Correction

Potassium Chloride (IV/Oral): For hypokalemia (K+ <3.5 mmol/L). IV KCl 10-20 mEq/hour via infusion pump (NEVER IV bolus). Oral potassium supplements for mild cases.

Sodium Bicarbonate (IV): For severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1). 1-2 mEq/kg IV slowly. Use cautiously.

5. Treat Underlying Cause

Oral Rehydration + Antibiotics for Cholera: ORS + Doxycycline 300mg single dose (adults) or Azithromycin 20mg/kg single dose (children/pregnant). Reduces stool output by 50%.

Antibiotics for Dysentery: Ciprofloxacin 500mg twice daily × 3 days for bloody diarrhea (Shigella). Metronidazole 400mg thrice daily × 5 days for Amoebic dysentery.

Antiemetics: Ondansetron 4mg sublingual/IV for persistent vomiting preventing ORS intake.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
ORS Powder (WHO Formula): Most critical product. Available at all Jan Aushadhi Kendras. Sachets.

Zinc Dispersible Tablets 20mg: WHO-recommended alongside ORS for diarrheal dehydration in children. Pack of 10.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For associated fever. Pack of 10.

Metronidazole Tablets IP 400mg: For amoebic dysentery causing dehydration. Pack of 10.

Norfloxacin Tablets IP 400mg: For bacterial diarrhea/dysentery. Pack of 10.

Ondansetron Tablets IP 4mg: Antiemetic for vomiting preventing oral rehydration. Pack of 10.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Zinc Tablets 20mg, 10's: ₹6.00

Metronidazole 400mg, 10's: ₹5.00
