diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Coconut Water (Narikela Jala): Nature's ORS — rich in potassium, sodium, magnesium, and natural sugars. Excellent for mild-moderate dehydration. 200-300ml every 2-4 hours. Most accessible in Southern and Coastal India.

Takra/Buttermilk (Chaas): Classical Ayurvedic rehydration drink. Buttermilk with rock salt, roasted cumin, and ginger is a traditional Indian electrolyte replacement. Grahi (absorbent) and Deepana (digestive).

Musta / Nagarmotha (Cyperus rotundus): Classical Atisaraghna (anti-diarrheal) and Grahi herb. 3-5g powder twice daily. Used in Mustakadi Kwatha for diarrheal diseases.

Kutaj / Kurchi (Holarrhena antidysenterica): Premier anti-diarrheal herb in Ayurveda. Bark extract effective against dysentery. Kutajghan Vati (Kutaj extract tablets) 2 tablets thrice daily. Published evidence for anti-diarrheal activity.

Bilva / Bael (Aegle marmelos): Unripe Bael fruit pulp is Grahi and Sangrahi (astringent/anti-diarrheal). Bael sherbet with sugar is traditional rehydration drink. Bilva Avaleha for chronic diarrhea.

Dadima / Pomegranate (Punica granatum): Fruit rind decoction is astringent and anti-diarrheal. Fresh juice is hydrating and nutritive. Rich in potassium and antioxidants.

Jeeraka/Cumin Water (Cuminum cyminum): Cumin boiled in water — traditional Deepana and Pachana remedy. Helps restore digestion and provides mineral supplementation.

Gangadhar Churna: Classical formulation for Atisara (diarrhea) containing Musta, Lodhra, Ativisha, and Shunthi. 3-5g twice daily with buttermilk.

Himalaya Diarex Tablets: Proprietary anti-diarrheal with Kutaj and Bilva. 2 tablets twice daily.

Dabur Kutajghan Vati: Branded classical Kutaj extract. Most widely used Ayurvedic anti-diarrheal. 2 tablets thrice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Diarrheal Disease — Leading Killer: Dehydration from acute diarrhea kills approximately 1 lakh children under 5 annually in India. Rotavirus, Cholera, and bacterial diarrhea during monsoon are primary causes. ORS + Zinc saves 90% of these lives.

Heat-Related Dehydration: India's extreme summers (45-50°C in many states) cause severe heat stroke and dehydration in outdoor workers (farmers, construction, laborers). Heat wave deaths are increasing annually.

Cholera Outbreaks: India remains cholera-endemic with outbreaks during monsoon. Severe cholera causes 'rice-water' diarrhea with fluid losses of 1 liter/hour, leading to fatal dehydration within hours if untreated.

Post-Surgical & Hospital-Acquired: Inadequate IV fluid management in resource-limited hospitals, especially during surgeries and postoperative recovery, contributes to iatrogenic dehydration.

Infant & Elderly Vulnerability: Infants have high body surface area to volume ratio — lose fluid faster. Elderly have blunted thirst response and often restrict fluids. Both groups decompensate rapidly.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — IV Fluids Save Lives in Severe Dehydration: Severe dehydration is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Lethargic/unconscious patients CANNOT drink ORS — they need IMMEDIATE IV Ringer's Lactate. Every minute of delay increases mortality. ASHA workers must recognize danger signs and refer immediately.

ORS + Zinc = Proven Life Saver: WHO estimates that ORS + Zinc prevents 93% of diarrheal deaths in children. ORS does NOT stop diarrhea — it replaces lost fluids while the body fights the infection. Parents must be taught: ORS after EVERY loose stool. Zinc for 14 days even after diarrhea stops.

Ayurvedic Remedies Are Complementary: Coconut water, buttermilk, Kutajghan Vati, and Bilva preparations support recovery, but CANNOT replace WHO ORS for fluid/electrolyte replacement in significant dehydration. In severe cases, NOTHING replaces IV fluids.

Do NOT Use Anti-Motility Drugs in Children: Loperamide (Imodium) is CONTRAINDICATED in children with acute diarrhea. It stops gut motility without addressing the cause, leading to toxic megacolon and worsening infection. Use ORS + Zinc ONLY.

Prevention — Clean Water & Handwashing: Boil drinking water, wash hands with soap after defecation and before eating/feeding. Rotavirus vaccination (now in India's UIP) prevents the most common cause of severe childhood diarrhea. Breastfed infants have significantly lower diarrheal dehydration risk.
confirmation_tests_curated: WHO Dehydration Assessment: No dehydration: <5% body weight loss. Some dehydration: 5-10% loss (restless, sunken eyes, drinks eagerly, skin pinch slow). Severe dehydration: >10% loss (lethargic/unconscious, sunken eyes, unable to drink, skin pinch VERY slow). This clinical assessment requires NO laboratory tests.

IMNCI Classification (Children): Ask: duration of diarrhea, blood in stool. Look/Feel: general condition (alert/restless/lethargic), sunken eyes, skin pinch, ability to drink. Classify: No dehydration → Plan A (home ORS). Some dehydration → Plan B (ORS at health facility). Severe → Plan C (IV fluids).
Capillary Refill Time (CRT): Press nail bed for 5 seconds, release, measure color return. Normal <2 seconds. 2-3 seconds = some dehydration. >3 seconds = severe dehydration/shock.

diseases.name: Neonatal Fever
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment (IMNCI/HBNC Framework)

IMNCI Danger Signs in Young Infants (0-2 months): NOT feeding well, convulsions, fast breathing (≥60/min), severe chest indrawing, temperature ≥38°C or <35.5°C, movement only when stimulated or no movement. ANY ONE danger sign = classify as POSSIBLE SERIOUS BACTERIAL INFECTION → urgent referral.

2. Sepsis Screening (Blood Tests)

Blood Culture (Aerobic): Gold standard for confirming neonatal sepsis. Collect 1ml blood from peripheral vein (minimum) BEFORE starting antibiotics. Takes 48-72 hours. Negative culture does not rule out sepsis.

Complete Blood Count (CBC) with Differential: Leukocytosis (>25,000) or leukopenia (<5,000) at birth or >5 days, neutropenia (<1,500), immature:total neutrophil ratio (I:T ratio) >0.2, thrombocytopenia (<100,000) all suggest sepsis.

C-Reactive Protein (CRP): Elevated CRP (>10 mg/L) supports sepsis diagnosis. Serial CRP useful for monitoring treatment response. Sensitivity improves if measured at 24-48 hours of illness onset.

Procalcitonin: More specific than CRP for bacterial infection. >2 ng/mL highly suggestive of sepsis. Physiologically elevated in first 48 hours of life — interpret cautiously in early-onset sepsis.

Micro-ESR: Simple test requiring minimal blood. Elevated in neonatal sepsis. Available at primary level.

3. Cerebrospinal Fluid Analysis (Lumbar Puncture)

CSF Analysis: Mandatory in all neonates with suspected sepsis to rule out meningitis. CSF cell count (>20 WBC/mm³ abnormal in neonates), glucose (<40 mg/dL or <60% of blood glucose), protein (>150 mg/dL in term, >170 in preterm), Gram stain, and culture.

4. Urine Tests

Urine Culture (Suprapubic Aspirate/Catheter): UTI is a common cause of neonatal fever. Clean-catch unreliable in neonates — suprapubic aspiration or catheterization needed for accurate culture.

5. Additional Tests

Chest X-ray: For respiratory distress — evaluate for pneumonia, RDS, pneumothorax.

Abdominal X-ray: If abdominal distension — evaluate for NEC (pneumatosis intestinalis) or bowel obstruction.

Blood Glucose: Hypoglycemia (<45 mg/dL) or hyperglycemia common in septic neonates.

Serum Electrolytes & Bilirubin: Metabolic derangements and jaundice often accompany neonatal sepsis.

6. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis (EONS): Within 72 hours of birth. Usually acquired from maternal birth canal (GBS, E. coli). Late-Onset Neonatal Sepsis (LONS): After 72 hours. Hospital-acquired (NICU) or community-acquired. Clinical Sepsis: Clinical signs + raised sepsis markers but negative cultures. Culture-Proven Sepsis: Positive blood/CSF/urine culture. In India, >50% of neonatal sepsis is culture-negative but clinically treated.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (Start IMMEDIATELY on suspicion)

Ampicillin + Gentamicin (First-Line EONS): WHO and NNF (India) recommended. Ampicillin 50mg/kg IV every 12 hours (increase to every 8 hours if meningitis suspected) + Gentamicin 5mg/kg IV once daily. Covers GBS, E. coli, Listeria.

Cefotaxime + Amikacin (Alternative / LONS): For hospital-acquired or Gentamicin-resistant infections. Cefotaxime 50mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours + Amikacin 15mg/kg IV once daily.

Piperacillin-Tazobactam / Meropenem: For NICU-acquired MDR sepsis. Meropenem 20-40mg/kg IV every 8 hours. Reserve for ESBL/MDR organisms confirmed on culture.

Vancomycin: For suspected MRSA or CoNS (coagulase-negative Staphylococcus) infections, especially in NICU setting with central lines. 15mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours.

2. Meningitis-Dose Antibiotics

If meningitis suspected or confirmed: Ampicillin dose increased to 100mg/kg every 6 hours + Cefotaxime 50mg/kg every 6 hours. Duration: minimum 14 days for GBS, 21 days for Gram-negative meningitis.

3. Supportive Care (CRITICAL)

Thermoregulation: Maintain temperature 36.5-37.5°C using radiant warmer, incubator, or Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC). Hypothermia worsens mortality.

IV Fluids & Glucose: 10% Dextrose at 60-80 ml/kg/day initially. Maintain blood glucose 45-120 mg/dL. Adjust fluids based on weight and electrolytes.

Oxygen Therapy: For SpO2 <90% (preterm target 90-95%, term 94-98%). Nasal prongs, CPAP, or mechanical ventilation as needed.

Feeding Support: Continue breastfeeding if neonate can feed. If not — expressed breast milk (EBM) via orogastric tube. Breast milk provides passive immunity and reduces NEC risk.

4. Prevention

Clean Delivery Practices: Institutional delivery, sterile cord cutting, hand hygiene. Chlorhexidine (4%) application to cord stump (WHO-recommended for home births in high-mortality settings like India).

Essential Newborn Care: Immediate drying, delayed cord clamping, early skin-to-skin contact, early breastfeeding initiation within 1 hour. Part of India's Navjaat Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (NSSK).
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Ampicillin Injection 500mg: First-line neonatal sepsis antibiotic. Available at Jan Aushadhi.

Gentamicin Injection 80mg/2ml: First-line aminoglycoside for neonatal sepsis.

Amikacin Injection 250mg: Alternative aminoglycoside for resistant infections.

Ceftriaxone Injection 250mg: For neonatal infections (avoid in jaundiced neonates — displaces bilirubin).

Paracetamol Drops (Pediatric): For neonatal fever. Weight-based dosing critical. Drops/syrup form.

ORS Powder: For associated diarrheal dehydration if present.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Ampicillin 500mg injection: ₹12.00

Gentamicin 80mg/2ml injection: ₹5.50

Amikacin 250mg injection: ₹18.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT NOTE — Ayurvedic Medicines Are Generally NOT Recommended for Neonates (0-28 days): Neonatal physiology is fundamentally different from older children/adults. Drug metabolism, renal clearance, and hepatic function are immature. Classical Ayurvedic texts recommend ONLY breast milk (Stanya) as the primary medicine for neonates. The following are traditional supportive practices ONLY:

Suvarna Prashana (Gold Bhasma with Honey & Ghee): Classical Ayurvedic immunization/Rasayana practice for infants. Suvarna (gold) Bhasma in minuscule quantity with honey and ghee on Pushya Nakshatra day. Practiced across India. Some published evidence for immunomodulation. ONLY from certified Ayurvedic practitioners.

Breast Milk (Stanya — Primary Ayurvedic Medicine): Ayurveda considers breast milk as Amrita (nectar) for neonates. Exclusive breastfeeding provides passive immunity (IgA), prebiotics, and optimal nutrition. This aligns perfectly with WHO recommendation of exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months.

Abhyanga (Gentle Oil Massage): Traditional neonatal massage with warm sesame oil or coconut oil. Improves circulation, weight gain, and bonding. Begin after umbilical cord stump falls off. Gentle strokes only — never vigorous.

Prakshalana (Herbal Bath): Mild herbal bath with Triphala or Neem water for skin hygiene after cord stump separation. NOT during acute illness.

Kumara Kalyanaka Ghrita: Classical formulation for infant health and development. Contains Brahmi, Vacha, and other Medhya herbs in ghee base. Given in very small quantities (drops) after 1 month of age. Practitioner-prescribed only.
diseases.india_specific:
Neonatal Sepsis — India's Major Killer: Neonatal sepsis accounts for 30-40% of neonatal deaths in India. India contributes to the largest share of global neonatal mortality. Risk factors include home deliveries without sterile technique, premature rupture of membranes, low birth weight, and lack of postnatal care.

Home Deliveries & Unhygienic Cord Care: Despite institutional delivery improvements under Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY), 15-20% of deliveries still occur at home. Application of cow dung, turmeric paste, or other substances to the umbilical cord stump is a major cause of neonatal tetanus and omphalitis (cord infection).

Antibiotic Resistance in Neonatal Units: Indian NICUs report extremely high rates of MDR organisms — ESBL-producing E. coli, Klebsiella, MRSA, and Acinetobacter. Empirical antibiotic selection must account for local resistance patterns.

Delayed Recognition: Families in rural India may not recognize neonatal danger signs (poor feeding, lethargy, fever, fast breathing) and delay seeking care. ASHA worker training in HBNC (Home-Based Newborn Care) is critical.

Low Birth Weight & Prematurity: India has the highest number of LBW babies globally (~20% of live births). LBW and preterm neonates are immunologically immature and at much higher risk for sepsis.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — Any Fever in Neonate = Hospital Emergency: A neonate (0-28 days) with fever ≥38°C (or hypothermia <36.5°C) MUST be taken to hospital IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT attempt home treatment with any medicine — Ayurvedic or allopathic. Neonatal sepsis can kill within hours. There is NO safe 'wait and watch' approach for neonatal fever.

Breast Milk Is the Best Medicine and Prevention: Exclusive breastfeeding from birth provides passive immunity that is the neonate's primary defense against infection. Colostrum (first milk) is especially rich in antibodies. Do NOT give honey, sugar water, or herbal preparations to neonates in the first 6 months.

Clean Cord Care Saves Lives: Apply NOTHING to the umbilical cord stump except Chlorhexidine 4% (for home births). No cow dung, ash, turmeric, or other traditional applications. These cause omphalitis and neonatal tetanus.

ASHA Workers — Recognize Danger Signs: During Home-Based Newborn Care (HBNC) visits (Days 1, 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42), assess for: not feeding well, fever/cold to touch, fast breathing, chest indrawing, lethargy, convulsions. ANY one sign → immediate referral to health facility.

Ayurvedic Herbs Not Safe for Neonates: The immature liver and kidneys of neonates cannot process herbal compounds safely. Do NOT give Guduchi, Tulsi, or any herbal decoctions to babies under 28 days. Suvarna Prashana should only be from certified practitioners after 28 days.
confirmation_tests_curated: IMNCI Danger Signs in Young Infants (0-2 months): NOT feeding well, convulsions, fast breathing (≥60/min), severe chest indrawing, temperature ≥38°C or <35.5°C, movement only when stimulated or no movement. ANY ONE danger sign = classify as POSSIBLE SERIOUS BACTERIAL INFECTION → urgent referral.

Blood Culture (Aerobic): Gold standard for confirming neonatal sepsis. Collect 1ml blood from peripheral vein (minimum) BEFORE starting antibiotics. Takes 48-72 hours. Negative culture does not rule out sepsis.

Complete Blood Count (CBC) with Differential: Leukocytosis (>25,000) or leukopenia (<5,000) at birth or >5 days, neutropenia (<1,500), immature:total neutrophil ratio (I:T ratio) >0.2, thrombocytopenia (<100,000) all suggest sepsis.

diseases.name: Common Cold & Flu
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Usually Sufficient)

Clinical Differentiation — Cold vs Flu: Common Cold: gradual onset, mild symptoms, prominent rhinorrhea/sneezing, low/no fever, functional. Influenza: ABRUPT onset, high fever, severe myalgia/headache/fatigue, less rhinorrhea, prostration. Clinical diagnosis is adequate for most cases — laboratory tests NOT routinely needed.

2. Laboratory Tests (Only for Severe/Complicated Cases)

Rapid Influenza Diagnostic Test (RIDT): Nasal/throat swab. Detects Influenza A & B antigens in 15 minutes. Sensitivity 50-70% (moderate). Available at sentinel surveillance labs.

RT-PCR for Influenza: Gold standard for influenza confirmation and subtyping (H1N1, H3N2, Influenza B). Nasopharyngeal swab. Available at NIV Pune network and ICMR labs. Results in 4-6 hours.

Complete Blood Count: Leukopenia with lymphocytosis typical of viral infection. Leukocytosis with neutrophilia suggests secondary bacterial infection.

Chest X-ray: Only if pneumonia suspected (persistent fever >5 days, progressive dyspnea, SpO2 <94%). Not routine for uncomplicated cold/flu.

3. When to Test / Refer

Test for influenza in: Hospitalized patients with severe respiratory illness, suspected outbreaks, immunocompromised patients, pregnant women with influenza-like illness. Refer if: Dyspnea, SpO2 <94%, persistent fever >5 days, confusion, dehydration, underlying comorbidity with worsening symptoms.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Symptomatic Treatment (MAINSTAY — Both Cold & Flu)

Paracetamol: 500-1000mg every 4-6 hours for fever, headache, and body aches. Weight-based in children. FIRST-LINE symptomatic treatment. Avoid Aspirin in children (Reye's Syndrome risk).

Antihistamines (Cetirizine/Levocetirizine): Cetirizine 10mg daily or Levocetirizine 5mg daily for rhinorrhea, sneezing, and nasal congestion. Chlorpheniramine 4mg at bedtime if sedation acceptable. Helps dry secretions.

Nasal Saline Drops/Spray: Normal saline nasal drops for congestion relief, especially in infants and children. Safe, no side effects, can be used frequently. Helps clear mucus before feeding in infants.

Steam Inhalation: Inhaling steam (plain water or with menthol/eucalyptus) for 10-15 minutes 2-3 times daily. Decongests nasal passages. Use safely — keep children away from hot water.

Cough Suppressants (Dextromethorphan): For dry, irritative cough disturbing sleep. 10-20mg every 4-6 hours. NOT for productive cough (don't suppress useful cough).

Decongestants (Pseudoephedrine/Oxymetazoline): Pseudoephedrine 60mg oral for nasal congestion. Oxymetazoline nasal spray for rapid relief (max 3 days to avoid rebound congestion). Avoid in hypertension.

2. Influenza-Specific Antiviral (When Indicated)

Oseltamivir (Tamiflu): 75mg twice daily for 5 days (adults), weight-based in children. Start within 48 hours of symptom onset for maximum benefit. Indicated for: confirmed/suspected influenza with severe illness, hospitalization, high-risk groups (pregnant, elderly, immunocompromised, comorbid). NOT for common cold.

3. CRITICAL — What NOT to Prescribe

NO ANTIBIOTICS for Common Cold or Uncomplicated Influenza: Antibiotics do NOT work against viruses. Prescribing Azithromycin/Amoxicillin/Cefixime for cold/flu is INAPPROPRIATE and drives antibiotic resistance. Reserve antibiotics ONLY for confirmed secondary bacterial infections (sinusitis >10 days, otitis media, bacterial pneumonia).

4. Prevention

Annual Influenza Vaccination: Recommended for high-risk groups (elderly >65, diabetics, COPD/asthma, pregnant, healthcare workers, immunocompromised). Quadrivalent vaccine covering H1N1, H3N2, and two Influenza B lineages.

Hand Hygiene & Respiratory Etiquette: Frequent handwashing with soap, cover mouth when coughing/sneezing, avoid touching face. Most effective prevention measure.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: First-line symptomatic treatment. Pack of 10.

Cetirizine Tablets IP 10mg: Antihistamine for rhinorrhea/sneezing. Pack of 10.

Levocetirizine Tablets IP 5mg: Non-sedating antihistamine. Pack of 10.

Chlorpheniramine Tablets IP 4mg: Sedating antihistamine for nighttime symptoms. Pack of 10.

Ambroxol Tablets IP 30mg: Mucolytic for productive cough. Pack of 10.

Normal Saline Nasal Drops: For nasal congestion relief in infants/children.

Cough Syrup (Dextromethorphan-based): For dry cough relief.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Paracetamol 500mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Cetirizine 10mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Levocetirizine 5mg, 10's: ₹5.50

Ambroxol 30mg, 10's: ₹6.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Kadha / Kwatha (Traditional Herbal Decoction): India's most widely used home remedy. Recipe: Tulsi leaves + Ginger + Black pepper + Cinnamon + Cloves + Turmeric boiled in 2 cups water reduced to 1 cup. Add honey when lukewarm. 2-3 cups daily. Genuinely effective for symptom relief — endorsed by AYUSH Ministry during COVID-19.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum — Holy Basil): Krimighna, Jwaraghna, and Shwasahara. Fresh leaves chewed or as tea. Contains eugenol with anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity. The most accessible medicinal herb in India — grown in most households.

Ginger / Shunthi (Zingiber officinale): Deepana, Pachana, Kasahara. Fresh ginger tea, ginger juice with honey, or dry ginger (Shunthi) powder. Anti-inflammatory, analgesic. Relieves sore throat, nausea, and body aches.

Turmeric / Haridra (Curcuma longa): Anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial. Haldi Doodh (Golden Milk — turmeric in warm milk with black pepper for bioavailability) is India's traditional cold/flu remedy. 1 cup at bedtime.

Black Pepper / Maricha (Piper nigrum): Part of Trikatu (Pippali + Maricha + Shunthi). Kaphahara and Deepana. Clears nasal/respiratory congestion. Enhances bioavailability of other herbs. 2-3 peppercorns crushed in honey.

Honey / Madhu: Kaphahara and Kasahara. Soothes sore throat, suppresses cough. WHO recommends honey for pediatric cough (>1 year). 1-2 tsp with warm water or herbal tea. Do NOT give to infants <1 year (botulism risk).

Mulethi / Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra): Soothes inflamed throat and respiratory mucosa. Chew small piece of Mulethi root or take powder 3-5g with honey. Demulcent and mild expectorant.

Sitopladi Churna: Classical formulation for Kasa (cough). 3g with honey 2-3 times daily. Effective for post-nasal drip cough and respiratory congestion.

Laxmi Vilas Ras: Classical formulation for Pratishyaya (common cold/coryza). Contains Abhrak Bhasma, Maricha. 125-250mg twice daily with honey.

Chyawanprash: Daily Rasayana for immune maintenance during cold/flu season. 10-20g daily with warm milk. Especially important for children and elderly.

Dabur Honitus Herbal Cough Remedy: Honey-based herbal cough syrup with Tulsi, Mulethi, Banaphsha. 10ml 2-3 times daily. Widely available OTC.

Himalaya Cold Balm / Zandu Balm: Topical decongestant containing menthol, camphor, eucalyptus oil. Apply to chest, throat, and temples. Provides relief from congestion and headache through vapor inhalation.
diseases.india_specific:
Seasonal Patterns in India: Common cold peaks in winter (November-February) and monsoon season. Influenza has year-round transmission in India with peaks during monsoon (June-September) and winter (December-February). H1N1 (Swine Flu) outbreaks recur periodically.

Antibiotic Misuse — India's Crisis: The biggest India-specific issue — antibiotics are prescribed for >70% of common cold/flu cases despite being viral diseases. This drives India's antibiotic resistance crisis. Azithromycin, Amoxicillin, and Cefixime are routinely over-prescribed for self-limiting viral infections.

Air Pollution Worsens Respiratory Infections: Delhi NCR and Indo-Gangetic plain winter smog (AQI 400-500+) damages respiratory mucosa, increasing susceptibility to viral infections and prolonging recovery.

H1N1 (Swine Flu) — Recurring Threat: India has experienced multiple H1N1 outbreaks since 2009 pandemic. Maharashtra, Rajasthan, Gujarat report most cases. H1N1 can cause severe pneumonia in pregnant women, children, and immunocompromised.

Traditional Home Remedies — Strong Existing Practice: India has one of the richest traditions of home remedies for cold/flu (Kadha, Haldi doodh, Tulsi tea, steam inhalation). These are genuinely effective for symptom relief and should be encouraged over unnecessary antibiotics.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — NO Antibiotics for Cold/Flu: Common cold and influenza are VIRAL infections. Antibiotics CANNOT treat them and their misuse is the primary driver of India's antibiotic resistance crisis. Educate patients: antibiotics are for BACTERIA, not viruses. Green/yellow mucus does NOT mean bacterial infection — it's normal viral infection progression.

Ayurvedic Kadha & Home Remedies — Genuinely Effective: Unlike many conditions where Ayurveda is complementary, traditional Indian cold/flu remedies (Kadha, Tulsi tea, Haldi Doodh, steam inhalation, honey) are genuinely first-line effective treatments. The AYUSH Ministry endorsed Kadha during COVID-19. Encourage these OVER pharmacy visits for uncomplicated cold/flu.

When to Seek Medical Attention: Uncomplicated cold/flu resolves in 7-10 days. Seek medical help if: fever >5 days, dyspnea, SpO2 <94%, blood in sputum, inability to drink fluids, severe headache/neck stiffness, dehydration signs, worsening after initial improvement (suggests secondary bacterial infection).

Influenza — Oseltamivir for High-Risk Groups: During H1N1/Influenza season, high-risk patients (pregnant women, elderly, diabetics, COPD/asthma, immunocompromised) with influenza-like illness should receive Oseltamivir within 48 hours. Do NOT wait for test confirmation in high-risk cases.

Prevention Is Better Than Cure: Annual influenza vaccination for high-risk groups, frequent handwashing, respiratory etiquette (cover cough/sneeze), adequate hydration, and daily Chyawanprash/Tulsi during winter/monsoon season are the best prevention strategies.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Differentiation — Cold vs Flu: Common Cold: gradual onset, mild symptoms, prominent rhinorrhea/sneezing, low/no fever, functional. Influenza: ABRUPT onset, high fever, severe myalgia/headache/fatigue, less rhinorrhea, prostration. Clinical diagnosis is adequate for most cases — laboratory tests NOT routinely needed.
Rapid Influenza Diagnostic Test (RIDT): Nasal/throat swab. Detects Influenza A & B antigens in 15 minutes. Sensitivity 50-70% (moderate). Available at sentinel surveillance labs.
RT-PCR for Influenza: Gold standard for influenza confirmation and subtyping (H1N1, H3N2, Influenza B). Nasopharyngeal swab. Available at NIV Pune network and ICMR labs. Results in 4-6 hours.

diseases.name: Malaria
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Physical/Clinical Assessments

Temperature Measurement: Persistent fever above 38°C with periodic chills suggests malaria in endemic regions.

Spleen Palpation: Enlarged spleen detected during abdominal examination can indicate chronic malaria infection.

Clinical History Assessment: History of fever with travel or residence in malaria-endemic areas.

2. Routine Blood/Lab Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): May show anemia (low hemoglobin), thrombocytopenia (low platelets), and leukopenia.

Hemoglobin Level: Reduced hemoglobin levels indicate malaria-related hemolysis.

Platelet Count: Platelet levels often drop below 150,000/µL in malaria infections.

Liver Function Tests (LFT): Elevated bilirubin and liver enzymes may occur in severe malaria.

3. Specific Diagnostic Tests

Peripheral Blood Smear (Thick and Thin): Gold standard test recommended by WHO for detecting Plasmodium parasites and species identification.

Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT): Detects malaria antigens such as HRP2 (P. falciparum) or pLDH. Widely used in Indian PHCs and ASHA programs.

Quantitative Buffy Coat (QBC) Test: Fluorescent microscopy technique for detecting malaria parasites.

PCR-Based Detection: Highly sensitive molecular test used in specialized laboratories to detect Plasmodium DNA.

4. Advanced/Specialized Diagnostics (If Needed)

Blood Glucose Test: Hypoglycemia is common in severe malaria, especially in children.

Serum Creatinine: Elevated levels indicate kidney involvement in severe malaria.

Arterial Blood Gas (ABG): Used in severe cases to detect metabolic acidosis.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria (Indian & WHO Guidelines)

WHO/ICMR Criteria: Presence of Plasmodium parasites on microscopy or positive RDT in a patient with fever or history of fever confirms malaria diagnosis.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Antimalarial Treatment

Artemether + Lumefantrine: Oral artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) used for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.

Artesunate: Injectable antimalarial used in severe malaria cases in hospital settings.

Chloroquine: Oral treatment used primarily for Plasmodium vivax malaria in regions where resistance is low.

Primaquine: Oral medication used to eliminate dormant liver forms (hypnozoites) of P. vivax and P. ovale.

2. Routine Medications for Malaria (Generic)

Paracetamol: Oral antipyretic used to reduce fever and relieve headache and body pain.

Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS): Used to manage dehydration caused by fever and vomiting.

Iron + Folic Acid: Given when anemia develops after malaria infection.

3. Supportive/Adjunct Treatment

Ondansetron: Antiemetic used to control vomiting in malaria patients.

Ibuprofen: Used for pain and fever management when appropriate.

4. Hospital-Based/Severe Case Treatments

Intravenous Artesunate: WHO recommended first-line treatment for severe malaria.

Blood Transfusion: Required in severe anemia caused by malaria.

Intravenous Fluids: Used to manage dehydration and electrolyte imbalance in severe cases.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
1. Relevant Jan Aushadhi Medicines

Artemether + Lumefantrine Tablets: Generic ACT available at PMBJP stores for malaria treatment.

Chloroquine Phosphate Tablets: Affordable antimalarial for treatment of P. vivax malaria.

Primaquine Tablets: Used to prevent relapse of malaria by eliminating liver-stage parasites.

Paracetamol Tablets: Used to manage fever associated with malaria.

ORS Sachets: Used to treat dehydration during febrile illness.

Iron + Folic Acid Tablets: Support recovery from malaria-related anemia.

2. Indicative PMBJP Prices

Paracetamol 500 mg (10 tablets): ₹5–8

ORS Sachet (WHO formula): ₹3–5

Iron + Folic Acid Tablets (10 tablets): ₹10–15

Chloroquine Tablets (10 tablets): ₹12–20
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Sudarshan Churna: Classical Ayurvedic formulation traditionally used for fever including malaria-like conditions.

Mahamrityunjaya Rasa: Used in Ayurveda to manage chronic fever and systemic infections.

Tribhuvan Kirti Rasa: Herbal-mineral preparation used for fever management.

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia): Known for immune-boosting and antipyretic properties.

Amrita Satva: Extract of Guduchi used to improve immunity and fight infections.

Dabur Giloy Tablets: Popular Ayurvedic product supporting immunity and fever control.

Himalaya Sudarshan Tablets: Herbal formulation used for recurrent fever conditions.

Kottakkal Sudarshan Tablets: Ayurvedic anti-pyretic formulation.

Guduchi Kashaya (Decoction): Traditional herbal decoction used for fever and immunity support.

Panchakarma Detoxification (Under supervision): Used in chronic or recurrent infections to improve immunity.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Severe Anemia: Common in malaria-endemic regions of India due to repeated infections and nutritional deficiencies. Monsoon Season Transmission: Increased mosquito breeding during monsoon leads to higher malaria incidence. Malnutrition: Undernourished individuals, especially children, have increased susceptibility to severe malaria. Poor Sanitation and Standing Water: Rural areas with stagnant water bodies provide breeding grounds for Anopheles mosquito
diseases.important_notes:
Immediate Referral: Severe symptoms such as unconsciousness, convulsions, severe anemia, or breathing difficulty require urgent hospital referral.

Diagnostic Confirmation Required: Fever alone is not sufficient for diagnosis; microscopy or RDT confirmation is essential before treatment.

Combination Therapy Importance: Artemisinin-based combination therapy must be completed fully to prevent drug resistance.

Avoid Self-Medication: Antimalarial drugs should not be taken without confirmed diagnosis due to risk of resistance and incorrect treatment.

Ayurvedic Support Only: Ayurvedic medicines may support recovery but should not replace standard antimalarial treatment in confirmed malaria.

diseases.name: Anaemia
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Physical/Clinical Assessments

Clinical Pallor Examination: Checking conjunctiva, palms and nail beds for pallor.

Pulse and Respiratory Rate: Tachycardia or rapid breathing may indicate moderate or severe anaemia.

Medical and Dietary History: Assess diet, menstrual history, pregnancy status and deworming history.

2. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): Measures hemoglobin and red blood cell indices.

Hemoglobin Test: Confirms anemia based on Hb level thresholds.

Hematocrit (PCV): Indicates proportion of red blood cells in blood.

Peripheral Blood Smear: Evaluates morphology of red blood cells.

3. Iron Studies

Serum Ferritin: Low levels indicate iron deficiency.

Serum Iron and TIBC: Helps differentiate iron deficiency from chronic disease anemia.

Reticulocyte Count: Indicates bone marrow response.

4. Vitamin Deficiency Tests

Vitamin B12 Test: Detects B12 deficiency anemia.

Serum Folate: Identifies folate deficiency.

5. Additional Diagnostics

Stool Examination for Parasites: Detects hookworm infection causing chronic blood loss.

Hemoglobin Electrophoresis: Detects thalassemia or sickle cell disease.

6. Key Diagnostic Criteria

WHO Hemoglobin Thresholds: Adult men <13 g/dL, adult women <12 g/dL, pregnant women <11 g/dL indicate anaemia.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Iron Supplementation Therapy

Ferrous Sulfate: Oral iron supplement used to treat iron deficiency anaemia.

Ferrous Fumarate: Common oral iron therapy used in public health programs.

Ferrous Ascorbate: Iron combined with vitamin C to enhance absorption.

2. Routine Medicines

Iron + Folic Acid Tablets: Standard therapy in national anemia control programs.

Folic Acid Tablets: Used for folate deficiency anemia.

Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin): Used for B12 deficiency anemia.

3. Adjunct Treatments

Albendazole: Deworming medicine used to eliminate hookworm infection.

Vitamin C Tablets: Improves absorption of dietary iron.

4. Severe Case Treatments

Iron Sucrose Injection: Intravenous iron therapy for moderate to severe anemia.

Blood Transfusion: Used in life-threatening severe anemia.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
1. Jan Aushadhi Medicines

Ferrous Ascorbate + Folic Acid Tablets: Affordable iron supplement available through PMBJP stores.

Folic Acid Tablets 5 mg: Used to correct folate deficiency.

Vitamin B12 Tablets: Used to manage vitamin B12 deficiency.

Albendazole Tablets: Deworming drug to treat parasitic infections contributing to anaemia.

Vitamin C Tablets: Helps improve iron absorption.

2. Indicative Prices

Folic Acid 5 mg (10 tablets): ₹3–5

Ferrous Ascorbate + Folic Acid (10 tablets): ₹20–25
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Punarnavadi Mandura: Classical Ayurvedic formulation used in Pandu (anaemia) to improve hemoglobin.

Lauha Bhasma: Ayurvedic iron preparation traditionally used to increase hemoglobin levels.

Navayasa Churna: Herbal powder used for treating Pandu and improving digestion.

Drakshasava: Ayurvedic tonic used to improve strength and appetite.

Guduchi (Giloy): Herbal medicine known to improve immunity and recovery.

Ashwagandha Churna: Used to increase strength and reduce fatigue.

Baidyanath Punarnava Mandur: Popular Ayurvedic product used in anaemia support.

Dabur Lauh Asava: Herbal iron tonic used traditionally for anaemia.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Iron Deficiency from Low-Iron Diet: Predominantly cereal-based diets with low heme iron intake increase risk in rural India. Hookworm Infection: Chronic intestinal blood loss due to parasitic infections common in poor sanitation areas. Pregnancy-Related Anaemia: Increased iron requirement during pregnancy leading to deficiency. Heavy Menstrual Bleeding: A common cause of iron deficiency among adolescent girls and women.
diseases.important_notes:
Medical Confirmation Required: Always confirm anaemia using hemoglobin testing before starting treatment.

Severe Anaemia Referral: Patients with breathlessness, chest pain or fainting should be referred immediately to a hospital.

Avoid Self Medication: Iron medicines should not be taken without medical advice as incorrect use can cause complications.

Dietary Improvement: Encourage iron-rich foods like green leafy vegetables, jaggery, pulses and fortified foods.

diseases.name: Chikungunya
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Clinical Assessment

Acute Fever with Severe Arthralgia: Key clinical indicator.

2. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): May show mild leukopenia.

CRP and ESR: Elevated inflammatory markers.

3. Specific Diagnostic Tests

RT‑PCR for Chikungunya Virus: Detects viral RNA in early phase.

IgM ELISA: Detects antibodies after day 5 of illness.

4. Differential Tests

Dengue NS1 Test: Helps differentiate dengue infection.

Malaria Rapid Test: Used where fever cause unclear.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Primary Symptomatic Treatment

Paracetamol: Used for fever and pain relief.

2. Anti‑Inflammatory Medicines

Ibuprofen: Used for joint pain after dengue ruled out.

Naproxen: Used for prolonged joint inflammation.

3. Supportive Care

Oral Rehydration Salts: Prevent dehydration.

Vitamin B Complex: Helps recovery from weakness.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
1. Relevant Jan Aushadhi Medicines

Paracetamol Tablets: Affordable fever relief medicine.

Ibuprofen Tablets: Used for inflammatory joint pain.

ORS Sachets: Prevent dehydration during fever.

Vitamin B Complex Tablets: Supports recovery.

2. Indicative Prices

Paracetamol 500 mg (10 tablets): ₹5‑8

ORS Sachet: ₹3‑5
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Sudarshan Churna: Ayurvedic formulation used for fever.

Guduchi (Giloy): Immunomodulatory herb used in viral fever.

Dashmool Kwath: Used for inflammation and joint pain.

Ashwagandha Churna: Helps reduce fatigue after illness.

Yograj Guggulu: Used for joint pain in Ayurveda.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Monsoon Mosquito Breeding: Aedes mosquitoes increase during rainy season. Urban Water Storage: Containers and tanks create mosquito breeding sites. Dengue Co‑infection Risk: Similar mosquito vectors cause overlapping outbreaks. Poor Vector Control: Limited mosquito control increases transmission.
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Rule Out Dengue: NSAIDs should only be given after dengue is excluded. Hydration Essential: Encourage ORS and fluids during fever. Vector Control: Remove stagnant water to prevent mosquito breeding. Medical Referral: Severe symptoms require referral to hospital.

diseases.name: Allergic Rhinitis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment (Usually Sufficient)

ARIA Classification: Intermittent (<4 days/week or <4 weeks) vs Persistent (>4 days/week AND >4 weeks). Mild (normal sleep, no impairment) vs Moderate-Severe (impaired sleep/daily activities). Guides treatment step.

Anterior Rhinoscopy: Pale, boggy, bluish-gray nasal mucosa with clear secretions (vs red mucosa in infectious rhinitis). Inferior turbinate hypertrophy. Nasal polyps in chronic cases.

2. Allergy-Specific Tests

Skin Prick Testing (SPT): Gold standard for identifying specific allergens. Panel includes house dust mites, pollen, mold, cockroach, pet dander. Results in 15-20 minutes. Available at allergy clinics.

Serum Total IgE: Elevated (>100 IU/mL) supports allergic etiology. Non-specific — also elevated in parasitic infections (common in India).

Serum Specific IgE (ImmunoCAP): Blood test for allergen-specific IgE antibodies. Alternative when skin testing not feasible. Quantitative results.

3. Additional Tests

Nasal Smear for Eosinophils: Eosinophils >10% in nasal secretion smear supports allergic rhinitis. Simple, inexpensive test.

CT Paranasal Sinuses: Only if complications suspected — chronic sinusitis, nasal polyps, structural abnormality. Not routine for allergic rhinitis.

Spirometry/PFT: Screen for coexisting asthma in all persistent allergic rhinitis patients. 40% have subclinical airway hyperreactivity.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Allergic rhinitis is clinically diagnosed by: ≥2 of (sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal obstruction, nasal itching) occurring for >1 hour on most days, PLUS evidence of allergic sensitization (positive SPT or specific IgE). Classify by duration (intermittent/persistent) and severity (mild/moderate-severe) per ARIA guidelines.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Intranasal Corticosteroids (First-Line for Moderate-Severe)

Fluticasone Furoate Nasal Spray: 27.5mcg/spray, 2 sprays each nostril once daily. Most effective single therapy for allergic rhinitis. Controls sneezing, rhinorrhea, congestion, and itch. Onset 12 hours, peak 2-3 days.

Mometasone Furoate Nasal Spray: 50mcg/spray, 2 sprays each nostril once daily. Similar efficacy to Fluticasone. Minimal systemic absorption. Safe for long-term use.

Budesonide Nasal Spray: 64mcg/spray. Affordable option available in India. Effective for seasonal and perennial rhinitis.

2. Oral Antihistamines (First-Line for Mild)

Cetirizine: 10mg once daily. Second-generation, minimally sedating. Effective for sneezing, rhinorrhea, itch. OTC availability. India's most prescribed antihistamine.

Levocetirizine: 5mg once daily. Active enantiomer of Cetirizine — less sedating, similar efficacy at half the dose.

Fexofenadine: 120-180mg once daily. Truly non-sedating. Preferred for patients requiring alertness (students, drivers). Does not cross blood-brain barrier.

Loratadine: 10mg once daily. Non-sedating. Available OTC. Safe in pregnancy (Category B).

3. Decongestants (Short-Term Only)

Oxymetazoline Nasal Spray: 2-3 sprays per nostril, maximum 3-5 days. Rapid congestion relief. STRICT 5-day limit to prevent rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion).

Pseudoephedrine (Oral): 60mg every 6 hours. Oral decongestant for nasal congestion. Avoid in hypertension, cardiac disease, and glaucoma.

4. Other Therapies

Montelukast: 10mg once daily at bedtime. Leukotriene receptor antagonist. Add-on for rhinitis with asthma. Modest efficacy as monotherapy.

Nasal Saline Irrigation: Normal saline nasal wash using Neti pot or squeeze bottle. Mechanical clearance of allergens and mucus. Safe, drug-free, effective adjunct. Traditional Indian practice.

Allergen Immunotherapy (AIT): Subcutaneous (SCIT) or sublingual (SLIT) immunotherapy. Only disease-modifying treatment. 3-5 year course. Available at specialized allergy centres in India. Indicated for moderate-severe cases not responding to pharmacotherapy.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Cetirizine Tablets IP 10mg: First-line antihistamine. Pack of 10.

Levocetirizine Tablets IP 5mg: Less sedating antihistamine. Pack of 10.

Loratadine Tablets IP 10mg: Non-sedating antihistamine. Pack of 10.

Montelukast Tablets 10mg: Leukotriene antagonist for rhinitis + asthma. Pack of 10.

Fluticasone Nasal Spray: Intranasal corticosteroid. Available at select PMBJP stores.

Normal Saline Nasal Drops: For nasal irrigation and congestion relief.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Cetirizine 10mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Levocetirizine 5mg, 10's: ₹5.50

Loratadine 10mg, 10's: ₹8.00

Montelukast 10mg, 10's: ₹18.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Haridra Khanda (Turmeric Preparation): Classical formulation specifically indicated for Pratishyaya (rhinitis) and allergic conditions. Curcumin has published anti-allergic and anti-inflammatory activity. 3-5g twice daily with warm milk.

Trikatu Churna (Pippali + Maricha + Shunthi): Classical Kaphahara formulation. Clears nasal congestion, improves digestion of Ama. 1-3g with honey twice daily before meals. Enhances bioavailability of other herbs.

Sitopaladi Churna: Classical formulation for Pratishyaya and Kasa. Reduces nasal congestion and post-nasal drip. 3g with honey thrice daily.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum): Natural antihistamine and mast-cell stabilizer properties. Tulsi leaves chewed or as decoction. 5-7 leaves daily. Published evidence for anti-allergic activity.

Vyoshadi Vatakam: Classical Ayurvedic lozenge for throat/nasal congestion. Contains Trikatu, Chitraka, and jaggery. Dissolve in mouth 2-3 times daily.

Anu Taila (Nasal Oil): Classical Pratimarsha Nasya (nasal oil application). 2-3 drops of medicated sesame oil in each nostril daily. Lubricates nasal mucosa, reduces inflammation, and prevents allergen adhesion. Traditional preventive practice.

Dashamoola Kashaya: Ten-root decoction with anti-inflammatory properties. Used for Vata-Kapha conditions including chronic rhinitis. 15-30ml twice daily.

Dabur Honitus Lozenges: Herbal throat lozenges with Tulsi and Mulethi for post-nasal drip throat irritation.

Himalaya Bresol Tablets: Proprietary anti-allergic with Haridra, Tulsi, Vasaka. 1 tablet twice daily. Widely available OTC.

Jala Neti (Nasal Saline Wash — Yogic Practice): Traditional yogic nasal irrigation using lukewarm saline in a Neti pot. Physically removes allergens, clears mucus. Published RCT evidence for improving rhinitis symptoms. Daily practice recommended.
diseases.india_specific:
Rising Prevalence with Urbanization: Allergic rhinitis affects 20-30% of Indian urban population. Delhi NCR, Bengaluru, Mumbai show highest prevalence. Air pollution (PM2.5, PM10) and diesel exhaust particles worsen allergic sensitization.

Seasonal & Perennial Patterns: Seasonal rhinitis peaks during pollen seasons — September-November (Parthenium/Congress grass, Prosopis) and February-April. Perennial rhinitis from house dust mites (Dermatophagoides), cockroach allergens, and mold is year-round.

Indoor Allergens — Indian Homes: House dust mites thrive in Indian climate (humidity). Old mattresses, carpets, stuffed toys, and curtains are reservoirs. Cockroach allergens are a major trigger in urban Indian homes.

Asthma-Rhinitis Link (United Airway): 40% of allergic rhinitis patients develop asthma. Treating rhinitis improves asthma control. ARIA guidelines recommend evaluating all rhinitis patients for asthma and vice versa.

Under-diagnosis & OTC Self-medication: Most patients self-treat with over-the-counter antihistamines and nasal decongestant sprays. Prolonged decongestant spray use (>5 days) causes rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion).
diseases.important_notes:
Intranasal Steroids Are Safe for Long-Term Use: Patients and parents often fear 'steroid' nasal sprays. Educate that intranasal corticosteroids have minimal systemic absorption, are NOT the same as oral/injectable steroids, and are the most effective treatment. They are safe for daily long-term use including in children.

STOP Decongestant Spray Abuse: Oxymetazoline/Xylometazoline nasal sprays provide instant relief but MUST NOT be used beyond 5 days. Prolonged use causes rhinitis medicamentosa (severe rebound congestion worse than original). This is a massive problem in India due to OTC availability.

Ayurvedic Nasya & Jala Neti — Genuinely Effective: Anu Taila Nasya and Jala Neti (saline nasal wash) are traditional Indian practices with genuine scientific evidence for allergic rhinitis management. They can be practiced daily as preventive measures alongside pharmacotherapy.

Allergen Avoidance Is Foundation: Encase mattresses/pillows in dust-mite-proof covers, wash bedding weekly in hot water, remove carpets, control cockroaches, use exhaust fans to reduce humidity. During high-pollen days, keep windows closed.

Screen for Asthma: 40% of allergic rhinitis patients have or will develop asthma. All patients with persistent rhinitis should have spirometry. Treating rhinitis improves asthma control. United airway — one disease, two manifestations.
confirmation_tests_curated: ARIA Classification: Intermittent (<4 days/week or <4 weeks) vs Persistent (>4 days/week AND >4 weeks). Mild (normal sleep, no impairment) vs Moderate-Severe (impaired sleep/daily activities). Guides treatment step.
Anterior Rhinoscopy: Pale, boggy, bluish-gray nasal mucosa with clear secretions (vs red mucosa in infectious rhinitis). Inferior turbinate hypertrophy. Nasal polyps in chronic cases.

Skin Prick Testing (SPT): Gold standard for identifying specific allergens. Panel includes house dust mites, pollen, mold, cockroach, pet dander. Results in 15-20 minutes. Available at allergy clinics.

diseases.name: Chickenpox
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Usually Sufficient)

Clinical Recognition: Chickenpox is primarily a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS. The characteristic rash (vesicles in different stages, centripetal distribution, 'crops') is pathognomonic. Laboratory confirmation not usually needed for typical cases.

2. Laboratory Confirmation (When Needed)

Tzanck Smear: Scrape base of fresh vesicle, stain with Giemsa/Wright. Shows multinucleated giant cells. Quick bedside test. Confirms herpesvirus but cannot distinguish VZV from HSV.

VZV PCR (Vesicle Fluid/Scab): Gold standard for VZV confirmation. PCR of vesicle fluid or crusted scab. Highly sensitive and specific. Available at reference/tertiary labs.

VZV IgM Antibody (ELISA): Positive IgM indicates acute VZV infection. Detectable from day 4-5 of illness. Useful for retrospective confirmation.

VZV IgG Antibody: Indicates past infection or vaccination (immunity). Useful for pre-vaccination screening in adults unsure of chickenpox history.

Direct Fluorescent Antibody (DFA): Vesicle fluid tested with fluorescent-labeled VZV antibodies. Rapid specific diagnosis. Limited availability in India.

3. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: May show leukopenia initially, then normal or mild leukocytosis. Thrombocytopenia in severe cases.

Liver Function Tests: Mild transaminitis common. Significant elevation suggests varicella hepatitis (rare but serious).

Chest X-ray: Only if respiratory symptoms present. Varicella pneumonia shows bilateral nodular/interstitial infiltrates.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Typical chickenpox: Generalized vesicular rash in successive crops with all stages (macule, papule, vesicle, crust) present simultaneously, centripetal distribution, associated fever and itch. In immunocompromised patients, atypical presentations require laboratory confirmation.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antiviral Therapy

Acyclovir (Oral): 800mg five times daily for 5-7 days (adults/adolescents >40kg). 20mg/kg/dose four times daily for 5 days (children). Start within 24 hours of rash for maximum benefit. Recommended for: adults, adolescents, immunocompromised, chronic skin/lung disease patients. NOT routinely recommended for healthy children with mild disease.

Valacyclovir (Oral): 1000mg three times daily for 5-7 days (adults). Better bioavailability than Acyclovir, more convenient dosing. Alternative to Acyclovir.

IV Acyclovir: 10mg/kg every 8 hours for 7-10 days. For severe/disseminated varicella, varicella pneumonia, encephalitis, immunocompromised patients, and neonatal varicella. Hospital/ICU setting.

2. Symptomatic Treatment

Calamine Lotion: Topical application on lesions for itch relief and drying of vesicles. Apply 3-4 times daily. Safe, widely available, first-line for itch.

Cetirizine/Hydroxyzine (Antihistamines): Cetirizine 10mg daily (non-sedating, daytime) or Hydroxyzine 25mg at bedtime (sedating, helps sleep). For itch control.

Paracetamol: For fever and pain. Do NOT use Aspirin (Reye's Syndrome risk in children) or Ibuprofen (increased risk of necrotizing fasciitis with VZV).

Mupirocin Ointment (2%): Topical antibiotic for secondary bacterial infection of lesions. Apply to infected lesions twice daily.

3. Prevention

Varicella Vaccine (Live Attenuated): 2 doses — first at 12-15 months, second at 4-6 years. 98% effective after 2 doses. IAP recommended. Not yet in India's UIP. Cost: ₹1,500-2,500/dose (private).

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis: Varicella vaccine within 3-5 days of exposure (for unvaccinated contacts). Varicella-Zoster Immunoglobulin (VZIG) for high-risk contacts (immunocompromised, pregnant, neonates).

Isolation: Isolate patient until ALL lesions have crusted (5-7 days from rash onset). Airborne + contact precautions in hospital.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Acyclovir Tablets IP 800mg: Antiviral for chickenpox. Pack of 10 (marketed as 35 tablets for full course).

Cetirizine Tablets IP 10mg: Antihistamine for itch. Pack of 10.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: Antipyretic/analgesic. Pack of 10.

Calamine Lotion: Topical anti-itch and drying agent. Bottle.

Mupirocin Ointment 2%: Topical antibiotic for secondary infection.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Acyclovir 800mg, 10's: ₹55.00

Cetirizine 10mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Paracetamol 500mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Calamine Lotion 100ml: ₹25.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Neem (Azadirachta indica — Nimba): Traditional first-line Ayurvedic remedy for chickenpox. Neem leaves placed on bed/around patient. Neem leaf paste applied on lesions for anti-itch and antimicrobial effect. Neem water bath after crusting. Deeply embedded in Indian cultural practice for chickenpox.

Neem Leaf Bath: Boil handful of neem leaves in bathing water. Bathe with lukewarm neem water once lesions begin crusting. Antimicrobial and soothing. Most widely practiced traditional chickenpox remedy across India.

Sandalwood Paste (Chandana): Cooling, anti-itch application on lesions. Mix sandalwood powder with rose water to make paste. Apply on crusted lesions to reduce scarring and cool the skin (Pittashamaka).

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia): Classical Raktashodhaka (blood purifier). Used for skin diseases including viral exanthems. Helps reduce post-chickenpox scarring. 3-5g powder twice daily.

Sariva/Anantamoola (Hemidesmus indicus): Classical Raktaprasadaka and cooling herb. Used for skin conditions and fever. 15-30ml decoction twice daily.

Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia): Immunomodulatory Rasayana. Supports immune clearance of virus and reduces fever. 500mg-1g twice daily.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum): Antiviral, antipyretic, immunomodulatory. Tulsi tea 2-3 cups daily during illness.

Yashtimadhu / Mulethi (Glycyrrhiza glabra): Anti-inflammatory and skin-healing. Used as gargle for oral vesicles. 3-5g powder with honey.

Kumkumadi Taila: Classical Ayurvedic facial oil for post-chickenpox scar management. Apply after complete healing (all crusts fallen). Reduces hyperpigmentation and pock-mark scarring.

Dietary Recommendations (Pathya): Light, easily digestible, cooling diet — rice kanji (congee), moong dal khichdi, coconut water, pomegranate juice. Avoid spicy, oily, fried food (Pitta-aggravating). This dietary advice is genuinely helpful.
diseases.india_specific:
Highly Contagious in Indian Settings: Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV) spreads via airborne droplets and direct vesicle contact. Attack rate >90% in susceptible household contacts. Crowded Indian households and schools facilitate rapid transmission.

Vaccine Not in UIP: Unlike many developed countries, Varicella vaccine is NOT part of India's Universal Immunization Programme. It's available privately (₹1,500-2,500/dose). This means most Indian children get natural chickenpox. IAP recommends 2 doses.

Adult Chickenpox — More Severe: Adults who escaped childhood chickenpox face significantly worse disease — higher fever, more lesions, higher complication rate (pneumonia, hepatitis, encephalitis). Military recruits and migrants from rural to urban areas are at risk.

Neonatal Varicella: If mother develops chickenpox 5 days before to 2 days after delivery, neonate is at risk of severe disseminated varicella (mortality 30%). Requires Varicella-Zoster Immunoglobulin (VZIG) and IV Acyclovir.

Secondary Bacterial Infections in Tropical Climate: India's hot, humid climate promotes bacterial superinfection of vesicles. Impetigo from scratching is extremely common, especially in children with poor hygiene access.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Do NOT Use Aspirin or NSAIDs: Aspirin in children with chickenpox causes Reye's Syndrome (fatal liver-brain disease). Ibuprofen increases risk of severe skin/soft tissue infection (necrotizing fasciitis). Use ONLY Paracetamol for fever and pain.

Neem Is Genuinely Beneficial: Unlike many conditions, neem applications for chickenpox have genuine traditional merit — antimicrobial properties reduce secondary skin infection, the most common complication. Neem baths, neem leaves on bedding, and neem paste on lesions are safe and effective adjuncts.

Prevent Scratching — Prevent Scars: Trim children's fingernails short, consider cotton mittens at night. Secondary bacterial infection from scratching causes permanent pock-mark scars. Calamine lotion + antihistamines are the key combination.

Isolation Is Essential: Patient is contagious from 1-2 days BEFORE rash until ALL lesions crust (5-7 days). Keep away from: pregnant women (risk of congenital varicella), neonates, immunocompromised individuals. These groups face life-threatening complications.

Vaccination — Recommend for All Children: Although not in India's UIP, varicella vaccine is highly effective and recommended by IAP. Two doses provide 98% protection. Cost ₹1,500-2,500/dose at private clinics. Especially important for adolescents/adults who haven't had chickenpox.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Recognition: Chickenpox is primarily a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS. The characteristic rash (vesicles in different stages, centripetal distribution, 'crops') is pathognomonic. Laboratory confirmation not usually needed for typical cases.
Tzanck Smear: Scrape base of fresh vesicle, stain with Giemsa/Wright. Shows multinucleated giant cells. Quick bedside test. Confirms herpesvirus but cannot distinguish VZV from HSV.
VZV PCR (Vesicle Fluid/Scab): Gold standard for VZV confirmation. PCR of vesicle fluid or crusted scab. Highly sensitive and specific. Available at reference/tertiary labs.

diseases.name: Cholera
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Treat Immediately — Don't Wait for Lab)

Clinical Case Definition: Any patient >5 years with acute watery diarrhea with severe dehydration, OR any patient >2 years with acute watery diarrhea in an area with confirmed cholera outbreak. In endemic India, any profuse rice-water stool = treat as cholera pending confirmation.

2. Microbiological Confirmation

Stool Culture on TCBS Agar: Gold standard. Yellow colonies of V. cholerae on Thiosulfate-Citrate-Bile Salts-Sucrose agar. Takes 18-24 hours. Collect rectal swab or fresh stool in Cary-Blair transport medium.

Crystal VC Rapid Dipstick Test: Point-of-care immunochromatographic test detecting V. cholerae O1 and O139 antigens in stool. Results in 15 minutes. Sensitivity 90-95%. Ideal for field/outbreak use. Available at district labs.

Dark-Field Microscopy: Fresh stool examined under dark-field shows characteristic 'shooting star' motility of V. cholerae. Rapid presumptive test at microscopy centers.

3. Serotyping & Antibiotic Sensitivity

Serotyping (O1/O139): V. cholerae O1 (Classical/El Tor biotypes, Ogawa/Inaba serotypes) and O139 cause epidemic cholera. Non-O1/O139 cause sporadic diarrhea. Important for epidemiological tracking.

Antibiotic Sensitivity Testing: Test against Doxycycline, Azithromycin, Ciprofloxacin, Erythromycin, Tetracycline. Resistance patterns guide treatment in outbreaks.

4. Routine Blood Tests

Serum Electrolytes: Hypokalemia, hyponatremia, metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate). Critical for guiding IV fluid composition.

Blood Urea & Creatinine: Elevated in severe dehydration (pre-renal AKI). Monitor renal recovery with rehydration.

Blood Glucose: Hypoglycemia common in children with severe cholera. Treat with IV dextrose.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Confirmed Cholera: V. cholerae O1 or O139 isolated from stool culture. Suspected Cholera: Clinical case meeting definition in endemic/epidemic area. In outbreaks, first 5-10 cases should be culture-confirmed; subsequent cases treated on clinical grounds.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Rehydration Therapy (CORNERSTONE — Start Immediately)

WHO Plan C — Severe Dehydration (IV): Ringer's Lactate IV — 100 ml/kg over 3 hours. First 30 ml/kg in 30 minutes (adults) or 1 hour (infants), then 70 ml/kg in 2.5 hours (adults) or 5 hours (infants). If RL unavailable, use Normal Saline. May need 10-20 liters in first 24 hours for severe cholera.

WHO Plan B — Some Dehydration (ORS): 75 ml/kg ORS over 4 hours at health facility. Reassess and reclassify after 4 hours.

WHO Plan A — No Dehydration (Home ORS): ORS after every loose stool: <2 yrs 50-100ml, 2-10 yrs 100-200ml, >10 yrs as much as wanted.

2. Antibiotic Therapy (Reduces Duration & Volume)

Doxycycline: 300mg single dose (adults). First-line. Reduces stool volume by 50% and duration by 1-2 days. Not for children <8 years or pregnant women.

Azithromycin: 1g single dose (adults), 20mg/kg single dose (children). Alternative to Doxycycline. Preferred for children and pregnant women.

Ciprofloxacin: 1g single dose. Alternative. Increasing resistance in some regions.

Erythromycin: 250mg four times daily for 3 days (adults). Alternative for children when Azithromycin unavailable.

3. Supportive Care

Zinc Supplementation: 20mg/day for 10-14 days (children >6 months), 10mg/day (<6 months). Reduces diarrhea duration and recurrence. WHO/UNICEF recommended.

Continue Feeding/Breastfeeding: Do NOT stop feeding during cholera. Continue breastfeeding. Resume normal diet as soon as vomiting stops. Malnutrition worsens outcome.

4. Prevention

Oral Cholera Vaccine (OCV): Shanchol (Shantha Biotechnics/India) — 2 doses 14 days apart. WHO-prequalified. Used in outbreak response. Provides 65% protection for 5 years. Being used in endemic hotspots in India.

WASH Interventions: Safe drinking water (boiling/chlorination), hand washing with soap, safe sanitation, proper food hygiene. The ONLY long-term solution.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Kutaj / Kurchi (Holarrhena antidysenterica): Classical anti-diarrheal herb. Kutajghan Vati (Kutaj bark extract tablets) 2 tablets thrice daily. Named 'antidysenterica' for its traditional anti-diarrheal reputation. Supportive only — NOT a replacement for ORS/IV fluids.

Bilva / Bael (Aegle marmelos): Unripe fruit pulp is Grahi (astringent/anti-diarrheal). Bilva sherbet or Bilva Avaleha traditionally used for diarrheal diseases.

Musta / Nagarmotha (Cyperus rotundus): Classical Atisaraghna and Grahi. Used in Mustakadi Kwatha for diarrhea management. 3-5g powder twice daily.

Coconut Water (Narikela Jala): Natural electrolyte-rich fluid. Excellent supportive rehydration alongside ORS. 200-300ml every 2-4 hours.

Buttermilk/Takra with Rock Salt & Cumin: Traditional Indian rehydration drink. Provides fluids, electrolytes, and probiotics. Grahi and Deepana.

Gangadhar Churna: Classical formulation for Atisara (diarrhea). Contains Musta, Lodhra, Ativisha, Shunthi. 3-5g with buttermilk twice daily.

Jeeraka Water (Cumin Water): Cumin boiled in water. Digestive, carminative. Provides mineral supplementation.

Dabur Kutajghan Vati: Branded classical preparation. Most popular Ayurvedic anti-diarrheal. 2 tablets thrice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Endemic in India: India remains a major cholera-endemic country. Outbreaks occur annually during monsoon (June-September) and post-monsoon in states with poor water/sanitation — West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, UP, Kerala. Vibrio cholerae O1 El Tor biotype predominates.

Monsoon Flooding & Water Contamination: Flooding mixes sewage with drinking water sources — the classic trigger for cholera outbreaks. Tube wells and open wells become contaminated. Coastal saline intrusion creates Vibrio-friendly environments.

Open Defecation & Poor Sanitation: Despite Swachh Bharat Mission progress, open defecation persists in many rural areas. Fecal contamination of water sources directly causes cholera transmission. Community-level WASH interventions are the ultimate solution.

Under-reporting & Stigma: India officially reports far fewer cholera cases than likely occur due to diagnostic limitations at primary level, political sensitivity around outbreak declaration, and confusion with 'acute watery diarrhea' reporting.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — IV Fluids Immediately: Severe cholera kills within hours without IV rehydration. A patient losing rice-water stools needs Ringer's Lactate IV IMMEDIATELY — potentially 10-20 liters in 24 hours. ORS alone is NOT sufficient for severe cholera. Every minute of delay increases mortality.

ORS Does NOT Stop Diarrhea — It Saves Lives: ORS replaces lost fluid and electrolytes while the body fights infection. Diarrhea will continue for 1-3 days even with treatment. Parents/patients must understand: continuing diarrhea on ORS is NORMAL — the ORS is working by preventing dehydration.

Ayurvedic Herbs Are Supportive Only: Kutajghan Vati and Bilva are traditional anti-diarrheal herbs with some activity, but they CANNOT replace ORS for fluid replacement or antibiotics for reducing Vibrio load. Use as adjuncts during recovery phase, not during acute severe disease.

Outbreak Response — Report Immediately: Any cluster of acute watery diarrhea cases should be reported to the District Health Officer and IDSP (Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme) immediately. Early outbreak detection enables rapid response — OCV deployment, water chlorination, and case management setup.

Boil Water — Simplest Prevention: During monsoon and in endemic areas, BOIL all drinking water. Chlorination (Halogen tablets/bleaching powder) for community water sources. Handwashing with soap before eating and after defecation. These simple measures prevent cholera.

diseases.name: Copd
diseases.tests:
1. Spirometry (ESSENTIAL for Diagnosis)

Post-Bronchodilator Spirometry: GOLD standard for COPD diagnosis. FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 AFTER bronchodilator (Salbutamol 400mcg) confirms persistent airflow limitation. Must be performed in all suspected cases. Available at district hospital level.

GOLD Severity Classification: Based on post-BD FEV1: GOLD 1 (Mild) ≥80% predicted, GOLD 2 (Moderate) 50-79%, GOLD 3 (Severe) 30-49%, GOLD 4 (Very Severe) <30%. Combined with symptoms (CAT/mMRC) and exacerbation history for ABCD assessment.

2. Assessment Tools

mMRC Dyspnea Scale: Grade 0-4 patient-reported breathlessness scale. Grade ≥2 = more symptoms. Simple, takes 30 seconds. Guides treatment decisions.

CAT Score (COPD Assessment Test): 8-question patient questionnaire (score 0-40). CAT ≥10 = significant symptom burden. Monitors treatment response over time.

3. Imaging

Chest X-ray: Hyperinflation (flattened diaphragm, increased retrosternal space, >6 anterior ribs visible), bullae, tubular heart shadow. Also excludes other diagnoses (TB, lung cancer, pneumonia).

CT Chest (HRCT): Demonstrates emphysema pattern, bronchial wall thickening, bronchiectasis. Useful for phenotyping and excluding other diagnoses. Not routine for all COPD.

4. Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Polycythemia (raised Hb/Hct) from chronic hypoxemia. Eosinophils >300 suggest overlap with asthma (ACO).

Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Level: Screen in early-onset COPD (<45 years), non-smokers, or strong family history. Genetic cause of emphysema.

Arterial Blood Gas (ABG): For suspected respiratory failure (SpO2 <92%). Type 2 respiratory failure (raised PaCO2 + low PaO2) in advanced COPD. Guides oxygen therapy.

5. Additional Tests

Sputum Culture: During exacerbations with purulent sputum. Common organisms: H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, M. catarrhalis, Pseudomonas (in severe COPD).

ECG & Echocardiography: For suspected cor pulmonale (right heart failure). P pulmonale, right axis deviation on ECG.

6. Key Diagnostic Criteria (GOLD 2024)

COPD is diagnosed when: Dyspnea + chronic cough + sputum production + risk factor exposure (smoking/biomass/occupational) AND post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 on spirometry. Spirometry is MANDATORY for diagnosis — clinical features alone are insufficient.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Bronchodilators (Cornerstone of COPD Treatment)

Salbutamol Inhaler (SABA): 100mcg/puff, 1-2 puffs as needed for acute breathlessness. Rescue inhaler. Not for regular use as sole therapy.

Ipratropium Bromide Inhaler (SAMA): 20mcg/puff, 2 puffs four times daily. Short-acting anticholinergic. Can combine with SABA for additive effect.

Tiotropium (LAMA): 18mcg once daily via dry powder inhaler. Long-acting anticholinergic — cornerstone of COPD maintenance therapy. Reduces exacerbations by 20-30%. First-line for Group B-D COPD.

Formoterol / Salmeterol (LABA): Formoterol 12mcg twice daily or Salmeterol 50mcg twice daily. Long-acting bronchodilator. Often combined with ICS or LAMA.

2. Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS — Selective Use Only)

Budesonide/Formoterol or Fluticasone/Salmeterol (ICS+LABA): For patients with frequent exacerbations (≥2/year) AND eosinophils >300. ICS in COPD is NOT universal (unlike asthma). Risk of pneumonia with ICS in COPD.

3. Exacerbation Management

Systemic Corticosteroids: Prednisolone 40mg daily for 5 days during exacerbations. Shortens recovery and reduces treatment failure.

Antibiotics for Exacerbations: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg thrice daily for 5-7 days, or Azithromycin 500mg daily for 3 days, or Doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 5 days. Indicated when sputum becomes purulent.

Oxygen Therapy: Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD (NOT >94% — risk of CO2 retention). Controlled oxygen via Venturi mask 24-28%. Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) ≥15 hrs/day if PaO2 <55 mmHg.

Nebulization: Salbutamol 2.5mg + Ipratropium 0.5mg nebulized every 4-6 hours during exacerbations.

4. Non-Pharmacological (Equally Important)

Smoking/Biomass Cessation: MOST IMPORTANT intervention. Only intervention proven to slow FEV1 decline. Nicotine replacement therapy (patches/gum), Varenicline, Bupropion for smoking cessation.

Pulmonary Rehabilitation: Supervised exercise training + education program. Improves exercise tolerance, reduces dyspnea, decreases hospitalizations. Underutilized in India.

Vaccination: Annual Influenza vaccine + Pneumococcal vaccine (PCV13 + PPSV23). Reduces exacerbation-related hospitalizations.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vasa / Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica): Classical Shwasahara and bronchodilatory. Vasicine is the precursor of Bromhexine (modern mucolytic). Vasa Avaleha 10g twice daily. Reduces cough, clears sputum.

Kantakari (Solanum surattense): Part of Dashamoola. Potent Kasahara and Shwasahara. Used in chronic respiratory conditions. 3-5g powder twice daily or as decoction.

Agastya Haritaki Rasayana: Classical formulation for chronic Shwasa (respiratory disease). Contains Haritaki, Dashamoola, Bharangi, Pushkaramoola. 10g twice daily. Specific for COPD-like conditions in Ayurveda.

Kanakasava: Classical Asava for Tamaka Shwasa (bronchial asthma/COPD). Contains Dhattura, Pippali, Bharangi. 15-30ml twice daily. Practitioner supervision essential.

Pippali Rasayana (Vardhamana Pippali): Graduated dosing protocol with Long Pepper. Classical for chronic Shwasa and Kasa. Enhances respiratory function over time.

Chyawanprash: Daily Rasayana for lung protection and immune support. 10-20g daily, especially during winter.

Haridra / Turmeric (Curcuma longa): Anti-inflammatory properties relevant to chronic airway inflammation. Haldi doodh daily.

Pranayama (Breathing Exercises): Anulom-Vilom (alternate nostril breathing), Bhramari, and Pursed-lip breathing improve lung function, reduce dyspnea, and improve exercise tolerance. Published evidence supports pranayama as adjunct in COPD rehabilitation.

Dabur Vasavaleha: Branded Vasa preparation for chronic cough. 10g twice daily.

Himalaya Bresol: Proprietary respiratory support tablets. 1 tablet twice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Massive Undiagnosed Burden in India: India has an estimated 55-65 million COPD patients — second largest globally after China. Prevalence 4-10% in adults >30 years. Vastly under-diagnosed — most patients present late with advanced disease.

Biomass Fuel Smoke — India's #1 COPD Cause: Unlike the West (smoking-dominant), 50-70% of Indian COPD cases are attributed to biomass fuel (chulha) smoke exposure. Affects rural women disproportionately — they spend 4-6 hours daily near chulha. PM2.5 exposure from biomass exceeds 1000 μg/m³ (vs WHO limit 25 μg/m³).

Tobacco — Bidi & Cigarette: India has 267 million tobacco users. Bidi smoking (unfiltered, higher tar) is more COPD-genic than cigarettes. Hookah/chillum use in rural areas adds further risk. Passive smoking in enclosed homes.

Post-TB Lung Disease: Pulmonary TB causes permanent lung damage (fibrosis, bronchiectasis, cavitation). Post-TB airflow obstruction mimics/coexists with COPD. With India's massive TB burden, this is a significant COPD contributor.

Outdoor Air Pollution: India's urban air pollution (PM2.5 regularly 5-10x WHO limits) is an independent COPD risk factor. Crop stubble burning (Punjab/Haryana) creates seasonal spikes affecting millions.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Quit Smoking/Biomass Exposure: The ONLY intervention proven to slow COPD progression is stopping the causative exposure. For smokers: quit completely (Nicotine replacement available). For rural women: transition from chulha to LPG (Ujjwala Yojana). No medicine — allopathic or Ayurvedic — can compensate for continued smoke exposure.

Inhalers Are Essential — Overcome Stigma: COPD patients need daily inhaler therapy (Tiotropium/Formoterol) for life. Same inhaler stigma as asthma applies. Educate that inhalers deliver medicine directly to lungs — more effective and safer than oral tablets like Deriphyllin.

Oxygen — Too Much Is Dangerous in COPD: Unlike other conditions, COPD patients on supplemental oxygen should target SpO2 88-92%, NOT >94%. High-flow oxygen can suppress respiratory drive (CO2 narcosis) and cause respiratory arrest. This is critical for rural health workers managing oxygen therapy.

Pulmonary Rehabilitation Works: Supervised exercise + breathing techniques + education significantly improve quality of life. Pranayama and Yoga are accessible Indian forms of pulmonary rehabilitation with published evidence.

Exacerbations — Act Early: Increased breathlessness, increased sputum volume/purulence, or increased cough = exacerbation. Start Prednisolone + Antibiotics early. Delayed treatment leads to hospitalization and accelerated lung function decline. Each exacerbation permanently worsens COPD.
confirmation_tests_curated: Post-Bronchodilator Spirometry: GOLD standard for COPD diagnosis. FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 AFTER bronchodilator (Salbutamol 400mcg) confirms persistent airflow limitation. Must be performed in all suspected cases. Available at district hospital level.

GOLD Severity Classification: Based on post-BD FEV1: GOLD 1 (Mild) ≥80% predicted, GOLD 2 (Moderate) 50-79%, GOLD 3 (Severe) 30-49%, GOLD 4 (Very Severe) <30%. Combined with symptoms (CAT/mMRC) and exacerbation history for ABCD assessment.
mMRC Dyspnea Scale: Grade 0-4 patient-reported breathlessness scale. Grade ≥2 = more symptoms. Simple, takes 30 seconds. Guides treatment decisions.

diseases.name: Diarrhea
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment (IMNCI Framework — Primary Level)

WHO/IMNCI Dehydration Assessment: Ask about duration, frequency, blood in stool, vomiting. Look/Feel: general condition, sunken eyes, skin pinch, ability to drink. Classify: No dehydration → Plan A. Some dehydration → Plan B. Severe → Plan C. This assessment requires NO lab tests.

Classify Type of Diarrhea: Acute watery diarrhea (<14 days, no blood), Dysentery (blood in stool), Persistent diarrhea (>14 days). Each has different treatment approach.

2. Stool Tests (When Indicated)

Stool Routine & Microscopy: WBCs, RBCs, parasites (Entamoeba, Giardia), ova. Indicated for: bloody diarrhea, persistent diarrhea (>14 days), suspected parasitic infection.

Stool Culture & Sensitivity: For bloody diarrhea (Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter), severe disease, or outbreak investigation. Takes 48-72 hours.

Stool for Rotavirus Antigen: Rapid lateral-flow test. Positive in Rotavirus diarrhea (most common cause in children). Confirms viral etiology — antibiotics not needed.

Hanging Drop Preparation: For suspected cholera — shows motile Vibrio organisms. Rapid bedside test.

3. Blood Tests (Complicated Cases Only)

Serum Electrolytes: For severe dehydration, persistent vomiting, IV fluid-requiring patients. Guides replacement therapy.

Complete Blood Count: Leukocytosis suggests bacterial infection. Eosinophilia suggests parasitic cause.

Blood Culture: For suspected enteric fever or bacteremia with systemic toxicity.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Acute Diarrhea: ≥3 loose/watery stools in 24 hours, duration <14 days. Dysentery: Diarrhea with visible blood in stool. Persistent Diarrhea: >14 days duration. Chronic Diarrhea: >4 weeks (requires extensive workup for malabsorption, IBD, celiac disease, etc.).
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Rehydration Therapy (CORNERSTONE)

ORS (WHO Low-Osmolarity Formula): First-line for all diarrhea. Dose by dehydration severity: Plan A (home), Plan B (health facility ORS over 4 hours), Plan C (IV fluids). ORS does NOT stop diarrhea — it replaces lost fluids to prevent death.

Zinc Supplementation: 20mg/day for children >6 months (10mg <6 months) for 10-14 days. Reduces diarrhea duration by 25% and recurrence for 2-3 months. WHO/UNICEF/IMNCI recommended. MUST be given alongside ORS.

IV Ringer's Lactate: For severe dehydration or inability to take oral fluids. 100ml/kg per WHO Plan C protocol.

2. Antibiotics (ONLY for Specific Indications)

Ciprofloxacin: 500mg twice daily × 3 days (adults), 15mg/kg twice daily (children). For bloody diarrhea (suspected Shigella). First-line empirical for dysentery in India.

Azithromycin: 500mg daily × 3 days (adults). Alternative for dysentery, especially in children. Also for traveler's diarrhea.

Metronidazole: 400mg thrice daily × 5 days for Amoebic dysentery (Entamoeba histolytica) and Giardia. 35-50mg/kg/day in children.

DO NOT prescribe antibiotics for acute watery diarrhea — most are viral. ORS + Zinc only.

3. Anti-Motility Agents

Loperamide: 4mg initial then 2mg after each loose stool (max 16mg/day adults). For watery diarrhea in adults ONLY. CONTRAINDICATED in children, bloody diarrhea, and fever. Stops motility without treating cause.

4. Prevention

Rotavirus Vaccine (Rotavac/Rotasiil): Part of India's UIP. 3 oral doses at 6, 10, and 14 weeks. Prevents 40% of severe childhood diarrhea.

WASH: Safe drinking water (boil/chlorinate), handwashing with soap, safe sanitation, food hygiene. Breastfeeding protects infants.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
ORS Powder (WHO Formula): MOST CRITICAL product. Sachets.

Zinc Dispersible Tablets 20mg: WHO-recommended alongside ORS. Pack of 10.

Metronidazole Tablets IP 400mg: For amoebic/giardia diarrhea. Pack of 10.

Ciprofloxacin Tablets IP 500mg: For bloody diarrhea/dysentery. Pack of 10.

Norfloxacin Tablets IP 400mg: Alternative for bacterial diarrhea. Pack of 10.

Ondansetron Tablets IP 4mg: Antiemetic for vomiting preventing ORS. Pack of 10.
