diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Kutaj / Kurchi (Holarrhena antidysenterica): Classical Atisaraghna (anti-diarrheal). The most important Ayurvedic herb for diarrhea. Kutajghan Vati 2 tablets thrice daily. Published evidence for anti-diarrheal activity.

Bilva / Bael (Aegle marmelos): Unripe fruit is Grahi (astringent). Bilva sherbet traditional remedy. Bilva Avaleha for chronic diarrhea.

Musta (Cyperus rotundus): Atisaraghna and Deepana. Used in Mustakadi Churna and Mustakadi Kwatha. 3-5g twice daily.

Dadima / Pomegranate (Punica granatum): Fruit rind decoction is astringent. Pomegranate juice is nutritive and soothing.

Coconut Water: Natural electrolyte replacement. 200-300ml every 2-4 hours.

Buttermilk/Takra: Traditional rehydration. With rock salt and cumin. Probiotic effect.

Gangadhar Churna: Classical for Atisara. Contains Musta, Lodhra, Ativisha. 3-5g with buttermilk.

Rice Water / Kanji: Boiled rice water with salt — traditional home ORS in India. Provides glucose and electrolytes.

Himalaya Diarex: Proprietary anti-diarrheal with Kutaj and Bilva. 2 tablets twice daily.

Dabur Kutajghan Vati: Branded classical preparation. 2 tablets thrice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Second Biggest Child Killer in India: Diarrheal diseases kill approximately 1 lakh children under 5 years annually in India. Most deaths are from dehydration — preventable with ORS + Zinc. Rotavirus is the leading cause in children.

Monsoon & Waterborne Outbreaks: 60-70% of diarrheal episodes occur during monsoon (June-September) due to water contamination, flooding, and poor food hygiene. States with poor WASH infrastructure bear the highest burden.

Rotavirus — Now Vaccine-Preventable: Rotavirus causes 40% of severe childhood diarrhea in India. Rotavac (Bharat Biotech) — Indian-made, WHO-prequalified vaccine — now part of India's UIP. Being rolled out across all states.

Antibiotic Misuse: Antibiotics are prescribed for >60% of acute diarrhea cases in India despite most being viral (requiring only ORS + Zinc). This drives antibiotic resistance without clinical benefit.

Persistent Diarrhea & Malnutrition Cycle: Diarrhea → malabsorption → malnutrition → impaired immunity → more diarrhea. This vicious cycle is a major contributor to childhood stunting in India.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — ORS + Zinc Saves Lives: This is the single most important message for diarrheal disease. ORS replaces lost fluids; Zinc reduces duration and recurrence. Together they prevent 93% of diarrheal deaths in children. Every ASHA worker, parent, and health worker must know this.

DO NOT Stop Feeding: A dangerous myth in India is that food should be withheld during diarrhea. Continue breastfeeding, continue age-appropriate diet. Starving a child with diarrhea worsens malnutrition and delays recovery.

NO Antibiotics for Watery Diarrhea: Most acute diarrhea is viral (Rotavirus, Norovirus). Antibiotics are useless and harmful. Reserve antibiotics ONLY for: bloody diarrhea (dysentery), cholera, and confirmed parasitic infections. India's massive antibiotic overuse for diarrhea drives AMR.

Loperamide — NEVER in Children: Loperamide (Imodium) is CONTRAINDICATED in children. It stops gut motility, trapping bacteria and toxins inside, potentially causing toxic megacolon. Adults may use cautiously for non-bloody, non-febrile watery diarrhea only.

Rotavirus Vaccine + WASH = Prevention: Rotavac vaccine (India's UIP) prevents the most common cause of severe childhood diarrhea. Clean water, handwashing, and sanitation prevent the rest. Invest in prevention over treatment.
confirmation_tests_curated: WHO/IMNCI Dehydration Assessment: Ask about duration, frequency, blood in stool, vomiting. Look/Feel: general condition, sunken eyes, skin pinch, ability to drink. Classify: No dehydration → Plan A. Some dehydration → Plan B. Severe → Plan C. This assessment requires NO lab tests.
Classify Type of Diarrhea: Acute watery diarrhea (<14 days, no blood), Dysentery (blood in stool), Persistent diarrhea (>14 days). Each has different treatment approach.
Stool Routine & Microscopy: WBCs, RBCs, parasites (Entamoeba, Giardia), ova. Indicated for: bloody diarrhea, persistent diarrhea (>14 days), suspected parasitic infection.

diseases.name: Epilepsy
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment (Foundation of Diagnosis)

Detailed Seizure History: Eyewitness account is MOST IMPORTANT. Ask: what happened before (aura?), during (movements, consciousness, duration), and after (confusion, sleepiness, weakness). Video recording by family is invaluable.

Neurological Examination: May be normal between seizures. Look for focal neurological deficits suggesting structural brain lesion.

2. Electroencephalography (EEG)

Standard EEG (Scalp): Records brain electrical activity. Interictal epileptiform discharges (spikes, sharp waves) support epilepsy diagnosis. Normal EEG does NOT rule out epilepsy (30-50% have normal first EEG). Sleep-deprived EEG increases yield.

Video-EEG Monitoring: Simultaneous video and EEG recording to capture actual seizure. Gold standard for seizure classification and differentiating epileptic from non-epileptic events. Available at tertiary epilepsy centres.

3. Neuroimaging

MRI Brain (With Epilepsy Protocol): First-line imaging for all new-onset epilepsy. Detects structural causes: mesial temporal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, tumors, vascular malformations, neurocysticercosis. Must use epilepsy-specific MRI protocol with thin coronal cuts through hippocampi.

CT Brain: If MRI unavailable or in emergency (status epilepticus, trauma). Detects calcified neurocysticercosis, tumors, hemorrhage. Less sensitive than MRI for subtle lesions.

4. Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count, LFT, KFT, Electrolytes: Baseline before starting AEDs (many require monitoring). Hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, and hypocalcemia can cause seizures.

AED Drug Levels: Therapeutic drug monitoring for Phenytoin (10-20 mcg/ml), Carbamazepine (4-12), Valproate (50-100), Phenobarbital (15-40). Guides dosing and compliance assessment.

Neurocysticercosis Serology: ELISA for Cysticercus antibodies. Positive in neurocysticercosis — the most common preventable cause of epilepsy in India.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Epilepsy is diagnosed when: ≥2 unprovoked seizures occurring >24 hours apart, OR 1 unprovoked seizure with high recurrence risk (structural lesion, EEG abnormality), OR diagnosis of an epilepsy syndrome. A single provoked seizure (from fever, hypoglycemia, drugs) is NOT epilepsy.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Anti-Epileptic Drugs (AEDs)

Sodium Valproate: 500-2000mg/day in 2 divided doses. Broad-spectrum — effective for both focal and generalized seizures. First-line for GTCS and generalized epilepsies. AVOID in women of childbearing age (teratogenic). Monitor LFT, CBC.

Carbamazepine: 400-1600mg/day in 2-3 doses. First-line for focal/partial seizures. Also for trigeminal neuralgia. Side effects: rash, hyponatremia, diplopia. Monitor CBC, Na+.

Levetiracetam: 500-3000mg/day in 2 doses. Broad-spectrum, well tolerated, minimal interactions. Increasingly preferred first-line due to safety profile. Main side effect: irritability/behavioral changes.

Phenytoin: 200-400mg/day in 1-2 doses. Effective for focal and GTCS. Narrow therapeutic index — monitor drug levels. Long-term: gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, osteomalacia. Cheap and widely available in India.

Lamotrigine: 100-400mg/day. Broad-spectrum. Preferred for women of childbearing age (safer in pregnancy than Valproate). Slow titration needed (risk of Stevens-Johnson Syndrome).

2. Second-Line & Add-On AEDs

Clobazam: 10-40mg/day. Benzodiazepine add-on for refractory seizures. Useful in Lennox-Gastaut syndrome.

Topiramate: 100-400mg/day. Add-on for focal and generalized seizures. Also causes weight loss (useful in obese patients).

3. Emergency Treatment (Status Epilepticus)

IV Lorazepam: 0.1mg/kg IV (max 4mg) over 2 minutes. First-line for status epilepticus. Repeat once after 5 minutes if seizure continues.

IV Phenytoin/Fosphenytoin: Loading dose 20mg/kg IV at ≤50 mg/min. Second-line if benzodiazepine fails. Monitor ECG during infusion.

IV Levetiracetam: Loading dose 40-60mg/kg IV. Alternative second-line. Fewer cardiac side effects than Phenytoin.

IV Midazolam / Thiopentone: For refractory status epilepticus. ICU setting with ventilator support.

4. Specific Treatment

Albendazole + Dexamethasone: For neurocysticercosis — Albendazole 15mg/kg/day for 28 days + Dexamethasone (to prevent inflammation from dying cysts). Treats the most common preventable cause of epilepsy in India.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi): Classical Medhya Rasayana with anticonvulsant activity. Published research demonstrates GABAergic mechanism. Used in Apasmara (Ayurvedic term for epilepsy). 250-500mg root powder twice daily.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri): Premier Medhya (nootropic) herb. Neuroprotective and anticonvulsant in animal studies. Improves cognition impaired by seizures and AEDs. 300-500mg standardized extract daily.

Shankhpushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis): Classical Medhya Rasayana. Anticonvulsant activity demonstrated in research. Used traditionally for Apasmara and Unmada. 3-5g powder twice daily.

Vacha (Acorus calamus): Classical herb for Apasmara. Neuroprotective and anticonvulsant. Contains beta-asarone (use regulated form). 250-500mg powder twice daily. Practitioner supervision essential.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera): Adaptogenic, neuroprotective, anxiolytic. Supports overall neurological health. May reduce seizure frequency as adjunct. 300-600mg extract twice daily.

Smriti Sagar Ras: Classical herbo-mineral formulation for Apasmara and neurological conditions. Contains Suvarna Bhasma, Mauktik Bhasma. Strict practitioner supervision required.

Saraswatarishta: Classical Asava for neurological and psychiatric conditions. Contains Brahmi, Shatavari, Ashwagandha, Haritaki. 15-30ml with equal water twice daily.

Panchagavya Ghrita: Classical medicated ghee for Apasmara. Contains cow's milk, curd, ghee, urine, and dung products. Traditional formulation. Practitioner supervision required.

Himalaya Mentat Tablets: Proprietary formulation with Brahmi, Ashwagandha, Jatamansi for cognitive and neurological support. 1-2 tablets twice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
AETIOLOGY IN INDIA

Neurocysticercosis (NCC) — most common preventable cause of epilepsy in India

Tuberculous meningitis — post-meningitic epilepsy

Perinatal hypoxic-ischaemic injury — birth asphyxia, common in home deliveries

Head trauma — road traffic accidents (high incidence in India)

Febrile seizures in children — risk factor for temporal lobe epilepsy later

Genetic epilepsies — JME, childhood absence, Dravet syndrome

Mesial temporal sclerosis — post-infectious or febrile seizure origin

TREATMENT GAP

Treatment gap in India 50–90% rural areas — millions with epilepsy never receive AED

National Programme for Treatment of Epilepsy (NPTE) under NMHP — scale-up ongoing

DRUG AVAILABILITY

Phenobarbitone most widely used in rural India due to cost — high efficacy but sedation, tolerance

Phenytoin widely available but highly variable absorption and narrow therapeutic index

Newer AEDs (Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine) available at Jan Aushadhi — improving access

SOCIAL

Driving restriction — legally required in India (no driving for 2 years post-last seizure)

Marriage difficulties — disclosure stigma in arranged marriage context

Occupational restrictions — teachers, pilots, bus drivers, machine operators cannot work near machinery

WOMEN AND EPILEPSY

Enzyme-inducing AEDs (CBZ, Phenytoin, Phenobarb) reduce oral contraceptive efficacy — unintended pregnancy risk

Valproate teratogenicity — spina bifida, cognitive developmental delay (avoid in reproductive-age women)

Seizure frequency changes in pregnancy — close monitoring required

Folic acid 5 mg/day mandatory in all epilepsy women of childbearing age on AEDs
diseases.important_notes:
NEVER STOP AEDs ABRUPTLY: Sudden discontinuation of antiepileptic drugs precipitates status epilepticus — a life-threatening emergency. All patients must be counselled never to stop medication without physician guidance, even if seizure-free. Non-adherence is the most common cause of breakthrough seizures in India.

CARBAMAZEPINE + HLA-B*1502 TESTING: South Asian and Han Chinese populations have high prevalence of HLA-B*1502 allele — associated with 10x higher risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN) with carbamazepine. Test before prescribing carbamazepine in South Asian patients whenever possible. If positive — use alternative (Oxcarbazepine has lower SJS risk but cross-sensitivity possible; Levetiracetam preferred).

VALPROATE IN WOMEN — MUST COUNSEL: Sodium Valproate causes neural tube defects and neurodevelopmental harm in children born to women taking it. ANY woman of childbearing age with epilepsy on Valproate must be counselled about this risk and offered alternative AEDs (Lamotrigine, Levetiracetam) before pregnancy planning. If Valproate cannot be replaced (JME), mandatory folic acid 5 mg/day and high-resolution anomaly scan.

NEUROCYSTICERCOSIS AND EPILEPSY: In India, a single ring-enhancing lesion on MRI/CT brain with new-onset focal epilepsy in a young adult = neurocysticercosis (NCC) until proven otherwise. Diagnostic Cysticercus ELISA + neuroimaging. Treatment: Albendazole 15 mg/kg/day for 8 days + Prednisolone + AED. Lesion resolution confirms NCC. Do not use antihelmintics without steroids — inflammatory flare can worsen seizures.

diseases.name: Filariasis
diseases.tests:
1. Parasitological Tests (Definitive Diagnosis)

Nocturnal Blood Smear (Thick & Thin): Collect blood between 10 PM-2 AM (microfilariae exhibit nocturnal periodicity in India). Giemsa-stained thick smear — see microfilariae of W. bancrofti or B. malayi. Gold standard but requires night collection.

Immunochromatographic Card Test (ICT/FTS): Rapid finger-prick test detecting W. bancrofti circulating filarial antigen (CFA). Can be done ANYTIME (day or night). Results in 10 minutes. Sensitivity >96%. Used in MDA surveillance.

2. Serological Tests

Brugia Rapid Test: Rapid test for Brugia malayi antibodies. Used in B. malayi endemic areas (Kerala, parts of NE India).

IgG4 Antibody ELISA: Elevated IgG4 against filarial antigens. Used in research and surveillance.

3. Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Marked eosinophilia (>10% or >3000/μL) suggests TPE or active filarial infection. Microfilaremia may be present.

Serum IgE: Markedly elevated (often >1000 IU/mL) in tropical pulmonary eosinophilia.

4. Imaging

Scrotal Ultrasound ('Filarial Dance Sign'): Ultrasound of hydrocele may show live adult worms moving in dilated lymphatics — characteristic 'filarial dance' pattern. Diagnostic for active W. bancrofti infection.

Lymphoscintigraphy: Nuclear medicine test showing lymphatic drainage pattern. Demonstrates lymphatic obstruction. Available at tertiary centres.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Filariasis is diagnosed by: Microfilariae in nocturnal blood smear (definitive), OR positive ICT/FTS card test (programmatic), OR clinical features (lymphedema, hydrocele, chyluria) in endemic area + eosinophilia. Endemic area residence is key clinical context for diagnosis.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Anti-Filarial Treatment

DEC (Diethylcarbamazine): 6mg/kg/day for 12 days. Kills microfilariae and some adult worms. Standard treatment for active lymphatic filariasis. Also used in MDA as single dose (6mg/kg) + Albendazole.

IDA Triple-Drug Therapy (MDA): Single annual dose of Ivermectin (200μg/kg) + DEC (6mg/kg) + Albendazole (400mg). India is scaling up IDA for MDA — clears 99% of microfilariae with single dose. Target: filariasis elimination by 2027.

Albendazole: 400mg single dose with DEC in MDA programme. Anti-helminthic with partial activity against adult filarial worms.

Doxycycline: 200mg/day for 4-6 weeks. Targets Wolbachia endosymbiont bacteria in filarial worms. Leads to adult worm death. Used for individual treatment, not MDA.

2. Morbidity Management (MMDP)

Lymphedema Care Package: Daily washing of affected limb with soap and clean water, careful drying, elevation, exercise (lymphatic drainage), antifungal cream for intertrigo, comfortable footwear. This simple regimen PREVENTS progression and reduces ADLA episodes by 50%.

Hydrocelectomy: Surgical excision of hydrocele. Simple day-care surgery. NFEP provides free hydrocelectomy camps. Transforms quality of life for millions of affected men.

Antibiotics for ADLA: Amoxicillin/Flucloxacillin for acute bacterial superinfection episodes. Prophylactic Penicillin V in patients with frequent ADLA episodes.

3. Prevention

Annual MDA Compliance: Take the MDA tablets distributed by health workers during annual campaigns. Single dose once a year prevents filariasis. Encourage community participation.

Vector Control: Use insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, reduce Culex breeding (stagnant dirty water in drains, cesspools).
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Albendazole Tablets IP 400mg: Anti-helminthic for MDA and treatment. Pack of 1.

DEC Tablets IP 100mg: Primary anti-filarial drug. Available through NFEP free distribution during MDA.

Amoxicillin Capsules IP 500mg: For ADLA secondary infections. Pack of 10.

Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: For pain and inflammation during ADLA episodes. Pack of 10.

Antifungal Cream (Clotrimazole 1%): For intertrigo in lymphedematous limbs.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Albendazole 400mg: ₹3.00

Amoxicillin 500mg, 10's: ₹12.00

Clotrimazole Cream 1% 15g: ₹8.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Punarnava (Boerhaavia diffusa): Classical Shothaghna (anti-edema) herb. Specific for Shotha (swelling) in Ayurveda. Diuretic and anti-inflammatory. May support lymphatic drainage. 3-5g powder twice daily or Punarnavadi Kwatha.

Kanchanara Guggulu: Classical formulation for Gandamala (lymph node/lymphatic swelling). Contains Kanchanara bark and Guggulu. Used for lymphatic disorders. 2 tablets twice daily.

Dashamoola Kashaya: Ten-root decoction with anti-inflammatory and Vata-Kapha pacifying properties. Used for chronic swelling and pain. 15-30ml twice daily.

Varunadi Kashaya: Classical decoction for urinary and lymphatic conditions. Contains Varuna, Punarnava, and other herbs. May help with chyluria.

Eranda Taila (Castor Oil): External application for lymphedematous limbs. Gentle massage (avoiding infected areas) improves circulation. Traditional practice.

Triphala Kashaya (External Wash): Triphala decoction used for washing lymphedematous limbs. Antimicrobial and astringent properties help prevent secondary skin infections.
diseases.india_specific:
India — Largest Global Burden: India accounts for ~40% of global filariasis cases. Endemic in 256 districts across 20 states/UTs. Bihar, UP, Jharkhand, Odisha, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and AP are highest-burden states. Wuchereria bancrofti (transmitted by Culex mosquitoes) accounts for 99.4% of Indian cases.

Mass Drug Administration (MDA): India's National Filaria Elimination Programme conducts annual MDA with DEC + Albendazole (now adding Ivermectin — IDA triple-drug therapy) in endemic districts. Target: elimination by 2027.

Occupational Risk: Outdoor workers (farmers, fishermen, night-shift workers) in endemic areas have highest exposure to Culex mosquito bites (which bite at night).

Morbidity Management Gap: While MDA prevents new infections, approximately 2.3 crore people already living with lymphedema/hydrocele need lifelong morbidity management and disability support (MMDP).
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — MDA Compliance Saves Communities: Annual Mass Drug Administration is the ONLY way to eliminate filariasis from endemic areas. Every eligible person (excluding children <2, pregnant women, severely ill) MUST take the free DEC + Albendazole tablets distributed during MDA campaigns. Side effects are minor and transient.

Limb Care Is Lifelong: For patients with lymphedema/elephantiasis, daily limb washing, drying, elevation, and exercise can PREVENT worsening and reduce painful ADLA episodes by 50%. This simple regimen must be practiced EVERY DAY for life. No medicine reverses established elephantiasis.

Hydrocelectomy — Simple Surgery, Life-Changing: Free hydrocelectomy camps are conducted under NFEP. A 30-minute surgery restores normal anatomy and dignity. Encourage all eligible men to undergo surgery — it transforms quality of life.

Ayurvedic Support for Swelling: Punarnava and Kanchanara Guggulu have traditional use for lymphatic swelling and may support fluid drainage, but CANNOT replace anti-filarial drugs (DEC/Doxycycline) for killing the parasite or daily limb care for managing lymphedema.

Vector Control at Home: Culex mosquitoes breed in dirty stagnant water — blocked drains, cesspools, and open sewage. Clean drains weekly, use bed nets, and support community sanitation drives to prevent filariasis transmission.
confirmation_tests_curated: Nocturnal Blood Smear (Thick & Thin): Collect blood between 10 PM-2 AM (microfilariae exhibit nocturnal periodicity in India). Giemsa-stained thick smear — see microfilariae of W. bancrofti or B. malayi. Gold standard but requires night collection.

Immunochromatographic Card Test (ICT/FTS): Rapid finger-prick test detecting W. bancrofti circulating filarial antigen (CFA). Can be done ANYTIME (day or night). Results in 10 minutes. Sensitivity >96%. Used in MDA surveillance.
Brugia Rapid Test: Rapid test for Brugia malayi antibodies. Used in B. malayi endemic areas (Kerala, parts of NE India).

diseases.name: Giardiasis
diseases.tests:
1. Stool Examination (Primary Diagnostic)

Stool Microscopy (Ova & Cyst): Examine fresh stool for Giardia cysts (oval, 8-12μm, 4 nuclei) or trophozoites (pear-shaped, 'falling leaf' motility in fresh sample). Sensitivity of single sample only 50-70% — examine 3 samples on alternate days.

Stool Giardia Antigen Test (ELISA/Rapid): Detects Giardia-specific antigen in stool. Sensitivity 85-98%, specificity >95%. Better than microscopy. Available as rapid kit. Recommended first-line where available.

2. Additional Tests

Duodenal Aspirate/Biopsy (String Test): Direct examination of duodenal fluid for trophozoites. Used when stool tests are negative but clinical suspicion high. 'Enterotest' (string capsule) is a simple alternative.

Stool PCR: Molecular detection of Giardia DNA. Most sensitive test. Research/reference lab tool. Not routine in India.

3. Supportive Tests

Stool for Fat Globules: Sudan staining shows excess fat globules — confirms malabsorption/steatorrhea.

D-Xylose Absorption Test: Measures small intestinal absorption capacity. Low in chronic giardiasis with mucosal damage.

Complete Blood Count: May show mild eosinophilia. Usually normal (Giardia does NOT typically cause significant eosinophilia unlike helminths).

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Giardiasis diagnosed by: Giardia cysts/trophozoites on stool microscopy (3 samples), OR positive stool Giardia antigen test. In endemic settings with compatible symptoms (chronic bloating, foul diarrhea, malabsorption), empirical treatment with Metronidazole may be appropriate without waiting for lab confirmation.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Treatment

Metronidazole: 400mg thrice daily for 5-7 days (adults), 15mg/kg/day in 3 doses (children). Cure rate 80-95%. Most widely used in India. Side effects: metallic taste, nausea. Avoid alcohol during and 48 hours after treatment.

Tinidazole: 2g single oral dose (adults), 50mg/kg single dose (children, max 2g). Cure rate >90%. Better tolerated and more convenient than Metronidazole. Preferred where available.

2. Second-Line / Resistant Cases

Nitazoxanide: 500mg twice daily for 3 days (adults), 100-200mg twice daily for 3 days (children 1-11 years). Effective against Metronidazole-resistant Giardia. Also active against other intestinal parasites.

Albendazole: 400mg daily for 5 days. Alternative, especially when co-treatment for helminths desired. Slightly lower cure rate for Giardia.

3. Supportive Care

Oral Rehydration Salts: For dehydration from diarrhea. Standard ORS protocol.

Zinc Supplementation: For children with diarrhea. WHO-recommended 10-14 day course.

Lactose-Free Diet (Temporary): Chronic giardiasis causes secondary lactose intolerance. Avoid milk/dairy for 2-4 weeks after treatment until gut mucosa recovers.

Iron + Folic Acid / Multivitamins: For malabsorption-related nutritional deficiencies.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Metronidazole Tablets IP 400mg: First-line for giardiasis. Pack of 10.

Albendazole Tablets IP 400mg: Alternative + deworming. Pack of 1.

ORS Powder: For diarrhea-related dehydration. Sachets.

Zinc Dispersible Tablets 20mg: For children. Pack of 10.

Iron + Folic Acid Tablets: For malabsorption-related anemia.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Metronidazole 400mg, 10's: ₹5.00

Albendazole 400mg: ₹3.00

ORS sachet: ₹3.50
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vidanga (Embelia ribes): Classical Krimighna (anti-helminthic/anti-parasitic) herb. Widely used in Ayurveda for intestinal parasites. 3-5g powder with honey on empty stomach.

Kutaj (Holarrhena antidysenterica): Anti-diarrheal and Krimighna. Kutajghan Vati for diarrhea from parasitic infections. 2 tablets thrice daily.

Pippali (Piper longum): Deepana, Pachana, and Krimighna. Improves digestion and has anti-parasitic properties. Part of Trikatu formulation.

Haritaki (Terminalia chebula): Part of Triphala. Krimighna and digestive. 3-5g powder at bedtime with warm water.

Hingu / Hing (Ferula asafoetida): Carminative, reduces bloating and flatulence. Traditional Indian kitchen remedy. Small pinch with food or in buttermilk.

Ajwain (Trachyspermum ammi): Carminative and anti-parasitic. Ajwain water (boiled seeds) for bloating. Traditional Indian digestive remedy.

Dabur Kutajghan Vati: Branded anti-diarrheal. 2 tablets thrice daily.

Himalaya Gasex: Proprietary carminative for bloating and flatulence. 2 tablets after meals.
diseases.india_specific:
Waterborne Transmission & Sanitation: Giardia lamblia cysts survive in water for months. Contaminated drinking water is the primary transmission route in India. Cysts resist standard chlorination — boiling or filtration needed.

Childhood Prevalence: Giardia infection rates of 10-30% in Indian children, especially in slums, orphanages, and tribal areas. Contributes significantly to childhood malnutrition and stunting.

Daycare & Institutional Spread: Person-to-person fecal-oral transmission in daycare centres, hostels, and residential institutions. Outbreaks common.

Asymptomatic Carriage: Many infected individuals are asymptomatic carriers, shedding cysts and maintaining community transmission.
diseases.important_notes:
Complete Full Antibiotic Course: Incomplete Metronidazole course leads to resistant Giardia and recurrence. Complete full 5-7 day course even if symptoms improve after 2-3 days.

Test & Treat Family Members: Giardia spreads person-to-person in households. If one member is positive, screen and treat entire family to break reinfection cycle.

Boil Drinking Water: Giardia cysts resist standard chlorination. BOILING is the only reliable household water treatment. Filtration with <1μm filters also effective.

Address Malnutrition: Chronic giardiasis in children causes significant malnutrition and growth retardation. After treatment, provide high-calorie, high-protein diet and multivitamin supplementation for catch-up growth.

Ayurvedic Anti-Parasitics Are Supportive: Vidanga and Kutaj have traditional anti-parasitic use, but their efficacy against Giardia is not established at the level of Metronidazole/Tinidazole. Use as digestive support alongside standard treatment.
confirmation_tests_curated: Stool Microscopy (Ova & Cyst): Examine fresh stool for Giardia cysts (oval, 8-12μm, 4 nuclei) or trophozoites (pear-shaped, 'falling leaf' motility in fresh sample). Sensitivity of single sample only 50-70% — examine 3 samples on alternate days.

Stool Giardia Antigen Test (ELISA/Rapid): Detects Giardia-specific antigen in stool. Sensitivity 85-98%, specificity >95%. Better than microscopy. Available as rapid kit. Recommended first-line where available.

Duodenal Aspirate/Biopsy (String Test): Direct examination of duodenal fluid for trophozoites. Used when stool tests are negative but clinical suspicion high. 'Enterotest' (string capsule) is a simple alternative.

diseases.name: Heart Failure
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment & Classification

NYHA Functional Classification: Class I (no limitation), II (slight limitation — comfortable at rest), III (marked limitation — symptoms with less than ordinary activity), IV (symptoms at rest). Guides treatment intensity.

Framingham Criteria: Clinical diagnosis requiring 2 Major criteria OR 1 Major + 2 Minor. Major: PND, neck vein distension, rales, cardiomegaly, acute pulmonary edema, S3 gallop. Minor: bilateral ankle edema, night cough, dyspnea on ordinary exertion, hepatomegaly, pleural effusion.

2. Essential Investigations

Echocardiography (2D Echo): MOST IMPORTANT test. Measures Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF): HFrEF (≤40%), HFmrEF (41-49%), HFpEF (≥50%). Also assesses valvular disease, wall motion, diastolic function, pulmonary pressures.

12-Lead ECG: Detects arrhythmias (AF, VT), LVH, prior MI (Q waves, ST changes), conduction abnormalities. Abnormal in >90% of heart failure patients.

Chest X-ray (PA): Cardiomegaly (CTR >0.5), pulmonary venous congestion (upper lobe diversion), Kerley B lines, pleural effusion, bat-wing pattern (pulmonary edema).

NT-proBNP / BNP: Natriuretic peptide blood test. BNP >100 pg/mL or NT-proBNP >300 pg/mL strongly suggests heart failure. Useful for diagnosis and monitoring treatment response. Emerging availability in Indian urban labs.

3. Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Anemia worsens heart failure. Polycythemia in chronic hypoxia.

Renal Function (Creatinine, eGFR): Cardiorenal syndrome common. Guides ACEi/ARB dosing.

Serum Electrolytes (Na+, K+): Hyponatremia (dilutional, poor prognosis). Hypo/hyperkalemia from diuretics/ACEi.

Liver Function Tests: Congestive hepatopathy — raised bilirubin, transaminases.

Thyroid Function: Hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism can cause/worsen heart failure. Treatable cause.

HbA1c: Screen for diabetes. Optimize glycemic control.

Lipid Profile: Cardiovascular risk assessment. Statin therapy if indicated.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Heart failure diagnosed by: Symptoms (dyspnea, fatigue, edema) + Signs (elevated JVP, rales, S3, peripheral edema) + Objective evidence of cardiac dysfunction (reduced LVEF on Echo, elevated BNP, or structural heart disease). Echocardiography is essential for classification (HFrEF vs HFpEF) and treatment planning.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Foundational Medications (HFrEF — LVEF ≤40%)

ACE Inhibitors (Enalapril/Ramipril): Cornerstone therapy. Enalapril 2.5-20mg twice daily, Ramipril 1.25-10mg daily. Reduces mortality by 20-25%. Titrate to maximum tolerated dose. Monitor K+ and creatinine.

Beta-Blockers (Carvedilol/Metoprolol/Bisoprolol): Start low, go slow after stabilization. Carvedilol 3.125-25mg twice daily. Reduces mortality by 30-35%. Do NOT start during acute decompensation.

Spironolactone/Eplerenone (MRA): Spironolactone 25-50mg daily. Reduces mortality. Monitor K+ (hyperkalemia risk). Gynecomastia with Spironolactone — switch to Eplerenone if occurs.

Sacubitril/Valsartan (ARNI): Replaces ACEi in stable HFrEF for superior outcomes. 24/26mg to 97/103mg twice daily. Reduces mortality and hospitalizations beyond ACEi. Increasingly available in India.

2. Diuretics (Symptom Relief)

Furosemide (Loop Diuretic): 20-80mg oral/IV daily. Relieves fluid overload (edema, breathlessness). Titrate to dry weight. Monitor electrolytes. Not a mortality-reducing drug but essential for symptom control.

Torsemide: 10-40mg daily. Better bioavailability than Furosemide. Alternative loop diuretic.

3. Additional Medications

Empagliflozin/Dapagliflozin (SGLT2i): Now recommended for ALL HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. Reduces mortality and hospitalization. Landmark trials (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced).

Digoxin: 0.125-0.25mg daily. For rate control in HF with atrial fibrillation, and symptom improvement in HFrEF. Narrow therapeutic index — monitor levels.

Ivabradine: 5-7.5mg twice daily. For HFrEF with HR >70 despite maximum beta-blocker. Reduces hospitalization.

4. Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (ADHF)

IV Furosemide: 40-80mg IV bolus or infusion. Rapid diuresis for acute pulmonary edema.

IV Nitroglycerin: For acute pulmonary edema with hypertension. 5-200 μg/min infusion. Reduces preload.

Oxygen/NIV/Ventilation: For severe respiratory distress. CPAP/BiPAP for acute pulmonary edema. Intubation if respiratory failure.

Enalapril Tablets IP 5mg: ACE inhibitor cornerstone. Pack of 10.

Ramipril Capsules IP 2.5mg: ACE inhibitor. Pack of 10.

Carvedilol Tablets IP 3.125mg: Beta-blocker for heart failure. Pack of 10.

Spironolactone Tablets IP 25mg: MRA for heart failure. Pack of 10.

Digoxin Tablets IP 0.25mg: For rate control and HF symptoms. Pack of 10.
