Ivermectin 12mg, 1 tablet: ₹12.00

Cetirizine 10mg, 10's: ₹3.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Neem (Azadirachta indica): Most widely used traditional scabies remedy in India. Neem oil topical application has published evidence for scabicidal activity (azadirachtin compound). Neem paste on lesions, neem water bath. Safe, affordable, widely available.

Turmeric + Neem Paste: Traditional combination — turmeric paste mixed with neem leaf paste applied on scabies lesions. Anti-inflammatory (turmeric) + scabicidal (neem). Published evidence for efficacy in small studies.

Karanja Oil (Pongamia pinnata): Traditional parasiticide for skin. Applied topically. Published insecticidal activity.

Sulfur + Coconut Oil: Mix sulfur powder in coconut oil base for topical application. Sulfur has been used for scabies for >2000 years. Traditional formulation.

Haridra Khanda: Internal use for chronic itch and skin allergy. Anti-pruritic and anti-inflammatory.

Gandhaka Rasayana: Classical sulfur-based preparation for skin diseases. Krimighna and Kushthahara. 250-500mg twice daily with milk.

Neem Soap: Daily bathing with neem soap for prevention and adjunctive treatment.
diseases.india_specific:
Highly Prevalent in Indian Settings: Scabies affects 50-200 million people globally. In India, prevalence ranges from 5-30% in some communities, especially in overcrowded settings — slums, hostels, boarding schools, military barracks, prisons, tribal communities.

Crowding & Shared Bedding: Transmission requires prolonged skin-to-skin contact or shared bedding/clothing. Large Indian families sharing beds, overcrowded hostels, and poor ventilation facilitate rapid spread.

Post-Streptococcal Complications: In India's tropical climate, scabies-related secondary Streptococcal skin infection can lead to post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (kidney disease) and acute rheumatic fever. This makes scabies treatment a kidney/heart disease prevention strategy.

Mass Drug Administration for Scabies: WHO now recommends community-wide Mass Drug Administration of Ivermectin for scabies-endemic communities. India piloting MDA approaches in high-burden districts.

Permethrin Resistance: Emerging Permethrin resistance reported from some Indian regions, potentially complicating first-line treatment.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Treat ALL Contacts Simultaneously: The #1 reason scabies recurs is failure to treat all household contacts at the same time. Even people who aren't itching yet may be incubating scabies (4-6 week incubation). Treat EVERYONE in the household on the same day.

Neem Is Genuinely Effective for Scabies: Unlike many conditions, neem oil/paste has published evidence for scabicidal activity and is a legitimate traditional treatment. In resource-limited settings where Permethrin is unavailable, neem is a reasonable first option for mild scabies.

Post-Treatment Itch Is Normal: Itch may persist for 2-4 weeks AFTER successful treatment due to allergic reaction to dead mites. This does NOT mean treatment failed. Do NOT repeat scabicide unnecessarily — use antihistamines for post-treatment itch.

Scabies → Kidney Disease Connection: In Indian children, scabies-related secondary Streptococcal skin infection can cause Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (kidney disease). Treating scabies prevents not just skin disease but potentially kidney disease. This is an important public health argument for scabies treatment programmes.

Wash Bedding & Clothing: On the day of treatment, wash all bedding, towels, and recently worn clothing in hot water (>60°C). Items that can't be washed should be sealed in a plastic bag for 72 hours.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Recognition: Scabies is diagnosed CLINICALLY: Intense nocturnal pruritus + characteristic distribution of papulovesicular rash + burrows in web spaces/wrists + family members/contacts also itching. Lab confirmation rarely needed.

Skin Scraping with Mineral Oil / KOH: Scrape burrow with scalpel blade (#15), place on slide with mineral oil or 10% KOH, examine under microscope. Positive if mite, eggs, or scybala (fecal pellets) seen. Sensitivity only 40-50% — negative scraping does NOT rule out scabies.
Dermoscopy (Handheld Dermatoscope): Shows 'jet with contrail' sign — triangular dark structure (mite head) with trailing burrow. Quick, non-invasive, increasingly available. Sensitivity 86-97%.

diseases.name: Leptospirosis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Suspicion (Critical for Early Treatment)

Clinical Case Definition: Acute febrile illness + severe myalgia (calf tenderness) + conjunctival suffusion + history of exposure to floodwater/animal urine/contaminated environment in last 4-14 days. In monsoon season, this combination should trigger immediate treatment.

2. Serological Tests

MAT (Microscopic Agglutination Test): Gold standard. Detects serovar-specific antibodies. 4-fold rise between acute and convalescent sera confirms diagnosis. Takes 2-3 weeks. Available at reference labs.

IgM ELISA (Leptospira): Rapid serological test. Positive from day 5-7 of illness. Sensitivity 82-97%. Available at sentinel surveillance labs. Most practical diagnostic for India.

Rapid Immunochromatographic Tests: Point-of-care IgM/IgG card tests. Results in 15-20 minutes. Variable sensitivity. Useful for field/PHC-level screening.

3. Molecular & Culture Tests

PCR for Leptospira DNA: Blood PCR positive in first 7-10 days (leptospiremic phase). Highly specific. Available at reference labs.

Blood/Urine Culture: EMJH medium. Very slow growth (2-4 weeks). Research tool, not useful for clinical management.

4. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia (unlike dengue which shows leukopenia). Thrombocytopenia (milder than dengue). Anemia if hemorrhage.

Liver Function Tests: Elevated bilirubin (direct), mildly elevated transaminases (AST/ALT typically <200 — unlike hepatitis where ALT >1000). ALP elevated.

Renal Function: Elevated creatinine. Hypokalemia (distinctive — most AKI causes hyperkalemia). BUN elevated.

Coagulation (PT/APTT): Prolonged in Weil's disease. DIC in severe cases.

5. Diagnostic Criteria

Suspected Leptospirosis: Acute fever + myalgia + exposure history. Probable: Suspected + positive IgM ELISA or rapid test. Confirmed: Positive MAT (4-fold rise) or PCR. In endemic monsoon settings, start empirical Doxycycline on clinical suspicion.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antibiotic Treatment (Start Empirically on Suspicion)

Doxycycline: 100mg twice daily for 7 days (mild-moderate). First-line. Start on clinical suspicion — do NOT wait for lab confirmation. Also used as prophylaxis (200mg weekly) during flood exposure.

IV Penicillin G: 1.5 million units IV every 6 hours for 7 days. For severe/Weil's disease requiring hospitalization.

IV Ceftriaxone: 1-2g IV daily for 7 days. Alternative for severe cases. Equally effective as Penicillin G.

Azithromycin: 500mg daily for 3-5 days. Alternative for Doxycycline-intolerant or pregnant patients.

2. Supportive Care (Severe Leptospirosis / Weil's Disease)

IV Fluids: Careful fluid management — maintain renal perfusion without overloading (risk of pulmonary hemorrhage).

Dialysis: For oliguric renal failure. Usually temporary — renal function recovers in most cases within 2-4 weeks.

Blood Products: Platelet/FFP for coagulopathy with hemorrhage. Packed RBC for anemia.

Mechanical Ventilation: For pulmonary hemorrhage/ARDS. High PEEP strategy.

3. Prevention

Doxycycline Prophylaxis: 200mg weekly during flood exposure period. Recommended for flood rescue workers, military, and high-risk occupational groups.

Protective Footwear: Waterproof boots/shoes when wading through floodwater. Cover skin wounds with waterproof dressings.

Rodent Control: Reduce rat population around homes, grain stores, and fields. Proper solid waste management.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Doxycycline Capsules IP 100mg: First-line antibiotic + prophylaxis. Pack of 8.

Azithromycin Tablets IP 500mg: Alternative antibiotic. Pack of 3.

Ceftriaxone Injection 1g: For severe/Weil's disease.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For fever. Pack of 10.

ORS Powder: Hydration support.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Doxycycline 100mg, 8's: ₹10.00

Ceftriaxone 1g injection: ₹28.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia): Immunomodulatory and hepatoprotective Rasayana. May support recovery from leptospirosis-related liver involvement. 500mg-1g twice daily after acute phase.

Punarnava (Boerhaavia diffusa): Renoprotective and diuretic. Traditional support for kidney conditions. May help during renal recovery phase. 3-5g powder twice daily.

Bhumyamalaki (Phyllanthus niruri): Hepatoprotective. For jaundice management in Weil's disease recovery. 500mg-1g twice daily.

Kutki (Picrorhiza kurroa): Hepatoprotective for liver involvement. 500mg twice daily.

Arogyavardhini Vati: Classical for jaundice and liver support. 2 tablets twice daily during recovery.
diseases.india_specific:
Monsoon Flooding & Occupational Exposure: Leptospirosis peaks during and after monsoon flooding across India. Rats are the primary reservoir — contaminated floodwater enters skin via cuts/abrasions. Kerala, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Andaman & Nicobar report most cases. Mumbai floods regularly cause outbreaks.

Agricultural & Occupational Risk: Farmers (rice paddy workers), sewer workers, animal handlers, and military personnel are at highest risk. Barefoot wading in contaminated water is the primary exposure route.

Under-diagnosis & Misdiagnosis: Leptospirosis is frequently misdiagnosed as dengue, malaria, hepatitis, or 'viral fever' due to overlapping symptoms. During monsoon, always consider leptospirosis in the differential of any acute febrile illness.

Pulmonary Hemorrhage: Severe Pulmonary Hemorrhage Syndrome (SPHS) — massive lung bleeding — is emerging as a major cause of leptospirosis mortality, especially in Kerala and Andaman Islands. Mortality >50% with SPHS.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Start Doxycycline on Suspicion: Leptospirosis mortality increases dramatically with delayed treatment. During monsoon, any patient with fever + myalgia + exposure to floodwater should receive Doxycycline immediately. Do NOT wait for lab results. Early antibiotics can prevent progression to Weil's disease.

Differentiate from Dengue: In monsoon season, leptospirosis and dengue present similarly. Key differences: Leptospirosis has calf tenderness, conjunctival suffusion, leukocytosis. Dengue has leukopenia, rash, and thrombocytopenia. Both may coexist. If unsure, treat for both (Doxycycline + adequate fluids).

Monsoon Prophylaxis: Flood rescue workers, farmers wading in water, and sewer workers should take Doxycycline 200mg weekly as prophylaxis during monsoon. Wear waterproof boots. Cover wounds.

Ayurvedic Herbs for Recovery Only: Leptospirosis is an acute bacterial infection that can kill within days (pulmonary hemorrhage, renal failure). Guduchi, Punarnava, and hepatoprotective herbs support recovery AFTER standard antibiotic treatment, not during acute severe illness.

Report Outbreaks: Clusters of leptospirosis cases during flooding should be reported to IDSP/District Health Office for outbreak response — Doxycycline prophylaxis distribution, rat control, public health messaging.

diseases.name: Snakebite Envenomation
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment (Immediate — at Bedside)

20-Minute Whole Blood Clotting Test (WBCT20): Place 2ml venous blood in clean, dry glass test tube. Leave undisturbed for 20 minutes at room temperature. Tilt — if blood remains liquid (non-clotting) = positive = viper envenomation with coagulopathy. THE single most important bedside test. Available everywhere. Repeat every 6 hours for 24 hours.

Neurological Assessment: Check for ptosis (ask patient to look up — eyelid droops), diplopia (double vision), difficulty swallowing (ask to drink water), difficulty breathing (count respiratory rate, single-breath count test — normal >20). Document every 1-2 hours.

Local Examination: Mark swelling margin with pen and time. Measure limb circumference at fixed points hourly. Document progressive swelling, blistering, necrosis.

2. Laboratory Tests

Coagulation Profile: PT/INR, APTT, Fibrinogen, FDP/D-Dimer. Fibrinogen <100 mg/dL and elevated FDP confirm consumption coagulopathy (DIC) from viper venom.

Complete Blood Count: May show hemolysis (fragmented RBCs), thrombocytopenia.

Renal Function (Creatinine, BUN, Electrolytes): Monitor for AKI — creatinine rising, hyperkalemia, oliguria. Especially with Russell's viper.

Urine: Dark/red urine = myoglobinuria/hemoglobinuria. Dipstick positive for blood without RBCs on microscopy.

3. Species Identification

Clinical Syndromic Approach: In India where species identification is often impossible, classify by syndrome: (a) Local envenomation only (swelling without systemic signs), (b) Hemotoxic (non-clotting blood ± bleeding ± AKI), (c) Neurotoxic (ptosis → paralysis ± minimal local signs), (d) Both (Russell's viper can cause all three).

4. Key Criteria for ASV Administration

Indications for ASV (WHO/India STG): Any ONE of: (a) Positive WBCT20 (non-clotting blood), (b) Spontaneous systemic bleeding, (c) Neurotoxic signs (ptosis, paralysis), (d) AKI (oliguria, rising creatinine), (e) Rapidly progressive local swelling involving >half the bitten limb. ASV should be given ONLY for clear envenomation — NOT for all snakebites.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) — Life-Saving Treatment

Indian Polyvalent ASV: Effective against Big Four (Russell's Viper, Cobra, Krait, Saw-Scaled Viper). Reconstitute 10 vials (100ml) in 200ml Normal Saline, infuse IV over 1 hour. Repeat dose if WBCT20 remains positive after 6 hours (hemotoxic) or paralysis progresses after 1-2 hours (neurotoxic).

ASV Reaction Management: Anaphylaxis/anaphylactoid reactions occur in 10-40% of cases. Pre-medicate with IV Hydrocortisone 200mg + SC Adrenaline 0.25mg (subcutaneous, low dose). Keep Adrenaline (1:1000) ready for anaphylaxis (0.5mg IM for adults).

2. First Aid (BEFORE Hospital)

DO: Immobilize bitten limb (splint like fracture — Pressure Immobilization for neurotoxic bites), reassure, transport to hospital IMMEDIATELY, remove tight jewelry/clothing.

DO NOT: Apply tourniquet (causes gangrene), cut/suck wound, apply ice/heat, give traditional remedies, waste time with faith healers. These are harmful and delay life-saving ASV.

3. Organ-Specific Management

Respiratory Failure: Intubate and ventilate. Neostigmine test for Krait/Cobra (Neostigmine 0.05mg/kg IV with Atropine 0.6mg — if improvement = acetylcholinesterase inhibitor-responsive neurotoxicity, continue Neostigmine). Ventilate until paralysis reverses (may take 24-72 hours for Krait).

Renal Failure: Aggressive IV fluids to maintain urine output >30ml/hr. Dialysis if oliguric/anuric AKI. Alkalinize urine (IV NaHCO3) for myoglobinuria.

Coagulopathy: ASV is the ONLY treatment (FFP/platelets are NOT effective as venom continues consuming clotting factors until neutralized by ASV). Repeat WBCT20 every 6 hours.

4. Wound Management

Tetanus Prophylaxis: TT/Td for all snakebite patients. TIG if indicated.

Antibiotics: For secondary infection of necrotic wounds. Amoxicillin-Clavulanate + Metronidazole.

Surgical: Fasciotomy for compartment syndrome. Debridement for necrosis. Skin grafting if needed.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Adrenaline Injection (Epinephrine 1:1000): For ASV anaphylaxis management.

Hydrocortisone Injection 100mg: Pre-medication for ASV.

Neostigmine Injection 0.5mg: For neurotoxic envenomation.

Atropine Injection 0.6mg: With Neostigmine.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For pain.

Note: Anti-Snake Venom is manufactured by Haffkine, VINS Bioproducts, Bharat Serums, Premium Serums in India. Supplied to government hospitals through state procurement. Cost: ₹300-500 per vial (10 vials per dose = ₹3,000-5,000).
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT NOTE — No Ayurvedic/Herbal Antidote for Snakebite: There is NO proven Ayurvedic, herbal, or traditional remedy that neutralizes snake venom. Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) is the ONLY effective treatment. Going to faith healers/ojhas instead of hospital KILLS patients. However, traditional first-aid and recovery support exists:

Guduchi/Giloy: Published research on anti-venom/anti-inflammatory properties but INSUFFICIENT as standalone treatment. May support post-envenomation recovery. Not a substitute for ASV.

Andrographis paniculata (Kalmegh): Some in-vitro anti-venom activity in research. NOT clinically proven. Do NOT rely on this.

Supportive Recovery Herbs: After hospital discharge, Ashwagandha (stress recovery), Guduchi (immune support), and Punarnava (renal recovery for AKI patients) may aid rehabilitation.

Traditional Tourniquet/Incision — HARMFUL: Traditional practices of tight tourniquets, wound incision, suction, and cauterization cause more harm than the venom itself — leading to gangrene, infection, and tissue loss.
diseases.india_specific:
India — World's Snakebite Capital: India has ~58,000 snakebite deaths annually (Lancet 2020) — highest globally. Most deaths occur in rural farming communities (June-October, during monsoon/harvest when snake-human encounters peak). Bihar, UP, MP, Jharkhand, Rajasthan, and AP are highest-burden states.

Big Four Venomous Snakes: Russell's Viper (Daboia russelii), Common Cobra (Naja naja), Common Krait (Bungarus caeruleus), and Saw-Scaled Viper (Echis carinatus) cause >95% of envenomation deaths in India.

Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) Access Gap: Many PHCs and CHCs lack ASV stock, cold chain, or trained staff for ASV administration. Transport delays from rural bite sites to ASV-equipped hospitals are a major mortality factor. ASV is manufactured by 4 companies in India but distribution is uneven.

Traditional Healers & Delays: 60-70% of snakebite victims first go to traditional healers (ojhas, tantrics) for mantras, herbal treatment, and tourniquet application. Average delay to hospital: 4-8 hours. This delay kills.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — Hospital + ASV Only: Snakebite kills 58,000 Indians annually. The ONLY life-saving treatment is Anti-Snake Venom given in a hospital. Every minute spent with traditional healers instead of travelling to hospital increases death risk. ASHA workers must educate communities: go to hospital FIRST, not the ojha.

First Aid — Immobilize, Don't Tourniquet: Immobilize bitten limb like a fracture (splint), keep patient calm, transport to nearest hospital with ASV. Do NOT apply tight tourniquet (causes gangrene), do NOT cut or suck wound, do NOT apply herbs/stones.

WBCT20 — Simplest Life-Saving Test: The 20-Minute Whole Blood Clotting Test requires only a glass tube and 20 minutes. It can be done at ANY health facility. Non-clotting blood = viper envenomation = give ASV. This simple test guides treatment and saves lives.

No Ayurvedic Anti-Venom Exists: Despite centuries of traditional claims, NO herbal preparation has been proven to neutralize snake venom in humans. Published in-vitro studies do not translate to clinical efficacy. Relying on herbal treatment for snakebite is FATAL.

Protect Against Bites: Sleep on raised beds (not on floor), use mosquito nets, clear vegetation around houses, use torchlight when walking at night, wear closed shoes in fields. Most bites occur at night (Krait) or while walking barefoot in fields (Viper).
confirmation_tests_curated: 20-Minute Whole Blood Clotting Test (WBCT20): Place 2ml venous blood in clean, dry glass test tube. Leave undisturbed for 20 minutes at room temperature. Tilt — if blood remains liquid (non-clotting) = positive = viper envenomation with coagulopathy. THE single most important bedside test. Available everywhere. Repeat every 6 hours for 24 hours.

Neurological Assessment: Check for ptosis (ask patient to look up — eyelid droops), diplopia (double vision), difficulty swallowing (ask to drink water), difficulty breathing (count respiratory rate, single-breath count test — normal >20). Document every 1-2 hours.
Local Examination: Mark swelling margin with pen and time. Measure limb circumference at fixed points hourly. Document progressive swelling, blistering, necrosis.

diseases.name: Rabies
diseases.tests:
1. Post-Bite Assessment (Critical for PEP Decision)

WHO Category Classification: Category I: touching/feeding animal, lick on intact skin → NO PEP needed. Category II: nibbling on uncovered skin, minor scratch without bleeding, lick on broken skin → Vaccine only. Category III: Single/multiple transdermal bites, scratches with bleeding, licks on mucous membranes, bat exposure → Vaccine + RIG (Rabies Immunoglobulin). This classification GUIDES treatment.

Animal Observation: If biting dog can be identified and observed: observe for 10 days. If healthy after 10 days, rabies excluded. However, START PEP immediately — do not wait for observation result.

2. Diagnosis of Clinical Rabies (Once Symptoms Appear)

Clinical Diagnosis: Hydrophobia + aerophobia in a patient with history of animal bite is virtually diagnostic. Lab confirmation is post-mortem in most cases.

Nuchal Skin Biopsy: Full-thickness skin biopsy from nape of neck (hair follicle area). Direct Fluorescent Antibody (DFA) test for rabies antigen in nerve fibers. Antemortem diagnostic test.

Saliva RT-PCR: Detects rabies virus RNA in saliva. Sensitivity improves with repeated sampling.

Corneal Impression Smear: DFA testing of corneal epithelial cells. Less sensitive than skin biopsy.

3. Post-Mortem Confirmation

Brain DFA/PCR: Gold standard. Examination of brain tissue (hippocampus) for Negri bodies (intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies) and rabies antigen by DFA.

4. Key Diagnostic Criteria

RABIES IS DIAGNOSED CLINICALLY: History of animal bite + incubation period + hydrophobia/aerophobia = rabies. Lab confirmation is useful but NOT required to initiate palliative care. Once symptoms appear, rabies is 100% fatal — focus shifts to post-exposure prophylaxis of contacts and palliative care.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) — LIFE-SAVING (Before Symptoms)

Wound Washing (IMMEDIATE — Most Critical First Aid): Wash bite wound immediately with running water and soap/detergent for 15 minutes. Apply povidone-iodine. This single step reduces rabies risk by up to 90%. Must be done at the SCENE before going to hospital.

Anti-Rabies Vaccine (ARV): Intramuscular (Essen regimen): 5 doses on days 0, 3, 7, 14, 28. Intradermal (Updated Thai Red Cross regimen): 4 visits — 2 sites on days 0, 3, 7, 28. ID regimen saves 60-80% vaccine. India recommends both regimens.

Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG): For Category III bites. Infiltrate maximum dose around wound, remainder IM at site distant from vaccine. ERIG: 40 IU/kg. HRIG: 20 IU/kg. Provides immediate passive immunity until vaccine generates active immunity.

2. Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP)

For High-Risk Groups: Veterinarians, lab workers, animal handlers, wildlife workers. 3 doses on days 0, 7, 21-28 (IM or ID). If bitten after PrEP: only 2 booster doses needed (days 0, 3) — NO RIG needed.

3. Clinical Rabies — Palliative Care Only

Once symptoms appear, rabies is 100% fatal. Milwaukee Protocol (intensive care attempt) has near-zero success. Palliative care: sedation (Midazolam/Morphine), pain management, family counseling. Compassionate end-of-life care.

4. Animal Bite Prevention

Dog Population Management: Animal Birth Control (ABC) programme. Stray dog sterilization and vaccination. India's ABC programme is the largest globally.

Community Education: Do not approach/provoke stray dogs. Supervise children around dogs. Report stray dog packs to municipal authorities.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Povidone-Iodine Solution 5%: For wound cleaning. Essential first aid.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For pain at bite site.

Note: Anti-Rabies Vaccine (ARV) and Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) are provided FREE at government Anti-Rabies Clinics in district hospitals. Private clinics charge ₹300-500 per ARV dose and ₹350-8,000 per RIG vial depending on type (ERIG/HRIG).
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic/Herbal Cure for Rabies: Rabies is 100% fatal once symptoms develop. There is NO herbal, Ayurvedic, homeopathic, or traditional remedy that can treat rabies. The ONLY effective intervention is post-exposure prophylaxis (vaccine + RIG) given BEFORE symptoms appear.

Wound Washing Is the Best First Aid: Thorough washing of the bite wound with soap and running water for 15 minutes is the single most effective first-aid measure — reducing rabies risk by up to 90%. This can be done at home immediately after a bite.

Turmeric Paste on Wound: Traditional Indian practice of applying haldi paste on wounds has mild antisepial properties but is NOT a substitute for proper wound washing with soap + povidone-iodine. Apply AFTER thorough washing if desired.

Post-PEP Recovery: Ashwagandha and Guduchi may support recovery from the stress and anxiety of dog bite and PEP process, but have NO anti-rabies activity.
diseases.india_specific:
India — 36% of Global Rabies Deaths: India accounts for approximately 20,000 rabies deaths annually — the highest globally. Stray dog bites are the primary cause (>95%). Dog population: estimated 60 million stray dogs.

Dog Bite Epidemic: India reports 17+ million dog bites annually. Only 50-60% receive proper post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP). Major gaps: rural areas lack anti-rabies vaccine (ARV) and Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) stock.

Child Victims: Children aged 5-15 are most vulnerable — they are bitten more often (playing with dogs) and may not report bites to parents. Bites on face/head in children have shortest incubation (as short as 7-10 days).

Cost of RIG: Equine RIG (ERIG) costs ₹350-600 per vial. Human RIG (HRIG) costs ₹5,000-8,000 per vial. Multiple vials needed based on body weight. Cost is a major barrier to complete PEP in India.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — Rabies Is 100% Fatal Without PEP: Once clinical symptoms appear, NO treatment can save the patient. Death is certain within 7-10 days. The ONLY chance of survival is post-exposure prophylaxis (vaccine + RIG) given AFTER bite but BEFORE symptom onset.

Wash the Wound IMMEDIATELY — 15 Minutes with Soap: The single most important first aid. Washing with soap and running water for 15 minutes can reduce rabies risk by up to 90%. Do this at the scene BEFORE travelling to hospital. Apply povidone-iodine after washing.

NEVER Delay PEP for Traditional Treatment: Taking the victim to a traditional healer instead of hospital for anti-rabies vaccine is a DEATH SENTENCE. ASHA workers must educate: EVERY dog/cat/monkey bite needs immediate hospital visit for PEP assessment.

Complete All Vaccine Doses: Many patients take 1-2 ARV doses and skip the rest. INCOMPLETE vaccination may not provide full protection. Complete all 4-5 doses as scheduled.

Category III Bites Need RIG + Vaccine: Deep bites, multiple bites, face/head bites, and bites with bleeding MUST receive RIG (immunoglobulin) in addition to vaccine. Vaccine alone takes 14 days to produce antibodies — RIG provides immediate protection for this gap period.
confirmation_tests_curated: WHO Category Classification: Category I: touching/feeding animal, lick on intact skin → NO PEP needed. Category II: nibbling on uncovered skin, minor scratch without bleeding, lick on broken skin → Vaccine only. Category III: Single/multiple transdermal bites, scratches with bleeding, licks on mucous membranes, bat exposure → Vaccine + RIG (Rabies Immunoglobulin). This classification GUIDES treatment.
Animal Observation: If biting dog can be identified and observed: observe for 10 days. If healthy after 10 days, rabies excluded. However, START PEP immediately — do not wait for observation result.
2. Diagnosis of Clinical Rabies (Once Symptoms Appear)
Clinical Diagnosis: Hydrophobia + aerophobia in a patient with history of animal bite is virtually diagnostic. Lab confirmation is post-mortem in most cases.

diseases.name: Tetanus
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Tetanus Is a Clinical Diagnosis)

Clinical Recognition: Trismus + generalized rigidity + reflex spasms in patient with wound history (or neonatal cord contamination) = tetanus. NO laboratory test confirms tetanus. Diagnosis is ENTIRELY clinical. Culture of wound for C. tetani is unreliable and NOT required.

Spatula Test: Touch posterior pharyngeal wall with spatula. Normal response: gag and push spatula out. Tetanus: reflex jaw spasm — patient bites the spatula. Sensitivity 94%, Specificity 100%. Simple, bedside, definitive.

2. Severity Grading (Ablett Classification)

Grade I (Mild): Trismus, mild spasticity, no spasms, no respiratory compromise. Grade II (Moderate): Moderate trismus/rigidity, mild-moderate spasms, tachycardia. Grade III (Severe): Severe rigidity, frequent prolonged spasms, respiratory compromise, tachycardia >120. Grade IV (Very Severe): Grade III + autonomic instability.

3. Blood Tests (Supportive Only)

Complete Blood Count: Usually normal. Leukocytosis may indicate wound infection.

Serum Creatine Kinase (CK): Elevated from massive muscle spasm/rhabdomyolysis.

ABG: Respiratory acidosis from hypoventilation during spasms.

Anti-Tetanus Antibody Level: >0.1 IU/mL = protective. If antibody levels are protective, diagnosis of tetanus is unlikely. Research use only.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Neutralize Unbound Toxin

Human Tetanus Immunoglobulin (TIG): 3000-5000 IU IM (single dose). Neutralizes circulating (unbound) tetanus toxin. Given as soon as possible. DOES NOT reverse already bound toxin (hence treatment only limits progression).

Equine Anti-Tetanus Serum (ATS): 10,000-20,000 IU IV/IM if TIG unavailable. Risk of anaphylaxis — test dose first. Less preferred than TIG.

2. Control Spasms & Rigidity

IV Diazepam: First-line for spasm control. 10-40mg IV every 1-8 hours. High doses may be needed (up to 200mg/day). Monitor for respiratory depression.

IV Midazolam: Continuous infusion 0.1-0.3mg/kg/hr. For ICU-managed severe tetanus. Titratable.

IV Magnesium Sulfate: 40mg/kg loading then 1-3g/hr infusion. Controls spasms AND autonomic dysfunction. Emerging first-line in many centres.

3. Airway & Respiratory Management

Tracheostomy: Early tracheostomy recommended for Grade III-IV tetanus. Secures airway, prevents death from laryngospasm.

Mechanical Ventilation: Muscle relaxants (Vecuronium/Pancuronium) + ventilation for severe spasms unresponsive to sedation. May need ventilation for 2-4 weeks.

4. Wound Management

Wound Debridement: Clean, debride contaminated wound under anesthesia. Remove foreign bodies, necrotic tissue — the source of ongoing toxin production.

Metronidazole: 500mg IV every 8 hours for 7-10 days. Kills C. tetani at wound site. Preferred over Penicillin (which has GABA-antagonist properties that may worsen spasms).

5. Active Immunization

Tetanus Toxoid (TT/Td): Full primary vaccination course during recovery — tetanus infection does NOT provide natural immunity. Patient must be actively immunized.

6. Prevention

Tetanus Toxoid in UIP: DPT/Pentavalent at 6, 10, 14 weeks + boosters at 16-24 months and 5-6 years. TT for pregnant women (TT1+TT2 or Td). Adult boosters every 10 years.

Clean Wound Care: Proper wound cleaning with soap/water + antiseptic for all injuries. TT booster if >5 years since last dose for contaminated wounds.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Metronidazole Injection 500mg/100ml: Antibiotic for C. tetani. Available at Jan Aushadhi.

Diazepam Injection 5mg/ml: Spasm control.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For pain. Pack of 10.

Note: TIG (Human Tetanus Immunoglobulin) and ATS (Anti-Tetanus Serum) are available at government hospitals. TT vaccine provided free under UIP.
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Cure for Tetanus: Tetanus is caused by Clostridium tetani neurotoxin — one of the most potent biological toxins known. There is NO herbal or Ayurvedic remedy that neutralizes tetanus toxin. Treatment requires TIG + ICU care. However:

Ashwagandha: May support recovery from prolonged ICU stay and muscle wasting. 300-600mg twice daily during convalescence.

Bala (Sida cordifolia): Classical Balya (strength-promoting) herb for post-illness muscle recovery. Part of Bala taila for massage.

Abhyanga (Oil Massage): Gentle oil massage during recovery phase to restore muscle tone and reduce stiffness after prolonged rigidity. Bala Ashwagandhadi taila recommended.

Nutritional Recovery: Chyawanprash and high-protein diet essential during recovery from the extreme metabolic demands of tetanus spasms.
diseases.india_specific:
Tetanus Still Kills in India: India reports 500-700+ tetanus deaths annually (likely underreported). Neonatal tetanus deaths reduced but still occur from home deliveries with unhygienic cord practices. Adult tetanus from wounds, especially in agricultural workers.

Wound Contamination in Rural Settings: Soil contamination of wounds, nail pricks, thorn injuries during barefoot farming, rusty metal cuts — common in rural India where Clostridium tetani spores persist in soil.

Incomplete Vaccination: Many Indian adults never completed primary tetanus vaccination. TT boosters every 10 years are rarely taken. Elderly are most under-vaccinated group.

Neonatal Tetanus — Cord Care: Application of cow dung, ghee, ash, or other non-sterile substances to umbilical cord stump remains practiced in some communities despite education efforts.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — Tetanus Is Preventable: A ₹10 TT vaccine prevents a disease that costs ₹5-10 lakhs to treat in ICU and still kills 10-50%. Ensure COMPLETE vaccination: childhood DPT/Pentavalent + adult boosters every 10 years + TT in pregnancy.

Neonatal Tetanus — Clean Cord Care: Apply NOTHING to umbilical cord except Chlorhexidine 4% (for home births). No cow dung, ash, ghee, turmeric, or any traditional application. Maternal TT vaccination protects the newborn through transplacental antibodies.

Wound Care — Simple Prevention: Thorough washing of any wound (cuts, nail pricks, thorns) with soap and clean water + antiseptic application. TT booster if >5 years since last dose for contaminated/dirty wounds.

Tetanus Has NO Lab Test — Clinical Diagnosis: Trismus (lockjaw) + rigidity + spasms + wound history = tetanus. Do NOT wait for lab confirmation — it doesn't exist. The Spatula Test (biting reflex) is 100% specific. Start treatment immediately on clinical suspicion.

No Ayurvedic Substitute: Tetanus toxin is irreversibly bound to nerve endings. No herbal preparation can unbind it. Only TIG prevents further binding, and ICU support maintains life while toxin effect wears off (2-6 weeks). Herbal remedies are for recovery phase only.

Clinical Recognition: Trismus + generalized rigidity + reflex spasms in patient with wound history (or neonatal cord contamination) = tetanus. NO laboratory test confirms tetanus. Diagnosis is ENTIRELY clinical. Culture of wound for C. tetani is unreliable and NOT required.

Spatula Test: Touch posterior pharyngeal wall with spatula. Normal response: gag and push spatula out. Tetanus: reflex jaw spasm — patient bites the spatula. Sensitivity 94%, Specificity 100%. Simple, bedside, definitive.

Grade I (Mild): Trismus, mild spasticity, no spasms, no respiratory compromise. Grade II (Moderate): Moderate trismus/rigidity, mild-moderate spasms, tachycardia. Grade III (Severe): Severe rigidity, frequent prolonged spasms, respiratory compromise, tachycardia >120. Grade IV (Very Severe): Grade III + autonomic instability.

diseases.name: Rheumatic Fever
diseases.tests:
1. Revised Jones Criteria (2015) — Diagnosis of Acute Rheumatic Fever

Jones Criteria (Moderate-High Risk Populations like India): Initial ARF: 2 Major criteria, OR 1 Major + 2 Minor criteria, PLUS evidence of preceding GAS infection. Major criteria: Carditis (clinical/subclinical on echo), Arthritis (polyarthritis OR monoarthritis OR polyarthralgia), Chorea, Erythema marginatum, Subcutaneous nodules. Minor criteria: Polyarthralgia, Fever ≥38.5°C, ESR ≥30 or CRP ≥3, Prolonged PR interval on ECG.

2. Evidence of Preceding GAS Infection

Throat Swab Culture / Rapid Antigen Detection Test (RADT): Positive for Group A Streptococcus. Confirms recent GAS pharyngitis. RADT gives results in 10 minutes.

ASO Titer (Anti-Streptolysin O): Elevated >200 IU/mL (adults), >400 (children). Rises 1-3 weeks after infection, peaks at 3-5 weeks. Most commonly used serological evidence.

Anti-DNase B Titer: Alternative/complementary to ASO. Elevated values confirm recent GAS infection.

3. Cardiac Assessment

Echocardiography: Essential for ALL suspected ARF. Detects subclinical carditis (valvular regurgitation not audible on auscultation). Identifies chronic valvular damage. WHO recommends echo-based RHD screening.

12-Lead ECG: Prolonged PR interval (first-degree heart block) is a minor criterion. Also detects AF, LVH in chronic RHD.

Chest X-ray: Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion in heart failure. Straightening of left heart border in mitral stenosis.

4. Blood Tests

ESR and CRP: Elevated (ESR ≥30, CRP ≥3 mg/dL). Acute phase reactants confirming inflammation.

Complete Blood Count: Mild leukocytosis, anemia in chronic RHD.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

First episode ARF in India (moderate-high risk): 2 Major OR 1 Major + 2 Minor criteria + GAS evidence. Recurrent ARF: 2 Major OR 1 Major + 2 Minor OR 3 Minor + GAS evidence. Chronic RHD: Echo findings of valve damage (mitral stenosis, regurgitation) even without history of documented ARF.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Acute Rheumatic Fever Treatment

Benzathine Penicillin G (IM): 600,000 IU for <27kg, 1.2 million IU for ≥27kg — single dose. Eradicates GAS from pharynx. First-line. Alternative: Oral Penicillin V 250-500mg 2-3 times daily for 10 days, or Amoxicillin 50mg/kg/day for 10 days.

Aspirin (Anti-Inflammatory): 80-100mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for 2 weeks, then taper over 4-6 weeks. For arthritis — dramatic response within 24-48 hours. Monitor for salicylism (tinnitus, GI upset).

Prednisolone (For Carditis): 1-2mg/kg/day for 2-3 weeks, then taper over 4-6 weeks. For moderate-severe carditis. Suppresses inflammation but does NOT prevent long-term valve damage.

Heart Failure Management: Furosemide, ACE inhibitors, Digoxin as indicated for congestive heart failure from acute carditis.

2. Secondary Prophylaxis (LIFELONG for RHD)

Benzathine Penicillin G (IM): 1.2 million IU every 3-4 weeks. LIFELONG for patients with RHD (or until age 40, whichever is longer). Every 21 days preferred in India (high recurrence rate). THE most important intervention — prevents recurrent ARF attacks that worsen valve damage.

Oral Penicillin V: 250mg twice daily. If IM injection not feasible. Less reliable due to compliance issues.

3. Chronic RHD Management

Anticoagulation: Warfarin for AF with RHD (target INR 2-3). Mechanical valve replacement requires lifelong anticoagulation.

Valve Surgery: Balloon Mitral Valvotomy (BMV) for severe mitral stenosis. Valve repair/replacement for severe regurgitation. Available at cardiac surgery centres.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Benzathine Penicillin G Injection 1.2MU: Secondary prophylaxis. Available at government hospitals FREE.

Aspirin Tablets IP 325mg: Anti-inflammatory for arthritis. Pack of 10.

Prednisolone Tablets IP 5mg: For carditis. Pack of 10.

Furosemide Tablets IP 40mg: For heart failure. Pack of 10.

Enalapril Tablets IP 5mg: ACE inhibitor for heart failure. Pack of 10.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Aspirin 325mg, 10's: ₹4.00

Prednisolone 5mg, 10's: ₹3.50

Furosemide 40mg, 10's: ₹3.50
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna): Classical Hridya (cardiotonic). Published evidence for cardiac support. 500mg-1g bark powder twice daily with milk. Arjunarishta 15-30ml twice daily. Supports cardiac recovery in chronic RHD.

Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic cardioprotective. Supports recovery from inflammatory conditions. 300-600mg twice daily.

Guduchi/Giloy: Anti-inflammatory Rasayana. May help modulate autoimmune inflammatory response. 500mg-1g twice daily.

Punarnava: Diuretic and cardiotonic. Supports fluid management in heart failure. Punarnavadi Kwatha.

Dabur Arjunarishta: Branded cardiotonic preparation. 15-30ml twice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
India — Largest RHD Burden Globally: India has an estimated 10-12 million people with RHD — more than any other country. RHD causes 25-50% of all cardiac hospitalizations in India. 250,000+ die annually from RHD-related complications.

Poverty-Driven Streptococcal Infections: Overcrowded living, poor hygiene, and limited healthcare access mean Group A Streptococcal (GAS) pharyngitis often goes untreated → triggers autoimmune response → rheumatic fever. Prevention requires a 10-day course of Penicillin for every sore throat.

Missed Secondary Prophylaxis: Lifelong monthly Benzathine Penicillin injections are needed to prevent recurrent ARF attacks and progressive valve damage. But compliance is <50% in India — painful injections, healthcare access, and follow-up gaps cause dropout.

Late Surgical Intervention: Advanced RHD requires valve repair/replacement surgery. Long waiting lists, limited cardiac surgery centres in rural India, and cost barriers delay surgery until patients are severely ill.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Treat Every Sore Throat: Rheumatic fever is ENTIRELY PREVENTABLE by treating GAS pharyngitis with a complete 10-day course of Penicillin/Amoxicillin. A ₹20 antibiotic course prevents a lifelong heart disease. ASHA workers must educate: sore throat in children aged 5-15 = see doctor = complete antibiotic course.

Secondary Prophylaxis Is Lifelong: Monthly Benzathine Penicillin injections prevent recurrent ARF attacks that progressively worsen valve damage. Stopping injections = worsening RHD = heart failure = death in young adulthood. Despite pain, injections must continue as prescribed.

Ayurveda for Cardiac Support Only: Arjuna and Arjunarishta have genuine cardiotonic properties and can support chronic RHD management. They CANNOT replace Penicillin prophylaxis, Aspirin for arthritis, or surgery for severe valve disease.

Echo Screening in Schools: WHO recommends echocardiographic screening for subclinical RHD in high-prevalence regions like India. School-based screening can detect silent valve damage and initiate secondary prophylaxis before clinical ARF occurs.

RHD = Preventable Heart Disease: RHD is a disease of poverty — it occurs where children's sore throats go untreated. India's massive RHD burden is entirely preventable with improved primary healthcare access, sore throat treatment, and secondary prophylaxis compliance.
