Jones Criteria (Moderate-High Risk Populations like India): Initial ARF: 2 Major criteria, OR 1 Major + 2 Minor criteria, PLUS evidence of preceding GAS infection. Major criteria: Carditis (clinical/subclinical on echo), Arthritis (polyarthritis OR monoarthritis OR polyarthralgia), Chorea, Erythema marginatum, Subcutaneous nodules. Minor criteria: Polyarthralgia, Fever ≥38.5°C, ESR ≥30 or CRP ≥3, Prolonged PR interval on ECG.
Throat Swab Culture / Rapid Antigen Detection Test (RADT): Positive for Group A Streptococcus. Confirms recent GAS pharyngitis. RADT gives results in 10 minutes.
ASO Titer (Anti-Streptolysin O): Elevated >200 IU/mL (adults), >400 (children). Rises 1-3 weeks after infection, peaks at 3-5 weeks. Most commonly used serological evidence.

diseases.name: Goiter & Iodine Deficiency
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment

Thyroid Palpation & WHO Goiter Grading: Palpate thyroid from behind patient. Grade goiter 0/1/2. Assess for nodules, tenderness, fixity, lymphadenopathy. ASHA workers can be trained for basic goiter screening.

2. Thyroid Function Tests

Serum TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone): SINGLE BEST screening test. Elevated TSH (>4.5 mIU/L) = hypothyroidism. Suppressed TSH (<0.1) = hyperthyroidism. Neonatal TSH screening identifies congenital hypothyroidism.

Free T4 (Thyroxine): Low free T4 with elevated TSH = overt hypothyroidism. Normal free T4 with elevated TSH = subclinical hypothyroidism.

Free T3: Elevated in hyperthyroidism. Less useful for IDD diagnosis.

3. Iodine Status Assessment

Urinary Iodine Concentration (UIC): Population-level indicator. Median UIC <100 μg/L = iodine deficiency. <20 μg/L = severe deficiency. Used in NIDDCP surveillance — NOT for individual diagnosis.

4. Imaging

Thyroid Ultrasound: Measures thyroid volume (confirms goiter), identifies nodules, guides FNAC. Available at CHC/district level.

FNAC (Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology): For thyroid nodules to rule out malignancy. USG-guided FNAC at district hospital.

Thyroid Scan (Tc-99m/I-131): Nuclear medicine scan for functional assessment of nodules (hot/cold). Tertiary centre only.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Iodine Deficiency Disorders is a POPULATION-level diagnosis based on: (a) Goiter prevalence >5% in school children, (b) Median urinary iodine <100 μg/L. Individual hypothyroidism: TSH >4.5 mIU/L + low Free T4. Neonatal screening: TSH >20 mIU/L on heel-prick sample → confirm with serum thyroid function tests.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Iodine Supplementation

Iodized Salt (Universal): The PRIMARY intervention. 15-40 ppm iodine at production level (translates to 15 ppm at consumer level). ALL cooking salt must be iodized — this is law in India.

Iodized Oil (For Severely Deficient Areas): Single oral dose of iodized oil (400mg iodine) provides protection for 1 year. Used in severe endemic areas where salt iodization coverage is inadequate.

2. Thyroid Hormone Replacement (For Hypothyroidism)

Levothyroxine (T4): 1.6 μg/kg/day for adults (start low 25-50 μg in elderly/cardiac patients). Titrate to normalize TSH. Lifelong in permanent hypothyroidism. Take on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast.

Congenital Hypothyroidism: Levothyroxine 10-15 μg/kg/day started within first 2 weeks of life to prevent intellectual disability. Neonatal screening detects this. EARLY treatment = NORMAL development.

3. Goiter-Specific Treatment

Small Goiter: Iodized salt + Levothyroxine (TSH suppression therapy). Many goiters shrink with adequate iodine.

Large/Compressive Goiter: Thyroidectomy (surgical removal) for compressive symptoms, cosmetic concerns, or suspected malignancy.

4. Prevention (Population Level)

NIDDCP Programme: Universal salt iodization, monitoring salt quality at production/retail, urinary iodine surveillance, IEC campaigns. India's most successful micronutrient programme.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Levothyroxine Tablets IP 25/50/100mcg: Thyroid replacement. Pack of 100.

Iodized Salt: Available at Jan Aushadhi stores.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Levothyroxine 50mcg, 100's: ₹35.00

Levothyroxine 100mcg, 100's: ₹55.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Kanchanara Guggulu: Classical formulation for Galaganda (goiter) in Ayurveda. Contains Kanchanara (Bauhinia variegata) bark and Guggulu. Specific for thyroid/neck swelling. 2 tablets twice daily. Published evidence for thyroid-supportive activity.

Varunadi Kashaya: Classical decoction for Gandamala (lymphatic/glandular swelling). Contains Varuna (Crataeva nurvala). 15-30ml twice daily.

Jalakumbhi (Pistia stratiotes): Natural source of iodine in Ayurvedic texts. Used in iodine-deficient areas traditionally.

Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic with thyroid-stimulatory activity. Published evidence for improving TSH and T4 levels in subclinical hypothyroidism. 300-600mg twice daily.

Guggulu (Commiphora mukul): Traditional Medohara and metabolism-supporting resin. Guggulsterones may support thyroid function. Part of Kanchanara Guggulu formulation.

Himalaya Guggul/Kanchanara: Branded formulation for thyroid and metabolic support.
diseases.india_specific:
India's Iodine Deficiency Belt: The sub-Himalayan belt (J&K, HP, Uttarakhand, NE states), tribal areas, and flood-prone regions have iodine-depleted soil → iodine-deficient crops/water. 200+ million Indians were at IDD risk before universal salt iodization.

Universal Salt Iodization (USI): India's NIDDCP (National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme) mandated iodized salt nationwide. Coverage now >90%. However, non-iodized salt (rock salt/sendha namak, crystal salt) is still used in some communities, especially during festivals and religious fasting — undermining iodization efforts.

Iodine Loss in Cooking: Even iodized salt loses 20-50% of iodine content when added to boiling food, exposed to sunlight, or stored in open containers. Educate: add salt at end of cooking, store in closed containers away from heat and moisture.

Dual Burden: Some areas now show EXCESS iodine intake (from iodized salt + iodine-containing medications) causing iodine-induced thyrotoxicosis. Monitoring via urinary iodine surveys is ongoing.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Use ONLY Iodized Salt: The simplest way to prevent IDD is using iodized salt for ALL cooking. Non-iodized rock salt (sendha namak), sea salt, or Himalayan pink salt used exclusively can cause iodine deficiency. Even during Navratri fasting, use iodized sendha namak where available.

Congenital Hypothyroidism — Screen All Newborns: Neonatal TSH screening within 48-72 hours of birth detects congenital hypothyroidism. Treatment with Levothyroxine within 2 weeks prevents irreversible intellectual disability. Push for universal newborn screening in India.

Kanchanara Guggulu — Genuine Ayurvedic Thyroid Support: This classical formulation has established use for Galaganda (goiter) and published evidence supporting thyroid function. It can complement Levothyroxine therapy but CANNOT replace it in established hypothyroidism.

Levothyroxine Is Lifelong: Patients with permanent hypothyroidism need daily Levothyroxine for life. Monitor TSH every 6-12 months. Stopping medication → return of symptoms and complications.

Store Iodized Salt Properly: Keep in closed container, away from sunlight and moisture. Add to food at END of cooking (not beginning) to minimize iodine loss from heat.

diseases.name: Rickets & Vitamin D Deficiency
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment

Growth Chart Monitoring: Plot weight/height on WHO growth charts. Faltering growth + skeletal deformities = suspect rickets.

Skeletal Examination: Systematically check for bowing, wrist widening, rachitic rosary, Harrison's sulcus, craniotabes, delayed fontanelle closure.

2. Blood Tests

Serum 25(OH) Vitamin D: THE definitive test. Deficient: <20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L). Insufficient: 20-29 ng/mL. Sufficient: 30-100 ng/mL. Measure in ALL suspected cases.

Serum Calcium (Total & Ionized): Low or low-normal in Vitamin D deficiency. Severe hypocalcemia (<7 mg/dL) causes tetany and seizures.

Serum Phosphate: Low in nutritional rickets. High in renal rickets.

Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP): Elevated — sensitive marker of active rickets. ALP >500 IU/L strongly suggests rickets.

Serum PTH (Parathyroid Hormone): Elevated (secondary hyperparathyroidism) in response to low calcium/Vitamin D.

3. Radiology

X-ray Wrist (AP View): MOST USEFUL radiological investigation. Shows cupping, fraying, and widening of metaphyses. Loss of provisional zone of calcification. 'Paintbrush' appearance of distal radius/ulna.

X-ray Knee: Widened growth plates, cupping of metaphyses, bowing deformity.

4. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Rickets diagnosed by: Clinical features (skeletal deformities) + Low 25(OH)D (<20 ng/mL) + Elevated ALP + Characteristic X-ray changes (cupping/fraying of metaphyses). In resource-limited settings, clinical features + elevated ALP alone may warrant empirical Vitamin D treatment.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Treatment of Vitamin D Deficiency Rickets

Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) — Stoss Therapy: Single high dose — 60,000 IU oral weekly for 6-8 weeks (total 360,000-480,000 IU), then 400-1000 IU daily maintenance. OR single dose 300,000-600,000 IU IM (for compliance concerns). Dramatic improvement in 2-4 weeks.

Calcium Supplementation: Elemental calcium 30-75 mg/kg/day for 3-6 months during healing phase. Calcium carbonate or calcium citrate. Essential alongside Vitamin D for bone mineralization.

2. Prevention (Population Approach)

Vitamin D Supplementation for ALL Infants: 400 IU/day from birth to 1 year (especially exclusively breastfed). IAP recommendation. Continue 600 IU/day for 1-18 years.

Maternal Supplementation: 1000-2000 IU/day during pregnancy and lactation. Prevents neonatal deficiency.

Sun Exposure: 20-30 minutes of midday sun exposure (10 AM-3 PM) on face, arms, and legs — 2-3 times weekly. More needed for dark-skinned individuals. Not always practical in polluted urban India.

Food Fortification: India's FSSAI has approved Vitamin D fortification of milk and edible oils. Encourage fortified dairy products.

3. Surgical Correction

Orthopedic Surgery: For severe, established bowing that does not correct with medical treatment and growth. Corrective osteotomy after Vitamin D repletion and skeletal maturity.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) Sachets 60,000 IU: For weekly supplementation. Available at Jan Aushadhi.

Calcium Carbonate Tablets IP 500mg: Calcium supplementation.

Calcium + Vitamin D3 Tablets: Combination supplement.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Cholecalciferol 60,000 IU sachet: ₹15.00

Calcium Carbonate 500mg, 10's: ₹5.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Praval Pishti (Coral Calcium): Classical Ayurvedic calcium supplement. Contains natural calcium carbonate from coral. Cooling (Pittashamaka). 250-500mg twice daily with milk. Traditional bone-strengthening preparation.

Mukta Pishti (Pearl Calcium): Classical calcium-rich preparation from pearls. Balya (strengthening) and calcium supplement. 125-250mg twice daily.

Laksha Guggulu: Classical formulation for Asthi-Sandhi Vikara (bone and joint disorders). Contains Laksha (shellac), Ashwagandha, Nagabala, Arjuna, Guggulu. Used for bone healing and strengthening. 2 tablets twice daily.

Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic, promotes bone density and muscle strength. Published evidence for improving calcium balance. 300-600mg twice daily.

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus): Calcium-rich Rasayana. Supports bone health, especially in women. 3-5g powder with milk.

Cow's Milk (Go-Ksheera): Ayurveda's primary Asthi-Dhatu nourishing food. Rich in calcium and (when fortified) Vitamin D. 2 glasses daily for children. Traditional and still the best dietary calcium source.

Til/Sesame (Sesamum indicum): Rich in calcium (1000mg per 100g). Include tahini, til laddu, and til chikki in diet.
diseases.india_specific:
Paradox of the Sunny Country: Despite abundant sunlight, India has one of the highest Vitamin D deficiency burdens globally — 50-90% of Indians are deficient. Reasons: dark skin pigmentation (melanin blocks UV-B), cultural practices of covering skin/staying indoors, urban air pollution blocking UV-B, and vegetarian diet (low in Vitamin D-rich foods).

Exclusive Breastfeeding Without Supplementation: Breast milk contains very little Vitamin D (10-80 IU/L vs recommended 400 IU/day for infants). Exclusively breastfed Indian infants NOT receiving Vitamin D drops are at high rickets risk.

Maternal Deficiency → Neonatal Deficiency: 70-90% of Indian pregnant women are Vitamin D deficient. Neonates are born with depleted stores, starting life in deficit.

Cultural Practices: Purdah/ghunghat system, preference for light skin (avoiding sun), infants kept bundled indoors, and limited outdoor play in urban apartments all reduce sun exposure.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Supplement ALL Breastfed Infants: Breast milk alone does NOT provide enough Vitamin D. Every exclusively breastfed infant needs 400 IU Vitamin D drops daily from birth. This prevents rickets entirely. Pediatricians and ASHA workers must recommend this routinely.

India's Vitamin D Paradox: Despite living in a sunny country, 50-90% of Indians are Vitamin D deficient. Dark skin, indoor lifestyle, pollution, and covered clothing all reduce Vitamin D synthesis. Don't assume sun exposure is adequate — supplement.

Ayurvedic Calcium Supports Are Genuine: Praval Pishti, Mukta Pishti, and Laksha Guggulu are traditional calcium-rich preparations with established use in bone health. They can supplement but should not replace standardized Cholecalciferol and Calcium supplementation for confirmed rickets.

Weekly Vitamin D3 Sachet (60,000 IU): Affordable, practical, and effective for treating deficiency. Available at Jan Aushadhi for ~₹15/sachet. 6-8 weekly sachets can correct most Vitamin D deficiency.

Don't Ignore Bowed Legs in Toddlers: Mild bowing is normal up to 2 years, but progressive bowing with wrist widening after 2 years needs investigation. Early Vitamin D treatment reverses rickets completely — delayed treatment leads to permanent deformity.
confirmation_tests_curated: Growth Chart Monitoring: Plot weight/height on WHO growth charts. Faltering growth + skeletal deformities = suspect rickets.
Skeletal Examination: Systematically check for bowing, wrist widening, rachitic rosary, Harrison's sulcus, craniotabes, delayed fontanelle closure.
Serum 25(OH) Vitamin D: THE definitive test. Deficient: <20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L). Insufficient: 20-29 ng/mL. Sufficient: 30-100 ng/mL. Measure in ALL suspected cases.

diseases.name: Conjunctivitis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Usually Sufficient)

Clinical Differentiation: Viral: bilateral, watery discharge, preauricular lymph node, follicular conjunctiva. Bacterial: unilateral initially, mucopurulent discharge, papillary conjunctiva. Allergic: bilateral, intense itch, watery/stringy discharge, chemosis, history of atopy.

2. Laboratory Tests (Special Situations Only)

Conjunctival Swab Culture & Sensitivity: For severe bacterial conjunctivitis not responding to empirical therapy, neonatal conjunctivitis, and suspected gonococcal/chlamydial infection.

Gram Stain: Rapid identification — Gram-negative intracellular diplococci (Gonococcus), inclusion bodies (Chlamydia).

Chlamydia PCR/DFA: For suspected trachoma or chlamydial conjunctivitis.

3. Key Assessment

Slit Lamp Examination: To rule out corneal involvement (keratitis), anterior uveitis, and assess severity. Available at ophthalmology clinics.

Visual Acuity Check: MUST check in all conjunctivitis cases. Decreased vision = NOT simple conjunctivitis → urgent referral.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Bacterial Conjunctivitis

Chloramphenicol Eye Drops 0.5%: 1-2 drops every 2 hours for 2 days, then every 4 hours for 5 more days. First-line in India. Broad-spectrum and affordable.

Moxifloxacin Eye Drops 0.5%: 1 drop thrice daily for 7 days. For moderate-severe bacterial conjunctivitis. Broader spectrum.

Tobramycin Eye Drops 0.3%: Alternative. 1 drop every 4 hours for 7 days.

2. Viral Conjunctivitis

Supportive Care Only: Cold compresses, artificial tears for comfort. Antibiotics NOT needed for viral conjunctivitis. Self-limiting in 7-14 days. Hygiene to prevent spread — frequent handwashing, separate towels.

3. Allergic Conjunctivitis

Olopatadine Eye Drops 0.1%: Dual-action antihistamine + mast cell stabilizer. 1 drop twice daily. First-line for allergic conjunctivitis.

Oral Cetirizine: 10mg daily for systemic allergic symptoms accompanying eye allergy.

Cold Compresses: Soothing symptom relief for allergic conjunctivitis.

4. Neonatal Conjunctivitis

Ceftriaxone IM (Gonococcal): 25-50mg/kg single dose (max 125mg) for gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum. EMERGENCY — refer immediately.

Azithromycin Oral (Chlamydial): 20mg/kg/day for 3 days.

Prophylaxis at Birth: 1% Tetracycline or 0.5% Erythromycin eye ointment within 1 hour of birth. Part of essential newborn care.

5. Trachoma

Azithromycin: Single dose 20mg/kg (mass treatment in endemic communities). Part of WHO SAFE strategy.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Chloramphenicol Eye Drops 0.5%: First-line antibiotic eye drops. Bottle.

Ciprofloxacin Eye Drops 0.3%: Alternative antibiotic. Bottle.

Cetirizine Tablets IP 10mg: For allergic conjunctivitis. Pack of 10.

Normal Saline Eye Drops: For eye irrigation and comfort.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Chloramphenicol Eye Drops: ₹8.00

Ciprofloxacin Eye Drops: ₹10.00

Cetirizine 10mg, 10's: ₹3.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Triphala Eye Wash: Classical Ayurvedic eye care. Triphala (Amla + Haritaki + Bibhitaki) soaked overnight in water, strained through fine cloth, used as eye wash. Antimicrobial and soothing. Traditional Netra Prakshalana (eye cleansing).

Rose Water (Gulab Jal): Traditional cooling eye drops. 1-2 drops in each eye. Soothing for mild irritation. Anti-inflammatory.

Chandrodaya Varti (Collyrium): Classical Ayurvedic eye preparation (Anjana). Applied around the eye. Contains Rasanjana, Saindhava, Maricha. For Netra Roga (eye diseases).

Haridra (Turmeric): Anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial. Turmeric water compresses on closed eyelids for external soothing.

Neem Water Wash: Mild neem decoction used for eye hygiene. Antimicrobial. Cool to room temperature before use.

Himalaya Ophthacare Eye Drops: Proprietary herbal eye drops for conjunctivitis relief.
diseases.india_specific:
Monsoon & Summer Outbreaks: Adenoviral conjunctivitis outbreaks ('Madras eye'/'Chennai eye') occur regularly during hot-humid months in India. Highly contagious — spreads rapidly in schools, communities, and families.

Trachoma (Chlamydial) — Still Endemic: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis) remains endemic in parts of rural India (Rajasthan, UP, tribal areas). Chronic infection causes scarring, trichiasis, and preventable blindness. WHO SAFE strategy (Surgery, Antibiotics, Facial cleanliness, Environmental improvement) being implemented.

Ophthalmia Neonatorum: Neonatal conjunctivitis (within 28 days) from Neisseria gonorrhoeae or Chlamydia during birth. Can cause corneal perforation and blindness within 24-48 hours if gonococcal. Prevented by prophylactic eye drops at birth.

Over-the-Counter Steroid Eye Drops: Dangerous Indian practice — patients self-prescribing Dexamethasone/Betamethasone eye drops for red eyes. Steroid drops worsen herpes keratitis (causing corneal melt), raise IOP (steroid glaucoma), and promote fungal infections. NEVER use steroid eye drops without ophthalmologist prescription.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — NEVER Use Steroid Eye Drops Without Ophthalmologist: Over-the-counter Dexamethasone/Betamethasone eye drops are dangerous when self-prescribed. They worsen herpes keratitis (causing blindness), raise eye pressure (glaucoma), and promote fungal infections. This is a major problem in India.

Viral Conjunctivitis — No Antibiotics Needed: Most conjunctivitis outbreaks in India are viral (Adenovirus). Antibiotics are useless and wasteful. Cold compresses + hygiene + patience (7-14 days) is all that's needed. Save antibiotics for confirmed bacterial cases.

Triphala Eye Wash — Genuine Ayurvedic Eye Care: Triphala Prakshalana (eye wash) is one of Ayurveda's most established eye care practices. Safe, antimicrobial, and soothing when properly prepared (filtered through fine cloth to remove all particles). Can be used as adjunct.

Prevent Spread: Frequent handwashing, separate towels, avoid touching/rubbing eyes, don't share eye drops or cosmetics. In outbreaks, isolate affected children from school for duration of symptoms.

Red Flags — Refer to Ophthalmologist: Photophobia, decreased vision, severe pain, contact lens wear, unilateral severe redness, or failure to improve in 1 week → these are NOT simple conjunctivitis and need specialist evaluation.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Differentiation: Viral: bilateral, watery discharge, preauricular lymph node, follicular conjunctiva. Bacterial: unilateral initially, mucopurulent discharge, papillary conjunctiva. Allergic: bilateral, intense itch, watery/stringy discharge, chemosis, history of atopy.
Conjunctival Swab Culture & Sensitivity: For severe bacterial conjunctivitis not responding to empirical therapy, neonatal conjunctivitis, and suspected gonococcal/chlamydial infection.
Gram Stain: Rapid identification — Gram-negative intracellular diplococci (Gonococcus), inclusion bodies (Chlamydia).

diseases.name: Dental Caries & Oral Health
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Oral Examination

Visual Examination: Inspect teeth for cavities, discoloration, plaque, calculus. Examine gums for redness, swelling, bleeding. Check for mucosal lesions (leukoplakia, erythroplakia — premalignant). Available at any health facility.

Dental Probing: Use dental explorer to detect cavity depth, softened dentin. Periodontal probe for gum pocket depth (>3mm = periodontitis).

2. Radiography

Intraoral Periapical X-ray (IOPA): Shows individual tooth structure, periapical pathology (abscess), and interproximal caries. Available at dental clinics.

Orthopantomogram (OPG): Panoramic view of all teeth, jaws, and TMJ. Assesses overall dental status, impactions, bone loss.

3. Additional Assessments

Pulp Vitality Testing: Cold test / Electric pulp test to determine if tooth nerve is alive. Guides treatment — filling vs root canal.

Oral Cancer Screening: Visual inspection + palpation of oral mucosa for suspicious lesions. Biopsy of persistent ulcers (>2 weeks), white/red patches. Toluidine blue staining for screening.

4. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Dental Caries Classification (ICDAS): Score 0-6 from sound tooth to extensive cavitation. Early detection at white-spot stage (Score 1-2) allows remineralization without drilling. Advanced caries (Score 5-6) requires restoration or extraction.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Pain Management

Ibuprofen: 400-600mg every 6-8 hours. Best evidence-based dental analgesic. Anti-inflammatory.

Paracetamol: 500-1000mg every 4-6 hours. Alternative/combination with Ibuprofen.

Diclofenac: 50mg twice-thrice daily. Alternative NSAID.

2. Dental Infections

Amoxicillin: 500mg thrice daily for 5-7 days. First-line for dental abscess/infection.

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate: 625mg thrice daily for 5-7 days. For resistant infections.

Metronidazole: 400mg thrice daily. Add to Amoxicillin for mixed anaerobic infections (common in dental abscess).

3. Definitive Dental Treatment

Dental Filling (Restoration): Glass Ionomer Cement (GIC) — releases fluoride, bonds to tooth. Preferred in field settings (ART — Atraumatic Restorative Treatment). Composite resin for permanent teeth.

Root Canal Treatment: For deep caries with pulp involvement. Removes infected nerve, preserves tooth.

Extraction: For irreversibly damaged teeth. Simple extraction at PHC dental clinic. Surgical extraction for impactions.

Scaling & Root Planing: Professional cleaning to remove calculus/plaque. Treats gingivitis and early periodontitis.

4. Prevention

Fluoride Toothpaste (1000-1500 ppm): Brush twice daily with fluoride toothpaste. Single most effective caries prevention measure.

Professional Fluoride Application: Fluoride varnish (22,600 ppm NaF) applied biannually by dentist. Proven caries reduction in children.

Pit & Fissure Sealants: Protective coating on molar biting surfaces of children aged 6-12. Prevents 80% of molar caries.

Sugar Reduction: Limit sugar-sweetened beverages and between-meal snacking. WHO recommends <10% of energy from free sugars.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: Dental analgesic. Pack of 10.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: Alternative analgesic. Pack of 10.

Amoxicillin Capsules IP 500mg: For dental infections. Pack of 10.

Metronidazole Tablets IP 400mg: For anaerobic dental infections. Pack of 10.

Clotrimazole Mouth Paint: For oral thrush.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Ibuprofen 400mg, 10's: ₹5.00

Amoxicillin 500mg, 10's: ₹12.00

Metronidazole 400mg, 10's: ₹5.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Clove Oil / Lavanga Taila (Syzygium aromaticum): THE most famous traditional dental remedy globally. Eugenol in clove oil is a proven dental analgesic and antiseptic. Apply directly on painful tooth with cotton. Published evidence for analgesic efficacy comparable to Benzocaine. FDA-recognized dental agent.

Oil Pulling (Kavala/Gandusha): Traditional Ayurvedic oral hygiene practice. Swish 1 tablespoon sesame/coconut oil in mouth for 15-20 minutes, spit out. Published evidence for reducing S. mutans count, plaque, and gingivitis. Daily practice.

Neem Twig (Datun): Traditional Indian toothbrush. Neem bark contains nimbidin with antibacterial activity against oral pathogens. Fibrous bristles clean teeth mechanically. Still used by millions in rural India.

Triphala Mouthwash: Triphala decoction as mouthwash. Published evidence for anti-cariogenic and anti-gingivitis activity. Gargle twice daily.

Yashtimadhu / Mulethi: Anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial. Mulethi stick chewed as natural dental care. Glycyrrhizin inhibits S. mutans.

Irimedadi Taila: Classical Ayurvedic oil for Danta Roga (dental diseases). Oil pulling specifically formulated for oral health.

Dashana Samskara Churna: Classical Ayurvedic tooth powder. Contains Triphala, Trikatu, Saindhava. Traditional alternative to toothpaste.

Himalaya Complete Care Toothpaste: Herbal toothpaste with Neem, Pomegranate, Triphala. Available across India.
diseases.india_specific:
India's Oral Health Crisis: Dental caries prevalence: 50-70% in children, 80-90% in adults. India has ~3 lakh dentists but >80% practice in urban areas, leaving rural India severely underserved. Dentist:population ratio in rural India is ~1:300,000 vs recommended 1:7,500.

Sugar Consumption & Oral Hygiene: Rising sugar consumption (sugar-sweetened beverages, sweets, processed foods) combined with poor brushing habits drives the caries epidemic. Only 50% of Indians brush twice daily.

Tobacco & Oral Cancer: India has one of the highest oral cancer rates globally. Tobacco chewing (gutka, pan masala, khaini), betel nut, and areca nut cause oral submucous fibrosis, leukoplakia, and squamous cell carcinoma. Oral cancer screening should be part of every dental visit.

Fluoride — Deficiency and Excess: Some Indian regions have fluoride-deficient water (caries-prone), while others have excess fluoride causing dental/skeletal fluorosis (15+ states affected). Community water fluoridation must be tailored to local levels.
diseases.important_notes:
Clove Oil — Genuinely Effective Dental First Aid: Clove oil (eugenol) is a proven dental analgesic accepted even by modern dentistry. For acute toothache in rural areas without dental access, clove oil applied on cotton to the painful tooth provides genuine relief while patient arranges dental visit.

Brush Twice Daily with Fluoride Toothpaste: The single most effective action for preventing dental disease. Fluoride toothpaste (1000-1500 ppm) is non-negotiable. Neem datun is a good adjunct but NOT a replacement for fluoride toothpaste.

Dental Abscess = Emergency: A swollen face with dental pain and fever indicates dental abscess that can spread to neck spaces (Ludwig's angina) or brain. This is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY requiring antibiotics and drainage. Do not delay for home remedies.

Stop Tobacco for Oral Health: Gutka, pan masala, khaini, and tobacco chewing cause oral submucous fibrosis (irreversible) and oral cancer. India accounts for 1/3 of global oral cancer cases. Screening for oral lesions should be routine at every health contact.

Oil Pulling — Genuine Ayurvedic Prevention: Kavala (oil pulling) with sesame or coconut oil has published evidence for reducing oral bacteria and gingivitis. Safe, affordable daily practice that can complement (not replace) brushing with fluoride toothpaste.
confirmation_tests_curated: Visual Examination: Inspect teeth for cavities, discoloration, plaque, calculus. Examine gums for redness, swelling, bleeding. Check for mucosal lesions (leukoplakia, erythroplakia — premalignant). Available at any health facility.
Dental Probing: Use dental explorer to detect cavity depth, softened dentin. Periodontal probe for gum pocket depth (>3mm = periodontitis).
Intraoral Periapical X-ray (IOPA): Shows individual tooth structure, periapical pathology (abscess), and interproximal caries. Available at dental clinics.

diseases.name: Ascariasis
diseases.tests:
1. Stool Examination

Stool Microscopy (Ova Detection): Ascaris eggs — characteristic bile-stained, mammillated (bumpy-surfaced), oval, 60x45μm. Both fertilized (embryonated) and unfertilized eggs may be seen. Direct smear or concentration technique.

Kato-Katz Quantitative: Egg count per gram of stool determines intensity: Light (<5000 EPG), Moderate (5000-50,000), Heavy (>50,000). WHO recommended for programme monitoring.

2. Imaging

Abdominal Ultrasound: Can visualize live worms in intestine (characteristic 'railway track' sign — parallel echogenic lines), bile ducts, and gallbladder. Useful for hepatobiliary ascariasis.

Abdominal X-ray: For suspected intestinal obstruction — dilated bowel loops, air-fluid levels. Worm bolus may be visible.

3. Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Eosinophilia (5-15%) in active infection or during larval migration (Löffler's phase).

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Ascariasis confirmed by: Ascaris eggs in stool OR visualization of adult worm (passed or on imaging). In endemic settings with consistent symptoms, empirical Albendazole is appropriate without waiting for stool results.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Anti-Helminthic Treatment

Albendazole: 400mg single oral dose. WHO/NTDD recommended. >95% cure rate. First-line for all soil-transmitted helminths. Single dose makes it ideal for mass treatment.

Mebendazole: 500mg single dose or 100mg twice daily for 3 days. Alternative to Albendazole.

Pyrantel Pamoate: 11mg/kg single dose (max 1g). Alternative. Safe in pregnancy (when Albendazole contraindicated in first trimester).

2. Complication Management

Intestinal Obstruction: Initial conservative management — NGT decompression, IV fluids, Piperazine citrate 75mg/kg (paralyzes worms for expulsion). Surgery if conservative management fails.

Biliary Ascariasis: ERCP for worm extraction from bile duct. Conservative approach often successful — antihelminthic + antispasmodics.

3. Prevention

National Deworming Day: Biannual Albendazole (February/August) for all children 1-19 years. FREE. Ensure 100% participation.

WASH: Handwashing with soap before eating and after defecation. Wash fruits/vegetables thoroughly. Proper toilet use. Eliminate open defecation.

Food Hygiene: Avoid raw/unwashed vegetables and salads from areas using human fecal matter as fertilizer.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Albendazole Tablets IP 400mg: First-line dewormer. FREE on National Deworming Day.

Mebendazole Tablets IP 500mg: Alternative dewormer.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For abdominal pain. Pack of 10.

ORS Powder: For associated diarrhea.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Albendazole 400mg: ₹3.00

Mebendazole 500mg: ₹5.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vidanga (Embelia ribes): Classical Krimighna (anti-helminthic) — the most important Ayurvedic deworming herb. 3-5g powder with honey on empty stomach for 3-5 days. Published anti-helminthic activity against Ascaris.

Palasha Beeja (Butea monosperma): Classical Krimighna. Seed powder 3-5g on empty stomach. Traditional deworming remedy.

Kampillaka (Mallotus philippinensis): Classical Krimighna. Red powder from fruit glands used as vermifuge. 1-3g with warm water.

Tulsi Seeds (Ocimum sanctum): Traditional remedy for intestinal worms in Indian households. 1 tsp seeds with warm water on empty stomach.

Ajwain (Trachyspermum ammi): Carminative and anti-parasitic. Ajwain water for abdominal pain from worms. 3-5g seeds boiled in water.

Neem Juice: Nimbidin and azadirachtin have anti-helminthic properties. 10-20ml fresh neem juice on empty stomach for 3 days.
diseases.india_specific:
Most Common Helminth in India: Ascaris lumbricoides infects an estimated 200+ million Indians. Prevalence 20-50% in children in rural/tribal India. Soil-transmitted through fecal contamination of soil → hand-to-mouth.

Soil Contamination & Open Defecation: Ascaris eggs persist in soil for years. Children playing on contaminated soil, eating unwashed vegetables, or practicing geophagia acquire infection. Open defecation directly contaminates agricultural soil.

National Deworming Day: India's biannual Albendazole mass deworming programme (February and August) targets all children aged 1-19 years. World's largest deworming programme.

Monsoon Season Peak: Warm, moist soil conditions during and after monsoon favor egg maturation and larval development.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — National Deworming Day Compliance: Albendazole 400mg given FREE biannually to all children aged 1-19 years. This single tablet treats Ascaris, hookworm, and whipworm. Ensure every child participates. Minor side effects (nausea, mild abdominal pain, diarrhea from dead worms) are normal and transient.

Don't Panic When Worms Are Passed: Parents are often terrified when children pass live worms after deworming. This is EXPECTED and means the medicine is working. Reassure and maintain hygiene (proper disposal, handwashing).

Ayurvedic Dewormers Are Traditional: Vidanga and Palasha have centuries of traditional use as Krimighna (anti-helminthic). They can be used as supplementary home remedies but Albendazole remains the most effective, proven, single-dose treatment.

Handwashing Is Key Prevention: Ascaris eggs are transmitted hand-to-mouth. Teaching children to wash hands with soap before eating and after using the toilet is the most effective prevention alongside deworming.

Intestinal Obstruction = Emergency: Heavy worm burden in young children can cause intestinal blockage — presenting with severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and inability to pass stool. This is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Refer immediately.
confirmation_tests_curated: Stool Microscopy (Ova Detection): Ascaris eggs — characteristic bile-stained, mammillated (bumpy-surfaced), oval, 60x45μm. Both fertilized (embryonated) and unfertilized eggs may be seen. Direct smear or concentration technique.
Kato-Katz Quantitative: Egg count per gram of stool determines intensity: Light (<5000 EPG), Moderate (5000-50,000), Heavy (>50,000). WHO recommended for programme monitoring.
Abdominal Ultrasound: Can visualize live worms in intestine (characteristic 'railway track' sign — parallel echogenic lines), bile ducts, and gallbladder. Useful for hepatobiliary ascariasis.

diseases.name: Heat Stroke
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (IMMEDIATE — Do Not Wait for Lab)

Heat Stroke Diagnostic Criteria: Core temperature >40°C (104°F) + CNS dysfunction (confusion, seizures, coma) + history of heat exposure. This is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS requiring immediate cooling. Do NOT delay cooling for laboratory confirmation.

Differentiation: Heat Exhaustion (temperature <40°C, sweating present, CNS intact, responds to cooling/rest) vs Heat Stroke (>40°C, CNS dysfunction, may have stopped sweating, medical emergency).

2. Laboratory Tests (During/After Initial Cooling)

Core Temperature (Rectal): Most accurate. Axillary/oral unreliable in heat stroke. Target cooling to <39°C.

Complete Blood Count: Hemoconcentration (high Hct), leukocytosis.

Serum Electrolytes: Hypernatremia or hyponatremia depending on hydration. Hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis/AKI.

Renal Function (Creatinine, BUN): Elevated in AKI. Monitor urine output hourly.

Creatine Kinase (CK): Markedly elevated (>10,000 IU/L) in exertional heat stroke with rhabdomyolysis. CK >100,000 = severe rhabdomyolysis.

Liver Function Tests: AST/ALT may rise dramatically (hepatic injury from heat). Peak at 48-72 hours.

Coagulation (PT/INR, APTT, Fibrinogen): DIC with prolonged PT, elevated D-dimer, low fibrinogen.

Urine: Dark brown (myoglobinuria). Dipstick positive for blood without RBCs on microscopy = myoglobin.

Arterial Blood Gas: Initially respiratory alkalosis → metabolic acidosis (lactic acidosis from tissue hypoxia).
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. IMMEDIATE COOLING (Start at Scene — Every Minute Counts)

Cold Water Immersion: Immerse patient in cold water (1-5°C) up to neck. THE most effective cooling method. Reduces temperature fastest (cooling rate 0.15-0.35°C/minute). Gold standard for exertional heat stroke. Use any available container — bathtub, trough, tarp with ice water.

Evaporative Cooling (If Immersion Unavailable): Remove clothing, spray/sponge entire body with lukewarm water, fan vigorously. Apply ice packs to neck, axillae, and groin (large vessel areas). Cooling rate 0.03-0.06°C/minute (slower than immersion but practical in resource-limited settings).

Target Temperature: Cool to <39°C (102.2°F) rectally within 30 minutes. STOP active cooling at 38.5°C to prevent overshoot hypothermia.

2. IV Fluid Resuscitation

Normal Saline: 1-2 liters IV rapidly, then titrate to urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr. Cold IV fluids (4°C) provide additional internal cooling. Avoid overhydration in elderly/cardiac patients.

3. Organ Support (ICU Management)

Seizure Control: IV Benzodiazepines (Lorazepam/Midazolam). Avoid antipyretics (Paracetamol/Aspirin — ineffective in heat stroke as mechanism is thermoregulatory failure, not pyrexia. Also hepatotoxic in already-damaged liver).

Rhabdomyolysis Management: Aggressive IV fluids (200-300 ml/hr) to maintain UO >200 ml/hr. Alkalinize urine (IV NaHCO3) if pH <6.5. Dialysis for refractory AKI.

DIC Management: FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets as indicated. Treat the cause (cooling) rather than the coagulopathy alone.

4. Prevention (Most Important)

Avoid Peak Heat: Stay indoors 12 PM-3 PM during heat waves. Reschedule outdoor labor to early morning/evening.

Hydration: Drink water regularly BEFORE feeling thirsty. ORS for laborers. 150-200ml every 15-20 minutes during outdoor work.

Clothing: Light-colored, loose, cotton clothing. Wet cloth on head/neck. Hats/umbrellas for sun protection.

Recognize Heat Exhaustion: Educate workers/communities to recognize early warning signs (heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, dizziness) and STOP, REST, COOL, HYDRATE before heat stroke develops.

Normal Saline 500ml: IV rehydration for heat stroke.

Paracetamol — DO NOT USE: Paracetamol is INEFFECTIVE and potentially HARMFUL in heat stroke (not a fever — it's thermoregulatory failure).

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT NOTE — Heat Stroke Is a Medical Emergency, NOT Treatable by Ayurveda: Heat stroke requires IMMEDIATE cooling and hospital care. No Ayurvedic preparation can substitute for rapid body cooling. However, Ayurveda offers excellent heat-prevention wisdom:

Khus/Vetiver (Chrysopogon zizanioides) Sherbet: Classical Pittashamaka (cooling) drink. Khus syrup with cold water. Traditional Indian summer coolant.

Chandanasava / Sandalwood Water: Classical cooling preparation. Sandalwood paste applied on forehead and body has genuine cooling effect.

Gulkand (Rose Petal Preserve): Traditional Pittashamaka. 1-2 tsp daily during summer. Cooling, mild laxative, rich in antioxidants.

Sattu (Roasted Gram Flour) Drink: Traditional Bihar/UP summer drink. Mixed with water, salt, and lemon. Provides carbohydrates, protein, and electrolytes. Excellent heat-prevention drink for laborers.

Coconut Water: Nature's electrolyte. 200-300ml multiple times during hot days.

Buttermilk/Chaas: Traditional cooling, electrolyte-replacing drink. With salt, cumin, and mint. Daily during summer.

Bael (Aegle marmelos) Sherbet: Traditional summer coolant in North India. Cooling, digestive, Pittashamaka. Bael pulp with water and jaggery.

Ayurvedic Summer Regimen (Grishma Ritucharya): Ayurveda prescribes detailed seasonal lifestyle — light cotton clothes, cool baths, afternoon rest, Pitta-pacifying diet (sweet, bitter, cool foods), and avoiding excessive sun exposure. This traditional wisdom is genuinely preventive.
diseases.india_specific:
India's Extreme Heat — Growing Crisis: India experiences heat waves (>40°C for 4+ consecutive days) across central, western, and southern India (April-June). 2023 recorded 159 heat wave days across India. Heat-related deaths: officially 700-1000+/year (likely underreported by 10-50x).

Occupational Heat Exposure: Agricultural laborers, construction workers, brick kiln workers, foundry workers, and outdoor vendors — India's most vulnerable workers — lack heat protection. Working through peak heat (12-3 PM) is common due to economic necessity.

Urban Heat Island Effect: Indian cities (Delhi, Ahmedabad, Hyderabad, Nagpur) are 3-7°C hotter than surrounding rural areas. Concrete/asphalt, loss of green cover, and limited cooling access amplify urban heat exposure.

Ahmedabad Heat Action Plan: Following the 2010 heat wave (1,344 excess deaths), Ahmedabad developed India's first Heat Action Plan — now a model replicated across 130+ Indian cities. Includes early warning systems, cool roof programs, water distribution, and public cooling centres.

Elderly & Infants Most Vulnerable: Elderly (impaired thermoregulation, medications that impair sweating/vasodilation) and infants (<4 years, high surface area:volume ratio) are at highest risk for classic heat stroke.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — Cool First, Transport Later: Every minute of delay in cooling increases mortality. If someone collapses with confusion in heat, START COOLING IMMEDIATELY at the scene — pour cold water on them, move to shade, fan vigorously. Do NOT wait for ambulance. Cool first, then transport to hospital.

Paracetamol Does NOT Work for Heat Stroke: Heat stroke is thermoregulatory failure, NOT infection-mediated fever. Paracetamol/Aspirin are INEFFECTIVE and potentially harmful (liver already stressed). The only treatment is PHYSICAL COOLING (ice water immersion or evaporative cooling).

Ayurvedic Summer Drinks — Genuine Prevention: Sattu, Khus sherbet, Bael sherbet, buttermilk, and coconut water are traditional Indian heat-prevention drinks with genuine hydrating and cooling properties. They PREVENT heat illness when consumed regularly during summer. Encourage these over carbonated/sugary drinks.

Occupational Heat Safety: Outdoor laborers should: drink water every 15-20 minutes, take shade breaks hourly, wear light loose clothing, use ORS, and recognize early warning signs (cramps, excessive sweating, nausea = STOP WORK and cool down).

India's Heat Action Plan: Support and follow state/city Heat Action Plans. Key messages: stay hydrated, stay indoors during peak heat, check on elderly neighbors, recognize symptoms, and call emergency services for suspected heat stroke.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (IMMEDIATE — Do Not Wait for Lab)

Heat Stroke Diagnostic Criteria: Core temperature >40°C (104°F) + CNS dysfunction (confusion, seizures, coma) + history of heat exposure. This is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS requiring immediate cooling. Do NOT delay cooling for laboratory confirmation.
Differentiation: Heat Exhaustion (temperature <40°C, sweating present, CNS intact, responds to cooling/rest) vs Heat Stroke (>40°C, CNS dysfunction, may have stopped sweating, medical emergency).
Core Temperature (Rectal): Most accurate. Axillary/oral unreliable in heat stroke. Target cooling to <39°C.

diseases.name: Scrub Typhus
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Suspicion (Most Critical Step)

Clinical Triad: Fever >5 days + eschar + lymphadenopathy in endemic area/season = highly probable scrub typhus. Even without eschar, undifferentiated fever not responding to antimalarials/antibiotics in endemic season should prompt Doxycycline trial.

Eschar Search Protocol: Systematically examine axillae, groin, perineum, behind ears, nape of neck, waistband area, under breasts (women). Use good lighting. Eschar may be hidden in body folds.

2. Serological Tests

Scrub Typhus IgM ELISA: Detects IgM antibodies from day 7-10. Sensitivity 80-95%. Available at sentinel labs. Most practical confirmatory test in India.

Weil-Felix Test (OXK Agglutination): Old, cheap test. OXK titer ≥1:160 suggestive. LOW sensitivity (50%) and specificity. Should NOT be used as sole diagnostic but still widely available at PHC/CHC level.

Immunofluorescence Assay (IFA): Gold standard. Detects antibody rise ≥4-fold in paired sera. Available at reference labs only.

3. Molecular Tests

Scrub Typhus PCR (Eschar/Blood): Highly specific. Detects O. tsutsugamushi DNA. Positive even after antibiotics started. Available at ICMR/reference labs.

4. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Normal or low WBC (unlike bacterial sepsis). Thrombocytopenia common (mimics dengue). Elevated AST/ALT (2-5x normal). Elevated LDH.

Liver Function Tests: Transaminitis (AST/ALT 100-500 IU/L) with mildly elevated bilirubin. ALP may be elevated.

Renal Function: Elevated creatinine in severe cases with AKI.

CSF Analysis (If Meningoencephalitis): Lymphocytic pleocytosis (10-500 cells), mildly elevated protein, normal glucose. Helps differentiate from bacterial meningitis.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Probable: Acute fever + eschar OR fever + IgM ELISA positive in endemic area. Confirmed: Clinical features + IFA 4-fold rise or PCR positive. In practice: Fever not responding to standard empirical therapy (antimalarials + Ceftriaxone) + any supporting feature (eschar, rash, raised LFT) in endemic season = start Doxycycline empirically.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antibiotic Treatment (Start Empirically — Don't Wait for Labs)

Doxycycline: 100mg twice daily for 7-14 days (adults). 2.2mg/kg twice daily (children — AAP/WHO now permits Doxycycline in ALL ages for Rickettsial diseases). DRAMATIC response — fever resolves within 24-48 hours. This rapid defervescence is near-diagnostic. First-line per WHO/ICMR.

Azithromycin: 500mg daily for 5-7 days (adults), 10mg/kg/day (children). Alternative for pregnant women and Doxycycline-intolerant patients. Slightly less effective but adequate.

Chloramphenicol: 500mg four times daily for 7 days. Historical alternative. Risk of bone marrow suppression limits use. Still used where Doxycycline unavailable.

2. Severe Scrub Typhus (ICU Management)

IV Doxycycline: For patients unable to take oral (encephalitis, vomiting, shock). 100mg IV twice daily. Switch to oral once improving.

IV Azithromycin: 500mg IV daily. Alternative for severe cases in pregnant women.

Organ Support: Mechanical ventilation for ARDS, vasopressors for shock, dialysis for AKI, anticonvulsants for seizures. Standard sepsis management protocols alongside Doxycycline.

3. Supportive Care

Paracetamol: For fever and headache. Avoid NSAIDs if thrombocytopenic.

IV Fluids: Careful fluid management — avoid overload in myocarditis/ARDS.

4. Prevention

Personal Protection: DEET-based insect repellent on exposed skin, Permethrin-treated clothing/uniforms (military, forest workers), avoid sitting/lying on scrub vegetation, tuck trousers into boots.

Sudarshan Ghana Vati: Classical broad-spectrum Jwarahara for persistent fever. 2 tablets thrice daily as adjunct during recovery.

diseases.india_specific:
Major AES Cause — Often Missed: Scrub typhus caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi (transmitted by Leptotrombidium chigger mites) is now recognized as a leading cause of Acute Encephalitis Syndrome (AES) in India — responsible for 20-50% of AES cases in many states, rivaling JE. States: Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Tamil Nadu, Meghalaya, Nagaland, Rajasthan, Kerala, Odisha.

Seasonal — Post-Monsoon Scrub Vegetation: Peaks September-November when chigger mites are active in scrub/bush vegetation. Farmers, military personnel, trekkers, and people near forest/periforest areas at highest risk.

Under-Diagnosis & Misdiagnosis: Routinely misdiagnosed as dengue, malaria, typhoid, or 'viral fever' because clinicians don't suspect it and don't look for eschar. Simple Doxycycline cures it — delay kills.

No Vaccine, No Prophylaxis: Unlike many vector-borne diseases, there is no vaccine or chemoprophylaxis for scrub typhus. Personal protection (DEET repellent, permethrin-treated clothing, avoid scrub vegetation) is the only prevention.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Doxycycline Is the Cure: Scrub typhus has a specific, cheap, universally available cure — Doxycycline 100mg twice daily. Delay in starting Doxycycline is the primary cause of mortality. Every doctor seeing undifferentiated fever in endemic areas during monsoon/post-monsoon MUST consider scrub typhus and start Doxycycline empirically.

LOOK FOR THE ESCHAR: The eschar (black necrotic scab at mite bite site) is pathognomonic but often MISSED because it's in hidden body areas (axillae, groin, under breasts). Systematically search for it in every fever patient in endemic areas. Finding it is diagnostic.

Doxycycline Is Safe in Children: Despite old concerns about tooth staining, AAP and WHO now permit Doxycycline for ALL ages for Rickettsial diseases (including scrub typhus). A short course (7-14 days) does NOT cause permanent teeth staining. Do NOT withhold this life-saving drug from children.

Ayurveda Cannot Treat Active Scrub Typhus: O. tsutsugamushi is an obligate intracellular bacterium that kills without antibiotics. Guduchi and Sudarshan Vati support recovery but CANNOT replace Doxycycline. Untreated severe scrub typhus has 30-40% mortality.

Think Scrub Typhus in AES: In any patient with fever + altered sensorium during monsoon/post-monsoon, consider scrub typhus alongside JE and malaria. A therapeutic trial of Doxycycline can be diagnostic — if fever breaks in 24-48 hours, it was almost certainly Rickettsial.

diseases.name: Pertussis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (High Index of Suspicion)

WHO Clinical Case Definition: Cough lasting ≥2 weeks with ≥1 of: paroxysms of coughing, inspiratory whoop, post-tussive vomiting, in absence of other apparent cause. In infants: apnea ± cyanosis with or without cough.

2. Laboratory Confirmation

Nasopharyngeal Swab Culture: B. pertussis culture on Bordet-Gengou or Regan-Lowe media. Gold standard but sensitivity only 30-60% (best in first 2 weeks). Takes 5-7 days. Requires special transport media.

PCR (Nasopharyngeal Swab): Detects B. pertussis DNA. Sensitivity 70-99%. Positive even after antibiotics started. Available at reference/tertiary labs. Fastest confirmatory method.

Serology (Anti-PT IgG): Single elevated anti-pertussis toxin IgG in patients not recently vaccinated. Useful for late-presenting cases (>3 weeks) when culture/PCR may be negative.

3. Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Marked lymphocytosis (WBC 20,000-100,000/mm³ with >60% lymphocytes) — characteristic in infants and young children. Severe lymphocytosis (>50,000) is a predictor of fatal pertussis in infants.

Chest X-ray: May show perihilar infiltrates, atelectasis, or 'shaggy heart' sign. Pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum from violent coughing.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Confirmed Pertussis: Clinical case + culture positive or PCR positive. Probable: Clinical case + epidemiological link to confirmed case. Clinical: Meets WHO clinical case definition in absence of lab confirmation.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antibiotic Treatment (Reduces Transmission — Start Early)

Azithromycin: 10mg/kg/day for 5 days (children), 500mg day 1 then 250mg days 2-5 (adults). First-line. Most effective if started in catarrhal phase (first 1-2 weeks). After paroxysmal phase established, antibiotics reduce transmission but may not shorten illness.

Erythromycin: 40-50mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days. Alternative. More GI side effects than Azithromycin.

Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX): For macrolide-allergic patients. 8mg/kg/day TMP for 14 days.

2. Supportive Care (CRITICAL for Infants)

Hospital Admission for Infants <6 Months: All infants with suspected pertussis should be hospitalized for apnea monitoring, oxygen supplementation, and feeding support. ICU for severe cases.

Oxygen & Suction: Supplemental oxygen during paroxysms/desaturation. Gentle nasopharyngeal suctioning to clear mucus plugs.

Nutrition: Small frequent feeds between paroxysms. NG tube feeding if oral intake inadequate. IV fluids if dehydrated.

Avoid Cough Suppressants: Codeine and Dextromethorphan are INEFFECTIVE and potentially harmful in pertussis. Do NOT prescribe.

3. Post-Exposure Prophylaxis

Azithromycin for Contacts: Same treatment dose for ALL household contacts regardless of vaccination status. Critical for protecting infants in the household.

4. Prevention

DPT/Pentavalent Vaccine (UIP): Primary series at 6, 10, 14 weeks + DPT boosters at 16-24 months and 5-6 years. Provides 80-90% protection for 5-10 years.

Tdap Booster (IAP Recommended): At 10-12 years. NOT yet in India's UIP but important for reducing adolescent/adult reservoir.

Maternal Tdap (IAP Recommended): Single Tdap dose at 27-36 weeks pregnancy. Passive antibodies protect newborn for first 2-3 months (before infant's own vaccination). Increasingly practiced in Indian private healthcare.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT NOTE — Pertussis Requires Antibiotics: Bordetella pertussis is a bacterial infection. Ayurvedic herbs cannot eradicate it. Supportive Ayurvedic care focuses on respiratory comfort and recovery:

Sitopaladi Churna: Classical Kasahara for cough relief as adjunct. 1-3g with honey thrice daily. May soothe airways between paroxysms.

Yashtimadhu / Mulethi (Glycyrrhiza glabra): Demulcent, soothes irritated pharyngeal mucosa. Mulethi powder with honey.

Tulsi + Honey: Traditional cough remedy. Tulsi juice with honey 2-3 times daily. Honey is WHO-recommended for pediatric cough (>1 year only).

Vasa / Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica): Classical Kasahara and bronchospasmolytica. May help reduce cough severity. 10-20ml leaf juice or Vasa Avaleha.

Chyawanprash: Post-illness immune recovery Rasayana. 5-10g daily after acute phase.
diseases.india_specific:
Under-Diagnosed in India: Pertussis is vastly under-reported in India. Despite UIP vaccination (DPT/Pentavalent), outbreaks continue due to waning immunity (vaccine protection fades after 5-10 years), incomplete vaccination, and poor surveillance. True incidence estimated 5-10x higher than reported.

Infant Mortality: Infants <3 months (too young for first DPT dose) are at highest risk of severe/fatal pertussis. Maternal vaccination (Tdap) in third trimester provides passive protection — NOT yet in India's UIP but recommended by IAP.

Adolescent/Adult Reservoir: Older children and adults with waned immunity develop mild pertussis ('persistent cough') — they become the reservoir infecting vulnerable unvaccinated infants. Tdap booster at 10-12 years recommended by IAP but not yet in UIP.

Misdiagnosis as Asthma/Bronchitis: Prolonged cough (>2 weeks) with paroxysms in older children/adults is frequently misdiagnosed as asthma, reactive airway, or chronic bronchitis. Consider pertussis in ANY cough lasting >2 weeks with paroxysmal character.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Suspect Pertussis in Prolonged Cough: Any cough lasting >2 weeks with paroxysmal character, post-tussive vomiting, or inspiratory whoop should raise pertussis suspicion — even in vaccinated individuals (immunity wanes). Start Azithromycin empirically.

Infants <6 Months = Highest Risk: Pertussis kills infants through apnea, not cough. ALL infants with suspected pertussis must be hospitalized for monitoring. Apnea may be the ONLY presenting sign — without any cough or whoop.

Cough Suppressants Don't Work: Codeine, Dextromethorphan, and other cough medicines are INEFFECTIVE in pertussis and may be harmful. Do NOT prescribe them. The cough mechanism in pertussis is toxin-mediated and does not respond to standard antitussives.

Vaccinate On Schedule: Ensure all children receive DPT/Pentavalent at 6, 10, 14 weeks with boosters at 16-24 months and 5-6 years per UIP. Delayed vaccination leaves infants vulnerable during their highest-risk period.

Prophylaxis ALL Household Contacts: When pertussis is diagnosed, give Azithromycin to ALL household members — especially if there's an unvaccinated infant in the house. This is life-saving.
confirmation_tests_curated: WHO Clinical Case Definition: Cough lasting ≥2 weeks with ≥1 of: paroxysms of coughing, inspiratory whoop, post-tussive vomiting, in absence of other apparent cause. In infants: apnea ± cyanosis with or without cough.

Nasopharyngeal Swab Culture: B. pertussis culture on Bordet-Gengou or Regan-Lowe media. Gold standard but sensitivity only 30-60% (best in first 2 weeks). Takes 5-7 days. Requires special transport media.
PCR (Nasopharyngeal Swab): Detects B. pertussis DNA. Sensitivity 70-99%. Positive even after antibiotics started. Available at reference/tertiary labs. Fastest confirmatory method.

diseases.name: Diphtheria
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (START TREATMENT IMMEDIATELY on Suspicion)

Clinical Recognition: Sore throat + adherent gray-white membrane on tonsils/pharynx + cervical lymphadenopathy + relatively low fever with disproportionate toxicity = diphtheria until proven otherwise. Do NOT wait for lab confirmation — START Antitoxin + Antibiotics immediately.

2. Microbiological Confirmation

Throat Swab Culture: Swab from beneath membrane edge. Culture on Löffler's serum slope (shows metachromatic granules) and Tellurite agar (black colonies). Takes 24-48 hours. Simultaneously send to reference lab for toxigenicity testing (Elek test or PCR for tox gene).

Albert's Stain (Gram Stain Alternative): Shows characteristic Chinese-letter arrangement of gram-positive bacilli with metachromatic granules (volutin). Quick presumptive identification.

Elek Test / Tox Gene PCR: Confirms toxin production by the isolated C. diphtheriae strain. Essential because non-toxigenic strains exist. Available at reference labs.

3. Complication Monitoring

ECG (Serial — Daily): Monitor for myocarditis — ST-T changes, heart block (first-degree → complete), arrhythmias. ECG changes may appear 1-2 weeks after symptom onset.

Cardiac Enzymes (Troponin/CK-MB): Elevated in myocarditis. Serial monitoring.

Complete Blood Count, Renal Function, Liver Function: Baseline and monitoring for systemic toxicity.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Suspected: Compatible clinical features (pharyngeal membrane + toxicity). Probable: Suspected + positive Albert stain or culture of C. diphtheriae. Confirmed: Probable + toxigenicity confirmed (Elek/PCR positive). In India, any pharyngeal membrane with cervical lymphadenopathy must be treated as diphtheria pending lab confirmation.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Diphtheria Antitoxin (DAT) — LIFE-SAVING (Give Immediately)

Diphtheria Antitoxin (Equine): 20,000-100,000 units IV/IM based on severity. MUST be given within 48 hours — neutralizes only CIRCULATING (unbound) toxin. Delayed administration dramatically increases mortality. Skin test for horse serum sensitivity before IV administration. Available through state health departments — request immediately on clinical suspicion.

2. Antibiotic Treatment

Penicillin G (IV/IM): 100,000 IU/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days. Kills C. diphtheriae and stops toxin production. First-line.

Erythromycin: 40mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days. Alternative for Penicillin-allergic. Also first-line for carrier eradication.

3. Supportive Care

Airway Management: Close monitoring for airway obstruction. Tracheostomy if stridor/respiratory distress from laryngeal membrane.

Cardiac Monitoring: Continuous ECG monitoring for 2-3 weeks. Temporary pacemaker for complete heart block. Bed rest for 2-3 weeks to reduce cardiac workload.

Isolation: Strict droplet precautions until 2 consecutive negative throat cultures 24 hours apart after antibiotic completion.

4. Contacts & Prevention

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis: ALL close contacts — throat culture + Erythromycin 7-10 days (or single Benzathine Penicillin) + Td/DPT booster if >5 years since last dose.

DPT/Pentavalent Vaccine: Primary series at 6, 10, 14 weeks + boosters. Active immunization during convalescence (diphtheria infection does NOT confer reliable immunity).

Carrier Treatment: Erythromycin 40mg/kg/day for 7-10 days. Reculture after treatment to confirm clearance.
