Erythromycin Tablets IP 250/500mg: First-line antibiotic. Pack of 10.

Amoxicillin Capsules IP 500mg: Alternative antibiotic. Pack of 10.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: Symptomatic relief. Pack of 10.

Note: Diphtheria Antitoxin (DAT) is supplied through Government health channels. DPT/Pentavalent vaccine provided FREE under UIP.
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Substitute for Antitoxin: Diphtheria toxin causes fatal myocarditis and neuropathy. Only DAT (Diphtheria Antitoxin) neutralizes the toxin. No herbal preparation can substitute. Ayurvedic support is for recovery only:

Guduchi/Giloy: Immunomodulatory recovery support after acute phase. 500mg-1g twice daily.

Yashtimadhu: Demulcent for sore throat relief. Gargle with Yashtimadhu decoction. 3-5g with honey.

Sitopaladi Churna: For associated cough during recovery. 1-3g with honey.

Chyawanprash: Immune recovery Rasayana post-illness. 10-20g daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Outbreaks Continue Despite Vaccination: India reports periodic diphtheria outbreaks — recent clusters in Kerala (2016), Gujarat, Rajasthan, and NE states. Under-vaccinated pockets, waning immunity, and inadequate booster coverage allow transmission to persist. India reported 3,000-6,000 cases/year in recent years.

Vaccination Gap: DPT/Pentavalent 3-dose completion rate is ~85-90% nationally but lower in specific populations (migrants, tribal, remote). Booster doses (16-24 months, 5-6 years) often missed. Adult Td boosters rarely given.

Antitoxin (DAT) Availability: Diphtheria Antitoxin is the CRITICAL life-saving treatment but faces chronic supply shortages in India. Central Drug Laboratory, Kasauli and Serum Institute produce DAT but availability at district level is inconsistent.

Carrier State: Asymptomatic carriers of toxigenic C. diphtheriae maintain community transmission. Carrier rates of 0.5-5% reported in some Indian studies.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — Give Antitoxin Within 48 Hours: Diphtheria Antitoxin must be given as EARLY as possible on clinical suspicion. It neutralizes only unbound toxin — once toxin binds to heart/nerves, it CANNOT be reversed. Every hour of delay worsens outcome. Do NOT wait for culture confirmation.

Vaccination Prevents Diphtheria Entirely: A fully vaccinated child will NEVER get diphtheria. Ensure complete DPT/Pentavalent schedule (6, 10, 14 weeks + boosters). This ₹-free vaccine prevents a disease requiring ₹50,000+ worth of ICU care + scarce antitoxin.

Monitor Heart for 3 Weeks: Myocarditis can develop 1-2 weeks AFTER pharyngeal symptoms improve. Patient must be on ECG monitoring and bed rest for 2-3 weeks even if throat membrane has resolved.

No Ayurvedic Treatment for Active Diphtheria: Diphtheria toxin-mediated organ damage requires antitoxin + antibiotics + ICU care. Ayurvedic herbs support recovery phase only. Delay in seeking modern medical treatment is fatal.

Report Every Case: Diphtheria is a notifiable disease. Every case must be reported to IDSP. Outbreak investigation includes contact tracing, throat cultures of contacts, prophylactic antibiotics, and ring vaccination.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (START TREATMENT IMMEDIATELY on Suspicion)

Clinical Recognition: Sore throat + adherent gray-white membrane on tonsils/pharynx + cervical lymphadenopathy + relatively low fever with disproportionate toxicity = diphtheria until proven otherwise. Do NOT wait for lab confirmation — START Antitoxin + Antibiotics immediately.

Throat Swab Culture: Swab from beneath membrane edge. Culture on Löffler's serum slope (shows metachromatic granules) and Tellurite agar (black colonies). Takes 24-48 hours. Simultaneously send to reference lab for toxigenicity testing (Elek test or PCR for tox gene).
Albert's Stain (Gram Stain Alternative): Shows characteristic Chinese-letter arrangement of gram-positive bacilli with metachromatic granules (volutin). Quick presumptive identification.

diseases.name: Mumps
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary Method)

Clinical Recognition: Acute parotid gland swelling (lifts earlobe, obscures mandible angle) + fever + pain on chewing = mumps. Bilateral involvement is highly characteristic. Lab confirmation rarely needed for typical cases.

2. Laboratory Confirmation (When Needed)

Mumps IgM ELISA (Serum/Saliva): Positive from symptom onset. Confirms acute infection. Available at tertiary/reference labs.

Mumps RT-PCR (Throat Swab/Urine): Detects viral RNA. Most sensitive test. Available at virology labs.

Serum Amylase: Elevated in parotitis (salivary amylase) AND pancreatitis (pancreatic amylase). Lipase specifically elevated in pancreatitis.

3. Complication Assessment

Testicular Ultrasound: For orchitis — shows enlarged, hypoechoic testis with increased blood flow on Doppler.

CSF Analysis: If meningitis suspected — lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein.

Complete Blood Count: Normal or mild leukopenia with lymphocytosis.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Suspected Mumps: Acute-onset unilateral/bilateral parotid swelling lasting ≥2 days without other apparent cause. Confirmed: Suspected + positive IgM or PCR. In India, clinical diagnosis is usually sufficient.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. NO Antiviral — Treatment Is Entirely Supportive

Paracetamol/Ibuprofen: For pain and fever. 500-1000mg Paracetamol or 400mg Ibuprofen every 6-8 hours.

Warm/Cold Compresses: Applied to swollen parotids for pain relief. Warm compresses generally preferred.

Soft Diet: Avoid acidic, sour foods (trigger parotid pain). Soft, bland diet — khichdi, dal, porridge, banana. Adequate fluids.

2. Orchitis Management

Bed Rest + Scrotal Support: Ice packs, supportive underwear/jockstrap, bed rest. NSAIDs for pain. Short course Prednisolone (40mg/day × 5 days) sometimes used for severe orchitis (controversial evidence).

3. Prevention

MMR Vaccine: 2 doses — 12-15 months and 4-6 years. 88% effective after 2 doses. IAP recommended. NOT in India's UIP (only MR is). Available in private practice for ₹250-500/dose.

Isolation: Isolate from school/work for 5 days after parotid swelling onset. Respiratory droplet precautions.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For pain and fever. Pack of 10.

Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: Anti-inflammatory for pain. Pack of 10.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Paracetamol 500mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Ibuprofen 400mg, 10's: ₹5.00

Note: MMR vaccine available privately. MR vaccine provided FREE under UIP.
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Pippali + Haridra Paste (External): Warm paste of Pippali and turmeric applied over swollen parotid glands. Traditional anti-inflammatory. Provides local warmth and swelling reduction.

Shirisha / Siris (Albizia lebbeck): Classical Shothahara (anti-edema) herb. Used for glandular swelling in Ayurveda. 3-5g powder twice daily.

Guduchi/Giloy: Immunomodulatory and Jwaraghna. 500mg-1g twice daily for immune support during illness.

Kanchanara Guggulu: Classical for Gandamala (glandular swelling). 2 tablets twice daily. Supports resolution of parotid swelling.

Warm Sesame Oil Massage: Gentle external massage around (not directly on) swollen area with warm sesame oil to improve circulation and reduce discomfort.
diseases.india_specific:
India — Low Mumps Vaccination Coverage: Mumps vaccine (as MMR) is NOT in India's Universal Immunization Programme — only MR (Measles-Rubella) is included. MMR is given in private practice only. This means most Indian children are susceptible to mumps.

Outbreaks in Schools & Colleges: Mumps outbreaks occur regularly in Indian schools, hostels, and military barracks. Close quarters facilitate transmission via respiratory droplets.

Adult Mumps — More Complications: Adults who didn't get childhood mumps are at higher risk of orchitis, oophoritis, pancreatitis, and meningitis. Migration from rural (lower exposure) to urban settings creates susceptible adult populations.

Under-Reporting: Mumps is not a notifiable disease in India — cases are vastly under-reported. True incidence is unknown.
diseases.important_notes:
Supportive Care Is Sufficient for Most Cases: Mumps is self-limiting in 7-10 days. Paracetamol, soft diet, rest, and warm compresses are adequate. No antiviral or antibiotic needed. Do NOT prescribe antibiotics for mumps (it's viral).

Watch for Orchitis in Post-Pubertal Males: 25-40% of post-pubertal males develop orchitis. Reassure: complete infertility from mumps is very rare. Bilateral complete testicular atrophy (needed for sterility) occurs in <5% of orchitis cases.

MMR Vaccine Recommended but Not in UIP: IAP recommends MMR (Measles-Mumps-Rubella) for all children. Since India's UIP only includes MR (not MMR), parents who can afford should get MMR privately. Advocate for MMR inclusion in UIP.

Ayurvedic Anti-Inflammatory Support Is Reasonable: Kanchanara Guggulu and local warm paste applications for parotid swelling have genuine traditional basis and provide symptomatic comfort. They complement supportive care well.

Isolation for 5 Days: Mumps is contagious via respiratory droplets. Keep child home from school for 5 days after parotid swelling onset to prevent outbreak spread.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Recognition: Acute parotid gland swelling (lifts earlobe, obscures mandible angle) + fever + pain on chewing = mumps. Bilateral involvement is highly characteristic. Lab confirmation rarely needed for typical cases.
Mumps IgM ELISA (Serum/Saliva): Positive from symptom onset. Confirms acute infection. Available at tertiary/reference labs.
Mumps RT-PCR (Throat Swab/Urine): Detects viral RNA. Most sensitive test. Available at virology labs.

diseases.name: Peptic Ulcer & Gastritis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment

Alarm Features Screening: Age >55 new-onset dyspepsia, unintentional weight loss, GI bleeding (hematemesis/melena), progressive dysphagia, persistent vomiting, anemia, palpable mass, family history of GI cancer. ANY alarm feature = urgent endoscopy.

2. H. pylori Testing (Test-and-Treat Strategy)

Urea Breath Test (UBT): Non-invasive gold standard. Patient ingests C13/C14-labeled urea — H. pylori urease produces labeled CO2 measured in breath. Sensitivity/specificity >95%. Available at gastroenterology clinics.

Stool H. pylori Antigen Test: Non-invasive stool test. Sensitivity 90-95%. Useful for diagnosis and confirming eradication. Increasingly available.

Rapid Urease Test (CLO Test): During endoscopy — biopsy placed in urea medium. Color change within hours = positive. Sensitivity 90-95%.

H. pylori Serology (IgG): Blood test. Cannot distinguish active from past infection. NOT useful for confirming eradication. Inexpensive but less accurate.

3. Endoscopy

Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD): Direct visualization of esophagus, stomach, and duodenum. Identifies ulcers, erosions, tumors, varices. Biopsy for H. pylori and histopathology (rule out malignancy). MANDATORY for alarm features.

4. Other Tests

Complete Blood Count: Anemia (iron deficiency from chronic blood loss). Low MCV/MCH.

Stool Occult Blood Test: Positive = chronic occult GI bleeding.

Serum Gastrin: If refractory ulcers — to exclude Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma).

Erect Abdominal X-ray: For suspected perforation — free gas under diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum).

5. Diagnostic Criteria

Peptic Ulcer: Mucosal break ≥5mm seen on endoscopy in stomach (gastric ulcer) or duodenum (duodenal ulcer). Gastritis: Endoscopic inflammation of gastric mucosa. H. pylori-positive: Positive on any validated H. pylori test. Functional Dyspepsia: Symptoms without structural cause on endoscopy.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPI) — Cornerstone of Acid Suppression

Pantoprazole: 40mg once daily before breakfast for 4-8 weeks. India's most prescribed PPI. Heals 90%+ of ulcers.

Omeprazole: 20mg once daily. Alternative PPI. Equally effective.

Rabeprazole/Esomeprazole: Alternatives. Esomeprazole 40mg for severe erosive disease.

2. H. pylori Eradication (Triple/Quadruple Therapy)

Standard Triple Therapy: PPI (Pantoprazole 40mg) + Amoxicillin 1g + Clarithromycin 500mg — all twice daily for 14 days. First-line where Clarithromycin resistance is <15%.

Bismuth Quadruple Therapy: PPI + Bismuth subsalicylate + Metronidazole 500mg + Tetracycline 500mg — all four times daily for 14 days. For Clarithromycin-resistant areas or failed first-line. Increasingly used in India due to resistance.

Sequential/Concomitant Therapy: PPI + Amoxicillin for 5-7 days → PPI + Clarithromycin + Metronidazole for 5-7 days. Alternative approach.

3. Other Medications

H2 Receptor Blockers (Ranitidine/Famotidine): Ranitidine 150mg twice daily. Less effective than PPIs but useful for mild symptoms and maintenance. Famotidine 20mg twice daily preferred (Ranitidine recalled in many countries for NDMA contamination).

Sucralfate: 1g four times daily before meals. Mucosal protectant — coats ulcer base. Useful adjunct.

Antacids (Aluminium/Magnesium Hydroxide): Quick symptom relief. Neutralize gastric acid. Not for primary treatment but useful PRN.

4. GI Bleeding Management

NPO + IV PPI (Pantoprazole 80mg bolus then 8mg/hr infusion): For acute upper GI bleeding from peptic ulcer.

Urgent Endoscopy: Within 24 hours for hemodynamically stable, within 12 hours for unstable. Endoscopic hemostasis (injection, cautery, clipping).

Blood Transfusion: Packed RBC if Hb <7 g/dL or hemodynamically unstable.

Surgery: For refractory bleeding, perforation, or gastric outlet obstruction.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Pantoprazole Tablets IP 40mg: First-line PPI. Pack of 10.

Omeprazole Capsules IP 20mg: Alternative PPI. Pack of 10.

Amoxicillin Capsules IP 500mg: For H. pylori eradication. Pack of 10.

Clarithromycin Tablets IP 500mg: For H. pylori eradication. Pack of 4.

Ranitidine/Famotidine Tablets: H2 blocker. Pack of 10.

Antacid Suspension (Al/Mg Hydroxide): Quick relief. Bottle.

Sucralfate Tablets IP 1g: Mucosal protectant. Pack of 10.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Pantoprazole 40mg, 10's: ₹16.00

Omeprazole 20mg, 10's: ₹8.00

Amoxicillin 500mg, 10's: ₹12.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Yashtimadhu / Mulethi (Glycyrrhiza glabra): Classical Pittashamaka and mucosal protectant. Contains glycyrrhizin with anti-ulcer activity (stimulates mucus production). Published evidence for ulcer healing. Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) 380-760mg before meals. Mulethi powder 3-5g with milk/honey twice daily.

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus): Classical Pittashamaka Rasayana. Published evidence for gastroprotective and anti-ulcer activity (comparable to Ranitidine in animal studies). 3-5g powder with milk twice daily.

Amla/Amalaki (Emblica officinalis): Potent antioxidant and Pittashamaka. Reduces gastric acid secretion. Amla juice or Amalaki Rasayana.

Avipattikar Churna: Classical formulation for Amlapitta (acid peptic disease). Contains Trikatu, Triphala, Musta, Vidanga, Ela. 3-5g with warm water before meals. Most prescribed Ayurvedic antacid in India.

Kamdudha Ras (Mauktik Yukta): Classical herbo-mineral antacid containing Praval Pishti, Mukta Pishti, Shankha Bhasma. Cooling, Pittashamaka. 125-250mg twice daily with milk.

Sutshekhar Ras: Classical for Amlapitta and Pitta disorders. Contains Suvarna Bhasma, Shankha Bhasma, Tamra Bhasma. Practitioner supervision required. 125-250mg twice daily.

Coconut Water: Natural Pittashamaka. Alkaline, soothing for gastric mucosa. 200-300ml twice daily.

Isabgol / Psyllium Husk: Mucilaginous fiber that coats and protects gastric mucosa. 1-2 tsp with water at bedtime.

Himalaya Gasex: Proprietary carminative and antacid. 2 tablets after meals.

Dabur Avipattikar Churna: Branded classical formulation. 3-5g before meals.
diseases.india_specific:
H. pylori — Very High Prevalence: Helicobacter pylori infection rate in India is 60-80% of adults (one of highest globally). Acquired in childhood via fecal-oral route. Main cause of peptic ulcer and gastric cancer risk. Testing and eradication underutilized.

NSAID/Aspirin Overuse: Widespread OTC availability of Diclofenac, Ibuprofen, and Aspirin in India. Self-medication for body aches, headaches, and joint pain without gastroprotection causes NSAID-induced gastropathy — a major cause of GI bleeding.

Stress, Spicy Food & Lifestyle: While not direct causes, Indian lifestyle factors — high spice consumption, irregular meals, tobacco/alcohol, and chronic stress — exacerbate gastritis symptoms and delay healing.

Delayed Endoscopy: Most Indian patients with dyspepsia receive empirical PPI therapy without endoscopy. Alarm features (>55 years onset, weight loss, bleeding, dysphagia, anemia) require endoscopy to exclude gastric cancer — which presents late in India.
diseases.important_notes:
Test and Treat H. pylori: 60-80% of Indian adults carry H. pylori — the main cause of peptic ulcers. Testing (Urea Breath Test or Stool Antigen) and eradication with triple therapy CURES most peptic ulcers permanently. Without eradication, ulcers recur despite PPI therapy. Advocate test-and-treat over lifelong PPI use.

STOP NSAID Abuse: OTC Diclofenac/Ibuprofen is the second biggest cause of peptic ulcer in India after H. pylori. Educate: never take NSAIDs on empty stomach, limit duration, use gastroprotection (PPI) if chronic NSAID needed, prefer Paracetamol for simple pain.

Ayurvedic Antacids Are Genuinely Effective for Mild Disease: Yashtimadhu, Avipattikar Churna, and Shatavari have published evidence for gastroprotection and acid reduction. They are legitimate first-line options for mild gastritis/dyspepsia (Amlapitta) before escalating to PPIs.

Alarm Features = Urgent Endoscopy: Weight loss, GI bleeding, dysphagia, persistent vomiting, anemia, or new dyspepsia after age 55 MUST be evaluated by endoscopy to exclude gastric cancer. Do NOT assume it's 'just acidity'.

Long-Term PPI Risks: While PPIs are effective, prolonged use (>1 year) carries risks — vitamin B12 deficiency, magnesium depletion, increased fracture risk, C. difficile infection. Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration. Step down to H2 blockers or Ayurvedic alternatives when possible.

diseases.name: Burns
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Assessment (ATLS Approach)

Airway Assessment: Look for signs of inhalation injury — singed nasal hairs, soot in mouth, hoarse voice, stridor. If suspected, early intubation BEFORE edema develops (airway can obstruct within hours).

TBSA Estimation (Wallace Rule of 9s): Adults: head 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, perineum 1%. Children: Lund-Browder chart more accurate (head proportionally larger). Palm of patient's hand = ~1% TBSA. Accurate TBSA determines fluid resuscitation volume.

Burn Depth Assessment: Superficial (red, painful, blanches), Partial thickness (blisters, weeping, painful), Full thickness (painless, leathery/charred, doesn't blanch). Depth may evolve over 48-72 hours (burn wound conversion).

2. Laboratory Tests (For Major Burns >15-20% TBSA)

Complete Blood Count: Hemoconcentration initially (raised Hct from plasma loss). Anemia later from hemolysis and blood loss.

Serum Electrolytes: Hyperkalemia (from tissue destruction), hyponatremia (dilutional after resuscitation).

Renal Function: Monitor for AKI from hypovolemia, myoglobinuria (if associated electrical/crush injury).

Blood Group & Crossmatch: For anticipated blood product needs.

ABG: For inhalation injury — assess oxygenation, ventilation, carboxyhemoglobin level (CO poisoning from enclosed-space fires).

Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb): Elevated in smoke inhalation. >10% = significant exposure. >40% = severe poisoning. Standard pulse oximetry CANNOT detect CO — gives falsely normal SpO2.

3. Wound Assessment

Wound Swab Culture: When infection suspected (increased pain, purulent discharge, perilesional erythema, systemic sepsis). Common burn wound pathogens: Pseudomonas, Staphylococcus, Acinetobacter.

4. Key Criteria

Minor Burns: <10% TBSA partial thickness (adult), <5% (child). Outpatient management. Moderate: 10-20% TBSA or significant face/hands/feet/genitalia/joints involvement. Hospital admission. Major/Critical: >20% TBSA adult (>10% child), inhalation injury, electrical/chemical burns, burns with other injuries (polytrauma), circumferential burns. Burns centre transfer if possible.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Emergency First Aid (AT THE SCENE — Critical)

COOL the Burn: Run cool (NOT ice-cold) water over burn for 20 minutes within first 3 hours. This is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT first aid. Reduces pain, limits burn depth progression, and improves outcome. Works even if delayed up to 3 hours post-burn.

Remove Clothing/Jewelry: Remove clothing near burn (unless stuck to skin). Remove rings, bangles before swelling develops.

Cover with Clean Cloth: After cooling, cover with clean, non-fluffy material (clean sheet/cling film). Keep patient warm (hypothermia risk in large burns).

DO NOT Apply: Toothpaste, egg, potato, mud, butter, oil, ink, or any home remedy to burn wounds. These contaminate the wound, increase infection risk, and delay healing.

2. Fluid Resuscitation (>15-20% TBSA — Parkland Formula)

Ringer's Lactate: 4 ml × body weight (kg) × %TBSA. Give half in first 8 hours (from TIME OF BURN, not time of arrival), remaining half over next 16 hours. Titrate to urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr (adults), >1 ml/kg/hr (children). Start 2 large-bore IVs immediately.

3. Wound Care

Silver Sulfadiazine 1% Cream: Apply 2-3mm thick layer on partial-thickness burns. Change dressing daily. Broad-spectrum antimicrobial. First-line topical in Indian burns units.

Collagen Dressings / Amniotic Membrane: Biological dressings promoting healing. Increasingly available at burns centres.

Escharotomy: Emergent longitudinal incision through full-thickness circumferential eschar for limb/chest compartment release.

4. Pain Management

Paracetamol + NSAIDs: For mild-moderate burns.

Tramadol / Morphine: For moderate-severe pain. IV Morphine 0.1mg/kg for severe burns. Adequate pain control is often neglected.

Procedural Sedation: Ketamine IV for painful dressing changes.

5. Surgical Management

Tangential Excision & Skin Grafting: For deep partial and full-thickness burns. Early excision (within 5-7 days) reduces sepsis and mortality. Split-thickness skin graft (STSG) is standard.

Contracture Release & Reconstruction: Long-term reconstructive surgery for established contractures limiting function.

6. Prevention

LPG Transition: Support Ujjwala Yojana — LPG replaces dangerous kerosene stoves.

Safe Cooking Practices: Keep children away from cooking area. Stable stove placement. Never leave cooking unattended.

Fire Safety: Working smoke alarms. Keep water bucket near kitchen. Stop-drop-roll education.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Silver Sulfadiazine Cream 1%: First-line burn wound cream. Tube.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: Pain relief. Pack of 10.

Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: Anti-inflammatory analgesic. Pack of 10.

ORS Powder: For oral rehydration in minor burns.

Povidone-Iodine Solution 5%: Wound antiseptic.

Normal Saline 500ml: For wound irrigation.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Silver Sulfadiazine Cream 50g: ₹25.00

Povidone-Iodine 100ml: ₹18.00

Paracetamol 500mg, 10's: ₹3.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT NOTE — Burns Require Medical Treatment: Ayurvedic preparations should NOT be applied to fresh burn wounds. However, Ayurveda has established applications for burn healing and scar management:

Jatyadi Ghrita / Jatyadi Taila: Classical medicated ghee/oil for Vrana Ropana (wound healing). Contains Jati (Jasminum), Nimba (Neem), Haridra (Turmeric), Patola, Karanja. Published evidence for promoting wound healing. Apply on healing (NOT acute) burn wounds after 3-5 days.

Kumkumadi Taila: For post-burn SCAR management. Apply after wound has fully healed (epithelialized). Reduces hyperpigmentation and scar contracture.

Aloe Vera (Kumari/Ghritkumari): Cooling, soothing for superficial burns. Published evidence for burn wound healing. Apply fresh Aloe gel on first-degree (superficial) burns after cooling with water.

Honey (Madhu): Published evidence for burn wound healing — antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, promotes granulation. Medical-grade honey dressings used in modern wound care. Apply on superficial-partial thickness burns after initial cooling.

Coconut Oil: For healed burn scar moisturization and massage. Softens scar tissue. NOT on fresh/open wounds.
diseases.india_specific:
India — Highest Global Burn Burden: India accounts for ~1 million burn injuries annually, with ~1 lakh hospitalizations and ~25,000 deaths. Leading cause: open-flame cooking on kerosene stoves and chulhas. Women and children in kitchens are most vulnerable.

Dowry-Related Burns & Self-Immolation: India has a tragic burden of intentional burns — dowry-related 'stove bursting' (reported as 'accidents'), bride burning, and self-immolation (suicide). These carry extremely high mortality and represent a criminal/social crisis.

Kerosene & LPG Accidents: Kerosene stoves (still used by millions of households without LPG) cause frequent burns from spillage, explosion, and clothing catching fire. LPG cylinder leaks/explosions also cause severe burns. Ujjwala Yojana (LPG access) is a burn prevention strategy.

Acid Attacks: Concentrated acid thrown on face/body — causes devastating chemical burns with permanent disfigurement, blindness, and airway damage. India strengthened acid attack laws (Section 326A IPC) with minimum 10-year imprisonment.

Inadequate Burns Care Infrastructure: India has very few dedicated burns centres (<50 nationwide for 1.4 billion people). Most burns patients are treated at district hospitals with limited expertise, leading to high mortality and preventable disability.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL FIRST AID — Cool Running Water for 20 Minutes: This is the MOST IMPORTANT intervention for ANY burn. Run cool tap water over the burn for 20 minutes as soon as possible. Effective even up to 3 hours post-burn. Reduces pain, prevents deepening, and improves long-term outcome.

DO NOT Apply Toothpaste/Egg/Potato: These are harmful Indian myths. Toothpaste, egg, butter, ink, mustard oil, and potato CONTAMINATE the burn wound, introduce infection, and delay healing. They must be painfully removed at hospital, further damaging the wound. Cool water ONLY.

Aloe Vera & Honey — Evidence-Based for Minor Burns: Unlike most home remedies, Aloe vera and medical-grade honey have genuine published evidence for promoting superficial burn healing. They can be used for first-degree burns AFTER initial cooling with water.

Inhalation Injury = Hidden Killer: Any burn in an enclosed space (house fire, kerosene stove explosion) may have inhalation injury even if skin burns appear minor. Singed nasal hair, soot in mouth, hoarse voice = suspect inhalation injury → intubate early before airway swells shut.

Prevention — LPG Saves Lives: Kerosene stove fires are the leading cause of burns in Indian women. Ujjwala Yojana (free LPG connection for BPL households) is the most impactful burn prevention programme in India. Support LPG adoption.
confirmation_tests_curated: Airway Assessment: Look for signs of inhalation injury — singed nasal hairs, soot in mouth, hoarse voice, stridor. If suspected, early intubation BEFORE edema develops (airway can obstruct within hours).

TBSA Estimation (Wallace Rule of 9s): Adults: head 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, perineum 1%. Children: Lund-Browder chart more accurate (head proportionally larger). Palm of patient's hand = ~1% TBSA. Accurate TBSA determines fluid resuscitation volume.

Burn Depth Assessment: Superficial (red, painful, blanches), Partial thickness (blisters, weeping, painful), Full thickness (painless, leathery/charred, doesn't blanch). Depth may evolve over 48-72 hours (burn wound conversion).

diseases.name: Organophosphate Poisoning
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (TREAT IMMEDIATELY — Don't Wait for Labs)

Clinical Recognition: Cholinergic syndrome (miosis + excessive secretions + bradycardia + muscle fasciculations) + history/smell of pesticide exposure = OP poisoning. Start Atropine IMMEDIATELY on clinical grounds. Do NOT wait for lab confirmation.

2. Laboratory Confirmation

Serum Cholinesterase (Pseudocholinesterase/Butyrylcholinesterase): Low levels confirm OP exposure. Mild: 20-50% of normal. Moderate: 10-20%. Severe: <10%. Useful for diagnosis and monitoring recovery. Available at most hospital labs.

Red Cell Acetylcholinesterase (RBC-AChE): More specific than serum cholinesterase for assessing true enzyme inhibition. Less widely available.

3. Routine Blood Tests

ABG: Respiratory acidosis from hypoventilation. Metabolic acidosis from tissue hypoxia.

Serum Electrolytes, Renal Function, Liver Function: Baseline and monitoring.

Blood Glucose: Hyperglycemia common from sympathetic stimulation.

ECG: QTc prolongation, arrhythmias. Continuous monitoring essential.

4. Toxicology

Gastric Lavage Returns: Save and send for toxicological analysis if substance identity unknown.

Urine OP Metabolites: For confirming specific compound. Reference lab test.

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

OP Poisoning diagnosed CLINICALLY: Cholinergic crisis + exposure history. Serum cholinesterase confirms but should NOT delay treatment. Every minute of delay in Atropine and airway management increases mortality.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Immediate Resuscitation (ABC)

Airway & Breathing: Suction secretions aggressively. Intubate early if respiratory distress/failure. Mechanical ventilation often needed for hours-days. Oxygen via bag-mask while preparing intubation.

Decontamination: Remove clothing, wash entire body with soap and water. Healthcare workers must wear gloves to avoid secondary exposure. If ingested: do NOT induce vomiting. Gastric lavage ONLY within 1 hour of ingestion with protected airway.

2. Atropine — LIFE-SAVING Antidote

Atropine Protocol: 1.8-3mg IV bolus (adults), 0.05mg/kg (children). Repeat every 5 minutes, DOUBLING the dose each time until: (1) secretions dry up, (2) heart rate >80/min, (3) pupils dilate. May require massive doses — 10-100+ mg in first 24 hours for severe cases. Then continuous infusion titrated to dry secretions. Do NOT under-dose — inadequate Atropine kills. Target: dry secretions, NOT pupil size or heart rate.

3. Pralidoxime (PAM) — Oxime Reactivator

Pralidoxime (2-PAM): 1-2g IV over 15-30 minutes, then 500mg/hr continuous infusion for 24-48 hours (or repeated boluses every 4-6 hours). Reactivates inhibited AChE if given within 24-48 hours (before 'aging'). Most effective for nicotinic symptoms (fasciculations, weakness). Start alongside Atropine.

4. Seizure Management

Diazepam: 5-10mg IV for seizures. May need repeated doses. Midazolam continuous infusion for refractory seizures.

Avoid Succinylcholine: Contraindicated for intubation (prolonged paralysis due to impaired pseudocholinesterase). Use Rocuronium instead.

5. Monitoring & Complications

ICU Monitoring: Continuous ECG, SpO2, frequent ABG. Mechanical ventilation readiness. Monitor for Intermediate Syndrome (day 2-4: proximal muscle weakness → respiratory failure even after initial recovery).

OPIDN: Organophosphate-Induced Delayed Neuropathy — distal sensorimotor neuropathy developing 2-4 weeks post-exposure. Supportive treatment only.

6. Prevention

Pesticide Access Restriction: Lock pesticides away from children and distressed individuals. India has banned several highly toxic OPs (Monocrotophos, Phorate) but enforcement is poor.

Protective Equipment: Mask, gloves, full-body cover when spraying. Do NOT spray in windy conditions or towards face.

Poison Helpline: National Poison Information Centre (AIIMS) — 011-26589391.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Atropine Injection 0.6mg/ml: CRITICAL life-saving antidote. Must be stocked at ALL health facilities.

Pralidoxime (PAM) Injection 500mg: Oxime antidote. Available at district hospitals.

Diazepam Injection 5mg/ml: For seizure control.

Normal Saline 500ml: IV fluid resuscitation.

Note: Atropine and PAM must be available at EVERY PHC/CHC in agricultural districts. Stock adequacy should be audited regularly.
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Antidote for OP Poisoning: Organophosphate poisoning is an acute medical emergency requiring Atropine (antidote), airway management, and ICU care. There is NO Ayurvedic herb that reverses cholinesterase inhibition. Every minute spent on non-medical treatment increases mortality.

Post-Recovery Support: After hospital discharge, Ashwagandha (adaptogenic neuroprotective), Brahmi (cognitive recovery), and nutritious diet support neurological recovery from chronic OP effects.

Visha Chikitsa (Toxicology in Ayurveda): Ayurvedic texts describe Visha (poison) management principles including Shodhana (detoxification) and Shamana (pacification), but these are NOT applicable to acute OP poisoning which requires modern intensive care.
diseases.india_specific:
India — Global Epicenter of Pesticide Poisoning: India reports ~100,000-200,000 pesticide poisoning cases annually with ~25,000-30,000 deaths. Organophosphate compounds (Monocrotophos, Chlorpyrifos, Malathion, Phorate) are most common. Agricultural states (Maharashtra, AP, Telangana, Punjab, Kerala) have highest burden.

Suicide by Pesticide: Pesticide ingestion is the most common method of suicide in rural India. Easy availability of highly toxic pesticides in farming households enables impulsive self-harm. Farmer suicides frequently involve OP pesticides. Restricting access to toxic pesticides is the single most effective suicide prevention strategy.

Occupational Exposure: Farmers spraying pesticides without protective equipment (masks, gloves) absorb OP compounds through skin and inhalation. Chronic low-level exposure causes long-term neurological damage.

Children — Accidental Ingestion: Pesticides stored in unlabeled bottles, food containers, or accessible locations. Children accidentally ingest thinking it's food/drink. Proper storage in locked, labeled containers prevents this.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — Atropine Saves Lives: Every PHC/CHC in agricultural India must have adequate Atropine stock. A patient arriving with cholinergic crisis needs Atropine within minutes. Give 1.8-3mg IV immediately, double the dose every 5 minutes until secretions dry up. Under-dosing is the most common fatal error.

Do NOT Induce Vomiting: If OP was ingested, do NOT induce vomiting (aspiration risk with impaired consciousness and excessive secretions). Gastric lavage only with protected airway within 1 hour of ingestion.

Restrict Pesticide Access — Saves Lives: Storing pesticides in locked cabinets away from kitchens and living areas prevents both accidental childhood poisoning AND impulsive suicide. India's ban on highly toxic pesticides must be enforced.

Intermediate Syndrome — Second Wave: Even after initial recovery, patients may develop sudden respiratory failure at day 2-4 from proximal muscle weakness (Intermediate Syndrome). Keep all OP patients monitored in hospital for at least 5-7 days.

No Ayurvedic Treatment for Acute Poisoning: OP poisoning has a specific, proven antidote (Atropine + Pralidoxime) that must be given urgently. No herbal preparation can substitute. Any delay for alternative treatment is potentially fatal.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (TREAT IMMEDIATELY — Don't Wait for Labs)

Clinical Recognition: Cholinergic syndrome (miosis + excessive secretions + bradycardia + muscle fasciculations) + history/smell of pesticide exposure = OP poisoning. Start Atropine IMMEDIATELY on clinical grounds. Do NOT wait for lab confirmation.

Serum Cholinesterase (Pseudocholinesterase/Butyrylcholinesterase): Low levels confirm OP exposure. Mild: 20-50% of normal. Moderate: 10-20%. Severe: <10%. Useful for diagnosis and monitoring recovery. Available at most hospital labs.
Red Cell Acetylcholinesterase (RBC-AChE): More specific than serum cholinesterase for assessing true enzyme inhibition. Less widely available.

diseases.name: Preeclampsia & Eclampsia
diseases.tests:
1. Antenatal Screening (MOST IMPORTANT — Every Visit)

BP Measurement: Check at EVERY antenatal visit. ≥140/90 on 2 occasions 4 hours apart = hypertension in pregnancy. ≥160/110 = severe — urgent action.

Urine Dipstick for Protein: At every visit after 20 weeks. ≥1+ proteinuria = send for urine protein:creatinine ratio. Combined with HTN = preeclampsia.

2. Laboratory Tests (Once Preeclampsia Suspected)

Complete Blood Count: Platelet count — falling platelets (<150,000 and especially <100,000) indicate worsening preeclampsia/HELLP.

Liver Function Tests: AST/ALT elevation (>70 IU/L) = liver involvement. Part of HELLP criteria.

Renal Function (Creatinine): Rising creatinine indicates renal involvement. Normal pregnancy creatinine is <0.8 mg/dL — anything above 1.0 is concerning.

LDH: Elevated (>600 IU/L) in hemolysis/HELLP. Non-specific but useful marker.

Peripheral Smear: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) = microangiopathic hemolytic anemia of HELLP.

Uric Acid: Elevated (>6 mg/dL) — correlates with preeclampsia severity. Reflects renal dysfunction.

Coagulation Profile: PT/INR, APTT, Fibrinogen. DIC in severe HELLP.

24-Hour Urine Protein OR Spot Protein:Creatinine Ratio: Quantifies proteinuria. ≥300mg/24hr or PCR ≥0.3 confirms significant proteinuria.

3. Fetal Assessment

Non-Stress Test (NST/CTG): Fetal heart rate monitoring. Non-reassuring pattern indicates fetal compromise.

Ultrasound: Fetal growth (IUGR common in preeclampsia), amniotic fluid volume, umbilical artery Doppler (abnormal Doppler indicates placental insufficiency).

Biophysical Profile: Comprehensive fetal wellbeing assessment.

4. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Preeclampsia: HTN (≥140/90) + proteinuria (≥300mg/24hr) after 20 weeks gestation. OR HTN + any organ dysfunction (thrombocytopenia, renal impairment, liver involvement, pulmonary edema, cerebral/visual symptoms) without proteinuria. Severe Preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110 OR any organ dysfunction OR symptoms (headache, visual changes, epigastric pain). Eclampsia: Preeclampsia + seizures.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Magnesium Sulfate — LIFE-SAVING for Eclampsia Prevention & Treatment

MgSO4 (Pritchard Regimen — India Standard): Loading: 4g IV over 20 minutes + 5g IM each buttock (total 14g). Maintenance: 5g IM alternate buttock every 4 hours for 24 hours after delivery or last seizure. Monitor: patellar reflex (must be present), respiratory rate (must be >16/min), urine output (must be >25ml/hr). Antidote for toxicity: Calcium Gluconate 1g IV over 10 minutes.

MgSO4 (Zuspan Regimen): Loading: 4g IV over 20 minutes. Maintenance: 1g/hr IV continuous infusion. Preferred in settings with IV pump availability.

2. Antihypertensive Treatment

Labetalol (IV): 20mg IV bolus, repeat 40mg, 80mg every 10-15 minutes (max 300mg). First-line for acute severe hypertension (≥160/110) in pregnancy.

Nifedipine (Oral): 10-20mg oral, repeat after 30 minutes if needed. Alternative to IV Labetalol. Widely used in India due to oral route convenience.

Methyldopa (Oral): 250-500mg 2-3 times daily. First-line for chronic/maintenance antihypertensive in pregnancy. Safe long-term.

3. Definitive Treatment — DELIVERY

Delivery Is the ONLY Cure: Preeclampsia resolves ONLY after delivery of placenta. Timing depends on gestational age and severity: Severe preeclampsia at ≥34 weeks → deliver. Severe preeclampsia <34 weeks → stabilize with MgSO4 + antihypertensives, corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, then deliver within 24-48 hours.

4. Supportive Care

Corticosteroids for Fetal Lung Maturity: Dexamethasone 6mg IM × 4 doses 12 hours apart (or Betamethasone 12mg IM × 2 doses 24 hours apart) if <34 weeks gestation. Accelerates fetal lung maturation before preterm delivery.

5. Postpartum Monitoring

Continue MgSO4 for 24 hours postpartum. Monitor BP for at least 72 hours (eclampsia can occur postpartum). Antihypertensives as needed. Follow up at 6 weeks for BP resolution.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Magnesium Sulfate Injection 50% (2ml/5ml ampoules): ESSENTIAL life-saving drug. Must be available at ALL delivery points.

Nifedipine Tablets IP 10mg: Oral antihypertensive for preeclampsia. Pack of 10.

Methyldopa Tablets IP 250mg: Maintenance antihypertensive in pregnancy. Pack of 10.

Calcium Gluconate Injection 10%: MgSO4 toxicity antidote.

Urine Protein Dipsticks: For antenatal screening.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Nifedipine 10mg, 10's: ₹5.00

Methyldopa 250mg, 10's: ₹12.00

Note: MgSO4 injection should be available FREE at all government delivery facilities.
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CRITICAL NOTE — Eclampsia Is a Medical Emergency: Preeclampsia/eclampsia requires MgSO4, antihypertensives, and timely delivery. NO Ayurvedic herb can substitute for these interventions. Delay for alternative treatment risks maternal and fetal death.

Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina): Has traditional antihypertensive use but is NOT safe in pregnancy (uterotonic effects). DO NOT use for gestational hypertension.

Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic but NOT studied for safety in preeclampsia. Avoid during active preeclampsia.

Jatamansi: Mild antihypertensive but insufficient for preeclampsia management. Not recommended.

Post-Delivery Recovery: After delivery and stabilization, Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus — galactagogue and Rasayana), Dashamoola Kwatha (anti-inflammatory for postpartum recovery), and nutritious diet support maternal recovery.

Dietary Prevention: Adequate calcium intake (1g/day — WHO recommendation for low-calcium-diet populations like India) during pregnancy may reduce preeclampsia risk by 50%. Calcium supplementation is recommended by WHO for all pregnant women in India.
diseases.india_specific:
Major Cause of Maternal Mortality in India: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy account for ~10% of maternal deaths in India — the third leading cause after hemorrhage and sepsis. India contributes the largest absolute number of eclampsia deaths globally.

Late Antenatal Booking & Irregular Visits: Many Indian women, especially in rural areas, book late for antenatal care (>20 weeks) and attend irregularly. BP and urine protein screening at each visit would detect preeclampsia early — but these visits are often missed.

MgSO4 Availability & Training Gap: Magnesium Sulfate is the life-saving drug for eclampsia prevention and treatment. Despite being on India's Essential Drug List, MgSO4 is unavailable at ~30-40% of Indian PHCs/CHCs. Many health workers are not trained to administer it.

Home Deliveries & Delayed Referral: 10-15% of Indian deliveries still occur at home. Eclampsia at home with delayed transport to hospital carries very high mortality. Janani Suraksha Yojana incentivizes institutional delivery.

Adolescent Pregnancies: Girls <18 (child marriages, still prevalent in parts of India despite legal prohibition) have higher preeclampsia risk due to physiological immaturity.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — MgSO4 at Every Delivery Point: Magnesium Sulfate prevents 50% of eclamptic seizures and treats established eclampsia. EVERY health facility conducting deliveries MUST have MgSO4 and staff trained to administer it. Its absence at delivery points is the primary driver of preventable eclampsia deaths in India.

Check BP & Urine at EVERY Antenatal Visit: Preeclampsia is detected by simple BP measurement + urine dipstick — both feasible at subcenter level. Missing these checks means missing preeclampsia until seizures occur. ASHA workers can be trained for basic BP screening.

Delivery Is the Only Cure: No medicine — allopathic or Ayurvedic — cures preeclampsia. Only delivery of the placenta resolves the condition. Timing of delivery balances maternal safety against fetal maturity.

No Ayurvedic Treatment for Preeclampsia: This is a life-threatening pregnancy complication with maternal mortality of 1-5% for eclampsia. Sarpagandha and Jatamansi are NOT safe or effective in this context. Modern obstetric management (MgSO4 + antihypertensives + timely delivery) is the only approach.

Calcium Supplementation in Pregnancy: WHO recommends 1.5-2g/day calcium for all pregnant women in low-calcium-diet populations (most of India). This simple, cheap intervention reduces preeclampsia risk by ~50%. Include alongside iron-folic acid supplementation.
confirmation_tests_curated: BP Measurement: Check at EVERY antenatal visit. ≥140/90 on 2 occasions 4 hours apart = hypertension in pregnancy. ≥160/110 = severe — urgent action.
Urine Dipstick for Protein: At every visit after 20 weeks. ≥1+ proteinuria = send for urine protein:creatinine ratio. Combined with HTN = preeclampsia.
Complete Blood Count: Platelet count — falling platelets (<150,000 and especially <100,000) indicate worsening preeclampsia/HELLP.

diseases.name: Sickle Cell Disease
diseases.tests:
1. Screening & Diagnosis

Solubility Test (Sickling Test): Simple screening test — hemoglobin in reducing solution (sodium dithionite). Turbid = HbS present. Cannot distinguish trait (HbAS) from disease (HbSS). Point-of-care test for community screening under NSCEM.

Hemoglobin Electrophoresis / HPLC: Definitive diagnostic test. HbSS: >80% HbS, absent HbA. HbAS (trait): 30-40% HbS, >50% HbA. Also identifies HbSC, HbS-Beta Thalassemia. Available at district hospital level.

Newborn Screening (Heel Prick): IEF (Isoelectric Focusing) or HPLC on dried blood spot from heel prick. Identifies SCD at birth — enables early intervention. Being scaled up under NSCEM.

2. Crisis Assessment

Complete Blood Count: Baseline Hb 6-10 g/dL (HbSS). During crisis: may drop further. Reticulocyte count elevated (compensatory). Leukocytosis suggests infection triggering crisis.

Peripheral Blood Smear: Sickle-shaped RBCs, target cells, Howell-Jolly bodies (functional asplenia). Characteristic morphology.

Chest X-ray: For Acute Chest Syndrome — new pulmonary infiltrate involving at least one complete lung segment.

Blood Culture: For febrile episodes to identify bacterial sepsis (functional asplenia makes SCD patients vulnerable to overwhelming infection).

Transcranial Doppler (TCD): Annual screening in children 2-16 years. Cerebral blood flow velocity >200 cm/sec = high stroke risk → start chronic transfusion programme. Life-saving screening.

3. Key Diagnostic Criteria

SCD confirmed by: Hb electrophoresis/HPLC showing HbSS (>80% HbS, absent HbA), or HbSC, or HbS-Beta Thalassemia. Sickle Cell Trait (carrier): HbAS with 30-40% HbS — carriers are generally asymptomatic but can pass the gene to children.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Disease-Modifying Therapy

Hydroxyurea: 15-35mg/kg/day. THE most important drug for SCD. Increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) which prevents sickling. Reduces VOC frequency by 50%, reduces ACS, reduces transfusion need, reduces mortality. Should be offered to ALL SCD patients from age 9 months. Monitor CBC monthly (can cause myelosuppression).

2. Pain Crisis Management

Paracetamol + NSAIDs: First-line for mild-moderate pain. Ibuprofen 400-600mg every 8 hours + Paracetamol 1g every 6 hours.

Tramadol: 50-100mg every 6 hours for moderate pain.

Morphine/Hydromorphone: For severe VOC. Morphine 0.1-0.15mg/kg IV every 3-4 hours. Patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) in hospital. Do NOT under-treat pain — SCD pain is real and severe.

IV Fluids: Aggressive hydration (1.5x maintenance) to reduce sickling. NS or D5-0.45NS.

3. Infection Prevention

Penicillin V Prophylaxis: 125mg twice daily (under 5 years), 250mg twice daily (over 5 years). Continue until at least age 5. Prevents overwhelming pneumococcal sepsis from functional asplenia. Compliance is life-saving.

Vaccinations: Pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23), Meningococcal, H. influenzae b, annual Influenza. CRITICAL given functional asplenia.

Malaria Prophylaxis: In endemic areas, insecticide-treated bed nets and prompt malaria treatment are essential. SCD patients with malaria have higher mortality.

4. Acute Chest Syndrome

Oxygen + Antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin) + Exchange transfusion if severe. Incentive spirometry preventive in hospitalized patients.

5. Blood Transfusion

Simple Transfusion: For severe anemia (Hb <5 g/dL), aplastic crisis, splenic sequestration. Target Hb 10 g/dL — do NOT over-transfuse (hyperviscosity).

Exchange Transfusion: For acute stroke, severe ACS, multiorgan failure. Reduces HbS% to <30%.

6. Curative Treatment

Bone Marrow/Stem Cell Transplant: Only CURE for SCD. Matched sibling donor required. 85-90% cure rate. Limited availability in India — few centres (CMC Vellore, TMH Mumbai, SGPGI Lucknow).

Gene Therapy: Emerging curative option. Clinical trials ongoing globally. Not yet available in India.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Hydroxyurea Capsules IP 500mg: Disease-modifying therapy. Pack of 10.

Folic Acid Tablets IP 5mg: For chronic hemolysis. Pack of 10.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: Pain management. Pack of 10.

Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: Pain/anti-inflammatory. Pack of 10.

Penicillin V Tablets IP 250mg: Infection prophylaxis. Pack of 10.

Iron + Folic Acid: For associated nutritional deficiency.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Hydroxyurea 500mg, 10's: ₹30.00

Folic Acid 5mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Penicillin V 250mg, 10's: ₹8.00
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia): Immunomodulatory Rasayana. Supports immune recovery between crises. 500mg-1g twice daily.

Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic, anti-stress. May support general health and reduce stress-triggered crises. 300-600mg twice daily.

Turmeric (Curcuma longa): Anti-inflammatory. Curcumin may reduce chronic inflammation in SCD. 500mg-1g daily.

Punarnava (Boerhaavia diffusa): Classical for Pandu (anemia) and Shotha (swelling). May support renal health. 3-5g twice daily.

Punarnavadi Mandura: Classical formulation for Pandu with iron (Mandura Bhasma). Supports hemoglobin levels. 2 tablets twice daily. Practitioner supervision.

Wheat Grass Juice: Some patients report reduced crisis frequency with regular wheat grass juice. Anecdotal evidence — no RCT confirmation. 30ml daily.
diseases.india_specific:
India's Tribal Belt — Massive Burden: India has the world's second-largest SCD population after Africa. Estimated 1.5-2 crore carriers (HbAS trait) and 14-20 lakh SCD patients. Concentrated in tribal populations of Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Jharkhand, Odisha, Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Kerala.

National Sickle Cell Anaemia Elimination Mission (NSCEM): Launched by PM Modi in July 2023. India's first dedicated national programme for SCD — targets universal screening, early diagnosis, and comprehensive care. Aims for elimination of SCD as a public health problem by 2047.

Newborn Screening Gap: Despite NSCEM, universal newborn screening for SCD is still being rolled out. Many children are diagnosed only when they present with first crisis (6 months-2 years). Early diagnosis enables prophylactic Penicillin and Hydroxyurea.

Malaria Protection Paradox: Sickle trait (HbAS) provides ~50% protection against severe falciparum malaria. This explains the high HbS gene frequency in malaria-endemic tribal areas. However, SCD (HbSS) itself carries higher malaria mortality.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Hydroxyurea for ALL SCD Patients: Hydroxyurea reduces pain crises by 50%, prevents organ damage, and extends life. It should be offered to EVERY SCD patient from age 9 months. Under-utilization of Hydroxyurea is the biggest treatment gap in Indian SCD care.

Penicillin Prophylaxis Saves Young Lives: Functional asplenia makes SCD children extremely vulnerable to fatal pneumococcal sepsis. Daily Penicillin V from diagnosis until at least age 5 prevents this. Compliance must be emphasized at every visit.

NSCEM — Screen All Tribal Newborns: The National Sickle Cell Anaemia Elimination Mission provides free screening and treatment. Ensure all newborns in tribal/endemic areas are screened. Early diagnosis → early Hydroxyurea + Penicillin → better outcomes.

Ayurveda Cannot Replace Hydroxyurea: While Guduchi and Ashwagandha support general health, they cannot prevent sickling, reduce crises, or prevent organ damage the way Hydroxyurea does. Use Ayurvedic herbs as complementary support alongside standard medical management.

Pain Management — Don't Under-Treat: SCD pain crises are among the most severe pain experiences. Adequate analgesia (including opioids for severe crises) is a patient right. Undertreated pain is inhumane and drives patients to self-medicate dangerously.

diseases.name: Thalassemia
diseases.tests:
1. Screening & Diagnosis

Complete Blood Count + Peripheral Smear: Microcytic hypochromic anemia (low MCV <70 fL, low MCH <25 pg). Target cells, basophilic stippling on smear. Mentzer Index: MCV/RBC count — <13 suggests thalassemia, >13 suggests iron deficiency.

Hemoglobin Electrophoresis / HPLC: Definitive. Thal Major (β0/β0): HbF 60-90%, HbA2 variable, HbA absent/minimal. Thal Intermedia: HbF 20-60%. Thal Trait (carrier): HbA2 >3.5% (typically 4-7%), mild microcytosis. HbE-Beta Thal: HbE + HbF predominant. Available at district hospital level.

Genetic/DNA Analysis: For precise mutation identification. Useful for prenatal diagnosis and genetic counseling. PCR-based detection of common Indian mutations (IVS1-5, 619bp deletion, IVS1-1, Codon 41/42).

2. Iron Overload Monitoring

Serum Ferritin: Every 3 months. Target <1000 ng/mL with chelation. >2500 = high risk of cardiac complications.

Liver Iron Concentration (LIC) by MRI: T2* or R2 MRI quantifies liver iron. Gold standard for iron loading. Available at tertiary centres.

Cardiac T2* MRI: Measures cardiac iron. T2* <20ms = cardiac iron loading. <10ms = high risk of heart failure/arrhythmia. Annual screening from age 10.

3. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Thalassemia Major: Transfusion-dependent anemia (Hb <7 without transfusion), elevated HbF, absent/reduced HbA on electrophoresis, onset <2 years. Thalassemia Intermedia: Anemia not requiring regular transfusions (Hb 7-10), moderate HbF elevation. Thalassemia Trait (Carrier): Asymptomatic, mild microcytic anemia (Hb 10-12), elevated HbA2 (>3.5%).
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Transfusion Therapy (Lifeline for Thal Major)

Regular Red Cell Transfusions: Every 2-4 weeks to maintain pre-transfusion Hb 9.5-10.5 g/dL. Leukoreduced, phenotype-matched packed RBCs. Extended phenotyping (Rh, Kell) to minimize alloimmunization. Lifelong unless cured by transplant.

2. Iron Chelation (EQUALLY IMPORTANT as Transfusion)

Deferasirox (Oral): 20-40mg/kg/day once daily dispersible tablet. First-line oral chelator. Convenient, good adherence. Monitor renal and hepatic function. Most prescribed chelator in India.

Deferoxamine (SC Infusion): 25-50mg/kg/day via subcutaneous infusion pump 8-12 hours/day, 5-7 days/week. Gold standard efficacy but cumbersome. Used for cardiac iron loading or when oral chelators fail.

Deferiprone (Oral): 75-100mg/kg/day in 3 doses. Particularly effective for cardiac iron removal. Can cause agranulocytosis — monitor CBC weekly. Often combined with Deferoxamine for severe iron overload.

3. Supportive Care

Folic Acid: 5mg daily. For increased demand from chronic hemolysis.

Calcium + Vitamin D: For bone health (osteoporosis risk).

Endocrine Monitoring: Annual assessment for growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism, diabetes, hypogonadism. Hormone replacement as needed.

Vaccinations: Hepatitis B (transfusion risk), Pneumococcal, annual Influenza.

4. Curative Treatment

Bone Marrow Transplant (BMT): Only CURE. Matched sibling donor gives 80-90% cure rate (Pesaro Class I-II). Haploidentical transplant expanding access. Centres in India: CMC Vellore, Tata Memorial, AIIMS, SGPGI.

Gene Therapy: Emerging curative option (Exagamglogene autotemcel/Casgevy approved globally). Not yet available in India but represents future hope.

5. Prevention (Most Impactful)

Premarital/Prenatal Screening: Screen both partners. If both are carriers → 25% chance of affected child. Offer prenatal diagnosis (CVS at 10-12 weeks) and genetic counseling. Can prevent 100% of new cases.

National Thalassemia Screening: Some states (Gujarat, Maharashtra) have mandatory premarital screening programmes.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Folic Acid Tablets IP 5mg: For chronic hemolysis. Pack of 10.

Deferasirox Dispersible Tablets 250/500mg: Oral iron chelator. (Available through government thalassemia programmes.)

Calcium + Vitamin D3 Tablets: Bone health support.

Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For transfusion-related fever.

Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):

Folic Acid 5mg, 10's: ₹3.00

Calcium + D3 tablets, 10's: ₹8.00

Note: Transfusions and chelation drugs provided free/subsidized under Thalassemia Bal Sewa Yojana and various state programmes.
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Punarnavadi Mandura: Classical for Pandu (anemia). Iron-containing preparation with Punarnava. May support hemoglobin in Thal Intermedia. 2 tablets twice daily. Practitioner supervision essential — avoid if iron overloaded.

Wheat Grass Juice (Godhuma Ankura Rasa): Published Indian studies show 0.5-1 g/dL Hb increase and reduced transfusion frequency in some thalassemia patients. 30-60ml fresh juice daily. Evidence is promising but not definitive — encourage as adjunct, not replacement.

Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic support for chronic illness fatigue. 300-600mg twice daily.

Guduchi/Giloy: Immunomodulatory. Supports immunity between transfusions. 500mg-1g twice daily.

IMPORTANT — Avoid Iron-Containing Ayurvedic Preparations in Thal Major: Thalassemia major patients are already IRON OVERLOADED from transfusions. Lauha Bhasma, Navayasa Churna, and other iron-containing Ayurvedic preparations can WORSEN iron overload and accelerate organ damage. These are CONTRAINDICATED.
diseases.india_specific:
India — Largest Global Burden: India has 1-1.5 crore thalassemia carriers (HbE-Beta Thal common in NE India, Beta-Thal in Gujarat, Sindhi communities, Maharashtra, UP). 10,000-15,000 thalassemia major children born annually. Without adequate transfusion and chelation, most die by age 20-30.

Consanguineous Marriage & Carrier Frequency: High carrier rates (3-17% in different communities) combined with consanguineous/endogamous marriage practices maintain high disease incidence. Premarital screening can prevent affected births.

Blood Supply Challenge: Each thalassemia major child needs 200-300 units of blood in lifetime. India's blood supply (1.1 crore units/year) is already insufficient. Regular safe blood access is the biggest practical challenge.

Cost of Care: Estimated lifetime cost: ₹40-80 lakhs for transfusion + chelation. Bone marrow transplant: ₹10-20 lakhs. Economically devastating for families. Government support programmes (Thalassemia Bal Sewa Yojana) provide partial coverage.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Chelation Is As Important As Transfusion: Regular blood transfusions save lives but cause fatal iron overload. Without chelation therapy, iron deposits destroy the heart, liver, and endocrine organs — killing patients by age 20-30. Chelation (Deferasirox daily) must accompany EVERY transfusion programme.

NEVER Give Iron Supplements to Thal Major: Thalassemia major patients are already iron-overloaded. Iron tablets, iron-containing Ayurvedic preparations (Lauha Bhasma, Mandura), and iron-rich tonics are HARMFUL and potentially fatal. This is a critical counseling point.

Premarital Screening Prevents New Cases: If both partners are carriers, 25% of children will have Thal Major. A simple HPLC test before marriage identifies carriers. Genetic counseling empowers informed reproductive choices. Some Indian states mandate premarital thalassemia screening.

Wheat Grass Juice — Promising Adjunct: Indian studies show modest transfusion-interval extension with regular wheat grass juice. Encourage as nutritional supplement alongside standard treatment — NOT as replacement for transfusions.

Bone Marrow Transplant = Only Cure: BMT with matched sibling donor cures 80-90% of Thal Major patients. Families should be counseled about HLA typing of siblings. Thalassemia Bal Sewa Yojana provides financial support for transplant.

diseases.name: Mucormycosis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Suspicion (Critical for Early Diagnosis)

Clinical Red Flags: Unilateral facial pain/swelling/nasal discharge/proptosis in a diabetic/immunosuppressed patient = suspect mucormycosis. Black eschar on nasal/palatal examination is near-pathognomonic. DO NOT wait for biopsy results to start treatment.

2. Tissue Diagnosis (Definitive)

Nasal/Palatal Biopsy with KOH + Histopathology: Biopsy of necrotic tissue. KOH mount shows broad, ribbon-like, pauci-septate hyphae with 90° branching (distinguishes from Aspergillus which has acute-angle branching). Histopathology shows tissue invasion and vascular thrombosis with fungal elements. DIAGNOSTIC.

Fungal Culture: Tissue culture on SDA. Growth of Rhizopus/Mucor species. Takes 3-7 days. Culture negative in ~50% — negative culture does NOT rule out mucormycosis.

3. Imaging

CT Paranasal Sinuses: Sinus opacification, bone erosion, orbital extension. Essential for surgical planning.

MRI Brain + Orbits (With Contrast): Evaluates extent of spread — orbital, intracranial, cavernous sinus. Black turbinate sign on MRI (non-enhancing turbinate = infarcted tissue).

4. Blood Tests

Blood Glucose & HbA1c: Hyperglycemia/undiagnosed DM in vast majority. Aggressive glucose control essential alongside antifungal therapy.

Renal Function: Baseline and monitoring (Amphotericin B is nephrotoxic).

5. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Proven Mucormycosis: Histopathological evidence of tissue invasion by Mucorales hyphae + clinical features. Probable: Compatible clinical features + positive culture/KOH without histopathological invasion demonstrated. Possible: Compatible clinical features in high-risk patient pending investigation. In India during COVID epidemic, clinical suspicion was sufficient to start treatment.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antifungal Therapy (START IMMEDIATELY)

Liposomal Amphotericin B (L-AmB): 5-10mg/kg/day IV. FIRST-LINE per ICMR guidelines. Less nephrotoxic than conventional AmB. Duration: 4-6 weeks minimum. Total dose guided by clinical response and imaging.

Amphotericin B Deoxycholate (Conventional): 1-1.5mg/kg/day IV if L-AmB unavailable. More nephrotoxic — aggressive IV hydration + potassium/magnesium supplementation essential. Was the workhorse during India's COVID mucormycosis epidemic when L-AmB supply was exhausted.

Posaconazole: 300mg IV/oral twice on day 1, then 300mg daily. Step-down therapy after initial AmB. Also for patients unable to tolerate AmB. Oral formulation enables outpatient continuation.

Isavuconazole: 200mg IV/oral thrice daily for 6 doses, then 200mg daily. Alternative to Posaconazole.

2. Surgical Debridement (EQUALLY CRITICAL)

Aggressive Surgical Debridement: SURGERY IS ESSENTIAL — antifungals alone are insufficient. Remove ALL necrotic tissue. May require: endoscopic sinus surgery, maxillectomy, orbital exenteration (if orbit involved and vision lost), palatal resection. Repeated debridements often needed. Surgery + antifungal together = best survival.

3. Control Underlying Risk Factors

Aggressive Glycemic Control: Blood glucose 140-180 mg/dL target. Insulin infusion. Correct DKA. This is as important as antifungal therapy.

Reduce Immunosuppression: Taper/stop steroids. Reduce other immunosuppressive medications where possible.

4. Supportive Care

Renal Monitoring: Serum creatinine, potassium, magnesium daily during AmB therapy. Aggressive hydration (1L NS pre-infusion).

Nutritional Support: High-protein diet. NG feeding if palatal/oral involvement prevents eating.

Rehabilitation: Post-surgical prosthetics (obturator for palatal defects), ocular prosthetics. Psychological support for disfigurement.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Note: Mucormycosis treatment drugs (Liposomal Amphotericin B, Posaconazole) are specialized medications typically available at tertiary hospitals, not standard Jan Aushadhi products. During the 2021 epidemic, the Indian Government ensured centralized procurement and distribution.

Insulin Vials/Pens: For aggressive glycemic control. Available at Jan Aushadhi.

Potassium Chloride Syrup: For AmB-induced hypokalemia.
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Treatment for Mucormycosis: Mucormycosis is an aggressive, rapidly fatal fungal infection requiring IV antifungals + surgery. NO herbal preparation can treat invasive mucormycosis. Ayurvedic consultation during active mucormycosis causes fatal delay.

Post-Treatment Recovery Support: After completing antifungal course and surgical healing, immunomodulatory Rasayanas (Ashwagandha, Guduchi, Chyawanprash) may support general immune recovery.

Diabetes Management (Ayurvedic Adjunct): Gudmar, Vijaysar, and Karela can support glycemic control alongside insulin/oral antidiabetics — helping prevent mucormycosis recurrence by maintaining better sugar control.
diseases.india_specific:
India's COVID-19 Mucormycosis Epidemic: India experienced an unprecedented mucormycosis epidemic during COVID-19 second wave (May-July 2021) — reporting >47,000 cases (declared epidemic/notifiable disease). Caused by Rhizopus/Mucor species invading immunosuppressed, hyperglycemic COVID patients.

Diabetes — Primary Risk Factor: Uncontrolled diabetes (especially DKA) is the leading risk factor in India — present in 70-80% of mucormycosis cases. Hyperglycemia + acidosis + elevated free iron create ideal conditions for Mucorales growth. India's massive diabetes burden (101M) provides the substrate.

Steroid Overuse in COVID: Inappropriate/excessive corticosteroid use during COVID-19 (Dexamethasone given too early, too high dose, or to mild cases) caused immunosuppression + steroid-induced hyperglycemia — the perfect storm for mucormycosis.

Poor Glycemic Control During Hospitalization: Many Indian COVID patients had undiagnosed diabetes or poorly controlled sugar during hospitalization. Lack of blood glucose monitoring in overwhelmed COVID wards contributed to the epidemic.
diseases.important_notes:
ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — Surgery + Antifungal Together: Mucormycosis has 50-80% mortality without treatment. Antifungals ALONE are insufficient — surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue is essential. Delay in either antifungals or surgery dramatically increases mortality.

Control Blood Sugar — The Root Cause: Uncontrolled diabetes is present in 70-80% of Indian mucormycosis cases. Aggressive insulin therapy to maintain blood glucose 140-180 mg/dL is as important as antifungal treatment. Without sugar control, antifungals cannot work effectively.

Do NOT Overuse Steroids: The COVID-19 mucormycosis epidemic was largely iatrogenic — caused by inappropriate steroid use. Dexamethasone should ONLY be used in hospitalized COVID patients requiring oxygen. Never in mild/moderate COVID. Never for prophylaxis.

No Ayurvedic Substitute: Mucormycosis kills within days-weeks without IV Amphotericin B + surgery. There is no herbal antifungal that can treat this invasive infection. Ayurvedic herbs for post-treatment recovery only.

Black Eschar = Act Immediately: Any black discoloration on nasal mucosa or palate in a diabetic/immunosuppressed patient must be biopsied and treated as mucormycosis until proven otherwise. Hours matter.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Red Flags: Unilateral facial pain/swelling/nasal discharge/proptosis in a diabetic/immunosuppressed patient = suspect mucormycosis. Black eschar on nasal/palatal examination is near-pathognomonic. DO NOT wait for biopsy results to start treatment.

Nasal/Palatal Biopsy with KOH + Histopathology: Biopsy of necrotic tissue. KOH mount shows broad, ribbon-like, pauci-septate hyphae with 90° branching (distinguishes from Aspergillus which has acute-angle branching). Histopathology shows tissue invasion and vascular thrombosis with fungal elements. DIAGNOSTIC.
Fungal Culture: Tissue culture on SDA. Growth of Rhizopus/Mucor species. Takes 3-7 days. Culture negative in ~50% — negative culture does NOT rule out mucormycosis.

diseases.name: Acute Otitis Media
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Examination (Primary Diagnostic Method)

Otoscopic Examination: Visualization of tympanic membrane. Diagnostic findings: bulging, erythema, opacification, decreased mobility, fluid level, perforation with discharge. EVERY health facility should have an otoscope. Train PHC doctors and ANMs in otoscopy.

Pneumatic Otoscopy: Assesses tympanic membrane mobility. Decreased mobility = middle ear effusion. More accurate than simple otoscopy.

2. Additional Tests (When Indicated)

Tympanometry: Objective measurement of middle ear pressure and compliance. Type B (flat) tympanogram = middle ear effusion. Available at ENT clinics.

Pure Tone Audiometry: For children with recurrent/chronic OM — assess hearing loss. Available at district hospital ENT departments.

Ear Discharge Culture & Sensitivity: For CSOM or AOM not responding to first-line antibiotics. Identifies pathogen (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis, P. aeruginosa in CSOM) and guides targeted therapy.

3. Imaging

CT Temporal Bone: Only for suspected complications — mastoiditis, intracranial extension (rare).

4. Key Diagnostic Criteria

AOM diagnosed by: (1) Acute onset of symptoms (ear pain, irritability, fever), (2) Middle ear effusion (bulging TM, decreased mobility, otorrhea), (3) Signs of middle ear inflammation (erythema, otalgia). AAP criteria: moderate-severe bulging of TM, or mild bulging + recent ear pain or intense erythema, or new otorrhea not from otitis externa.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Pain Management (PRIORITY)

Paracetamol: 15mg/kg every 4-6 hours. FIRST intervention for AOM — pain relief is the immediate priority, not antibiotics.

Ibuprofen: 10mg/kg every 6-8 hours. Anti-inflammatory. Can alternate with Paracetamol for severe pain.

Topical Analgesic Ear Drops (Lignocaine + Phenazone): 3-4 drops in affected ear for rapid local pain relief. Available as Otocalm/Otalgia drops. Effective for 20-30 minutes.

2. Antibiotic Therapy

Amoxicillin: 80-90mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses for 7-10 days. FIRST-LINE antibiotic when indicated. High-dose to cover resistant Pneumococcus.

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate: 90/6.4mg/kg/day for treatment failure after 48-72 hours on Amoxicillin. Covers beta-lactamase producing H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis.

Ceftriaxone IM: 50mg/kg single dose for 3 days. For vomiting child unable to take oral antibiotics, or treatment failure.

Azithromycin: 10mg/kg day 1, then 5mg/kg days 2-5. For Penicillin-allergic patients.

3. When to Give Antibiotics vs Watch & Wait

Immediate Antibiotics: All children <6 months, severe symptoms (temp ≥39°C, severe pain, bilateral AOM), otorrhea. Watch & Wait (48-72 hours): Children ≥2 years with mild unilateral AOM without otorrhea — reassess if not improving. Many resolve spontaneously.

4. CSOM Management

Topical Antibiotic-Steroid Ear Drops: Ciprofloxacin + Dexamethasone ear drops for CSOM with active discharge. Aural toilet (dry mopping) before instillation.

Surgical: Tympanoplasty/Myringoplasty for CSOM with persistent perforation after infection control. Available at district hospital ENT.

5. Prevention

Breastfeeding: Exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months reduces AOM risk by 50%.

Pneumococcal Vaccine (PCV13): Reduces pneumococcal AOM by 30%. Part of India's UIP.

Avoid Bottle-Propping: Never feed infant lying flat with bottle — milk enters Eustachian tube.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Karpoora / Camphor + Neem Oil: Traditional ear pain remedy — warm (NOT hot) neem oil with dissolved camphor (tiny amount), 1-2 drops in ear. Provides mild analgesic and antiseptic effect. Use with caution — only if eardrum is intact (no discharge).

Garlic Oil (Lahsun Taila): Crushed garlic infused in warm sesame oil. 1-2 drops in ear. Traditional analgesic. Published evidence for antibacterial properties. Use only if no perforation.

Tulsi Juice: 2-3 drops of fresh Tulsi leaf juice in ear — traditional remedy. Antimicrobial.

Warm Sesame Oil: Plain warm sesame oil 2-3 drops in ear for pain relief. Traditional ear care.

Sarivadi Vati: Classical formulation for Karnasrava (ear discharge). 1-2 tablets twice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Extremely Common in Indian Children: AOM affects 50-80% of children by age 3. Peak incidence: 6-24 months. Indian prevalence studies show 5-10% of school children have chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM) — one of the highest rates globally.

CSOM & Preventable Hearing Loss: Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media (persistent ear discharge >2 weeks through perforated eardrum) affects millions of Indian children. Untreated CSOM causes permanent conductive hearing loss — a major cause of learning disability in India.

Antibiotic Overuse: AOM is the leading reason for antibiotic prescription in Indian children. Many cases are viral (resolve spontaneously in 48-72 hours) but routinely receive antibiotics. AAP/NICE guidelines allow 48-72 hour 'watchful waiting' for mild AOM in children >2 years.

Malnutrition & Overcrowding: Malnutrition, indoor air pollution, overcrowded living, and lack of breastfeeding increase AOM risk and chronicity in Indian children.
diseases.important_notes:
CRITICAL — Pain Relief First: The FIRST priority in AOM is pain management (Paracetamol/Ibuprofen), NOT antibiotics. Many parents and doctors skip pain relief and go straight to antibiotics — the child suffers unnecessarily for 24-48 hours until antibiotics take effect.

Not All Ear Infections Need Antibiotics: Many AOM episodes (especially in children >2 years with mild symptoms) resolve spontaneously in 48-72 hours. Watchful waiting with pain management is appropriate per international guidelines. This reduces unnecessary antibiotic use.

CSOM = Preventable Hearing Loss: Chronic ear discharge in children MUST be treated properly (aural toilet + antibiotic ear drops). Neglected CSOM causes permanent hearing loss affecting learning, speech development, and lifetime potential.

Traditional Ear Oil Remedies — Use With Caution: Garlic oil and warm sesame oil are traditional Indian remedies with some analgesic benefit. However, NEVER put any oil/drops in an ear with active discharge (indicates perforation) — drops can enter middle ear and worsen infection.

Breastfeeding Is Best Prevention: Exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months halves AOM risk. Combined with PCV13 vaccination and avoiding supine bottle-feeding, most AOM episodes can be prevented.
confirmation_tests_curated: Otoscopic Examination: Visualization of tympanic membrane. Diagnostic findings: bulging, erythema, opacification, decreased mobility, fluid level, perforation with discharge. EVERY health facility should have an otoscope. Train PHC doctors and ANMs in otoscopy.
Pneumatic Otoscopy: Assesses tympanic membrane mobility. Decreased mobility = middle ear effusion. More accurate than simple otoscopy.
Tympanometry: Objective measurement of middle ear pressure and compliance. Type B (flat) tympanogram = middle ear effusion. Available at ENT clinics.

diseases.name: Pellagra & B-Vitamin Deficiency
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (3 Ds Pattern)

Clinical Recognition: Symmetric photosensitive dermatitis + diarrhea + neuropsychiatric symptoms in a patient with poor diet (maize/jowar-dependent) or alcoholism = pellagra. Casal's necklace is pathognomonic.

2. Laboratory Tests

Urinary N-Methylnicotinamide: Low levels (<0.5 mg/day) confirm niacin deficiency. Most specific test but not widely available.

Serum Niacin/NAD Levels: Low in deficiency but not routinely available in India.

Complete Blood Count: May show macrocytic anemia (if B12/folate co-deficiency) or normocytic.

Serum Vitamin B12 & Folate: Check for co-existing deficiencies.

Serum Albumin: Low in severe malnutrition.

3. Therapeutic Trial

Response to Niacin: Dramatic clinical improvement within 24-48 hours of niacin supplementation is practically diagnostic. Skin improves within days, diarrhea resolves in 1-2 days, mental symptoms over weeks.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Pellagra diagnosed clinically by: ≥2 of the 3 Ds (Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia) + risk factors (poor diet/alcoholism) + response to Niacin treatment. Lab confirmation desirable but not essential for treatment initiation.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Niacin Replacement (Curative)

Nicotinamide (Niacinamide): 100mg thrice daily for 3-4 weeks. Preferred over Nicotinic acid (less flushing). Dramatic improvement in 24-48 hours — diarrhea resolves, skin begins healing, mental symptoms improve over weeks. Continue until clinical resolution then maintenance through diet.

Nicotinic Acid (Niacin): 50-100mg thrice daily. Alternative. Causes flushing (harmless but uncomfortable).

2. B-Complex Supplementation

Vitamin B Complex: Multiple B vitamins often co-deficient. Supplement all: B1 (Thiamine), B2 (Riboflavin), B6 (Pyridoxine), B12, and Folic acid alongside Niacin.

3. Nutritional Rehabilitation

High-Protein Diet: Include dal/pulses, eggs, milk, fish, groundnuts — all rich in tryptophan (niacin precursor). Dietary diversity is the long-term solution.

4. Treat Underlying Cause

Alcohol Cessation: For alcoholic pellagra — address alcohol dependence.

Dietary Education: Diversify from maize/jowar monoculture. Include fortified cereals, pulses, groundnuts.

5. Prevention

Food Fortification: India's FSSAI-mandated fortification of rice and wheat flour with B vitamins. Support fortified food distribution through PDS.
