diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Groundnut / Peanut (Moongphali): Richest common Indian food source of niacin (12.9mg/100g). Encourage daily groundnut consumption in pellagra-prone communities. Affordable, available, and nutritionally dense.

Til/Sesame (Sesamum indicum): Rich in B vitamins and minerals. Traditional Indian ingredient. Include in diet.

Amla/Amalaki: Vitamin C-rich Rasayana. Supports overall nutritional recovery.

Chyawanprash: Multi-herb Rasayana for general nutritional rehabilitation. 10-20g daily.

Ashwagandha: For neuropsychiatric recovery and strength building. 300-600mg twice daily.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri): Medhya Rasayana for cognitive recovery from pellagra-related dementia. 300-500mg daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Maize/Jowar-Dependent Populations: Pellagra (Niacin/Vitamin B3 deficiency) occurs in communities whose staple diet is maize (corn) or jowar (sorghum) — niacin in these cereals is in bound form (niacytin) that is not bioavailable. Deccan plateau (Maharashtra, AP, Telangana, Karnataka) populations historically affected.

Chronic Alcoholism: Alcohol impairs niacin absorption and metabolism. Alcoholic pellagra is common in Indian males with chronic alcohol dependence.

Other B-Vitamin Deficiencies (Often Coexist): Thiamine (B1) deficiency — Beriberi (cardiac/neurological). Riboflavin (B2) deficiency — angular cheilitis, glossitis. Pyridoxine (B6) deficiency — peripheral neuropathy. Cobalamin (B12) deficiency — megaloblastic anemia, neuropathy. Multiple B-vitamin deficiencies often coexist in malnourished populations.

Food Processing & Fortification: Nixtamalization (lime/alkali treatment of corn) releases bound niacin — practiced in Mexico but NOT in India. India's food fortification initiative (fortifying rice, wheat flour with B vitamins) is addressing this.
diseases.important_notes:
Pellagra Is 100% Curable & Preventable: Niacin (Nicotinamide) 100mg thrice daily CURES pellagra within days. A disease that kills (4th D = Death) is easily reversed with a vitamin costing <₹1/day. Early recognition saves lives.

Dietary Diversity Is the Long-Term Solution: Maize/jowar-dependent communities need dietary diversification — add groundnuts, pulses, eggs, and fortified cereals. PDS distribution of fortified rice is a game-changer.

Suspect Pellagra in 'Dermatitis + Diarrhea + Confusion': In any malnourished patient or chronic alcoholic presenting with symmetric sun-exposed rash + diarrhea + mental changes, consider pellagra and start Niacin immediately. Response is diagnostic.

Multiple B-Deficiencies Coexist: Never treat niacin alone — always give B-Complex (B1, B2, B6, B12, Folate) as multiple deficiencies commonly coexist in malnourished populations.

Fortified Food — Support FSSAI Initiative: India's food fortification programme (rice, wheat flour, oil, milk with vitamins and minerals) is the most scalable solution to prevent pellagra and other micronutrient deficiency diseases.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Recognition: Symmetric photosensitive dermatitis + diarrhea + neuropsychiatric symptoms in a patient with poor diet (maize/jowar-dependent) or alcoholism = pellagra. Casal's necklace is pathognomonic.
Urinary N-Methylnicotinamide: Low levels (<0.5 mg/day) confirm niacin deficiency. Most specific test but not widely available.
Serum Niacin/NAD Levels: Low in deficiency but not routinely available in India.

diseases.name: Migraine & Headache
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (ICHD-3 Criteria)

Migraine Without Aura (Most Common): ≥5 attacks with: headache 4-72 hours + ≥2 of (unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe intensity, aggravated by activity) + ≥1 of (nausea/vomiting, photophobia+phonophobia). NO investigation needed for typical migraine.

Migraine With Aura: ≥2 attacks with: fully reversible visual/sensory/speech symptoms + ≥3 of (at least 1 symptom spreads over ≥5 min, 2+ symptoms occur in succession, each lasts 5-60 min, followed by headache within 60 min).

Tension-Type Headache: ≥10 episodes, 30 min-7 days, ≥2 of (bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, not aggravated by activity), NO nausea/vomiting.

2. Red Flag Headaches (SNOOP Criteria — Need Investigation)

Red Flags Requiring Imaging/LP: Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, cancer, HIV), Neurological signs (focal deficit, papilledema), Onset sudden ('thunderclap'), Onset after age 50 (new headache), Pattern change (progressive worsening). Any red flag → CT/MRI brain + CSF analysis to exclude secondary causes.

3. Investigations (Only for Red Flags)

MRI Brain (With/Without Contrast): For atypical headache, red flags, neurological signs. Excludes tumor, AVM, venous sinus thrombosis.

CT Brain: Emergency setting — for sudden severe headache to exclude subarachnoid hemorrhage.

Lumbar Puncture: After imaging — for suspected meningitis, SAH (xanthochromia), idiopathic intracranial hypertension.

4. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Primary headache (migraine, tension-type, cluster) is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS based on ICHD-3 criteria. No blood test or scan diagnoses migraine. Investigations are ONLY for excluding secondary causes (red flags).
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Acute Migraine Treatment (Abort the Attack)

Paracetamol 1g + Metoclopramide 10mg: First-line combination for mild-moderate migraine. Metoclopramide treats nausea AND improves Paracetamol absorption.

Ibuprofen/Naproxen: Ibuprofen 400-600mg or Naproxen 500-750mg. NSAIDs are effective first-line for mild-moderate attacks. Take early in attack for best effect.

Sumatriptan: 50-100mg oral or 6mg subcutaneous. GOLD STANDARD for moderate-severe migraine. Triptan-specific — works on serotonin 5-HT1B/1D receptors. Take at onset of headache (not during aura). Contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension, coronary artery disease, stroke history.

Avoid Analgesic Overuse: Limit acute medications to ≤10 days/month (triptans) or ≤15 days/month (simple analgesics) to prevent Medication Overuse Headache.

2. Preventive Therapy (If ≥4 attacks/month or disabling)

Propranolol: 40-160mg daily in 2 divided doses. First-line preventive. Also helps performance anxiety. Avoid in asthma.

Amitriptyline: 10-75mg at bedtime. Dual benefit for migraine + depression/insomnia. Dry mouth, drowsiness common initially.

Topiramate: 50-100mg daily. Effective preventive. Side benefit: weight loss. Side effects: cognitive slowing, paresthesias, kidney stones.

Flunarizine: 5-10mg at bedtime. Calcium channel blocker widely used in India for migraine prevention. Weight gain is main limitation.

Valproate: 500-1000mg daily. Effective but teratogenic — avoid in women of childbearing age.

CGRP Monoclonal Antibodies (Emerging): Erenumab, Fremanezumab — monthly injections. Highly effective, well-tolerated. Expensive in India but becoming available.

3. Non-Pharmacological (Important Adjuncts)

Trigger Avoidance: Maintain headache diary to identify triggers. Common Indian triggers: disrupted sleep, skipped meals, dehydration, heat, strong odors, alcohol, MSG, processed food.

Regular Exercise: 30 minutes moderate aerobic exercise 5 days/week reduces migraine frequency by 40%.

Stress Management: Yoga, meditation, CBT — published evidence for migraine reduction.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Shirashooladi Vajra Ras: Classical herbo-mineral for Shirahshoola (headache). Contains Suvarna Bhasma, Mauktik Bhasma. Practitioner supervision essential. 125mg twice daily.

Pathyadi Kwatha / Godanti Bhasma: Godanti (gypsum) Bhasma is a classical Pitta-shamaka and Shirahshoolahara. 250-500mg twice daily with honey. Traditional migraine remedy.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri): Medhya Rasayana. Reduces stress-triggered migraine. Published anxiolytic activity. 300-500mg daily.

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi): Classical sedative and headache remedy. 250-500mg at bedtime. Calms Vata.

Pippali + Maricha + Shunthi (Trikatu): Kaphahara and Deepana. For sinus-type migraine with congestion. 1-3g with honey.

Nasya (Nasal Oil Therapy): Anu Taila or Shadbindu Taila nasal drops. 2-3 drops each nostril daily. Classical Shirahshoola treatment through nasal route. Published evidence for migraine relief.

Yoga & Pranayama: Anulom-Vilom, Bhramari, Shavasana, meditation — published RCT evidence for migraine prevention. Daily practice reduces attack frequency.
diseases.india_specific:
Massive Prevalence — Under-treated: India has an estimated 150+ million people with migraine. Prevalence 14-25% in studies. Yet majority never receive proper diagnosis — labeled 'sinus headache', 'weak eyes', 'gas', or 'tension'. <5% receive preventive therapy.

Self-Medication & Over-the-Counter Abuse: Widespread use of OTC analgesic combinations (Paracetamol+Caffeine+analgesic cocktails) and ergotamine without prescription. Leads to Medication Overuse Headache (MOH) — chronic daily headache from rebound. Major problem in India.

Cultural Triggers: Indian-specific triggers include strong spice aromas, incense/agarbatti smoke, fasting (religious fasts — Navratri, Ramadan), heat exposure, and disrupted meals during festivals.

Gender Gap: Migraine is 3x more common in women (hormonal influence). Menstrual migraine affects 60% of female migraineurs. Often dismissed as 'normal' period pain.
diseases.important_notes:
Migraine Is a Real Neurological Disease: Migraine is NOT 'just a headache' or psychosomatic. It's a genuine neurological disorder affecting 150+ million Indians. Proper diagnosis and treatment dramatically improve quality of life. Take it seriously.

Avoid Medication Overuse Headache: Taking painkillers (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen, Sumatriptan) more than 10-15 days/month CAUSES chronic daily headache (MOH). If needing acute treatment this frequently, switch to preventive therapy (Propranolol, Amitriptyline).

Ayurvedic Migraine Management — Genuine Efficacy: Nasya therapy, Godanti Bhasma, Brahmi, and Yoga have genuine evidence for migraine prevention. They are legitimate components of an integrative migraine management plan alongside trigger avoidance and appropriate pharmacotherapy.

Headache Diary Is Essential: Keep a diary recording: date, duration, severity, triggers (food, sleep, stress, menstruation, weather), medications taken, and response. This is the most valuable tool for migraine management — helps identify patterns and triggers.

Red Flag Headaches Need Investigation: Sudden 'thunderclap' headache, headache with fever/neck stiffness, headache with neurological signs, new headache after age 50, progressively worsening headache = NOT migraine until proven otherwise. Get CT/MRI to exclude serious causes.
confirmation_tests_curated: Migraine Without Aura (Most Common): ≥5 attacks with: headache 4-72 hours + ≥2 of (unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe intensity, aggravated by activity) + ≥1 of (nausea/vomiting, photophobia+phonophobia). NO investigation needed for typical migraine.

Migraine With Aura: ≥2 attacks with: fully reversible visual/sensory/speech symptoms + ≥3 of (at least 1 symptom spreads over ≥5 min, 2+ symptoms occur in succession, each lasts 5-60 min, followed by headache within 60 min).
Tension-Type Headache: ≥10 episodes, 30 min-7 days, ≥2 of (bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, not aggravated by activity), NO nausea/vomiting.
2. Red Flag Headaches (SNOOP Criteria — Need Investigation)

diseases.name: Lower Back Pain
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Assessment (Sufficient for Most Cases)

History & Red Flag Screening: Assess pain character, duration, radiation, neurological symptoms. Screen for RED FLAGS: age <20 or >55 with new back pain, history of cancer, unexplained weight loss, fever, night pain, bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, progressive neurological deficit, thoracic pain, recent trauma. Red flags → immediate investigation.

Neurological Examination: Straight Leg Raise (SLR) — positive if pain radiates below knee at <60°. Test motor power (L4: knee extension, L5: big toe dorsiflexion, S1: plantarflexion). Test sensation (L4: medial leg, L5: dorsum foot, S1: lateral foot). Test ankle reflex (S1).

2. Imaging (Only When Indicated)

MRI Lumbosacral Spine: ONLY for red flags, progressive neurological deficit, or pain not improving after 6 weeks of conservative treatment. Do NOT MRI every acute LBP case — leads to overdiagnosis and unnecessary surgery.

X-ray Lumbosacral Spine: For trauma, suspected fracture, age >50, osteoporosis risk. Shows alignment, fractures, spondylolisthesis.

3. Blood Tests (If Red Flags Present)

ESR/CRP: Elevated in infection (spinal TB, discitis) or inflammatory cause (ankylosing spondylitis).

Complete Blood Count: For suspected infection or malignancy.

HLA-B27: In young males (<40) with inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise, worse with rest) — suspect ankylosing spondylitis.

Serum Calcium, ALP, Vitamin D: For osteoporotic fractures.

PSA / Cancer Markers: If metastatic disease suspected.

4. Key Classification

Non-Specific LBP (85-90%): No specific pathological cause identifiable. Mechanical pain. Self-limiting in most cases (90% resolve within 6 weeks). Radiculopathy (5-10%): Nerve root compression — disc herniation, spinal stenosis. Serious Pathology (<1%): Fracture, malignancy, infection, cauda equina syndrome.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Acute LBP (First-Line Conservative)

Paracetamol: 1g every 6 hours. First-line analgesic. Safe for short-term use.

NSAIDs (Ibuprofen/Naproxen/Diclofenac): Ibuprofen 400mg thrice daily or Diclofenac 50mg twice daily for 7-14 days. Most effective pharmacotherapy for acute LBP. Use lowest dose for shortest duration. Gastroprotection with PPI if needed.

Muscle Relaxants (Thiocolchicoside/Cyclobenzaprine): Thiocolchicoside 4-8mg twice daily for 5-7 days. For acute muscle spasm. Short-term use only — sedation common.

Stay Active: MOST IMPORTANT advice — stay as active as possible. Bed rest WORSENS LBP. Resume normal activities within pain tolerance. Walking is therapeutic.

2. Neuropathic Pain / Radiculopathy

Pregabalin/Gabapentin: Pregabalin 75-150mg twice daily or Gabapentin 300-1200mg thrice daily. For shooting, nerve-type pain. Effective for sciatica.

Amitriptyline: 10-25mg at bedtime. For chronic LBP with neuropathic component + sleep disturbance.

Epidural Steroid Injection: For severe radiculopathy not responding to conservative treatment. Performed under fluoroscopic guidance. Provides 2-6 weeks relief.

3. Chronic LBP Management

Multimodal Approach: Exercise therapy (core strengthening, flexibility) + pharmacotherapy + psychological support (CBT for pain catastrophizing) + ergonomic advice. No single treatment is sufficient for chronic LBP.

Physical Therapy: Supervised exercise programme is the MOST effective treatment for chronic LBP. Core stability exercises, McKenzie exercises, aquatic therapy.

Yoga: Published RCT evidence (multiple Indian studies) showing Yoga reduces chronic LBP pain and disability. Recommended by international guidelines.

4. Surgical (Very Selective — <5% of LBP Patients)

Microdiscectomy: For disc herniation with progressive neurological deficit or failed 6-12 weeks conservative treatment. >90% success rate for appropriate indications.

Spinal Decompression: For lumbar spinal stenosis with claudication.

Spinal Fusion: For instability, spondylolisthesis. Highly selective indication.

Cauda Equina Syndrome: EMERGENCY decompressive surgery within 24-48 hours to prevent permanent bladder/bowel dysfunction.

Kati Basti (Warm Oil Pooling): Classical Ayurvedic Panchakarma procedure for Katigraha (lower back pain). Warm medicated oil pooled on lumbosacral area within a dough boundary for 30-45 minutes. Deeply relaxing and therapeutic. Published evidence for pain reduction.

Abhyanga (Oil Massage) with Mahanarayana Taila: Warm medicated oil full-body massage. Mahanarayana Taila is the classical oil for Vata-related musculoskeletal pain. Reduces muscle spasm and improves circulation.

Pinda Sweda (Bolus Fomentation): Medicated bolus fomentation using Njavara rice or sand bolus. Applied over lumbosacral area. Heat + herbs provide analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects.

Guggulu Preparations: Yogaraja Guggulu (for Vata disorders), Laksha Guggulu (for bone/joint), Simhanada Guggulu (for chronic back pain). 2 tablets twice daily.

Ashwagandha: Anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and muscle-strengthening. Published evidence for musculoskeletal pain. 300-600mg twice daily.

Shallaki / Boswellia (Boswellia serrata): Published evidence for anti-inflammatory activity (5-LOX inhibition). 300-500mg thrice daily. Reduces pain and improves mobility.

Nirgundi (Vitex negundo): Classical Shoolahara and Shothaghna. Nirgundi taila for external application. Nirgundi leaves fomentation. Published anti-inflammatory activity.

Dashamoola Kashaya: Ten-root decoction for Vata-Kapha disorders. Anti-inflammatory and analgesic. 15-30ml twice daily.

Yoga Therapy: Bhujangasana (Cobra pose), Shalabhasana (Locust pose), Setu Bandhasana (Bridge pose), Marjariasana (Cat-Cow), Shavasana. Published RCT evidence for chronic LBP. Daily practice essential.
diseases.india_specific:
Massive Burden in India: LBP prevalence in India: 42-62% in various studies. Rising due to sedentary office work, smartphone posture, poor ergonomics, and manual labor without protection. Affects all age groups from college students to elderly.

Occupational Back Pain: Agricultural laborers (rice planting — prolonged forward bending), construction workers (heavy lifting), auto-rickshaw/truck drivers (whole-body vibration), and office workers (prolonged sitting) are high-risk Indian occupational groups.

Over-Investigation & Over-Treatment: MRI is performed for most acute LBP in Indian urban settings — leading to overdiagnosis of 'disc bulges' that are often incidental (30-50% of asymptomatic individuals have disc bulges on MRI). This leads to unnecessary surgeries and interventions.

Traditional Practices — Mixed Impact: Yoga (evidence-based benefit) and Ayurvedic massage (supportive) are positive Indian traditions. However, aggressive traditional bone-setting, forceful spinal manipulation by untrained practitioners, and prolonged bed rest advice are harmful practices.
diseases.important_notes:
Stay Active — Bed Rest WORSENS Back Pain: The most important message for acute LBP. Prolonged bed rest deconditions muscles and increases disability. Walk, move, stay active within pain tolerance. Resume normal activities as soon as possible.

Do NOT MRI Every Back Pain: 85-90% of LBP has no specific pathological cause and resolves within 6 weeks. MRI often shows 'disc bulges' that are incidental (present in 30-50% of asymptomatic people). Unnecessary MRI → unnecessary anxiety → unnecessary surgery. MRI only for red flags or non-improving pain after 6 weeks.

Ayurvedic Treatments Are Genuinely Effective for LBP: Kati Basti, Abhyanga, Pinda Sweda, and Yoga have published evidence for chronic LBP management. They are legitimate components of multimodal pain management alongside exercise and appropriate pharmacotherapy. India's Ayurvedic tradition offers some of the best non-pharmacological back pain treatments globally.

Red Flags = Urgent Investigation: Cauda equina syndrome (bladder/bowel dysfunction + saddle numbness), progressive weakness, unexplained weight loss, fever, night pain, or history of cancer with new back pain require URGENT MRI and specialist referral. Do not delay these cases.

Exercise Is the Best Long-Term Treatment: For chronic LBP, supervised exercise (core strengthening, flexibility, aerobic fitness) is MORE effective than any medication. Yoga specifically has multiple Indian RCTs supporting its efficacy. Prescribe exercise like medicine.
confirmation_tests_curated: History & Red Flag Screening: Assess pain character, duration, radiation, neurological symptoms. Screen for RED FLAGS: age <20 or >55 with new back pain, history of cancer, unexplained weight loss, fever, night pain, bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, progressive neurological deficit, thoracic pain, recent trauma. Red flags → immediate investigation.

Neurological Examination: Straight Leg Raise (SLR) — positive if pain radiates below knee at <60°. Test motor power (L4: knee extension, L5: big toe dorsiflexion, S1: plantarflexion). Test sensation (L4: medial leg, L5: dorsum foot, S1: lateral foot). Test ankle reflex (S1).

MRI Lumbosacral Spine: ONLY for red flags, progressive neurological deficit, or pain not improving after 6 weeks of conservative treatment. Do NOT MRI every acute LBP case — leads to overdiagnosis and unnecessary surgery.

diseases.name: Psoriasis
diseases.tests:
1. Primary Clinical Assessment

Dermatological Examination: Visual inspection for silvery scales, erythematous base, symmetrical distribution. Assess Body Surface Area (BSA) — 1 palm = 1% BSA.

PASI Score (Psoriasis Area and Severity Index): 0–72 scale measuring redness, thickness, scaling, and area. PASI <10 = mild; 10–20 = moderate; >20 = severe. Required for biologic therapy eligibility.

Auspitz Sign: Gentle scale removal reveals pinpoint bleeding — positive in psoriasis; differentiates from eczema and fungal infections.

Koebner Phenomenon Assessment: Document lesions at trauma sites; confirms pathergy response.

DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index): 10-item questionnaire; score >10 = major QoL impact. Mandatory before systemic/biologic therapy in IADVL guidelines.

2. Routine Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count (CBC): Mild leukocytosis in pustular/erythrodermic psoriasis. Anaemia possible with high inflammatory burden.

ESR & CRP: Elevated in active moderate-severe psoriasis — baseline before systemic therapy.

Liver Function Tests (LFT): Mandatory before and during Methotrexate. Psoriasis patients have higher NAFLD risk.

Renal Function Tests (RFT): Baseline before Cyclosporine — nephrotoxic agent.

Fasting Blood Sugar & HbA1c: Screen for T2DM (psoriasis independent CV risk factor); annually in all Indian patients.

Lipid Profile: Screen dyslipidaemia; psoriasis is an independent cardiovascular risk factor. TNF inhibitors can worsen lipid profile.

3. Specific Diagnostic Tests

Skin Biopsy (Punch Biopsy): Histopathology shows acanthosis, elongated rete ridges, parakeratosis, Munro's microabscesses, and dilated dermal capillaries. Gold standard for confirmation.

KOH Mount: Differentiates psoriasis plaques from tinea corporis/capitis in scalp and groin lesions.

Anti-Streptolysin O (ASO) Titre: Elevated in Guttate Psoriasis — identifies precipitating Streptococcal infection.

HIV Testing: Recommended in young patients with sudden severe or treatment-resistant psoriasis.

4. Psoriatic Arthritis / Severe Disease Tests

X-Ray Joints: Pencil-in-cup deformity, periostitis, enthesopathy in PsA. MRI preferred for early PsA detection.

HLA-B27: Positive in some PsA patients with axial (spinal) involvement.

Anti-CCP: Differentiates PsA from Rheumatoid Arthritis — usually negative in PsA.

Dermatoscopy: Shows regularly distributed dotted vessels with white scales; differentiates psoriasis from seborrheic dermatitis and pityriasis rosea.

5. Classification Criteria (Indian Guidelines)

IADVL Guidelines 2021: Mild = BSA <10%, PASI <10, DLQI <10. Moderate-Severe = BSA >10% or PASI >10 or DLQI >10. Required for systemic/biologic therapy eligibility decision.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Treatments (Mild-Moderate Psoriasis)

Betamethasone Dipropionate 0.05% Cream/Ointment: Potent topical corticosteroid, first-line for plaque psoriasis. Apply once/twice daily for 2–4 weeks; avoid prolonged use on face/flexures.

Calcipotriol 0.005% Cream/Ointment (Vit D3 analogue): Anti-proliferative; reduces plaque thickness. Highly effective combined with betamethasone (Daivobet). Max 100g/week.

Coal Tar 1–5% Cream/Shampoo: Antipruritic and anti-proliferative. Used for scalp psoriasis and chronic plaque psoriasis.

Salicylic Acid 2–6% Ointment: Keratolytic; removes thick scales before topical steroid/calcipotriol application — enhances penetration.

Tacrolimus 0.1% / Pimecrolimus 1% Cream: Steroid-free calcineurin inhibitors for face, flexures, and genitalia.

2. Systemic Treatments (Moderate-Severe Psoriasis)

Methotrexate 7.5–25mg once weekly (oral/IM): Gold standard systemic therapy. Co-prescribe Folic Acid 5mg/day (not on MTX day). Monitor LFT + CBC regularly.

Acitretin 25–50mg/day oral: Oral retinoid for pustular, erythrodermic, and palmoplantar psoriasis. Highly teratogenic — strict contraception mandatory.

Cyclosporine 2.5–5mg/kg/day oral: Rapid-acting immunosuppressant. Short courses (3–6 months) due to nephrotoxicity and hypertension risk.

Apremilast 30mg BD oral: PDE4 inhibitor; oral targeted therapy used when MTX fails or is not tolerated. GI side effects common.

3. Biologic Therapies (Severe/Refractory Psoriasis)

Adalimumab (Anti-TNF): SC 80mg initial → 40mg fortnightly. TB screening (Mantoux/IGRA) mandatory before initiation in India.

Secukinumab (Anti-IL17A): SC 300mg weekly x5 doses → monthly. Highly effective; preferred for nail and PsA involvement.

Ustekinumab (Anti-IL12/23): SC 45/90mg (weight-based) at 0, 4 weeks then 12-weekly. PASI 75 in ~67% patients.

4. Phototherapy

Narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) Phototherapy: 311nm UVB, 3x/week, 20–30 sessions per course. Highly effective and safe in pregnancy. Preferred for widespread psoriasis where biologics are unaffordable.

PUVA (Oral Psoralen 0.3–0.6mg/kg + UVA): Effective for palmoplantar psoriasis. Risk of skin cancer with long-term use.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream (15g/30g): First-line affordable topical corticosteroid for plaque psoriasis. Available at PMBJP stores.

Methotrexate Tablets 2.5mg (10 tablets): First-line systemic therapy for moderate-severe psoriasis and PsA. Available at Jan Aushadhi Kendras.

Salicylic Acid Ointment 6% (25g): Keratolytic for scale removal; available at PMBJP.

Coal Tar Shampoo (100ml): For scalp psoriasis; available as generic at Jan Aushadhi stores.

Folic Acid 5mg Tablets (10 tablets): Mandatory co-prescription with Methotrexate to prevent toxicity. All PMBJP stores.

Acitretin 10mg/25mg Capsules: Generic oral retinoid for severe psoriasis. Limited Jan Aushadhi availability.

Vitamin D3 + Calcium Tablets (60 tablets): Supportive supplement; psoriasis patients are frequently Vitamin D deficient.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Panchatikta Ghrita Guggulu: Classical formulation for Kushtha (skin diseases). Contains Nimba (Neem), Patola, Guduchi, Vasa, and Guggulu. Anti-inflammatory and blood-purifying. 2 tablets twice daily with warm water/ghee.

Khadirarishta: Fermented Ayurvedic liquid with Khadira (Acacia catechu). Traditional blood purifier for chronic skin diseases including psoriasis. 15–20ml with equal water twice daily after meals.

Mahamanjisthadhi Kashaya: Classical decoction with Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia), Neem, and Guduchi. Powerful blood-purifier and anti-inflammatory for Vata-Pitta Kushtha. 15ml twice daily before meals.

Arogyavardhini Vati: Classical tablet with Kutki, Triphala, and purified minerals. Used for skin diseases with liver dysfunction. 2 tablets twice daily.

Neem (Azadirachta indica) Capsules/Tablets: Anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and immunomodulatory activity. Supports management of psoriatic plaques.

Turmeric (Curcumin) Extract Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India): Evidence-based anti-inflammatory (NF-kB, COX-2 inhibition). 1000–1500mg/day. Widely available in India.

Wrightia tinctoria (Pala Indigo) Oil: Traditional topical oil for psoriasis in South India. Anti-psoriatic activity demonstrated in clinical studies.

Indukantham Kashayam (Kerala Ayurveda): Classical Ayurvedic decoction used in Panchakarma preparation and skin disease management.

Virechana (Therapeutic Purgation — Panchakarma): Under qualified Ayurvedic physician, Virechana using Trivrit Avaleha + Castor oil for Pitta-dominant psoriasis. Removes accumulated Ama.

Chandana-Manjistha Lepam: Topical paste of Chandana (sandalwood), Manjistha, and Neem powder in coconut oil for anti-inflammatory and cooling effect on plaques.

Himalaya Lukoskin Tablets + Ointment: Clinically studied Ayurvedic product. Contains Bavachi (Psoralea corylifolia) and Manjistha. Used for psoriasis and pigmentation disorders.

Dabur Mahamarichyadi Tail: Ayurvedic medicated oil for anti-inflammatory and scale-softening action on psoriatic plaques.

Tiktaka Ghrita: Classical medicated ghee used internally for deep-seated chronic skin diseases in Ayurveda. Dose 10–20ml on empty stomach under physician guidance.
diseases.india_specific:
Psoriatic Arthritis Underdiagnosis: India has ~1.5–2 million psoriasis patients; PsA develops in 20–30% but is frequently missed in rural settings — leads to permanent joint damage if untreated.

Stress-Triggered Flares: Psychosocial stress from socioeconomic pressures and urban migration activate the HPA axis, worsening psoriatic inflammation.

Infections as Triggers: Streptococcal throat infections trigger Guttate Psoriasis; HIV-associated psoriasis is often severe and treatment-resistant — both more prevalent in India.

Unlicensed Topical Products: Many Indian patients use steroid-containing 'Ayurvedic' fairness creams, causing skin atrophy and severe rebound flares on discontinuation.

Metabolic Comorbidities: Indian psoriasis patients have elevated risk of T2DM, hypertension, and metabolic syndrome — all highly prevalent in India.
diseases.important_notes:
Never Use Topical Steroids Unsupervised Long-Term: Prolonged use of potent topical corticosteroids on face, folds, or genitalia causes skin atrophy, stretch marks, and rebound flares. Many unlicensed creams and 'Ayurvedic' preparations in India contain undisclosed steroids — rural health workers must warn patients explicitly.

Methotrexate Safety Protocol: MTX requires CBC + LFT monitoring every 4–6 weeks. Strictly avoid alcohol during MTX therapy. Do not use NSAIDs with MTX without supervision — significantly increases toxicity. Folic acid 5mg/day (not on MTX day) is mandatory and non-negotiable.

TB Screening Mandatory Before Biologics: Active and latent TB must be excluded (Mantoux/IGRA) before starting any biologic (Adalimumab, Secukinumab, Ustekinumab). India's high TB burden makes biologic-induced TB reactivation a real and serious risk.

Acitretin Absolute Contraindication in Pregnancy: Acitretin is highly teratogenic. Women of childbearing age must use two reliable contraceptive methods during therapy AND for 3 years after stopping. Counsel clearly before dispensing.

Refer to Dermatologist: Moderate-severe psoriasis (PASI >10 or BSA >10% or DLQI >10), erythrodermic psoriasis, pustular psoriasis, and psoriatic arthritis all require specialist referral. Do not attempt systemic/biologic therapy initiation at PHC level.

diseases.name: Atopic Dermatitis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Hanifin & Rajka / UK Working Party Criteria)

Hanifin & Rajka Criteria: Minimum 3 major + 3 minor criteria. Major: Pruritus, chronic/relapsing course, typical morphology/distribution, personal/family atopy history.

SCORAD Index: 0–103. Mild <25, Moderate 25–50, Severe >50. Measures extent (rule of nines), intensity (redness, swelling, oozing, scratch marks, lichenification, dryness), and subjective symptoms.

EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index): 0–72. Mild <1.1, Moderate 1.1–21, Severe >21.

POEM (Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure): 7-item patient-reported score 0–28. Clear 0–2, Mild 3–7, Moderate 8–16, Severe 17–28.

IGA (Investigator Global Assessment): 5-point scale (0=clear to 4=severe). Standard endpoint in clinical trials.

2. Allergy & Immunological Tests

Total Serum IgE: Markedly elevated (often >2000 IU/mL) in AD. Correlates with severity; supports atopic diagnosis.

Specific IgE (RAST/ImmunoCAP): For house dust mite, cockroach, molds, food allergens (egg, milk, wheat). Guides allergen avoidance.

Skin Prick Testing (SPT): Identifies type I hypersensitivity. Performed by allergist; not available at PHC level.

3. Routine Laboratory Tests

CBC with differential: Eosinophilia (>500 cells/µL) characteristic of atopy. Elevated eosinophils support diagnosis.

Serum Vitamin D Level: Vitamin D deficiency common in India; strongly associated with more severe AD. Supplementation improves outcomes.

Serum Zinc Level: Zinc deficiency impairs skin barrier repair — common in rural Indian children.

4. Microbiology Tests (Superinfected AD)

Skin Swab Culture & Sensitivity: For crusted/weeping worsening lesions. S. aureus colonization in >90% of AD skin; identifies MRSA.

Tzanck Smear / PCR for HSV: For suspected Eczema Herpeticum (widespread painful vesicles + fever) — urgent; requires systemic antiviral.

5. Patch Testing & Indian Guidelines

Patch Test (European Baseline Series): For allergic contact dermatitis component from topical product sensitization. Specialist centre only.

IAP Guidelines 2019: Clinical diagnosis; Vitamin D supplementation for all Indian AD patients; zinc + Vitamin A via NHM programs.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Emollients & Skin Barrier Repair (First-Line for ALL Severity)

White Petrolatum (Petroleum Jelly): Most cost-effective emollient for Indian patients per IADVL. Apply liberally within 3 minutes of bathing to restore hydration and reduce transepidermal water loss.

Glycerine Lotion/Cream: Humectant; affordable, widely available. Apply daily after bathing — especially useful in dry Indian climate.

Paraffin Soft (White/Yellow 50% in Aqueous Cream): Standard emollient for AD. Cost-effective and readily available.

2. Topical Anti-Inflammatory Treatments

Hydrocortisone 1% Cream: Mild topical steroid for face, skin folds, and infants. Short-term use for flares — twice daily.

Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream/Ointment: Moderate-potent topical steroid for body and limb plaques. Max 2 weeks per flare course.

Tacrolimus 0.03%/0.1% Ointment (Protopic): Calcineurin inhibitor; safe for face/folds; steroid-sparing. 0.03% for children, 0.1% for adults.

Pimecrolimus 1% Cream (Elidel): Mild calcineurin inhibitor for mild-moderate AD; suitable for sensitive areas.

3. Antihistamines (Symptomatic)

Cetirizine 10mg / Levocetirizine 5mg oral: Second-generation, minimal sedation, once daily — for pruritus control. Syrup for children.

Hydroxyzine 25mg oral: Sedating antihistamine; useful for nocturnal pruritus at bedtime. Not daytime use in children.

4. Antibiotics (Superinfected AD)

Cloxacillin / Flucloxacillin 500mg QID oral (7–10 days): First-line for S. aureus superinfection.

Mupirocin 2% Ointment: Topical for localized impetigo/infected eczema patches.

5. Systemic Immunosuppressants (Severe/Refractory AD)

Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral: Short courses (7–10 days) for acute severe flares. Avoid long-term — rebound flares.

Methotrexate 7.5–15mg/week oral: Moderate-severe refractory AD; monitor LFT + CBC.

Cyclosporine 3–5mg/kg/day: Fastest-acting systemic option; short-term (3–6 months); monitor BP and creatinine.

6. Biologic (Severe Refractory AD)

Dupilumab (Anti-IL-4/13): SC 600mg loading then 300mg fortnightly. Highly effective for severe refractory AD. CDSCO approved; expensive in India.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Nalpamaradi Tailam (Kottakkal/Nagarjuna): Traditional Kerala Ayurvedic oil with Nalpamara bark (Ficus species) and turmeric in coconut oil. Topically reduces pruritus, moisturizes, and treats eczematous plaques.

Madhuyashtyadi Tailam: Classical oil with Yashtimadhu (Licorice), Chandana, and Manjistha in sesame base. Anti-inflammatory topical for eczema plaques.

Nimbadi Churna: Powder with Neem, Haridra (turmeric), Khadira, and Chandana. Internal blood purifier for chronic skin diseases including atopic dermatitis.

Aragwadha (Cassia fistula) Decoction: Mild laxative and blood-purifying properties; used in Pitta-predominant skin diseases.

Haridra (Turmeric) Capsules (Organic India/Himalaya): Curcumin's anti-inflammatory activity (NF-kB, COX-2 inhibition) supports atopic inflammation management. 500–1000mg twice daily.

Ashwagandha + Brahmi Tablets: Adaptogenic; reduces cortisol-mediated immune dysregulation that triggers AD flares.

Panchanimba Churna: Classical combination of five Neem-family plants with potent anti-allergic and anti-inflammatory properties.

Avipattikar Churna: Pitta-pacifying digestive formulation; useful for AD with GI association.

Chyawanprash (10g daily): Immunomodulator with Amla (Vitamin C) and 40+ herbs. Supports immune regulation in atopic children.

Abhyanga with Bala Ashwagandhadi Tailam: Daily medicated oil massage for Vata-Pitta balance, skin moisturization, and stress management.

Mandukaparni (Centella asiatica) Extract: Anti-inflammatory, wound-healing, and skin barrier-supporting herb. Clinical evidence for both topical and internal use in eczema.

Kayyunyadi Kashayam (Kerala Ayurveda): Classical decoction specifically for skin disorders with pruritus.

Shatavari Ghrita: Nourishing and healing medicated ghee for Vata-Pitta skin conditions especially in females and children.
diseases.india_specific:
Hard Water & Pollution: India's northern states have high water hardness (>200 mg/L CaCO3); hard water damages skin barrier, worsening AD severity. Urban PM2.5 air pollution is an established AD trigger.

Tropical Climate & Sweating: India's hot, humid climate leads to excessive sweating — a strong AD trigger causing miliaria-like heat rash and worsening pruritus. Cooling access is limited in rural areas.

Nutritional Deficiencies: Zinc, Vitamin D, and essential fatty acid deficiencies — highly prevalent in Indian children — impair skin barrier integrity and immune regulation, increasing AD susceptibility.

Filaggrin Gene Mutations: South Asian populations have distinct FLG gene mutation patterns affecting skin barrier integrity — a genetic risk factor for early-onset and severe atopic dermatitis.

Tropical Superinfections: AD skin in India can become superinfected with Eczema Herpeticum (HSV) — a medical emergency requiring immediate IV acyclovir and urgent hospital referral.
diseases.important_notes:
Emollients Are Non-Negotiable First-Line Treatment: Moisturizers must be applied at least 2–3 times daily even when skin appears clear. White petroleum jelly (affordable, widely available) is as effective as expensive branded products — teach families this explicitly.

Topical Steroid Education (Both Overuse and Phobia): India has both steroid overuse (inappropriate potent steroids on face/folds) and steroid phobia (refusal of effective treatment). Topical corticosteroids are safe when used correctly — right potency, right duration, right body site. Educate health workers on this balance.

Identify and Avoid Triggers: Key AD triggers for Indian patients: hard water, harsh soap/detergents, synthetic clothing, heat/sweating, dust mites, and pet hair. Soap substitutes (petroleum jelly-based washes) should replace regular soap.

Secondary Infection Referral: Yellow crusting, honey-colored exudate, spreading redness, or fever = secondary bacterial infection — needs prompt antibiotic treatment. Eczema herpeticum (sudden painful clustered vesicles, fever) is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY requiring immediate hospital referral and IV acyclovir.

Ayurvedic Product Caution: Several Ayurvedic preparations contain botanical allergens that can sensitize atopic skin. Patch-test new topical Ayurvedic products before use. Inform treating physician of all traditional remedies used concurrently.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Hanifin & Rajka / UK Working Party Criteria)
Hanifin & Rajka Criteria: Minimum 3 major + 3 minor criteria. Major: Pruritus, chronic/relapsing course, typical morphology/distribution, personal/family atopy history.
SCORAD Index: 0–103. Mild <25, Moderate 25–50, Severe >50. Measures extent (rule of nines), intensity (redness, swelling, oozing, scratch marks, lichenification, dryness), and subjective symptoms.
EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index): 0–72. Mild <1.1, Moderate 1.1–21, Severe >21.
POEM (Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure): 7-item patient-reported score 0–28. Clear 0–2, Mild 3–7, Moderate 8–16, Severe 17–28.

diseases.name: Vitiligo
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis

Clinical Examination: Assess distribution (generalized, segmental, focal, mucosal), activity (Koebner's sign, active spread), extent (% BSA), and stability (VIDA score).

Wood's Lamp Examination (354nm UV): Accentuates depigmented patches — bright blue-white fluorescence. Detects early/subclinical lesions and distinguishes from other hypopigmentation disorders.

VASI (Vitiligo Area Scoring Index): Assessment tool for extent — 0 (no depigmentation) to 100 (complete depigmentation). Monitors treatment response.

VIDA Score (Vitiligo Disease Activity): Grades activity from +4 (rapid active spread) to -1 (stable >2 years). Determines whether patient needs disease-arresting vs repigmenting treatment.

2. Differentiating Tests

KOH Mount: Rules out pityriasis versicolor (shows spaghetti-and-meatballs on mount); also rules out tinea. Hypopigmentation vs achromia distinction.

Sensation Testing (Cotton Wool Test): Vitiligo patches have NORMAL sensation; leprosy patches have REDUCED sensation. Simple, critical screening tool for rural health workers in endemic regions.

Skin Biopsy (if uncertain): Complete absence of melanocytes on histology; Masson-Fontana stain negative for melanin. Differentiates from post-inflammatory hypopigmentation.

Leprosy Slit Skin Smear: Rules out hypopigmented leprosy patch — especially in endemic rural areas.

3. Autoimmune Screening (Mandatory for All Vitiligo Patients in India)

Thyroid Function Tests (TSH, T3, T4): Hashimoto's thyroiditis co-occurs in 10–20% of vitiligo patients. Screen at diagnosis and annually. Add Anti-TPO antibodies if TSH is elevated.

ANA (Antinuclear Antibody): Screens for associated autoimmune diseases (SLE, Sjogren's).

Fasting Blood Sugar: Screen for Type 1 DM association, especially in younger patients.

CBC: Screens for pernicious anaemia (Vitamin B12 deficiency autoimmune) associated with vitiligo.

4. Nutritional & Supportive Tests

Serum Vitamin B12 + Folate: Deficiencies common in Indian vegetarians; B12 deficiency associated with vitiligo progression.

Serum Copper Level: Copper is a cofactor for tyrosinase (melanin synthesis enzyme). Sub-clinical deficiency may contribute.

Serum Vitamin D Level: Low Vitamin D strongly associated with vitiligo in multiple Indian studies. Supplement if deficient.

5. Indian Guidelines

IADVL Vitiligo Guidelines 2021: VIDA scoring for disease activity; Wood's lamp for all patients; mandatory thyroid screening; NB-UVB phototherapy as first-line for generalized vitiligo >3% BSA.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Treatments (Limited BSA, Stable Disease)

Tacrolimus 0.1% Ointment: Best evidence for face and neck vitiligo. Apply twice daily for 6–12 months. Safe, steroid-sparing; best combined with NB-UVB phototherapy.

Betamethasone Dipropionate 0.05% Cream: Topical corticosteroid in short pulses for active stable vitiligo. Avoid face/folds. Risk of atrophy with prolonged use.

Topical Psoralen (Meladinine/Methoxsalen): Applied before UVA exposure for PUVA therapy. For localized vitiligo. Highly photosensitizing — strict solar exposure control.

2. Phototherapy (Generalized/Widespread Vitiligo)

Narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) Phototherapy: Gold standard for generalized vitiligo. 3x/week, 6–12 months for repigmentation. Best response on face, neck, trunk. Poor on acral sites (hands, feet, lips).

PUVA (Oral Methoxypsoralen 0.3–0.6mg/kg + UVA): For extensive vitiligo. Requires ophthalmology follow-up; UV-protective eyewear mandatory.

Excimer Laser/Lamp (308nm): Targeted phototherapy for localized, stable vitiligo patches. Faster response than general NB-UVB. Available at tertiary centres.

3. Systemic Treatments (Active/Spreading Vitiligo)

Oral Minipulse Betamethasone (OMBP): Betamethasone 5mg on 2 consecutive days per week for 3–6 months. Standard IADVL Indian protocol for rapidly spreading vitiligo to halt progression.

Oral Dexamethasone 2.5mg (pulse): Alternative pulse steroid for active spreading vitiligo.

4. Surgical Treatments (Stable Vitiligo >2 Years)

Suction Blister Grafting/Split-thickness Grafting: For stable segmental/focal vitiligo not responding to medical therapy. Specialist referral required.

Melanocyte Keratinocyte Transplant Procedure (MKTP): Government-supported procedure at selected centres; best for stable resistant vitiligo.

5. Photoprotection & Supportive

Sunscreen SPF 30+ (Broad-Spectrum UVA+UVB): Mandatory for all vitiligo patients — depigmented skin burns easily. Prevents Koebner phenomenon from solar-induced injury.

Vitamin B12 + Folic Acid Tablets: Supplementation for commonly associated deficiency; may slow disease progression.

Copper supplements: Copper supplementation supports tyrosinase enzyme function in melanin synthesis.

Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream (15g): Topical steroid for active stable vitiligo patches. Available at PMBJP stores.

Betamethasone 0.5mg Tablets: For oral minipulse protocol in active vitiligo. PMBJP stores.

Sunscreen SPF 30+ Lotion (50g): Photoprotection for depigmented patches. PMBJP stores.

Vitamin D3 + Calcium Tablets (60 tabs): Supplementation for Vitamin D deficiency associated with vitiligo.

Vitamin B12 500mcg Tablets: For B12 deficiency associated with vitiligo progression. Jan Aushadhi Kendras.

Folic Acid 5mg Tablets: For folate deficiency; supports repigmentation. PMBJP stores.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
BAKUCHI — THE CLASSICAL AYURVEDIC VITILIGO TREATMENT

Bakuchi (Psoralea corylifolia) — most important Ayurvedic treatment for Shwitra (vitiligo); specifically described in Charaka Samhita Chikitsa Sthana Chapter 7; contains Psoralen and Bakuchiol — natural photosensitisers functionally equivalent to medical PUVA psoralen; topical Bakuchi oil + regulated sun exposure = traditional PUVA; oral Bakuchi seed powder 500 mg with milk

Critical instruction: Bakuchi requires calibrated sun exposure — apply topical oil, wait 30 minutes, expose to morning or evening gentle sun for 5–10 minutes initially; gradually increase by 5 minutes/session; overexposure = severe burns; never use without medical supervision

Chitraka (Plumbago zeylanica) — used alongside Bakuchi in classical Ayurvedic vitiligo formulations; photosensitising and melanocyte-stimulating

SYSTEMIC

Khadirarista — blood-purifying fermented formulation; Khadira (Acacia catechu) base; classic Kushtha (skin disease) treatment; anti-inflammatory

Tikta Ghrita — bitter-component ghee preparation; blood purifying in chronic skin disease; reduces autoimmune inflammatory component

Arogyavardhini Vati — systemic liver and blood-purifying action; anti-inflammatory for autoimmune skin

Haridra (Turmeric) oral — anti-inflammatory; reduces autoimmune activity; 500 mg BD

COPPER SUPPLEMENTATION

Copper is an essential cofactor for tyrosinase enzyme (converts L-tyrosine to melanin); Copper water therapy (Tamra Jal — water stored overnight in copper vessel, consumed in morning) — traditional Indian practice; copper supplementation adjunct; may support melanogenesis where copper deficiency is a factor

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Bakuchi Churna + Coconut oil — topical preparation; traditional practitioner formulation

Kanchanar Guggulu — thyroid and autoimmune component support; particularly where vitiligo + thyroid disease coexist

Brahmi + Ashwagandha — neural mechanism support (particularly relevant in segmental vitiligo with neural hypothesis)

DIET AND LIFESTYLE (Ayurvedic)

Avoid Viruddha Ahara (incompatible food combinations) — specifically dairy + fish combination; reduce sour, fermented, and spicy foods (Pitta-aggravating from Charaka's vitiligo dietary framework)

IMPORTANT NOTE

Bakuchi is the only Indian traditional treatment directly validated by pharmacological isolation of its active compound (Psoralen) which is also used in mainstream medical PUVA therapy. This is a textbook example of Ayurvedic ethnopharmacology validated by Western pharmacology. However, uncontrolled use without sun-timing calibration causes severe sunburn. Only use standardised Bakuchi preparations from quality-certified manufacturers under dermatologist guidance.
diseases.india_specific:
HIGHEST PREVALENCE IN WORLD

India has one of the globally highest vitiligo prevalence rates (1–2% vs 0.5–1% globally); likely related to genetic polymorphisms (HLA-A2, PTPN22, CTLA4 variants), UV-triggered autoimmune initiation, and consanguineous marriage increasing genetic risk clustering

SOCIAL STIGMA AND RURAL INDIA

In rural India, vitiligo on face, neck, hands = severe social exclusion; matrimonial rejection routinely documented; counselling and psychological support are as important as pharmacological treatment

BAKUCHI — INDIA'S TRADITIONAL PUVA

Bakuchi (Psoralea corylifolia) has been used in Indian traditional medicine for Shwitra (vitiligo) since Charaka Samhita (classical text ~400 BCE); contains natural Psoralen — the same photosensitiser used in medical PUVA therapy; most significant traditional Indian vitiligo treatment; requires dermatologist guidance

NB-UVB PHOTOTHERAPY ACCESS

NB-UVB phototherapy (gold standard for generalised NSV) is available at AIIMS, government medical colleges, and large private dermatology centres; expensive for smaller cities; significant inequity in access to evidence-based vitiligo treatment

JAK INHIBITOR BREAKTHROUGH

Ruxolitinib cream 1.5% (JAK1/2 inhibitor) is the first FDA-approved (2022) targeted therapy specifically for NSV; provides >75% facial repigmentation in clinical trials; not yet widely available in India due to cost; represents most significant advance in decades

SUN DAMAGE RISK

Depigmented patches have zero melanin UV protection; sunburn and long-term UV damage at lesion sites; SPF50+ mandatory on patches outdoors
diseases.important_notes:
ORAL MINIPULSE DEXAMETHASONE — INDIA'S CONTRIBUTION TO GLOBAL VITILIGO THERAPY: Oral Minipulse (OMP) therapy with Dexamethasone 2.5 mg on two consecutive days per week was developed and evidence-based in India by Professor Y.K. Pasricha at AIIMS New Delhi. This is one of the few genuinely India-originated clinical protocols that achieved global recognition. Key point: OMP is for STOPPING SPREAD (disease stabilisation) — NOT for causing repigmentation. Rapidly progressive vitiligo with new patches appearing weekly is the indication. Duration: 3–6 months. Monthly monitoring: fasting glucose (steroid hyperglycaemia), blood pressure, weight. Once stable × 3 months: stop OMP and initiate NB-UVB or topical tacrolimus for repigmentation phase.

JAK INHIBITORS — THE MOST SIGNIFICANT VITILIGO BREAKTHROUGH IN DECADES: Ruxolitinib cream 1.5% (JAK1/2 inhibitor) received FDA approval in July 2022 as the first-ever drug specifically approved for non-segmental vitiligo. Clinical trials show 30–75% repigmentation (VASI75) at 24 weeks, particularly on the face and acrofacial regions where previous therapies had limited efficacy. Mechanism: JAK1/2 inhibition blocks IFN-γ/CXCL9/10 signalling — the primary pathway by which cytotoxic T-cells destroy melanocytes. For Indian patients, this drug is currently expensive and imported, but oral JAK inhibitors (Tofacitinib 5 mg BD, Baricitinib) — already available in India for RA and other conditions — are showing significant vitiligo repigmentation in clinical trials and compassionate use at specialist centres.

VITILIGO STIGMA COUNSELLING IS A MANDATORY PART OF TREATMENT IN INDIA: Dermatologists treating vitiligo in India must address the psychosocial dimension as integral — not optional — to their management: (1) Proactively address stigma at first consultation; provide factual education that vitiligo is NOT contagious, NOT leprosy, NOT infectious; (2) Provide Vitiligo Society India (VSI) information; (3) Screen for depression using PHQ-9 and anxiety using GAD-7; (4) Refer to a psychologist when PHQ-9 ≥10; (5) Prescribe cosmetic camouflage IMMEDIATELY — waiting months for phototherapy to work while a patient faces daily social discrimination is unacceptable; camouflage provides rapid QOL improvement while phototherapy works. Phototherapy takes 3–6 months — patients need support in that interval.

diseases.name: Urticaria
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis & Classification

UAS7 (Urticaria Activity Score 7): Daily wheals (0–3) + itch intensity (0–3) summed over 7 days. Score 0–6=well controlled, 7–15=mild, 16–27=moderate, 28–42=severe. Monitors treatment response per EAACI/IADVL guidelines.

Duration Classification: Acute urticaria = <6 weeks. Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) = >6 weeks; daily/near-daily wheals. Diagnosis is primarily clinical.

Autologous Serum Skin Test (ASST): Intradermal injection of patient's own serum — positive whealing response in autoimmune CSU. Available at dermatology centres. Not routine at PHC.

Dermographometer Testing: Calibrated pressure applied to skin to confirm symptomatic dermographism.

2. Basic Investigations (Chronic Urticaria >6 Weeks)

CBC with Differential: Eosinophilia suggests parasitic infestation or drug reaction. Leukocytosis may indicate infection-triggered urticaria.

ESR & CRP: Elevated in urticarial vasculitis and systemic disease-associated urticaria. Differentiates from simple CSU.

Thyroid Function Tests (TSH, T3, T4) + Anti-TPO Antibodies: Mandatory in all CSU patients — autoimmune thyroid disease is a major reversible cause in India.

Total IgE Level: Elevated in allergic/atopic urticaria. Baseline before Omalizumab (anti-IgE biologic) therapy.

3. Trigger-Specific Tests

Stool Examination (Ova & Parasites): Mandatory in all CSU patients in India. Identifies Ascaris, Giardia, Strongyloides — hookworm as urticaria trigger. Simple, accessible at PHC level.

H. pylori Testing — Urea Breath Test / H. pylori Stool Antigen: Identifies gastric H. pylori — eradication resolves CSU in subset. Stool antigen test available at district hospitals.

ANA / Anti-dsDNA: Screens for SLE-associated urticaria (urticarial vasculitis).

Hepatitis B & C Serology: Hepatitis B/C chronic infection — recognized triggers of urticaria and vasculitis in Indian patients with high HBV prevalence.

4. Food/Drug Provocation (Specialist Centre)

Open or Double-Blind Food Challenge: For suspected food-triggered urticaria — conducted under medical supervision with resuscitation available. Not at PHC level.

5. Differentiation Tests

Skin Biopsy: For urticarial vasculitis (wheals lasting >24 hours, painful, leave bruising). Histology shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis — requires specialist management.

Complement Levels (C3, C4, CH50): Low in hereditary angioedema (HAE) — C1-INH deficiency. Presents with recurrent severe angioedema without wheals.

6. Indian Guidelines

IADVL Urticaria Guidelines 2021: CSU defined as >6 weeks; mandatory H. pylori + thyroid + parasite workup in India. UAS7 for monitoring. Stepwise: antihistamine → increase dose → Omalizumab.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line: Second-Generation Antihistamines (Non-Sedating)

Cetirizine 10mg once daily oral: First-choice. Onset 1 hour, 24-hour duration. Minimal sedation. Safe in pregnancy (avoid first trimester). Syrup available for children.

Levocetirizine 5mg once daily oral: More potent than Cetirizine. Preferred for severe pruritus. Once daily — high compliance.

Loratadine 10mg once daily oral: Non-sedating. Safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. First-line for daytime use.

Fexofenadine 120–180mg once daily oral: Most non-sedating antihistamine. Ideal for patients requiring alertness (drivers, machine operators).

Desloratadine 5mg once daily oral: Active metabolite of Loratadine; more potent. Once daily.

2. Up-Dosing (IADVL Step 2)

Cetirizine/Levocetirizine Up to 4x Standard Dose: IADVL/EAACI guidelines recommend up-dosing second-generation H1 antihistamines up to 4x daily dose before step-up to add-ons or biologic.

3. Add-On Therapies (Partial Responders)

Montelukast 10mg/day oral: H1-leukotriene receptor antagonist; add-on for aspirin/NSAID-exacerbated urticaria and chronic urticaria partially responding to antihistamines.

Ranitidine/Famotidine (H2 blockers): Add-on to H1 antihistamine; modest benefit in some CSU patients.

4. Short-Course Steroids (Acute Severe / Anaphylaxis)

Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (3–5 days): For acute severe urticaria flares or urticaria with early angioedema. Not for chronic use.

Hydrocortisone 200mg IV + Chlorphenamine 10mg IV: For anaphylaxis with urticaria — hospital emergency protocol.

5. Adrenaline (Anaphylaxis Emergency)

Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 0.5mg 1:1000 IM (thigh): FIRST and IMMEDIATE treatment for anaphylaxis with urticaria + bronchospasm/hypotension. ANMs/ASHAs trained in administration urgently.

6. Biologic (Step 4 — Refractory CSU)

Omalizumab 150–300mg SC every 4 weeks: Anti-IgE biologic. CDSCO approved. Highly effective for antihistamine-refractory CSU. Requires specialist centre.

7. Trigger-Specific Treatment

Albendazole 400mg single dose: Deworming for parasite-associated urticaria — simple, safe, and potentially curative in endemic India.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Haridra (Turmeric) Khand: Classical anti-allergic and antihistaminic Ayurvedic formulation. Haridra 3–5g with warm milk twice daily — traditional treatment for Sheetapitta (urticaria). Potent mast cell stabilizing activity.

Haridra/Turmeric Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India): Standardized curcumin extract. Clinical evidence for anti-inflammatory and anti-allergic activity. 500mg twice daily after meals.

Giloy (Guduchi) Tablets (Himalaya): Immunomodulatory herb clinically studied for chronic urticaria in CCRAS trials. Reduces frequency and severity of CSU flares. 1–2 tablets twice daily.

Khadirarishta 450ml (AVP/Dabur): Classical fermented formulation with Khadira (Acacia catechu). Blood purifier and anti-allergic. 15–20ml with equal water twice daily after meals.

Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) Powder: Mild laxative and detoxifying herb; removes Ama (toxins) that trigger Pitta-associated urticaria. 3g at bedtime with warm water.

Mahamanjisthadi Kashaya: Classical decoction with Manjistha, Neem, and Guduchi for blood purification in allergic skin diseases. 15ml twice daily before meals.

Panchatikta Ghrita: Classical medicated ghee for Pitta-dominant allergic conditions. Anti-inflammatory and immune-regulatory.

Shirisha (Albizia lebbeck) Bark Powder: Clinically studied Ayurvedic anti-allergic herb; demonstrated efficacy in urticaria and allergic rhinitis. 3–5g twice daily with honey.

Neem Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India): Blood purifier and antihistaminic properties for recurring urticaria associated with parasitic infestation and toxin accumulation.

Trikatu Churna: Digestive spice combination (Ginger, Black Pepper, Long Pepper) — improves Agni, reduces Ama, anti-allergic in Kapha-Pitta urticaria. 1–3g with honey before meals.

Triphala Churna: General detoxifier and antioxidant; useful when urticaria is associated with digestive dysfunction and parasitic infestation. 5g at bedtime.
diseases.india_specific:
Food and Drug Triggers: Common Indian dietary triggers include prawns, crabs, shellfish, groundnuts, and sesame. NSAIDs (widely self-purchased in India) are the most common drug trigger for chronic urticaria exacerbations.

Parasitic Infestation Co-Association: Intestinal parasites (Ascaris, Giardia, Strongyloides) are a significant cause of chronic urticaria in India. Deworming resolves urticaria in ~20% of cases in endemic regions — mandatory exclusion.

H. pylori Infection: Helicobacter pylori positivity rates in India are 60–80%; H. pylori-associated gastric infection is a recognized trigger of chronic spontaneous urticaria. Eradication therapy may resolve CSU.

Thyroid Autoimmunity: Autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's thyroiditis) is a significant cause of chronic spontaneous urticaria in Indian women — TSH + Anti-TPO mandatory screening.

Insect Bites and Stings: Hymenoptera (bee, wasp) stings and mosquito bites are common urticaria triggers in rural India — important differentiator from chronic spontaneous urticaria.
diseases.important_notes:
Anaphylaxis is a Life-Threatening Emergency: Urticaria with throat tightening, difficulty breathing, dizziness, or low blood pressure = ANAPHYLAXIS. Immediate Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 0.5mg IM is the ONLY first-line treatment. Adrenaline auto-injectors or vials should be available at every PHC/CHC. Transport to hospital IMMEDIATELY. Antihistamines and steroids are SECONDARY — they do not reverse anaphylaxis.

Mandatory Parasite Exclusion in India: All patients with CSU (>6 weeks) in India MUST have stool examination for ova and parasites BEFORE long-term antihistamine therapy. Treating the underlying helminth infection cures urticaria without long-term medications in a significant proportion of Indian patients.

Avoid NSAID Use: NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen, diclofenac) are the most common cause of urticaria flares in patients with CSU. Patients should avoid all OTC NSAIDs self-purchased from chemists. For pain, use Paracetamol only. Communicate this clearly.

Antihistamines Are Safe for Long-Term Use: Second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, levocetirizine, loratadine) are safe for long-term use. Patients must NOT stop antihistamines abruptly once started for CSU — this triggers rebound. Structured step-down under physician guidance only.

Consult Specialist for Chronic Cases: Urticaria persisting beyond 6 weeks without clear trigger warrants dermatologist referral for systematic workup including thyroid autoimmunity, H. pylori, and consideration of Omalizumab.
confirmation_tests_curated: UAS7 (Urticaria Activity Score 7): Daily wheals (0–3) + itch intensity (0–3) summed over 7 days. Score 0–6=well controlled, 7–15=mild, 16–27=moderate, 28–42=severe. Monitors treatment response per EAACI/IADVL guidelines.
Duration Classification: Acute urticaria = <6 weeks. Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) = >6 weeks; daily/near-daily wheals. Diagnosis is primarily clinical.
Autologous Serum Skin Test (ASST): Intradermal injection of patient's own serum — positive whealing response in autoimmune CSU. Available at dermatology centres. Not routine at PHC.
Dermographometer Testing: Calibrated pressure applied to skin to confirm symptomatic dermographism.

diseases.name: Acne Vulgaris
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Grading (Primary Tool — No Lab Tests Needed for Typical Acne)

Global Acne Grading System (GAGS): Face, chest, back, shoulders each scored by lesion type and density. Total score: Mild 1–18, Moderate 19–30, Severe 31–38, Very Severe >39.

IGA Scale (Investigator Global Assessment): 0 (clear) to 4 (severe). Standard clinical endpoint. Grade 0–1 = clear/almost clear; Grade 3–4 = moderate/severe.

Lesion Count: Count open/closed comedones, papules, pustules, nodules separately on face/trunk. Tracks treatment response. Simple tool for PHC monitoring.

Distribution Assessment: Forehead, nose, chin (T-zone) = typical adolescent acne. Jaw, chin, lower face = hormonal/PCOS acne. Back/chest = truncal acne. Guides treatment choice.

2. Hormonal/Metabolic Investigations (Female Patients with Adult Acne)

Serum Testosterone (Total & Free) + DHEA-S + LH/FSH Ratio: Elevated in PCOS — mandatory in adult females with persistent or worsening acne, irregular periods, hirsutism, or weight gain.

Serum Cortisol (8am): Elevated in Cushing's syndrome — rare but important cause of widespread sudden-onset acne.

Fasting Blood Sugar + Insulin Resistance (HOMA-IR): PCOS associated with insulin resistance — worsens hyperandrogenism driving hormonal acne.

3. Microbiological Tests (Atypical/Resistant Cases)

Comedone/Pustule Culture & Sensitivity: For gram-negative folliculitis (sudden pustular flares after prolonged antibiotic use). Rules out bacterial resistance.

KOH Mount / Malassezia Folliculitis Assessment: Pityrosporum (Malassezia) folliculitis mimics acne — monomorphic papulopustules on upper back/chest in humid conditions; responds to antifungals not antibiotics.

4. Investigations Before Isotretinoin

CBC: Baseline before Isotretinoin; Isotretinoin rarely causes leukopenia.

LFT (Liver Function Tests): Isotretinoin is hepatotoxic — baseline and monthly monitoring mandatory.

Fasting Lipid Profile: Isotretinoin raises triglycerides significantly — baseline and monthly testing required.

Pregnancy Test (Urine/Serum beta-hCG): MANDATORY before isotretinoin in all females of childbearing age — Isotretinoin is highly teratogenic (Category X).

5. Indian Guidelines

IADVL Acne Guidelines 2022: GAGS-based grading. Grade 1–2 = topical only. Grade 3 = topical + systemic antibiotics. Grade 4 = Isotretinoin. PCOS workup mandatory for adult females, PIH management integrated into Indian guidelines.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Treatments

Benzoyl Peroxide 2.5–5% Gel/Cream: Anti-comedonal + antibacterial. First-line for comedonal and mild inflammatory acne. Apply once nightly; start at 2.5% to reduce irritation. Does NOT cause antibiotic resistance.

Clindamycin 1% Gel/Lotion: Topical antibiotic; apply twice daily for papulopustular acne. ALWAYS combine with Benzoyl Peroxide to prevent C. acnes resistance development.

Adapalene 0.1%/0.3% Gel (Retinoid): Anti-comedonal, anti-inflammatory, prevents new comedone formation. Apply at night. First-line for comedonal acne. Photosensitizing — sunscreen mandatory.

Tretinoin 0.025–0.05% Cream/Gel: Topical retinoid. Comedolytic and anti-inflammatory. More effective but more irritating than Adapalene. Absolute contraindication in pregnancy.

Azelaic Acid 15–20% Gel: Anti-comedonal + antibacterial + hyperpigmentation-reducing. Excellent for PIH-associated acne in Indian skin. Safe in pregnancy.

Clindamycin + Benzoyl Peroxide Gel (Combination): Fixed-dose combination reduces resistance risk. Highly effective for Grade 2–3 papulopustular acne.

2. Systemic Antibiotics (Moderate-Severe Acne, Grade 3)

Doxycycline 100mg once/twice daily oral (10–12 weeks): First-line systemic antibiotic. Anti-inflammatory + antibacterial. Take with food; avoid lying down for 30 minutes (esophagitis risk). Photosensitizing.

Azithromycin 500mg alternate days or pulse dosing: Second-line systemic antibiotic; useful for antibiotic-intolerant patients. Lower resistance risk with pulse dosing.

Minocycline 50–100mg daily oral: Alternative to Doxycycline. More anti-inflammatory. Risk of drug-induced lupus and hyperpigmentation.

3. Hormonal Therapy (PCOS/Hormonal Female Acne)

Combined Oral Contraceptive Pill (Ethinyloestradiol + Norgestimate or Drospirenone): Anti-androgenic; reduces sebum and acne in females with PCOS. Requires gynecology co-prescription.

Spironolactone 50–100mg/day oral: Anti-androgen; effective for hormonal female acne. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Monitor potassium.

4. Oral Isotretinoin (Severe/Nodulo-cystic Acne — Grade 4)

Isotretinoin 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (6 months): Gold standard for severe/cystic acne. Achieves long-term remission in 80% after single course. Monthly LFT + lipids + pregnancy test mandatory.

5. Adjunctive

Sunscreen SPF 30+ broad-spectrum: Mandatory with topical retinoids (Adapalene, Tretinoin) and Isotretinoin. Prevents UV-worsened PIH.

Niacinamide 4% Cream/Serum: Reduces PIH, sebum control, mild anti-inflammatory. Excellent adjunct for Indian skin.

Neem (Azadirachta indica) Face Wash/Gel (Himalaya/Biotique): Topical antibacterial and anti-inflammatory. Neem contains nimbidin and azadirachtin which inhibit P. acnes growth. Apply twice daily face wash.

Neem Capsules 500mg (Himalaya): Internal blood purifier. Anti-androgenic, anti-inflammatory, and sebum-reducing properties. 1 capsule twice daily after meals.

Kumkumadi Tailam (Kottakkal/Biotique Starberry): Traditionally formulated saffron-based oil for Mukhadushika (acne). Reduces PIH, brightens skin, and has anti-inflammatory properties. Apply face drops at night.

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) Capsules: Blood purifier with anti-androgenic and antioxidant properties. Reduces PIH and recurrent acne in Pitta-predominant constitution. 500mg twice daily.

Haridra Khand (Turmeric preparation): Internal Pitta-pacifying formulation with anti-inflammatory and antibacterial curcumin. Reduces acne-associated inflammation. 3–5g with warm milk daily.

Chandanasavam (Sandal Fermented Liquid): Classical cooling Pitta-pacifying formulation for inflammatory acne with burning sensation. 15–20ml twice daily with water.

Shatavari Churna (for PCOS-related acne in females): Phytoestrogenic and adaptogenic herb; balances female hormonal dysregulation contributing to acne in PCOS. 3–5g with warm milk.

Kaishore Guggulu: Tridoshic blood purifier with Guggulu and Triphala. For deep-seated chronic cystic acne with Vata-Kapha involvement. 2 tablets twice daily.

Lodhradi Lepa: Classical topical paste with Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa), Dhanyaka, and Vacha for acne lesions.

Himalaya Acne-n-Pimple Cream: Widely available clinically-tested Ayurvedic formulation containing Lodhra, Vacha, and Manjistha. Apply twice daily on acne lesions.

Dabur Gulabari Rose Water: Natural astringent and anti-inflammatory toner. Reduces sebum, soothes inflammation, and minimizes pores in acne-prone Indian skin.

Aloe Vera Gel (Pure, cold-pressed): Antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, wound-healing. Reduces acne papules, soothes inflamed skin, and helps heal PIH macules.
diseases.india_specific:
POST-INFLAMMATORY HYPERPIGMENTATION (PIH) IN INDIAN SKIN

Fitzpatrick III-V Indian skin produces excessive melanin after ANY inflammatory insult; PIH from acne can be more distressing than acne itself; persists months-years; requires separate treatment (topical depigmenting agents)

HOT HUMID CLIMATE

Tropical heat and humidity increase sebum production, sweat-induced comedogenesis, and Malassezia co-infection worsening back acne; seasonal peaks in summer months

TOPICAL STEROID MISUSE — INDIA EPIDEMIC

OTC fairness creams and beauty creams in India widely contain undisclosed potent topical steroids (Clobetasol propionate most common); steroid-induced acne (perioral, papulopustular distribution), steroid rosacea, skin atrophy, and steroid dependence are epidemic; CDSCO has intervened but enforcement incomplete

Always ask acne patients: 'Which creams do you apply to your face?' and check all products specifically

ANTIBIOTIC RESISTANCE

C. acnes erythromycin resistance >50% in India; tetracycline resistance rising; prolonged antibiotic use without Benzoyl Peroxide combination drives resistance and reduces treatment efficacy

PCOS PREVALENCE

PCOS affects 10–20% reproductive-age Indian women — among highest global rates; acne is a primary PCOS feature; hormonal workup essential in ANY woman with persistent adult female-pattern acne

ISOTRETINOIN ACCESS AND MONITORING

Isotretinoin widely available in India with affordable generics; however routine monitoring (lipids, LFT, CBC, pregnancy test) frequently omitted in non-specialist settings; teratogenicity risks inadequately counselled
diseases.important_notes:
ISOTRETINOIN TERATOGENICITY — PREGNANCY PREVENTION IS NON-NEGOTIABLE: Isotretinoin causes severe, characteristic birth defects (craniofacial, cardiac, CNS malformations) in 25–30% of exposed pregnancies with no safe exposure threshold. ALL female patients of childbearing potential MUST have: (1) Negative urine pregnancy test before starting; (2) Two reliable contraceptive methods throughout treatment and for 1 month after stopping; (3) Monthly pregnancy test repeats. In India, isotretinoin is widely accessible but often prescribed without adequate counselling — this is a patient safety failure. Male patients: isotretinoin is not teratogenic via sperm; standard medical guidance does not restrict their partners.

STEROID ACNE FROM FAIRNESS CREAMS — INDIA'S DERMATOLOGY EPIDEMIC: CDSCO data and multiple Indian publications confirm OTC 'fairness creams' and 'beauty creams' sold across India commonly contain undisclosed potent topical steroids — most frequently Clobetasol propionate (the strongest Class I steroid). Long-term use causes: steroid-induced acne (perioral, papulopustular, follicular pattern), steroid rosacea, facial atrophy, telangiectasia, and rebound worsening on stopping. Always ask patients specifically which creams they use. Perioral acne pattern + telangiectasia + worsening on stopping any cream = steroid-induced dermatitis. Management: stop the offending cream (expect 2–6 weeks of worsening rebound), use gentle non-comedogenic moisturiser, add Azelaic acid and low-potency steroid taper.

NEVER PRESCRIBE ANTIBIOTICS FOR ACNE WITHOUT BENZOYL PEROXIDE: Antibiotic resistance in C. acnes is a growing global and Indian problem — erythromycin resistance >50%, tetracycline resistance rising. The evidence-based, guideline-recommended measure against resistance: ALWAYS co-prescribe Benzoyl Peroxide (minimum 2.5–5%) with ANY topical (clindamycin) or oral antibiotic. BPO is bactericidal — it kills bacteria rather than inhibiting them — and does not generate resistance itself. The combination is synergistic and eliminates the resistance risk. Duration: oral antibiotics maximum 3 months, then transition to retinoid + BPO maintenance-only regimen.

diseases.name: Pemphigus Vulgaris
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis

Nikolsky Sign Test: Firm lateral pressure on perilesional normal skin — positive if new bulla forms or epidermis peels. Highly suggestive of pemphigus; simple bedside test.

Asboe-Hansen Test: Pressure on intact bulla extends it laterally. Confirms intraepidermal location of split.

Oral Cavity Examination: Full assessment of extent of oral erosions — grading severity and anatomical involvement guides treatment intensity.

2. Histopathology (Gold Standard)

Skin Biopsy (H&E Staining): Peri-lesional or early lesional skin — shows suprabasal acantholysis (separation of epidermal cells), Tzanck cells (rounded acantholytic cells), and basal layer 'tombstoning'.

3. Immunofluorescence (Diagnostic Confirmation — Mandatory)

Direct Immunofluorescence (DIF): Biopsy of perilesional NORMAL-appearing skin. IgG (and often C3) deposition in intercellular spaces of epidermis — fishnet/chicken-wire pattern. Pathognomonic. Sensitivity 95–100%.

Indirect Immunofluorescence (IDIF): Patient serum tested on monkey oesophagus substrate. Detects circulating anti-desmoglein antibodies; titre correlates with disease activity. Available at dermatology teaching hospitals.

4. Serological Tests

Anti-Desmoglein 1 (Dsg1) IgG ELISA: Elevated in pemphigus foliaceus and facial/scalp-predominant disease. Index >20 = positive.

Anti-Desmoglein 3 (Dsg3) IgG ELISA: Elevated in pemphigus vulgaris — especially with mucosal involvement. Index >20 = positive. Both Dsg1+Dsg3 positive = mucocutaneous PV (most severe).

Anti-Dsg titre monitoring: Titres correlate with disease activity. Rise predicts relapse — used to guide immunosuppressant dose decisions.

5. Routine Workup and Pre-Treatment Tests

CBC, LFT, RFT, Fasting Blood Sugar, HbA1c: Baseline before systemic corticosteroids + immunosuppressants. Steroid-induced diabetes very common especially in Indian patients.

Serum Albumin + Total Protein: Hypoalbuminaemia from protein-losing cutaneous erosions — indicates disease severity and nutritional status.

Chest X-Ray + Mantoux Test / IGRA: Mandatory TB screening before starting immunosuppressants/rituximab in India. TB reactivation is a major cause of pemphigus mortality in India.

Bone Density (DEXA Scan): Before long-term corticosteroid therapy — high osteoporosis risk in Indian pemphigus patients.

Hepatitis B Surface Antigen (HBsAg) + Anti-HCV: Mandatory before Rituximab — HBV reactivation risk.

6. Indian Expert Guidelines

IADVL Pemphigus Guidelines 2020: DIF is mandatory for all suspected pemphigus; Rituximab + low-dose prednisolone as first-line (moderate-severe); anti-Dsg ELISA for monitoring. Pulse IV dexamethasone for rapid disease control.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Immunosuppression

Prednisolone 1–1.5mg/kg/day oral: Traditional high-dose corticosteroid for pemphigus. Taper once new blister formation stops. Prolonged high-dose causes significant steroid complications — move to steroid-sparing agents early.

Dexamethasone Pulse (IV Cyclophosphamide Pulse Therapy — DCP): IV Dexamethasone 100mg in 500ml NS over 8 hours x 3 days monthly + oral Cyclophosphamide 50mg daily in between. IADVL-recommended Indian protocol for cost-effective pemphigus management where Rituximab unavailable.

Rituximab 1000mg IV (2 doses 14 days apart) or 500mg x4 weekly: Anti-CD20 B-cell depleting biologic. Now first-line for moderate-severe pemphigus per international and IADVL 2020 guidelines. Long-term remission in 70–90% after single course.

2. Steroid-Sparing Agents (Adjuvant Immunosuppressants)

Azathioprine 1–2.5mg/kg/day oral: Most widely used steroid-sparing agent in India (affordability). TPMT enzyme testing before initiation ideal but not always available. Monitor CBC monthly.

Mycophenolate Mofetil 1.5–2g/day oral (divided doses): Effective steroid-sparing agent; better tolerated than Azathioprine. Increasingly available in India.

Cyclophosphamide 1–2mg/kg/day oral: For refractory pemphigus. Risk of haemorrhagic cystitis, infertility, and malignancy. Use with mesna if IV.

3. Wound Care and Supportive Treatment

Normal Saline Soaks / Potassium Permanganate 1:10000 Soaks: Twice daily wound soaks for denuded erosions — removes debris, prevents secondary infection.

Mupirocin 2% Ointment or Framycetin Gauze: Topical antibiotic dressing for infected erosions.

Oral Hygiene Protocol: Chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash every 4–6 hours for oral erosions — reduces secondary bacterial/fungal colonization.

4. Prophylaxis During Immunosuppression

Cotrimoxazole 480mg alternate days: PCP (Pneumocystis pneumonia) prophylaxis during high-dose steroid + immunosuppressant — mandatory.

Calcium 500mg + Vitamin D3 400IU twice daily: Osteoporosis prophylaxis with long-term corticosteroids — mandatory in all Indian pemphigus patients.

Proton Pump Inhibitor (Pantoprazole 40mg/day): Gastroprotection during high-dose corticosteroid therapy.

Fluconazole 150mg weekly oral: Antifungal prophylaxis for Candida superinfection of oral erosions during immunosuppression.
