diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Gandhaka Rasayana: Classical Ayurvedic formulation with purified Sulphur (Shuddha Gandhaka). Used as blood purifier and for chronic skin diseases including bullous dermatoses. Anti-inflammatory and antipruritic. 500mg–1g twice daily under physician supervision.

Mahatikta Ghrita: Classical medicated ghee with Tikta (bitter) herbs including Neem, Guduchi, Patola, and Kiratatikta. Comprehensive Panchakarma preparatory and oral formulation for severe chronic skin diseases. 10–20ml on empty stomach under supervision.

Panchatikta Ghrita Guggulu: Anti-inflammatory Pitta-Vata formulation for inflammatory skin diseases. Guduchi, Neem, Patola, and Guggulu combination. 2 tablets twice daily.

Kaishore Guggulu: Tridoshic blood purifier for deep-seated inflammatory skin conditions with systemic involvement. 2 tablets twice daily with warm water.

Neem Kashaya: Internal Neem bark decoction — blood purifier and anti-inflammatory for Pitta-dominant severe skin diseases. Supportive adjunct only.

Sariva (Hemidesmus indicus) Decoction: Classical blood cooler and purifier for Pitta diseases. Anti-inflammatory and antitoxic. 30ml twice daily before meals.

Triphala Guggulu: Wound-healing, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant classical formulation. Supportive role in erosion healing.

Jatyadi Tailam: Classical topical medicated oil with Jasminum grandiflorum, Neem, and wound-healing herbs. Apply to non-infected erosions for wound healing and pain relief.

Chandanadi Tailam: Cooling, anti-inflammatory topical oil for burning, painful erosions.

Virechana (Panchakarma): Therapeutic purgation under qualified Vaidya for Pitta removal in severe pemphigus. Only when patient is stable and able to tolerate Panchakarma.

CRITICAL NOTE — Ayurveda as Supportive Adjunct Only: Pemphigus vulgaris is a life-threatening autoimmune bullous disease requiring systemic immunosuppression (steroids + Rituximab/Azathioprine). Ayurvedic treatment is SUPPORTIVE ONLY — wound care assistance, nutritional support, improving quality of life, and managing steroid side effects. Ayurveda CANNOT replace systemic immunosuppression.
diseases.india_specific:
Highest Global Incidence in India: India has the highest incidence of pemphigus vulgaris globally — 4.4 per 100,000 in some studies. Particularly prevalent in Ashkenazi Jewish, Sephardic Jewish, and Indian (especially Jewish community in India) populations with specific HLA-DRB1 haplotypes.

Delayed Diagnosis — Oral Ulcer Misdiagnosis: In rural India, oral pemphigus erosions are frequently misdiagnosed as aphthous stomatitis or oral Herpes for 6–18 months. This delays immunosuppressive treatment and results in widespread skin involvement.

Corticosteroid Complications in Indian Patients: Long-term high-dose systemic corticosteroids (required for pemphigus control) cause steroid-induced diabetes, hypertension, osteoporosis, and cataracts — all conditions with high baseline prevalence in India.

Secondary Skin Infections: India's hot humid climate, poor wound care access, and widespread antibiotic-resistant organisms (MRSA, Gram-negative bacteria) cause severe secondary infections of pemphigus erosions — major cause of mortality.

Rituximab Access: Rituximab (biologic B-cell therapy) is now first-line alongside steroids per international guidelines. Access and affordability remain barriers in India — currently available through government schemes at select centres only.
diseases.important_notes:
EMERGENCY — Pemphigus is LIFE-THREATENING Without Treatment: Untreated pemphigus vulgaris has >90% mortality from sepsis, protein loss, and fluid/electrolyte imbalance from widespread denuded skin. Any patient with widespread blisters/erosions especially in mouth must be urgently referred to a dermatology specialist or tertiary hospital. Do NOT attempt management at PHC level beyond initial wound care and referral.

Early Diagnosis Saves Lives — Do Not Miss Oral Pemphigus: In India, oral pemphigus erosions are misdiagnosed for months as mouth ulcers. Key differentiator: pemphigus oral erosions are IRREGULAR, RAGGED, VERY PAINFUL, do NOT heal without treatment, and are NOT circular like aphthous ulcers. Any recurrent painful oral erosion not responding to routine treatment for >4 weeks requires immediate dermatology referral.

TB Screening Before Immunosuppression is Non-Negotiable: Every pemphigus patient must have Mantoux test or IGRA + chest X-ray BEFORE starting prednisolone, Azathioprine, or Rituximab. India's TB burden makes reactivation under immunosuppression a direct life-threatening risk. Active TB must be treated first; latent TB needs isoniazid prophylaxis.

Steroid Side Effect Monitoring is Mandatory: Long-term high-dose prednisolone causes steroid-induced diabetes, hypertension, cataracts, osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, and opportunistic infections. Monthly fasting blood sugar, blood pressure monitoring, and annual bone density is essential for all Indian pemphigus patients on steroids.

Patient Support and Diet: Extensive oral erosions require liquid/semi-liquid diet, high-protein nutritional supplements, and oral hygiene. All patients on chronic immunosuppressants need supervision by dermatologist + internal medicine — not primary care alone.
confirmation_tests_curated: Nikolsky Sign Test: Firm lateral pressure on perilesional normal skin — positive if new bulla forms or epidermis peels. Highly suggestive of pemphigus; simple bedside test.
Asboe-Hansen Test: Pressure on intact bulla extends it laterally. Confirms intraepidermal location of split.
Oral Cavity Examination: Full assessment of extent of oral erosions — grading severity and anatomical involvement guides treatment intensity.

diseases.name: Lichen Planus
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis

6 Ps Assessment: Classic flat-topped violaceous papules with Wickham's striae — clinical diagnosis in typical cases. Wickham's striae with dermatoscopy (white crossing lines) confirms LP without biopsy in classic presentations.

Oral Mucosal Examination: Reticular white bilateral buccal patterns = classic oral LP. Erosive or atrophic patterns need biopsy. Unilateral lesions or irregular white patches in tobacco users → mandatory biopsy to exclude SCC.

Nail Assessment: Longitudinal ridging + pterygium formation — clinical diagnosis of nail LP.

2. Histopathology (Gold Standard for Atypical/Oral/Erosive Cases)

Skin Biopsy (H&E): Saw-tooth rete ridges, dense band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at dermo-epidermal junction (lichenoid infiltrate), basal cell vacuolar degeneration, civatte bodies (colloid bodies — apoptotic keratinocytes). Pathognomonic histology.

Oral Mucosal Biopsy: Essential for erosive oral LP, leukoplakia differentiation, and malignancy exclusion. Shows same lichenoid histology as skin LP.

3. Immunofluorescence

Direct Immunofluorescence (DIF): Fluorescent fibrinogen deposits at dermo-epidermal junction (shaggy fibrin band); IgM + Civatte bodies. Differentiates from lupus (IgG+IgM continuous band), pemphigoid, and dermatitis herpetiformis.

4. Investigations for Underlying Triggers

Anti-HCV (Hepatitis C Antibody): Mandatory in generalised or severe LP — HCV association is well established. HCV positivity indicates need for antiviral treatment.

Anti-HBsAg: Hepatitis B can rarely trigger LP.

ANA + Anti-dsDNA: LP may coexist with or be mistaken for lupus erythematosus — particularly in photodistributed lichenoid eruptions.

Drug History (Detailed): All current and recent medications reviewed for lichenoid reaction potential — ACE inhibitors, thiazides, antimalarials, NSAIDs, Dapsone.

5. Monitoring

Annual Oral Examination + Photography: For all oral LP patients — malignant transformation surveillance. Biopsy any new white/red patch or non-healing ulcer.

DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index): 10-item validated questionnaire for treatment response monitoring in LP.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Therapy (First-Line for Localised Cutaneous LP)

Clobetasol Propionate 0.05% Ointment/Cream: Potent topical corticosteroid — first-line for cutaneous LP. Apply twice daily for 4–6 weeks. Restrict to small areas; avoid face and flexures long-term.

Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Ointment: Moderately potent topical steroid; second-line or maintenance. Suitable for flexural and facial LP.

Tacrolimus 0.1% Ointment: Topical calcineurin inhibitor — highly effective for oral LP (erosive) and genital LP. Steroid-sparing option. Apply twice daily. Mild burning initially common.

2. Oral Topical (Oral LP)

Triamcinolone Acetonide 0.1% Orabase Paste: Topical corticosteroid paste for oral LP erosions — apply to lesion 3–4 times daily after meals.

Dexamethasone 0.1mg/ml Mouthwash (Elixir): For widespread oral erosive LP — swish 5ml for 3 minutes and spit. Three times daily.

3. Systemic Therapy (Generalised/Severe/Oral Erosive LP)

Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (4–6 weeks, taper): For widespread cutaneous LP, severe nail LP, or erosive oral/genital LP causing pain and disability. Short courses to control flares.

Hydroxychloroquine 200–400mg/day oral: Effective for generalised cutaneous LP — antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory activity. Safe long-term (eye exam annually for retinopathy). NOT a trigger drug at therapeutic doses.

Acitretin 25–35mg/day oral (6 months): Systemic retinoid. Effective for hypertrophic lichen planus and LP unresponsive to steroids. Teratogenic — strict contraception in females.

Methotrexate 10–15mg weekly oral: For erosive oral LP and generalised LP unresponsive to above therapies. Monitor CBC + LFT monthly.

4. Antipruritic Adjuncts

Hydroxyzine 25mg at night oral: Antihistamine for nocturnal pruritus — sedating, useful at night to improve sleep.

Cetirizine 10mg once daily oral: Non-sedating antihistamine for daytime pruritus control.

5. Nail LP

Intralesional Triamcinolone Acetonide 5–10mg/ml Injection: Monthly injections at nail matrix through proximal nail fold — specialist procedure for destructive nail LP. Prevents permanent nail loss.

Khadirarishta 450ml (AVP/Dabur): Classical fermented formulation with Khadira (Acacia catechu). Blood purifier for chronic lichenoid skin diseases; anti-inflammatory and antibacterial. 15–20ml twice daily with water after meals.

Mahamanjisthadi Kashaya: Multi-herb decoction with Manjistha, Neem, Guduchi — Pitta and Rakta purification. For chronic inflammatory skin diseases with PIH. 15ml twice daily before meals.

Manjistha Powder (Rubia cordifolia): Blood purifier, anti-inflammatory, and hyperpigmentation-reducing herb. Useful for LP-associated PIH in Indian skin. 3–5g twice daily with warm water or honey.

Arogyavardhini Vati: Classical mercury-herbomineral formulation; Pitta detoxifier for chronic skin diseases. Under qualified Vaidya supervision only. 250mg twice daily.

Neem Capsules (Himalaya): Anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory for chronic LP. 1–2 capsules twice daily after meals.

Haridra (Turmeric) Khand: Curcumin-rich preparation; anti-inflammatory and anti-pruritic. 3–5g with warm milk twice daily.

Triphala Churna: Systemic antioxidant and mild detoxifier. Supports gut health underlying immune dysregulation in LP. 5g at bedtime.

Chandanasavam: Cooling Pitta-pacifying fermented formulation with Sandalwood. For LP with burning sensation in oral or genital involvement. 15–20ml twice daily with water.

Sariva (Hemidesmus indicus) Decoction: Classical Rakta-shodhaka (blood purifier) for Pitta-dominant skin conditions. Anti-inflammatory. 30ml twice daily before meals.

Trikatu Churna: Digestive formula — Agni improvement and Ama removal for lichenoid dermatitis with oral involvement. 1–2g with honey before meals.

Jatyadi Tailam (topical): Classical wound-healing oil for oral erosive LP — applied carefully on oral erosions for wound healing and pain relief. Use under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific:
High Prevalence Oral Lichen Planus: India has one of the highest global rates of oral lichen planus — partly linked to tobacco chewing (gutka, pan masala, khaini) which coexists with and may trigger oral LP. Difficult to distinguish from leukoplakia — biopsy mandatory in tobacco users.

Oral LP Malignant Transformation: Erosive oral lichen planus carries 0.5–2% lifetime risk of oral squamous cell carcinoma — significantly higher in India with tobacco chewing habit. Annual oral examination mandatory in all oral LP patients.

Hepatitis C Association: HCV infection is a recognized trigger of lichen planus, particularly cutaneous and oral. India's HCV prevalence (~1%) means anti-HCV testing is important in LP patients, especially with generalised disease.

Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation: In dark Indian skin (Fitzpatrick IV–VI), healed lichen planus lesions leave persistent, very dark PIH macules — frequently more distressing to patients than active disease. Residual PIH may take years to fade.

Drug-Induced Lichenoid Reactions: Many medicines widely used in India can trigger lichenoid reactions mimicking lichen planus — antimalarials (Hydroxychloroquine), ACE inhibitors, thiazide diuretics, NSAIDs, gold, and Dapsone. Drug history critical.
diseases.important_notes:
Oral LP Requires Regular Malignant Transformation Surveillance: Erosive oral lichen planus has 0.5–2% lifetime risk of squamous cell carcinoma, especially in Indian patients who also use tobacco. Every oral LP patient must have clinical oral examination every 6–12 months. Any new white patch, ulcer, or mass non-healing beyond 2 weeks → immediate biopsy. Stop all tobacco products.

Hepatitis C Testing is Mandatory: LP is strongly associated with HCV infection — particularly generalised or erosive LP. HCV anti-body test must be performed in all new LP patients. If HCV-positive, refer to gastroenterology/hepatology for antiviral treatment (DAAs — Direct-Acting Antivirals) which may also resolve the LP.

Distinguish Drug-Induced Lichenoid Reactions: Many commonly used Indian medications (hydrochlorothiazide, enalapril, chloroquine, NSAIDs, Dapsone) cause lichenoid drug reactions indistinguishable from true LP clinically and histologically. Review all medication history. Stopping the offending drug may resolve the condition completely.

PIH Management in Indian Patients: Dark PIH macules after LP resolution are often the primary patient complaint. Reassure that PIH fades slowly (months-years). Photoprotection with SPF 30+ sunscreen is essential. Topical Azelaic Acid 15% or Kojic Acid formulations may speed resolution.

Avoid Long-Term Potent Topical Steroids on Face/Flexures/Genital Skin: Clobetasol on face, groin, or oral mucosa long-term causes skin atrophy, striae, and secondary candidiasis. Use Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment (steroid-sparing calcineurin inhibitor) for facial, genital, and oral LP as safer long-term alternative.
confirmation_tests_curated: 6 Ps Assessment: Classic flat-topped violaceous papules with Wickham's striae — clinical diagnosis in typical cases. Wickham's striae with dermatoscopy (white crossing lines) confirms LP without biopsy in classic presentations.

Oral Mucosal Examination: Reticular white bilateral buccal patterns = classic oral LP. Erosive or atrophic patterns need biopsy. Unilateral lesions or irregular white patches in tobacco users → mandatory biopsy to exclude SCC.
Nail Assessment: Longitudinal ridging + pterygium formation — clinical diagnosis of nail LP.

diseases.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary — No Routine Tests Needed)

Clinical Distribution Assessment: Greasy yellowish scales in seborrheic areas (nasolabial folds, eyebrows, scalp, ears, chest) — clinical diagnosis in typical presentations.

Response to Antifungal Treatment: Therapeutic trial of ketoconazole shampoo or cream — improvement confirms seborrheic dermatitis (Malassezia-driven). Non-response suggests alternative diagnosis (psoriasis, tinea).

2. Dermoscopy

Dermoscopy (Trichoscopy for Scalp): Scalp SD shows dotted/comma vessels, branching vessels, and diffuse yellowish scales with follicular plugs — distinguishes from scalp psoriasis (red dots/globules) and tinea capitis.

3. Mycological Tests (To Exclude Tinea/Ringworm)

KOH Mount of Scalp Scrapings: Excludes tinea capitis (dermatophyte infection) in scalp SD — especially in children. Dermatophytes show branching hyphae; Malassezia shows bottle-shaped yeast (not routinely visible on KOH).

Fungal Culture: When KOH positive or tinea suspected — Sabouraud dextrose agar culture.

4. For Atypical/Severe/Extensive SD

HIV Testing (4th Generation Ag/Ab): Mandatory for all patients with severe, extensive, or treatment-resistant seborrheic dermatitis — HIV is a major trigger in India.

Neurological Assessment: For elderly patients — Parkinson's disease and other neurological conditions are associated with severe SD.

Skin Biopsy: Rarely needed; for atypical presentations where psoriasis, lupus, or pityriasis rosacea cannot be excluded clinically. Histology shows spongiotic dermatitis with parakeratosis and Malassezia spores at follicular ostia.

5. Differentiation

Scalp Scraping for Tinea Capitis (Children): Black dot alopecia + scaling — strongly suggests tinea capitis not SD in children. KOH mandatory before antifungal treatment.

6. Monitoring

DLQI/IGA Scales: For treatment response tracking in seborrheic dermatitis clinics.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antifungal Treatments (First-Line — Targets Malassezia)

Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo: First-line for scalp SD. Apply on scalp 3 times weekly, leave 5 minutes, rinse. Effective for both dandruff and seborrheic dermatitis. Also available as cream for facial SD.

Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo: Anti-Malassezia and anti-seborrheic; apply twice weekly. Available OTC in India. Effective for scalp SD and dandruff.

Zinc Pyrithione 1–2% Shampoo: Anti-Malassezia mechanism; milder option for dandruff/mild scalp SD. Use 3–4 times weekly.

Ciclopirox 1% Shampoo/Gel: Effective antifungal for seborrheic dermatitis; broad-spectrum anti-Malassezia activ ity.

Ketoconazole 2% Cream: For facial SD on nasolabial folds, eyebrows, and ears. Apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks.

2. Anti-Inflammatory Treatments

Hydrocortisone 1% Cream: Mild topical steroid; for short-term use (1–2 weeks) on face to control inflammation. Do NOT use higher-potency steroids on face.

Desonide 0.05% Cream/Lotion: Low-potency topical steroid suitable for facial and flexural SD. Safer for face than stronger steroids.

Tacrolimus 0.1%/Pimecrolimus 1% Cream: Steroid-sparing calcineurin inhibitors; ideal for facial SD and seborrheic blepharitis requiring long-term management. No skin atrophy risk.

3. Scalp-Specific

Fluocinolone Acetonide 0.01% in Coconut Oil (Scalp Solution): Anti-inflammatory and emollient scalp preparation. Useful for thick, crusted scalp SD to loosen scales and reduce inflammation.

Coal Tar Shampoo 1–2%: For thick crusted scalp SD. Anti-proliferative and anti-inflammatory. Use 2–3 times weekly.

Salicylic Acid 2–6% Shampoo/Scalp Solution: Keratolytic; loosens and removes thick adherent scales in severe scalp SD before antifungal shampoo application.

4. Systemic (Severe/Resistant SD)

Itraconazole 200mg/day for 1 week then 200mg 2 days/month oral: For severe facial or body SD unresponsive to topical treatment. Pulse maintenance dosing for recurrence prevention.

Fluconazole 150mg weekly oral: Alternative systemic antifungal for moderate-severe SD.

5. Seborrheic Blepharitis

Baby Shampoo Eyelid Scrubs: Gentle daily eyelid margin scrubbing to remove seborrheic scales — first-line for blepharitis.

Haridra (Turmeric) Paste: Fresh turmeric paste applied to affected facial areas — curcumin anti-inflammatory and anti-Malassezia activity. Apply thinly, leave 20 minutes, wash off. Traditional remedy.

Kumkumadi Tailam: Anti-inflammatory brightening facial oil for seborrheic dermatitis with hyperpigmentation on face. Applied as overnight face treatment (caution: avoid if oily/acne-prone).

Khadirarishta (Dabur/AVP): Internal blood purifier for chronic skin diseases with Malassezia-fungal component. 15–20ml twice daily with equal water after meals.

Bakuchi (Psoralea corylifolia) Oil — low concentration: Traditionally used for skin conditions; anti-inflammatory. Use cautiously — concentrated Bakuchi is photosensitizing.

Indukantha Ghrita: Medicated ghee for immunological skin conditions; improves skin barrier and reduces inflammation. Under Vaidya guidance. 5–10ml on empty stomach.
diseases.india_specific:
Malassezia Colonization in Indian Climate: Hot, humid Indian climate (especially coastal and tropical India) promotes excessive Malassezia yeast proliferation on scalp and skin — the primary pathogenic driver of seborrheic dermatitis. Monsoon season typically triggers flares.

HIV-Associated Severe Seborrheic Dermatitis: Severe, extensive, and treatment-resistant seborrheic dermatitis — particularly affecting face, trunk, and skin folds — is a common early sign of HIV immunodeficiency in India. All patients with severe atypical SD should be offered HIV testing.

Overuse of Coconut Oil: Traditional heavy application of coconut oil on scalp (common in South India) may worsen seborrheic dermatitis by providing substrate for Malassezia yeast growth. Patients should reduce/avoid heavy oil application on scalp.

Parkinson's Disease and Elderly Patients: India's aging population with Parkinson's disease has significantly higher rates of seborrheic dermatitis. Often under-recognized and untreated in elderly patients in rural India.

Misdiagnosis as Psoriasis: Scalp seborrheic dermatitis is frequently misdiagnosed as scalp psoriasis in India, leading to inappropriate potent steroid use. Key distinction: SD = greasy yellow scales, facial involvement, responds to antifungals; Psoriasis = silvery-white thick scales, nail changes, does NOT respond to antifungals.
diseases.important_notes:
Seborrheic Dermatitis is Chronic and Relapsing — Counsel Expectations: Patients must understand seborrheic dermatitis cannot be permanently cured — only controlled. Stopping antifungal treatment leads to recurrence within weeks to months. Long-term maintenance therapy (antifungal shampoo 1–2x/week) after initial clearance is essential to prevent relapse.

Test for HIV in Severe or Atypical Cases: Sudden-onset severe seborrheic dermatitis affecting face, trunk, and skin folds — especially in a young adult — is a clinical indicator of HIV immunodeficiency in India. HIV counselling and 4th-generation testing should be offered sensitively. Effective ART markedly improves HIV-associated SD.

Reduce Scalp Oil Application: Advise patients (despite traditional practice) to reduce heavy oil application on the scalp — particularly coconut, mustard, or castor oils. These oils provide nutrients for Malassezia growth. Light oil if required in small amounts only.

Avoid Potent Steroids on Face: Strongly advise against use of high-potency fluorinated steroids (Clobetasol, Betamethasone Dipropionate) on facial seborrheic dermatitis. These cause skin atrophy, telangiectasias, rosacea-like changes, and rebound flares after discontinuation — a very common problem in India with widespread OTC steroid availability.

Stress and Sleep Management: Address psychological stress as a major SD trigger — sleep hygiene, stress-reduction, and where possible, treatment of underlying anxiety/depression. Explain that SD flares with illness, fatigue, and stress are expected and temporary.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary — No Routine Tests Needed)
Clinical Distribution Assessment: Greasy yellowish scales in seborrheic areas (nasolabial folds, eyebrows, scalp, ears, chest) — clinical diagnosis in typical presentations.

Response to Antifungal Treatment: Therapeutic trial of ketoconazole shampoo or cream — improvement confirms seborrheic dermatitis (Malassezia-driven). Non-response suggests alternative diagnosis (psoriasis, tinea).

Dermoscopy (Trichoscopy for Scalp): Scalp SD shows dotted/comma vessels, branching vessels, and diffuse yellowish scales with follicular plugs — distinguishes from scalp psoriasis (red dots/globules) and tinea capitis.

diseases.name: Contact Dermatitis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis

Pattern and Distribution Analysis: Critical — geometric, patterned, or anatomically restricted distribution matching contactant location is most diagnostic. Examine entire body for clues.

Detailed Exposure History: Occupational exposure, cosmetics, jewellery, topical medications, plants, and clothing materials — systematic detailed history is the most important diagnostic tool.

Differentiation ICD vs ACD: ICD — onset rapid after first contact; burning/stinging; affects hands predominantly; no prior sensitization needed. ACD — delayed 24–72h; pruritic; requires sensitization; can affect any site.

2. Patch Testing (Gold Standard for ACD)

Patch Test (European Baseline Series): Standardized allergen panels (metals, fragrances, preservatives, rubber chemicals, PPD, epoxy resins) applied to back under occlusion for 48 hours; read at 48h and 96h. Identifies specific allergen in ACD. Available at IADVL-registered dermatology centres.

Indian Patch Test Series: Special series includes India-specific allergens — turmeric (curcumin), henna/PPD, kumkum, coconut oil, sindoor, jasmine oil, and bindi adhesives. Available at selected dermatology departments.

Photopatch Test: For suspected photoallergic contact dermatitis (sunscreens, fragrances) — patch test + UV irradiation.

3. Confirmatory Tests (Atypical Cases)

Skin Biopsy: Spongiotic dermatitis with variable eosinophilic infiltrate — not distinguishing between ACD and ICD histologically but excludes psoriasis and other diagnoses.

IgE RAST (Protein Contact Dermatitis): For immediate type reactions — especially food handlers with fish, vegetables, or latex contact urticaria.

4. Occupational Assessment

Repeat Open Application Test (ROAT): Patient applies suspected cosmetic/topical product to inner arm twice daily for 7 days — positive reaction confirms sensitisation in doubtful cases.

Workplace Visit/Safety Data Sheet Review: Identifies occupational chemical allergens in cases of occupational contact dermatitis in construction, healthcare, agriculture.

5. Investigations to Exclude Differential Diagnoses

Skin Scraping + KOH Mount: Excludes tinea (ringworm) which can mimic contact dermatitis on hands and feet.

6. Indian Guidelines

IADVL Contact Dermatitis Guidelines 2019: Detailed patch testing protocol for Indian allergen series. All suspected occupational ACD should have patch test profile including chromate, rubber chemicals, and PPD.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Treatments (First-Line)

Clobetasol Propionate 0.05% Ointment: Potent topical steroid for severe acute ACD — hands, body. Apply twice daily for 1–2 weeks. Rapid symptom control.

Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream: Moderate-potency topical steroid for moderate ACD/ICD. Apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks.

Hydrocortisone 1% Cream: Mild topical steroid for face and flexure contact dermatitis. Short-term only.

Tacrolimus 0.1% Ointment / Pimecrolimus 1% Cream: Steroid-sparing calcineurin inhibitors for chronic contact eczema on face, eyelids, and sensitive skin areas. Avoid long-term steroid complications.

2. Emollients and Barrier Repair

White Soft Paraffin (Petroleum Jelly): Barrier restoration in ICD — apply after washing, multiple times daily. Essential for hand ICD in housewives and healthcare workers.

Liquid Paraffin 10% + White Soft Paraffin 45% Cream (Emulsifying Ointment): Standard emollient for chronic contact eczema. Apply after every handwash.

3. Systemic Treatments (Severe/Widespread ACD)

Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (7–14 days, taper): For severe, widespread ACD — including PPD hair dye face/scalp reaction, poison ivy type extensive ACD. Rapid relief; short course.

4. Antipruritic Adjuncts

Hydroxyzine 25mg at night oral: Sedating antihistamine; controls nocturnal pruritus and helps sleep.

Cetirizine 10mg once daily oral: Non-sedating daytime antipruritic.

5. Wet Dressings (Acute Oozing ACD)

Potassium Permanganate 1:10000 Solution Soaks: For oozing, weeping ACD — antiseptic, astringent, drying. Use twice daily; dilute properly (stains purple/brown if concentrated). Available at district hospital pharmacies.

Normal Saline Wet Dressings: Cool, soothing soaks for acute weeping contact dermatitis — 15–20 minutes, 3–4 times daily.

6. Infection Management

Mupirocin 2% Ointment or Fusidic Acid 2% Cream: For secondarily infected contact dermatitis. Short-course topical antibiotic.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Clobetasol 0.05% Ointment 15g: Potent topical steroid for severe ACD. PMBJP stores.

Betamethasone 0.1% Cream 15g: Moderate potency topical steroid for contact dermatitis.

Hydrocortisone 1% Cream 15g: Mild steroid for facial contact dermatitis.

White Soft Paraffin 50g: Barrier emollient for ICD hand eczema. Jan Aushadhi.

Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): For severe widespread ACD short course.

Hydroxyzine 25mg Tablets (10 tabs): Sedating antihistamine for nocturnal itch.

Cetirizine 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Daytime antipruritic.

Mupirocin 2% Ointment 5g: Topical antibiotic for secondary infection.

Potassium Permanganate Crystals: Wet soaks for oozing ACD. District hospital.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Coconut Oil (Pure, Virgin): Barrier emollient for dry, irritated ICD hands — anti-inflammatory lauric acid content. Apply multiple times daily after handwashing. Widely available and affordable in India. Recommended by IADVL as affordable emollient.

Aloe Vera Gel (Pure): Anti-inflammatory, soothing, and wound-healing for acute ACD. Reduces erythema and burning. Apply cool gel 3–4 times daily on affected areas.

Neem Oil (diluted 1:5): Antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory for mildly infected or Pitta-dominant contact dermatitis. Apply topically on limited areas.

Haridra (Turmeric) Paste (topical): Fresh turmeric in coconut oil — traditional anti-inflammatory application for contact dermatitis. Curcumin inhibits NF-kB inflammatory pathway. Apply for 20 minutes before washing.

Chandanadi Tailam: Cooling, Pitta-reducing medicated oil for burning, erythematous contact dermatitis. Apply twice daily.

Dashanga Lepa: Classical Ayurvedic paste with ten cooling and astringent ingredients for inflammatory skin conditions. Applied topically.

Mahamarichyadi Tailam: Traditional medicated oil for contact-type inflammatory skin diseases.

Mahamanjisthadi Kashaya (oral): Blood purifier for systemic management of recurrent or widespread contact dermatitis — Pitta-Rakta etiological framework. 15ml twice daily before meals.

Neem Capsules (Himalaya): Internal blood purifier and anti-allergic for systemic background management of recurrent ACD with hypersensitivity mechanism.

Triphala Churna: Antioxidant, mild immunomodulatory — systemic support for chronic recurrent contact dermatitis. 5g at bedtime with warm water.

Sariva (Hemidesmus indicus) Decoction: Cooling blood purifier for Pitta-dominant contact dermatitis with burning. 30ml twice daily before meals.
diseases.india_specific:
Paraphenylenediamine (PPD) Hair Dye Allergy: India has one of the highest global rates of PPD (para-phenylenediamine) contact allergy from black chemical hair dye and henna. Severe ACD to PPD causes dramatic facial swelling, eyelid edema, severe pruritus, and may progress to PPD anaphylaxis. PPD cross-reacts with many medicines (sulphonamides, Dapsone, thiazides).

Cement Dermatitis in Construction Workers: Hexavalent chromate in cement is a major cause of allergic contact dermatitis in Indian construction workers. Estimated >5 million construction workers at risk. Chromate-positive patch test common. Water-based washable cement reduces risk.

Mehendi/Henna Reactions: Natural henna (pure Lawsonia) is low sensitiser; PPD-adulterated 'black henna' (widely used in India for mehndi designs) causes severe ACD. Young children increasingly sensitised from mehndi — sets lifelong PPD allergy.

Nickel Allergy from Jewellery: Nickel release from inexpensive metal jewellery (ear piercing posts, necklaces, waistbands, bindi adhesive) is a common cause of ACD in Indian women. 18-karat gold and sterling silver have lower nickel content.

Agarbatti (Incense Stick) and Floral Allergens: Compositae (chrysanthemum family) plants and fragrances in agarbatti are recognised triggers of airborne contact dermatitis in India — widespread household use. Periorbital and facial ACD in housewives commonly attributable.
diseases.important_notes:
IDENTIFY AND ELIMINATE THE CAUSE: Contact dermatitis cannot be cured without permanent identification and removal of the causative contactant. Medical treatment manages symptoms temporarily — the disease recurs if re-exposure continues. Detailed history including ALL cosmetics, jewellery, topical products, and occupational exposures is the most important consultation task. If PPD hair dye is the cause — it must be permanently discontinued.

PPD Hair Dye — Severe Risk Warning: PPD (black chemical hair dye and PPD-adulterated black henna) allergy can cause life-threatening angioedema, severe facial swelling, and anaphylaxis on re-exposure after sensitisation. Affected patients must NEVER use PPD-containing hair dyes again (including all chemical black hair dye brands). Sensitisation is lifelong. Advise use of certified PPD-free henna or ammonia-free hair color alternatives.

Protective Gloves and Barrier Measures: Patients with occupational hand contact dermatitis must use appropriate gloves (cotton inner liner + vinyl outer for latex allergy; rubber-free gloves for rubber chemical allergy). Emollient application after each handwash is essential for barrier repair in healthcare workers, housewives, and food handlers.

Avoid Long-Term Potent Topical Steroids, Especially on Face: Clobetasol or betamethasone dipropionate on facial contact dermatitis causes skin atrophy, telangiectasias, hyperpigmentation, and perioral dermatitis. Maximum 2 weeks on face, then switch to Tacrolimus or emollient. Commercial 'fairness creams' in India often contain unlabelled potent steroids — a serious public health concern.

Patch Testing is Essential for Chronic Recurrent ACD: Any patient with chronic or recurrent contact dermatitis not resolving with topical steroids must be referred for formal patch testing to identify the specific allergen. Without identifying the causative allergen, management remains symptomatic and incomplete. IADVL-certified dermatology departments across India offer patch testing with the Indian Standard Series.
confirmation_tests_curated: Pattern and Distribution Analysis: Critical — geometric, patterned, or anatomically restricted distribution matching contactant location is most diagnostic. Examine entire body for clues.
Detailed Exposure History: Occupational exposure, cosmetics, jewellery, topical medications, plants, and clothing materials — systematic detailed history is the most important diagnostic tool.

Differentiation ICD vs ACD: ICD — onset rapid after first contact; burning/stinging; affects hands predominantly; no prior sensitization needed. ACD — delayed 24–72h; pruritic; requires sensitization; can affect any site.

diseases.name: Cellulitis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

ALT-70 Score for Differentiating Cellulitis from Pseudocellulitis: Asymmetry (1pt) + Leukocytosis >10 (1pt) + Tachycardia >90 (1pt) + Age >70 (1pt). Score 0–1 = 83% cellulitis. Score 2–4 = more likely pseudocellulitis (lipodermatosclerosis, stasis dermatitis). Misdiagnosis of pseudocellulitis as cellulitis leads to unnecessary antibiotics in ~30% of admissions.

Pen Marking Test: Mark the advancing border of erythema with indelible ink — reassess hourly. Spreading beyond mark within 12–24 hours despite antibiotics = progression/MRSA/necrotising fasciitis — escalate urgently.

2. Laboratory Tests

CBC with Differential: Leukocytosis (WBC >11,000) with neutrophilia confirms bacterial infection. Severe leukocytosis (>20,000) or leukopenia = systemic sepsis. Serial CBC monitors treatment response.

CRP / ESR / Procalcitonin: Elevated in active bacterial infection. CRP serial monitoring guides antibiotic duration and confirms resolution. Procalcitonin >2 ng/ml indicates bacteraemia.

Blood Culture (2 sets): For all patients with systemic sepsis, fever >38.5°C, or immunocompromised state. Positive in ~5% of cellulitis. Essential for MRSA/unusual organism detection and antibiotic guidance.

Random Blood Glucose + HbA1c: Mandatory for all cellulitis patients — undiagnosed diabetes is common presenting factor in recurrent cellulitis in India.

3. Imaging

Ultrasound Soft Tissue: Differentiates cellulitis (diffuse echogenicity) from abscess (defined fluid collection requiring drainage). Essential if fluctuance palpable.

MRI Soft Tissue: For suspected necrotising fasciitis — fascial plane gas or edema. Emergency investigation. CT with contrast acceptable if MRI unavailable.

X-Ray Affected Limb: To exclude osteomyelitis (especially in diabetic foot cellulitis) and soft tissue gas (gas gangrene/necrotising fasciitis).

4. Microbiology (Wound/Skin)

Wound Swab Culture & Sensitivity: For ulcers, wounds, or entry points — guides targeted antibiotic therapy. Essential in diabetic foot and MRSA-suspected cellulitis.

Needle Aspiration of Leading Edge: Positive in 5–25% — identifies causative organism. Useful when empirical antibiotics failing.

5. Filariasis Workup (Endemic Areas)

Night Blood Film for Microfilariae: For recurrent lower limb cellulitis in filariasis-endemic regions (Bihar, UP, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh) — identifies filarial etiology and guides DEC treatment.

6. Indian Guidelines

IADVL + IDSA Cellulitis Guidelines: Lower limb cellulitis first-line = cefalexin (mild) or amoxicillin-clavulanate (moderate). IV benzylpenicillin + cloxacillin for hospitalised. MRSA-suspected: Linezolid or Vancomycin.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Mild Non-Purulent Cellulitis (Outpatient)

Cefalexin 500mg four times daily oral (7–10 days): First-line for uncomplicated non-purulent cellulitis (Streptococcal aetiology). Well tolerated, widely available in India.

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg (500/125mg) twice/thrice daily oral (7 days): For mild-moderate cellulitis — broader spectrum covering Staphylococcus + MSSA + anaerobes. First-line for animal bites.

Dicloxacillin 500mg four times daily oral: For Staphylococcus-suspected cellulitis (penicillinase-resistant penicillin).

2. Moderate Cellulitis (With Systemic Features — Consider Hospitalisation)

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1.2g IV three times daily: IV step-up for moderate cellulitis when oral treatment failing.

Benzylpenicillin 2.4g IV four times daily + Cloxacillin 2g IV four times daily: IADVL protocol for hospitalised non-MRSA cellulitis — covers Streptococcal + MSSA dual coverage.

3. Severe/MRSA-Suspected Cellulitis (Hospital)

Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV twice daily (dose adjusted to renal function and VCM trough levels): For MRSA cellulitis. Requires ICU/ward setting with IV access and renal monitoring.

Linezolid 600mg twice daily IV/oral: MRSA alternative — oral bioavailability enables step-down. Available in India.

Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800mg twice daily oral: For community MRSA step-down oral therapy in mild-moderate MRSA skin infections.

4. Necrotising Fasciitis (Surgical Emergency)

Meropenem 1g IV eight-hourly + Vancomycin + Metronidazole 500mg IV three times daily: Empirical combination for necrotising fasciitis pending culture. PLUS — urgent surgical debridement is the ONLY definitive treatment.

5. Recurrence Prevention

Clotrimazole 1% Cream (Feet): Treat tinea pedis to eliminate recurrent cellulitis entry point. Apply between toes twice daily for 4 weeks.

Benzathine Benzylpenicillin 1.2MU IM monthly: Long-term prophylaxis for recurrent cellulitis (≥3 episodes/year) — particularly in lymphoedema patients. IADVL recommended.

Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) 6mg/kg/day for 12 days: For filarial lymphoedema-associated recurrent cellulitis — eradicates microfilariae to reduce lymph node burden and recurrence risk.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Cefalexin 500mg Capsules (10 caps): First-line oral antibiotic for mild cellulitis. PMBJP stores.

Amoxicillin + Clavulanate 625mg Tablets (10 tabs): Broad spectrum for moderate cellulitis/animal bites.

Cotrimoxazole 480mg Tablets (10 tabs): MRSA oral step-down therapy. Jan Aushadhi Kendras.

Clotrimazole 1% Cream 15g: Treats tinea pedis entry point for cellulitis prevention.

Dicloxacillin 500mg Capsules (10 caps): MSSA-targeted antibiotic.

Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): For anaerobic component in mixed infections.

Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Fever and pain management. PMBJP.

Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) 100mg Tablets (10 tabs): For filarial lymphoedema-associated recurrent cellulitis.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Amoxicillin + Clavulanate 625mg (10 tablets): Rs.88

Cotrimoxazole 480mg (10 tablets): Rs.8

Clotrimazole 1% Cream 15g: Rs.22

Dicloxacillin 500mg (10 capsules): Rs.45

Metronidazole 400mg (10 tablets): Rs.10

Paracetamol 500mg (10 tablets): Rs.4

DEC 100mg (10 tablets): Rs.12
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Gandhaka Rasayana: Classical purified Sulphur formulation — antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and immunostimulatory. For recurrent bacterial skin infections and furunculosis. 500mg–1g twice daily under Vaidya supervision.

Mahatikta Ghrita: Tikta (bitter) medicated ghee with strong anti-inflammatory, antibacterial (Neem, Guduchi, Patola) activity. For severe inflammatory skin infections. 10–15ml on empty stomach under supervision.

Kaishore Guggulu: Tridoshic blood purifier with Guggulu — effective for deep-seated bacterial skin infections, abscesses, and cellulitis. Reduces Ama and clears Rakta (blood) toxicity. 2 tablets twice daily.

Neem (Azadirachta indica) Kashaya: Internal Neem bark decoction — potent antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and immunostimulatory. 30ml twice daily before meals for active skin infections.

Panchanimbadi Churna: Five bitter herbs including Neem — classical antibacterial and anti-Pitta formulation for inflammatory skin infections. 3–5g twice daily with honey.

Neem Paste (External): Fresh Neem leaf paste applied as cold poultice on cellulitis — haematoma reduction, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial. Apply and wrap; change twice daily. Adjunctive only.

Haridra (Turmeric) Milk: Warm milk with 1 teaspoon turmeric — anti-inflammatory curcumin for systemic support in skin infections. Pain and fever reduction role.

Eranda (Castor Oil) Warm Compress: Warm castor oil fomentation over the affected area — traditional Indian poultice for cellulitis. Anti-inflammatory and promotes drainage. Apply warm pad over oil.

Triphala Churna: Systemic antioxidant and mild immunostimulatory; general detoxifier supporting healing from infection. 5g at bedtime.

CRITICAL: Ayurveda as Supportive ONLY in Cellulitis: Cellulitis is a potentially life-threatening bacterial skin infection. Systemic antibiotics are mandatory — Ayurvedic treatment CANNOT replace antibiotics. Topical poultices may be used as comfort measures supplementing antibiotics but must NEVER delay medical care. Necrotising fasciitis warrants immediate surgical referral.
diseases.india_specific:
Diabetic Foot Cellulitis: Diabetes mellitus affects ~100 million Indians. Peripheral neuropathy + micro/macrovascular disease + immune dysfunction makes diabetic patients highly susceptible to rapidly progressing, treatment-resistant lower limb cellulitis and necrotising fasciitis. Diabetic foot cellulitis is a major cause of amputation in India.

Tinea Pedis (Athlete's Foot) as Entry Point: Untreated interdigital tinea pedis (fungal infection between toes) is the most common portal of entry for recurrent lower limb cellulitis in India. Treating tinea pedis with topical antifungals breaks the cellulitis recurrence cycle.

Filariasis-Associated Lymphoedema: Lymphatic filariasis (Wuchereria bancrofti) affects ~40 million Indians. Filarial-induced lymphoedema creates pre-existing lymphatic damage — dramatically increasing susceptibility to recurrent cellulitis of lower limbs (adenolymphangitis in filariasis). Recurrence can be 2–4 episodes yearly.

MRSA and Antibiotic Resistance: Community-acquired MRSA (Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) rates are rising in India. Cellulitis not responding to standard beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillin, cefalexin) within 48–72 hours should raise MRSA suspicion — especially in previously hospitalised patients or those with furunculosis.

Snake Bite and Animal Bite Cellulitis: India has globally highest snakebite mortality. Snakebite wound cellulitis and dog/cat bite-associated cellulitis (Pasteurella multocida) are common in rural India — require different antibiotic coverage including penicillin for animal bites.
diseases.important_notes:
Rapidly Spreading Cellulitis is a Medical Emergency — ACT FAST: If erythema continues to advance beyond marked borders despite 48–72 hours of oral antibiotics, or if patient develops high fever, severe pain disproportionate to appearance, skin blistering, skin darkening, or crepitus — IMMEDIATE hospital referral for IV antibiotics. Do NOT wait. Necrotising fasciitis can kill within hours.

Diabetic Patients Need Aggressive Early Treatment: Diabetic patients with lower limb cellulitis must be managed as hospital patients or very closely monitored outpatients. Impaired immune response allows rapid spread. Daily assessment, blood glucose optimisation, and IV antibiotics if not clearly improving in 24 hours. Diabetic foot cellulitis with bone involvement = hospitalisation + orthopaedic/vascular surgery review.

Treat Tinea Pedis to Prevent Recurrent Cellulitis: Inter-digital tinea pedis (athlete's foot) is the single most important preventable risk factor for recurrent lower limb cellulitis in India. All patients with recurrent cellulitis MUST be examined between the toes for fungal infection. Treat with topical Clotrimazole + counsel on foot hygiene, proper footwear, and foot drying.

Elevation and Rest are Essential Adjuncts: Elevating the affected limb above heart level reduces oedema, pain, and antibiotic delivery time by improving tissue perfusion. Enforce strict limb elevation during acute cellulitis. Compression bandaging after resolution in lymphoedema patients reduces recurrence.

Do Not Confuse Pseudocellulitis with True Cellulitis: Lipodermatosclerosis, venous stasis dermatitis, acute gout, DVT, and eosinophilic cellulitis (Wells syndrome) all mimic cellulitis — bilateral lower limb 'cellulitis' is almost never true cellulitis (bilateral simultaneous infection is extremely rare). Misdiagnosis leads to unnecessary antibiotics. Always consider pseudocellulitis in bilateral cases.
confirmation_tests_curated: ALT-70 Score for Differentiating Cellulitis from Pseudocellulitis: Asymmetry (1pt) + Leukocytosis >10 (1pt) + Tachycardia >90 (1pt) + Age >70 (1pt). Score 0–1 = 83% cellulitis. Score 2–4 = more likely pseudocellulitis (lipodermatosclerosis, stasis dermatitis). Misdiagnosis of pseudocellulitis as cellulitis leads to unnecessary antibiotics in ~30% of admissions.

Pen Marking Test: Mark the advancing border of erythema with indelible ink — reassess hourly. Spreading beyond mark within 12–24 hours despite antibiotics = progression/MRSA/necrotising fasciitis — escalate urgently.

CBC with Differential: Leukocytosis (WBC >11,000) with neutrophilia confirms bacterial infection. Severe leukocytosis (>20,000) or leukopenia = systemic sepsis. Serial CBC monitors treatment response.

diseases.name: Impetigo
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

Lesion Morphology Assessment: Honey-coloured golden crusts on erythematous base (non-bullous) or flaccid bullae (bullous impetigo) — classic clinical diagnosis. No investigations required for typical uncomplicated impetigo.

Nikolsky Sign: Negative in impetigo/SSSS localized; positive widespread in SSSS and pemphigus.

2. Microbiological Tests (Moderate-Severe / Recurrent / Treatment-Failure Cases)

Wound/Crust Swab Culture and Sensitivity: Most important investigation for impetigo not responding to empirical antibiotics within 3–5 days. Identifies causative organism (GAS vs MSSA vs MRSA) and antibiotic sensitivities. Available at PHC bacteriology labs.

Gram Stain of Vesicle Fluid: Gram-positive cocci in clusters (S. aureus) or chains (Streptococcus) — rapid orientation but culture is definitive.

3. Investigations for PSGN Complication Screening

Urine Routine Microscopy + Protein: MANDATORY 3–6 weeks after streptococcal impetigo in children — detects haematuria and proteinuria marking PSGN onset. PHC-level urine dipstick test is sufficient for screening.

Serum ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) Titre: Elevated in streptococcal skin infection — however ASO rise is LESS reliable after skin impetigo than throat infection. Anti-DNase B titre is a better serological PSGN marker.

Anti-DNase B Titres: More reliably elevated after streptococcal skin infection (impetigo) than ASO. Useful for retrospective confirmation of GAS skin infection if PSGN develops.

Serum Creatinine + BUN + Electrolytes: Baseline renal function if PSGN suspected (haematuria + proteinuria).

Complement C3 Level: Markedly depressed in acute PSGN (C3 < 81 mg/dL) — characteristic finding. Normalises in 6–8 weeks.

4. Neonatal Impetigo / SSSS Investigations

Blood Culture (Neonatal): Mandatory in neonatal impetigo with systemic features — bacteraemia risk high in neonates.

5. For MRSA Exclusion

Swab PCR for mecA Gene: Rapid MRSA identification in 4 hours — available at tertiary centres.

6. Indian Guidelines

IAP (Indian Academy of Pediatrics) Guidelines: Non-bullous impetigo in children — topical mupirocin first-line. Oral antibiotics for bullous, widespread, or systemic impetigo. PSGN surveillance mandatory.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Antibiotics (First-Line for Localised Impetigo)

Mupirocin 2% Ointment: First-line for localised non-bullous impetigo (<5 lesions). Apply three times daily for 5–7 days. Excellent activity against S. aureus and GAS. Avoid mucocutaneous membranes.

Fusidic Acid 2% Cream: Alternative topical antibiotic — comparable efficacy to mupirocin. Apply three times daily for 7 days. Anti-staphylococcal.

Retapamulin 1% Ointment: Newer topical pleuromutilin antibiotic — approved for impetigo. Apply twice daily for 5 days. Effective against MRSA topically.

2. Crust Removal Before Topical Antibiotic

Normal Saline or Povidone Iodine 5% Wash: Gentle soaking and removal of crusts before topical antibiotic application — improves penetration and reduces bacterial load. Done twice daily.

3. Systemic Antibiotics (Widespread / Bullous / Systemic Features)

Cefalexin 25–50mg/kg/day (children) or 500mg four times daily (adults) oral — 7 days: First-line systemic antibiotic for widespread non-bullous impetigo. Covers GAS + MSSA.

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 25–45mg/kg/day (children) or 625mg twice daily (adults) oral — 7 days: For moderate-severe impetigo with mixed flora or bite-associated.

Cloxacillin 12.5–25mg/kg/day (children) or 500mg four times daily (adults) oral — 7 days: Penicillinase-resistant; for MSSA impetigo.

4. MRSA-Suspected Impetigo

Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX) 8/40mg/kg/day (children) or 160/800mg twice daily (adults) oral — 7 days: Oral MRSA treatment for community-acquired MRSA impetigo.

Linezolid 10mg/kg twice daily (children <5y) or 600mg twice daily (adults) oral: For MRSA impetigo not responding to Cotrimoxazole.

5. SSSS (Neonatal — Hospital Treatment)

Cloxacillin 50–100mg/kg/day IV divided every 6 hours: IV anti-staphylococcal antibiotic for SSSS. Wound care + IV fluids + temperature maintenance.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Neem Paste (External): Fresh Neem leaf paste applied on impetigo lesions — potent antibacterial against S. aureus and Streptococcus. Traditional Indian first-response. Apply twice daily; wash off with clean water.

Haridra (Turmeric) + Neem Paste: Combined anti-inflammatory and antibacterial topical paste. Curcumin + nimbidin synergistic antibacterial action. Mix turmeric powder in Neem water; apply on lesions twice daily.

Jatyadi Tailam (External): Classical wound-healing medicated oil with Jasminum oil base and multiple antibacterial herbs. Applied on post-rupture impetigo erosions to promote wound healing and prevent scar formation.

Gandhaka Rasayana: Internal purified Sulphur formulation — antibacterial, immunostimulatory. Used under Vaidya supervision for recurrent impetigo in malnourished or immunocompromised children after acute phase managed medically.

Nimbidin Ointment (commercial Neem): External antibacterial application derived from Neem bark extract. Applied twice daily as topical antibacterial adjunct.

Haridra Khand (Turmeric preparation): Internal anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory. Supports immune response in recurrent childhood impetigo. 3g with warm milk twice daily for children.

Triphala Kashaya Wash: Triphala decoction used to clean and irrigate impetigo lesions — astringent, antibacterial wound wash. Apply warm decoction twice daily.

Coconut Oil (Virgin): Lauric acid in coconut oil has documented S. aureus antibacterial activity. Apply as softening and antibacterial agent on crusts before mechanical removal. Widely available and accepted.

Panchanimbadi Churna: Five bitter Neem-family herbs — internal antibacterial and blood purifier for recurrent childhood skin infections. 1–2g twice daily in children with honey.

Chyawanprash: Immunostimulatory nutritional Ayurvedic supplement with Amla, Ashwagandha, and 40+ herbs. Recommended for malnourished children with recurrent impetigo — improves nutritional status and immune function. 1–2 teaspoons daily.

CRITICAL NOTE: Topical Ayurveda Adjunctive, Antibiotics Primary: Impetigo with PSGN risk requires prompt antibiotic treatment. Ayurvedic topicals (Neem, turmeric) can supplement but CANNOT replace antibiotics in widespread, bullous, or systemic impetigo. PSGN is not prevented by Ayurvedic treatment.
diseases.india_specific:
Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN): CRITICAL COMPLICATION. Group A Streptococcus (GAS) impetigo — particularly nephritogenic strains (M types 2, 49, 55, 57, 60) — causes acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis 3–6 weeks after skin infection. India has one of the highest PSGN burdens globally. Presents with haematuria, proteinuria, hypertension, and oedema. PSGN cannot be prevented by antibiotics once established — prevention requires EARLY treatment of impetigo.

High Burden in Malnourished Children: Malnutrition (protein-energy malnutrition, micronutrient deficiencies, Vitamin A deficiency) dramatically impairs skin barrier and innate immunity — major risk factor for impetigo in Indian children under 5 years. Co-treat malnutrition.

Monsoon Season Epidemic: India's monsoon season (June–September) causes epidemic impetigo — humidity, skin maceration, insect bites, and flooding-related skin abrasions dramatically increase incidence. PHC and ASHA workers must be prepared.

Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS) in Neonates: S. aureus bullous impetigo in neonates can disseminate systemically causing SSSS — widespread exfoliation of skin (Ritter disease). A neonatal emergency requiring IV antibiotics and NICU management. Neonatal impetigo at nurseries in India is a recurring nosocomial problem.

Antibiotic Resistance (MRSA): Community MRSA strains causing impetigo are increasing in India, particularly in urban slums and among recently hospitalised children. Impetigo not responding to standard antibiotics within 3–5 days should prompt swab culture for MRSA.
diseases.important_notes:
MONITOR FOR POST-STREPTOCOCCAL GLOMERULONEPHRITIS: All children with streptococcal impetigo must be monitored for PSGN 3–6 weeks later. Key warning signs: tea-coloured/smoky urine (haematuria), facial puffiness in the morning (periorbital oedema), reduced urine output, high blood pressure. Advise parents to return immediately if these appear. Urine dipstick at PHC level is sufficient for screening. PSGN can cause permanent kidney damage if unrecognized.

Impetigo is HIGHLY CONTAGIOUS — Strict Hygiene Measures: Child should not share towels, washcloths, or bedding. Lesions should be covered with gauze. Hands washed frequently. School exclusion until 24–48 hours of antibiotic therapy — this must be explained to teachers and parents. Simultaneous treatment of family members with active lesions prevents re-infection.

Remove Crusts for Effective Topical Antibiotic Penetration: Honey-coloured crusts must be carefully softened (warm water soak or saline-soaked gauze for 5 minutes) and removed before applying mupirocin or fusidic acid — antibiotic cannot penetrate through intact crust. Parents must be taught this technique.

Address Underlying Malnutrition: Recurrent impetigo in Indian children is often a marker of underlying Vitamin A deficiency, protein-energy malnutrition, and iron deficiency anaemia. Assess nutritional status, provide Vitamin A supplementation (if deficient), and refer to ICDS/nutrition program. Malnourished children need additional support — skin barrier cannot heal without adequate nutrition.

Avoid Topical Steroid Creams on Impetigo: A very common error in India — applying topical steroid creams (or steroid-antibiotic combinations without indication) on impetigo lesions. Steroids suppress the immune response and cause rapid spread. Any topical steroid-antibiotic combination should avoid the steroid component on active bacterial skin infections.
confirmation_tests_curated: Lesion Morphology Assessment: Honey-coloured golden crusts on erythematous base (non-bullous) or flaccid bullae (bullous impetigo) — classic clinical diagnosis. No investigations required for typical uncomplicated impetigo.
Nikolsky Sign: Negative in impetigo/SSSS localized; positive widespread in SSSS and pemphigus.

Wound/Crust Swab Culture and Sensitivity: Most important investigation for impetigo not responding to empirical antibiotics within 3–5 days. Identifies causative organism (GAS vs MSSA vs MRSA) and antibiotic sensitivities. Available at PHC bacteriology labs.

diseases.name: Pityriasis Versicolor
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

Clinical Morphology: Fine powdery-scaled hypo/hyperpigmented macules on upper trunk in a young adult in tropical India = clinical diagnosis in >90% of cases. Investigations confirmatory only.

Darier's Sign: Gentle scratching reveals fine white bran-like scale — simple PHC-level confirmation test.

2. KOH Microscopy (Gold Standard Bedside Confirmation)

KOH Mount of Skin Scraping: Scale scraped from lesion surface into KOH 10–20% on microscope slide. Shows classic 'spaghetti and meatballs' (or 'banana and grapes') pattern — short curved hyphae (spaghetti) with round spores (meatballs) of Malassezia. Pathognomonic. Available at all district hospital labs.

Modified KOH (DMSO + Calcofluor White): Fluorescent stain improves hyphae visibility under fluorescence microscope. More sensitive than simple KOH.

3. Wood's Lamp Examination

Wood's Lamp: UV light examination in darkened room — Malassezia fluoresces golden-yellow/copper-orange. Confirms diagnosis and helps delineate extent of involvement including subclinical lesions. Available at dermatology OPD.

4. Dermoscopy

Dermoscopy: Pityriasis versicolor shows yellow-white diffuse pigmentation with white reticular pattern and follicular plugging without the blood vessel patterns of psoriasis or lichen planus.

5. Culture (Rarely Needed)

Sabouraud Dextrose Agar with Olive Oil Overlay (Dixon Medium): Malassezia is lipophilic and requires fatty acid supplementation for culture. Culture rarely necessary for clinical management but used for research and treatment-resistant cases.

6. Differentiation Tests

Leprosy Exclusion: Check for sensory loss (temperature, fine touch) in hypopigmented patches — sensory loss + hypopigmentation = leprosy not PV. Slit skin smear if leprosy suspected.

Vitiligo Differentiation: Vitiligo = completely white (achromic) under Wood's lamp (chalk-white fluorescence), smooth surface, no scale, normal melanocyte destruction pattern. PV = hypopigmented under Wood's lamp, fine scale, KOH positive.

7. Monitoring

Repeat KOH (After Treatment): Confirms mycological cure (negative KOH). Pigmentation may remain abnormal despite mycological cure — reassure patient skin colour returns with time and sun exposure.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Antifungals (First-Line)

Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo: Apply to entire affected area (trunk, shoulders) as lotion — leave for 10–15 minutes before rinsing. Once daily for 3–4 weeks. Most effective, widely available, and affordable in India.

Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo/Lotion: Apply to affected skin, leave 15–30 minutes, rinse. Use daily for 2 weeks then weekly for 1–2 months maintenance. Very cost-effective option.

Zinc Pyrithione 1–2% Shampoo: Anti-Malassezia shampoo applied to affected skin. Leave 5–10 minutes and rinse. Daily for 4 weeks.

Clotrimazole 1% Cream: Apply twice daily for 4–6 weeks on affected patches. Broadspectrum antifungal.

Miconazole 2% Cream: Apply twice daily for 4–6 weeks. Azole antifungal targeting Malassezia.

Terbinafine 1% Cream: Apply once daily for 2 weeks on affected areas. Anti-Malassezia activity — caution: oral terbinafine is NOT effective for PV (Malassezia needs different mechanism).

2. Systemic Antifungals (Extensive / Recurrent PV)

Itraconazole 200mg once daily for 5–7 days oral: Most effective systemic treatment for extensive or recurrent PV. Pulse: 200mg/day for 7 days per month for 3–6 months maintenance for recurrence prevention.

Fluconazole 300–400mg single dose or weekly for 4 weeks oral: Alternative systemic treatment. Single high-dose (300–400mg) simplest regimen — exercise immediately after to promote sweating and skin delivery.

Ketoconazole 200mg once daily for 10 days oral: Older systemic option. Hepatotoxicity risk limits long-term use — prefer Itraconazole or Fluconazole.

3. Maintenance Prophylaxis (Highly Recommended in India — High Recurrence)

Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo once weekly (body): Long-term monthly/weekly application during hot season — prevents recurrence. Selenium Sulphide or Zinc Pyrithione weekly shampoo acceptable alternatives.

Itraconazole 200mg monthly (pulse): Monthly pulse systemic prophylaxis during summer months for patients with frequent recurrence.

4. Adjunctive

Sunscreen SPF 30+ (for re-pigmentation): Regular sun exposure to treated areas accelerates melanocyte recovery and re-pigmentation. Paradoxically, avoid excessive sunscreen on treated areas — controlled mild UV exposure helps re-pigment.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Tea Tree Oil (5% in carrier oil): Strong topical anti-Malassezia activity. Clinical studies show comparable efficacy to topical antifungals for PV. Mix 5 drops pure tea tree oil in 10ml coconut-free carrier oil; apply to lesions twice daily.

Neem Oil (diluted 1:5 in sesame oil): Anti-Malassezia and anti-inflammatory. Neem's nimbidin has antifungal properties. Apply to affected patches daily; wash after 30 minutes. Traditional Indian antifungal remedy.

Turmeric Paste (External Low Concentration): Fresh turmeric in coconut-free oil — anti-Malassezia properties from curcumin. Apply on patches for 20 minutes daily. Note: may temporarily yellow skin.

Haridra (Turmeric) Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India — Internal): Internal curcumin for systemic anti-fungal and anti-inflammatory support. 500mg twice daily after meals.

Neem Capsules (Himalaya): Internal blood purifier with antifungal activity. For recurrent, widespread pityriasis versicolor. 1–2 capsules twice daily after meals.

Khadirarishta (AVP/Dabur): Classical fermented Khadira decoction — antifungal, anti-inflammatory blood purifier. For recurrent/constitutional predisposition. 15–20ml with equal water twice daily.

Gandhaka Rasayana: Purified sulphur internal formulation — potent antifungal activity confirmed in Ayurvedic literature and some clinical studies. For recurrent fungal skin conditions. Under Vaidya supervision.

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) + Neem Combination Tablets: Anti-Pitta, anti-Malassezia internal formulation for recurrent pityriasis and seborrheic states. Reduces fungal recurrence.

Coconut Oil with Camphor (Kapur): Traditional Indian application — coconut oil base with camphor (antifungal) applied to PV patches. Camphor has demonstrated in-vitro anti-Malassezia activity.

Aloe Vera Gel: Anti-fungal and anti-inflammatory topical application. Supportive for mild PV and post-treatment skin soothing.

Panchanimbadi Churna: Five bitter herbs — internal antifungal and blood purifier for recurrent Malassezia-associated skin conditions. 3–5g twice daily with honey.
diseases.india_specific:
Tropical Climate and Hyperhidrosis: India's hot humid tropical climate with prolonged heat and monsoon humidity dramatically promotes Malassezia furfur overgrowth — India has one of the world's highest prevalences of pityriasis versicolor (15–20% population affected). Profuse sweating and sebum production are primary drivers.

Misdiagnosis as Vitiligo or Leprosy: Hypopigmented PV is extremely commonly misdiagnosed as vitiligo or borderline leprosy (both hypopigmented skin conditions) in India, resulting in severe stigma, unnecessary investigations, and inappropriate treatments. Key differentiation: PV has fine scale (not present in vitiligo/leprous patches), normal sensation (unlike leprosy), responds to antifungals.

Coconut Oil — Malassezia Substrate: Widespread use of coconut oil as daily scalp and body emollient in India provides a rich fatty acid substrate specifically promoting Malassezia proliferation. Patients should be counselled to reduce body-area oil application.

Post-Treatment Hypopigmentation Persistence: Even after successful antifungal treatment and confirmed mycological cure, hypopigmentation may persist for months — until skin melanocytes in the affected areas recover. This is a common cause of apparent treatment 'failure' and non-compliance in India — explicit counselling is critical.

HIV and Immunocompromised Patients: Severe, extensive, or recalcitrant pityriasis versicolor involving face and multiple body areas should prompt HIV testing — as with seborrheic dermatitis, extensive PV is a marker of immunodeficiency in India.
diseases.important_notes:
Skin Colour MAY NOT Immediately Return After Treatment: This is the most critical counselling point for pityriasis versicolor in India. Even after successful antifungal treatment and negative KOH (mycological cure), hypopigmented patches can persist for 1–6 months until melanocytes recover. This is NOT treatment failure. Patients must understand this explicitly to prevent premature treatment change and unnecessary anxiety. Moderate sun exposure to treated areas accelerates re-pigmentation.

This is NOT Vitiligo, NOT Leprosy: Explicitly reassure patients and families. PV is a fungal skin infection, fully treatable, and NOT a sign of leprosy, not an autoimmune disease, and NOT permanent. In India, the stigma around vitiligo and leprosy is severe — a misdiagnosis causes enormous psychological harm. Distinguish clearly: PV has scale + responds to antifungals; vitiligo is chalk-white + no scale; leprosy has anaesthesia.

Long-Term Maintenance is Essential Due to High Recurrence in Indian Climate: Pityriasis versicolor has >80% recurrence within 2 years in tropical India without maintenance. After acute treatment and clearance, continue Ketoconazole or Selenium Sulphide shampoo applied to trunk once weekly, or monthly Itraconazole 200mg pulse during summer months. Maintenance therapy period = at least 6 months to years in India's climate.

Reduce Oil Application on Body: Counsel all patients to reduce or eliminate application of heavy vegetable oils (coconut oil, mustard oil) on chest, back, and arms — these provide fatty acids that directly feed Malassezia yeast. Light moisturizer without occlusive oils is acceptable.

Test for HIV / Immunodeficiency in Extensive Cases: Pityriasis versicolor involving the face, widespread body areas, and resistant to standard treatment should prompt HIV counselling and testing — as with seborrheic dermatitis, extensive or recalcitrant PV is a recognized opportunistic dermatological marker of HIV disease in India.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Morphology: Fine powdery-scaled hypo/hyperpigmented macules on upper trunk in a young adult in tropical India = clinical diagnosis in >90% of cases. Investigations confirmatory only.
Darier's Sign: Gentle scratching reveals fine white bran-like scale — simple PHC-level confirmation test.

KOH Mount of Skin Scraping: Scale scraped from lesion surface into KOH 10–20% on microscope slide. Shows classic 'spaghetti and meatballs' (or 'banana and grapes') pattern — short curved hyphae (spaghetti) with round spores (meatballs) of Malassezia. Pathognomonic. Available at all district hospital labs.

diseases.name: Alopecia Areata
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

Clinical Pattern Recognition: Round/oval smooth non-scarring patches of hair loss with exclamation mark hairs at margins = clinical diagnosis. Dermoscopy highly useful for confirmation.

Pull Test: Gentle traction of 60 hairs proximal to patch margin — >6 hairs extracted = positive active disease. Simple PHC-level test.

2. Trichoscopy (Dermoscopy of Scalp)

Dermoscopy Findings: Yellow dots (empty follicles — good prognosis), black dots (broken hairs — active disease), exclamation mark hairs (pathognomonic), short vellus hairs (regrowth sign). Dermoscopy distinguishes AA from trichotillomania and tinea capitis without biopsy.

3. Laboratory Investigations

TSH + Free T4 + Anti-TPO Antibodies: Mandatory screen for autoimmune thyroid disease — common treatable association in Indian patients with alopecia areata.

CBC (Complete Blood Count): Anaemia (iron deficiency), lymphocytosis (autoimmune pattern).

Serum Ferritin: Iron deficiency (ferritin <30 ng/ml) worsens hair shedding and should be corrected — though it is a co-factor not the primary cause of alopecia areata.

Serum Vitamin D (25-OH): Deficiency very common in India despite sun exposure (due to skin pigmentation, dietary patterns); associated with autoimmune hair diseases. Correct if <30 ng/ml.

Serum Zinc: Zinc deficiency impairs hair metabolism; correction simple and inexpensive.

ANA (Anti-Nuclear Antibody): For exclusion of SLE or other connective tissue autoimmune disease when alopecia is widespread or associated with systemic features.

4. Scalp Biopsy (Atypical or Uncertain Cases)

Scalp Punch Biopsy (4mm): Peribulbar lymphocytic infiltrate ('swarm of bees' around hair bulb) — pathognomonic histology. Horizontal sectioning required for accurate follicle assessment. Required when clinical diagnosis uncertain.

5. Differentiation

Tinea Capitis (KOH + Fungal Culture): Scalp ringworm mimics alopecia areata in children — always check KOH of scalp scrapings in children with patchy scalp hair loss before diagnosing alopecia areata.

Trichotillomania: Irregular, bizarrely shaped patches; hairs of different lengths; trichoscopy shows coiled broken hairs. Psychological assessment required.

Androgenetic Alopecia (AGA): Diffuse patterned thinning (temple recession + vertex) in men; frontoparietal thinning in women. No discrete patches. No exclamation mark hairs.

6. Disease Severity Tools

SALT Score (Severity of Alopecia Tool): Percentage scalp area affected. SALT 0 = no hair loss; SALT 100 = complete scalp hair loss. Standard monitoring tool. SALT ≤20 = mild; SALT >50 = severe.

7. Indian Dermatology Guidelines

IADVL Alopecia Areata Guidelines 2022: Topical/intralesional steroids for mild-moderate AA. Systemic immunosuppressants for extensive. Baricitinib/Ritlecitinib for severe refractory AA. Thyroid + iron workup mandatory.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Treatments (Mild-Moderate AA — <50% Scalp Involvement)

Clobetasol Propionate 0.05% Solution/Foam: Potent topical corticosteroid applied to alopecia patches once daily for 3 months. Monitor for local side effects (atrophy, folliculitis).

Minoxidil 5% Solution or Foam (Males) / 2% Solution (Females): Promotes hair regrowth via vasodilation and direct follicle stimulation. Apply twice daily to patches. NOT disease-modifying but improves regrowth speed alongside immunosuppression.

Anthralin (Dithranol) 0.5–1% Cream (Contact Sensitisation Therapy): Applied to patches for 20–60 minutes daily — contact sensitisation mechanism stimulates non-specific immune response. Effective for patchy AA. Short contact, rinse thoroughly.

2. Intralesional Steroids (Small Patches — Dermatology Procedure)

Triamcinolone Acetonide 5–10mg/ml Intralesional Injections: Most effective treatment for limited alopecia areata with <5 patches. Injected into patch every 4–6 weeks. Regrowth in 70% within 3 months. Specialist/dermatologist procedure.

3. Systemic Immunosuppressants (Extensive AA — >50%)

Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (4–6 weeks, taper): For rapidly progressive, widespread alopecia areata. Induces remission but high relapse rate on tapering. Short-course; not for long-term maintenance.

Oral minipulse Betamethasone 5mg on 2 consecutive days/week: IADVL/Indian protocol for extensive alopecia areata. Effective and reduces steroid total dose compared to daily prednisolone.

Methotrexate 10–25mg weekly oral + Folic Acid 5mg weekly: Steroid-sparing immunosuppressant for extensive alopecia areata. Effective; takes 3–6 months. Monitor LFT + CBC.

Cyclosporine 2.5–5mg/kg/day oral: For extensive rapidly progressive AA. Monitor blood pressure and creatinine. Not first-line in India due to cost and monitoring requirements.

4. JAK Inhibitors (Severe Refractory AA — CDSCO Approved)

Baricitinib 2–4mg/day oral: JAK1/2 inhibitor approved by CDSCO for severe AA. Significant scalp hair regrowth in clinical trials. Monthly CBC + LFT monitoring.

Ritlecitinib 50mg/day oral: Selective JAK3/TEC inhibitor approved for severe AA. Well tolerated.

5. Adjunctive

Folic Acid 5mg/week: During Methotrexate therapy — prevents hepatotoxicity and cytopenias.

Vitamin D3 60,000 IU weekly (if deficient): Corrects common Indian Vitamin D deficiency associated with autoimmune hair disease.
