diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Bhringraj (Eclipta alba) Oil (Dabur Mahabhringraj / Kesh King): Most widely used Ayurvedic hair oil in clinical Ayurvedic practice. Bhringaraja is the primary Kesha-vardhana (hair-promoting) herb. Scalp massage twice weekly — anti-inflammatory on scalp, promotes follicle stimulation. Multiple RCT evidence for hair growth promotion.

Babchi (Bakuchi / Psoralea corylifolia) Oil (diluted): Traditionally used for alopecia areata — psoralen content causes photosensitisation and contact immunostimulation. Apply to patches, expose to morning sunlight for 10–15 minutes. USE DILUTED — concentrated Bakuchi oil causes severe burns. Under Vaidya supervision.

Amla (Amalaki / Emblica officinalis) Oil + Brahmi Oil: Combined Amla-Brahmi hair oils — antioxidant (Vitamin C in Amla), adaptogenic (Brahmi reduces cortisol). Traditional scalp nourishment for stress-triggered hair loss.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Capsules (Himalaya/Dabur): Adaptogenic herb — reduces cortisol and psychological stress, which are key AA triggers in India. 300–600mg standardized extract twice daily. Clinical evidence for stress reduction.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) Tablets: Adaptogen and anxiolytic — reduces stress-induced alopecia areata triggers. 300mg twice daily. Supports sleep quality.

Narasimha Rasayana: Classical Ayurvedic immunomodulatory and Rasayana formulation for Khalitya (alopecia). Contains Ashwagandha, Bhallataka, and Amalaki — systemic tonic and hair-promoting.

Shatavari Churna (Female Patients): Phytoestrogenic and immunomodulatory adaptogen for female alopecia areata — particularly effective for PCOS-related or hormonal-trigger AA in Indian women. 3–5g with warm milk.

Thikthaka Ghrita: Medicated Tikta (bitter) ghee for autoimmune skin and hair diseases — anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory. 5–10ml on empty stomach under Vaidya supervision.

Neem + Haridra Scalp Paste: Anti-inflammatory topical paste applied on alopecia patches. Haridra (curcumin) reduces local autoimmune inflammation; Neem reduces secondary scalp infection.

Sesame Oil (Tila Taila): Classical Ayurvedic scalp massage oil — warm sesame oil massage stimulates scalp circulation, reduces Vata-Pitta imbalance, nourishes follicles. Applied 1 hour before hair wash.

Chyawanprash (Dabur): Comprehensive Rasayana formulation — Amla-based immunomodulatory tonic supplementing conventional AA treatment. Addresses underlying micronutrient deficiencies common in India. 1–2 teaspoons daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Thyroid Autoimmunity Association: Hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's thyroiditis) is strongly associated with alopecia areata — affecting up to 8–28% of alopecia areata patients. India has a high prevalence of both autoimmune thyroid disease and iodine deficiency. TSH + Anti-TPO screening mandatory in all alopecia areata patients.

Atopic Triad Association: Alopecia areata is strongly associated with atopic dermatitis, allergic rhinitis, and asthma. India's rising atopic disease prevalence (urbanisation, hygiene hypothesis) correlates with increasing alopecia areata incidence.

Nutritional Deficiency Misattribution: Alopecia areata in India is very commonly misdiagnosed as nutritional hair loss (iron deficiency, Vitamin D deficiency, protein deficiency) — leading to prolonged unnecessary supplementation without treatment of the underlying autoimmune condition. Ferritin, Vitamin D, and zinc should be checked and corrected but are RARELY the primary cause of patchy alopecia areata.

Stress-Precipitation in Indian Context: Academic examination stress, marital stress, bereavement, and financial hardship reliably trigger or worsen alopecia areata flares in genetically predisposed Indian individuals.

JAK Inhibitor Access and Affordability: Baricitinib and Ritlecitinib (JAK inhibitors) — newly approved by CDSCO for severe alopecia areata — are highly effective but very expensive (₹8,000–25,000/month). Affordability remains a critical barrier in India; government CGHS/PMJAY coverage is being expanded.
diseases.important_notes:
JAK INHIBITORS HAVE TRANSFORMED SEVERE ALOPECIA AREATA TREATMENT: Prior to JAK inhibitor approval, Alopecia Totalis and Alopecia Universalis had essentially no effective systemic treatments — only modest and temporary responses to steroids. Baricitinib (FDA approved August 2022) and Ruxolitinib (FDA approved June 2023) represent the first specifically approved treatments for severe AA. BRAVE-AA trials show approximately 33% of severe AA patients achieve SALT ≤20 (>80% scalp coverage) on Baricitinib 4 mg. In India, Tofacitinib (already approved for RA) is widely used off-label for severe AA at specialist centres. Important: JAK inhibitors suppress the disease but hair may re-fall after stopping — long-term treatment is often needed. Screen for TB (IGRA/Mantoux), hepatitis B/C, lipids, and CBC before starting.

INTRALESIONAL TRIAMCINOLONE IS THE MOST EFFECTIVE TREATMENT FOR LIMITED PATCHY AA: For patients with 1–3 patches of limited AA, intralesional triamcinolone acetonide (ILTA) at 5 mg/mL (or up to 10 mg/mL for scalp, 2.5 mg/mL for eyebrows) is the gold standard dermatological treatment. Monthly injections over 6 months produce 70–80% regrowth in localised AA. The technique matters: injections must be intradermal (not subcutaneous) to avoid subcutaneous atrophy. Limitation: not practical for extensive or total loss. Common India error: prescribing only oral supplements (biotin, zinc) for patchy AA — these have no evidence for AA and delay effective treatment.

SPONTANEOUS RECOVERY IS COMMON IN LIMITED AA — BUT PROGNOSIS WORSENS WITH EXTENSION: Approximately 50% of patients with limited patchy AA (<25% scalp) regrow within 1 year without treatment. However, this optimistic statistic must not lead to false reassurance for patients with adverse prognostic features: ophiasis pattern, AT/AU, nail involvement, early onset (<5 years), atopy comorbidity, family history, and >5 years duration. These patients have <10% chance of spontaneous complete recovery and require active treatment. Patient counseling must balance honest long-term prognosis with the genuine transformative potential of modern JAK inhibitor therapy.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Pattern Recognition: Round/oval smooth non-scarring patches of hair loss with exclamation mark hairs at margins = clinical diagnosis. Dermoscopy highly useful for confirmation.
Pull Test: Gentle traction of 60 hairs proximal to patch margin — >6 hairs extracted = positive active disease. Simple PHC-level test.

Dermoscopy Findings: Yellow dots (empty follicles — good prognosis), black dots (broken hairs — active disease), exclamation mark hairs (pathognomonic), short vellus hairs (regrowth sign). Dermoscopy distinguishes AA from trichotillomania and tinea capitis without biopsy.

diseases.name: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (EMERGENCY — Do Not Delay Treatment for Investigations)

Clinical Criteria for SJS: (1) Epidermal detachment <10% BSA + (2) Mucosal involvement (≥2 sites) + (3) History of new drug within 8 weeks. Clinical diagnosis; investigations are confirmatory and prognostic only.

Nikolsky Sign: Positive — gentle lateral pressure on apparently normal perilesional skin causes epidermal detachment. Perform carefully — causes pain.

SCORTEN Severity Scoring: Calculated on Day 1 of admission — 7 variables (see Col B). Guides ICU triage, burns unit transfer, and family counselling.

2. Histopathology (Optional — Confirmation if Uncertain)

Skin Biopsy (H&E): Full-thickness epidermal necrosis — necrotic keratinocytes extending from basal to superficial layers. Subepidermal cleavage below necrotic epidermis. Sparse dermal infiltrate. Distinguishes SJS from SSSS (subcorneal split) and pemphigus.

Frozen Section Biopsy: Available at tertiary centres — rapid (1 hour) histological diagnosis in atypical presentations.

3. Blood Investigations (Prognostic and Management Guidance)

CBC + Differential: Leukopenia indicates poor prognosis; eosinophilia suggests drug hypersensitivity; neutrophilia indicates secondary bacterial sepsis.

Serum Creatinine + BUN + Electrolytes: Renal failure (SCORTEN parameter); electrolyte monitoring for fluid replacement.

LFT (Liver Function Tests): Hepatic involvement common in SJS/TEN; bilirubinemia and transaminase elevation.

Serum Albumin + Total Protein: Low in extensive disease — protein loss from denuded skin. Guides nutritional support.

Blood Culture (2 sets): Secondary bacteraemia — Gram-positive cocci (Staphylococcus) most common; also Gram-negative. Mandatory when sepsis suspected.

4. Microbiological Swabs

Wound/Skin Swab Culture and Sensitivity: Daily swabs from denuded areas — Pseudomonas aeruginosa and MRSA colonization common in Indian ICU settings. Guides targeted antibiotic therapy.

5. Causative Drug Investigation

Drug History Timeline: Meticulous chronological history — identify the causative drug (onset typically 4–28 days after new drug introduction for SJS). All new medications in the preceding 8 weeks must be listed and reviewed against SJS causation probability.

6. Ophthalmic Assessment

Slit Lamp Examination: Mandatory in all SJS patients — documents pseudomembrane formation, corneal epithelial defects, symblepharon. Baseline for monitoring and medicolegal documentation.

7. Indian Guidelines

IADVL/Indian Dermatology SJS Guidelines 2021: Stop causative drug IMMEDIATELY. IV cyclosporine (3mg/kg/day) for progressive SJS — most evidence-based systemic treatment in India. Ophthalmology + burns surgery referral mandatory.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. IMMEDIATE — STOP CAUSATIVE DRUG

Causative Drug Discontinuation: The SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT INTERVENTION — stopping the causative drug IMMEDIATELY reduces mortality significantly. Even 1 additional day of continued drug increases mortality. Identify and stop ALL potentially causative drugs. Do not restart.

2. ICU Supportive Care (Burns Unit Equivalent)

IV Fluid Resuscitation: Parkland formula-guided fluid replacement for denuded BSA — same as burns management. 2–4ml/kg per %BSA involved per 24 hours. Default: Hartmann's/Ringer's lactate.

IV Analgesia: Morphine 2–5mg IV PRN or Tramadol 50–100mg IV for pain management of denuded skin.

Nasogastric Tube + High-Protein Liquid Feeds: Oral feeding is impossible — NGT mandatory. 35–40 kcal/kg/day with high protein.

3. Wound Care (Non-Adherent Dressings)

Paraffin-Impregnated Non-Adherent Gauze (Jelonet/Mepitel): Non-adherent wound dressings on denuded areas — prevents dressing-wound adherence and further trauma. Changed daily in aseptic conditions.

Chlorhexidine 0.05% Solution Wound Irrigation: Gentle irrigation of denuded skin areas — antiseptic without cytotoxicity at 0.05% concentration.

4. Immunomodulatory Treatment (IADVL Recommended)

Cyclosporine 3mg/kg/day IV (then oral) for 7–10 days: Most evidence-based treatment for progressive SJS in India — inhibits T-cell activation causing keratinocyte death. Start EARLY (first 48–72 hours) for maximum benefit.

IVIG (IV Immunoglobulin) 1g/kg/day for 3 days: For patients contraindicated to cyclosporine (renal failure, hypertension). Evidence is moderate. High cost limits use in India.

5. Eye Care (Mandatory)

Preservative-Free Artificial Tears Drops (every 1–2 hours): Lubricates conjunctiva; prevents corneal drying and ulceration during acute SJS.

Topical Cyclosporine 0.1% Eye Drops: Anti-inflammatory eye drops to prevent chronic ocular sequelae (dry eye, symblepharon). Start in acute phase.

Amniotic Membrane Transplant (AMT): Sutureless AM ring for severe acute ocular SJS — prevents corneal ulceration and permanent visual loss. Available at ophthalmic tertiary centres.

6. Infection Prophylaxis

Systemic Antibiotics: Target-based on wound swab cultures — avoid prophylactic antibiotics (increases resistance). Treat colonisation only when clinical sepsis present.

Antifungal Prophylaxis (Fluconazole 150mg alternate days): For patients with extensive denuded skin on immunosuppression — Candida superinfection risk.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Cyclosporine 50mg Capsules (10 caps): Most evidence-based immunomodulation for progressive SJS. PMBJP stores at tertiary level.

Morphine 10mg/ml Injection (1ml amp): IV analgesia for SJS pain. Hospital formulary.

Tramadol 100mg Injection (2ml amp): IV analgesia alternative for denuded skin pain.

Ringer's Lactate Solution 500ml: IV fluid resuscitation for denuded BSA loss.

Chlorhexidine 0.05% Solution 500ml: Wound irrigation antiseptic. PHC and district hospital.

Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Antifungal prophylaxis for immunosuppressed SJS patients.

Artificial Tears (Carmellose 0.5% Eye Drops 10ml): Lubricant eye drops for SJS ocular involvement.

Pantoprazole 40mg Injection: Stress ulcer prophylaxis during steroid/cyclosporine therapy.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Cyclosporine 50mg (10 capsules): Rs.385

Tramadol 100mg injection (1 amp): Rs.22

Ringer's Lactate 500ml: Rs.32

Chlorhexidine 0.05% Solution 500ml: Rs.38

Fluconazole 150mg (1 capsule): Rs.12

Carmellose 0.5% Eye Drops 10ml: Rs.35

Pantoprazole 40mg injection: Rs.28
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Jatyadi Tailam (External — Wound Healing): Classical medicated oil with wound-healing herbs (Jasmini, Neem, Sesame). Can be applied as a supportive wound dressing adjunct on actively healing denuded areas after acute phase — promotes re-epithelialisation and soothes raw surface.

Aloe Vera Gel (Pure, Preservative-Free): Anti-inflammatory, wound-healing, antimicrobial properties. Supportive topical application on healing SJS skin erosions in the recovery phase. Widely available and affordable.

Madhu (Honey — Medical Grade): Natural antiseptic and wound-healing agent. Applied to denuded skin as antibiotic-free wound dressing in recovery phase. Manuka honey has clinical burn-wound evidence.

Turmeric Milk (Internal — Recovery Phase): Anti-inflammatory curcumin via warm milk — supportive nutritional and anti-inflammatory role during SJS recovery when oral feeding resumes. NOT during acute phase.

Panchatikta Ghrita: Tikta-ghee for systemic immune modulation in autoimmune skin diseases. Adjunct in recovery phase to reduce post-inflammatory sequelae. Under Vaidya supervision only.

Triphala Eye Wash: Dilute Triphala decoction (cooled, filtered, sterile) used as Ayurvedic eye wash for conjunctival inflammation. Traditional anti-inflammatory eye care supporting conventional ophthalmological treatment.

Sariva Decoction: Cooling Rakta-shodhaka (blood purifier) for post-SJS inflammatory state in recovery. 30ml twice daily when oral feeding tolerated.

ABSOLUTE CRITICAL WARNING — SJS is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome is a LIFE-THREATENING dermatological emergency with up to 30% mortality. Causative drug must be stopped IMMEDIATELY. Patient MUST be hospitalised — burns unit or dermatology ICU. Ayurvedic treatment has NO role in the ACUTE management of SJS. Topical Ayurvedic applications are supportive ONLY in the recovery phase (re-epithelialisation stage) and only as adjuncts. Any delay in medical referral for Ayurvedic treatment in acute SJS may be fatal.
diseases.india_specific:
Allopurinol — Most Common SJS Trigger in India: Allopurinol (used for gout and hyperuricaemia — extremely widely prescribed in India) is the single most common cause of SJS/TEN in India, particularly in patients with HLA-B*5801 allele (prevalent in Han Chinese, Thai, and Indian — especially South Indian — populations). ALL new allopurinol prescriptions should be preceded by HLA-B*5801 testing where available.

Antiepileptic Drugs (Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Lamotrigine): India has a very high epilepsy burden. Carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN is strongly associated with HLA-B*1502 (prevalent in South and Southeast Asian populations). HLA screening before carbamazepine initiation is recommended by CDSCO in Asian patients.

Antibiotics (Cotrimoxazole, Sulphonamides, Penicillins): Cotrimoxazole widely used in India for UTI, PCP, and infections — a significant SJS trigger. HIV-positive patients on Cotrimoxazole have highest risk — 30–50x higher SJS risk.

NSAIDs (Oxicam Group): Piroxicam, tenoxicam, meloxicam — oxicam-type NSAIDs, widely available OTC in India, are among the most frequent SJS/TEN triggers — far more than ibuprofen/diclofenac.

Delayed Referral and Mortality: Mortality from SJS/TEN in India remains 15–30% in tertiary centres — significantly higher than global figures — due to delayed recognition at primary care level, inappropriate continued drug use, and limited burn-unit capacity. EARLY recognition and drug discontinuation saves lives.
diseases.important_notes:
MEDICAL EMERGENCY — IMMEDIATE DRUG STOP AND HOSPITAL REFERRAL: SJS is a dermatological emergency. Any patient with: new skin rash + fever + painful mouth/eye erosions + recently started new drug (especially Allopurinol, Carbamazepine, Cotrimoxazole, NSAIDs) must have the causative drug STOPPED IMMEDIATELY and be referred to a tertiary hospital/burns unit. Every extra day the drug is continued increases mortality. PHC/CHC physicians MUST recognise the early signs.

HLA-B*5801 and HLA-B*1502 Genetic Screening Before High-Risk Drugs: For Indian patients being prescribed Allopurinol (especially South Indian, Nepali, Sri Lankan populations) — HLA-B*5801 testing is now recommended before initiation. For Carbamazepine in South and Southeast Asian patients — HLA-B*1502 testing is CDSCO-recommended. These tests are available at AIIMS, PGI Chandigarh, SGPGI, and major medical colleges. Testing prevents a potentially fatal Stevens-Johnson syndrome.

Ophthalmology Referral is Mandatory — Prevent Blindness: Ocular SJS is the most devastating long-term complication — causing dry eye, trichiasis, corneal scarring, symblepharon, and permanent blindness in up to 35% of survivors. Ophthalmologist must be involved from Day 1. Amniotic membrane placement in first 72 hours dramatically reduces long-term ocular sequelae.

Document Causative Drug Permanently in Patient Records: The causative drug must be documented as a permanent ALLERGY in the patient's medical record, Aarogya Setu, and Ayushman Bharat records. Patient must be given a written drug allergy card to carry at all times. Patient and family must be educated never to take the drug again — cross-reactive drugs in the same class also to be avoided.

SJS Survivors Need Long-Term Follow-Up: Post-SJS chronic sequelae include: dry eye syndrome, vaginal stenosis, urethral strictures, oesophageal strictures, and pulmonary complications. Regular dermatology, ophthalmology, urology/gynaecology, and respiratory follow-up for at least 3–5 years post-recovery. PTSD and anxiety counselling frequently needed.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (EMERGENCY — Do Not Delay Treatment for Investigations)

Clinical Criteria for SJS: (1) Epidermal detachment <10% BSA + (2) Mucosal involvement (≥2 sites) + (3) History of new drug within 8 weeks. Clinical diagnosis; investigations are confirmatory and prognostic only.
Nikolsky Sign: Positive — gentle lateral pressure on apparently normal perilesional skin causes epidermal detachment. Perform carefully — causes pain.
SCORTEN Severity Scoring: Calculated on Day 1 of admission — 7 variables (see Col B). Guides ICU triage, burns unit transfer, and family counselling.

diseases.name: Pressure Ulcers
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Staging (Primary Tool — NPUAP/EPUAP Classification)

National Pressure Injury Advisory Panel (NPUAP) Staging: Stages 1–4 + Unstageable + Deep Tissue Injury — clinical examination at each PHC/ward visit. Complete staging documentation drives treatment intensity.

Wound Assessment (SIZE + TISSUE + INFECTION + MOISTURE + EDGE — TIME Framework): Wound size (length x width x depth in cm), tissue type (granulation/slough/eschar/bone), infection signs, exudate level, and wound edge undermining — documented at every assessment.

Braden Scale: Validated tool for pressure ulcer risk assessment — 6 domains: sensory perception (1–4), moisture (1–4), activity (1–4), mobility (1–4), nutrition (1–4), friction/shear (1–3). Score ≤18 = at risk; ≤12 = high risk. Guides preventive measures intensity.

2. Microbiological Investigations

Wound Swab Culture and Sensitivity (Qualitative): For infected PUs — identifies colonizing organisms (S. aureus, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, Bacteroides). Guides targeted topical and systemic antibiotic selection. Do NOT culture all pressure ulcers — culture ONLY when clinical infection present.

Deep Tissue Biopsy/Quantitative Culture: For deep Stage 3–4 ulcers with suspected osteomyelitis — quantitative culture >10^5 organisms/gram tissue = true infection (not colonisation).

Blood Culture (2 sets): For systemic sepsis from infected pressure ulcer — leukocytosis, fever, hypotension. Mandatory before IV antibiotics.

3. Imaging

X-Ray of Underlying Bone: For Stage 4 ulcers with bone exposure — periosteal reaction, bony destruction = osteomyelitis.

MRI Bone: Most sensitive for osteomyelitis — bone marrow edema. Essential for surgical planning in sacral and heel pressure ulcers with suspected osteomyelitis.

4. Nutritional and Metabolic Assessment

Serum Albumin + Pre-albumin + Total Protein: LOW serum albumin (<3.5g/dL) = severe protein depletion, very poor wound healing prognosis. Mandatory baseline. Pre-albumin is better short-term nutrition marker.

CBC: Anaemia (common in Indian PU patients) impairs oxygen delivery to wound — slows healing. Identify and treat iron deficiency anaemia.

HbA1c + Fasting Blood Sugar: Hyperglycaemia impairs wound neutrophil function and healing. Glycaemic optimisation mandatory for ALL diabetic pressure ulcer patients.

Serum Zinc + Vitamin C: Zinc and Vitamin C deficiency impair wound healing. Supplement if deficient.

5. Indian Guidelines

ISCCM (Indian Society of Critical Care Medicine) PU Guidelines: Braden scale on admission for all ICU patients. 2-hourly repositioning. Foam mattress minimum standard. Nutritional support targeting 1.2–1.5g protein/kg/day.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Wound Bed Preparation and Debridement

Collagenase Ointment (Enzymatic Debridement): Selective enzymatic debridement of slough and necrotic tissue — applied once daily. Preserves viable granulation tissue better than sharp debridement in neuropathic/elderly patients.

Normal Saline Wound Irrigation: Most recommended wound cleansing solution — physiological, non-cytotoxic. Irrigate with 35ml syringe and 18-gauge needle (100–150ml/cm^2 wound area) at each dressing change.

Povidone Iodine 5% Solution (Short-Term): Antimicrobial wound wash for infected pressure ulcers — however cytotoxic with chronic use, impairs granulation. Use maximum 1–2 weeks for heavily infected wounds only; transition to NS wound irrigation.

2. Wound Dressings (Evidence-Based)

Hydrocolloid Dressings (e.g., Duoderm): For Stage 2 ulcers — moist wound healing environment, autolytic debridement, periwound protection. Change every 3–5 days.

Foam Dressings (e.g., Allevyn): For moderately-heavily exudating Stage 2–3 ulcers — absorb exudate while maintaining moist environment. Change every 2–4 days.

Silver-Impregnated Wound Dressings (e.g., Mepilex Ag): For infected pressure ulcers — sustained antimicrobial release. Use for limited 2-week periods to reduce biofilm load.

Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT/VAC): For Stage 3–4 refractory ulcers — continuous or intermittent suction (−125mmHg) promotes granulation, reduces wound size. Available at district/teaching hospitals.

3. Systemic Antibiotics (For Infected Ulcers with Systemic Features ONLY)

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg three times daily oral (Mild-Moderate PU Infection): For polymicrobial PU infection — covers Staph, Strep, coliforms, and anaerobes.

Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5g IV every 6–8 hours: For severe PU infection/sepsis with Gram-negative organisms.

Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV twice daily: For MRSA pressure ulcer infection.

Metronidazole 500mg three times daily (IV or oral): Mandatory anaerobic cover for deep sacral or perianal pressure ulcers.

4. Nutrition (Foundational — Non-Negotiable)

High-Protein Nutritional Supplement (e.g., Ensure/Proteinex): 1.2–1.5g protein/kg/day mandatory for wound healing. Oral nutritional supplements if diet insufficient.

Zinc Sulphate 220mg twice daily oral: Wound healing micronutrient — supplement if serum zinc deficient.

Vitamin C 500mg twice daily oral: Collagen synthesis cofactor — supplementation recommended for all Stage 2+ pressure ulcers.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Normal Saline 0.9% 500ml Irrigation Bags: Wound cleansing standard solution. PMBJP.

Povidone Iodine 5% Solution 100ml: Short-term antimicrobial wound wash.

Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Anaerobic infection coverage for deep PUs.

Amoxicillin + Clavulanate 625mg Tablets (10 tabs): Oral polymicrobial infection cover.

Zinc Sulphate 220mg Capsules (10 caps): Wound healing micronutrient supplementation.

Vitamin C 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Collagen synthesis for wound healing.

Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Wound pain management.

Iron + Folic Acid Tablets (10 tabs): Correct anaemia impeding wound healing. PMBJP.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Jatyadi Tailam (External — Classical Wound Healing Oil): Most widely used Ayurvedic wound oil — contains Jasmine (Jasminum), Neem, and Sesame base with multiple healing herbs. Applied to clean, granulating pressure ulcer wounds — promotes re-epithelialisation, reduces inflammation, and provides antimicrobial action. Multiple traditional studies support wound healing. Apply after wound cleansing, cover with dressing.

Madhu (Honey — Medical Grade/Unprocessed): Applied topically as wound dressing on shallow pressure ulcers — potent antibacterial (hydrogen peroxide + methylglyoxal), osmotic debrider, and wound-healing promoter. Cover with gauze dressing. Change daily. Most accessible natural wound treatment in rural India.

Neem Paste Poultice: Fresh Neem leaf paste applied on infected pressure ulcer surface — antibacterial against MRSA and Pseudomonas in in-vitro studies. Applied as cool poultice, wrapped lightly; change twice daily. Supportive antimicrobial adjunct.

Haridra (Turmeric) + Coconut Oil Wound Application: Curcumin anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Applied as thin paste on clean Stage 1–2 pressure ulcer areas — reduces inflammation and secondary infection risk.

Brahmi Ghrita (Oral): Medicated Ghee with Brahmi (Bacopa) — anti-inflammatory and Vata-reducing properties. Internal support for bedridden patients; reduces Vata imbalance causing tissue wasting and skin fragility. 5–10ml twice daily under Vaidya supervision.

Chyawanprash (Oral): Comprehensive Rasayana — Amla-based antioxidant, immunostimulatory, and Vitamin C-rich nutritional supplement. Addresses micronutrient deficiencies common in malnourished bedridden patients at risk of pressure ulcers. 1–2 teaspoons twice daily.

Ashwagandha Churna (Oral): Anabolic and adaptogenic herb — promotes protein synthesis and muscle mass retention in debilitated bedridden patients. 3–5g with warm milk twice daily. Supports tissue healing and prevents further muscle wasting.

Eladi Keram (External): Classical Ayurvedic cooling oil for inflammatory, Stage 1–2 pressure areas on bony prominences — anti-inflammatory and skin-protective.

Nagaradi Tailam: Classical Ayurvedic oil with wound-healing and anti-Vata properties for bedridden patients. Apply on at-risk bony prominences as preventive pressure area care.

Triphala Kashaya Wound Wash: Triphala decoction (cooled, filtered) used as antiseptic/astringent wound wash for granulating pressure ulcers before dressing application.

Protein-Rich Ayurvedic Diet Counselling: For malnourished patients — emphasise Sattvic high-protein diet: dal, eggs, curd, milk, paneer, khichdi with ghee. Protein intake 1.2–1.5g/kg/day is the most evidence-based intervention for pressure ulcer healing.
diseases.india_specific:
Spinal Cord Injuries (Road Traffic Accidents): India has the world's second-highest road traffic accident mortality. Spinal cord injury patients (paraplegics, quadriplegics) are at extreme risk of pressure ulcers — sacrum, ischium, and heels under constant pressure without protective sensation. SCI patients in India often lack access to pressure-relieving equipment (air mattresses, wheelchair cushions) due to cost — dramatically increasing ulcer incidence.

Elderly Bedridden Patients: India's growing elderly population with strokes, fractures, and neurodegenerative diseases suffer high rates of hospital-acquired and community pressure ulcers. Undetected in Stage 1–2 in dark Indian skin (erythema less visible) — must palpate for warmth and boggy texture.

Malnutrition and Hypoalbuminaemia: Protein-energy malnutrition (endemic in India) causes severely impaired wound healing, skin fragility, and immune dysfunction. Serum albumin <3.5g/dL is one of the strongest predictors of non-healing pressure ulcers in Indian patients. Nutritional rehabilitation is non-negotiable.

Diabetes Mellitus: India's 100 million+ diabetic population has severely impaired wound healing, peripheral neuropathy, and immune dysfunction. Diabetic patients develop pressure ulcers faster and heal more poorly. Stage 3–4 ulcers in diabetics frequently lead to amputation.

Low Awareness and Late Presentation: Pressure ulcers in India are frequently not recognised until Stage 3–4 — by which time bone exposure and osteomyelitis have developed. Awareness campaigns among nursing staff, ASHA workers, and caregivers for early repositioning are critical.
diseases.important_notes:
PREVENTION IS THE ONLY CURE — 2-Hourly Repositioning is Non-Negotiable: Pressure ulcers are almost entirely preventable with timely repositioning. ALL bedridden patients (post-stroke, spinal injury, post-surgery, unconscious) MUST be repositioned every 2 hours — day and night. Stage 1 lesions detected early can be fully reversed. Once Stage 3–4 develops, healing takes months and surgery may be needed. Train families and ASHA workers in the 2-hour turning schedule from Day 1 of immobility.

Nutrition is the Most Under-Addressed Healing Factor in India: Serum albumin <3.5g/dL makes wound healing nearly impossible regardless of the dressing used. Before optimising wound dressings, OPTIMISE NUTRITION. Target 1.2–1.5g protein/kg/day. In malnourished patients, nutritional rehabilitation is more effective than any topical treatment. Protein supplements (Protinex, Ensure, or dal + milk diet) and iron supplementation for anaemia are foundational.

Pressure-Relieving Surfaces are Standard of Care: Non-powered foam mattresses (minimum standard for all bedbound patients), alternating pressure air mattresses (for high-risk patients), and heel-offloading boots are standard of care. In India, many hospitals and homes use hard cotton mattresses — a major preventable risk factor. ASHA workers and discharge planning must ensure appropriate mattress provision. Foam mattresses are available through government procurement schemes.

Osteomyelitis Requires Long-Term IV Antibiotics and Surgical Consultation: Stage 4 pressure ulcers with exposed bone — particularly over sacrum and heels — indicate likely osteomyelitis. MRI is diagnostic. Treatment requires 4–6 weeks of targeted IV antibiotics AND surgical debridement/sequestrectomy. These patients cannot be managed at PHC level — refer to tertiary surgical unit.

Caregiver Education is as Important as Medical Treatment: Pressure ulcer recurrence in India is nearly universal due to inadequate caregiver training. Every discharge must include training of at least one family member/caregiver in: repositioning schedule, skin inspection, pressure-relieving positioning techniques, wound dressing changes, and warning signs of infection (increased redness, pus, fever). Written pictorial instructions in regional language are essential.
confirmation_tests_curated: National Pressure Injury Advisory Panel (NPUAP) Staging: Stages 1–4 + Unstageable + Deep Tissue Injury — clinical examination at each PHC/ward visit. Complete staging documentation drives treatment intensity.

Wound Assessment (SIZE + TISSUE + INFECTION + MOISTURE + EDGE — TIME Framework): Wound size (length x width x depth in cm), tissue type (granulation/slough/eschar/bone), infection signs, exudate level, and wound edge undermining — documented at every assessment.

Braden Scale: Validated tool for pressure ulcer risk assessment — 6 domains: sensory perception (1–4), moisture (1–4), activity (1–4), mobility (1–4), nutrition (1–4), friction/shear (1–3). Score ≤18 = at risk; ≤12 = high risk. Guides preventive measures intensity.

diseases.name: Oral Candidiasis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

Scrape Test: Gentle scraping of white plaque with tongue depressor — pseudomembranous candidiasis scrapes off (leaving red base); leukoplakia does NOT scrape off. Simple bedside diagnosis at PHC level.

2. Microscopy (Rapid Confirmation)

KOH Mount (10% KOH) of Scraped Plaque: Scrape plaque onto glass slide, add 10% KOH, heat gently, examine under microscope — pseudohyphae + budding yeasts = positive for Candida. Available at all district hospital labs. Rapid 15-minute test.

Gram Stain of Oral Swab: Gram-positive budding yeasts and pseudohyphae on Gram stain — alternative to KOH at PHC level. Simple, cheap.

3. Culture and Sensitivity

Fungal Culture on Sabouraud Dextrose Agar (SDA): Swab on SDA — Candida colonies grow within 48–72 hours. Species identification by Germ Tube Test and CHROMagar. Mandatory for: recurrent, treatment-refractory, immunosuppressed patients, or HIV-positive patients (where resistant species likely).

Antifungal Susceptibility Testing (AFST) — Broth Microdilution: MIC values for Fluconazole, Amphotericin B, Caspofungin, Voriconazole. Essential in ICU candidemia, treatment failure, or non-albicans species isolated.

4. Mandatory Systemic Investigations (Adults with Unexplained Oral Candidiasis)

HIV 1+2 ELISA / Rapid HIV Test: Mandatory for all adults with oral candidiasis without obvious predisposing cause. HIV counselling and testing (ICTC) available at all government hospitals under NACO.

Fasting Blood Sugar + HbA1c: Uncontrolled DM is the second most common Indian risk factor — must screen at first visit.

CD4 Count (If HIV Positive): Guides prophylaxis and treatment intensity. CD4 <200 = risk of oesophageal extension; CD4 <50 = risk of systemic candidiasis.

5. Oesophageal Extension Investigation

Upper GI Endoscopy (OGD Scope): For dysphagia or odynophagia in HIV patient with oral candidiasis — white plaques on oesophageal mucosa = oesophageal candidiasis. Biopsy confirms invasive disease.

6. Differential Diagnosis

Aphthous Ulcers: Painful punched-out ulcers with yellow slough and red halo — NO white plaque, does NOT scrape. Recurrent. No Candida on microscopy.

Leukoplakia: White patch, cannot scrape — no Candida on KOH. Biopsy essential for pre-malignant/malignant exclusion.

Lichen Planus (Oral): Reticular white lines (Wickham's striae) on buccal mucosa — distinct lacy pattern, does not scrape. Biopsy for confirmation.

7. Indian Guidelines

NACO HIV OI Management Guidelines 2020: Fluconazole 150mg daily for 7–14 days for oral/oesophageal candidiasis in HIV. Antifungal prophylaxis (Fluconazole 150mg weekly) if CD4 <200.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Topical Antifungals (Mild-Moderate Oral Candidiasis)

Clotrimazole 10mg Troches/Lozenges: Dissolve slowly in mouth 5 times daily for 14 days — high local mucosal drug concentration with minimal systemic absorption. First-line for mild pseudomembranous candidiasis. Not commonly available in India; Nystatin is the local alternative.

Nystatin Oral Suspension 100,000 units/ml: 4–6ml four times daily swish and swallow for 7–14 days — fungicidal against Candida in oral cavity. Poorly absorbed systemically — safe for neonates, pregnant patients, and patients on multiple medications.

Miconazole Oral Gel 2%: Apply 2.5ml four times daily directly to oral lesions — good contact action. Available in India. Safe for infants and pregnant women. Useful for denture-associated candidiasis (also apply under denture).

2. Systemic Antifungals (Moderate-Severe, HIV, Refractory)

Fluconazole 150mg on Day 1, then 100mg daily for 7–14 days (oral/oesophageal): First-line systemic antifungal for oral candidiasis in immunosuppressed patients, HIV, and oesophageal extension. Well tolerated. Check for DDI with ARVs (Efavirenz/Nevirapine interaction).

Itraconazole 100mg BD for 7–14 days: Alternative for Fluconazole-refractory candidiasis — useful for non-albicans species. Take with food (solution preferred for oral bioavailability). Significant drug interactions.

Voriconazole 200mg BD (Refractory/Invasive): For Fluconazole-resistant Candida krusei and C. glabrata. Tertiary centre use.

3. Angular Cheilitis

Clotrimazole 1% Cream + Hydrocortisone 1% Cream (Combination): Apply BD to commissures — combined antifungal and anti-inflammatory for Candida+Staph angular cheilitis. Available as Candid-HC or Canesten-HC.

4. Neonatal Oral Thrush

Nystatin Oral Drops 100,000 units/ml: 1ml QID applied with cotton swab to oral lesions in neonates. Safe and effective. Standard protocol under IMNCI.

5. Predisposing Factor Management

Correct Inhaler Technique (Mouth Rinse after ICS use): Rinse mouth thoroughly with water and spit after every use of beclomethasone/fluticasone/budesonide inhaler — prevents oropharyngeal steroid deposition and Candida risk. No drug required — education only.

Denture Hygiene: Remove dentures at night; soak in Chlorhexidine or Nystatin solution; clean before replacement — prevents denture-associated erythematous candidiasis recurrence.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Systemic first-line antifungal for oral/oesophageal candidiasis. PMBJP widely available.

Fluconazole 50mg Tablets (10 tabs): Low-dose daily fluconazole for prophylaxis in HIV patients with CD4 <200.

Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Alternative systemic antifungal for fluconazole-refractory cases.

Clotrimazole 1% Cream 20g: Topical antifungal for angular cheilitis at commissures.

Nystatin Oral Suspension 30ml: Topical oral antifungal for neonatal thrush and mild adult candidiasis.

Miconazole Oral Gel 2% 15g: Apply directly to oral lesions 4x daily.

Chlorhexidine Gluconate 0.2% Mouthwash 100ml: Oral antiseptic — adjunct to reduce secondary bacterial infection in denuded oral mucosa.

Fluconazole 50mg (10 tablets): Rs.28

Miconazole Oral Gel 2% 15g: Rs.52
