diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Irimedadi Taila (Oil Pulling / Kavala Graha): Classical Ayurvedic medicated oil for oral disease (Mukha Roga) — 10ml held in mouth for 10–15 minutes, then spat out. Contains Vidanga, Triphala, and other antimicrobial herbs. Traditional oil pulling reduces oral Candida counts (supported by recent studies). Perform on empty stomach.

Triphala Decoction Gargle (Kavala): Warm Triphala decoction (Amala + Haritaki + Bibhitaki) gargled and held in mouth for 2–3 minutes, twice daily — astringent, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory. Reduces oral plaque biofilm. Widely available as Triphala Churna — boil 1 teaspoon in 200ml water, cool, filter, use as mouth rinse.

Haridra + Honey Oral Application: Fresh turmeric paste (Haridra) mixed with honey — direct application to oral Candida lesions. Curcumin (anti-Candida), honey (osmotic/antimicrobial) — evidence-based combination. Apply with cotton, hold 5 minutes, spit. Twice daily.

Yashtimadhu (Licorice / Glycyrrhiza glabra) Decoction Gargle: Glycyrrhizin demonstrates direct anti-Candida activity. 5g Yashtimadhu in 200ml water boiled, cooled, filtered — used as gargle twice daily. Also soothes mucosal inflammation. Available as tongue / mouth formulations.

Nimba (Neem / Azadirachta indica) Twig/Mouth Wash: Traditional Neem twig use for oral hygiene — contains azadirachtin which has documented anti-Candida properties. Neem leaf decoction rinse twice daily reduces oral Candida colonisation.

Trikatu Churna (Internal): Combination of Ginger + Black Pepper + Long Pepper — used in Kapha-dominant oral conditions causing excess mucus and white coating. 1–2g with honey twice daily. Reduces Kapha which in Ayurvedic concept underlies mucosal overgrowth.

Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder: Directly anti-parasitic and antimicrobial herb — traditional prescription for Mukha Roga (oral disease). 1–2g with warm water twice daily for oral Candida in ayurvedic practice.

Khadirarishta (Oral): Classical Ayurvedic fermentation formulation containing Khadira (Acacia catechu) — astringent, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory for oral and skin conditions. 15–25ml twice daily after meals. Useful for recurrent oral candidiasis.

Dashamoola Kashaya (Low-Immunity Background Cases): Immunostimulatory Ayurvedic decoction of 10 roots — for recurrent oral candidiasis associated with immunocompromised state. 20–30ml twice daily under Vaidya guidance.

Shatavari Powder (Female Patients / HIV-related Candidiasis Recovery): Adaptogen and immunomodulatory herb — supports mucosal immunity. 3–5g with warm milk daily during recovery phase.
diseases.india_specific:
HIV/AIDS: India's 2.4 million HIV population — oral candidiasis is the most common OI in HIV, present in >90% of patients with CD4 count <200. A CD4 count <200 cells/µl predisposes to oesophageal extension. NACO guidelines: ALL adult patients with oral candidiasis must be offered HIV testing.

Uncontrolled Diabetes Mellitus: India's 100 million+ diabetic population — hyperglycaemia creates a glucose-rich mucosal environment perfect for Candida proliferation. Oral candidiasis in non-immunosuppressed adults is a common first sign of undiagnosed or poorly-controlled diabetes. HbA1c mandatory in all adult oral candidiasis.

Inhaled Corticosteroid Use Without Mouth-Rinsing: Millions of Indian asthma patients use inhaled steroids (Beclomethasone, Budesonide, Fluticasone) but are not counselled to rinse mouth after each use — depositing steroid on oral mucosa, causing local immunosuppression and candidal overgrowth. This is entirely preventable with correct inhaler technique.

Oral Cancer Risk Link: Chronic hyperplastic candidiasis (non-scrapable white oral plaques) — particularly in Indian tobacco users (who have one of the world's highest betel nut/khaini/gutka consumption rates) — carries significant risk of oral epithelial dysplasia and malignant transformation. Must biopsy all non-scrapable oral white lesions.

Neonatal Thrush via Delivery: Mother's vaginal Candida colonisation is transmitted to neonate during vaginal delivery — causing neonatal oral thrush within 7–14 days of birth. Very common in India due to undetected maternal vulvovaginal candidiasis. Standard: treat maternal VVC before delivery; treat neonatal thrush promptly.
diseases.important_notes:
Oral Candidiasis in Adults is a Red Flag for HIV — Test Mandatory: Any adult presenting with oral candidiasis without obvious predisposing cause (antibiotics, dentures, steroids) should be offered HIV testing per NACO guidelines. In India, oral candidiasis is frequently the first and only presenting sign of previously undiagnosed HIV. Do not treat and discharge without investigating underlying immunosuppression.

Screen for Uncontrolled Diabetes at Every Presentation: HbA1c and fasting blood sugar must be checked in every adult with oral candidiasis. Recurrent oral candidiasis in a middle-aged or elderly Indian patient is a classic first symptom of uncontrolled Type 2 DM. Treating candidiasis without glycaemic control guarantees recurrence.

Correct Inhaler Technique Prevents Steroid-Associated Oral Candidiasis: Inhaled corticosteroid users must be counselled at every visit: use spacer device, rinse mouth with water and spit AFTER every inhalation. This prevents the most common iatrogenic cause of oropharyngeal candidiasis in asthma/COPD patients without any antifungal needed.

Non-Scrapable White Oral Lesions Must Be Biopsied: If an oral white patch does NOT scrape off with a tongue depressor — do NOT assume candidiasis. In India's high tobacco, betel nut, and khaini-using population, this may be leukoplakia or squamous cell carcinoma. Urgent oral surgery or ENT referral for biopsy is mandatory. Missing oral cancer is a critical diagnostic error.

Treat Mother and Neonate Simultaneously: Neonatal oral thrush and maternal nipple candidiasis are mutually re-infecting — treating one without the other causes prompt recurrence. Both must be treated concurrently: neonatal Nystatin oral drops + maternal topical Clotrimazole cream on nipples.
confirmation_tests_curated: Scrape Test: Gentle scraping of white plaque with tongue depressor — pseudomembranous candidiasis scrapes off (leaving red base); leukoplakia does NOT scrape off. Simple bedside diagnosis at PHC level.

KOH Mount (10% KOH) of Scraped Plaque: Scrape plaque onto glass slide, add 10% KOH, heat gently, examine under microscope — pseudohyphae + budding yeasts = positive for Candida. Available at all district hospital labs. Rapid 15-minute test.
Gram Stain of Oral Swab: Gram-positive budding yeasts and pseudohyphae on Gram stain — alternative to KOH at PHC level. Simple, cheap.

diseases.name: Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (First Presentation)

Symptom Triad: Vulval pruritus + white curdy odourless discharge + vulval erythema/satellite lesions = clinical diagnosis sufficient for first uncomplicated episode in non-pregnant woman. Treat empirically; no microscopy required initially.

2. Microscopy (Recurrent, Atypical, or Refractory Cases)

Vaginal Swab KOH Wet Mount (10%): Most important diagnostic test — vaginal discharge applied to slide with 10% KOH, examined at 40x. Pseudohyphae and budding yeasts = VVC confirmed. 50–70% sensitivity (only moderate; negative does not exclude).

Vaginal pH Testing: pH <4.5 = VVC (confirms Candida, which grows in acidic environment). pH >4.5 = Bacterial Vaginosis or Trichomoniasis. Simple, cheap, rapid test using pH indicator strips.

Amine (Whiff) Test: Add KOH drop to discharge — fishy amine odour = BV or T. vaginalis positive. VVC = NO fishy odour. Simple bedside discriminator.

3. Culture and Sensitivity

HVS (High Vaginal Swab) on Sabouraud Dextrose Agar: Essential for: (a) recurrent VVC (≥4/year), (b) fluconazole treatment failure, (c) severe VVC in HIV/DM. Species identification distinguishes C. albicans (fluconazole-sensitive) from C. glabrata/C. krusei (resistant). Report MIC values.

4. Systemic Investigations (Recurrent VVC)

Fasting Blood Sugar + HbA1c: Mandatory for all women with recurrent VVC to exclude undiagnosed DM.

HIV 1+2 ELISA/Rapid Test: Recurrent severe VVC without obvious risk factors — HIV mandatory.

Serum Ferritin + CBC: Iron deficiency anaemia associated with recurrent VVC.

5. STI Co-Testing

Cervical Swab for Gram Stain + Chlamydia NAAT + Gonorrhoea Culture: VVC can coexist with bacterial STIs — particularly relevant in symptomatic women who also have cervicitis signs (mucopurulent discharge, cervical friability).

Vaginal Swab for Trichomonas vaginalis (Wet Mount/NAAT): T. vaginalis causes frothy green-yellow discharge — co-infection with VVC possible.

6. Indian Guidelines

FOGSI (Federation of Obstetric & Gynaecological Societies of India) VVC Guidelines 2022: Single-dose Fluconazole 150mg for uncomplicated VVC. Intravaginal Clotrimazole 500mg for pregnancy. Fluconazole 150mg weekly x 6 months for recurrent VVC (after induction). HVS culture for recurrent/refractory.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Uncomplicated VVC (First episode, non-pregnant, non-diabetic)

Fluconazole 150mg single oral dose: Single-pill treatment — most convenient, highly effective (85–90% cure rate for C. albicans). First-line for uncomplicated VVC in non-pregnant women. Avoid in first trimester pregnancy.

Clotrimazole 500mg Single Pessary (Intravaginal): Single intravaginal tablet — 85% efficacy. First-line in pregnancy (safe; not systemically absorbed). Alternative for Fluconazole-intolerant patients.

Clotrimazole 1% Cream Intravaginal (7 days): 5g applicator at night for 7 days. For patients who prefer cream formulations. Less convenient than single-dose but equally effective.

Clotrimazole 2% External Cream: Applied to vulva and perianal area twice daily for 7 days — treats external vulval dermatitis component of VVC alongside intravaginal treatment.

2. Severe VVC (extensive erythema, fissuring, immunosuppressed)

Fluconazole 150mg Day 1 + Day 4 (Two doses): Two doses 72 hours apart for severe VVC. Preferred over single dose in severe disease.

Itraconazole 200mg BD for 1 day (oral): Two-dose oral regimen — alternative to Fluconazole.

3. Recurrent VVC (≥4 episodes/year) — Suppressive Therapy

Fluconazole 150mg weekly for 6 months (after induction): Induction — Fluconazole 150mg every 72 hours x 3 doses; then maintenance 150mg weekly for 6 months. Standard suppressive protocol (FOGSI-endorsed).

Boric Acid 600mg Vaginal Capsules: For C. glabrata or Fluconazole-refractory VVC. Intravaginal 600mg capsule daily for 14 days — evidence-based rescue therapy. IMPORTANT: ORAL USE IS FATAL — only intravaginal.

Flucytosine 1g Intravaginal Cream + Amphotericin B 3% Cream: Compounded intravaginal preparation for C. glabrata refractory to boric acid. Specialist compounding pharmacy required.

4. VVC in Pregnancy (Safe Topical Agents Only)

Clotrimazole 100mg Pessary for 7 nights: Safe, effective, and approved for all trimesters. Manual insertion only (avoid applicator in late pregnancy).

Nystatin Intravaginal (100,000 units daily x 14 days): Alternative topical safe in pregnancy — less effective than azoles but acceptable. Available as Candid gel.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Fluconazole 150mg Capsule (1 cap): Single-dose treatment for uncomplicated VVC. PMBJP widely available.

Clotrimazole 500mg Vaginal Pessary (1 tab): Single intravaginal tablet. First-line in pregnancy.

Clotrimazole 1% Cream 20g + Vaginal Applicator: 7-night intravaginal course.

Clotrimazole 2% External Cream 20g: For external vulval application.

Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): For Fluconazole-refractory or recurrent VVC.

Nystatin Vaginal Pessary 100,000 units (6 pessaries): Alternative safe in pregnancy.

Folic Acid 5mg Tablets (10 tabs): For pregnant patients with VVC — standard antenatal supplement.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Fluconazole 150mg (1 capsule): Rs.12

Clotrimazole 500mg Vaginal Pessary (1 tab): Rs.28

Clotrimazole 1% Cream 20g: Rs.30

Clotrimazole 2% External Cream 20g: Rs.28

Itraconazole 100mg (4 capsules): Rs.48

Nystatin Vaginal Pessary 6-pack: Rs.72
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Yoni Prakshalana (Vaginal Wash) with Triphala Kashaya: Classical Ayurvedic Yoni Prakshalana — douching with cooled, filtered Triphala decoction. Triphala's astringent, antimicrobial properties reduce vaginal Candida symptoms and restore acidic environment. Prepare fresh; use at body temperature. NOT more frequently than once daily — over-washing disrupts natural flora.

Neem Leaf Decoction Sitz Bath: Warm Neem leaf decoction sitz bath (pan ghalana / avagaha) for vulval pruritus and erythema — 10 minutes twice daily. Neem's anti-Candida and anti-inflammatory properties reduce vulval symptoms significantly. Traditional gynaecological practice widely used by Indian women.

Khadira (Acacia catechu) Kashaya Yoni Wash: Khadira decoction vaginal wash — strongly astringent (tannins), antifungal, anti-inflammatory. Traditional prescription for Shveta Pradara (white discharge) in Ayurvedic gynaecology. Applied as external vulval wash and gentle introital wash twice daily.

Dashamoolarishta (Internal): Classical fermented Ayurvedic tonic containing 10 roots — anti-inflammatory, immunostimulatory, and balances Vata-Pitta-Kapha imbalances underlying Shveta Pradara. 15–25ml twice daily after meals. Specific to recurrent VVC in Vata-Kapha constitution.

Chandraprabha Vati: Multi-ingredient classical tablet formula — for Mutrakriccha (urinary disorders) and Shveta Pradara — contains Shilajit, Guggulu, and antimicrobial herbs. 2 tablets twice daily. Anti-inflammatory and reduces dysuria.

Shatavari Ghrita or Churna: Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — primary Ayurvedic herb for female reproductive tract — phytoestrogenic, immunomodulatory, and demulcent for vaginal mucosa. 3–5g Shatavari Churna with warm milk twice daily. For recurrent VVC associated with hormonal imbalance.

Haridra (Turmeric) Vaginal Application (Diluted): Turmeric paste (Haridra + coconut oil, 1:4 dilution) external vulval application — anti-Candida, anti-inflammatory for vulval erythema and itch relief. External use only; not for intravaginal application. Warn patient about mild yellow staining.

Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) Decoction Vaginal Wash: Classical Yoni Dhoopana / Prakshalana herb — astringent, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial for Shveta Pradara. 5g in 200ml water, boil, cool, filter, use as external wash.

Probiotic Curd / Dahi Application (External and Oral): Traditional Indian practice of applying cold unsweetened curd (Dahi — contains Lactobacillus cultures) externally to vulva for itch relief. Also consuming plain curd 1–2 times daily replenishes gut Lactobacillus — particularly important during and after antibiotic courses. Supported by limited clinical evidence.

Avipattikar Churna: For VVC associated with constipation, and Pitta-dominant heat symptoms — reduces Pitta imbalance underlying genital inflammation. 3–5g with warm water before bedtime.
diseases.india_specific:
Diabetes Mellitus: India's massive diabetic population — uncontrolled hyperglycaemia creates an ideal vaginal glycogen-rich environment for Candida proliferation. Recurrent VVC is a diagnostic red flag for undiagnosed or poorly controlled DM. HbA1c must be tested in any woman with ≥2 episodes/year.

Antibiotic Usage Pattern in India: India's high antibiotic consumption (often without prescription) disrupts vaginal Lactobacillus-dominant flora, allowing Candida overgrowth. Antibiotic-associated VVC is extremely common. Probiotic (Lactobacillus rhamnosus GR-1) supplementation during antibiotic course significantly reduces VVC incidence.

Synthetic Underwear and Poor Hygiene Practices: Widespread use of synthetic non-breathable underwear in urban India and poor perineal hygiene habits (excessive vaginal washing/douching with soap — which disrupts healthy lactobacilar pH) are significant modifiable risk factors. Cotton underwear, air-drying, and avoidance of vaginal douching are key preventive counselling points.

Non-albicans Candida Resistance: Candida glabrata — increasingly prevalent in Indian gynaecology clinics — is intrinsically resistant to Fluconazole. Women with recurrent treatment-refractory VVC in India increasingly harbor C. glabrata or C. krusei. Culture and species identification are essential for recurrent VVC.

HIV and Immunosuppression: Recurrent, severe, intractable VVC is a sentinel OI in HIV-positive Indian women — often <CD4 200. Investigate immunosuppression in all women with severe/recurrent VVC who lack other risk factors.
diseases.important_notes:
Single-Dose Fluconazole is NOT Safe in First-Trimester Pregnancy: Oral fluconazole (all doses) is avoided in the first trimester due to teratogenicity concerns from cohort data. Topical Clotrimazole vaginal pessary is safe in all trimesters. Counsel all women of reproductive age being prescribed Fluconazole about pregnancy risk and contraception.

Recurrent VVC (≥4 Episodes/Year) Requires Investigation — NOT Repeated Empirical Treatment: The most common clinical error in India is repeatedly treating recurrent VVC empirically with Fluconazole without investigation. Non-albicans Candida resistance, undetected Diabetes, and HIV are the three key underlying causes that must be excluded. HVS culture with species ID, HbA1c, and HIV test are mandatory before starting suppressive therapy.

Treat Partner Only if Symptomatic: Routine male partner treatment does NOT reduce VVC recurrence (evidence-based). Treat male partner ONLY if he has symptomatic penile candidiasis (Candidal balanitis — redness, itch, white patches under foreskin). Avoid unnecessary partner treatment which wastes antifungals and promotes resistance.

Boric Acid Intravaginal is for Refractory Cases Only — Never Oral or Systemic: Boric acid 600mg intravaginal capsule is safe and effective for C. glabrata and refractory VVC. However, ORAL BORIC ACID IS ACUTELY TOXIC (potential fatal). Clear written instructions must specify intravaginal use only. Keep out of reach of children. Not to be used in pregnancy.

Probiotics and Cotton Underwear Prevent Recurrence: The two most effective preventive behavioral interventions for recurrent VVC in India are: (1) Lactobacillus rhamnosus GR-1 probiotic capsule twice daily (particularly during and after antibiotic courses) — restores Lactobacillus-dominant vaginal flora; (2) 100% cotton underwear — reduces periurethral moisture and Candida colonisation environment. These non-pharmacological measures should be an integral part of every VVC management consultation.
confirmation_tests_curated: Symptom Triad: Vulval pruritus + white curdy odourless discharge + vulval erythema/satellite lesions = clinical diagnosis sufficient for first uncomplicated episode in non-pregnant woman. Treat empirically; no microscopy required initially.

Vaginal Swab KOH Wet Mount (10%): Most important diagnostic test — vaginal discharge applied to slide with 10% KOH, examined at 40x. Pseudohyphae and budding yeasts = VVC confirmed. 50–70% sensitivity (only moderate; negative does not exclude).
Vaginal pH Testing: pH <4.5 = VVC (confirms Candida, which grows in acidic environment). pH >4.5 = Bacterial Vaginosis or Trichomoniasis. Simple, cheap, rapid test using pH indicator strips.

diseases.name: Invasive Aspergillosis
diseases.tests:
1. Radiological Investigations

CT Chest (High Resolution) — MOST IMPORTANT: More sensitive than CXR for IPA — detects halo sign, cavitating nodules, air-crescent sign, wedge-shaped infarcts. CT chest is MANDATORY for any febrile neutropenic patient not responding to antibiotics.

Chest X-Ray: Detects pulmonary infiltrates and cavities but has poor sensitivity in early IPA; insensitive during severe neutropaenia (inflammatory response reduced). CT always preferred.

CT Sinuses: For suspected invasive rhinosinusitis — mucosal thickening, bone erosion, orbital/intracranial extension.

MRI Brain: For suspected CNS aspergillosis — haemorrhagic infarcts, ring-enhancing lesions.

2. Biomarkers (Non-Culture Rapid Tests)

Serum Galactomannan (GM) ELISA (Aspergillus Cell Wall Mannan — BioRad Platelia): Sensitivity 70–80% in haematology patients. Optical density index (ODI) >0.5 positive. Twice-weekly monitoring in high-risk neutropenic patients. False positives with Piperacillin-Tazobactam (common in India) — interpret with caution.

BAL (Bronchoalveolar Lavage) Galactomannan: More sensitive than serum GM for pulmonary aspergillosis. ODI >1.0 positive. Requires bronchoscopy.

Serum Beta-D-Glucan (1,3-β-D-Glucan Test): Panfungal marker — positive in Aspergillus AND Candida AND PCP. More sensitive than GM but less specific. Useful in combination.

3. Microbiological Investigations

BAL Culture on Sabouraud Dextrose Agar + Aspergillus-Selective Media: Recovers Aspergillus in 30–50% of IPA. Growth of Aspergillus from BAL in immunosuppressed patient with compatible CT = probable IPA.

Blood Culture: Very low yield for Aspergillus (<5%) — NOT useful for diagnosis but mandatory to exclude concurrent bacteraemia in febrile neutropenic patients.

4. Histopathology (Definitive Diagnosis)

CT-Guided Needle Lung Biopsy / Surgical Biopsy: Tissue histopathology showing septate branching hyphae (45° branching pattern, 3–6µm width) with angioinvasion + positive Aspergillus culture = proven IPA. Required when biomarkers equivocal or other diagnosis (lymphoma, bacterial lung abscess) possible.

Nasal Endoscopy with Biopsy (Invasive Sinusitis): Black eschar biopsy — H&E + GMS stain shows septate hyphae (Aspergillus) vs non-septate (Mucor). Critical for treatment differentiation.

5. Molecular Diagnostics

Aspergillus PCR (Lateral Flow Assay for Serum/BAL): Rapid lateral flow immunochromatographic assay for Aspergillus galactomannan — available in India at major centres. High sensitivity in combination with CT.

6. Classification of Certainty

EORTC/MSG Criteria (Proven/Probable/Possible IPA): Proven = compatible histopathology + positive culture. Probable = host factor + clinical feature (CT sign) + mycological evidence (GM/culture). Possible = host factor + clinical feature without mycological evidence. Drives treatment decisions.

7. Monitoring During Treatment

Serum Galactomannan Twice Weekly: Declining GM = treatment response. Rising GM = failure/resistance — change antifungal.

LFT Weekly (Voriconazole Hepatotoxicity): Azole hepatotoxicity monitoring. Voriconazole visual side effects (photopsia) — ask at each visit.

Voriconazole Plasma Trough Levels: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) — trough 1–5.5µg/ml. Under-dosing = treatment failure; over-dosing = hepatotoxicity and neurotoxicity. Available at AIIMS, CMC Vellore.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Treatment — Voriconazole (IDSA/Indian Guidelines)

Voriconazole 6mg/kg IV loading dose every 12 hours on Day 1, then 4mg/kg every 12 hours IV: Gold standard for IPA — superior to Amphotericin B in clinical trials. IV loading then transition to oral. Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) recommended.

Voriconazole 200mg oral twice daily (after IV stabilisation): Excellent oral bioavailability (96%) — transition from IV to oral Voriconazole at 200mg BD when patient clinically stable. Monitor LFT, visual symptoms.

2. Alternative First-Line / Salvage

Isavuconazole 200mg TID loading for 6 doses then 200mg daily IV/oral: Non-inferior to Voriconazole with better tolerability — fewer visual side effects. Licensed in India for IPA. Standard treatment duration ≥6–12 weeks.

Liposomal Amphotericin B (L-AmB) 3–5mg/kg/day IV: For Voriconazole-refractory or intolerant cases. Less nephrotoxic than conventional AmB. Standard first-line in mucormycosis (NOT Aspergillus) — avoid conventional AmB formulations.

Posaconazole 300mg IV or 300mg oral (tablet) daily after loading: For refractory IPA or prophylaxis in very high-risk patients. Mould-active. IV formulation available in India.

3. Combination Therapy (Refractory IPA)

Voriconazole + Caspofungin 70mg loading then 50mg daily: Combination evidence mixed but used in refractory life-threatening IPA. Caspofungin inhibits beta-glucan synthesis; synergistic mechanism.

4. Prophylaxis (High-Risk Patients)

Posaconazole 300mg oral/IV daily: Standard prophylaxis for AML induction, allogeneic HSCT (IADVL/Indian Haematology guidelines). Reduces IPA incidence by 50% in high-risk haematology patients.

Voriconazole 200mg oral BD: Alternative prophylaxis if Posaconazole not available.

5. Supportive Management

G-CSF (Granulocyte Colony Stimulating Factor) — Filgrastim/Lenograstim: Accelerates neutrophil recovery in IPA setting — adjunct to antifungal therapy. Standard supportive treatment in neutropenic IPA.

Reduction of Immunosuppression (Where Possible): Reducing corticosteroid dose in steroid-induced IPA is critical — must balance against underlying disease activity.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Voriconazole 200mg Tablets (4 tabs): Oral IPA treatment. Available at PMBJP state stores and cancer hospital pharmacies.

Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Alternative azole for chronic pulmonary aspergillosis/aspergilloma management.

Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): For prophylaxis in moderate-risk only (NOT active IPA — Fluconazole lacks mould activity against Aspergillus).

Amphotericin B Injection 50mg (1 vial): Conventional AmB for IPA where Voriconazole unavailable — monitor nephrotoxicity closely.

Caspofungin 50mg Injection (1 vial): Available at select Jan Aushadhi specialty counters and government hospital formularies.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Voriconazole 200mg (4 tablets): Rs.380

Itraconazole 100mg (4 capsules): Rs.48

Amphotericin B 50mg injection (1 vial): Rs.142

Caspofungin 50mg injection (1 vial): Rs.2850
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CRITICAL WARNING — Invasive Aspergillosis is a Life-Threatening Emergency: Invasive Aspergillosis in immunosuppressed patients requires immediate medical treatment with systemic antifungals (Voriconazole/Liposomal AmB) in a hospital setting. There is NO Ayurvedic replacement for antifungal therapy in established invasive aspergillosis. Delay for Ayurvedic treatment is directly life-threatening.

Turmeric (Haridra / Curcumin): Curcumin has demonstrated anti-Aspergillus activity in laboratory studies — inhibits spore germination and mycelial growth. As an adjunctive supportive supplement in recovering, stable patients on antifungals, curcumin supplementation (500mg standardised extract three times daily) may provide modest immune and anti-inflammatory benefit. NOT a substitute for antifungal therapy.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum) Leaf Extract: Anti-fungal and immunostimulatory properties of eugenol and ursolic acid in Tulsi — traditional immunomodulation. During recovery from IPA, Tulsi kadha (decoction) as a supportive immunostimulant. 2–3g dry leaf in 200ml water, boil, filter, sip warm twice daily.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Standardised Extract: Adaptogenic and immunostimulatory — supports immune recovery during and after prolonged immunosuppression in post-transplant and oncology patients. 300–600mg extract twice daily during late recovery phase. NOT during acute active aspergillosis.

Chyawanprash: Amla-based comprehensive immune tonic — as nutritional and immunostimulatory support AFTER clinical stabilisation and discharge from hospital. Addresses underlying immune deficit in outpatient setting.

Trikatu (Ginger + Pippali + Maricha): Kaphahara herbal combination — traditional for chronic respiratory Kapha manifestations including productive cough, sputum retention. Supportive adjunct for chronic pulmonary aspergillosis (aspergilloma) in stable patients. 2g with honey twice daily.

Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica) Leaf Decoction: Bronchodilatory, expectorant, and antimicrobial — traditional treatment for haemoptysis and pulmonary infections. Useful supportive adjunct for haemoptysis in post-TB aspergilloma patients. 30ml twice daily.
diseases.india_specific:
Haematological Malignancies and Bone Marrow Transplants: AML induction chemotherapy (profound neutropaenia), allogeneic HSCT, and haematological transplants are the highest-risk scenarios. India's growing oncology centres report increasing IPA incidence in AML/ALL patients during induction. Prophylactic Posaconazole is now standard in high-risk centres.

Post-COVID Immunosuppression (CAPA): India had uniquely high rates of COVID-Associated Pulmonary Aspergillosis during 2021 wave. Critically ill COVID patients on dexamethasone, mechanically ventilated — extremely high CAPA risk. Any ICU COVID patient with non-responding fever + new pulmonary infiltrates should have BAL Galactomannan and CT chest performed.

Prolonged Corticosteroid Use: India's very high corticosteroid prescription rate — for conditions including TB paradoxical reaction, autoimmune disease, nephrotic syndrome, SLE, and chronic COPD — creates aspergillosis risk even without haematological malignancy. Aspergillus can infect patients on moderate-high steroid doses for >14 days.

Chronic Pulmonary Aspergillosis (CPA) on TB Background: India has 26% of the world's tuberculosis burden — healed TB cavities are the perfect growth medium for Aspergillus fumigatus colonisation, leading to the chronic pulmonary aspergillosis (CPA) spectrum: aspergilloma, chronic cavitary PA, subacute invasive PA. SA (simple aspergilloma) = Aspergillus ball in pre-existing TB cavity — haemoptysis in apparently otherwise healthy individual, often years post-TB treatment.

Environmental Spore Exposure: India's construction boom, agricultural dust, and monsoon humidity create extremely high Aspergillus fumigatus spore loads in air — immunosuppressed patients in Indian hospitals must be managed in HEPA-filtered rooms whenever possible. Hospital construction near haematology/transplant wards is a documented IPA outbreak risk.
diseases.important_notes:
Febrile Neutropaenic Patient Not Responding to Antibiotics in 72–96 Hours Requires Immediate Fungal Investigation: The critical rule in haematology and oncology wards: persistent or recurrent fever in neutropenic patient after 72–96 hours of adequate antibiotics = fungal infection until proven otherwise. CT chest and serum Galactomannan must be performed SAME DAY the decision is made. Empirical antifungal therapy (Voriconazole or Liposomal AmB) should be started without waiting for culture confirmation when clinical and CT evidence is consistent with IPA.

Voriconazole Requires Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM) in Indian Patients: Indian patients — particularly those with CYP2C19 genetic polymorphisms (highly prevalent in South Asian populations) — are at high risk of both sub-therapeutic and toxic Voriconazole levels. Poor metabolisers achieve very high plasma levels (neurotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, visual hallucinations); ultra-rapid metabolisers achieve sub-therapeutic levels (treatment failure). TDM at Day 5 of therapy is recommended at all Indian tertiary centres.

Aspergilloma in Post-TB Cavity Does NOT Require Voriconazole: Chronic stable aspergilloma (simple aspergillus ball in TB cavity) in an otherwise immunocompetent patient is NOT invasive aspergillosis — it does not require antifungal therapy in most cases. Management is: (1) observation if asymptomatic, (2) Itraconazole for symptomatic/progressive chronic cavitary aspergillosis, (3) surgical resection for severe or life-threatening haemoptysis. This distinction is critical — Voriconazole therapy reserved for invasive/subacute IPA, not simple aspergilloma.

HEPA Filtration in Haematology Wards is Patient Safety Infrastructure: Indian hospitals performing AML chemotherapy and bone marrow transplants must have HEPA-filtered positive-pressure rooms for neutropenic patients. Building construction near haematology/BMT wards must trigger enhanced air quality monitoring and antifungal prophylaxis upgrading. This is a preventable ICU mortality — requiring hospital administration engagement, not just clinical management.]
confirmation_tests_curated: CT Chest (High Resolution) — MOST IMPORTANT: More sensitive than CXR for IPA — detects halo sign, cavitating nodules, air-crescent sign, wedge-shaped infarcts. CT chest is MANDATORY for any febrile neutropenic patient not responding to antibiotics.
Chest X-Ray: Detects pulmonary infiltrates and cavities but has poor sensitivity in early IPA; insensitive during severe neutropaenia (inflammatory response reduced). CT always preferred.
CT Sinuses: For suspected invasive rhinosinusitis — mucosal thickening, bone erosion, orbital/intracranial extension.
MRI Brain: For suspected CNS aspergillosis — haemorrhagic infarcts, ring-enhancing lesions.

diseases.name: Cryptococcal Meningitis
diseases.tests:
1. Lumbar Puncture and CSF Analysis (Mandatory — Diagnostic and Therapeutic)

CSF Opening Pressure Measurement: Most critical first measurement at LP — record pressure in mmH2O. Normal <200; CM typically 250–400+. Therapeutic LP (drain to <200mmH2O or remove 20–30ml CSF) performed at same sitting if pressure elevated.

CSF India Ink Preparation: Drop CSF on slide, add ink, coverslip — encapsulated yeast cells appear as clear halos against dark background under microscope. Sensitivity 60–80% in HIV-related CM. Simple, available at all district hospital labs.

CSF Cryptococcal Antigen (CrAg) Latex Agglutination or LFA: Sensitivity 99%, specificity 97% — the gold standard CSF test for cryptococcal meningitis. Titre also guides treatment response (declining titre = response). Lateral flow assay (LFA) versions available cheaply and at POC level.

CSF Cell Count, Protein, Glucose: Classic CM picture — lymphocytic pleocytosis (typically <50 cells; sometimes near-normal in severe immunosuppression), elevated protein (50–150mg/dl), low glucose. Markedly elevated protein and near-normal cells = poor immune response = poor prognosis.

CSF Fungal Culture on SDA: Gold standard for definitive diagnosis and antifungal susceptibility. Cryptococcus grows as mucoid white creamy colonies in 3–5 days. Determines azole MIC for fluconazole resistance testing.

2. Blood Tests

Serum/Blood CrAg LFA: Point-of-care finger-prick test — 99% sensitivity for CM in HIV patients. NACO mandates serum CrAg testing in all HIV patients with CD4 <100. Positive serum CrAg in symptomatic patient = proceed to LP immediately.

Blood Fungal Culture (2 sets): Separate from bacteraemia screen — in disseminated cryptococcosis blood cultures positive in 50–70%.

HIV Rapid Test + CD4 Count: Mandatory in all CM — guides prognosis and timing of ART initiation.

3. Neuroimaging

CT Brain (Non-Contrast, Before LP if Papilloedema/Focal Neurology): To exclude mass lesion and prevent tonsillar herniation risk at LP. However: in the absence of focal deficits or papilloedema, CT is NOT required before LP — do not delay LP for CT scan in uncomplicated CM.

MRI Brain with Gadolinium: Cryptococcomas (focal brain lesions), dilated Virchow-Robin spaces ('soap bubble' lesions pathognomonic), leptomeningeal enhancement.

4. Intraocular Pressure and Fundoscopy

Ophthalmoscopy (Fundus Examination): Papilloedema, retinal haemorrhages, cranial nerve palsy assessment. Daily fundoscopy during first week of treatment. Permanent visual loss from optic neuropathy occurs in up to 15% of CM survivors without ICP management.

5. Indian Monitoring Protocol

Daily Therapeutic LP (First 2 Weeks): Core of CM management — daily LP to maintain CSF pressure <200mmH2O. Documented to dramatically reduce ICP-related mortality and blindness in Indian studies (ACTA trial).
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Induction Phase (Weeks 1–2) — FUNGICIDAL

Liposomal Amphotericin B (L-AmB) 3–4mg/kg/day IV for 14 days: WHO-preferred induction therapy for cryptococcal meningitis (superior to conventional AmB — less nephrotoxicity, equivalent efficacy). EXPENSIVE in India — available through government HIV programme procurement at major ART centres.

Amphotericin B Deoxycholate (Conventional AmB) 0.7–1mg/kg/day IV for 14 days: Less expensive alternative when L-AmB unavailable. Monitor renal function daily, potassium/magnesium supplementation mandatory. Pre-hydrate with 500ml NS before each dose. Daily creatinine monitoring.

Flucytosine (5-FC) 100mg/kg/day oral in 4 divided doses for 14 days: Given IN COMBINATION with AmB (AmB+5FC combination = most effective induction; superior to AmB alone in ACTA trial). Unfortunately 5-FC is not available in India at most centres — its absence significantly impacts outcomes.

Fluconazole 800mg/day oral: Alternative induction if AmB unavailable or intolerable — less effective but lifeboat option. Standard NACO fallback in resource-limited settings.

2. Consolidation Phase (Weeks 3–10)

Fluconazole 400mg/day oral for 8 weeks: After 2-week AmB-based induction — consolidation therapy. Oral outpatient treatment. Ensure CSF culture negative before switching.

3. Maintenance (Secondary Prophylaxis) — 12 Months Minimum

Fluconazole 200mg/day oral: Long-term secondary prophylaxis to prevent relapse. Continue until CD4 >200 for ≥3 months on ART. NACO provides free Fluconazole at ART centres.

4. ICP Management (CRITICAL — Prevents Blindness and Death)

Therapeutic Lumbar Puncture (Daily CSF Drainage): Remove 20–30ml CSF or drain until opening pressure halved — performed DAILY for the first 2 weeks or until ICP normalised. This intervention is as life-saving as antifungal therapy. Does NOT require acetazolamide or dexamethasone (both ineffective/harmful in CM).

Avoid Corticosteroids: Dexamethasone does NOT benefit CM and increases mortality in clinical trials — CONTRAINDICATED in cryptococcal meningitis (unlike bacterial meningitis).

5. ART Timing

Defer ART for 4–6 Weeks After CM Diagnosis: Starting ART immediately causes IRIS — which can be fatal. NACO 2020 guidelines: defer ART 4–6 weeks in patients with confirmed CM. Reduces IRIS-related mortality significantly.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Fluconazole 200mg Tablets (10 tabs): Secondary prophylaxis and consolidation phase. PMBJP available, free at ART centres.

Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (6 caps): Alternative pack size for consolidation (use two 150mg = 300mg for consolidation step-down).

Amphotericin B Injection 50mg (1 vial): Government HIV programme procurement — induction phase.

Potassium Chloride Injection 15% (10ml amp): Mandatory electrolyte replacement during AmB therapy (AmB causes renal potassium wasting).

Magnesium Sulphate Injection: AmB-induced hypomagnesaemia management.

Normal Saline 500ml (pre-hydration): Pre-infusion hydration before every AmB dose to reduce nephrotoxicity.

Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Fever and headache symptom relief.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Fluconazole 200mg (10 tablets): Rs.56

Amphotericin B 50mg injection (1 vial): Rs.142

Potassium Chloride 15% injection 10ml: Rs.18

Normal Saline 500ml: Rs.42

Paracetamol 500mg (10 tablets): Rs.4
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ABSOLUTE WARNING — Cryptococcal Meningitis is a Neurological Emergency: Cryptococcal meningitis has 30–70% in-hospital mortality in India without prompt antifungal therapy and ICP management. There is NO Ayurvedic treatment that can substitute for systemic Amphotericin B and daily therapeutic lumbar punctures. Any delay in medical treatment for Ayurvedic therapy in acute cryptococcal meningitis is likely fatal. The following supportive measures apply ONLY in the recovery/rehabilitation phase.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) Capsules — Recovery Phase: Neuroprotective and cognitive-enhancing adaptogen — supports neurological recovery in post-CM survivors with residual cognitive deficits, memory impairment, and attention difficulties. 300mg standardised extract twice daily. Clinical trial evidence for cognitive enhancement. Under neurologist/Vaidya guidance post-discharge.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — Recovery Phase: Adaptogenic and neuro-restorative — supports recovery from chronic neurological illness. 600mg standardised root extract daily. Also helps post-CM fatigue, immune restoration, and weight recovery.

Saraswatarishta: Classical Ayurvedic fermented tonic for neurological conditions (Unmada, Apasmara) — contains Brahmi, Shatavari, and multiple nerve-nourishing herbs. 15–25ml twice daily after food during outpatient recovery phase. For cognitive sequelae post-CM.

Chyawanprash: Comprehensive immune tonic — Amla-based antioxidant and immunomodulatory formula. Critical for immune restoration in HIV-positive post-CM patients on ART. 2 teaspoons daily.

Triphala Churna (Oral): Post-CM constipation management (common with prolonged AmB/Fluconazole and hospitalisation). 5g with warm water at bedtime promotes bowel regularity without gut flora disruption.

Warm Sesame Oil Scalp and Foot Massage (Abhyanga): Vata-pacifying practice for post-CM altered sensorium, headache, and neuropathy. Daily warm sesame oil massage promotes relaxation, reduces residual post-CM neuropathic symptoms, and supports general convalescence in ayurvedic recovery protocols.
diseases.india_specific:
HIV-Associated Cryptococcal Meningitis: CD4 <100 cells/µl is the dominant risk factor. India's HIV burden — particularly in high-prevalence states (Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Manipur, Nagaland) — means cryptococcal meningitis is a significant cause of preventable hospital mortality in HIV patients. NACO's CrAg LFA rollout across ICTC/ART centres is saving lives.

ART Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS): Paradoxical worsening of cryptococcal meningitis 2–8 weeks after ART initiation — due to recovering immune system's inflammatory response to residual antigen. Cryptococcal IRIS causes severe headache, fever, meningismus, and worsening CSF. WHO/NACO: delay ART for 4–6 weeks after stabilisation of cryptococcal meningitis to reduce IRIS risk.

Immunosuppressive Therapy in Non-HIV Patients: Transplant recipients (India's growing transplant programmes), SLE patients on high-dose steroids, rheumatoid arthritis patients on TNF-inhibitors, and lymphoma patients are at increasing risk. Cryptococcal meningitis in a non-HIV patient requires investigation of underlying immunosuppression.

Environmental Exposure — Pigeon Droppings: Cryptococcus neoformans exists in dried pigeon excreta — highly concentrated in Indian urban environments (temples, parks, government buildings with pigeon habitation). Inhaled route via lung → haematogenous spread to CNS. Pigeon-keeping and temple attendance are documented exposures.

Lumbar Puncture Monitoring Capacity: India's district hospitals frequently lack the capacity for daily therapeutic lumbar punctures needed for ICP management in cryptococcal meningitis. This structural gap severely limits optimal management at secondary care level.
diseases.important_notes:
CrAg Lateral Flow Assay Before ART is Life-Saving Population-Level Intervention: NACO 2020 mandates Cryptococcal Antigen (CrAg) LFA testing in ALL HIV-positive patients with CD4 <100 cells/µl BEFORE starting ART. This cheap, simple blood test (Rs.200–500) detects asymptomatic antigenemia — patients treated with Fluconazole preemptively do NOT develop cryptococcal meningitis. This single algorithm change at ART centres prevents 50,000+ deaths per year in high-burden settings. Ensure all ART centres follow NACO CrAg algorithm.

Dexamethasone is CONTRAINDICATED — Unlike Bacterial Meningitis: The most dangerous prescribing error in cryptococcal meningitis management is administering dexamethasone to 'reduce brain swelling'. Dexamethasone INCREASES mortality in cryptococcal meningitis (opposite to bacterial meningitis) by further suppressing the already inadequate immune response. Never use corticosteroids in CM unless specifically for cryptococcal IRIS. Daily therapeutic LP — not steroids — is the correct ICP management.

Delay ART 4–6 Weeks to Prevent Fatal IRIS: Starting ART immediately after CM diagnosis causes Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome — which can cause fatal CNS inflammation in the already inflamed meninges. NACO guidelines clearly state: defer ART for 4–6 weeks post-CM stabilisation, not at presentation. This is counterintuitive to many physicians used to same-day ART initiation — but adherence to this guideline reduces IRIS mortality.

India Ink and CrAg LFA are Available at District Level — Use Them: CSF India ink preparation is a simple microscopy skill available at every district hospital laboratory. CrAg LFA test kits (MPT64 or IMMY) are available at ART centres under NACO procurement. Both tests together provide near-100% diagnostic sensitivity for CM at district hospital level — no sophisticated equipment needed. The diagnosis should never be missed due to 'lack of equipment' at secondary care level.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Lumbar Puncture and CSF Analysis (Mandatory — Diagnostic and Therapeutic)

CSF Opening Pressure Measurement: Most critical first measurement at LP — record pressure in mmH2O. Normal <200; CM typically 250–400+. Therapeutic LP (drain to <200mmH2O or remove 20–30ml CSF) performed at same sitting if pressure elevated.

CSF India Ink Preparation: Drop CSF on slide, add ink, coverslip — encapsulated yeast cells appear as clear halos against dark background under microscope. Sensitivity 60–80% in HIV-related CM. Simple, available at all district hospital labs.

CSF Cryptococcal Antigen (CrAg) Latex Agglutination or LFA: Sensitivity 99%, specificity 97% — the gold standard CSF test for cryptococcal meningitis. Titre also guides treatment response (declining titre = response). Lateral flow assay (LFA) versions available cheaply and at POC level.

CSF Cell Count, Protein, Glucose: Classic CM picture — lymphocytic pleocytosis (typically <50 cells; sometimes near-normal in severe immunosuppression), elevated protein (50–150mg/dl), low glucose. Markedly elevated protein and near-normal cells = poor immune response = poor prognosis.

diseases.name: Onychomycosis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Diagnosis (Preliminary)

Clinical Algorithm (SCIO Score): Subungual hyperkeratosis + onycholysis + nail discolouration. Clinical diagnosis acceptable for treatment initiation in uncomplicated toenail onychomycosis — but laboratory confirmation recommended before systemic antifungal treatment.

2. KOH Preparation (Nail Clipping or Subungual Scraping)

KOH 20–30% Nail Clipping Mount: Nail clipping ground or scraped, dissolved in 20–30% KOH, heated gently, examined at 40x. Septate branching hyphae = dermatophyte. Budding yeasts = Candida. 80% sensitivity when adequate specimen taken. MUST include subungual debris (most fungal material here), not just nail plate.

KOH with Fluorescent Blankophor/Calcofluor White Stain: Dramatically improves sensitivity of KOH over plain mount — fluorescent chitin staining of fungal cell walls. Requires fluorescence microscope.

3. Nail Fungal Culture (Definitive — Species Identification)

Nail Culture on SDA + Dermasel Agar at 28°C: Grows dermatophytes in 2–3 weeks; moulds in 5–7 days. Species identification (T. rubrum vs T. interdigitale vs Candida vs NDM) guides appropriate antifungal choice. Mandatory for: treatment failure, atypical presentation, immunosuppression, Terbinafine-resistant cases.

Antifungal Susceptibility Testing (AFST): For T. rubrum with prior Terbinafine failure — Terbinafine resistance (MIC >4µg/ml) increasingly described. Voriconazole/Itraconazole may be required.

4. Histopathology (Atypical/Certainty)

PAS-Stained Nail Section (Nail Plate and Bed Biopsy): For definitive diagnosis when KOH negative but clinical suspicion high. Periodic Acid-Schiff stain highlights fungal elements in nail keratin. 90%+ sensitivity.

5. Dermoscopy of Nail (Videodermoscopy)

Nail Plate Dermoscopy: Spike pattern (flame-shaped discolouration), aurora borealis pattern (longitudinal striae) — supportive dermoscopic signs of onychomycosis. Helps distinguish from psoriatic nail, traumatic onychodystrophy.

6. Differential Diagnosis

Psoriatic Nail Disease: Pitting, oil-drop sign, subungual hyperkeratosis, onycholysis — very similar to onychomycosis. KOH negative. Family history of psoriasis. Systemic psoriasis features.

Traumatic Onychodystrophy: Nail thickening and discolouration from chronic footwear pressure — no fungal elements. Occupational history (tight shoes, sport).

7. Indian Clinical Guidelines

IADVL Tinea Unguium Guidelines 2023: KOH + nail culture before systemic antifungal. Terbinafine 250mg daily toenails x 12 weeks (fingernails x 6 weeks) as first-line. Itraconazole pulse for Candida onychomycosis.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Systemic Antifungals (Standard of Care for Onychomycosis — Topical Alone Insufficient)

Terbinafine 250mg once daily for 12 weeks (toenails) / 6 weeks (fingernails): First-line oral antifungal for dermatophyte onychomycosis — highest clinical and mycological cure rates (60–80%). Fungicidal; deposits in nail keratin. Monitor LFT at baseline and 6 weeks. Avoid in liver disease.

Itraconazole 200mg twice daily for 1 week per month (Pulse Therapy) — 3 pulses toenails, 2 pulses fingernails: Standard pulse regimen — cost-effective, fewer side effects than continuous. Effective for Candida and dermatophyte onychomycosis. Take with fatty meal (highest absorption). Monitor LFT.

Itraconazole 200mg once daily for 12 weeks (Continuous): Alternative to pulse therapy — higher total dose but better for extensive NDM or refractory toenail disease.

Fluconazole 150–300mg once weekly for 9–12 months (fingernails), 12–18 months (toenails): Weekly oral Fluconazole for patients intolerant to Terbinafine/Itraconazole or with Candida onychomycosis. Longer duration = lower cost per dose but longer treatment course.

2. Topical Antifungals (Adjunctive — Limited Efficacy Alone for Nail Disease)

Ciclopirox 8% Nail Lacquer (daily for 48 weeks): Only monotherapy-indicated topical for mild DLSO without matrix involvement. Apply daily; file nail weekly. 30% mycological cure — less effective than oral but suitable when oral agents contraindicated (pregnancy, liver disease).

Amorolfine 5% Nail Lacquer: Once or twice weekly — better nail penetration than Ciclopirox. Used as adjunct to oral therapy for faster response.

Clotrimazole 1% Cream (Periungual): For Candida perionychia (paronychia) — apply to nail fold BD. NOT effective for nail plate invasion.

3. Adjunctive Measures

Nail Avulsion (Chemical/Surgical): 40–50% urea cream under occlusive dressing for 7–14 days (chemical avulsion) or surgical nail avulsion — used for very thick nails where antifungal penetration is inadequate. Avulsion followed by topical/oral antifungal.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Terbinafine 250mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line oral dermatophyte onychomycosis treatment. PMBJP widely available.

Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Pulse therapy or continuous for Candida/mixed onychomycosis.

Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Weekly oral pulse for Candida onychomycosis.

Clotrimazole 1% Cream 20g: Periungual Candida paronychia.

Urea 40% Cream 25g: Chemical nail avulsion preparation — nail softening before mechanical debridement.

Ibuprofen 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Analgesic for nail pain during debridement or ingrown nail complication.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Terbinafine 250mg (10 tablets): Rs.48

Itraconazole 100mg (4 capsules): Rs.48

Fluconazole 150mg (1 capsule): Rs.12

Clotrimazole 1% Cream 20g: Rs.30

Urea 40% Cream 25g: Rs.62

Ibuprofen 400mg (10 tablets): Rs.8
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Tea Tree Oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) 100% Pure — Topical: Evidence-based natural antifungal for onychomycosis nail application — terpinen-4-ol inhibits dermatophyte growth. Apply undiluted (small area, nail) with cotton bud to infected nail twice daily. RCTs show comparable to Clotrimazole for mild onychomycosis. Widely available at Indian pharmacies. Safe and affordable.

Neem Oil (Cold Pressed) Nail Application: Anti-dermatophyte properties of nimbidin and azadirachtin. Apply Neem oil with cotton directly to infected nail twice daily — traditional and increasingly evidence-supported antifungal for mild-moderate toenail onychomycosis.

Coconut Oil (Caprylic Acid) Application: Medium chain triglycerides (caprylic, capric acid) in virgin coconut oil have antifungal membrane-disrupting action. Apply warm VCO to infected nails and nail folds twice daily — antimicrobial and also prevents dry nail cracking.

Garlic (Allium sativum) Juice / Paste Application: Allicin (organosulfur compound) — potent proven anti-fungal. Crushed fresh garlic juice applied directly to nail — or garlic paste under occlusion for 30 minutes. Pungent; caution for sensitive skin. Traditional and pharmacologically supported.

Haridra (Turmeric) Curcumin Paste: Curcumin anti-Candida and anti-dermatophyte in laboratory studies. Fresh turmeric paste or curcumin cream applied to nail and periungual skin twice daily. Mild yellow discolouration of skin — cosmetically limiting.

Triphala Kashaya Foot Soak: Warm Triphala decoction foot soak (20 minutes daily) — astringent, antimicrobial, antifungal. Traditional Ayurvedic foot care. Particularly beneficial for comorbid tinea pedis associated with toe-web onychomycosis.

Panchvalkal Kashaya (External Wash): Classical five-bark decoction — each bark has significant astringent and antimicrobial properties. Foot soak and nail wash — traditional for recurrent dermatophytosis and onychomycosis.

Marichadi Taila (Nail Application): Maricha (Black Pepper), Haritaki, sesame oil base — Ayurvedic medicated oil with anti-Kapha (anti-fungal, anti-inflammatory) action for Krimija nail diseases (fungal nail infections). Apply twice daily to infected nails.

Nimba + Haridra Churna Oral: Neem and turmeric powder oral combination — 2g of each with warm water twice daily. Systemic antimicrobial and blood-purifying support for chronic recurrent onychomycosis.
diseases.india_specific:
Tinea Pedis as Initiating Factor: India's warm, humid climate, use of shared bathrooms, open sandal wear in public spaces (temples, community gyms, public showers), and barefoot walking creates extremely high tinea pedis transmission. Dermatophytes from interdigital tinea pedis subsequently invade toenails → onychomycosis.

Diabetes Mellitus: India's diabetic population has dramatically higher onychomycosis prevalence (up to 50% vs 15–20% in non-diabetics). Diabetic onychomycosis breaks down the nail barrier → secondary bacterial paronychia, subungual abscess, diabetic foot infection, and osteomyelitis. Diabetic nail care is as important as diabetic foot skin care.

Tinea Incognito and Topical Steroid Misuse: Indian over-the-counter topical steroid-antifungal combination creams (Clotrimazole + Betamethasone) are extensively misused — applied to nail and periungual skin for months. Topical steroids applied to nails are completely ineffective against onychomycosis and induce immunosuppression of periungual skin, worsening the condition.

Non-Dermatophyte Moulds in India: Scopulariopsis brevicaulis, Fusarium spp., and Aspergillus spp. — non-dermatophyte mould onychomycosis — are increasingly prevalent in India, particularly in immunocompromised patients. NDM onychomycosis does NOT respond to Terbinafine (the standard dermatophyte treatment) — requires Itraconazole or Voriconazole. Culture is essential for atypical/refractory cases.

Pedicure and Nail Salon Transmission: Proliferation of nail salons in Indian urban areas — use of unsterilised nail equipment (files, clippers, cuticle pushers) causes instrumental onychomycosis transmission. Awareness required.
diseases.important_notes:
ALWAYS CONFIRM DIAGNOSIS MYCOLOGICALLY BEFORE STARTING ORAL ANTIFUNGAL: This is the most important principle in onychomycosis management and the most violated in India. Studies show that 40–50% of clinically diagnosed onychomycosis cases are NOT fungal — they are nail psoriasis, lichen planus unguis, traumatic onycholysis, or other mimics. Starting 3–12 months of Terbinafine or Itraconazole (with hepatotoxicity risk and drug interactions) for a non-fungal nail disorder is medically unjustifiable. Minimum standard before oral antifungal prescription: KOH examination of nail clipping from the subungual debris. In difficult cases: PAS stain of nail biopsy. Culture adds species identification and guides treatment choice between Terbinafine (dermatophytes) and Itraconazole (moulds, Candida).

TREAT TINEA PEDIS SIMULTANEOUSLY — CURE WITHOUT IT IS TEMPORARY: The foot acts as a continuous Dermatophyte reservoir. Toenail onychomycosis almost always coexists with tinea pedis (athlete's foot) — the same T. rubrum infection between the toes that seeded the nail originally. Treating the nail without simultaneously treating tinea pedis leads to rapid reinfection after expensive nail treatment concludes. Protocol: always examine the toe web spaces; apply Clotrimazole, Terbinafine, or Luliconazole topical cream to toe web spaces daily for 4–6 weeks concurrently with nail treatment. Advise: change socks daily, use antifungal foot powder, avoid communal bathing without protective footwear.

ONYCHOMYCOSIS IN DIABETICS IS A MEDICAL PRIORITY, NOT COSMETIC: Diabetic patients with onychomycosis face a qualitatively different risk: thickened, dystrophic fungal nails in a foot with peripheral neuropathy and vascular disease create a perfect environment for secondary bacterial infection, nail bed ulcer, and diabetic foot. The WHO diabetic foot guidelines and IDF (International Diabetes Federation) recommend active treatment of onychomycosis in all diabetics as part of diabetic foot care. In Indian clinical practice, diabetic foot physician and dermatologist coordination for nail disease is inadequate. Key action: screen all diabetic patients for onychomycosis at annual foot exam; treat aggressively; ensure Terbinafine LFT monitoring (Terbinafine + some diabetic drugs: check interactions); Itraconazole is safe alternative.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical Algorithm (SCIO Score): Subungual hyperkeratosis + onycholysis + nail discolouration. Clinical diagnosis acceptable for treatment initiation in uncomplicated toenail onychomycosis — but laboratory confirmation recommended before systemic antifungal treatment.
2. KOH Preparation (Nail Clipping or Subungual Scraping)

KOH 20–30% Nail Clipping Mount: Nail clipping ground or scraped, dissolved in 20–30% KOH, heated gently, examined at 40x. Septate branching hyphae = dermatophyte. Budding yeasts = Candida. 80% sensitivity when adequate specimen taken. MUST include subungual debris (most fungal material here), not just nail plate.
KOH with Fluorescent Blankophor/Calcofluor White Stain: Dramatically improves sensitivity of KOH over plain mount — fluorescent chitin staining of fungal cell walls. Requires fluorescence microscope.

diseases.name: Tinea Capitis
diseases.tests:
1. Clinical Examination

Scalp Hair Examination: Pattern recognition — grey patch (ectothrix), black dot (endothrix), kerion (inflammatory). Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy + scalp scaling + patchy alopecia = tinea capitis until proven otherwise in a child.

2. Wood's Lamp (UV Fluorescence)

Wood's Lamp Examination in Dark Room: Ectothrix Microsporum species (M. canis, M. audouinii) fluoresce bright green. However — T. violaceum and T. tonsurans (most common Indian species) do NOT fluoresce — Wood's lamp is unreliable in India as a standalone test. Use for screening but always proceed to microscopy.

3. Microscopy and Culture (Definitive)

Hair KOH Mount (Broken Hairs + Scale Scraping): Pull affected hairs with forceps, mount on slide with 10–20% KOH. Endothrix = arthroconidia INSIDE hair shaft. Ectothrix = spore chains OUTSIDE hair shaft. Microscopy in 15 minutes — rapid bedside test at dermatology clinic.

DTM (Dermatophyte Test Medium) or SDA Fungal Culture of Broken Hairs: Inoculate broken hairs and scale; incubate at 28°C for 2–4 weeks. Species identification by colony morphology and microscopy. T. violaceum grows slowly, pigmented colonies. DTM changes colour red = dermatophyte growth indicator.

4. Additional Investigations

CBC (in Kerion): Neutrophilia + elevated ESR during kerion. Distinguish from furuncle/scalp abscess requiring surgical drainage vs antifungal-alone kerion.

Serum Ferritin: Iron deficiency in children with tinea capitis — treat if <20ng/ml to improve immune response.

5. Differential Diagnosis

Alopecia Areata: Smooth non-scaly patches, no broken hairs; negative KOH; dermoscopy shows yellow dots + exclamation mark hairs.

Head-Lice (Pediculosis Capitis): Pruritic scalp, nits (white oval eggs) anchored to hair shafts, adult lice visible. No scaling or broken hairs. KOH negative.

Seborrhoeic Dermatitis: Greasy yellowish scale on scalp without alopecia; no cervical lymphadenopathy; KOH negative. Responds to antidandruff treatment, not antifungals.

Trichotillomania: Bizarre shaped patches; hairs of varying length; geometric pattern suggesting pulling; otherwise normal scalp; KOH negative.

Psoriasis of Scalp: Silvery scales, scalp thickening, no alopecia; may extend to the nape; family history; no lymphadenopathy.

6. Indian Guidelines

IADVL Paediatric Dermatophytosis Guidelines 2023: Terbinafine (preferred for T. violaceum/tonsurans) OR Griseofulvin (for Microsporum) for 6–8 weeks. Selenium sulphide or Ketoconazole shampoo adjunct. Treat household contacts.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Systemic Oral Antifungals (Mandatory — Topical Alone Is Insufficient for Scalp)

Terbinafine 3–6mg/kg/day oral for 6 weeks: Preferred for endothrix T. violaceum and T. tonsurans (most common in India). Weight-based dosing in children: <20kg = 62.5mg; 20–40kg = 125mg; >40kg = 250mg daily. Fungicidal; concentrates in hair keratin.

Griseofulvin Microsize 20–25mg/kg/day oral for 8–12 weeks: Older but effective for Microsporum species. Take with fatty meal (highest absorption). Still widely used in India due to availability and cost. Lower effectiveness for T. violaceum — use Terbinafine instead.

Itraconazole 3–5mg/kg/day oral for 4–6 weeks: Alternative for Griseofulvin/Terbinafine-refractory tinea capitis. Weight-based; available as oral solution for children. Take with food.

Fluconazole 3–6mg/kg/week oral for 8–16 weeks: Weekly pulse — less commonly used. For refractory or Candida tinea capitis. Oral suspension available.

2. Adjunctive Antifungal Shampoo (Reduces Transmission — Not Curative Alone)

Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo (twice weekly): Reduces viable spores on scalp surface — reduces household and school transmission. Apply, leave 5 minutes, rinse. Use during entire treatment course. Does NOT cure tinea capitis but dramatically reduces spore shedding.

Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo (twice weekly): Alternative antifungal shampoo for reducing scalp fungal load. Also controls seborrhoeic dermatitis if co-present.

3. Kerion Management

Systemic Antifungal (Terbinafine or Griseofulvin) — Primary Treatment: Kerion is treated with systemic antifungal alone — NOT incision and drainage (I&D). I&D is not indicated.

Prednisolone 0.5mg/kg/day for 7–14 days (Adjunct for Severe Kerion): Short-course oral steroid reduces the inflammatory scarring response in severe kerion — may reduce risk of permanent alopecia. Use WITH antifungal, never alone.

4. Household Transmission Control

Treat All Symptomatic Household Members: Examine and treat with antifungal shampoo all siblings and household contacts showing any scalp abnormality — prevents reinfection of treated patient.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Terbinafine 250mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line oral antifungal for T. violaceum tinea capitis. Scored for paediatric dosing. PMBJP widely available.

Griseofulvin 125mg Tablets (10 tabs): Traditional first-line for Microsporum tinea capitis. Widely available at government hospitals.

Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Paediatric alternative for refractory cases.

Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo 100ml: Antifungal shampoo adjunct — reduces scalp spore load.

Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Lotion 100ml: Scalp shampoo adjunct to reduce transmission. PMBJP or government hospital pharmacy.

Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): For severe kerion adjunctive anti-inflammatory use.

Ferrous Sulphate + Folic Acid Tablet (10 tabs): Iron supplementation for children with co-existing iron deficiency anaemia.
