diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Karanja (Pongamia pinnata) Oil — Scalp Application: Traditional Indian antimicrobial scalp oil — anti-dermatophyte activity of karanjin and pongamol. Apply to scalp patches twice daily; leave 30 minutes before washing. Widely used in rural India for scalp fungal infections.

Neem (Nimba) Oil Scalp Application: Direct anti-dermatophyte activity of nimbidin. Apply warm Neem oil to affected scalp areas, cover with cloth, leave overnight. Wash with Neem-based shampoo. Reduces scaling and itch while systemic antifungal takes effect.

Haridra (Turmeric) + Neem Paste (External): Fresh paste of equal parts turmeric and Neem leaf — applied to scalp patches and covered with cloth for 30–60 minutes. Traditional anti-ringworm scalp remedy with antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory actions. Use as adjunct.

Bhringaraj (Eclipta alba) Oil Scalp Massage: Anti-inflammatory and hair growth-promoting — key Ayurvedic trichological herb. Bhringaraj oil massage supports hair follicle recovery during and after tinea capitis treatment, reducing post-inflammatory alopecia.

Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Paste — External: Krimighna (antiparasitic/antifungal) herb — Vidanga paste applied directly to scalp lesions. Traditional treatment for Dadroo (ringworm) and Krimija Khalitya (fungal hair loss).

Triphala Churna Oral: Internal rasayana and immune-support — blood purifying Tridosha balancer. 5g Triphala with warm water at bedtime during tinea capitis treatment — supports systemic antifungal effect and immune response.

Mahamanjishtadi Kashayam (Oral): Classical blood-purifying Ayurvedic decoction — for recurrent or refractory tinea capitis associated with blood Dosha (Rakta Dushti). 15ml twice daily diluted with warm water.

Arogyavardhini Vati: Liver-protecting and immunostimulatory tablet used during long oral antifungal therapy — may provide hepatoprotective support during 6–8 week Griseofulvin or Terbinafine courses. 250–500mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific:
T. violaceum and T. tonsurans Dominance: Unlike Western countries (M. canis predominant), India predominantly harbours endothrix species T. violaceum and T. tonsurans — which do NOT fluoresce under Wood's lamp (UV) and have smaller ectothrix spores — making rapid screening by Wood's lamp unreliable in Indian practice. Direct microscopy of hair and culture are mandatory.

Urban Migrant Worker Household Transmission: Dense urban migrant worker housing in Indian cities creates ideal transmission settings — shared sleeping spaces, single bathroom for multiple families, sharing of hair-care items. T. tonsurans household epidemics are documented.

Barber Shop Transmission: Traditional Indian barber shops (Hajam) with shared unsterilised blades, combs, and scissors — documented source of tinea capitis transmission. Recommendations for sterilisation of barber instruments are poorly implemented.

Griseofulvin Resistance Emergence: India increasingly reports treatment failures with Griseofulvin (the oldest oral antifungal for tinea capitis) — due to both T. violaceum intrinsic reduced sensitivity and poor dissolution/absorption of generic Griseofulvin formulations. Terbinafine is now preferred by IADVL for Indian tinea capitis caused by T. violaceum/T. tonsurans.

Food Insecurity and Iron Deficiency Worsening Response: Iron deficiency anaemia (very common in Indian elementary school children) does not cause tinea capitis but impairs immune response and wound healing — children with kerion and iron deficiency have slower recovery. Iron supplementation is important adjunctive treatment.
diseases.important_notes:
Topical Antifungal Alone is Completely Ineffective for Tinea Capitis — Oral Treatment is Mandatory: The most critical education point: topical creams (Clotrimazole, Terbinafine, Ketoconazole) applied to the scalp DO NOT CURE tinea capitis. They may temporarily reduce scaling but the dermatophyte in the hair shaft and follicle is unreachable by topical agents. Only oral systemic antifungal (Terbinafine or Griseofulvin) for 6–12 weeks achieves mycological cure. Topical antifungal shampoo is useful only as an ADJUNCT to reduce scalp spore shedding and household transmission.

Kerion Must NOT be Incised or Drained — Treat with Antifungals: Kerion (the boggy inflammatory scalp mass with pus) is frequently mistaken for a scalp abscess and referred for surgical incision and drainage — this is incorrect and delays cure. Kerion is a hypersensitivity immune response to dermatophyte antigens, not a true bacterial abscess. Treatment is systemic antifungal (Terbinafine) ± short oral steroid (Prednisolone). I&D leads to secondary bacterial infection and permanent scarring. Educate PHC physicians and surgeons on this critical distinction.

Screen and Treat Household School-Age Contacts: Tinea capitis is highly contagious in household settings through shared combs, bedding, towels, and head-to-head contact. When one child is diagnosed, ALL siblings and close household contacts must be examined. Those with scalp scaling, broken hairs, or alopecia should be treated. Non-symptomatic household contacts should use antifungal shampoo (Ketoconazole or Selenium Sulphide) twice weekly for 4 weeks as prophylaxis. Failure to treat household contacts = guaranteed reinfection of the index patient.

Griseofulvin Must Be Taken with Fat for Absorption: Griseofulvin (microsize) has poor and variable oral bioavailability — absorption doubles when taken with a fatty meal. In malnourished Indian children taking Griseofulvin on an empty stomach or with minimal fat, therapeutic blood levels may NOT be achieved → apparent treatment failure. Instruct parents: give Griseofulvin with milk, full-fat yoghurt, or a meal containing ghee/oil. This is a simple instruction that dramatically improves treatment success rates.
confirmation_tests_curated: Scalp Hair Examination: Pattern recognition — grey patch (ectothrix), black dot (endothrix), kerion (inflammatory). Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy + scalp scaling + patchy alopecia = tinea capitis until proven otherwise in a child.

Wood's Lamp Examination in Dark Room: Ectothrix Microsporum species (M. canis, M. audouinii) fluoresce bright green. However — T. violaceum and T. tonsurans (most common Indian species) do NOT fluoresce — Wood's lamp is unreliable in India as a standalone test. Use for screening but always proceed to microscopy.

diseases.name: Histoplasmosis
diseases.tests:
1. Fungal Culture (Gold Standard — Slow)

Culture of BAL, Bone Marrow, Blood, or Tissue on SDA at 25°C and 37°C: Definitive — Histoplasma grows as white-brown mould at 25°C (tuberculate macroconidia = identifying feature). Blood cultures positive in 50–70% of PDH. Takes 2–6 weeks. Biosafety Level 3 handling required — not available at many Indian centres.

Bone Marrow Trephine (PDH): Most sensitive single specimen for PDH culture AND histopathology simultaneously — intracellular yeasts visible in macrophages on H&E/PAS/GMS stain.

2. Histopathology (Rapid Diagnosis from Tissue)

Biopsy of Accessible Lesion (Oral Ulcer, Lymph Node, Skin, Liver): H&E + PAS/GMS stain — intracellular small (2–4µm) oval yeasts within macrophage vacuoles ('negative staining halo'). Should be requested in ALL unexplained oral ulcers in immunosuppressed Indian patients.

Buffy Coat Peripheral Blood Smear (Severe PDH): Wright/Giemsa stained buffy coat — intracellular yeasts seen in neutrophils and monocytes. Rapid, cheap 15-minute test — positive in severe PDH with fungaemia. Can diagnose histoplasmosis at district lab level.

3. Antigen Detection (Fastest Clinical Test)

Urine Histoplasma Antigen (MVista EIA / LFA): Sensitivity 90%+ in PDH and 80% in acute severe pulmonary histoplasmosis. Point-of-care lateral flow assay available. Test cross-reacts with Paracoccidioides, Blastomyces — consider endemic geography. Urine is optimal specimen (more concentrated antigen than serum). Available at AIIMS, CMC Vellore, PGIMER.

Serum Histoplasma Antigen EIA: 80–85% sensitivity in PDH. Useful for monitoring treatment response — antigen titre falls with successful therapy.

4. Serology (Diagnosis in Subacute/Chronic Disease)

Histoplasma Antibody (Complement Fixation + Immunodiffusion): CF titres ≥1:32 or presence of M/H bands on ID = significant. Takes 4–6 weeks after infection for seroconversion. FALSE NEGATIVE in profoundly immunosuppressed (CD4 <100). Better for chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis than PDH.

5. Radiological

CT Chest: Diffuse nodular/miliary pattern (PDH), upper-lobe cavities (chronic), mediastinal lymphadenopathy and fibrosis (mediastinal histoplasmosis).

CT Abdomen: Hepatosplenomegaly, adrenal enlargement (bilateral) — strongly supports PDH diagnosis.

6. Indian Guidelines

IADVL/ISHAM Histoplasmosis Guidelines 2022: Amphotericin B (liposomal) for moderate-severe PDH; Itraconazole for mild and step-down/maintenance. Duration: 12 months total. Histoplasma antigen for diagnosis and monitoring.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Moderate-Severe PDH (Including HIV — Induction)

Liposomal Amphotericin B 3mg/kg/day IV for 2 weeks: Most effective induction for PDH — superior to conventional AmB, significantly less nephrotoxic. Standard of care at major Indian centres. Expensive; government HIV programme may provide.

Amphotericin B Deoxycholate 0.7–1mg/kg/day IV for 2 weeks: Alternative when L-AmB unavailable — effective but nephrotoxic. Pre-hydrate, replace potassium daily. Monitor creatinine every 2 days.

2. Mild-Moderate PDH and Chronic Pulmonary Histoplasmosis

Itraconazole 200mg three times daily for 3 days (loading), then 200mg twice daily for 12 months: Standard maintenance and mild PDH treatment. Take with fatty meal and acidic beverage (Coke/orange juice) for maximum absorption — critical for itraconazole capsule bioavailability.

3. Step-Down Therapy (After AmB Induction — Consolidation + Maintenance)

Itraconazole 200mg twice daily for minimum 12 months total duration: Step-down after 2 weeks AmB induction. Minimum 12 months for PDH; longer if immunosuppression continues. Serum Itraconazole levels >1µg/ml required for therapeutic response monitoring.

4. Acute Self-Limiting Pulmonary Histoplasmosis

Itraconazole 200mg once daily for 6–12 weeks: For symptomatic acute pulmonary histoplasmosis lasting >4 weeks. Mild acute cases need only supportive treatment and monitoring.

5. Secondary Prophylaxis (HIV PDH — CD4 <150)

Itraconazole 200mg/day indefinitely (until CD4 >150 on ART for ≥6 months): Secondary prophylaxis to prevent PDH relapse in HIV. NACO/WHO recommend continuation until immune reconstitution confirmed.

6. Adrenal Insufficiency Management

Hydrocortisone 20mg morning + 10mg afternoon oral daily: Replacement for adrenal histoplasmosis-associated adrenal insufficiency. Taper as Histoplasma treatment takes effect and adrenal function recovers (monitor ACTH stimulation test).
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Step-down and maintenance histoplasmosis treatment. PMBJP available.

Amphotericin B 50mg Injection (1 vial): Induction therapy where L-AmB unavailable.

Hydrocortisone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Adrenal insufficiency replacement.

Potassium Chloride SR 600mg Tablets (10 tabs): Electrolyte replacement during AmB therapy.

Normal Saline 500ml: Pre-hydration before AmB infusion.

Vitamin D3 60000 IU Capsules (4 caps): Bone health and immune support during prolonged antifungal therapy.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Itraconazole 100mg (4 capsules): Rs.48

Amphotericin B 50mg injection (1 vial): Rs.142

Hydrocortisone 10mg (10 tablets): Rs.14

Potassium Chloride SR 600mg (10 tablets): Rs.22

Normal Saline 500ml: Rs.42
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT: Histoplasmosis PDH is a Potentially Fatal Systemic Fungal Infection Requiring Medical Antifungal Treatment: Systemic Amphotericin B / Itraconazole is the life-saving treatment — Ayurvedic medicines serve only as supportive adjuncts in the recovery phase.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Standardised Extract: Immunoadaptogen — supports immune recovery during and after prolonged antifungal therapy. 600mg extract twice daily. Particularly useful in HIV-positive post-PDH patients on ART to counter fatigue and immune restoration.

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) Tablets or Kadha: Leading Ayurvedic immunomodulatory herb — Guduchi Ghana has documented immunostimulatory activity (lymphocyte and macrophage activation). 500mg standardised tablets twice daily or 30ml decoction twice daily. Supports non-specific immunity post-PDH.

Chyawanprash (Dabur / Himalaya): Comprehensive Rasayana for immune rebuilding — particularly during 12-month Itraconazole maintenance therapy. Vitamin C and antioxidant content supports mucosal immunity. 2 teaspoons daily.

Shatavari Churna: Female immunomodulatory Rasayana — supportive for female PDH patients recovering under prolonged antifungal therapy. 5g with warm milk twice daily.

Trikatu Churna: Digestive stimulant to enhance Itraconazole absorption in patients complaining of poor appetite or gastric intolerance. 1g before each Itraconazole dose with ginger tea for Kapha-dominant gut type.

Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica) Decoction: For chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis with productive cough and haemoptysis — bronchodilatory, expectorant, and haemostatic properties. 30ml twice daily as adjunct to Itraconazole therapy.

Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) Decoction: For hepatomegaly and oedema associated with PDH — traditional Ayurvedic hepatoprotective and diuretic. 30ml decoction twice daily under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific:
Endemic Zones in India: Histoplasma capsulatum is endemic in the Gangetic plains (West Bengal, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Assam), parts of Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh. Bats (roosting in old buildings and caves), bird-dropping-contaminated soil, and aerosol during construction/earth disturbance are the primary sources of India exposure.

HIV Co-infection: PDH is the defining AIDS OI in Histoplasma-endemic Indian states — analogous to Cryptococcal meningitis in HIV. CD4 <150 = high risk. NACO does not currently mandate Histoplasma screening at CD4 thresholds (unlike Cryptococcus) but endemic-area ART centres should maintain high suspicion.

Steroid and Biologic Immunosuppression: India's growing population of patients on prolonged corticosteroids (TB paradoxical reaction, SLE, nephrotic syndrome, organ transplant) and TNF-inhibitors (Rheumatoid Arthritis — Infliximab, Adalimumab) are at increasing PDH risk.

Misdiagnosis as TB with Inappropriate ATT: The single greatest harm in Indian histoplasmosis management is unrecognised PDH receiving 6–9 months ATT antitubercular therapy — with continued clinical deterioration, progressive immunosuppression from the fungal infection, and diagnostic delay. In any patient failing ATT or in whom TB is not histologically or microbiologically confirmed, histoplasmosis should be considered.

Bat-Cave Exposure Clusters: School trips to Ajanta/Ellora caves, Dandeli bat caves, and Bengal cave environments have caused documented histoplasmosis outbreak clusters in India — acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in >20 individuals from single exposures.
diseases.important_notes:
Histoplasmosis is the Most Under-Diagnosed Systemic Fungal Infection in India — Think Beyond TB: Any HIV-positive or immunosuppressed patient from the Gangetic plains, Bengal, or Maharashtra presenting with fever + weight loss + hepatosplenomegaly + pancytopenia who is NOT responding to antitubercular therapy must have urgent Histoplasma antigen (urine + serum) and bone marrow examination. A buffy coat smear in severe PDH can diagnose Histoplasma in 15 minutes with Wright stain. This diagnosis is being missed daily at Indian secondary and tertiary centres.

Itraconazole Absorption is Critically Diet-Dependent — Education is Treatment: Itraconazole capsule bioavailability is drastically reduced in fasting state and alkaline gastric pH. Patients on PPIs, H2-blockers, or antacids will have sub-therapeutic levels. Itraconazole MUST be taken immediately with a full fatty meal AND with an acidic beverage (cola, orange juice). Itraconazole oral solution (cyclodextrin-based) has better and food-independent absorption — preferred when available. If patient is fasting for any reason, switch to IV formulation. Measuring serum trough levels (target >1µg/ml) is the only reliable way to confirm therapeutic dosing.

Bilateral Adrenal Enlargement + Fever = CT Abdomen Immediately: In any febrile Indian patient with CT showing bilateral adrenal enlargement, always consider: Histoplasmosis, Tuberculosis, and less commonly other granulomatous infections. Both TB and Histoplasma adrenalitis are treatable — but require different treatments. Adrenal histoplasmosis causing Addisonian crisis in an HIV patient can be fatal if the diagnosis is delayed. Serum cortisol + ACTH stimulation test + Histoplasma antigen in all bilateral adrenal enlargement cases.

Bat Cave and Excavation Outbreaks — Report to Health Authorities: Documented cluster exposure at bat-containing caves or major excavation sites causing >2 cases of acute pulmonary histoplasmosis should be reported to the District Health Officer and ICMR as a potential outbreak. Environmental sampling of the exposure site and contact tracing/prophylaxis (Itraconazole 200mg daily x 12 weeks for severely exposed immunocompromised individuals) prevent secondary cases.
confirmation_tests_curated: Culture of BAL, Bone Marrow, Blood, or Tissue on SDA at 25°C and 37°C: Definitive — Histoplasma grows as white-brown mould at 25°C (tuberculate macroconidia = identifying feature). Blood cultures positive in 50–70% of PDH. Takes 2–6 weeks. Biosafety Level 3 handling required — not available at many Indian centres.
Bone Marrow Trephine (PDH): Most sensitive single specimen for PDH culture AND histopathology simultaneously — intracellular yeasts visible in macrophages on H&E/PAS/GMS stain.

Biopsy of Accessible Lesion (Oral Ulcer, Lymph Node, Skin, Liver): H&E + PAS/GMS stain — intracellular small (2–4µm) oval yeasts within macrophage vacuoles ('negative staining halo'). Should be requested in ALL unexplained oral ulcers in immunosuppressed Indian patients.

diseases.name: Amoebiasis
diseases.tests:
1. Stool Examination (Intestinal Amoebiasis)

Stool Microscopy — Wet Mount: Fresh stool examined within 30 minutes of collection — Entamoeba histolytica trophozoites seen as motile organisms with ingested red blood cells (erythrophagocytosis = diagnostic of E. histolytica pathogen vs non-pathogenic E. dispar). Cysts visible in formed stools. Low sensitivity — 3 stool samples increase yield.

Stool Antigen Detection (ELISA or RDT): Entamoeba histolytica-specific antigen test on stool — distinguishes pathogenic E. histolytica from non-pathogenic E. dispar (morphologically identical on microscopy). Sensitivity 86–93%. Available at reference labs.

Stool PCR: Most sensitive and specific — gold standard for research/reference. Identifies E. histolytica vs E. dispar vs E. moshkovskii genotypically. Not routinely available at district level.

2. Serology (Amoebic Liver Abscess — Most Useful)

Entamoeba histolytica IgG ELISA (Serum): Sensitivity 95–99% for ALA. Positive >1:128 titre = strong evidence for amoebic aetiology. May be negative in first week. Most important diagnostic test for ALA — available at AIIMS, PGIMER, major reference labs. Rapid IHA (Indirect Haemagglutination Assay) also widely used in India.

3. Imaging (ALA)

Ultrasound Abdomen: Most rapid and accessible first-line — hypoechoic or anechoic hepatic lesion in right lobe (85–90% right lobe), rounded, well circumscribed, close to hepatic capsule — typically single large abscess. Guides aspiration if needed.

CT Abdomen with Contrast: For atypical, multiple, or ruptured abscesses. Low-density, non-enhancing cavity with peripheral rim enhancement. Assesses complications (rupture into peritoneum, pleura, pericardium).

4. Diagnostic Aspiration (When Diagnosis Uncertain or No Drug Response)

Amoebic Abscess Aspirate: 'Anchovy sauce' or chocolate-brown thick pus (liquefied necrotic liver) = pathognomonic of ALA. However, Entamoeba trophozoites identified only in 20% of aspirate — serology + radiological features are more reliable.

5. Blood Investigations

CBC: Leucocytosis (WBC 15,000–25,000) in ALA — acute bacterial-like response despite fungal origin in amoebic abscess. Anaemia in chronic intestinal amoebiasis.

LFT: Moderately elevated bilirubin and ALP in ALA. Markedly elevated = biliary communication or rupture.

CRP/ESR: Very elevated in ALA — useful severity marker.

6. Colonoscopy (Amoebic Colitis — Atypical/Refractory)

Colonoscopy Biopsy: Flask-shaped ulcers with undermined edges typically in caecum/right colon on colonoscopy — E. histolytica in biopsy by PAS stain. Reserved for biopsy of colonic amoeboma or chronic/atypical presentation. Contraindicated in toxic megacolon.

7. Indian Guidelines

INASL (Indian National Association for Study of Liver) ALA Guidelines 2022: Metronidazole 400–800mg TDS x 10 days = first-line. Aspiration only if >5cm, left lobe, risk of rupture, or no response in 72h. Always follow with luminal amebicide (Diloxanide furoate).
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Tissue Amebicide (Active Against Invasive Disease)

Metronidazole 400mg three times daily oral for 10 days (Intestinal Amoebiasis): First-line for amoebic dysentery. Highly effective against invasive trophozoites. GI side effects (nausea, metallic taste) — take with food. AVOID alcohol during and 48 hours after treatment.

Metronidazole 800mg three times daily oral for 10 days (ALA): Standard high-dose oral regimen for amoebic liver abscess — 90% efficacy. Start BEFORE aspiration and imaging confirmation when clinically confident.

Metronidazole 500mg IV every 8 hours (Severe/Fulminant No Oral Route): IV Metronidazole for severe amoebic colitis, fulminant disease, or patients unable to take orals.

Tinidazole 2g once daily for 3–5 days (Intestinal and ALA): Single daily dose — better compliance than TID Metronidazole; fewer GI side effects; equivalent efficacy. ALA: 5 days; intestinal amoebiasis: 3 days.

Ornidazole 500mg twice daily for 5 days: Well tolerated; twice-daily dosing. Equivalent to Metronidazole for amoebiasis. Less metallic taste.

2. Luminal Amebicide (Mandatory After Tissue Treatment)

Diloxanide Furoate 500mg three times daily for 10 days: ESSENTIAL follow-up after every course of tissue amebicide — eradicates cysts in intestinal lumen (Metronidazole has poor luminal cyst activity). Without luminal amebicide clearance, relapse rate approaches 50%. Available as Furamide in India.

3. Adjunctive

Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) — WHO Formula: For dehydration from amoebic dysentery — maintain hydration with ORS throughout treatment. Freely available at all government health centres.

Aspiration (Therapeutic — Not Routinely Required): Ultrasound-guided percutaneous needle aspiration for: ALA >5cm, left-lobe abscess (risk of pericardial rupture), rupture impending, no response in 72 hours of Metronidazole, or diagnostic uncertainty. NOT routine first-line — Metronidazole alone cures 90% of ALA.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line amoebiasis treatment. PMBJP widely available.

Tinidazole 500mg Tablets (4 tabs): Single-dose equivalent alternative — better compliance.

Diloxanide Furoate 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Mandatory luminal clearance after tissue treatment.

ORS Sachet (Oral Rehydration Salts): Dehydration management. Available at all PMBJP outlets and government health centres.

Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Fever management in ALA.

Metronidazole 500mg/100ml IV Infusion Bag: For severe amoebiasis or fulminant colitis. Government hospital formulary.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Metronidazole 400mg (10 tablets): Rs.10

Tinidazole 500mg (4 tablets): Rs.22

Diloxanide Furoate 500mg (10 tablets): Rs.28

ORS Sachet (1 sachet, 1L): Rs.3

Paracetamol 500mg (10 tablets): Rs.4

Metronidazole 500mg/100ml IV bag: Rs.38
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Kutajarishta (Holarrhena antidysenterica Fermented Decoction): Most important classical Ayurvedic treatment for Atisar (dysentery) — particularly for Raktatisara (blood dysentery = amoebic). Kutaja (Holarrhena) alkaloids (conessine, kurchicine) have direct anti-amoebic activity confirmed in laboratory studies. 15–25ml twice daily after food. Widely available and affordable. Complementary to Metronidazole; do NOT use as replacement in severe disease.

Bilwadi Churna: Bilwa (Aegle marmelos / Bael fruit) — dried unripe Bael fruit has tannins and marmelosin with anti-amoebic and antidiarrhoeal properties. 3–5g twice daily with warm water or Kutajarishta. Traditional first-aid for amoebic dysentery in Indian households. Unripe Bael tablet/powder widely available.

Nagarmotha (Cyperus rotundus) Decoction: Kapha-Pitta balancing herb with carminative and antidiarrhoeal action — reduces tenesmus and colicky pain in amoebic dysentery. 30ml decoction twice daily. Anti-amoebic and anti-inflammatory.

Musta (Cyperus rotundus) + Dhataki (Woodfordia fruticosa) Compound: Components of Kutaja parpati and other classical formulations for Pravahika (dysentery with tenesmus). Astringent and anti-induction of intestinal mucosa.

Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) Churna: Tri-doshic herbomineral purifier — mild laxative and anthelmintic properties support elimination of amoebic organisms from gut in mild, sub-acute intestinal amoebiasis. 5g with warm water at bedtime.

Guduchi Kwatha (Tinospora cordifolia Decoction): Immunostimulatory — supports systemic immunity and hepatic function during ALA recovery. 30ml twice daily.

Chandanasava: Classical fermented Ayurvedic preparation — for pyrexia associated with ALA, particularly the burning and Pitta-excess constitution. Cooling, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic. 15ml after meals.

Panchatikta Ghrita: Tikta (bitter) ghee for systemic anti-inflammatory support in invasive amoebiasis — hepatoprotective and immunomodulatory. 5–10ml on empty stomach under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific:
Contaminated Municipal Water Supply: India's urban water supply — despite chlorination — frequently has post-chlorination contamination through broken sewage-crossing distribution pipes in dense urban areas. Entamoeba cysts are relatively resistant to chlorine at standard doses. Boiling drinking water eliminates Entamoeba risk.

Street Food Consumption: Raw vegetables, uncooked salads, cut fruit, sugarcane juice, and water at street stalls — contaminated by faecal E. histolytica cysts from unhygienic food handlers. Most Indian urban ALA cases are acquired through street food/water contamination.

Open Defecation Contaminating Agricultural Fields: Significant residual open defecation in rural India — faecal contamination of fields → contamination of irrigation water and produce → transmission. Swachh Bharat Mission progress has reduced but not eliminated this route.

Male Sex and Alcohol Predisposition for ALA: Males are 7–10x more likely than females to develop ALA despite similar intestinal infestation rates. Alcohol consumption — which is disproportionately high among working-age males in India — increases ALA susceptibility via hepatic immune suppression. ALA is a disease of working-age Indian men in 80% of cases.

Amoebic vs Pyogenic Abscess Distinction: Indian hospitals see both amoebic and pyogenic liver abscesses — key clinical differentiators: ALA = single large right-lobe abscess, age 20–50, male, acute illness <4 weeks, dramatic response to Metronidazole, anchovy-sauce pus; Pyogenic = older patient, fever >3 weeks, commonly associated with biliary disease, multiple small abscesses, requires drainage + prolonged antibiotics.
diseases.important_notes:
Always Follow Tissue Amebicide with Luminal Amebicide — Critical for Preventing Relapse: The single most common management error in amoebiasis treatment: prescribing Metronidazole or Tinidazole alone without following with Diloxanide Furoate. Metronidazole is a tissue amebicide — it kills invasive trophozoites in the bowel wall and liver but has POOR activity against luminal cysts. Without luminal clearance with Diloxanide Furoate 500mg TDS x 10 days, cyst excretion continues, household transmission persists, and the patient reinfects themselves. This step is mandatory after every amoebic dysentery or ALA course.

ALA Does NOT Routinely Require Percutaneous Drainage: Unlike pyogenic liver abscess (which requires drainage), >90% of amoebic liver abscesses resolve completely with Metronidazole alone. Routine percutaneous drainage is NOT recommended. Aspiration is indicated ONLY for: (1) left-lobe ALA (risk of pericardial rupture), (2) abscess >5cm with rupture risk, (3) no clinical response after 72 hours of adequate Metronidazole, (4) diagnostic uncertainty. Unnecessary drainage procedures carry risks of secondary infection and complications — resist the temptation to drain all liver abscesses.

AVOID ALCOHOL During and 48 Hours After Metronidazole / Tinidazole: Metronidazole and Tinidazole interact with alcohol to cause a disulfiram-like reaction — severe nausea, flushing, vomiting, tachycardia. In India's male ALA patient population (who frequently consume alcohol), this counselling point is medically essential and must be explicitly stated at prescription. Some patients actively conceal alcohol use — ask directly and matter-of-factly.

WASH Interventions are the Real Solution — Treat the Environment, Not Just the Patient: Amoebiasis is an entirely preventable disease with access to clean water and sanitation. Treating individual patients without addressing household water source (advise boiling/filtration), food hygiene, and sanitation perpetuates the transmission cycle. ASHA workers and PHC teams should provide WASH counselling at every amoebiasis consultation — hand washing, use of clean water for drinking and food preparation, covered food storage, and avoidance of raw vegetables/street food where water safety is uncertain.

diseases.name: Neurocysticercosis
diseases.tests:
1. Neuroimaging (Primary Diagnostic Tool)

MRI Brain with Gadolinium (Preferred): Most sensitive modality — detects all stages: (Stage 1) Vesicular: clear cyst with scolex (dot-in-hole sign = pathognomonic — bright scolex within cyst); (Stage 2) Colloidal vesicular: ring enhancement + perilesional oedema; (Stage 3) Granular nodular: solid nodule + enhancement; (Stage 4) Calcified nodule. MRI critical for ventricular/subarachnoid/spinal NCC.

CT Brain with Contrast: Detects ring-enhancing lesion with perilesional oedema (active colloidal stage) and calcified granulomata (old lesions). Less sensitive than MRI for small lesions, posterior fossa, ventricular, and subarachnoid NCC. Most commonly used in Indian district hospitals due to availability and cost.

2. Serology

EITB (Enzyme-Linked Immunoelectrotransfer Blot) — Serum/CSF: Gold standard serological test — 98% specificity; 50–94% sensitivity (lower for single lesions). Available at AIIMS, PGIMER, NIMHANS, CMC Vellore. Positive in multiple cysts; may be negative in SCG (single cyst).

Taenia solium Antibody ELISA (Serum): More widely available but lower specificity than EITB. Cross-reactivity with other helminths limits specificity in India's highly helmintic population.

3. CSF Analysis (When CNS Infection Suspected)

Lumbar Puncture + CSF Cell Count/Biochemistry/Cytology: For meningeal NCC — eosinophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein. CSF EITB highly sensitive for subarachnoid/meningeal NCC. LP only after CT/MRI to exclude raised ICP.

4. Stool Examination

Stool Microscopy for Taenia solium Eggs/Proglottids: Screen household members for active intestinal tapeworm carriage — the human taeniasis carrier is the transmission source. Three stool samples increase sensitivity. Positive = treat immediately with Praziquantel.

5. Imaging for Porcine Cysticercosis

X-Ray Muscle (Thigh, Calf, Arm): Calcified elliptical intramuscular cysticerci ('rice grain calcifications') — incidental finding supporting the diagnosis of T. solium lifecycle exposure in patient or household.

6. Del Brutto Diagnostic Criteria

Absolute Criteria (Diagnostic): (1) Histopathological demonstration of cysticercus in brain biopsy; (2) Cystic lesion with scolex on imaging; (3) Spontaneous resolution of small cystic lesion.

Major Criteria: Ring or disc-enhancing lesion on CT/MRI; positive EITB.

7. EEG

EEG: Type of seizure characterisation, monitoring AED response. Focal slowing or epileptiform discharge over lesion area. Not diagnostic of NCC but guides antiepileptic drug selection.

8. Indian Guidelines

NIMHANS / Indian Academy of Neurology NCC Guidelines 2021: Antiepileptics for seizure control mandatory. Antiparasitic (Albendazole) + anti-inflammatory (Steroids) for viable/transitional stage. Calcified lesions — no antiparasitic needed.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antiepileptic Drugs (Seizure Control — Priority)

Phenytoin 300mg/day oral (divided doses): Classical first-line AED in India for focal-onset seizures. Narrow therapeutic index — monitor levels (target 10–20µg/ml). Gingival hyperplasia, phenytoin facies with long-term use.

Carbamazepine 400–800mg/day oral (divided doses): Preferred for focal seizures — better tolerability than Phenytoin. Start 100–200mg BD, titrate. Monthly liver function.

Levetiracetam 500–1500mg twice daily: Newer, well-tolerated AED for NCC seizures — minimal drug interactions (important given concurrent Albendazole and corticosteroids). Replace Phenytoin with Levetiracetam where feasible.

2. Antiparasitic Treatment (Active/Viable Lesions — NOT Calcified)

Albendazole 400mg twice daily for 8–30 days (with fatty meal): First-line antiparasitic — disrupts Taenia solium cyst metabolism. MUST be combined with corticosteroids (steroid started 2–3 days before Albendazole and continued throughout) to manage pericystic inflammation from dying cysts. Do NOT treat calcified NCC with antiparasitic.

Praziquantel 50mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses for 14 days: Alternative or co-treatment antiparasitic — synergistic with Albendazole for heavy burden NCC. NOTE: Praziquantel is CONTRAINDICATED in ocular/spinal NCC. Drug interaction with steroids (reduces Praziquantel levels — use Cimetidine to increase levels).

Combination Albendazole + Praziquantel: For heavy burden (>2 cysts) NCC — recommended by current international guidelines as superior to either alone. 28-day course.

3. Corticosteroids (Anti-inflammatory — Mandatory with Antiparasitic)

Dexamethasone 0.1mg/kg/day for 1–2 weeks: Started 2–3 days before antiparasitic and continued throughout treatment — prevents pericystic inflammation (from dying cysts causing perilesional oedema and seizure exacerbation).

Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day (alternative oral steroid): For outpatient NCC management where IV Dexamethasone not needed.

4. Hydrocephalus Management

Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) Shunt (Neurosurgical): For obstructive hydrocephalus from ventricular NCC — requires neurosurgical referral. Shunt + antiparasitic + steroid together. Neuroendoscopic cyst removal preferred where available.

5. Household Tapeworm Treatment

Praziquantel 10mg/kg single oral dose (Household T. solium Carrier): Single-dose treatment for intestinal Taenia solium tapeworm in household carrier — eliminates ongoing transmission source. Critical public health intervention.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Antiparasitic treatment for viable NCC. PMBJP widely available.

Praziquantel 600mg Tablets (6 tabs): Antiparasitic for NCC and household tapeworm carrier treatment.

Phenytoin 100mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line antiepileptic for NCC seizures. Government health programme widely supplied.

Carbamazepine 200mg Tablets (10 tabs): Alternative AED — focal seizures from NCC.

Dexamethasone 0.5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Anti-inflammatory for perilesional oedema during antiparasitic therapy.

Levetiracetam 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Newer well-tolerated AED. PMBJP list.

Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Outpatient corticosteroid alternative to Dexamethasone.

Omeprazole 20mg Capsules (10 caps): Gastric protection during prolonged steroid therapy.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder (Oral): Primary Krimighna (antiparasitic) herb in Ayurveda — used for intestinal worms and parasitic infections. 2–3g powder with honey twice daily. Clinical studies demonstrate activity against cestodes (tapeworms). Supportive antiparasitic role; most relevant for household intestinal Taenia clearance.

Pippali (Piper longum) + Vidanga Combination: Classical Krimighna combination from Charaka Samhita — Pippali+Vidanga for intestinal parasite elimination. Frequently prescribed together in compound formulations.

Vacha (Acorus calamus) Root Powder: Medhya Rasayana (brain tonic) — nootropic and antiepileptic properties described in Charaka. Traditionally used for epilepsy (Apasmara). 1–2g with honey twice daily as adjunct alongside AEDs during NCC seizure management.

Brahmi Ghrita or Brahmi Vati: Classical Medhya Rasayana for brain-related conditions — cognitive support and anti-convulsant adjunct. 500mg twice daily or 5–10ml Brahmi Ghrita. Supports cortical recovery from pericystic inflammation and seizure-related brain stress.

Saraswatarishta: Compound fermented brain tonic (Brahmi + Shatavari + multiple rasayanas) — for post-NCC cognitive deficits, memory impairment, and neurological rehabilitation. 15–25ml twice daily after food.

Ashwagandha Churna: Adaptogenic and neuroprotective — supports long-term neurorecovery from NCC inflammation. 5g with warm milk twice daily. May also reduce epilepsy anxiety and improve sleep quality.

Panchagavya Ghrita: Traditional preparation used in Apasmara (epilepsy) — under classical Ayurvedic Vaidya prescription only. Contains bovine products with neuroprotective role in Ayurvedic neurological practice.

Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) Syrup: Leading Ayurvedic nervine tonic and mild antiepileptic — reduces anxiety, supports memory, mild anti-convulsant effect on animal models. 10–20ml syrup twice daily. Widely available commercially (Himalaya, Dabur, Patanjali brands).
diseases.india_specific:
Pig-Rearing Without Sanitation: NCC transmission cycle requires: (1) Human consumes undercooked pork containing Taenia solium cysticerci → adult tapeworm in intestine; (2) Human tapeworm-carrier contaminates environment with proglottid/eggs; (3) Pigs (or humans — through faecal-oral ingestion of eggs) ingest eggs → cysticerci form in muscle/brain. India's significant pig-rearing communities — particularly in Bihar, UP, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Northeast states, and Andhra Pradesh — with poor human-pig sanitation interface create high transmission risk.

NCC as India's Leading Cause of Acquired Epilepsy: Population studies estimate 30–50% of all new-onset epilepsy in young adults in rural India may be NCC-related — making it the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT PREVENTABLE CAUSE of epilepsy in India. Neuroimaging at onset of every new seizure in India is therefore both diagnostic and epidemiologically critical.

Taenia solium Taeniasis in Household Members: When a patient is diagnosed with NCC, household members should be screened for intestinal Taenia solium tapeworm (stool examination for proglottids/eggs) — a tapeworm carrier in the household is the most likely source of ongoing transmission. Treat tapeworm carrier with Praziquantel (single dose) immediately.

Calcified NCC as Seizure Trigger: Old, calcified, involuted NCC lesions continue to cause seizures in India — perilesional gliosis and ongoing low-grade inflammation around calcified granulomata are important seizure mechanisms even after the live cyst is gone. These lesions do NOT require antiparasitic treatment — antiepileptic drug management only.

Steroid-Induced Complications from Long-Term Use: NCC treatment protocols require prolonged steroids (perilesional oedema management) — in India's diabetic and TB-prevalent population, steroid use requires close monitoring for steroid-induced hyperglycaemia and reactivation of latent TB.
diseases.important_notes:
Neuroimaging at Every New-Onset Seizure in India is Not Optional — It is Essential: India has the world's highest burden of NCC-related epilepsy. The single most impactful clinical action is ensuring CT or MRI brain is performed for every new-onset seizure at any age — including in resource-limited settings where this requires referral. Starting antiepileptic drugs without neuroimaging misses the treatable parasitic diagnosis, fails to identify patients who need antiparasitic therapy, continues the transmission cycle, and potentially condemns patients to unnecessary lifelong AEDs. Work with district hospitals to ensure CT access for new seizure evaluation.

Calcified NCC Does NOT Need Antiparasitic Treatment — Only Seizure Management: Calcified NCC granulomata (old, dead cysts) are frequently treated with Albendazole in Indian practice — this is incorrect and unnecessary. Calcified lesions contain only remnant calcified parasitic debris — no living cyst. Albendazole has no activity against calcification. Antiparasitic treatment is indicated ONLY for viable (vesicular/colloidal) stage lesions — identifiable by scolex on MRI or ring-enhancement on CT/MRI. Treating calcified lesions with unnecessary Albendazole exposes patients to drug side effects without benefit.

Corticosteroids MUST Start Before Antiparasitic Drugs — Never Antiparasitic Alone: Starting Albendazole without corticosteroid cover is dangerous — massive pericystic inflammation from dying cysts can cause severe cerebral oedema, acute seizure exacerbation, or herniation. Steroid must be started 2–3 days BEFORE Albendazole and continued throughout the antiparasitic course. This sequence is the single most critical treatment protocol point for NCC management at all levels.

Treat the Tapeworm Carrier — Not Just the NCC Patient: NCC prevention requires interrupting the T. solium lifecycle. When NCC is diagnosed, examine stool of ALL household members for T. solium eggs/proglottids. If a carrier is identified, single-dose Praziquantel (10mg/kg) treats the adult tapeworm in the gut and eliminates ongoing egg-shedding into the household environment. This public health intervention is as important as the individual NCC treatment — yet it is routinely omitted in Indian clinical practice. ASHA workers and PHC physicians should be empowered to identify and treat household tapeworm carriers.
confirmation_tests_curated: MRI Brain with Gadolinium (Preferred): Most sensitive modality — detects all stages: (Stage 1) Vesicular: clear cyst with scolex (dot-in-hole sign = pathognomonic — bright scolex within cyst); (Stage 2) Colloidal vesicular: ring enhancement + perilesional oedema; (Stage 3) Granular nodular: solid nodule + enhancement; (Stage 4) Calcified nodule. MRI critical for ventricular/subarachnoid/spinal NCC.

CT Brain with Contrast: Detects ring-enhancing lesion with perilesional oedema (active colloidal stage) and calcified granulomata (old lesions). Less sensitive than MRI for small lesions, posterior fossa, ventricular, and subarachnoid NCC. Most commonly used in Indian district hospitals due to availability and cost.

EITB (Enzyme-Linked Immunoelectrotransfer Blot) — Serum/CSF: Gold standard serological test — 98% specificity; 50–94% sensitivity (lower for single lesions). Available at AIIMS, PGIMER, NIMHANS, CMC Vellore. Positive in multiple cysts; may be negative in SCG (single cyst).

diseases.name: Toxoplasmosis
diseases.tests:
1. Serology (Primary Diagnostic Approach)

Toxoplasma IgM ELISA (Serum): Marks acute/recent primary infection — rises within 1–2 weeks of infection, persists 6–9 months. Positive IgM in a pregnant woman/immunocompetent adult = acute infection diagnosis requiring confirmation.

Toxoplasma IgG ELISA (Serum): Marks past infection/chronic carriage — positive in ≥90% of cerebral Tox cases (reactivation). Negative IgG in a suspected cerebral Tox case strongly argues against the diagnosis (consider alternative).

IgG Avidity Test: Distinguishes recent from remote primary infection in pregnant women — high avidity = infection >3 months ago (low foetal risk); low avidity = recent primary infection (high foetal transmission risk). Essential for antenatal Toxoplasma IgG+/IgM equivocal interpretation.

2. Neuroimaging (Cerebral Toxoplasmosis)

MRI Brain with Gadolinium: Multiple (or single) ring-enhancing lesions — predilection for basal ganglia, thalamus, and corticomedullary junction. Concentric/target ring pattern ('target lesion') on MRI = characteristic but not pathognomonic. Perilesional oedema and mass effect. Start empirical treatment — do not delay for definitive diagnosis.

CT Brain with Contrast: Ring-enhancing hypodense lesions with mass effect. Less sensitive than MRI for small or brainstem lesions. First-line in Indian settings.

3. PCR (Molecular Diagnosis)

Toxoplasma PCR — CSF: For CNS Tox — sensitivity 50–60% in CSF; specificity >97%. Positive CSF PCR is highly specific for active cerebral Tox.

Toxoplasma PCR — Blood/BAL: Blood PCR for systemic disease; BAL PCR for pulmonary Toxoplasma.

Toxoplasma PCR — Amniotic Fluid: Gold standard for congenital Tox diagnosis in pregnancy — amniocentesis at ≥18 weeks for confirmed maternal acute infection. High sensitivity for foetal infection.

4. Ophthalmological Examination

Fundoscopy (Slit-Lamp + Indirect Ophthalmoscopy): Retinochoroidal lesion — active white-yellow focal retinitis adjacent to pigmented chorioretinal scar ('satellite lesion'). Anterior uveitis in active ocular Tox. Fluorescein angiography for activity assessment.

5. Tissue Diagnosis (Biopsy — When Empirical Tx Fails)

Brain Biopsy (Stereotactic): For ring-enhancing lesions not responding to 2-week empirical anti-Tox therapy — identifies Toxoplasma bradyzoites/tachyzoites in necrotic brain tissue. Also excludes TB, fungal, or CNS lymphoma.

6. Neonatal Investigations (Congenital Tox)

Neonatal Toxoplasma IgM/IgA + PCR Blood/CSF + CT Brain + Ophthalmology: Full congenital Tox workup at birth — cranial US and CT for calcifications, eye exam for chorioretinitis, neurology assessment.

7. Indian ART Guidelines

NACO ART Cerebral Toxoplasmosis Protocol: Presumptive treatment (Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin) for 2 weeks → repeat MRI. If >50% clinical-radiological improvement = confirmed Tox. Continue 6 weeks total induction, then maintenance.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Standard Treatment — Cerebral and Systemic Toxoplasmosis (Induction)

Pyrimethamine 200mg oral loading dose Day 1, then 50–75mg/day: First-line anti-Toxoplasma — inhibits dihydrofolate reductase. MUST be combined with Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) to prevent bone marrow suppression (Pyrimethamine-induced folate antagonism). Available as branded Daraprim in India.

Sulfadiazine 1–1.5g orally four times daily: Synergistic with Pyrimethamine — combined = classic 'Gold standard' cerebral Tox regimen. Maintain high fluid intake (≥2L/day) to prevent sulfa crystalluria and nephrotoxicity. Unavailable at many Indian centres — Clindamycin is the substitute.

Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) 10–20mg/day orally: MANDATORY with Pyrimethamine — prevents haematological toxicity (neutropaenia, thrombocytopaenia). Continue throughout Pyrimethamine course.

2. Alternative Therapy (When Sulfadiazine Unavailable — Common in India)

Pyrimethamine 50–75mg/day + Clindamycin 600mg IV/oral every 6 hours: Second-line alternative when Sulfadiazine unavailable — widely used in Indian ART centres. Clindamycin + Pyrimethamine has comparable efficacy. Continue Leucovorin.

Co-trimoxazole (Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole) 5/25mg/kg twice daily: Alternative for resource-limited settings — slightly inferior to Pyr+Sulfa but widely available and inexpensive. Also used as primary and secondary prophylaxis.

3. Anti-Inflammatory (Cerebral Tox with Raised ICP/Mass Effect)

Dexamethasone 4mg IV/oral every 6 hours: Used for severe cerebral oedema and mass effect from Toxoplasma abscesses — taper once lesion responds. Avoid prolonged unnecessary steroids in HIV patients.

4. Maintenance Therapy (After Induction — Secondary Prophylaxis)

Pyrimethamine 25mg/day + Sulfadiazine 500mg–1g twice daily: Lifelong secondary prophylaxis until CD4 >200 for ≥6 months on ART. Prevents relapse.

Co-trimoxazole DS 960mg (Septran DS) once daily: Primary prophylaxis for Tox in HIV at CD4 <200; also prevents PJP and Isospora.

5. Ocular Toxoplasmosis

Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin for 4–6 weeks + topical/systemic corticosteroid: Standard ocular Tox treatment. Ophthalmologist-supervised. Systemic Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day for active inflammation — never steroids alone without antiparasitic cover.

6. Congenital Toxoplasmosis

Pyrimethamine 1mg/kg/day + Sulfadiazine 100mg/kg/day + Leucovorin 10mg three times/week for 12 months: Standard neonatal congenital Tox treatment — 12-month course the standard of care.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Co-trimoxazole (Septran DS) 960mg Tablets (10 tabs): Primary and secondary prophylaxis; alternative treatment. Core PMBJP HIV OI formulary.

Pyrimethamine 25mg Tablets (10 tabs): Standard induction and maintenance. Available at ART centres.

Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) 5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Mandatory bone marrow protection with Pyrimethamine.

Clindamycin 300mg Capsules (10 caps): Alternative combination partner for Pyrimethamine.

Dexamethasone 0.5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Cerebral oedema management.

Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Ocular Tox anti-inflammatory.

Normal Saline 1000ml: Hydration during Sulfadiazine therapy to prevent crystalluria.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT — Cerebral and Congenital Toxoplasmosis are Medical Emergencies Requiring Immediate Antifungal/Antiparasitic Treatment: Ayurvedic medicines are SUPPORTIVE ONLY — no classical or modern Ayurvedic formulation replaces Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine in cerebral Tox or congenital Tox. These conditions are life-threatening and sight-threatening.

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) Tablets/Decoction: Immunomodulatory — macrophage and lymphocyte activation helps systemic immune response to T. gondii. 500mg standardised extract twice daily or 30ml kwatha twice daily. Most useful for immune support in HIV patients on ART maintenance prophylaxis.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Extract: Adaptogenic — reduces treatment fatigue, supports adrenal axis during prolonged Pyrimethamine therapy and HIV immune reconstitution. 600mg extract twice daily.

Amalaki (Phyllanthus emblica / Indian Gooseberry): High natural Vitamin C and antioxidant — immune potentiation, reduction of oxidative stress from cerebral Tox inflammation. 500mg standardised extract twice daily or Triphala Churna 5g nightly.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) Tablets: Medhya Rasayana — cognitive rehabilitation after cerebral Tox neurological injury. Supports memory, concentration, and cortical recovery. 300mg standardised extract twice daily; Syrup 10ml twice daily for children with congenital Tox neurological sequelae.

Shatavari Granules: Female reproductive and immune tonic — supportive for pregnant women at elevated Toxoplasma risk, particularly for immune priming and foetal nutrition support. 5g with warm milk twice daily. NOT a prevention strategy — proper food hygiene and antenatal care are essential.

Triphala Churna: Gentle bowel tonic and antioxidant — supports GI health during prolonged antiparasitic drug therapy and prevents Pyrimethamine-induced constipation. 5g at bedtime.

Punarnava Mandura: Classical iron-containing Ayurvedic preparation — for anaemia from Pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow suppression after Leucovorin supplementation. Use under Vaidya guidance; monitor Hb.
diseases.india_specific:
HIV Co-Infection and Cerebral Toxoplasmosis Epidemic: Cerebral toxoplasmosis is one of the top three CNS OIs in HIV-positive Indians (alongside TB meningitis and Cryptococcal meningitis) — particularly in South India where HIV prevalence is higher. NACO ART guidelines mandate Co-trimoxazole prophylaxis at CD4 <200 to prevent all three infections simultaneously.

Undercooked Meat and Maternal Risk in India: Mutton (goat meat), pork, and bushmeat from Northeast India consumed undercooked — common source of T. gondii tissue cysts. Indian women with poor antenatal care who consume undercooked meat are at highest risk of gestational primary Toxoplasma infection.

Congenital Toxoplasmosis and Neurodevelopmental Disability: India lacks national gestational Toxoplasma screening — unlike European systems. Congenital Tox causes intellectual disability, blindness, epilepsy, and hydrocephalus in affected infants — a significant unquantified burden of childhood neuro-disability attributable to untreated congenital toxoplasmosis.

Immunosuppressive Drugs and Reactivation: TNF-inhibitors (Rheumatoid Arthritis), calcineurin inhibitors (organ transplant), prolonged corticosteroids — all increase reactivation risk in Toxoplasma IgG-positive individuals.

Differential Diagnosis from Cerebral TB and Lymphoma: In India, ring-enhancing brain lesions in an HIV patient = Toxoplasma vs TB tuberculoma vs CNS lymphoma. Empirical anti-Toxoplasma trial (Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine) for 2 weeks with imaging at 14 days is the standard Indian ART programme approach — improvement confirms Toxoplasma; no improvement mandates biopsy.
diseases.important_notes:
Co-trimoxazole (Septran DS) Must Be Given to All HIV Patients at CD4 <200 — It Prevents Three Killers at Once: Co-trimoxazole prophylaxis at CD4 <200 simultaneously prevents (1) Cerebral Toxoplasmosis, (2) Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia, and (3) Isospora/Cystoisospora intestinal disease. This is one of the most cost-effective interventions in HIV medicine — one cheap tablet daily prevents multiple potentially fatal OIs. Adherence to Co-trimoxazole prophylaxis must be emphasised at every ART clinic visit. NACO ART guidelines mandate this; many patients discontinue it prematurely after feeling well on ART.

Empirical Treatment Trial — Do Not Wait for Definitive Diagnosis in Cerebral Tox: In an HIV-positive patient with CD4 <100 and multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions on CT, START Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine (or Co-trimoxazole) immediately without waiting for brain biopsy or CSF PCR results. Delay while awaiting diagnostics can result in herniation and death. Proceed empirically; if there is clinical AND radiological improvement at 2 weeks, the diagnosis of cerebral Tox is confirmed. Only perform brain biopsy if there is NO response at 2 weeks — to exclude CNS lymphoma or TB.

Leucovorin Is Not Optional — Bone Marrow Suppression Can Be Fatal Without It: Pyrimethamine is a potent dihydrofolate reductase inhibitor — it depletes folate stores and causes severe bone marrow suppression (neutropaenia, thrombocytopaenia, anaemia) particularly in HIV patients whose marrow is already compromised. Leucovorin (folinic acid) 10–20mg/day must be co-prescribed throughout the entire Pyrimethamine course AND prophylaxis period. Prescribing Pyrimethamine without Leucovorin is a serious prescribing error. Note: use FOLINIC acid (Leucovorin) — NOT folic acid — as folic acid may antagonise the antiparasitic effect.

Ocular Toxoplasmosis — Never Use Steroids Alone: A common and dangerous error in managing Toxoplasma chorioretinitis: prescribing ocular or systemic corticosteroids for uveitis WITHOUT antiparasitic cover. In Toxoplasma retinochoroiditis, steroids alone suppress the immune response to the parasite, allowing unrestricted retinal replication — causing massive irreversible retinal destruction and vision loss. Anti-Tox therapy (Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine) must ALWAYS be started before or simultaneously with any steroid for ocular Tox.
confirmation_tests_curated: Toxoplasma IgM ELISA (Serum): Marks acute/recent primary infection — rises within 1–2 weeks of infection, persists 6–9 months. Positive IgM in a pregnant woman/immunocompetent adult = acute infection diagnosis requiring confirmation.

Toxoplasma IgG ELISA (Serum): Marks past infection/chronic carriage — positive in ≥90% of cerebral Tox cases (reactivation). Negative IgG in a suspected cerebral Tox case strongly argues against the diagnosis (consider alternative).

IgG Avidity Test: Distinguishes recent from remote primary infection in pregnant women — high avidity = infection >3 months ago (low foetal risk); low avidity = recent primary infection (high foetal transmission risk). Essential for antenatal Toxoplasma IgG+/IgM equivocal interpretation.

diseases.name: Hydatid Disease
diseases.tests:
1. Ultrasound Abdomen (Primary Diagnostic Tool)

Abdominal Ultrasound — WHO-IWGE Classification: Most important first-line investigation. Characteristic findings: (CE1) Simple unilocular cyst with 'snowstorm' sign (cyst sand); (CE2) Multivesicular — 'honeycomb' appearance (daughter cysts); (CE3) Daughter cysts + detached membrane; (CE4) Heterogeneous with calcification; (CE5) Heavily calcified inactive cyst. Cyst stage guides treatment. Pathognomonic findings: 'water lily sign' (detached membranes floating in cyst fluid) and 'double-arch sign'.

2. CT Scan Abdomen/Chest

CT Abdomen with Contrast: Defines cyst number, size, location, relationship to biliary/vascular structures — essential pre-operative planning. Detects calcification and daughter cysts. CT chest for pulmonary hydatid.

3. Serology

Echinococcus IgG ELISA (Serum): Most widely used — sensitivity 64–90% for hepatic hydatid; lower (50–60%) for pulmonary and other locations. Cross-reactive with Taenia, Toxocara. Positive ELISA + compatible US/CT = confirms diagnosis.

Indirect Haemagglutination Assay (IHA): Widely available in India — operational test for field/district settings. IHA titre ≥1:256 = significantfor hydatid diagnosis.

Western Blot / Immunoblot (Arc5 and B epitopes): Most specific confirmatory test — Arc5 band = pathognomonic for Echinococcus granulosus. Available at AIIMS, PGIMER.

4. Intradermal Casoni Test: Historical — no longer recommended due to marked false-positive rate. Replaced by ELISA/IHA.

5. Histopathology (Surgical or PAIR Specimen)

Laminated Membrane + Protoscolices in Specimen: Pathognomonic on histology — PAS-positive laminated eosinophilic membrane + hooklets + scolices. Confirms diagnosis if serology negative.

6. CXR and CT Chest (Pulmonary Hydatid)

CXR: Round, well-defined opacity — 'coin lesion' appearance. Signs of rupture: 'water lily sign' (collapsed membrane floating in air fluid level), 'meniscus sign' (air between outer cyst and pericyst).

7. Blood Count

CBC — Eosinophilia: Peripheral blood eosinophilia (>500 cells/µl) present in 20–34% of hydatid cases — non-specific but supportive of parasitic diagnosis.

8. Indian Surgical Guidelines

IAGES Hydatid Disease management protocol: PAIR for uncomplicated hepatic CE1/CE3a; surgery for CE2/CE3b/CE4/CE5 and pulmonary/complicated cysts. Perioperative Albendazole mandatory.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Antiparasitic (Pericidal Drug — Mandatory Pre- and Post-Operatively)

Albendazole 400mg twice daily with fatty meal (PAIR / Surgery): Perioperative use: Started 3–7 days before PAIR/surgery (renders protoscolices non-viable) and continued 28 days post-procedure. Also used as sole therapy for CE4/CE5 inactive cysts (observation + Albendazole) and in inoperable cases.

Albendazole 400mg twice daily in 3-month cycles (4-week on, 2-week off): Primary medical treatment for small CE1 cysts <5cm or inoperable hydatid — 3 cycle minimum; up to 12 months. Monitor LFTs every 2 weeks — hepatotoxicity is a major side effect especially for long treatment cycles.

2. PAIR Procedure (Minimally Invasive — First-Line for Hepatic CE1/CE3a)

PAIR Protocol: Percutaneous puncture (US-guided) → Aspiration of cyst content → Injection of scolicidal agent (20% hypertonic saline or 95% ethanol) for 15 minutes → Reaspiration. Performed with Albendazole cover. WHO-endorsed as first-line for uncomplicated hepatic hydatid. Less morbidity and shorter hospital stay than surgery.

Hypertonic Saline 20% (Scolicidal Agent): Intraoperative injection into cyst cavity to kill protoscolices before incision and prevent peritoneal seeding. Also used in PAIR procedures. MUST avoid spillage into bile ducts (hyperosmolar cholangitis risk).

3. Surgical (Complex/Pulmonary/Complicated Cysts)

Surgical Cystectomy / Subtotal Pericystectomy / Liver Resection: For CE2 (multivesicular), CE3b, complicated (biliary communication, pulmonary, cardiac, bone) cysts. Surgical techniques: total cystectomy (removes entire cyst + pericyst), subtotal pericystectomy (leaves part of pericyst), liver resection (for deeply embedded cysts). Pulmonary hydatid: cystotomy + capitonnage or pulmonary resection.

4. Anaphylaxis Management (Cyst Rupture Emergency)

Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 0.5mg IM: Immediate first-line for anaphylaxis from cyst rupture — IM antero-lateral thigh. Life-saving.

Chlorpheniramine 10mg IV + Hydrocortisone 200mg IV: Adjuncts to Adrenaline for anaphylaxis management.

5. Post-Procedure Monitoring

LFT Monitoring every 2 weeks during Albendazole: Hepatotoxicity is the major Albendazole complication — elevated transaminases (>5x ULN) requires drug interruption. Restart after normalisation.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMPORTANT — Hydatid Disease Requires Medical and/or Surgical Treatment: Echinococcal cysts do not regress spontaneously and carry life-threatening anaphylaxis rupture risk. Ayurvedic medicines are supportive adjuncts — primarily for immune strengthening and hepatoprotection during Albendazole therapy. No Ayurvedic formulation kills Echinococcus or substitutes for Albendazole/surgery.

Arogyavardhini Vati: Classical Ayurvedic hepatoprotective — supports liver function and hepatocyte regeneration during prolonged Albendazole therapy and post-surgery liver regeneration. 250–500mg twice daily after food under Vaidya guidance. Well-studied hepatoprotective Ayurvedic formulation.

Kutki (Picrorhiza kurroa) Tablets: Most potent Ayurvedic hepatoprotective herb — documented hepatocyte protective and regenerative activity. 250–500mg twice daily. Supports liver during long-cycle Albendazole hepatotoxicity risk. Available as Himalaya Livfit/Picrorhiza.

Phyllanthus niruri (Bhumyamalaki) Extract: Hepatoprotective and antioxidant — used for liver disease of all aetiologies in Ayurveda. 500mg standardised extract twice daily. Evidence-based hepatoprotection supporting LFT normalisation.

Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa): Hepatomegaly and oedema reduction — Punarnava decoction 30ml twice daily for hepatic hydatid post-PAIR/surgery oedema management. Traditional hepatoprotective.

Guduchi Satva (Tinospora cordifolia Extracted Starch): Immunomodulatory rasayana — supports immune defence against Echinococcus and recovery post-surgery. 500mg twice daily.

Ashwagandha Extract: Adaptogenic — post-surgical recovery, fatigue management, immune support. 600mg extract twice daily.

Triphala Churna: Gentle bowel regulariser and antioxidant — during prolonged Albendazole therapy. 5g at bedtime with warm water.
diseases.india_specific:
India's Epidemiology — Rajasthan, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, J&K, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh: Cystic echinococcosis is hyperendemic in pastoral states of India — particularly Rajasthan, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu & Kashmir (sheep-rearing), and some parts of South India (Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh). Hospital series from Rajasthan show hydatid as the most common surgical hepatic cystic lesion.

Dog-Sheep Lifecycle and Rural Transmission: Echinococcus granulosus lifecycle: (1) Adult tapeworm in dog intestine → eggs in dog faeces contaminating pasture, soil, water; (2) Sheep/cattle/humans ingest eggs → oncospheres hatch in intestine → bloodstream → liver/lung cyst development. Dogs fed infected sheep offal (liver, lungs) = lifecycle completion. Dog deworming programmes and prohibition of feeding raw offal to dogs are primary prevention.

Difficulty Accessing Surgical Care: Complex hydatid surgery (e.g. subtotal pericystectomy, liver resection) requires tertiary surgical expertise and long-term follow-up — not accessible to most hydatid patients in endemic pastoral areas. PAIR (Puncture, Aspiration, Injection, Reaspiration) under expert guidance is more accessible and less resource-intensive.

Misdiagnosis as Liver Cyst or Abscess: Hepatic hydatid cysts are frequently misdiagnosed as 'simple liver cysts' (which require no treatment) or amoebic/pyogenic liver abscesses on ultrasound at primary care. Aspiration of a misidentified hydatid cyst without Albendazole cover and anaphylaxis preparedness can be life-threatening — serology + expert review before any liver cyst aspiration is essential.
diseases.important_notes:
Never Aspirate a Liver Cyst Without Excluding Hydatid First — Anaphylaxis Risk: The most dangerous clinical error in managing unknown liver cysts in India: blind aspiration of a hepatic cyst without first excluding Echinococcal (hydatid) aetiology by serology and careful ultrasound. Aspiration of a hydatid cyst without anaphylaxis preparation and Albendazole pre-treatment can cause fatal anaphylaxis from cyst fluid release. Before ANY liver cyst aspiration: perform Echinococcus IgG ELISA + expert ultrasound review of cyst morphology. If hydatid cannot be excluded, refer for surgical/PAIR evaluation with full anaphylaxis preparedness.

Albendazole Must Be Started Before PAIR and Surgery — It Makes Protoscolices Non-Viable: Perioperative Albendazole (400mg BD starting 3–7 days before procedure) renders protoscolices non-viable within the cyst — dramatically reducing the risk of secondary seeding if cyst fluid spills during surgery or PAIR. Starting Albendazole post-procedure retroactively has significantly lower efficacy. This pre-operative protocol is mandatory and must not be omitted due to time pressure — delay the procedure by a few days if Albendazole has not been started.

Hepatotoxicity is the Major Albendazole Side Effect — LFT Monitoring Every 2 Weeks is Non-Negotiable: Long-cycle Albendazole therapy (28-day cycles with 14-day gaps) causes significant hepatotoxicity in up to 15% of patients — elevated ALT/AST often asymptomatic until severe. Every patient on Albendazole for hydatid disease MUST have LFTs checked every 2 weeks. Stop immediately if AST/ALT >5x ULN. Restart at lower dose or switch monitoring frequency after normalisation. Failure to monitor LFTs has led to severe drug-induced liver injury in Indian patients on prescribed Albendazole.

WHO-IWGE Ultrasound Classification Should Guide Treatment — Not All Cysts Need Surgery: CE4 and CE5 cysts (calcified, inactive) require observation and Albendazole monitoring only — surgery is unnecessary and carries risk. CE1 and CE3a (simple unilocular, uncomplicated) are best managed by PAIR + Albendazole — less morbid than surgery. CE2 and CE3b (complex, multivesicular) and pulmonary/cardiac hydatid require surgical referral. Knowing the WHO cyst classification prevents both under-treatment (missing active cysts) and over-treatment (unnecessary surgery on inactive cysts).
confirmation_tests_curated: Abdominal Ultrasound — WHO-IWGE Classification: Most important first-line investigation. Characteristic findings: (CE1) Simple unilocular cyst with 'snowstorm' sign (cyst sand); (CE2) Multivesicular — 'honeycomb' appearance (daughter cysts); (CE3) Daughter cysts + detached membrane; (CE4) Heterogeneous with calcification; (CE5) Heavily calcified inactive cyst. Cyst stage guides treatment. Pathognomonic findings: 'water lily sign' (detached membranes floating in cyst fluid) and 'double-arch sign'.

CT Abdomen with Contrast: Defines cyst number, size, location, relationship to biliary/vascular structures — essential pre-operative planning. Detects calcification and daughter cysts. CT chest for pulmonary hydatid.

diseases.name: Strongyloidiasis
diseases.tests:
1. Stool Examination (Microscopy — Limited Sensitivity)

Stool Wet Mount Microscopy — Rhabditiform Larvae (L1): Fresh stool (must examine within 30 minutes — larvae actively motile) — rhabditiform L1 larvae with short buccal cavity. Low sensitivity (25–50% for single stool) — 3 concentrated stool samples with repeat testing required.

Baermann Technique: Concentrates Strongyloides larvae by migration through warm water from fresh stool — sensitivity higher than direct smear. Stool placed in warm water funnel; larvae migrate to bottom and sediment for microscopy.

Koga Agar Plate Culture: Semi-quantitative culture method — stool on agar plate, Strongyloides larvae migrate creating characteristic tracks visible after 2–3 days. More sensitive than direct microscopy. Available at reference labs.

2. Serology (Most Sensitive for Chronic Infection)

Strongyloides stercoralis IgG ELISA (Serum): Most sensitive screening test for chronic strongyloidiasis — sensitivity 85–95%. Cross-reactive with Filaria and Toxocara in India (common co-endemic areas). Positive ELISA in India = strongly indicates Strongyloides infection even in absence of larvae on stool.

3. Duodenal Aspirate / Jejunal Biopsy

Duodenal Aspirate or Enteric Capsule (Enterotest): For detection when stool negative — rhabditiform larvae in duodenal aspirate. Jejunal biopsy shows larvae in mucosa with eosinophilic enteritis. Most sensitive but invasive.

4. Blood Investigations

CBC — Peripheral Eosinophilia: Eosinophilia (500–3000 cells/µl) present in 60–70% of strongyloidiasis. Absence of eosinophilia in immunosuppressed patients with hyperinfection (steroids suppress eosinophilia) is a poor prognostic sign.

5. BAL / Sputum (Hyperinfection)

BAL or Sputum Microscopy — Filariform Larvae (L3): Filariform L3 larvae in BAL = hyperinfection/dissemination — indicating massive pulmonary migration. Urgent treatment initiation.

6. CSF Analysis (Disseminated with CNS Involvement)

CSF Microscopy: Strongyloides larvae in CSF wet preparation on lumbar puncture — with eosinophilic pleocytosis. Diagnosis of CNS dissemination.

7. CT Chest

CT Thorax: Bilaterally patchy ground-glass opacities in hyperinfection — diffuse interstitial pattern from migrating larvae and secondary infection.

8. Indian Treatment Guidelines

ICMR / WHO NTD Guidelines for Strongyloides: Ivermectin is superior to Albendazole — first-line recommended treatment. Screen immunosuppressed patients before steroids. Hyperinfection = prolonged/repeated Ivermectin courses.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Treatment

Ivermectin 200mcg/kg/day orally for 2 days (on empty stomach): First-line and most effective treatment for all forms of strongyloidiasis. Single/2-day course achieves cure in 85–95% of uncomplicated cases. Must be taken on empty stomach (30 min before food) for maximum absorption. Repeat after 2 weeks for immunocompromised patients.

Ivermectin 200mcg/kg/day for 7 days or longer (Hyperinfection/Disseminated): Extended course for hyperinfection — continue until 2 consecutive negative stool examinations. Some guidelines recommend 2-weekly courses until stool-negative for immunosuppressed patients.

2. Alternative / Second-Line Treatment

Albendazole 400mg twice daily for 7 days: Less effective than Ivermectin for Strongyloides (70–80% cure rate) — prescribed where Ivermectin unavailable. Extend to 14 days in hyperinfection.

3. Prophylaxis (Before Immunosuppression)

Ivermectin 200mcg/kg single dose (Pre-Steroid / Pre-Transplant Screening Protocol): For patients from endemic areas or positive Strongyloides IgG starting long-term corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, or organ transplant — single pre-treatment course clears Strongyloides and prevents hyperinfection.

4. Adjunctive (Hyperinfection)

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics (Pipercillin-Tazobactam / Meropenem / Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole): For Gram-negative sepsis complicating hyperinfection — larvae carry enteric bacteria through gut wall. IV antibiotics mandatory in hyperinfection with fever/sepsis features.

ORS / IV Fluids: Electrolyte replacement for diarrhoea and paralytic ileus in heavy infection and hyperinfection.

5. HTLV-1 Co-Infected Patients

Ivermectin — Extended and Repeated Cycles (Monthly, Long-Term): HTLV-1 co-infected patients cannot control Strongyloides autoinfection immunologically — require long-term intermittent Ivermectin maintenance. Monthly single dose Ivermectin indefinitely in HTLV-1 + Strongyloides co-infection.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Ivermectin 6mg Tablets (4 tabs): First-line treatment for strongyloidiasis. PMBJP supplies; Government NTD programme.

Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Second-line alternative where Ivermectin unavailable. Widely available PMBJP.

ORS Sachets: Symptomatic hydration management. Available at all health centres.

Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Adjunct antibacterial for enteric bacterial co-infection in hyperinfection.

Ceftriaxone 1g Injection (1 vial): IV sepsis management in hyperinfection syndrome.

Vitamin B Complex Tablets (10 tabs): Nutritional support during malabsorption from intestinal strongyloidiasis.
