diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder: Most important classical Krimighna (antiparasitic) herb in Ayurveda for intestinal worm infections — activity against nematodes confirmed in laboratory studies. 2–3g powder with honey on empty stomach twice daily. Supportive antiparasitic role alongside Ivermectin for chronic strongyloidiasis.

Krimikuthar Ras: Classical Ayurvedic herbomineral antiparasitic Rasa formulation — Vidanga, Trikatu, Tankana (borax) combination — for Krimi (intestinal parasite) infections. 250mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance. Traditional broad-spectrum antiparasitic for helminthic infestations.

Nimba (Neem / Azadirachta indica) Oil or Leaf Decoction: Neem extract has documented in-vitro anthelmintic activity — Nimba decoction (30ml twice daily) or neem leaf capsules (500mg twice daily). Traditional Ayurvedic first-line intestinal antiseptic and antiparasitic.

Bilwadi Churna (Bael Compound): Antidiarrhoeal, astringent, and intestinal mucosal healing — bael/bilwa (Aegle marmelos) for diarrhoea management in intestinal strongyloidiasis. 3–5g twice daily with warm water.

Trikatu Churna: Digestive stimulant — Pippali (Piper longum), Maricha (black pepper), Shunthi (ginger). Enhances bioavailability of Ivermectin and improves intestinal absorption. 1g before meals twice daily in Kapha-dominant individuals with sluggish gut.

Kutaja Ghana Vati (Holarrhena antidysenterica Extract): Classical anti-dysenteric and intestinal antiparasitic — reduces diarrhoea and intestinal inflammation from Strongyloides enteritis. 500mg twice daily after food.

Guduchi Churna or Satva: Immunostimulatory rasayana — supports cellular immune response regulation in chronic strongyloidiasis and prevention of autoinfection exacerbation. 500mg twice daily.

Ashwagandha Granules: Adaptogenic and immunomodulatory — supports adrenal and immune axis during heavy Strongyloides infection with systemic symptoms; post-hyperinfection recovery support. 5g twice daily with warm milk.
diseases.india_specific:
Soil-Transmitted Helminth Endemic Zone — Tropical India: Strongyloides is prevalent throughout India's tropical belt — particularly Assam, West Bengal, Bihar, Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, and Tamil Nadu. High-risk activities: barefoot farming, walking on contaminated soil, agricultural workers, and children playing in soil contaminated with human faeces (open defecation).

HTLV-1 Co-Infection in Northeast India: Human T-cell Lymphotropic Virus type 1 (HTLV-1) is endemic in parts of Northeast India (Assam, Manipur) — HTLV-1 severely impairs the Th2-eosinophil response that controls Strongyloides autoinfection → dramatic hyperinfection risk. HTLV-1 + Strongyloides co-infection has very high hyperinfection mortality.

Corticosteroid Use in India's Chronic Disease Burden: India's large population of patients on long-term corticosteroids (nephrotic syndrome, asthma, RA, SLE, inflammatory bowel disease, organ transplant) — combined with endemic Strongyloides — creates an unrecognised hyperinfection risk. Ivermectin prophylaxis or screening before steroids is the standard of care at informed Indian tertiary centres.

Malnutrition and Immune Suppression: Protein-energy malnutrition in children (common in rural India) impairs Th2 immunity — increasing susceptibility to heavy Strongyloides infection. Malnutrition + Strongyloides hyperinfection has synergistic mortality.

Ineffective Mass Drug Administration Coverage: NTD mass drug administration (MDA) programmes using Albendazole in India cover soil-transmitted helminths — but Albendazole has POOR activity against Strongyloides (unlike Ivermectin). Strongyloides is being excluded from MDA benefit while Ascaris, Trichuris, and hookworm are controlled — contributing to underestimated Strongyloides prevalence.
diseases.important_notes:
SCREEN FOR STRONGYLOIDES BEFORE PRESCRIBING SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS — Non-Negotiable in Endemic India: The most preventable cause of death from Strongyloides is hyperinfection precipitated by corticosteroid prescription. Any patient from endemic rural tropical India who is about to start systemic corticosteroids (for any indication — asthma, RA, SLE, nephrotic syndrome, COPD, organ transplant) should have Strongyloides IgG ELISA performed. If positive → treat with Ivermectin 200mcg/kg for 2 days → then confirm negative stool before starting steroids. This screening protocol is standard at AIIMS and CMC Vellore; it must become universal at all tertiary centres. The cost of Ivermectin is negligible compared to the cost of managing hyperinfection ICU admission.

Mass Drug Administration with Albendazole Does NOT Cover Strongyloides — Ivermectin is Required: India's national NTD mass drug administration programme uses Albendazole for soil-transmitted helminth control. Albendazole effectively reduces Ascaris, Trichuris, and hookworm — but has POOR efficacy against Strongyloides (cure rate only 30–60% with Albendazole vs 85–95% with Ivermectin). In Strongyloides-endemic areas, Ivermectin should be incorporated into MDA rounds. Physicians should not assume MDA-treated patients are free of Strongyloides.

Eosinophilia with GI Symptoms in an Indian Patient = Stool Examination for Parasites First: High eosinophilia (>1000 cells/µl) + epigastric pain + skin itching in a patient from tropical rural India = parasitic helminthiasis until proven otherwise. Strongyloides, Ascaris, hookworm, Toxocara, and Filaria must all be evaluated before attributing eosinophilia to allergy, asthma, or idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome. Three stool examinations + Strongyloides ELISA + filaria card test = comprehensive initial screen. Treating eosinophilia with corticosteroids (e.g. for presumed hypereosinophilic syndrome) without excluding Strongyloides is dangerous.

Cure Must Be Confirmed — Follow-Up Stool Examination Two Weeks After Treatment is Mandatory: Unlike many other parasite treatments where clinical response is sufficient, Strongyloides cure MUST be confirmed by follow-up stool examination (2 weeks post-Ivermectin) due to the autoinfection cycle. Larvae persisting in the colon will reinfect in the absence of confirmed clearance — particularly dangerous if the patient undergoes immunosuppression. Repeat Ivermectin at 2-week interval if stool positive on follow-up. Documentation of stool-negative clearance is the only assurance of safe subsequent immunosuppression.
confirmation_tests_curated: Stool Wet Mount Microscopy — Rhabditiform Larvae (L1): Fresh stool (must examine within 30 minutes — larvae actively motile) — rhabditiform L1 larvae with short buccal cavity. Low sensitivity (25–50% for single stool) — 3 concentrated stool samples with repeat testing required.

Baermann Technique: Concentrates Strongyloides larvae by migration through warm water from fresh stool — sensitivity higher than direct smear. Stool placed in warm water funnel; larvae migrate to bottom and sediment for microscopy.

Koga Agar Plate Culture: Semi-quantitative culture method — stool on agar plate, Strongyloides larvae migrate creating characteristic tracks visible after 2–3 days. More sensitive than direct microscopy. Available at reference labs.

diseases.name: Enterobiasis
diseases.tests:
1. Scotch Tape / Cellophane Tape Test (Primary Diagnostic Test — Extremely Simple)

Perianal Scotch Tape Test (Graham's Test): Clear adhesive tape pressed firmly on perianal skin immediately on waking (BEFORE bathing or defecation) — mounted on glass slide — examined under low-power microscopy. Enterobius eggs = ovoid, flattened on one side, 50–60µm. Single test sensitivity 50–60%; 3 consecutive morning tests = 90%+ sensitivity. This is one of the easiest diagnostic tests in medicine — should be performed in any child with nocturnal perianal pruritus.

2. Stool Examination (Low Sensitivity — Not Recommended as Primary Test)

Stool Microscopy: Eggs found in <5–15% of infected patients. Not recommended as the primary test because female worms deposit eggs on perianal skin, NOT inside the colon. Stool exam may detect eggs only incidentally.

3. Direct Worm Visualisation (Often Diagnostic Without Laboratory Test)

Inspection of Perianal Skin at Night (9–11 PM) with Torchlight: Parent inspects perianal region of sleeping child with torch 2–3 hours after sleep onset — white, actively moving 8–13mm threadworms visible. Pathognomonic diagnosis without any laboratory test.

4. Blood Count

CBC — Eosinophilia: Mild or absent eosinophilia — Enterobius does NOT typically cause significant blood eosinophilia (unlike tissue-invasive helminths). Presence of significant eosinophilia with Enterobius = consider additional helminths.

5. Clinical Diagnosis (In Practice — Most Common Approach)

Clinical Diagnosis: In children with classic nocturnal perianal pruritus + sleep disturbance in an Indian urban or mixed urban-rural setting — clinical diagnosis is entirely appropriate, and empirical family-wide treatment is both safe and cost-effective. Diagnostic testing reserved for atypical presentations or treatment failure.

6. Gynaecological Evaluation (Girls with Vulvovaginitis)

Vaginal Swab + Microscopy: For persistent vulvovaginitis — worm/egg identification on swab or periurethral inspection. Exclude other causes of vulvovaginitis in parallel.

7. No Serology Available: There is no validated commercial serological test for Enterobius — diagnosis relies on tape test, direct inspection, and clinical assessment.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. First-Line Treatment (Virtually 100% Effective)

Mebendazole 100mg single oral dose — repeat after 2 weeks: First-line treatment; chewable tablet available. Single dose kills adult worms — repeat dose at 2 weeks kills worms from eggs that hatched after initial treatment (eggs unaffected by Mebendazole). TREAT ALL HOUSEHOLD MEMBERS SIMULTANEOUSLY on both dose days.

Albendazole 400mg single oral dose — repeat after 2 weeks: Equally effective alternative — same 2-dose protocol with 2-week interval. ENTIRE HOUSEHOLD simultaneously. Takes with or without food. Both doses mandatory for complete eradication.

2. Repeat Treatment Course (For Persistent Perianal Pruritus After Initial Treatment)

Mebendazole 100mg — third dose 2 weeks after second dose: If symptoms persist after 2-dose course, re-examine with tape test; if positive, third dose and enhanced hygiene measures. Consider re-infection from school or household member who was not treated.

3. Topical Symptomatic Relief

Zinc Oxide Ointment / Calamine Lotion — Perianal Application: Topical antipruritic for overnight perianal application — reduces scratching and secondary bacterial infection from excoriation. Non-medicated approach to break the scratch → egg-transfer → autoinfection cycle.

Mild Antihistamine (Cetirizine 5–10mg at Bedtime): For severe nocturnal pruritus disrupting sleep — short-course antihistamine allows undisturbed sleep while drug treatment is working. Not a treatment for Enterobius — symptomatic adjunct only.

4. No IV/Hospital Treatment Required: Enterobiasis is entirely managed in the community. No hospital admission, parenteral treatment, or specialist referral required in uncomplicated cases.

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder: Classical primary Krimighna (antiparasitic/anthelmintic) herb — Vidanga for Krimi (intestinal worms) in children is described extensively in Charaka and Sushruta Samhita. 1–2g powder with honey on empty stomach twice daily for 7 days. Activity against Enterobius documented in preliminary studies. Well-tolerated in children — primary Ayurvedic antiparasitic for childhood enterobiasis.

Krimikuthar Ras: Classical Ayurvedic compound rasa formulation for intestinal parasitic infections — Vidanga base with Trikatu, Tankana (borax), and metallic-herbal combination. 125–250mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance for children >5 years. Traditional staple for school-age Krimi management in Ayurvedic practice.

Palasha Beeja (Butea monosperma / Flame of Forest Seeds) Powder: Traditional anthelmintic for roundworm and pinworm — 2–3g powder with warm water on empty stomach. Available at Ayurvedic pharmacies and traditional Vaidyas.

Shatpushpa + Vidanga Churna Compound: Combined carminative and antiparasitic — reduces intestinal cramping and expels worms. Traditional household preparation for children in Western India.

Nimba (Neem) Leaf Powder: Internal antiparasitic and antimicrobial — 1–2g neem leaf powder with honey in children twice daily for 5 days. External use: dilute neem oil for perianal application to reduce egg viability on skin.

Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) Churna: Mild laxative and bowel-cleansing agent — helps eliminate worms from large intestine. 3g with warm water at bedtime for adults; 1g with honey for children. Tridoshic bowel regulator.

Hingwashtak Churna: Carminative formula (Hing/Asafoetida based) — reduces abdominal colic, flatulence, and bowel cramping associated with enterobiasis. 3–5g with warm water before meals.

Bala (Sida cordifolia) Syrup or Gripe Water Compound: Paediatric gut tonic used in children — reduces night restlessness and improves sleep quality as symptomatic adjunct. Available as commercial paediatric Ayurvedic syrup.
diseases.india_specific:
Urban Prevalence Despite Better Sanitation: Unlike most soil-transmitted helminths (Ascaris, hookworm) which are Rural predominant, Enterobius thrives in URBAN India — transmission does not require soil contact. Crowded apartments, shared bathrooms, domestic helpers, and school environments sustain urban transmission. Enterobius is India's most democratic parasite — infects children across all socioeconomic classes.

Boarding Schools and Madrasas: India's many residential schools, hostels, and madrasas are high-density Enterobius amplification environments. Periodic whole-school treatment rounds are extremely effective and should be part of school health programmes.

Misdiagnosis as Behavioural Problem: Nocturnal restlessness, night crying, and hyperirritability from sleep-disrupted Enterobius are frequently misdiagnosed as ADHD, night terrors, or behavioural problems — Enterobius excluded, child referred to psychiatrist, while the parasite continues undisturbed. A single Scotch tape test would have resolved the question in 5 minutes.

Re-infection Rate Without Environmental Decontamination: Treatment with Mebendazole/Albendazole is virtually 100% effective — but re-infection from environmental egg contamination (eggs survive on surfaces for up to 3 weeks) is extremely common if environmental hygiene measures are not implemented simultaneously with drug treatment.

Appendiceal Enterobiasis: Enterobius in the appendix is a common incidental finding at appendicectomy — not a cause of appendicitis. However, eosinophilic appendicitis triggered by Enterobius is described. Any appendicectomy specimen from India should be routinely examined histologically for Enterobius.
diseases.important_notes:
Treat the ENTIRE Household Simultaneously — Treating One Person is Never Enough: The single most important management principle for enterobiasis: Enterobius spreads so efficiently within a household that treating one infected child while leaving household members untreated results in almost certain re-infection from the untreated reservoir. ALL household members — regardless of whether they have symptoms — must receive Mebendazole 100mg (or Albendazole 400mg) on the same day, AND repeat the dose together after 2 weeks. Failure to treat the whole household is by far the most common reason for 'treatment failure' in Indian clinical practice.

Environmental Decontamination is Essential — Eggs Survive 3 Weeks on Surfaces: Mebendazole kills adult worms but has no effect on eggs already deposited in the environment. Eggs on bed linen, underwear, toilet seats, and door handles are the re-infection source. On the day of treatment: (1) Wash all bed linen, pyjamas, and underwear in hot water >60°C; (2) Vacuum or wipe all bedroom surfaces including mattress; (3) Clean bathroom/toilet surfaces with disinfectant; (4) Ensure all household members wash fingernails with brush daily for 2 weeks. Without these hygiene measures, drug treatment alone has high re-infection rates.

Scotch Tape Test — The Simplest, Cheapest Diagnostic Test Should Be Used More in India: Indian primary care physicians rarely perform the Scotch Tape (Graham's) test despite its extreme simplicity, negligible cost (clear adhesive tape + glass slide), and high diagnostic yield. Any child presenting with nocturnal perianal itching or vulvovaginitis should have a tape test before referral or specialist workup. The test can be performed by parents at home (tape pressed to perianal skin on waking, brought to clinic on glass slide). Expanding tape test use at PHC and primary school health level would dramatically improve detection of this massively prevalent infection.

Pinworm Does Not Cause Major Nutritional Deficiency — Reassure Parents: Unlike Ascaris (which causes malnutrition) or hookworm (which causes iron-deficiency anaemia), Enterobius vermicularis causes minimal metabolic harm. Its main impact is sleep quality and secondary behavioural effects — it does NOT cause severe disease. Parents alarmed by worm sightings should be reassured that their child will suffer no serious harm, that treatment is simple and completely effective, and that seeing worms is diagnostically helpful. Excessive parental alarm leads to over-investigation and unnecessary investigations in a completely manageable condition.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Scotch Tape / Cellophane Tape Test (Primary Diagnostic Test — Extremely Simple)

Perianal Scotch Tape Test (Graham's Test): Clear adhesive tape pressed firmly on perianal skin immediately on waking (BEFORE bathing or defecation) — mounted on glass slide — examined under low-power microscopy. Enterobius eggs = ovoid, flattened on one side, 50–60µm. Single test sensitivity 50–60%; 3 consecutive morning tests = 90%+ sensitivity. This is one of the easiest diagnostic tests in medicine — should be performed in any child with nocturnal perianal pruritus.
2. Stool Examination (Low Sensitivity — Not Recommended as Primary Test)

Stool Microscopy: Eggs found in <5–15% of infected patients. Not recommended as the primary test because female worms deposit eggs on perianal skin, NOT inside the colon. Stool exam may detect eggs only incidentally.
3. Direct Worm Visualisation (Often Diagnostic Without Laboratory Test)

diseases.name: Cryptosporidiosis
diseases.tests:
1. Stool Microscopy — Modified Ziehl-Neelsen (Kinyoun) Stain (Primary Diagnostic Test)

Modified AFB (Cold Kinyoun) Staining of Stool: Brilliant red/pink 4–6µm round oocysts against blue/green background — PATHOGNOMONIC appearance. Most cost-effective and widely available test for Cryptosporidiosis. Should be requested as 'modified ZN / Kinyoun stain for Cryptosporidium' — NOT routine stool AFB (which uses hot Ziehl-Neelsen and is too hot for oocysts). Available at all medical college labs.

Auramine-Rhodamine Fluorescence Microscopy: More sensitive than Kinyoun for oocyst detection — fluorescent yellow-green oocysts. Available at reference labs; higher throughput for large-volume screening.

2. Antigen Detection (ELISA / RDT — More Sensitive than Microscopy)

Cryptosporidium Stool Antigen ELISA or Lateral Flow RDT: Sensitivity 90–99%; specificity 99%. Superior to microscopy for low-burden infections. Detects both C. parvum and C. hominis. Commercially available rapid test kits (TechLab, IDEXX, others). Available at AIIMS, CMC, PGIMER, and increasingly major private labs.

3. Molecular Diagnosis (PCR — Reference Standard)

Stool Cryptosporidium PCR: Most sensitive and typing-capable method — differentiates C. parvum (zoonotic) from C. hominis (human-only) for outbreak investigation. Not routinely required clinically but valuable for epidemiological investigation.

4. Intestinal Biopsy (For Atypical/Refractory Cases)

Duodenal/Jejunal Biopsy Histopathology: Comma-shaped intracellular parasites on luminal surface of enterocytes on H&E/PAS — typically at the brush border. For refractory or atypical cases where diagnosis remains uncertain.

5. Blood Tests

CD4 Count (HIV-Associated): Essential in all Cryptosporidiosis confirmed cases — CD4 <50 = severe HIV-associated crypto; immediate ART optimisation required.

Electrolytes, BUN/Creatinine, Albumin: Assess dehydration severity, electrolyte disturbance, and nutritional status.

6. MRCP / ERCP (Biliary Cryptosporidiosis)

MRCP: Non-invasive assessment of biliary strictures — beaded irregular ducts, intrahepatic biliary dilatation in biliary cryptosporidiosis.

7. Water Quality Testing (Outbreak Investigation)

Environmental Oocyst Detection in Water: Immunofluorescence/PCR testing of municipal water supply for Cryptosporidium oocysts during outbreak investigation. Performed by environmental health departments.

8. Indian Diagnostic Guidelines

NACO HIV OI Diagnosis Protocol: Modified AFB/Kinyoun stool stain for Cryptosporidium in all HIV-positive patients with watery diarrhoea lasting >7 days. Antigen test if Kinyoun negative with high clinical suspicion.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Treatment in Immunocompetent Adults and Children

Nitazoxanide 500mg twice daily for 3 days (Adults): Most effective anti-Cryptosporidium drug for immunocompetent individuals — 80–90% clinical cure rate. Take with food for maximum absorption.

Nitazoxanide 100mg twice daily for 3 days (Children 1–3 years); 200mg twice daily (4–11 years): Paediatric-dosed Nitazoxanide oral suspension — effective for childhood cryptosporidiosis including malnourished children in Indian endemic settings.

2. Oral Rehydration — Most Important Intervention at All Severity Levels

ORS (WHO Low-Osmolarity Formula) — Maintain Hydration: ORS is the cornerstone of management across all severity levels — prevents dehydration-related mortality in children. IV access and IV fluids when unable to maintain ORS. Zinc 20mg/day for 14 days (children) alongside ORS per IMNCI.

3. HIV-Associated Cryptosporidiosis — ART is the Only Definitive Treatment

Antiretroviral Therapy (ART) — Immediate Optimisation: In HIV-positive patients with Cryptosporidiosis, raising CD4 above 100 cells/µl is the ONLY reliable treatment. Nitazoxanide has limited/no efficacy in those with CD4 <50. ART optimisation is the primary intervention — clinical cure of cryptosporidiosis follows immune reconstitution.

Nitazoxanide 500mg twice daily (adjunct in HIV — CD4 50–200): Modest benefit as adjunct to ART in HIV patients with moderate immune function. Not effective as monotherapy with CD4 <50.

4. Nutritional Support (Critical for Children)

Zinc Sulphate 20mg/day for 14 days (Children — IMNCI Protocol): Reduces diarrhoea severity and duration — essential co-prescription with ORS in all under-5 cryptosporidiosis cases.

High-Calorie Nutritional Rehabilitation: Protein-calorie nutritional support during recovery — RTUF (Ready to Use Therapeutic Food) for severe acute malnutrition with cryptosporidiosis.

5. Biliary Cryptosporidiosis

ERCP + Biliary Stenting: For biliary strictures with obstructive jaundice — endoscopic biliary drainage. Definitive treatment remains ART-driven immune reconstitution.

Ursodeoxycholic Acid 300mg twice daily: Supportive biliary agent for HIV cholangiopathy management.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Nitazoxanide 500mg Tablets (6 tabs): Primary treatment for cryptosporidiosis in immunocompetent patients. PMBJP listed.

Nitazoxanide 200mg/5ml Oral Suspension (30ml): Paediatric formulation for childhood cryptosporidiosis.

ORS Sachets (WHO Formula): Core hydration management — widely available at all government health centres.

Zinc Sulphate 20mg Dispersible Tablets (14 tabs): Mandatory co-therapy with ORS for under-5 diarrhoea per IMNCI.

Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): For co-infection with Giardia or amoeba (commonly co-detected in Indian childhood diarrhoea).

IV Normal Saline 500ml: For moderate-severe dehydration where ORS cannot be used.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Nitazoxanide 500mg (6 tablets): Rs.68

Nitazoxanide 200mg/5ml suspension (30ml): Rs.45

ORS Sachet (1 sachet, 1L preparation): Rs.3

Zinc Sulphate 20mg (14 dispersible tablets): Rs.18

Metronidazole 400mg (10 tablets): Rs.10

IV Normal Saline 500ml: Rs.42
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Kutajarishta (Holarrhena antidysenterica Fermented Decoction): Most relevant classical Ayurvedic formulation for Atisara (diarrhoea) and intestinal infections — Kutaja alkaloids have direct in-vitro antiprotozoal and antibacterial activity. 15–25ml twice daily after food. Complementary to ORS and Nitazoxanide; particularly suitable for convalescent phase diarrhoea management and reducing relapse risk.

Bilwadi Churna (Bael / Aegle marmelos Compound): Antidiarrhoeal astringent — tannins in unripe bael fruit reduce intestinal hypersecretion and mucosal permeability. Particularly useful for watery diarrhoea and intestinal mucosal protection. 3–5g twice daily with warm water.

Ativisha (Aconitum heterophyllum) Churna: Classical paediatric Ayurvedic herb for childhood diarrhoea — Ativisha is the most important Ayurvedic herb for Bal Atisara (childhood diarrhoea) in classical texts. 125–250mg with honey for children. Under Vaidya prescription — significant activity for diarrhoeal conditions in children.

Nagarmotha (Cyperus rotundus) Decoction: Antiprotozoal, antidiarrhoeal, and carminative — reduces cramping, frequency, and gut motility dysregulation. 30ml decoction twice daily as intestinal mucosal supportive therapy.

Dadimadi Ghrita: Digestive and mucosal healing ghee-based formulation with Dadima (pomegranate) — supports intestinal epithelial repair after Cryptosporidium-induced villous atrophy. 5–10ml on empty stomach.

Ashwagandha + Shatavari Combination: Immune and nutritional rebuilding — for post-cryptosporidiosis malnutrition recovery and immune rehabilitation, particularly in HIV-positive patients on ART. 5g each with warm milk twice daily.

Triphala Churna: Gentle intestinal tonic and antioxidant — regularises bowel function post-cryptosporidiosis gastroenteritis recovery. 5g at bedtime with warm water.

Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) Decoction: Diuretic and anti-oedema — for nutritional oedema and protein-energy malnutrition recovery state post-prolonged Cryptosporidium diarrhoea. 30ml twice daily under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific:
Chlorine-Resistant Oocysts in Municipal Water: Unlike most waterborne pathogens, Cryptosporidium parvum oocysts resist standard chlorine disinfection concentrations used in Indian municipal water treatment. The only reliable household-level decontamination is boiling or WHO-certified UV water purifiers. Most Indian economic water filters (basic ceramic, sand) do NOT reliably remove Cryptosporidium — only 1-micron absolute filter or reverse osmosis is effective.

HIV Programme Gap — Cryptosporidium Not Routinely Tested: In India's NACO ART programme, Cryptosporidium is not routinely tested in HIV-positive patients with diarrhoea — empirical antibacterial treatment is the default. However, antibiotics have no effect on Cryptosporidiosis — patients receive unnecessary antibiotics while the parasite continues causing diarrhoea. Modified AFB/Kinyoun staining of stool for oocysts is cheap and efficient — should be part of every HIV diarrhoea workup.

Malnourished Under-5 Children — IMNCI Gap: IMNCI (Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness) guidelines do not specifically address Cryptosporidiosis — PHC-level management of childhood diarrhoea focuses on ORS and zinc, which are appropriate but do not address the specific nutritional rehabilitation and immune support needed for Crypto-malnutrition cycle.

Animal Handlers and Veterinary Workers — Zoonotic Risk: Indian veterinarians, dairy farmers, calf handlers, and abattoir workers have high zoonotic C. parvum exposure risk — occupational protection (gloves, hand hygiene, protective clothing) and awareness are key prevention measures in this group.
diseases.important_notes:
Cryptosporidium Oocysts are Chlorine-Resistant — Boiling is the Only Reliable Household Water Protection: This is the most clinically important public health fact about Cryptosporidiosis. India's entire municipal water chlorination infrastructure — which adequately controls most waterborne bacteria — provides NO protection against Cryptosporidium. All vulnerable populations (children under 5, HIV-positive individuals, pregnant women, immunosuppressed patients) should be advised to boil drinking water OR use a UV steriliser OR reverse osmosis filter with <1-micron absolute pore size. Basic Bisleri/bottled water manufacturers use ozone/UV treatment and ARE safe. Household clay pot filters and standard water purifiers without UV are NOT reliably effective against Cryptosporidium.

In HIV with Watery Diarrhoea — Check CD4 and Stool Kinyoun Stain Before Antibiotics: The reflexive prescription of antibiotics (Ciprofloxacin, Metronidazole) for diarrhoea in HIV patients missing Cryptosporidiosis as the cause is a common and consequential error. Nitazoxanide is ineffective in CD4 <50, and antibiotics have no anti-Cryptosporidium activity — so the patient receives days of ineffective treatment while dehydration, malabsorption, and wasting progress. Every HIV-positive patient presenting with diarrhoea >7 days should have: (1) CD4 count, (2) Modified AFB (Kinyoun) stool stain for Cryptosporidium oocysts, (3) Stool for Giardia antigen and microscopy. If CD4 <50, ART intensification is the primary treatment strategy — not antibiotics.

ORS and Zinc are Life-Saving in Children — Never Delay Them for Diagnostics: In children under 5 with watery diarrhoea from Cryptosporidiosis, dehydration is the immediate killer — NOT the parasite itself. ORS administration must begin immediately and continue throughout illness regardless of whether Cryptosporidium testing is available. Zinc sulphate 20mg/day for 14 days dramatically reduces diarrhoea duration and severity in children. Never delay ORS while waiting for stool results. Diagnostic confirmation can proceed simultaneously but must not delay rehydration.

Modified AFB (Kinyoun) Stain is Available and Cheap — Request it Specifically: Many Indian clinicians do not specifically request modified AFB staining for Cryptosporidium — they request 'stool routine and microscopy' which does NOT include the special Kinyoun method. Cryptosporidium oocysts are invisible on standard wet mount (they resemble yeast) and easily missed. The request must specifically state 'modified Kinyoun / modified AFB for Cryptosporidium'. The stain is cheap, uses reagents available at all medical college labs, and takes 30 minutes. Training PHC and district lab microscopists to recognise the brilliant red oocysts on Kinyoun stain is a high-impact, low-cost diagnostic capability improvement.

Modified AFB (Cold Kinyoun) Staining of Stool: Brilliant red/pink 4–6µm round oocysts against blue/green background — PATHOGNOMONIC appearance. Most cost-effective and widely available test for Cryptosporidiosis. Should be requested as 'modified ZN / Kinyoun stain for Cryptosporidium' — NOT routine stool AFB (which uses hot Ziehl-Neelsen and is too hot for oocysts). Available at all medical college labs.

Auramine-Rhodamine Fluorescence Microscopy: More sensitive than Kinyoun for oocyst detection — fluorescent yellow-green oocysts. Available at reference labs; higher throughput for large-volume screening.
2. Antigen Detection (ELISA / RDT — More Sensitive than Microscopy)

Cryptosporidium Stool Antigen ELISA or Lateral Flow RDT: Sensitivity 90–99%; specificity 99%. Superior to microscopy for low-burden infections. Detects both C. parvum and C. hominis. Commercially available rapid test kits (TechLab, IDEXX, others). Available at AIIMS, CMC, PGIMER, and increasingly major private labs.

diseases.name: Toxocariasis
diseases.tests:
1. Serology (Primary Diagnostic Test)

Toxocara canis IgG ELISA — Excretory/Secretory Antigen (TES-ELISA): Most important diagnostic test — sensitivity 78–91%, specificity 92–95% with TES antigen. Standard diagnostic test globally and at Indian reference labs. Available at AIIMS, CMC Vellore, PGIMER, NIMHANS, and increasingly private reference labs.

Western Blot Confirmation: For indeterminate ELISA results — 32-kDa band on WB is highly specific for Toxocara. Available at major reference labs.

2. Blood Count — Eosinophilia (Key Supportive Finding)

CBC with Differential: Marked eosinophilia (>1000 cells/µl; often 5000–30,000+ in active VLM) is a cardinal feature. Anaemia in young children with heavy worm burden.

3. Ophthalmological Examination (OLM)

Indirect Ophthalmoscopy (Dilated Fundus Examination by Ophthalmologist): Peripheral granuloma in vitreous or retina, vitreous bands/haze, dragged disc — in OLM. B-scan ultrasound for posterior pole lesion characterisation. Fluorescein angiography for activity.

Ocular Ultrasound (B-Scan): Useful for dense vitreous haze or media opacification — identifies posterior segment granuloma. Essential for differentiating OLM from retinoblastoma (calcification present in retinoblastoma, absent in OLM).

4. Imaging — VLM

Abdominal Ultrasound: Hepatic granulomata — multiple hypoechoic small lesions in liver parenchyma corresponding to Toxocara larval tracks. Characteristic honeycomb pattern of microabscesses.

CT Abdomen: Multiple small hypodense non-enhancing hepatic lesions — multiple oval low-density granulomata corresponding to larval migration paths. Pulmonary CT shows transient GGO/consolidation with eosinophilic pattern.

5. BAL / Bronchoalveolar Lavage (Pulmonary VLM)

BAL — Eosinophilic Alveolitis: Marked BAL eosinophilia (>25%) in pulmonary toxocariasis. Not routinely required — clinical/serological diagnosis sufficient in most cases.

6. Liver Biopsy (Atypical/Histological Confirmation)

Liver Biopsy Histopathology: Eosinophilic granuloma with Charcot-Leyden crystals and necrotic centre — larvae may be visible within granuloma or in their tracks. Practical only for atypical VLM presentations.

7. CSF Analysis (NLM)

CSF — Eosinophilic Pleocytosis: CSF eosinophilia + elevated protein + positive Toxocara CSF ELISA in neurological toxocariasis.

8. Differential Diagnosis Workup

Retinoblastoma Exclusion Panel: For leucocoria — orbital MRI (calcification = retinoblastoma indicator; absent in OLM), LDH, fundoscopy by paediatric ophthalmologist. Toxocara ELISA in ALL leucocoria cases before any invasive intervention.
diseases.generic_medicines:
1. Anti-Toxocara Treatment (VLM and Covert Toxocariasis)

Albendazole 400mg twice daily for 5 days (Adults/Children >2 years): Standard first-line treatment for VLM — kills migrating larvae by blocking microtubule polymerisation. Take with fatty meal. Some guidelines extend to 10–14 days for heavy VLM with systemic involvement.

Mebendazole 100–200mg twice daily for 5 days: Alternative to Albendazole — effective but variable GI absorption. Less commonly used for VLM in India.

2. Anti-Inflammatory (OLM and Severe VLM — Mandatory Adjunct)

Prednisolone 1–2mg/kg/day for 2–4 weeks (VLM with Severe Eosinophilia or OLM): Corticosteroids suppress the eosinophilic inflammatory response to migrating larvae — reduces hepatic and pulmonary granuloma formation severity and prevents retinal scarring in OLM. In OLM: ALWAYS start Prednisolone WITH Albendazole (not steroids alone which may worsen larval migration).

Periocular Steroid Injection (OLM with Active Vitritis): Intravitreal or sub-Tenon Triamcinolone for active OLM vitreous inflammation — under ophthalmologist supervision. Suppresses vitreous inflammation and prevents tractional retinal detachment.

3. Ophthalmological Interventions (OLM Complications)

Laser Photocoagulation (Retinal Toxocara Granuloma): To destroy accessible retinal Toxocara larvae directly and prevent retinal extension. Under retinal specialist evaluation.

Vitrectomy (OLM with Tractional Retinal Detachment): For OLM causing vitreous membranes and tractional detachment — surgical vitreoretinal intervention.

4. Bronchodilators (Pulmonary VLM with Wheezing)

Salbutamol MDI 100mcg (2 puffs as needed) + Montelukast 5–10mg/day: Symptomatic bronchodilation for Loeffler syndrome wheezing — NOT the definitive treatment (Albendazole + Prednisolone are). Withdraw once Toxocara treatment complete and eosinophilia resolves.
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line anti-Toxocara treatment. PMBJP widely available.

Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Anti-inflammatory adjunct — VLM and OLM.

Mebendazole 100mg Tablets (6 tabs): Alternative antiparasitic.

Cetirizine 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Antihistamine for urticarial and eosinophilic hypersensitivity symptoms.

Salbutamol 100mcg MDI (Inhaler): Bronchodilator for pulmonary Loeffler syndrome wheezing.

Montelukast 5mg Chewable Tablets (10 tabs): Anti-leukotriene for eosinophilic asthma/VLM pulmonary component in children.

Omeprazole 20mg Capsules (10 caps): Gastric protection during steroid course.

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Albendazole 400mg (10 tablets): Rs.22

Prednisolone 10mg (10 tablets): Rs.5

Mebendazole 100mg (6 tablets): Rs.14

Cetirizine 10mg (10 tablets): Rs.8

Salbutamol 100mcg inhaler (200 doses): Rs.52

Montelukast 5mg chewable (10 tablets): Rs.28

Omeprazole 20mg (10 capsules): Rs.10
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Churna: Primary Krimighna herb — traditional for systemic intestinal and extra-intestinal worm infections. 2–3g twice daily with honey on empty stomach for children. Supportive antiparasitic alongside Albendazole in visceral larva migrans.

Krimikuthar Ras: Compound classical rasa antiparasitic — for Krimi in systemic organs as well as intestinal worms. 250mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance. Traditional for multi-organ helminthic invasion.

Triphala Ghrita: Internal administration — ghee infused with Triphala for systemic infection with hepatic involvement. Hepatoprotective and antioxidant; supports hepatic granuloma resolution during VLM. 5–10ml on empty stomach.

Nimba (Neem) Leaf Capsules: Antiparasitic and anti-inflammatory — neem extract has activity against multiple helminths and reduces systemic inflammation. 500mg capsules twice daily.

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) Extract: Immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory — reduces excessive eosinophilic inflammatory response in VLM and supports reticuloendothelial system. 500mg standardised extract twice daily.

Saptamruta Lauha: Classical Ayurvedic ophthalmic and rasayana formulation — used in eye diseases caused by internal infections and for liver-eye axis conditions (Netra Roga from Yakrit Vikar). Under Vaidya guidance for OLM supportive management.

Traiphala Ghrita Eye Drops: Ayurvedic ophthalmic preparation for vitreous clarity and retinal support in OLM — traditional Netra Tarpana with medicated ghee under Vaidya supervision. Supportive only.

Amalaki (Indian Gooseberry) Powder: High antioxidant and immune-supportive — reduces systemic oxidative stress from migrating Toxocara larvae and reduces eosinophilic tissue injury. 5g twice daily with warm water.
diseases.india_specific:
Stray Dog Density and Soil Contamination: India's massive stray dog population contaminates urban and rural soils ubiquitously with Toxocara canis eggs. Unlike in countries with comprehensive stray dog management and deworming programmes, India has limited systematic stray dog anthelmintic treatment — leading to continuous environmental egg contamination.

Sandpit and Park Contamination: Indian urban parks, school sandpits, playgrounds, and roadsides are heavily contaminated with Toxocara eggs from undewormed pet dogs and stray dogs. Closing or protecting sandpits and regular decontamination (sunlight, drying) reduces egg viability.

Pet Dog Deworming Gaps: Indian pet dog owners have extremely low awareness of regular anthelmintic use — many dogs are never dewormed or dewormed only once. Toxocara canis is the most common intestinal worm in Indian puppies and young dogs. A puppy excretes hundreds of millions of Toxocara eggs per day. Regular deworming of all pet dogs (every 3 months) is the most impactful prevention intervention.

Retinoblastoma vs OLM Misclassification: Retinoblastoma is the most common intraocular malignancy of Indian children — any leucocoria mandates urgent ophthalmological evaluation. Toxocara OLM must be excluded serologically before orbital MRI and management for retinoblastoma to avoid unnecessary enucleation.

Food-Borne Infection from Raw Liver: Consumption of raw or undercooked chicken/pork/beef/lamb liver containing paratenic host larvae — reported as an infection route in some Asian countries and relevant in North Indian communities consuming raw liver (Kaleji) preparations.
diseases.important_notes:
Deworm Your Pets Regularly — The Most Important Prevention for Toxocariasis in India: Toxocariasis is 100% preventable at source by regular anthelmintic treatment of all dogs and cats. Pet dogs should receive Pyrantel Pamoate or Fenbendazole deworming at 2, 4, 6, and 8 weeks of age for puppies; then every 3 months for adult dogs. Pet cats: same protocol. This practice, standard in Europe and North America, is almost entirely absent among Indian pet owners who are largely unaware of zoonotic Toxocara risk to children. Every veterinarian and paediatrician in India should advise pet owners: 'Deworm your pet every 3 months to protect your children from Toxocara worm larvae.' Stray dog management through ANIMAL BIRTH CONTROL programme which includes deworming would have enormous public health benefit.

Leucocoria in an Indian Child = Urgent Ophthalmology + Toxocara ELISA Before Any Invasive Procedure: White pupil reflex in a child in India carries two major diagnoses: Retinoblastoma (ocular malignancy requiring urgent enucleation if confirmed) and Ocular Toxocara granuloma (infectious, treatable with Albendazole + steroids, eye preserved). These must be DISTINGUISHED before any intervention — unnecessary enucleation of a Toxocara-affected eye has occurred in India due to failure to test for Toxocara. Every child with leucocoria should have: (1) Urgent paediatric ophthalmology evaluation; (2) Orbital MRI (calcification = retinoblastoma, absent = OLM); (3) Toxocara IgG ELISA serum; (4) B-scan ocular ultrasound. Only confirmed retinoblastoma should proceed to enucleation.

Massive Hypereosinophilia (>3000 cells/µl) in an Indian Child with Hepatomegaly = VLM First: In Indian paediatric practice, very high blood eosinophilia is most commonly caused by: (1) Toxocariasis (VLM), (2) Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia (Wuchereria bancrofti/Brugia malayi), (3) Ascariasis with heavy migration, (4) Drug hypersensitivity. Before labelling a child with 'hypereosinophilic syndrome' or starting corticosteroids, full helminth serology panel must be performed: Toxocara ELISA + Filaria card test/ELISA + stool examination for Ascaris. Treating helminthic hypereosinophilia with steroids alone (without antiparasitic drug) is dangerous — it can accelerate larval migration and organ damage.

Corticosteroids Must NEVER Be Given Alone in Suspected VLM or OLM — Albendazole Must Come First (or Together): Steroids suppress eosinophilic immunity that contains Toxocara larvae in granulomata — if given alone, larvae escape containment and spread further in tissues. This causes catastrophic worsening of VLM (larvae invade new organs) and OLM (retinal destruction extends). Albendazole must always be prescribed simultaneously or before steroids. The steroid role is to suppress the anti-larval inflammatory response WHILE the antiparasitic works to kill the larvae — the two must always be given together.
confirmation_tests_curated: Toxocara canis IgG ELISA — Excretory/Secretory Antigen (TES-ELISA): Most important diagnostic test — sensitivity 78–91%, specificity 92–95% with TES antigen. Standard diagnostic test globally and at Indian reference labs. Available at AIIMS, CMC Vellore, PGIMER, NIMHANS, and increasingly private reference labs.
Western Blot Confirmation: For indeterminate ELISA results — 32-kDa band on WB is highly specific for Toxocara. Available at major reference labs.
CBC with Differential: Marked eosinophilia (>1000 cells/µl; often 5000–30,000+ in active VLM) is a cardinal feature. Anaemia in young children with heavy worm burden.

diseases.name: Schizophrenia
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5 / ICD-11)

DSM-5: ≥2 of 5 symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech, disorganised behaviour, negative symptoms) for ≥1 month; social/occupational dysfunction ≥6 months

ICD-11: Persistent delusions, hallucinations, disorganised thinking, passivity experiences, or negative symptoms for ≥1 month

Structured clinical interviews: MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI), SCID-5

RATING SCALES

PANSS (Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale) — gold standard for symptom severity

BPRS (Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale) — widely used in Indian settings

CDSS (Calgary Depression Scale for Schizophrenia) — comorbid depression

GAF (Global Assessment of Functioning)

COGNITIVE TESTING

MCCB (MATRICS Consensus Cognitive Battery) — research standard

MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) — brief cognitive screen

BIOLOGICAL INVESTIGATIONS (exclude organic causes)

Haemogram, blood glucose, LFT, RFT, thyroid function (rule out medical causes)

Serum ceruloplasmin, urine copper (rule out Wilson's disease in young onset)

EEG — rule out temporal lobe epilepsy with psychiatric features

MRI brain — rule out space-occupying lesion, ADEM, anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

Anti-NMDA receptor antibody, anti-VGKC antibody — rule out autoimmune encephalitis

Urine drug screen — rule out substance-induced psychosis (cannabis, amphetamines)

CLOZAPINE MONITORING (when prescribed)

Baseline FBC; weekly WBC for 18 weeks then fortnightly; monthly thereafter

Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) — must be >2000/mm³ to continue clozapine

Serum clozapine level (target 350–600 ng/mL; toxic >1000 ng/mL)

ECG (QTc prolongation risk)

Fasting blood glucose, lipid profile — annually for all antipsychotics
diseases.generic_medicines:
TYPICAL (FIRST-GENERATION) ANTIPSYCHOTICS

Haloperidol 1.5–20 mg/day oral (decanoate depot IM monthly for adherence)

Trifluoperazine 5–20 mg/day oral

Chlorpromazine 75–300 mg/day oral (rarely used — sedation, photosensitivity)

Fluphenazine decanoate 12.5–25 mg IM every 2–4 weeks

ATYPICAL (SECOND-GENERATION) ANTIPSYCHOTICS

Risperidone 2–8 mg/day oral (first-line in India — affordable, effective)

Olanzapine 5–20 mg/day oral (good broad-spectrum; weight gain risk)

Quetiapine 150–800 mg/day (useful when sedation or sleep benefit needed)

Aripiprazole 10–30 mg/day (weight-neutral, dopamine partial agonist)

Amisulpride 300–800 mg/day (good for negative symptoms)

Ziprasidone 40–160 mg/day (QTc monitoring required)

REFRACTORY SCHIZOPHRENIA

Clozapine 150–900 mg/day — gold standard for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (WBC monitoring mandatory)

ANTIDEPRESSANTS (augmentation for negative symptoms or comorbid depression)

Mirtazapine 15–30 mg/day augmentation

ADJUNCTIVE

Trihexyphenidyl 2–6 mg/day — EPS management (parkinsonism, acute dystonia, akathisia)

Promethazine 25–50 mg IM/oral — acute agitation; akathisia

Propranolol 20–80 mg/day — akathisia management

Benzodiazepines (Lorazepam, Diazepam) — short-term adjunct for agitation

CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive support, calming adaptogen; 300–600 mg extract/day

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogenic, anxiolytic, neuroprotective; 300–600 mg/day

Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — nervine tonic, cognitive enhancement

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — Ayurvedic sedative-anxiolytic; 250–500 mg/day

Vacha (Acorus calamus) — medhya (intellect-promoting), used carefully (mild anticonvulsant)

BRANDED AYURVEDIC

Brahmi Ghrita — classical ghee preparation for CNS disorders

Ashwagandharishtam — fermented preparation for stress and CNS support

PRACTICES

Yoga Nidra and pranayama — adjunct for relaxation and sleep

Panchakarma (under supervision) — detoxification for chronic cases

IMPORTANT NOTE

Ayurvedic medicines are ADJUNCTIVE ONLY — never replace antipsychotics in schizophrenia

Some formulations contain heavy metals (gold, mercury) — must be from licensed GMP manufacturers
diseases.india_specific:
TREATMENT GAP

Treatment gap estimated at 70–80% — majority never receive psychiatric care

First contact with traditional / faith healers before psychiatry in most rural areas

SOCIOCULTURAL FACTORS

Severe stigma — patients hidden, chained (Erwadi tragedy, 2001) or abandoned

Caregiver burnout — primary caregivers are family members with no support

Marriage and employment discrimination severely impact recovery

HEALTHCARE SYSTEM

Limited district mental health services outside NIMHANS, PGI, AIIMS tier

National Mental Health Programme (NMHP) District MH Program aims outreach

Shortage of psychiatrists India ~0.3 per 1 lakh vs recommended 3 per 1 lakh

METABOLIC AND MEDICAL

Metabolic syndrome risk from antipsychotics (weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidaemia)

Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS) common with typical antipsychotics (Haloperidol)

Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) — rare but life-threatening

High cardiovascular mortality — life expectancy 15–20 years shorter than general population

Comorbid tuberculosis risk in institutionalised patients
diseases.important_notes:
CLOZAPINE SAFETY: Mandatory weekly white blood cell (WBC/ANC) count for 18 weeks, then fortnightly; clozapine must be STOPPED if ANC <1500/mm³ (severe neutropenia risk — agranulocytosis in 0.5–2%)

NEVER ABRUPT DISCONTINUATION: Stopping antipsychotics without tapering leads to rapid relapse; depot formulations (Haloperidol decanoate, Fluphenazine decanoate) improve adherence in non-compliant patients

EPS MANAGEMENT: Haloperidol and other typical antipsychotics cause acute dystonia, parkinsonism, akathisia — manage with Trihexyphenidyl; Tardive Dyskinesia risk with prolonged use — monitor ABNORMAL INVOLUNTARY MOVEMENT SCALE (AIMS) every 6 months

SUICIDE RISK: 5–10% of schizophrenia patients die by suicide; risk highest in first year of illness and following hospital discharge — routine safety assessment mandatory

METABOLIC MONITORING: All antipsychotics (especially Olanzapine, Clozapine) cause weight gain and metabolic syndrome — monitor BMI, fasting glucose, lipids every 3–6 months

diseases.name: Bipolar Disorder
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

DSM-5 Bipolar I: Manic episode ≥7 days (or any duration if hospitalisation required); not attributable to substances or medical condition

DSM-5 Bipolar II: Hypomanic episodes + major depressive episodes; no full manic episode

STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS

MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI) — standard in India

SCID-5 — research standard

RATING SCALES

Young Mania Rating Scale (YMRS) — 11-item clinician-administered; gold standard for mania

HAMD (Hamilton Depression Rating Scale) or MADRS for depressive episodes

MDQ (Mood Disorder Questionnaire) — screening tool; sensitivity 73%, specificity 90%

CGI-BP (Clinical Global Impressions — Bipolar) — overall severity and change

BIOLOGICAL (exclude organic causes and monitor treatment)

Thyroid function test — hypothyroidism mimics depression; hyperthyroidism mimics mania

Serum cortisol — rule out Cushing's (euphoria) or Addison's (depression)

MRI brain — rule out frontal lobe tumour, vascular lesion mimicking mania

EEG — rule out frontal/temporal lobe epilepsy

HIV serology, VDRL — neurosyphilis, HIV encephalopathy

Serum B12, folate — nutritional deficiency causing mood disorder

Urine drug screen — cocaine, amphetamines, cannabis-induced mania

LITHIUM MONITORING

Serum lithium level (draw 12 hours after last dose): therapeutic range 0.8–1.2 mEq/L for acute mania, 0.6–0.8 mEq/L maintenance

Serum creatinine, eGFR — lithium nephrotoxicity

Thyroid function (TSH) — lithium-induced hypothyroidism

Serum calcium — lithium-induced hyperparathyroidism

ECG — T-wave changes on lithium

VALPROATE MONITORING

Serum valproate level (target 50–100 mcg/mL for acute mania)

LFT — valproate hepatotoxicity

FBC — valproate thrombocytopenia

Pregnancy test and counselling before prescribing in women of childbearing age
diseases.generic_medicines:
MOOD STABILISERS — FIRST-LINE

Lithium carbonate 900–2400 mg/day in divided doses (most proven agent; monitor levels)

Sodium Valproate (Divalproex) 750–2000 mg/day oral (acute mania; avoid in pregnancy)

Lamotrigine 25–200 mg/day — bipolar depression and maintenance (titrate slowly to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome)

Carbamazepine 400–1200 mg/day — acute mania and maintenance (enzyme inducer, drug interactions)

ANTIPSYCHOTICS (acute mania and maintenance)

Olanzapine 10–20 mg/day — effective for acute mania; FDA-approved maintenance

Risperidone 2–6 mg/day — acute mania

Quetiapine 300–800 mg/day — acute mania AND bipolar depression (only antipsychotic with level 1 evidence for bipolar depression)

Aripiprazole 15–30 mg/day — acute mania, weight neutral

Haloperidol 5–10 mg/day — acute severe mania with agitation (adjunct)

BENZODIAZEPINES (ACUTE MANIA — SHORT-TERM ONLY)

Lorazepam 1–4 mg IM/oral — acute agitation in mania

Clonazepam 1–4 mg/day — adjunct for agitation, sleep

ANTIDEPRESSANTS (use with CAUTION — only with mood stabiliser)

Quetiapine preferred for bipolar depression

SSRIs (Sertraline, Escitalopram) only with concurrent mood stabiliser — risk of manic switch if given alone

Avoid TCAs and MAOIs in bipolar — high switch risk

diseases.india_specific:
MISDIAGNOSIS

Most commonly misdiagnosed as unipolar depression — mania overlooked or culturally normalised

Antidepressant monotherapy (without mood stabiliser) given for depressive episodes → precipitates manic switch and rapid cycling

SOCIOCULTURAL

Hypersexual behaviour during mania leads to family and social ostracism

Financial devastation during manic episodes — family savings exhausted

Marriage-related stressors are common precipitants of first episodes

Postpartum bipolar episodes — high risk; often first presentation

TREATMENT

Lithium remains most affordable and effective mood stabiliser — available at Jan Aushadhi

Lithium monitoring poorly available in peripheral centres (serum levels, RFT, TFT)

Sodium Valproate widely used — neural tube defect risk in women of childbearing age

SEASON AND CLIMATE

Manic episodes more common in summer in India — light exposure, heat, sleep disruption

Depressive episodes more common in winter / monsoon

SUICIDE

Suicide risk 20–30x higher than general population; mixed episodes carry highest acute risk

India's suicide prevention resources extremely limited at primary care level
diseases.important_notes:
LITHIUM TOXICITY RISK: Narrow therapeutic window (0.8–1.2 mEq/L therapeutic; toxic >1.5 mEq/L). Early signs: coarse tremor, GI upset, polyuria, polydipsia. Severe: ataxia, confusion, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias. Common precipitants in India: NSAIDs, dehydration (fever, summer heat), low-salt diet, diuretics — all increase lithium levels. Check levels every 3–6 months once stable.

ANTIDEPRESSANT CAUTION: Antidepressant monotherapy WITHOUT a mood stabiliser in bipolar disorder can precipitate rapid cycling (≥4 episodes/year), manic switch, or mixed state. Always co-prescribe mood stabiliser first. Quetiapine or Lithium augmentation preferred for bipolar depression.

VALPROATE AND PREGNANCY: Sodium Valproate causes neural tube defects (spina bifida — 1–2%), facial abnormalities, and neurodevelopmental harm. Contraindicated in women of childbearing age unless adequate contraception. Switch to Lamotrigine or Lithium before planned pregnancy (with specialist guidance).

SUICIDE RISK IN MIXED STATES: Mixed episodes (simultaneous manic energy with depressive hopelessness) carry the highest suicide risk — more so than pure depression or mania. Urgent psychiatric review, hospitalisation if safety risk, and never leave patient alone.

diseases.name: Major Depressive Disorder
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)

≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks; must include depressed mood or anhedonia; not attributable to substances or medical condition; causes significant distress or functional impairment

STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS

MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI) — validated in India

SCID-5 — research standard

PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) — validated primary care screening tool; score ≥10 = moderately severe; widely used in India

RATING SCALES

HAM-D (Hamilton Depression Rating Scale) — 17-item; gold standard for severity

MADRS (Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale) — more sensitive to change

BDI-II (Beck Depression Inventory) — self-rated; widely used

CGI (Clinical Global Impressions) — brief treatment response assessment

BIOLOGICAL (rule out organic causes)

Thyroid function test (TSH) — hypothyroidism (commonest medical mimic); hyperthyroidism (anxiety/depression)

CBC — anaemia causing fatigue and depressive symptoms

Serum B12 and folate — deficiency causing depression and cognitive symptoms

Serum Vitamin D — deficiency associated with depression (very common in India)

Blood glucose, HbA1c — diabetes associated with depression

LFT — alcoholic liver disease, Wilson's disease

Serum cortisol — Cushing's syndrome, Addison's disease

HIV serology — HIV-associated depression

MRI brain — if neurological signs, first episode psychotic depression, treatment resistance

Sleep study (polysomnography) — if suspected sleep apnoea causing depressive symptoms

SUICIDE RISK ASSESSMENT

Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) — validated, standardised

SAL (Suicidal Ideation Attributes Scale) — brief structured assessment

Safe messaging guidelines and safety planning — mandatory for all positive screens
diseases.generic_medicines:
SSRI — FIRST-LINE (preferred for most patients; safest in overdose)

Escitalopram 10–20 mg/day (most well-tolerated SSRI; minimal drug interactions)

Sertraline 50–200 mg/day (first-line in post-MI depression, postpartum; safest in pregnancy)

Fluoxetine 20–60 mg/day (long half-life; useful for adherence issues; PMDD)

Paroxetine 20–50 mg/day (useful for comorbid anxiety; discontinuation syndrome risk)

Fluvoxamine 50–300 mg/day (comorbid OCD)

SNRI

Venlafaxine 75–225 mg/day (effective for depression + anxiety; blood pressure monitoring)

Duloxetine 60–120 mg/day (comorbid pain, diabetic neuropathy)

TCAs (LOW COST — useful at Jan Aushadhi; higher toxicity in overdose)

Amitriptyline 25–150 mg/day (also for neuropathic pain, insomnia; sedating)

Imipramine 75–200 mg/day

Clomipramine 75–250 mg/day (also for OCD)

NDRI

Bupropion 150–450 mg/day (activating; no sexual side effects; smoking cessation)

NaSSA

Mirtazapine 15–45 mg/day (sedating, appetite-stimulating; good for insomnia and weight loss)

AUGMENTATION STRATEGIES

Add Lithium 300–600 mg/day to antidepressant for treatment-resistant depression

Aripiprazole 2–5 mg/day augmentation for partial responders

Quetiapine 50–300 mg augmentation (sedating; good for insomnia comorbidity)

ECT — severe, psychotic, suicidal, or medication-resistant depression

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neuroprotective, antidepressant, anxiolytic properties; 300–600 mg extract/day

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen, anti-cortisol, mood-lifting; 300–600 mg/day

Saffron (Crocus sativus / Kesar) — Level 1b evidence for mild-moderate depression; 30 mg/day saffron extract (equivalent to Fluoxetine 20 mg in some RCTs)

Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — nervine tonic, anti-anxiety; syrup 10–20 mL BD

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — sedative, anti-depressant, reduces cortisol; 250–500 mg/day

Giloy / Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator, anti-fatigue, anti-stress

Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) — sleep promoting in insomnia-predominant depression; 0.5–2 g/day

CLASSICAL COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Saraswatarishta 15–20 mL BD — nervine rejuvenator for depression with cognitive slowing

Manasamitra Vatakam — traditional compound for psychosis and affective disorders

Ashwagandharishtam — fermented preparation, general nervine and anti-depressant

BRANDED AYURVEDIC

Mentat (Himalaya) — Brahmi combination; evidence for mild depression and anxiety

Stresscom (Dabur) — Ashwagandha standardised extract

PRACTICES

Yoga Nidra — guided deep relaxation; reduces cortisol comparable to antidepressants in mild MDD

Surya Namaskar and aerobic yoga — physical exercise component; neurotrophic effect (BDNF increase)

IMPORTANT NOTE

Saffron and St. John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum) have some evidence for mild-moderate depression but interaction with SSRIs (serotonin syndrome risk with St. John's Wort) — inform all physicians
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

India has the highest absolute number of depression cases globally (approximately 56 million)

Suicide rate in India is 12.9 per 100,000 — predominantly from depression in productive age group

GENDER AND SOCIAL FACTORS

Women: Domestic violence, dowry harassment, restricted autonomy, early marriage, postpartum depression — significant risk amplifiers

Men: Occupational stress, societal expectation against help-seeking ('real men don't cry'), farmer suicides — high-risk group

Students: Competitive exam pressure (NEET, IIT-JEE, UPSC), academic failure, ragging — leading cause of youth suicide

HEALTHCARE BARRIERS

Somatisation of depression — patients present to general physician with physical complaints; psychiatric cause missed

Average delay to psychiatric care in India is 2–6 years from onset

SSRIs now available at Jan Aushadhi centres — improving affordability

iCall, Vandrevala Foundation, NIMHANS helpline — free telephone counselling

COMORBIDITIES

Chronic pain syndromes, irritable bowel syndrome, diabetes comorbid with depression — bidirectional relationship

Hypothyroidism highly prevalent in India and mimics/causes depression — test TSH

Comorbid cardiovascular disease — post-MI depression carries 3x higher mortality

ELECTROCONVULSIVE THERAPY (ECT)

ECT widely available at Indian government psychiatric hospitals and underutilised

Indicated for severe, psychotic, or medication-resistant depression and when rapid response needed

Modified ECT (with anaesthesia) — safer; should not be stigmatised
diseases.important_notes:
SUICIDAL RISK ASSESSMENT IS MANDATORY: All patients with MDD must be assessed for suicidal ideation at every visit using a structured tool (C-SSRS, PHQ item 9). Patients with active suicidal ideation with plan should be hospitalised. Prescribe limited quantities of medications to high-risk patients — TCAs (Amitriptyline, Imipramine) are highly lethal in overdose; prescribe minimum supply.

ANTIDEPRESSANT ACTIVATION IN FIRST 2 WEEKS: SSRIs and SNRIs can cause increased energy and agitation before mood lifts — this creates a window of heightened suicide risk (patient now has energy to act on suicidal thoughts). Warn patients and caregivers; review within 1–2 weeks of initiation.

ADEQUATE TREATMENT TRIAL: Antidepressants require 4–6 weeks at therapeutic dose to show full response. Premature discontinuation due to lack of response within 2 weeks is a major cause of treatment failure. Educate patients that response is gradual.

ECT FOR SEVERE DEPRESSION: Electroconvulsive Therapy is UNDERUSED in India. It is the fastest and most effective treatment for severe, psychotic, postpartum, or medication-resistant depression and is LIFE-SAVING for highly suicidal patients. Available at most government psychiatric hospitals. Should not be stigmatised or withheld out of fear.

ST. JOHN'S WORT INTERACTION: Hypericum perforatum (used as herbal antidepressant) induces CYP3A4 and can reduce levels of many drugs including oral contraceptives, antiretrovirals, and warfarin. Combined with SSRIs, risk of serotonin syndrome. Patients must always disclose herbal use.

diseases.name: Generalised Anxiety Disorder
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)

Excessive anxiety and worry ≥6 months; ≥3 of 6 symptoms; causing significant distress/impairment; not attributable to substances or medical condition

SCREENING TOOLS

GAD-7 (Generalised Anxiety Disorder 7-item scale) — score ≥10 = moderate-severe; widely validated in India

GAD-2 — ultra-brief 2-item screen for primary care settings

STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS

MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview — validates GAD diagnosis

RATING SCALES

HAMA (Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale) — 14-item; psychic and somatic subscales

BAI (Beck Anxiety Inventory) — somatic-focused, self-rated

Penn State Worry Questionnaire (PSWQ) — worry severity specific to GAD

BIOLOGICAL (rule out organic causes — ESSENTIAL)

Thyroid function test (TSH, FT4) — hyperthyroidism mimics GAD exactly

Serum glucose, HbA1c — hypoglycaemia can mimic anxiety

Serum calcium — hypercalcaemia (anxiety, constipation, weakness)

ECG — rule out arrhythmia causing palpitations (AF, SVT)

Serum catecholamines / urine VMA — rule out phaeochromocytoma in episodic hypertension + anxiety

Serum cortisol — Cushing's syndrome

CBC — anaemia-related anxiety symptoms

SPECIAL INVESTIGATIONS (if indicated)

Holter monitor — for palpitations attributed to anxiety but with irregular rhythm

Polysomnography — if poor sleep is dominant and sleep apnoea suspected

EEG — rule out focal seizures presenting with anxiety equivalent
diseases.generic_medicines:
FIRST-LINE — SSRIs AND SNRIs

Escitalopram 10–20 mg/day (preferred SSRI for GAD; best tolerability profile)

Sertraline 50–200 mg/day

Paroxetine 20–50 mg/day (strong anti-anxiety; high discontinuation syndrome risk)

Venlafaxine XR 75–225 mg/day (SNRI; approved for GAD; monitor blood pressure)

Duloxetine 60–120 mg/day (comorbid painful somatic symptoms)

SECOND-LINE

Buspirone 15–60 mg/day in divided doses (non-addictive; slow onset 2–4 weeks; useful adjunct)

Mirtazapine 15–45 mg/day (insomnia-predominant GAD with low appetite)

Pregabalin 150–600 mg/day (good anxiolytic; approved for GAD in Europe; dependence potential)

ADJUNCTIVE / SHORT-TERM

Benzodiazepines (Lorazepam 1–2 mg, Clonazepam 0.5–2 mg) — SHORT-TERM ONLY (2–4 weeks); dependency and cognitive side effects risk

Beta-blockers (Propranolol 10–40 mg PRN) — performance anxiety, somatic symptoms (palpitations, tremor)

Hydroxyzine 25–50 mg as needed — antihistamine with anxiolytic properties; no dependency

PSYCHOTHERAPY (FIRST-LINE — combined with pharmacotherapy)

CBT (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy) — gold standard; effective as medication in mid-long term

Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) — effective for GAD

Relaxation training, worry postponement techniques

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen, cortisol-reducing, anti-anxiety; 300–600 mg/day

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces anxiety, improves cognitive performance; 300 mg/day

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — nervine sedative for anxiety and insomnia; 250–500 mg/day

Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — nervine tonic, anti-anxiety, cognitive support

Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) — sleep-promoting and anxiolytic (sleep-onset anxiety); 0.5–2 g/day

Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina) — classical antihypertensive and anxiolytic (reserpine source; use cautiously)

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Saraswatarishta 15–20 mL BD — nervine, memory, and anxiety support

Ashwagandharishtam 15 mL BD — stress resilience and anti-anxiety

Brahmi Vati — classic preparation for mental disorders including anxiety

BRANDED AYURVEDIC

Mentat (Himalaya) — Brahmi + Ashwagandha combination; evidence for anxiety reduction

Stress Guard (Himalaya) — Ashwagandha-based combination

PRACTICES

Pranayama (Anulom-Vilom, Bhramari) — immediate anxiolytic effect; 10–15 minutes daily

Yoga Nidra — deep relaxation technique; reduces GAD severity comparable to CBT in mild cases

Abhyanga (warm oil massage) — calms vata; reduces somatic anxiety
diseases.india_specific:
SOCIOCULTURAL STRESSORS

Financial insecurity, job uncertainty, exam pressure, family expectations — primary triggers in India

Farmer anxiety — crop failure, debt, weather uncertainty; suicidality risk in agrarian GAD

Marriage-related anxiety in women — dowry, in-law conflict, domestic roles

Academic performance anxiety in students — IIT-JEE, NEET, UPSC coaching-related burnout

DIAGNOSTIC CHALLENGES

GAD heavily somatises in Indian population — patients present with 'gas trouble', 'heart beating fast', 'body weakness' to GPs not to psychiatrists

Frequently misdiagnosed as cardiac disease (palpitations) or GI disorder

Average delay before correct diagnosis: 5–10 years

COMORBIDITIES

Highly comorbid with major depression (60%), panic disorder, health anxiety, OCD

Chronic pain syndromes — fibromyalgia, IBS, tension headache

Thyroid disorders (hyperthyroidism — distinguish clinically)

Diabetes — anxiety and glycaemic control interact bidirectionally

TREATMENT

SSRIs and SNRIs available at Jan Aushadhi; psychotherapy (CBT) scarce outside urban centres

Benzodiazepine overuse very common in India — dependency risk; prescribe with time limits
diseases.important_notes:
BENZODIAZEPINE DEPENDENCY RISK: Benzodiazepines (Diazepam, Clonazepam, Lorazepam) are widely overprescribed for anxiety in India. They provide rapid relief but cause dependence within 4–6 weeks of daily use. Tolerance develops requiring dose escalation. Withdrawal can cause seizures. Restrict to 2–4 weeks maximum; taper gradually if stopping; prefer SSRI/SNRI as first-line long-term treatment.

THYROID MUST BE EXCLUDED FIRST: Hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease) is clinically indistinguishable from GAD — anxiety, palpitations, tremor, restlessness, insomnia. Check TSH and FT4 in all new anxiety presentations. Treating anxiety with antidepressants when the cause was hyperthyroidism is a serious diagnostic error.

CBT IS AS EFFECTIVE AS MEDICATION: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy has equal or superior long-term outcomes compared to medication alone. Psychoeducation about worry, thought challenging, and behavioural experiments should be included in treatment. NIMHANS and district hospitals may offer psychology services; also available via iCall and Vandrevala Foundation (online).

diseases.name: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)

Criterion A–H: Exposure to traumatic event + ≥1 intrusion + ≥1 avoidance + ≥2 negative cognitions/mood + ≥2 arousal/reactivity; duration >1 month; causes significant distress/impairment

SCREENING TOOLS

PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist for DSM-5) — 20-item self-report; score ≥33 = PTSD likely; most widely used

IES-R (Impact of Event Scale — Revised) — 22-item; intrusion, avoidance, hyperarousal subscales

Primary Care PTSD Screen (PC-PTSD-5) — 5-item ultra-brief screen for primary care

STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS

CAPS-5 (Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale for DSM-5) — gold standard for diagnosis and severity

MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (PTSD module)

RATING SCALES

CAPS-5 total score — symptom severity and frequency

CGI for PTSD

BIOLOGICAL (exclude comorbidities and organic causes)

Thyroid function (rule out hyperthyroidism — overlapping hyperarousal symptoms)

Cortisol levels — PTSD associated with low basal cortisol (opposite of depression)

Haemogram, LFT — screen for comorbid substance use disorder

MRI brain / EEG — rule out TBI-related PTSD (head injury as traumatic event)

Urine drug screen — comorbid substance use (alcohol, cannabis, opioids)

ECG, Holter — rule out cardiac dysrhythmia in patients with palpitations

Neuropsychological assessment — memory, attention, executive function impairment

DISSOCIATION ASSESSMENT

DES (Dissociative Experiences Scale) — screens for dissociative symptoms

MID (Multiscale Dissociation Inventory) — more detailed dissociative profile
diseases.generic_medicines:
FIRST-LINE (FDA and NICE approved)

Sertraline 50–200 mg/day (FDA-approved for PTSD; most widely used in India)

Paroxetine 20–60 mg/day (FDA-approved for PTSD; discontinuation syndrome with abrupt stop)

Venlafaxine XR 75–225 mg/day (strong evidence; particularly for hyperarousal and sleep)

SECOND-LINE

Fluoxetine 20–60 mg/day (good evidence; useful when depression comorbid)

Mirtazapine 15–45 mg/day (nightmares, insomnia, and depressive symptoms)

Prazosin 1–15 mg at bedtime — alpha-1 blocker; specifically for PTSD nightmares (strong RCT evidence)

ADJUNCTIVE / SPECIFIC SYMPTOM TREATMENT

Propranolol 40–80 mg/day — hyperarousal, reactivity, palpitations

Clonidine 0.1–0.4 mg/day — hyperarousal, sleep, nightmares (alpha-2 agonist)

Quetiapine 25–200 mg — insomnia, nightmares, hyperarousal (adjunct)

AVOID IN PTSD

Benzodiazepines — no evidence for core PTSD symptoms; may worsen dissociation and increase substance use risk

PSYCHOTHERAPY — FIRST-LINE (superior to medication alone)

Trauma-Focused CBT (TF-CBT) — gold standard

EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing) — WHO recommended

Prolonged Exposure (PE) — evidence-based for avoidance

Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT) — addresses distorted trauma cognitions

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces cortisol, adaptogenic; helps with hyperarousal; 300–600 mg/day

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neuroprotective, reduces anxiety and stress; 300 mg daily

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — calming, anti-stress; reduces nightmares and arousal; 250–500 mg/day

Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) — sedative for nightmares and insomnia; 0.5–2 g/day

Mandukaparni (Centella asiatica) — nervine tonic, trauma-related cognitive impairment

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Saraswatarishta — nervine tonic, reduces stress and anxiety; 15–20 mL BD after meals

Ashwagandharishtam — adaptogenic; general stress and PTSD adjunct

Brahmi Ghrita — classical ghee preparation; neuroprotective, trauma recovery

BRANDED AYURVEDIC

Mentat (Himalaya) — anxiety and stress; Brahmi-based

Stresscom (Dabur) — Ashwagandha extract for stress and hyperarousal

THERAPEUTIC PRACTICES

Yoga Nidra — PTSD recovery evidence; regulates autonomic nervous system

Shirodhara — continuous oil flow on forehead; profound calming effect on hypervigilance

Pranayama (Bhramari — humming bee breath) — immediate calming of acute hyperarousal

IMPORTANT NOTE

Ayurvedic adjuncts may help with anxiety, insomnia, and hyperarousal — but PTSD requires trauma-focused psychotherapy (TF-CBT, EMDR). Herbs do not process traumatic memories.
diseases.india_specific:
TRAUMA SOURCES IN INDIA

Sexual violence and domestic abuse — high incidence; massive underreporting due to shame and legal barriers

Religious and communal riots — partition-related intergenerational PTSD, Gujarat 2002, Delhi 2020

Natural disasters — floods (Bihar, Assam, Kerala), earthquakes (Bhuj 2001, Nepal 2015 affecting India)

Industrial disasters — Bhopal gas tragedy (1984) — multigenerational PTSD and health impact

Military veterans — Kargil, counterinsurgency operations; PTSD underdiagnosed in armed forces

Agricultural: Crop failure, debt — farmer suicides carry secondary PTSD in surviving family members

Childhood trauma — physical and sexual abuse, corporal punishment in schools

BARRIERS TO CARE

Silence and stigma — sexual trauma survivor faces family pressure not to report or seek help

Victim blaming — societal attitude prevents disclosure and treatment seeking

Very few trained trauma therapists outside major metropolitan areas

Medico-legal complications — PTSD treatment intersects with criminal proceedings in rape/abuse cases

SECONDARY COMPLICATIONS

High comorbidity with depression, substance use disorders, somatic symptom disorders

Domestic violence perpetration by PTSD-affected individuals

Intergenerational trauma transmission — children of PTSD parents at risk
diseases.important_notes:
PSYCHOTHERAPY IS THE PRIMARY TREATMENT: For PTSD, trauma-focused psychotherapy (TF-CBT, EMDR, Prolonged Exposure) is superior to medication alone. Medication addresses symptom severity but does not process the traumatic memory. Both should be combined when possible. In India, trained trauma therapists are scarce outside metro cities — refer to iCall, NIMHANS, or online TF-CBT services.

PRAZOSIN FOR NIGHTMARES: Prazosin (alpha-1 adrenergic blocker, primarily an antihypertensive) has strong RCT evidence for PTSD-related nightmares. Start at 1 mg at bedtime and titrate to 3–15 mg. Monitor for orthostatic hypotension. Highly underutilised in India for this indication.

BENZODIAZEPINES ARE CONTRAINDICATED IN PTSD: Despite widespread use in India for anxiety, benzodiazepines do NOT treat PTSD core symptoms and can worsen outcomes — they may block memory consolidation needed for trauma processing, increase avoidance behaviours, and increase substance dependency risk in an already vulnerable population.

MANDATORY SAFETY PLANNING IN SEXUAL TRAUMA: PTSD from sexual violence in India carries compounded risks — victim blaming, family pressure to remain silent, and social ostracism create additional layers. Multi-agency coordination (psychiatric, social, legal) required. One-Stop Centres (Sakhi Centres) — government-run — provide integrated support.
