diseases.name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)

Presence of obsessions, compulsions, or both; time-consuming (>1 hour/day) or causing distress or impairment; not attributable to substances or medical condition; not better explained by another mental disorder

RATING SCALES

Y-BOCS (Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) — gold standard; 10-item; 0–40 score; severe ≥24

OCI-R (OC Inventory — Revised) — 18-item self-report; screen and dimensional profile

DOCS (Dimensional Obsessive-Compulsive Scale) — 20-item; symptom dimensions

STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS

SCID-5 OCD module — diagnostic confirmation

MINI (OCD and related disorders module)

BIOLOGICAL (exclude neurological and organic causes)

MRI brain — basal ganglia, frontal-subcortical circuits abnormalities; rule out Sydenham's chorea (PANDAS precursor), Huntington's, Wilson's disease mimicking OCD

ASLO titre, anti-DNase B, throat culture — PANDAS/PANS (Paediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal Infections) — sudden onset OCD post-strep in children

EEG — rule out seizure disorder with ritualistic behaviour (temporal lobe epilepsy)

Serum copper, ceruloplasmin — Wilson's disease (psychiatric onset common in Wilson's)

LFT — liver disease and neuropsychiatric complications

CBC, metabolic panel, TFT — general screen for metabolic causes

SPECIAL FOR REFRACTORY OCD

Neuropsychological testing — frontal-executive function, set-shifting

PET-CT brain (research setting) — hyperactivity of orbitofrontal cortex, caudate, thalamus (OCD circuit)

DBS assessment protocol — for treatment-refractory severe OCD (≥2 failed adequate medication trials + ≥1 adequate ERP trial)
diseases.generic_medicines:
FIRST-LINE — SSRIs (at higher doses than for depression)

Fluvoxamine 100–300 mg/day (first SSRI approved for OCD; particularly for religious/aggressive obsessions)

Sertraline 100–250 mg/day (well-tolerated; good for paediatric OCD as well)

Fluoxetine 40–120 mg/day (long half-life; forgiving for missed doses)

Paroxetine 40–60 mg/day (approved for OCD)

Escitalopram 20–40 mg/day (evidence mounting; good tolerability)

FIRST-LINE — TCA

Clomipramine 150–300 mg/day (most potent anti-OCD agent; first-line alongside SSRIs; serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; cardiac side effects — ECG monitoring)

AUGMENTATION STRATEGIES (for partial response)

Add Risperidone 0.5–3 mg/day to SSRI — best evidence for augmentation in OCD

Add Quetiapine 25–200 mg/day — insomnia + anxiety + OCD

Add Aripiprazole 5–15 mg/day — activating; weight neutral

Add Mirtazapine 15–30 mg — OCD with prominent depression/insomnia

REFRACTORY OCD

Clomipramine IV infusion (off-label) — for severe, refractory cases

Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) — anterior limb of internal capsule; approved by FDA for refractory OCD

PSYCHOTHERAPY — GOLD STANDARD

ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention) — first-line; prevents compulsive response to obsessional trigger

Inference-Based CBT (I-CBT) — for overvalued ideation

ACT (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy) — useful adjunct for distress tolerance

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces repetitive cognitive patterns, anxiolytic; 300–600 mg/day

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — anti-anxiety, reduces hyperactivation of OCD circuit; 300–600 mg/day

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — calming, reduces compulsive urge states; 250–500 mg/day

Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — frontal lobe function support, neuroprotective

Shankhapushpi — nervine tonic; traditional use for obsessive mental states

Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina) — classical sedative for agitation and thought-racing (OCD intrusion)

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Saraswatarishta 15–20 mL BD — nervine rejuvenator for mental clarity and obsessive patterns

Brahmi Vati — classical tablet for mental disorders with compulsive and agitated components

Manasamitra Vatakam — traditional formulation for psychic and compulsive disorders

BRANDED AYURVEDIC

Mentat (Himalaya) — cognitive support, anxiety reduction in OCD spectrum

PRACTICES

Pranayama (Nadi Shodhana) — calms overactive thought processes

Mindfulness meditation — breaking automatic compulsive response to obsessive thought (supported in literature)

IMPORTANT NOTE

No Ayurvedic herb has demonstrated specific anti-OCD efficacy. ERP psychotherapy and SRI pharmacotherapy are the only evidence-based treatments. Ayurvedic medicines are strictly adjunctive.
diseases.india_specific:
RELIGIOUS SCRUPULOSITY

Religious OCD is extremely prevalent and culturally amplified in India — excessive temple rituals, purification rites, fear of committing religious transgressions, excessive pilgrimage compulsions

Difficult to distinguish from devout religious practice — requires clinical judgement and disability assessment

CULTURAL FACTORS

Purity-pollution concepts in Hindu caste system amplify contamination OCD

Gender pressures — women's OCD often involves contamination, checking household safety, and child harm obsessions in perinatal period (perinatal OCD)

Hoarding exacerbated by poverty-related beliefs about discarding — 'what if I need it someday'

DELAY TO DIAGNOSIS

Average delay before correct OCD diagnosis in India: 10–15 years

Patients often first seen by neurologists (tics), cardiologists (body-focused), gastroenterologists (contamination affecting eating)

TREATMENT ACCESS

ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention) — gold-standard psychotherapy; extremely scarce in India

High-dose SSRIs available at Jan Aushadhi — affordability improved

Severe refractory OCD: Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) — available at NIMHANS, AIIMS

PERINATAL OCD

Onset or worsening of OCD in pregnancy and postpartum is common; infanticide fears (ego-dystonic harm obsessions) are NOT a sign of risk but must be differentiated from actual psychosis
diseases.important_notes:
HIGH-DOSE SSRIs REQUIRED: OCD responds to higher SSRI doses than depression. Sertraline up to 200–250 mg, Fluoxetine up to 80–120 mg, Fluvoxamine up to 300 mg. Adequate trial = 12 weeks at maximum tolerated dose. Premature dose escalation plateau or early switching is a common cause of treatment failure.

ERP IS THE GOLD STANDARD: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) — confronting feared stimuli without performing the compulsion — achieves up to 70% response rate. Combined ERP + SSRI is more effective than either alone. Urgently refer to OCD-specialised psychologist where available. Online ERP-based resources and apps (NOCD app) now accessible in India.

FAMILY ACCOMMODATION HARMS OUTCOME: Family members who perform rituals on behalf of the patient, provide reassurance, or modify household routines to accommodate OCD maintain and worsen the disorder. Psychoeducation for family about not accommodating compulsions is a core part of treatment.

PANDAS IN CHILDREN — URGENT INVESTIGATION: Sudden dramatic onset of OCD symptoms in a child aged 3–12 years following strep throat should trigger immediate investigation (ASLO titre, anti-DNase B, throat culture) to rule out PANDAS. Treat streptococcal infection + standard OCD treatment. Prophylactic antibiotics may be required.

diseases.name: Parkinsons Disease
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (UK Brain Bank Criteria / MDS Criteria)

Bradykinesia + ≥1 of rigidity, rest tremor, or postural instability; asymmetric onset; L-DOPA responsive

RATING SCALES

MDS-UPDRS (Movement Disorder Society Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale) — standard comprehensive assessment

Hoehn and Yahr Scale — staging I–V

Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39) — quality of life

MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) — cognitive screening; <26 indicates impairment

NEUROIMAGING

MRI brain — rule out vascular parkinsonism, PSP, MSA, normal pressure hydrocephalus; PD = normal MRI

DaTscan (FP-CIT SPECT) — dopamine transporter imaging; confirms nigrostriatal degeneration; distinguishes PD from essential tremor and drug-induced Parkinsonism

FDG-PET brain — distinguishes PD from MSA, PSP (research and specialist centres)

AUTONOMIC TESTING

Tilt table test — orthostatic hypotension assessment (drop ≥20 mmHg systolic on standing)

BLOOD TESTS

Serum copper, ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urine copper — exclude Wilson's disease (young-onset)

Serum ceruloplasmin and kayser-Fleischer rings — Wilson's

Thyroid function — hypothyroidism can cause bradykinesia

Serum B12 — deficiency mimicking some PD features

GENETIC TESTING

LRRK2, PARKIN, PINK1, SNCA mutations — young-onset PD; counselling required

LEVODOPA TRIAL (DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC)

Good Levodopa response (>30% UPDRS improvement) — supports PD diagnosis

Poor response — raises suspicion for atypical Parkinsonism (PSP, MSA)

OLFACTORY TESTING

Smell identification test — anosmia supports PD (absent in essential tremor)
diseases.generic_medicines:
DOPAMINERGIC — FIRST-LINE

Levodopa + Carbidopa 25/100 mg tablets — cornerstone of PD motor treatment; multiple doses/day; protein interactions

Levodopa + Carbidopa + Entacapone (Triple combination) — extended ON time; reduces motor fluctuations

Pramipexole 0.375–4.5 mg/day (agonist; first-line young-onset; impulse control disorder risk)

Ropinirole 3–24 mg/day (agonist)

Cabergoline 1–6 mg/day (agonist; once-daily dosing)

ADJUNCTS (to Levodopa; reduce wearing-off)

Entacapone 200 mg with each Levodopa dose (COMT inhibitor; prolongs Levodopa effect)

Rasagiline 1 mg/day (MAO-B inhibitor; neuroprotective; early or adjunct)

Selegiline 5–10 mg/day (MAO-B inhibitor)

Amantadine 100–300 mg/day (anti-dyskinesia; antiviral with dopaminergic activity; reduces L-DOPA dyskinesia)

ANTICHOLINERGIC (tremor — younger patients without cognitive impairment)

Trihexyphenidyl 2–6 mg/day — tremor; AVOID in elderly (confusion, urinary retention, glaucoma)

Benztropine 0.5–6 mg/day

NON-MOTOR SYMPTOM TREATMENT

Quetiapine 12.5–100 mg (PD psychosis — avoids worsening Parkinsonism; avoids typical antipsychotics)

Clonazepam 0.25–1 mg at bedtime (REM sleep behaviour disorder)

Donepezil 5–10 mg (Parkinson's Disease Dementia)

Rivastigmine 3–12 mg/day (only cholinesterase inhibitor approved for PDD)

Fludrocortisone 0.1 mg/day / Midodrine 2.5–10 mg TID (orthostatic hypotension)

SURGICAL

Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) — subthalamic nucleus or globus pallidus; for motor fluctuations/dyskinesia unresponsive to medical management

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Mucuna pruriens (Kapikacchu / Velvet Bean) Seed Powder — natural L-DOPA source; 5–30 g defatted powder/day. Significant clinical evidence — multiple RCTs vs Levodopa showing comparable motor benefit. The most important Ayurvedic contribution to Parkinsonism treatment.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — neuroprotective; reduces neuroinflammation; adaptogen; 300–600 mg/day

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive support in PDD; neuroprotective, cholinergic enhancement; 300 mg/day

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator, neuroprotective, anti-inflammatory; 500 mg/day

Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — neuroprotective, antioxidant, cognitive preservation

Shilajit (Asphaltum punjabianum) — antioxidant, mitochondrial support, mineral-rich; 250–500 mg/day

Vacha (Acorus calamus) — CNS tonic; mild dopaminergic-like effect; traditional Indian PD adjunct

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Ksheerabala Taila — classical medicated oil for neuromuscular conditions; Nasya (nasal oil) use

Dhanwantara Taila — traditional oil for Vatavyadhi (neurological-musculoskeletal conditions)

Brahmi Ghrita — neuroprotective ghee preparation; classical use in neurological diseases

PANCHAKARMA THERAPIES

Shirodhara — continuous oil stream; reduces rigidity and tremor symptomatically

Abhyanga (warm oil massage) — reduces rigidity, improves circulation, reduces muscle pain

Nasya karma (Medicated nasal instillation) — traditional approach for CNS diseases

IMPORTANT NOTE

Mucuna pruriens contains natural L-DOPA — can interact with Levodopa therapy; timing and dose coordination required with neurologist
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

India — approximately 5.8 million PD patients; 2nd most common neurodegenerative disease

Onset typically 55–65 years; Young-Onset PD (<50 years) more common in India than Western populations

Genetic — LRRK2, PARKIN2, PINK1 mutations; Parkin mutation most common in young-onset Indian PD

TREATMENT ACCESS

Levodopa/Carbidopa widely available and affordable at Jan Aushadhi

Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) available at NIMHANS, AIIMS, CMC Vellore — publicly funded but wait times long

Caregiver burden — falls, dementia, incontinence in advanced PD create extreme strain on Indian family caregivers

DRUG-INDUCED PARKINSONISM

Common cause of secondary Parkinsonism in India — Metoclopramide (widely used antiemetic), Haloperidol, Risperidone, Reserpine-containing preparations (traditional antihypertensives used widely in rural India)

Drug-induced Parkinsonism resolves after stopping offending drug — crucial to check medication history in new Parkinsonism

DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP) — downgaze palsy, early falls, axial rigidity

Multiple System Atrophy (MSA) — autonomic failure, cerebellar, poor Levodopa response

Vascular Parkinsonism — lower body parkinsonism, no tremor, MRI white matter lesions

Wilson's disease — young onset; serum copper, ceruloplasmin, slit lamp
diseases.important_notes:
NEVER USE TYPICAL ANTIPSYCHOTICS IN PARKINSON'S PSYCHOSIS: Haloperidol, Risperidone, Olanzapine (strongly avoid), and most typical antipsychotics block D2 receptors and will catastrophically worsen Parkinsonism — leading to rigidity, akinesia, falls, and aspiration pneumonia. For PD psychosis: use ONLY Quetiapine (low-dose, off-label) or Clozapine (evidence-based; WBC monitoring). Pimavanserin (5-HT2A inverse agonist, no D2 blockade) — approved in USA but unavailable in India.

LEVODOPA TIMING AND PROTEIN INTERACTIONS: Levodopa is absorbed in the duodenum via amino acid transporters — large neutral amino acids from protein meals compete with L-DOPA absorption and cause significant fluctuations. Patients should take Levodopa 30–60 minutes before protein-containing meals. Protein can be redistributed to evening meal for complex patients. This is practically very important for Indian diet (dal, lentils, milk — protein-dense staples).

IMPULSE CONTROL DISORDERS WITH DOPAMINE AGONISTS: Pramipexole and Ropinirole can cause pathological gambling, hypersexuality, binge eating, and compulsive spending in up to 15% of patients. These are drug side effects — not character flaws. Patients and families must be specifically warned. If ICD develops — reduce or stop dopamine agonist; switch to Levodopa.

MUCUNA PRURIENS EVIDENCE: Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) seed powder contains 4–7% L-DOPA and has clinical trial evidence for comparable motor benefit to standard Levodopa in some trials. It is an important and affordable option for patients who cannot access Levodopa. Coordinate with neurologist — Mucuna DOPA content varies between preparations; standardised preparations preferred.

diseases.name: Alzheimers Disease
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (NIA-AA 2011 / DSM-5)

Cognitive decline significantly interfering with daily functioning; gradual onset and progressive decline; not explained by delirium or other psychiatric disorder; memory + ≥1 other cognitive domain

COGNITIVE SCREENING

MMSE (Mini-Mental State Examination) — 30-point scale; <24 = dementia; widely used in India

MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) — more sensitive for MCI (mild cognitive impairment); <26 = impairment

HMSE (Hindi Mental State Examination) — validated Hindi-language version; used in Indian population

ACE-III (Addenbrooke's Cognitive Examination) — more detailed; available in Hindi

CDR (Clinical Dementia Rating) — staging: 0.5 = MCI; 1 = mild; 2 = moderate; 3 = severe

NEUROIMAGING

MRI brain (structural) — hippocampal atrophy (medial temporal), parietal/frontal atrophy; rules out other causes (tumour, SDH, NPH, vascular)

CT brain — if MRI unavailable; identifies vascular lesions, atrophy, NPH (dilated ventricles)

FDG-PET brain — characteristic temporoparietal hypometabolism in Alzheimer's (specialist centres)

Amyloid-PET (florbetapir, florbetaben) — detects beta-amyloid; positive in Alzheimer's years before symptoms (research/specialist)

BLOOD TESTS — MANDATORY (exclude treatable causes)

TSH — hypothyroidism (very common in India; treatable cause)

Serum B12 and folate — deficiency causes dementia; highly treatable

CBC, LFT, RFT, electrolytes — metabolic causes

VDRL / TPHA — neurosyphilis

HIV serology — HIV-associated dementia

Fasting glucose — diabetes

Serum copper, ceruloplasmin — Wilson's disease if age <50

CSF ANALYSIS

CSF Amyloid-beta 42, p-Tau, t-Tau ratio — biomarker confirmation of Alzheimer's pathology; available specialist centres only

CSF 14-3-3 protein — Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) if rapid progression

NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL TESTING

Comprehensive neuropsychological battery — memory, language, visuospatial, executive function — mandatory for MCI and early dementia characterisation

GENETIC TESTING

ApoE4 genotype — risk factor (not diagnostic; not recommended routinely)

PSEN1, PSEN2, APP mutations — early-onset familial Alzheimer's (<65 years)
diseases.generic_medicines:
CHOLINESTERASE INHIBITORS (symptomatic; delay progression — not curative)

Donepezil 5–10 mg/day at bedtime (mild to severe Alzheimer's; most widely used; GI side effects)

Rivastigmine 3–12 mg/day oral (capsules); 4.6–13.3 mg/24 hours patch (Parkinson's Disease Dementia + Alzheimer's)

Galantamine 8–24 mg/day (allosteric nicotinic modulator additionally; GI side effects)

NMDA RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST

Memantine 5–20 mg/day (moderate to severe Alzheimer's; reduces glutamate excitotoxicity; used as monotherapy or combined with Donepezil)

COMBINATION THERAPY

Donepezil 10 mg + Memantine 20 mg — superior to either alone in moderate-severe Alzheimer's

BPSD (Behavioural and Psychological Symptoms) — specific management

Depression comorbid with Alzheimer's: Sertraline 50 mg, Escitalopram 10 mg (first-line SSRIs — mild but acceptable evidence)

Agitation/aggression: Quetiapine 12.5–100 mg (avoid typical antipsychotics in elderly — high stroke, mortality risk — 'Black Box' warning)

Sleep disturbance: Melatonin 3–9 mg at bedtime (first-line for Alzheimer's sleep disruption; safe)

Mirtazapine 15–30 mg (sleep + depression + agitation; appetite stimulation)

Wandering and severe agitation: Very low-dose Haloperidol 0.5–1 mg (only when other options exhausted — short-term due to mortality and stroke risk in elderly dementia)

VASCULAR DEMENTIA RISK FACTOR MANAGEMENT

Antihypertensives, statins, aspirin — for vascular contribution to dementia; reduces progression

NOVEL (unavailable India currently)

Lecanemab, Donanemab — anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies; approved by FDA 2023; awaiting India approval
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Donepezil 5 mg tablet

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — most extensively studied Ayurvedic herb for cognitive decline; 300–600 mg standardised extract/day; RCTs show improvement in memory, attention, information processing speed

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces beta-amyloid plaque deposition in animal models; anti-inflammatory neuroprotective; 300–600 mg/day

Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — cognitive enhancement, memory support; traditional Medhya Rasayana

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator, anti-neuroinflammatory; antioxidant; 500 mg/day

Gotu Kola / Mandukaparni (Centella asiatica) — nerve growth factor stimulation; cognitive preservation; 500 mg/day

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — neuroprotective; antioxidant; supports memory and cognitive vitality

MEDHYA RASAYANA (classical group of four cognitive tonics in Charaka Samhita)

Brahmi, Shankhapushpi, Guduchi, Mandukaparni — traditionally used for Smriti Bhramsha (cognitive decline and memory disorder)

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Brahmi Ghrita — classical ghee preparation for cognitive impairment

Saraswatarishta — fermented nervine tonic; general cognitive support

Brento (Himalaya) — Brahmi + Ashwagandha combination for elderly cognitive decline

PANCHAKARMA

Nasya (medicated nasal oil — Brahmi oil) — traditional route for CNS drugs; Alzheimer's Nasya is actively researched

Shirodhara — calming; reduces BPSD agitation; cortisol reduction

IMPORTANT NOTE

Brahmi and Ashwagandha have the strongest evidence base among Ayurvedic herbs for cognitive support. They should be prescribed as adjunctive treatment alongside Donepezil/Memantine — not as replacements.
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

India has approximately 8.8 million dementia patients (Alzheimer's & Related Disorders Society of India, ARDSI)

Dementia prevalence 7.4% age ≥65, rising sharply with age

Vascular dementia more prevalent in India compared to Alzheimer's — hypertension, diabetes, stroke (most common dementia aetiologies in India may be vascular)

RISK FACTORS IN INDIA

Hypertension, diabetes, obesity — major modifiable vascular risk factors

Cardiovascular risk factor burden higher in India than Western countries

Low education — education level protective against dementia; high illiteracy in older Indian cohorts increases risk

ApoE4 allele — genetic risk factor (lower frequency in Indian populations than European)

Head trauma (historical) — contributes to CTE-like dementia

CULTURAL AND SOCIAL

Dementia often considered normal aging in India — 'buddhape ka lakshan' (sign of old age) — late presentation

Family caregivers (daughters-in-law, daughters) bear almost entire burden — caregiver burnout, depression, economic hardship are enormous

No residential care culture in India — institutionalisation stigmatised; family expected to keep patient at home indefinitely

ARDSI — Alzheimer's and Related Disorders Society of India — support groups, telephones helpline

Limited dementia-specialist care outside major cities

TREATABLE CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (MUST EXCLUDE)

Hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, neurosyphilis, NPH (Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus), subdural haematoma, Wilson's disease, thiamine deficiency
diseases.important_notes:
EXCLUDE REVERSIBLE CAUSES BEFORE LABELLING ALZHEIMER'S: India's high prevalence of B12 deficiency (common in vegetarians), hypothyroidism, and neurosyphilis (still endemic in some areas) means that some dementia cases are from treatable metabolic/infectious causes. Every dementia patient MUST have TSH, B12, folate, CBC, LFT, RFT, VDRL tested — B12 deficiency dementia and hypothyroid dementia can fully reverse with treatment.

CAREGIVER SUPPORT IS AS IMPORTANT AS PATIENT TREATMENT: Alzheimer's caregiver burden in India falls almost entirely on women family members (daughters, daughters-in-law) without any support infrastructure. Caregiver depression, burnout, and abandonment of patients are predictable consequences without intervention. Every Alzheimer's clinic visit must address caregiver mental health. ARDSI (Alzheimer's and Related Disorders Society of India) provides helpline and support groups across major cities.

ANTIPSYCHOTICS IN ELDERLY DEMENTIA CARRY BLACK BOX WARNING: Atypical antipsychotics (Risperidone, Olanzapine, Quetiapine, Haloperidol) increase stroke and all-cause mortality in elderly dementia patients — FDA Black Box Warning. They should be used as LAST RESORT for severe, dangerous agitation or psychosis unresponsive to non-pharmacological management. Start lowest dose possible for shortest duration. Non-pharmacological approaches first: routine, structured environment, visual cues, caregiver training.

DRIVING MUST STOP: Progressive memory, visuospatial, and reaction time deterioration makes Alzheimer's patients unsafe drivers from early-moderate stage. Physicians in India have a legal duty to advise cessation of driving. Family should be clearly counselled to prevent driving. Road safety and patient safety are both at risk.

diseases.name: Migraine
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (ICHD-3 Criteria)

Migraine without aura: ≥5 attacks; 4–72 hours; unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, aggravated by activity; nausea/vomiting or photo/phonophobia

Migraine with aura: ≥2 attacks; ≥1 aura symptom (visual, sensory, speech, motor, cerebellar, retinal) fully reversible; spreading over ≥5 min; lasting 5–60 min; headache within 60 min of aura

WHEN TO IMAGE (RED FLAGS — SNNOOP10)

Sudden onset thunderclap headache (worst-ever), New headache >50 years, Progressive worsening headache, Neurological signs, Meningism, Positional headache, Triggered by Valsalva, HIV/Cancer, or fever → MRI/CT brain mandatory

MRI brain — normal in typical migraine; rules out structural causes (tumour, AVM, aneurysm, Chiari)

MRI with MRV — if cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) suspected (headache + visual + papilloedema + young woman on OCP)

NO ROUTINE IMAGING needed for typical migraine meeting ICHD-3 criteria with normal neurological examination

BLOOD TESTS (acute attack in ER)

CRP, ESR — temporal arteritis (>50 years, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness)

Thyroid function — hypothyroid headache mimicker

Blood pressure measurement — hypertensive crisis headache

DIARY DOCUMENTATION

Clinical management tool — headache diary records frequency, duration, severity, triggers, medication use (distinguishes episodic vs chronic migraine and identifies MOH)
diseases.generic_medicines:
ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT (stratified by severity)

MILD-MODERATE ATTACKS

Aspirin 900–1000 mg + Metoclopramide 10 mg (evidence-based; highly effective; Metoclopramide reduces nausea and improves absorption)

Ibuprofen 400–800 mg (first-line NSAID for acute migraine)

Naproxen 500–1000 mg

Paracetamol 1000 mg (if NSAIDs contraindicated — less effective)

MODERATE-SEVERE ATTACKS (Triptans — 5-HT1B/1D agonists)

Sumatriptan 50–100 mg oral (first triptan; gold standard) / 6 mg SC injection (fastest onset) / 20 mg nasal spray

Rizatriptan 5–10 mg oral (faster absorption; ODT wafer for vomiting)

Zolmitriptan 2.5–5 mg oral or nasal

Almotriptan 12.5 mg (fewer AEs, good tolerability)

ANTI-EMETICS (adjunct)

Metoclopramide 10 mg IV/IM/oral

Domperidone 10 mg oral

Prochlorperazine 12.5 mg IM (effective acute treatment in ER)

PREVENTIVE THERAPY (if ≥4 migraine days/month or significantly disabling)

Beta-blockers: Propranolol 40–240 mg/day, Metoprolol 50–200 mg/day (first-line; most evidence)

Topiramate 50–200 mg/day (first-line; weight loss AE may be beneficial; cognitive dulling AE)

Amitriptyline 10–75 mg at bedtime (also helps comorbid sleep and depression)

Sodium Valproate 500–1500 mg/day (effective; avoid in women of reproductive age — teratogenic)

Flunarizine 5–10 mg/day (calcium channel blocker; highly effective; widely used in India and Asia; not available in USA)

CGRP pathway (new — limited India availability)

Fremanezumab, Erenumab, Galcanezumab — monthly/quarterly injections; very effective prevention; expensive

Rimegepant, Ubrogepant (CGRP antagonist) — acute and preventive

MENSTRUAL MIGRAINE PREVENTION

Short-course Naproxen 500 mg BD from day -2 to +3 of period

Frovatriptan 2.5 mg BD from day -2 (mini-prophylaxis)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces headache frequency; anti-stress; neuroprotective

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — stress-reduction (major migraine trigger); adaptogen; 300–600 mg/day

Butterbur (Petasites hybridus) — strong clinical trial evidence for migraine prevention (comparable to pharmacotherapy); uncommon in India but relevant

Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) — clinical evidence for migraine prevention; inhibits platelet aggregation and 5-HT release; 50–150 mg/day dried leaf

Shatavari — hormonal balance (beneficial for menstrual migraine); adaptogen

Triphala — digestive and liver support; reduces inflammatory triggers

Peppermint oil (Mentha piperita) — topical 10% peppermint oil on forehead: RCT evidence comparable to Paracetamol for tension-type and mild migraine

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Shirashooladi Vajra Ras — classical Ayurvedic preparation specifically for Shiro Shoola (headache disorders)

Mahayograja Guggulu — vatashaman (vata-pacifying) for chronic headache

Saraswatarishta — nervine tonic, migraine-associated stress

PANCHAKARMA

Nasya (nasal oil instillation — Anu Taila, Brahmi Taila) — classical Ayurvedic treatment for headache and nasal-sinus disorders

Shirodhara — warm oil stream induces deep relaxation; reduces cortisol and sympathetic tone; clinical trials show benefit in chronic migraine

IMPORTANT NOTE

Feverfew and Butterbur have the strongest Western herbal medicine evidence for migraine prevention. Peppermint oil topically has RCT evidence for acute mild migraine. Shirodhara + relaxation techniques reduce migraine frequency via stress modulation.
diseases.india_specific:
PREVALENCE

Migraine prevalence 14.2% in India (approximately 175 million people) — one of world's highest burdens

Women 3x more common than men (hormonal triggers — menstrual migraine in 50% of female migraineurs)

TRIGGERS (common in India)

Skipped meals — fasting triggers are common in India (religious fasting — Navratri, Ekadashi)

Sleep irregularity — common with irregular shift work, late-night social events

Strong sunlight and heat — significant trigger in Indian climate

Menstruation — perimenstrual migraine (drop in oestrogen triggers)

Stress — examinations, workplace, domestic stress

Dietary — cheese, chocolate, citrus, alcohol (red wine), MSG (very common in Indian snacks and instant noodles)

MEDICATION OVERUSE HEADACHE IN INDIA

Analgesic overuse is EXTREMELY common in India — aspirin, ibuprofen, paracetamol freely available OTC; patients self-medicate daily

MOH = taking simple analgesics ≥15 days/month or triptans ≥10 days/month

MENSTRUAL MIGRAINE

70% of women have worsening migraine around menses; 25–30% pure menstrual migraine (migraine only days -2 to +3)

Hormone replacement and contraceptive choices critically affect migraine (OCP with oestrogen can worsen — combined OCP contraindicated in migraine with aura)
diseases.important_notes:
MEDICATION OVERUSE HEADACHE — DIAGNOSE AND BREAK THE CYCLE: MOH is the most common cause of chronic daily headache in India and the most common reason for preventive therapy failure. ANY patient using analgesics (aspirin, ibuprofen, paracetamol) ≥15 days/month or triptans ≥10 days/month is in medication overuse. To treat MOH — stop the overused medication (may cause 7–14 days of worsening withdrawal headache), bridge with prednisolone 60 mg tapered over 5 days, then start preventive therapy. This must be done WITH an explanation to the patient — not just abruptly stopping medication.

COMBINED ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILL CONTRAINDICATED IN MIGRAINE WITH AURA: The combination of oestrogen-containing contraceptive pill + migraine with aura increases ischaemic stroke risk by 6–9x. Women with migraine with aura must NOT be given combined OCP (Mala-D, Loette, Novelon). They should use progestogen-only pill, copper-T (IUCD) or barrier methods. Pure menstrual migraine management is different — short-course NSAIDs or Frovatriptan peri-menstrually.

TRIPTANS AND CARDIOVASCULAR CONTRAINDICATIONS: Triptans (Sumatriptan, Rizatriptan) cause coronary vasoconstriction and are CONTRAINDICATED in uncontrolled hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, prior stroke, Raynaud's, peripheral vascular disease, and hemiplegic migraine. Hemiplegic migraine with motor aura = absolute contraindication to triptans — use Aspirin + Metoclopramide or NSAID instead.

diseases.name: Multiple Sclerosis
diseases.tests:
REVISED McDONALD CRITERIA 2017 (space + time dissemination)

DIS: ≥2 different CNS locations on MRI (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal) OR ≥2 clinical attacks involving different CNS regions

DIT: ≥2 attacks OR simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions OR new T2 lesion on follow-up MRI

MRI BRAIN AND SPINE — CORNERSTONE

T2/FLAIR: Periventricular (ovoid; perpendicularly oriented = Dawson's fingers), juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal lesions

Gadolinium-enhancing: Active lesions (blood-brain barrier breakdown; new/acute lesions enhance; activity marker)

Spinal cord MRI: Focal lesion ≤2 vertebral segments (partial cord); myelitis

MRI spine T2 criterion: Long-segment cord lesion >3 levels suggests NMOSD not MS

VISUAL EVOKED POTENTIALS (VEP)

Prolonged P100 latency with preserved waveform — demyelinated optic nerve; confirms subclinical optic neuritis; helps DIS

SOMATOSENSORY, BRAINSTEM EVOKED POTENTIALS — additional lesion documentation

CSF ANALYSIS

Oligoclonal IgG bands (OCB) on CSF IEF — present in 90% MS; confirms intrathecal IgG synthesis

IgG index elevation

CSF cells (mild lymphocytosis <50 WBC) — higher cells suggest infection or NMOSD

SERUM ANTIBODIES (CRITICAL IN INDIA — MS MIMICS)

Anti-aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibody — NMOSD; negative in MS

Anti-MOG (Myelin Oligodendrocyte Glycoprotein) antibody — MOGAD; negative in MS

BLOOD TESTS

Serum B12, folate — subacute combined degeneration (very important mimic in India)

ANA, anti-dsDNA — SLE CNS involvement mimic

ACE, calcium — neurosarcoidosis

HTLV-1, VDRL, HIV serology

Vitamin D levels (25-OH Vitamin D)
diseases.generic_medicines:
ACUTE RELAPSE TREATMENT

IV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3–5 days — shortens relapse duration (does NOT alter long-term outcome)

Oral Methylprednisolone 500 mg/day equivalent — non-inferior to IV in PROMISE trial

DISEASE MODIFYING THERAPIES (DMT) — prevent relapse and slow disability accumulation

FIRST-LINE (injectable)

Interferon beta-1a (Avonex/Rebif) 30 mcg IM weekly / 44 mcg SC 3x/week — reduce relapse rate 30%

Interferon beta-1b (Betaferon) 250 mcg SC alternate days

Glatiramer acetate 20 mg SC daily — immune modulation; safe in pregnancy

FIRST-LINE (oral)

Dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera) 240 mg BD — moderate efficacy; flushing, GI AEs; PML risk (rare)

Teriflunomide 14 mg/day — oral; moderate efficacy; teratogenic

HIGH-EFFICACY DMTs (2nd line / aggressive RRMS)

Natalizumab 300 mg IV monthly (anti-VLA-4; 68% relapse reduction; PML risk with JC virus positivity — requires JC antibody index monitoring)

Ocrelizumab 600 mg IV 6-monthly (anti-CD20; also first-line in Primary Progressive MS; hepatitis B reactivation screen mandatory)

Cladribine (Mavenclad) 3.5 mg/kg total oral — 2-year pulse treatment; high efficacy

Alemtuzumab IV 2 courses — very high efficacy; significant autoimmune AEs (thyroid disease, ITP, nephropathy monitoring)

PROGRESSIVE MS

Ocrelizumab — only DMT approved for primary progressive MS

Siponimod — approved for active secondary progressive MS

SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT

Baclofen 10–80 mg/day (spasticity)

Amantadine 200 mg/day (fatigue)

Modafinil 100–200 mg/day (fatigue — off-label but effective)

Oxybutynin 5–15 mg/day / Solifenacin 5 mg (bladder urgency)

Amitriptyline 25–75 mg (neuropathic pain, depression)

Fampridine 10 mg BD (walking speed in stable MS)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — immunomodulator (Th1/Th2 balancing); neuroprotective; anti-inflammatory; adaptogen; 300–600 mg/day

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulatory; anti-neuroinflammatory; IMPORTANT — Guduchi can theoretically stimulate immunity; caution in autoimmune conditions; use under supervision

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive and neuroprotective support; reduces MS-associated cognitive decline; 300 mg/day

Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — potent anti-inflammatory; inhibits 5-LOX (leukotriene pathway); reduces neuroinflammation; 300–600 mg/day BD

Turmeric (Curcumin from Curcuma longa) — anti-inflammatory, antioxidant; curcumin has demonstrable anti-inflammatory effects on Th17 and cytokine pathways; piperine enhances bioavailability; 500–1000 mg/day

Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — remyelination-promoting (animal studies); neuroprotective; myelin-associated protein expression

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Ksheerabala Taila — traditionally used for Vata-vyadhi (neuromuscular conditions); Nasya + Abhyanga

Dhanwantara Taila — external use for spasticity, weakness; warm oil massage

Mahayograja Guggulu — anti-arthritic, anti-inflammatory; vata-pacifying; 1–2 tablets TID

PANCHAKARMA

Abhyanga + Svedana (warm oil massage + steam) — reduces spasticity; no RCT evidence but symptomatic benefit

Basti (medicated enema) — classically used in Ayurveda for all Vatavyadhi (neurological conditions);  oleation and elimination therapy

IMPORTANT NOTE

MSherbs study — curcumin and Boswellia are the strongest adjuncts. Immunostimulant herbs like Guduchi require caution in autoimmune conditions. DMTs must continue. Panchakarma is complementary and can help spasticity and fatigue.
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

MS prevalence in India 7–30 per 100,000 — lower than Europe/North America but rising rapidly

Younger age of onset in India (peak 25–35 years) — neurologically active reproductive years most affected

OPTICOSPINAL MS IN INDIA

Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) — anti-AQP4 antibody disease — more common in Asia and India. Mimics MS but different pathology, much more severe relapses (transverse myelitis + optic neuritis), different treatment (worse with some MS DMTs)

Anti-MOG-associated disease (MOGAD) — another MS mimic; common in South and Southeast Asia for opticospinal presentations

DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS IN INDIA — MUST EXCLUDE

Neurosarcoidosis — Indian subcontinent has high TB/sarcoid burden

Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis — young woman with relapses

Infections — HTLV-1, Lyme disease, Cryptococcal meningitis — mimicking MS

Vit B12 deficiency subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord — very common in India (vegetarians); mimics MS myelopathy

Spinocerebellar ataxias (genetic) — common in India

TREATMENT ACCESS

Basic DMTs (Interferon-beta, Glatiramer) with difficulty in government hospitals

High-efficacy DMTs (Natalizumab, Ocrelizumab, Cladribine) — expensive; private sector only

Ocrelizumab available through ROCHE Patient Assistance Program in India

VITAMIN D

Vitamin D deficiency extremely common in India despite high sun exposure (due to sunscreen use, indoor lifestyle, clothing, darker skin) — deficiency is a major modifiable MS risk factor
diseases.important_notes:
DISTINGUISH MS FROM NMOSD BEFORE STARTING DMTs: Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) and MOGAD are far more common in India than in Western populations and present very similarly to MS (optic neuritis + myelitis). CRITICAL — many MS DMTs (Interferon-beta, Natalizumab, Fingolimod) can worsen NMOSD by reducing regulatory immune responses. Anti-AQP4 and anti-MOG antibodies MUST be tested before starting MS DMTs, especially when spinal lesions are long (>3 vertebral levels), optic neuritis is severe, or recovery is poor.

VITAMIN D IN MS: Vitamin D is a major MS susceptibility gene and disease modifier. Target serum 25-OH Vitamin D ≥50 ng/mL in all MS patients. Supplementation with 2000–5000 IU/day is commonly used. This is particularly relevant in India where paradoxical Vit D deficiency is epidemic despite high sun exposure. Deficiency correlates with more relapses and faster disability progression.

JC VIRUS MONITORING FOR NATALIZUMAB: Natalizumab-associated PML (Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy) from JC virus reactivation is a potentially fatal complication. ALL patients on Natalizumab must be tested for JC antibody index every 6 months. High index (>0.9) + duration >24 months = high PML risk — switch DMT or intensify surveillance with MRI every 3 months. This is a genuinely life-threatening safety issue requiring strict monitoring.

diseases.name: Guillain-Barre Syndrome
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (Brighton Collaboration criteria / Asbury criteria)

Progressive bilateral weakness of limbs (more legs than arms) + ↓/absent deep tendon reflexes

Onset to nadir: days to 4 weeks

NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES (NCS) / ELECTROMYOGRAPHY (EMG) — CRITICAL

AIDP (demyelinating): Reduced conduction velocity, prolonged distal latencies, conduction block, temporal dispersion, prolonged F-waves

AMAN/AMSAN (axonal): Reduced/absent CMAP with relatively preserved conduction velocity (axon intact in AMAN initially before axonal degeneration)

NCS helps determine subtype and prognosis (AIDP recovers well; AMSAN slower recovery)

CSF ANALYSIS — ALBUMINOCYTOLOGICAL DISSOCIATION

Elevated CSF protein (50–400 mg/dL) with <10 cells/mm3 (WBC) — hallmark of GBS after 1–2 weeks

Normal CSF protein in first week (does NOT rule out GBS early)

CSF cells >50 — should raise suspicion for alternative diagnosis (HIV, Lyme, CMV radiculitis, carcinomatous meningitis)

ANTI-GANGLIOSIDE ANTIBODIES (IMPORTANT SUBTYPE MARKERS)

Anti-GQ1b — Miller Fisher Syndrome (95% sensitivity/specificity)

Anti-GM1, anti-GD1a — AMAN subtype (especially post-Campylobacter)

STOOL CULTURE

Campylobacter jejuni culture — positive in recent cases; ELISA serology 3–4 weeks post-onset

RESPIRATORY MONITORING

Spirometry — vital capacity (VC) and NIF (negative inspiratory force) monitoring

ABG / SpO2 monitoring

20/15/10/30 rule: Intubate if VC <20 mL/kg OR NIF worse than -30 cmH2O OR >30% decline in 24 hours

CARDIAC MONITORING

Continuous ECG telemetry — autonomic arrhythmias, heart block in severe GBS

STOOL FOR CAMPYLOBACTER if diarrhoea preceded neurological symptoms
diseases.generic_medicines:
ACUTE IMMUNOTHERAPY — EQUALLY EFFECTIVE (use one, not both)

Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIg) 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days (total 2 g/kg) — enhances recovery; neutralises pathogenic antibodies; first-line in India when available

Plasma Exchange (PE / Plasmapheresis) 5 exchanges over 1–2 weeks — removes pathogenic anti-ganglioside antibodies; equally effective as IVIg; requires specialised equipment

NO BENEFIT FROM STEROIDS: Prednisolone / Methylprednisolone has been tested in multiple trials and does NOT improve GBS outcomes — may be marginally harmful. Do NOT give corticosteroids for GBS.

SUPPORTIVE CARE (CRUCIAL)

Heparin 5000 IU SC BD or Low Molecular Weight Heparin — DVT prophylaxis (bed-bound + paralysed patients extremely high DVT/PE risk)

Bladder catheterisation — if urinary retention

NG tube — if bulbar weakness and aspiration risk

Morphine / Gabapentin / Carbamazepine — pain management (GBS pain severe and undertreated)

MECHANICAL VENTILATION

Intubation and ICU mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure — supportive; no specific drugs

AUTONOMIC

Short-acting antihypertensives (IV Labetalol, Esmolol) — hypertension

IV fluids + Noradrenaline — hypotension

Atropine — symptomatic bradycardia

REHABILITATION

Physiotherapy starting in ICU — prevent contractures, deconditioning

Graduated ambulation; occupational therapy; orthoses
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Heparin Sodium 5000 IU injection (DVT prophylaxis)

Enoxaparin 40 mg injection

Gabapentin 300 mg capsule (pain)

Carbamazepine 100 mg tablet (pain)

Morphine 5 mg/mL injection

Tramadol 50 mg capsule

Paracetamol 500 mg tablet

Methylprednisolone injection (NOT for GBS; included for completeness — NOT indicated)

Jan Aushadhi Prices

NOTE: IVIg and Plasmapheresis are not available at Jan Aushadhi — these are hospital-level expensive treatments

IVIg 5 g vial (private market estimate): Rs.4500–6000 per vial (10 vials required)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
SUPPORT IN RECOVERY PHASE ONLY (not acute treatment)

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — nerve regeneration support; neroprotective; promotes axonal regrowth; anti-inflammatory; 300–600 mg/day

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neuroprotective in recovery phase; 300 mg/day

Bala (Sida cordifolia) — traditional Ayurvedic herb for Vatashula (nerve pain) and muscle weakness; nervine tonic; 9–18 g powder/day

Ashwagandha + Bala combination — classical Vatavyadhi (neurological weakness) treatment

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator in recovery phase; general health restoration

OILS FOR NERVE REHABILITATION

Ksheerabala Taila — Abhyanga daily oil massage; classical neurological recovery oil; reduces stiffness and promotes limb movement

Karpasasthyadi Taila — traditional formulation for nerve weakness and paralysis

Dhanwantara Taila — classical Vata-vyadhi oil; neuroprotective massage

PANCHAKARMA IN RECOVERY

Abhyanga + Svedana — daily warm oil massage followed by steam; reduces residual weakness, promotes nerve recovery

Basti (oil Basti — Anuvasana Basti) — classically prescribed in Ayurveda for all nerve and muscle conditions (Vatavyadhi); medicated oil enema

Nasya — medicated oil nasal instillation for cranial nerve involvement recovery

IMPORTANT NOTE

NO Ayurvedic herb is a substitute for IVIg or Plasma Exchange in the acute phase of GBS. Ayurvedic interventions are ONLY appropriate in the recovery and rehabilitation phase to support nerve regeneration, reduce fatigue, manage residual pain, and assist in regaining mobility.
diseases.india_specific:
PRECEDING INFECTIONS (Antecedent event in 70% of GBS)

Campylobacter jejuni — most common trigger globally and in India (25–40%); particularly for axonal subtypes (AMAN/AMSAN); preceding diarrhoea 1–3 weeks; contaminated water and chicken, both extremely common in India

CMV (Cytomegalovirus) — young adults

EBV (Epstein-Barr virus)

Influenza, SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) — well-documented GBS trigger

Zika virus — epidemic-associated GBS outbreak (Brazil 2015–16; relevant to India)

AXONAL GBS IN INDIA

AMAN (Acute Motor Axonal Neuropathy) and AMSAN — anti-GM1, anti-GD1a antibody subtypes — more prevalent in India and China than Europe (where AIDP dominates). Post-Campylobacter axonal GBS

AMAN has faster and more complete recovery than AMSAN; AMSAN has severe sensorimotor axonal involvement with poor prognosis

ICU CAPACITY

Ventilator access critical in India — 25–30% GBS requires mechanical ventilation; rural India lacks this capacity

Early transfer to ventilator-equipped hospital is essential when respiratory function declining

ENDEMIC DIARRHOEAL DISEASE

India's high burden of Campylobacter and enteric infections makes GBS incidence relatively high

Post-monsoon rise in GBS incidence has been documented in India — follows seasonal waterborne infections
diseases.important_notes:
DO NOT GIVE STEROIDS IN GBS: Multiple RCTs (Dutch Guillain-Barré trial, others) have demonstrated that corticosteroids (Prednisolone, Methylprednisolone) provide no benefit in GBS and may slightly worsen outcomes. In India, where GI and systemic steroid complications are common (TB reactivation, hyperglycaemia, GI bleed), steroid use in GBS is doubly harmful. This is a very common and dangerous prescribing error — particularly in resource-limited settings where IVIg unavailable and steroids seem cheap/readily available.

RESPIRATORY MONITORING IS LIFE-SAVING: GBS is one of the few neurological emergencies where death is preventable through vigilant respiratory monitoring. VC (vital capacity) must be measured every 4–6 hours in admitted patients. Elective intubation before respiratory failure is SAFER than emergency intubation. Emergency intubation in a panicking hypoxic patient with autonomic instability is extremely dangerous. Apply the 20/15/10/30 rule: Intubate if VC <20 mL/kg OR NIF worse than -30 cmH2O OR >30% decline in <24 hours EVEN if SpO2 still normal.

IVIg AND PLASMA EXCHANGE ARE EQUALLY EFFECTIVE: Both IVIg (2 g/kg over 5 days) and Plasmapheresis (5 exchanges) reduce time to recovery and ventilator dependence by approximately 50%. They are strictly equivalent — do NOT combine (ICE trial showed no additional benefit). In India, IVIg is preferred where accessible due to simpler administration. In hospitals with plasma exchange infrastructure, either can be used. Cost of IVIg (Rs. 45,000–60,000 for full course) is a major barrier — seek Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY) coverage or hospital assistance funds.
confirmation_tests_curated: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (Brighton Collaboration criteria / Asbury criteria)
Progressive bilateral weakness of limbs (more legs than arms) + ↓/absent deep tendon reflexes
Onset to nadir: days to 4 weeks
AIDP (demyelinating): Reduced conduction velocity, prolonged distal latencies, conduction block, temporal dispersion, prolonged F-waves

diseases.name: Myasthenia Gravis
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL TESTS

Ice pack test — apply ice to ptotic eye for 2 minutes; ptosis improves in MG by ≥2 mm (sensitivity 80%); myasthenia-specific; easy, bedside, immediate

Fatigability test — sustained upgaze for 1 minute; ptosis increases; repeated deltoid testing — weakness worsens

Tensilon (Edrophonium) test — IV edrophonium 2 mg test dose then 8 mg; dramatic improvement in ptosis/diplopia within 30–60 seconds; SHORT-ACTING (90 seconds); CARDIAC MONITORING required (bradycardia/asystole risk); keep atropine 0.6 mg IV ready

NEUROPHYSIOLOGY

Repetitive Nerve Stimulation (RNS) — 3 Hz; decremental response >10% in CMAP = abnormal; sensitivity 50–80% generalised MG

Single Fibre EMG (SFEMG) — increased jitter; most sensitive test (95%); specialist investigation

ANTIBODY TESTS — CONFIRMATORY

Anti-AChR antibody (anti-acetylcholine receptor) — positive in 85% generalised MG; 50% ocular MG; GOLD STANDARD

Anti-MuSK antibody — positive in 40% of seronegative generalised MG; associated with severe bulbar/respiratory disease; female-predominant; different treatment response

Anti-LRP4 antibody — minority of double-seronegative MG

IMAGING

CT thorax (with contrast) — mandatory to exclude thymoma (anterior mediastinal mass) in ALL MG patients

MRI brain — if brainstem/CNS pathology needs exclusion

GENERAL INVESTIGATIONS

TFTs — thyroid disease associated with MG

ANA panel — other autoimmune diseases co-occur with MG (SLE, RA)

IGRA / Mantoux, CXR — before immunosuppression (TB screen)

Fasting glucose, CBC — baseline before steroids
diseases.generic_medicines:
SYMPTOMATIC TREATMENT

Pyridostigmine (Neostigmine methyl sulphate analog) 30–120 mg every 4–6 hours orally — acetylcholinesterase inhibitor; first-line symptomatic; dose titrated to symptoms; max ~480 mg/day to avoid cholinergic crisis

IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE THERAPY

Prednisolone 10–60 mg/day or alternate day dosing — most widely used; start LOW and increase slowly to avoid paradoxical worsening; long-term use with bone protection (Calcium + Vit D, Alendronate)

Azathioprine 2–3 mg/kg/day — first-line steroid-sparing agent; onset 6–18 months; check TPMT before starting; monitor CBC, LFT

Mycophenolate mofetil 1000–2000 mg/day — alternative to Azathioprine

Cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day — second-line; renal toxicity monitoring

MYASTHENIC CRISIS — ACUTE

IVIg 2 g/kg over 5 days — short-term rapid improvement

Plasma exchange (PE) 5 exchanges — equivalent to IVIg; faster onset; preferred if anti-MuSK positive

Mechanical ventilation if VC <20 mL/kg

AVOID PRECIPITATING DRUGS (see India-Specific section)

THYMECTOMY

Surgical — for thymoma (mandatory) and AChR+ generalised MG <65 years (MGTX trial): improves 3-year remission rate

Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) preferred

NOVEL AGENTS (specialist approval)

Eculizumab (anti-C5) — for severe refractory AChR+ MG

Efgartigimod (Vyvgart) — neonatal Fc receptor blocker; reduces IgG antibody levels; approved 2021

Rituximab — for anti-MuSK MG (off-label but growing evidence base)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — neuroprotective, anti-neuroinflammatory; adaptogen; may modulate neuromuscular junction; 300–600 mg/day

Bala (Sida cordifolia) — classical Ayurvedic nerve and muscle tonic; specifically indicated for vatavyadhi (neuromuscular disease); improves muscle strength; 9–18 g powder/day or extract

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neurotransmitter modulation; neuroprotective; cognitive support; 300 mg/day

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — immune modulation; adaptogen; helpful as adjunct in autoimmune myasthenic states

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator (CAUTION: in autoimmune conditions, stimulating immunity may theoretically worsen disease — careful use at low dose under supervision)

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Ashwagandha Churna + Bala combination — Vatashaman (nerve-muscle restoration); classical Rasayana pairing for neuromuscular weakness

Ksheerabala Taila — daily abhyanga (oil massage) and oral; neuroprotective Ksheerabala number 7, 21, 101 preparations (progressively processed)

Dhanwantara Taila — traditional oil for all neurological and musculoskeletal conditions

PANCHAKARMA — RECOVERY SUPPORT

Abhyanga + Svedana — warm oil massage; reduces fatigability, improves blood flow to muscles

Basti (Anuvasana Basti with Ksheerabala Taila) — neuromuscular restoration in Vatavyadhi

IMPORTANT NOTE

No Ayurvedic formulation treats the autoimmune pathology of MG. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and immunosuppressants are irreplaceable. Ayurvedic therapies may provide supportive benefit in fatigue management and general neuromuscular support in the stable phase. Guduchi use requires caution in autoimmune conditions.
diseases.india_specific:
PREVALENCE

Estimated 60,000–100,000 cases in India; likely significant underdiagnosis

Mean age of onset bimodal: young women 20–30 years, older men 60–70 years

DRUGS TO AVOID IN MG — EXTREMELY IMPORTANT IN INDIA

Aminoglycosides (Gentamicin, Amikacin, Streptomycin, Kanamycin) — widely used in India for TB, UTI, sepsis; precipitate myasthenic crisis

Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin, Moxifloxacin) — very widely prescribed in India; can worsen NMJ transmission

Beta-blockers (Propranolol widely used in India) — worsen MG

Magnesium sulfate (used in eclampsia — common obstetric drug in India; may cause MG crisis in pregnant woman with undiagnosed MG)

Chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine — anti-malarials widely used in India and for autoimmune diseases

Statins — mild worsening possible

Quinidine, quinine — anti-malarials

Penicillamine — used in rheumatoid arthritis and Wilson's disease in India; can induce drug-related MG

TUBERCULOSIS AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION

Azathioprine and steroids used for MG significantly increase TB reactivation risk — MUST screen with Mantoux/IGRA and CXR before immunosuppression; TB prophylaxis (Isoniazid) if IGRA positive

DIAGNOSIS DELAY

MG is frequently misdiagnosed in India as psychiatric (hysteria), thyroid eye disease, CVA, or brainstem tumour

THYMOMA

CT thorax mandatory in all MG diagnoses to exclude thymoma (anterior mediastinal mass)
diseases.important_notes:
DRUG SAFETY — MEDICATIONS TO ABSOLUTELY AVOID: Every MG patient must receive a written list of drugs to avoid. Most dangerous in India: Aminoglycosides (Gentamicin, Amikacin) — widely used for TB (Streptomycin), UTI, and sepsis; may precipitate fatal myasthenic crisis. Cipro/Fluoroquinolones — prescribed for virtually every infection in India. Magnesium sulfate — standard eclampsia treatment used in unbooked pregnancies where MG may be undiagnosed. Beta-blockers — used in hypertension and migraine. Patients must carry a MedAlert card listing these drugs so that any treating doctor is immediately aware.

INITIAL STEROID PARADOXICAL WORSENING: Starting Prednisolone in MG can cause dramatic worsening of weakness and even myasthenic crisis in the first 2–3 weeks, before improvement begins typically at 4–6 weeks. This is a well-documented phenomenon. Start LOW dose (10–20 mg/day) and increase gradually over months. Hospitalisation or very close monitoring recommended at steroid initiation in moderate-severe MG. The paradoxical worsening occurs because steroids transiently increase ACh release while immune effect is delayed.

TB SCREEN MANDATORY BEFORE IMMUNOSUPPRESSION: India has the world's highest TB burden. Azathioprine + Prednisolone significantly increase risk of TB reactivation. ALL MG patients must have IGRA/Mantoux test and CXR BEFORE starting immunosuppressive therapy. IGRA positive = Isoniazid 300 mg/day for 6 months prophylaxis before azathioprine. Do not skip this step — TB reactivation during immunosuppression in India carries high mortality.
confirmation_tests_curated: Ice pack test — apply ice to ptotic eye for 2 minutes; ptosis improves in MG by ≥2 mm (sensitivity 80%); myasthenia-specific; easy, bedside, immediate
Fatigability test — sustained upgaze for 1 minute; ptosis increases; repeated deltoid testing — weakness worsens

Tensilon (Edrophonium) test — IV edrophonium 2 mg test dose then 8 mg; dramatic improvement in ptosis/diplopia within 30–60 seconds; SHORT-ACTING (90 seconds); CARDIAC MONITORING required (bradycardia/asystole risk); keep atropine 0.6 mg IV ready

diseases.name: Huntingtons Disease
diseases.tests:
GENETIC TESTING — DEFINITIVE DIAGNOSIS

CAG Repeat Analysis (HTT gene, chromosome 4): Normal ≤26 repeats; Intermediate 27–35; Reduced Penetrance 36–39; Full Penetrance ≥40 (virtually 100% develop HD if live long enough)

Sensitivity 100% for full penetrance mutation; specificity 100% — no further confirmation needed with positive genetic test

CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Chorea + unequivocal family history (autosomal dominant) = clinical HD

Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale (UHDRS) — motor, cognitive, behavioural, functional capacity subscales

PSYCHOMETRIC TESTING

MMSE, MoCA — cognitive screening; neuropsychological battery (Symbol Digit Modalities Test — most sensitive early cognitive marker)

NEUROIMAGING

MRI brain — progressive bicaudate ratio increase; caudate nucleus atrophy on coronal T1; putamen atrophy; cortical atrophy

FDG-PET — early caudate hypometabolism before structural atrophy visible

DaTscan — reduced striatal dopamine transporter binding

PRESYMPTOMATIC TESTING PROTOCOL

Genetic testing in presymptomatic (at-risk) individuals requires strict International Huntington Association protocol: pre-test counselling (minimum 3 sessions) + psychiatric assessment + post-test support

NOT done without protocol — suicide risk in receiving positive result without support is documented

BLOOD TESTS (exclude other chorea causes if atypical)

Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titre — Sydenham's chorea (rheumatic fever in India)

Serum copper, ceruloplasmin — Wilson's disease (young-onset chorea in India; treatable)

ANA, antiphospholipid antibodies — lupus chorea

Thyroid function — hyperthyroid chorea

Acanthocytes on blood smear — neuroacanthocytosis
diseases.generic_medicines:
NO DISEASE-MODIFYING TREATMENT EXISTS for HD. All current pharmacotherapy is SYMPTOMATIC.

CHOREA SUPPRESSION

Tetrabenazine 12.5–100 mg/day (VMAT2 inhibitor; most effective anti-choreic agent; FDA/EMA approved; available in India; AE: depression, parkinsonism, sedation, QTc; CONTRAINDICATED with MAO-inhibitors)

Deutetrabenazine 12–48 mg/day (improved tolerability; less available in India)

Haloperidol 0.5–5 mg/day (dopamine antagonist; effective for chorea; also treats psychosis; extrapyramidal side effects)

Clonazepam 0.5–2 mg (mild chorea; anxiety; dystonia)

Amantadine 100–300 mg/day (modest anti-choreic; augments tetrabenazine)

PSYCHIATRIC SYMPTOMS

Depression: Sertraline 50–200 mg, Escitalopram 10–20 mg, Mirtazapine 15–45 mg (avoid SNRI/bupropion = seizure risk; avoid TCA in chorea)

Anxiety: SSRIs; Buspirone 15–60 mg/day (anxiolytic without sedation)

Irritability/aggression: SSRIs, Clonazepam, Quetiapine

Psychosis: Low-dose antipsychotics (Quetiapine, Haloperidol — also treats chorea)

Apathy: No reliably effective treatment; Methylphenidate may help

SLEEP

Mirtazapine, Melatonin, Clonazepam — sleep disturbance in HD

DYSPHAGIA / NUTRITION

Speech therapy assessment; modified diet consistency; PEG tube (percutaneous gastrostomy) in advanced disease

High-calorie nutrition — HD patients have markedly elevated caloric needs due to chorea; malnutrition directly worsens prognosis

INVESTIGATIONAL (active trials)

ASO (Antisense Oligonucleotide) targeting mutant HTT mRNA — Wave Life Sciences PRECISION-HD trials (failed recently), Roche GENERATION-HD1 trial halted; ongoing research

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive neuroprotector; anti-stress; modest evidence for slowing cognitive decline in neurodegeneration; 300–600 mg/day

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — potent neuroprotective (mitochondrial support), anti-neuroinflammatory; reduces oxidative stress which contributes to HD striatal neurotoxicity; 300–600 mg/day

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — antioxidant, anti-inflammatory; general neuroprotective

Shankhapushpi — nervine tonic; reduces psychiatric symtom burden adjunctively

Shatavari — adaptogen; general vitality; nutritional support

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Brahmi Ghrita — neuroprotective ghee; classical Medhya Rasayana

Saraswatarishta — nervine tonic; cognitive and mood support

Manasamitra Vatakam — classical compound for psychiatric and neurological conditions

NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT

Chyawanprash — antioxidant-rich Rasayana; supports overall vitality

Shatavari Kalpa / Ashwagandha Lehyam — Rasayana preparations for nutritional and neurological support

IMPORTANT NOTE

HD is a relentlessly progressive fatal condition. No Ayurvedic treatment alters the neurodegenerative trajectory. Ashwagandha and Brahmi may provide modest neuroprotective benefit as adjuncts. Nutritional optimisation is actually very important in HD (high caloric need) — Rasayana (nutritious tonics) serve a real role in nutritional support. Psychological support for patient and family is paramount.
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

HD prevalence estimated 1–5 per 100,000 in India; lower than European populations (CAG repeat length and founder effects differ)

Pan-ethnic — all Indian communities affected

AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT — 50% inheritance risk

Each child of affected parent has 50% risk; equal sex distribution

New (de novo) mutations rare — almost all cases familial

ANTICIPATION

CAG repeat expansions tend to increase in subsequent generations (anticipation) — earlier onset, more severe disease in each generation

Paternal transmission more likely to show anticipation than maternal

GENETIC COUNSELLING CHALLENGES IN INDIA

Predictive testing is medically available in India (AIIMS, CMC Vellore, NIMHANS)

Cultural and family pressure to know or not know genetic status

No pre-symptomatic treatment available — psychosocial preparation critical

Family planning decisions (PGD — preimplantation genetic diagnosis) — technically available at specialist fertility centres

STIGMA

HD symptoms (bizarre movements, psychiatric disturbance) cause extreme stigma; patients misdiagnosed as psychiatric or alcohol-related

Family concealment of diagnosis common in India — prevents other at-risk family members from seeking testing

SOCIAL SUPPORT DEFICIT

HD Society of India — limited infrastructure; family support groups rare outside major cities

Advanced care planning and palliative care integration essential — no curative treatment exists
diseases.important_notes:
GENETIC TESTING REQUIRES PRE- AND POST-TEST COUNSELLING PROTOCOL: Predictive genetic testing for Huntington's in at-risk individuals (parent affected) must follow the International Huntington Association and European HD Network protocol — minimum 2–3 counselling sessions before testing, psychiatric assessment, and guaranteed post-result support regardless of outcome. In India, ad hoc testing without this framework carries serious risk of suicide, depression, and family destruction. Positive result in presymptomatic testing is life-altering — partner decisions, career, children.

TETRABENAZINE AND DEPRESSION / SUICIDE RISK: Tetrabenazine effectively reduces chorea but carries significant risk of: depression (dose-dependent, worsens HD's inherent depression), suicidality (HD patients already have 4–6x suicide risk), parkinsonism, and sedation. CONTRAINDICATED if: active depression, suicidal ideation, hepatic failure, or concurrent MAO inhibitor. Start at 12.5 mg BD and titrate slowly. Monitor depression at every visit. If depression develops after starting Tetrabenazine — reduce or stop before adding antidepressant.

NUTRITIONAL MANAGEMENT IS A PRIORITY: Huntington's patients have markedly elevated caloric requirements (up to 4000–5000 cal/day in advanced disease) due to constant involuntary movement. Malnutrition, weight loss, and sarcopenia significantly worsen HD prognosis and increase fall risk. High-calorie, high-protein diet with texture modification as dysphagia progresses is medically essential — not merely supportive. Percutaneous Endoscopic Gastrostomy (PEG) feeding tube should be discussed in advance directive planning before the patient loses cognitive capacity to decide.
confirmation_tests_curated: CAG Repeat Analysis (HTT gene, chromosome 4): Normal ≤26 repeats; Intermediate 27–35; Reduced Penetrance 36–39; Full Penetrance ≥40 (virtually 100% develop HD if live long enough)
Sensitivity 100% for full penetrance mutation; specificity 100% — no further confirmation needed with positive genetic test
Chorea + unequivocal family history (autosomal dominant) = clinical HD

diseases.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder
diseases.tests:
ASD IS A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — no blood test, brain scan, or genetic test definitively diagnoses ASD

DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (DSM-5 / ICD-11)

DSM-5: Social communication deficits (Domain 1) across multiple contexts + restricted/repetitive behaviours (≥2 of Domain 2) + symptoms present in early developmental period + cause clinically significant impairment

STANDARDISED DIAGNOSTIC TOOLS

ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, Second Edition) — gold standard semi-structured observational assessment; 5 modules across ages; requires trained examiner

ADI-R (Autism Diagnostic Interview-Revised) — structured parent interview; covers developmental history; gold standard with ADOS-2

CARS-2 (Childhood Autism Rating Scale) — widely used, practical clinical tool; available Indian versions

M-CHAT-R/F (Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers) — 16–30 month screening tool; widely used in India

Developmental Behaviour Checklist (DBC) — co-occurring mental health

DEVELOPMENTAL AND COGNITIVE ASSESSMENT

Full-scale IQ — Vineland Adaptive Behaviour Scales (adaptive functioning); standardised language assessment

Developmental Paediatrician, Child Psychiatrist, Neurologist multidisciplinary team

MEDICAL INVESTIGATIONS (to exclude or identify comorbidities — not for ASD diagnosis)

Chromosomal microarray — first-line for ASD + ID (finds chromosomal abnormality in 10–15%)

Fragile X DNA testing — most common single-gene cause of ASD+ID in males; FMR1 CGG repeat analysis

MECP2 sequencing — Rett syndrome (girls with regression)

TSH — thyroid disease

Lead level — particularly in children with pica (eating non-food items)

EEG — if seizures suspected (20–30% ASD have epilepsy; clinical seizures or subclinical)

Hearing assessment (BERA / audiometry) — mandatory; hearing loss can mimic ASD

MRI brain — if seizures, focal deficits, regression, or macrocephaly

Sleep study — polysomnography if significant sleep issues
diseases.generic_medicines:
THERE IS NO MEDICATION THAT TREATS THE CORE FEATURES OF ASD (social communication, repetitive behaviours). Pharmacotherapy is for ASSOCIATED/CO-OCCURRING CONDITIONS ONLY.

IRRITABILITY, AGGRESSION, SELF-INJURY

Risperidone 0.25–3 mg/day — FDA-approved for ASD-related irritability in children 5–16 years; efficacy well-established; metabolic side effects (weight gain, hyperprolactinaemia), EPS

Aripiprazole 2–15 mg/day — FDA-approved for ASD irritability; less metabolic and prolactin AEs than Risperidone

ADHD CO-OCCURRING

Methylphenidate 0.3–1 mg/kg/day — effective for ADHD in ASD; increases tics and irritability at higher rates than neurotypical ADHD; start low

Atomoxetine 1.2 mg/kg/day — NRI; alternative to methylphenidate; fewer tic issues; QTc monitoring

ANXIETY

Fluoxetine 5–20 mg/day (SSRIs — modest evidence for anxiety in ASD; some RCT evidence; gastrointestinal activation at initiation)

Sertraline 25–100 mg/day

Buspirone 5–30 mg/day

SLEEP

Melatonin 0.5–10 mg at bedtime — first-line; excellent evidence in ASD; safe; improves sleep onset latency

REPETITIVE BEHAVIOURS / OCD-LIKE

Fluoxetine / Fluvoxamine — some RCT evidence for reducing repetitive behaviours at higher doses; not robustly replicated

GASTROINTESTINAL

Dietary interventions — gluten-free/casein-free (GFCF) diet: no robust RCT evidence but widely used; nutritional supervision required if implemented

Manage constipation with increased fibre, Lactulose, Polyethylene glycol

EPILEPSY COMORBID WITH ASD

Standard AEDs appropriate to seizure type — Levetiracetam preferred (fewer behavioural AEs VS older AEDs)

AVOID Sodium Valproate in girls of childbearing age (even if post-puberty)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — most clinically studied herb for neurodevelopmental conditions; multiple RCTs show improvement in attention, memory, behaviour in children; 150–300 mg/day in children

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces anxiety, improves adaptive functioning; neuroprotective; 125–300 mg/day in children

Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — traditionally prescribed for intellectual development in children; Medhya Rasayana; nerve growth factor stimulation; cognitive support

Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — classical Medhya Rasayana for intellectual development; memory enhancement

Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra / Licorice) — anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective; used in pediatric formulations

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Mentat syrup/tablet (Himalaya) — Brahmi + Bacopa + Mandukaparni combination; commonly used in India for ASD and developmental conditions; RCT evidence for ADHD symptoms

Saraswatarishta or Brahmi syrup — sweet traditional formulations; suitable for children

Manasamitra Vatakam — classical compound; psychiatry and neurology conditions

DIETARY APPROACHES

Cow ghee (Sarpi / Ghruta) — Rasayana component; good lipids for neurological development; Brahmi Ghrita (ghee infused with Brahmi) is classical

Triphala — constipation common in ASD; Triphala churnam 1–2 g at bedtime is effective and gentle

A2 milk and traditional whole foods — reducing ultra-processed food and artificial additives widely recommended

PANCHAKARMA (adapted for children)

Abhyanga (warm oil massage by parent) — sensory integration support; reduces anxiety; improves sleep; can be performed daily by parents at home

Shirodhara — older children; reduces anxiety and improves sleep

IMPORTANT NOTE

Brahmi has the best evidence base of any herbal intervention for neurodevelopmental conditions. Melatonin is an OTC supplement with excellent ASD sleep evidence. Abhyanga (daily home oil massage) is practical, free, evidence-informed, and doubles as sensory integration therapy for ASD children.
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

Prevalence estimates: 1 in 68–100 children in India based on limited population surveys (Goa study, AIIMS data) — likely underestimate

Recognition improving but vast diagnosis gap persists

DIAGNOSIS DELAY IN INDIA

Average age of ASD diagnosis in India: 4–6 years; vs 2–3 years in high-income countries

Causes of delay: familial attribution to late talking, maternal dietary/health explanations, pressure to NOT label child, lack of developmental paediatricians outside cities

CULTURAL FACTORS

Stigma of disability and neurodevelopmental conditions in Indian society — affects school inclusion, marriage prospects (family members), employment of family members

Boys 4:1 to girls ratio — girls often underdiagnosed and later diagnosed (masking more common in girls)

COMORBIDITIES IN INDIA — HIGH BURDEN

Gastrointestinal symptoms — constipation, food selectivity (restrictive diet); diet often limited to very specific foods

Sleep disorders — 80% of ASD children have sleep problems; particularly insomnia

Sensory processing disorder — severe in Indian context (loud festivals, crowded public spaces, strong food odours)

EARLY INTERVENTION ACCESS

ACT NOW programme, NIMHANS, AIIMS — public autism intervention programs but limited capacity

Private sector ABA therapy, speech therapy, OT — major financial burden (Rs. 5000–15,000/month)

National Trust Act (1999) and Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 — legal frameworks for disability recognition

EDUCATION

RTE 2009 mandates inclusive education — implementation highly variable

Special schools vs mainstream inclusion — debate ongoing; parental choice important
diseases.important_notes:
EARLY IDENTIFICATION AND INTERVENTION IS THE ONLY PROVEN INTERVENTION FOR CORE ASD: The single most impactful intervention for ASD is EARLY intensive behavioural and developmental intervention — ABA (Applied Behaviour Analysis), ESDM (Early Start Denver Model), speech therapy, OT — begun before age 3–4 years. Every month of delay in diagnosis = lost neuroplasticity. In India, parents must be empowered to seek assessment at the first sign of developmental concerns (no eye contact 6 months, no babbling 12 months, no words 16 months, no two-word phrases 24 months, ANY regression). M-CHAT-R screening at every 18-month well-child visit is the minimum standard.

AVOID MIRACLE CURE EXPLOITATION: ASD families in India are extremely vulnerable to expensive unproven treatments marketed as cures — stem cell therapy (Rs. 2–5 lakh/session), chelation therapy (dangerous — removes essential minerals; children have died), secretin injections, hyperbaric oxygen, highly restrictive dietary supplements sold as ASD treatments. None of these have peer-reviewed RCT evidence of efficacy. Chelation therapy, in particular, has caused deaths and is actively harmful. Physicians must pre-emptively warn families and provide reliable evidenced information.

MELATONIN IS FIRST-LINE FOR ASD SLEEP PROBLEMS: Sleep disorders affect 80% of ASD children and are among the most distressing clinical problems for families. Melatonin 0.5–5 mg at bedtime is safe, effective (meta-analysis level evidence), inexpensive (Jan Aushadhi: Rs.22 for 10 tabs), and should be first-line before any sedating medication. Prescribing antihistamines (Chlorpheniramine, Promethazine) routinely for ASD sleep is inappropriate — they cause rebound effects, cognitive dulling, and do not address the circadian disorder underlying ASD sleep dysfunction.
confirmation_tests_curated: ASD IS A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — no blood test, brain scan, or genetic test definitively diagnoses ASD

DSM-5: Social communication deficits (Domain 1) across multiple contexts + restricted/repetitive behaviours (≥2 of Domain 2) + symptoms present in early developmental period + cause clinically significant impairment
ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, Second Edition) — gold standard semi-structured observational assessment; 5 modules across ages; requires trained examiner

diseases.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
diseases.tests:
SLICC 2012 CRITERIA (need ≥4 of 11 clinical + immunological criteria OR biopsy-proven lupus nephritis WITH ANA or anti-dsDNA)

ACR/EULAR 2019 CRITERIA — more accurate; entry criterion ANA, then additive scoring across domains

ANA (ANTINUCLEAR ANTIBODY) — SCREENING TEST

ANA IF (Indirect Immunofluorescence) ≥1:80 — entry criterion; highly sensitive (95%) but NOT specific

Homogeneous nuclear pattern = most common in SLE

SPECIFIC SLE ANTIBODIES

Anti-dsDNA (double-stranded DNA) — highly specific (95%) for SLE; titres correlate with disease activity (especially nephritis); useful for monitoring

Anti-Sm (Smith) — pathognomonic for SLE; specific (99%) but less sensitive (25%); does not correlate with activity

Anti-Ro/SSA, Anti-La/SSB — Sjogren's overlap, neonatal lupus, SCLE, congenital heart block

Antiphospholipid antibodies (aCL, anti-beta2GP1, Lupus Anticoagulant) — 30% SLE; thrombosis and obstetric risk

Anti-Histone — drug-induced lupus

COMPLEMENT

C3, C4 — LOW in active SLE (consumed by immune complexes); useful activity marker

CH50 (total haemolytic complement) — very low in active lupus nephritis

COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT

Haemolysis: Coombs test (direct); reticulocyte count; peripheral film for spherocytes

Leucopenia, lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia

RENAL EVALUATION

Urine dipstick — protein, blood; Spot urinary protein:creatinine ratio; 24-hour urine protein

Serum creatinine, eGFR, urine microscopy (casts)

Renal biopsy — mandatory if nephritis suspected; ISN/RPS class guides treatment intensity

IMAGING

Echo (pericardial effusion, Libman-Sacks, left ventricular function)

CT chest (lung involvement, pleuritis)

MRI brain (NPSLE, stroke, white matter lesions)
diseases.generic_medicines:
BACKGROUND THERAPY — ALL SLE PATIENTS

Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) 200–400 mg/day (max 5 mg/kg/day) — mandatory unless contraindicated; reduces flares, organ damage, mortality; requires annual ophthalmology screening (retinopathy risk at >5 years or >200g cumulative dose)

MILD-MODERATE SLE

NSAIDs (Naproxen, Ibuprofen) — arthralgia, serositis, fever; use cautiously in renal disease, APS

Topical sunscreens (SPF ≥50) — photosensitivity management; lifestyle cornerstone

Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day — acute flares; taper over weeks to months

Azathioprine 1.5–2.5 mg/kg/day — mild-moderate SLE maintenance; skin, joints, haem, serositis

Methotrexate 15–25 mg/week — skin and joint manifestations; folate supplementation

LUPUS NEPHRITIS (class III/IV/V)

INDUCTION (first 3–6 months)

Pulse Methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg IV ×3 days then oral Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day

Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 2–3 g/day — first-line induction (especially Black, Hispanic, Asian patients — superior to CYC in ALMS study)

IV Cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus regimen: 500 mg every 2 weeks ×6 — less toxic) or NIH regimen (0.75–1 g/m2 monthly ×6) — alternative

MAINTENANCE (after remission)

MMF 1–2 g/day or Azathioprine 1.5–2 mg/kg/day for ≥3 years

SEVERE/REFRACTORY SLE

Belimumab (BLyS inhibitor) 10 mg/kg IV monthly — approved for non-renal SLE; available India; expensive

Rituximab (anti-CD20) — off-label but widely used for refractory SLE nephritis; significant India evidence

ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID SYNDROME (secondary to SLE)

Warfarin (INR 2–3 arterial; 2.5–3.5 venous) — thrombosis

Aspirin 75–100 mg/day — obstetric APS

LMWH (Enoxaparin) — pregnancy with APS
