diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator; anti-inflammatory; reduces ESR, CRP; traditionally used for Jwara (autoimmune fever); CAUTION: may stimulate immunity — use at physiological doses only; 500 mg/day

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — immunomodulatory (bidirectional); reduces cortisol; reduces autoimmune inflammation; 300–600 mg/day

Turmeric / Curcumin (Curcuma longa) — potent NF-kB inhibitor; anti-inflammatory in SLE animal models; inhibits IL-6, TNF-alpha; 500 mg BD with piperine

Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — anti-inflammatory (5-LOX inhibitor); reduces joint pain, ESR; 300–500 mg BD

Neem (Azadirachta indica) — anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory; skin disease application; 250–500 mg/day

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Rakta-shodhana (blood purification); skin conditions; anti-inflammatory; 500 mg BD

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Kaisora Guggulu — Rakta-dosha and skin/joint conditions; anti-inflammatory

Aarogyavardhini Vati — liver-protective, anti-inflammatory; hepatic health during immunosuppression

Mahamanjisthadi Kwatha — decoction for rakta-dushti (blood disorders) and skin conditions in SLE

PANCHAKARMA

Virechana (therapeutic purgation) — Pitta-pacifying; reduces autoimmune inflammatory load

Raktamokshana (blood letting) — classical for rakta-dushti (theoretical; modern use restricted)

Abhyanga with coconut/appropriate oils — joint and skin care

IMPORTANT NOTE

Curcumin has emerging evidence in SLE — reduces proteinuria and disease activity in small trials. Guduchi stimulates immunity — use under supervision; avoid during active severe flares. Hydroxychloroquine is MANDATORY and must never be replaced with herbal therapy. These are strictly adjunctive.
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

SLE prevalence in India approximately 3–4 per 100,000; 9:1 female-to-male ratio; onset 15–45 years

Darker skin = higher SLE risk and more severe presentation (anti-dsDNA titres higher, renal involvement more common)

INFECTIONS

Indian SLE patients have higher infection risk — Urinary tract infections (frequent), pneumonia, TB (TB reactivation on immunosuppression — critically important)

Infections are the most common cause of death in Indian SLE (overtaking lupus activity or organ damage)

TUBERCULOSIS

TB reactivation on cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate, azathioprine, steroids is a major India-specific concern

Mandatory IGRA/Mantoux + CXR before aggressive immunosuppression; TB prophylaxis if IGRA positive

LUPUS NEPHRITIS IN INDIA

Lupus nephritis more prevalent and severe in Indian/South Asian patients than European cohorts

CKD progression faster; ESRD earlier in Indian SLE

Hydroxychloroquine — mandatory background therapy in all SLE — reduces flares, mortality, organ damage by 30–40%

HYDROXYCHLOROQUINE AVAILABILITY

HCQ widely available in India — COVID-19 stockpiling controversy; now available; Jan Aushadhi and generic -- essential medicine

DRUG-INDUCED LUPUS IN INDIA

Hydralazine (antihypertensive — used in India), Isoniazid (TB treatment — VERY common), Procainamide, Minocycline → drug-induced lupus (ANA positive, anti-histone positive, spares kidneys and CNS)
diseases.important_notes:
HYDROXYCHLOROQUINE IS MANDATORY IN ALL SLE: HCQ 200–400 mg/day must be prescribed to virtually every SLE patient unless there is a specific contraindication (retinopathy, G6PD deficiency causing haemolysis). It reduces overall mortality by 30–40%, flare frequency, organ damage accumulation, and reduces clotting risk in APS. Many Indian physicians under-prescribe HCQ — this is a significant practice gap. Annual ophthalmological review is required but retinopathy is rare in first 5 years at correct dosing.

TUBERCULOSIS REACTIVATION — THE INDIA-SPECIFIC EMERGENCY: The triple combination of high SLE disease activity + high-dose steroid + cyclophosphamide in a country with 25% TB prevalence creates a perfect storm for TB reactivation. IGRA or Mantoux testing and CXR are mandatory before Cyclophosphamide or Mycophenolate initiation. IGRA positive = Isoniazid 300 mg/day for 6 months before starting immunosuppression. Monitor for TB symptoms throughout treatment. TB on cyclophosphamide is often disseminated (miliary) or extrapulmonary — difficult to diagnose.

PHOTOSENSITIVITY MANAGEMENT IN INDIA: Over 90% of SLE patients in India are photosensitive — direct sun exposure triggers skin flares and systemic uraemia. SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen DAILY (not just when going out — UV penetrates window glass). Long-sleeve cotton clothing, hats. Avoid peak sun hours 10am–4pm. In India, where outdoor activity, markets, religious events, and work often involve prolonged sun exposure, photosensitivity counselling must be extremely specific and culturally tailored.

diseases.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
diseases.tests:
ACR/EULAR 2010 CLASSIFICATION CRITERIA (score ≥6 = RA)

Joint involvement (score 0–5) + Serology (RF/ACPA; 0–3) + Acute phase reactants (ESR/CRP; 0–1) + Duration ≥6 weeks (0–1)

SEROLOGY — KEY TESTS

Rheumatoid Factor (RF) IgM — positive in 70–80% of RA; also positive in many other conditions (low specificity); high titre RF = more erosive, extra-articular disease

Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) / ACPA — high specificity (95-98%) for RA; present years before clinical onset; predicts erosive disease; Gold standard serology for RA

ANA — ANA positive in 30% RA; if strongly positive consider SLE/MCTD overlap

INFLAMMATORY MARKERS

ESR (erythrocyte sedimentation rate) — elevated; correlates with activity

CRP (C-reactive protein) — more sensitive, faster response to activity changes; monitors treatment response

HAEMATOLOGY

Haemogram — anaemia of chronic disease (normocytic normochromic); leucocytosis (active inflammation); thrombocytosis

IMAGING

X-ray hands and feet (PA view) — periarticular osteopenia, joint space narrowing, erosions, deformities (staging; baseline; serial monitoring for damage)

Ultrasound joints (Doppler) — synovitis, tenosynovitis, power Doppler = vascularity = active synovitis; more sensitive than exam for subclinical synovitis

MRI hands — most sensitive for early erosions, bone marrow oedema (signal of future erosion); used in difficult diagnostic cases

SYNOVIAL FLUID ANALYSIS

Aspirate: Inflammatory — WBC 2000–75,000/mm3, predominantly PMNs; protein elevated; glucose normal; no crystals (distinguishes from gout/pseudogout)

Culture/sensitivity — when septic arthritis needs exclusion

DISEASE ACTIVITY SCORES

DAS28 (Disease Activity Score 28) — calculated; remission <2.6; low activity 2.6–3.2

RAPID3, CDAI, SDAI — practical alternatives
diseases.generic_medicines:
TREAT TO TARGET: Start csDMARD immediately at diagnosis. Assess every 4–8 weeks. Escalate if not at target (remission/LDA) by 3–6 months.

FIRST-LINE csDMARDs

Methotrexate 7.5–25 mg/week (single first-line DMARD; folic acid 5 mg/week mandatory; monitor CBC, LFT; hold in infection, surgery; contraindicated in pregnancy)

Hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day (mild RA; combination partner; cardioprotective benefits)

Sulfasalazine 500 mg–3 g/day (moderate RA; useful in combination)

Leflunomide 10–20 mg/day (equally effective to MTX; teratogenic; washout with cholestyramine if pregnancy needed)

COMBINATION csDMARD (for moderate-severe RA or inadequate MTX response)

MTX + HCQ + SSZ (triple therapy) — RCT-proven efficacy comparable to biologic in MTX-naive RA (O'Dell trial)

BIOLOGIC DMARDs (after csDMARD failure)

TNF inhibitors — Etanercept, Adalimumab, Infliximab, Golimumab, Certolizumab; reduce joint destruction, CV risk; TB screen mandatory

Abatacept (CTLA4-Ig) — T-cell co-stimulation blocker; lower TB risk than TNF-i

Rituximab (anti-CD20) — second-line biologics; cost-effective; Indian biosimilar available; monthly infusions ×2

Tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor inhibitor) — effective for RA and systemic features; can mask fever of infection

JAK INHIBITORS (targeted synthetic DMARDs)

Baricitinib 2–4 mg/day, Tofacitinib 5 mg BD, Upadacitinib 15 mg/day — oral; effective; thrombosis, HZV reactivation, lipid risk; not first-line

SYMPTOM RELIEF (bridge therapy while awaiting DMARD effect)

NSAIDs — short-term; gastroprotection (PPI) mandatory

Prednisolone 5–10 mg/day bridge (taper when DMARDs take effect); or intra-articular steroids for few active joints

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — potent anti-inflammatory (5-LOX inhibitor, inhibits leukotriene synthesis); multiple RCTs in RA showing pain and swelling reduction; 400–750 mg extract BD; Sallaki branded tablet widely available

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — anti-arthritic properties in multiple animal models; reduces RF, ESR; adaptogen for RA-related fatigue; 300–600 mg/day

Guggulu (Commiphora wightii / Mukul Myrrh) — anti-inflammatory, anti-arthritic; multiple clinical trials; the cornered of Ayurvedic arthritis management

Turmeric / Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; curcumin 500–1500 mg/day with piperine; pain and swelling reduction RCTs in RA; inhibits TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6

Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) — external application as leaf pack and oil for joint pain; anti-inflammatory

Eranda (Ricinus communis / Castor oil) — traditional Vatavyadhi (joint disease) treatment; Eranda Paka

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Yogaraj Guggulu — widely used tablet for all forms of joint disease; anti-inflammatory, Vata-pacifying

Mahayograja Guggulu — stronger; severe arthritis

Rasnadi Guggulu — anti-inflammatory for Ama-vata (classical Ayurvedic equivalent of RA)

Simhanada Guggulu — Ama-pachana (toxin elimination) and arthritis

Punarnavadi Mandura — anaemia of chronic disease; iron supplement

PANCHAKARMA

Virechana (therapeutic purgation) — Ama nishkarana (excreting accumulated inflammatory products); classic RA treatment

Basti (Kshara Basti, Dashamoola Basti) — joint inflammation reduction; Vatahara

Janubasti (knee-specific oil pooling) — local oil treatment for knee RA

Pinda Sweda (rice bolus fomentation) — reduces joint pain and stiffness

IMPORTANT NOTE

Shallaki (Boswellia) and Curcumin have the strongest Ayurvedic evidence for RA — equivalent to moderate NSAIDs in some RCTs. However, they do NOT prevent joint erosion and progression. DMARDs (Methotrexate) must continue. The combination of evidence-based DMARDs + Shallaki + Curcumin adjunctively is reasonable and may reduce overall NSAID requirements.
diseases.india_specific:
PREVALENCE

RA prevalence 0.5–1% in India; approximately 5–7 million patients

Women 3–4x more common; onset 35–55 years

TREATMENT GAP

Huge treatment gap — early RA often diagnosed late in India; rural patients present with established deformities

Treat-to-target (T2T) strategy underimplemented; rheumatology workforce limited

TUBERCULOSIS AND DMARDS

Methotrexate — mild increase in infection risk; pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia risk (rare)

Leflunomide — similar risk profile

Biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors: Etanercept, Adalimumab) — SIGNIFICANT TB REACTIVATION RISK

India TB guideline: MANDATORY IGRA/Mantoux + CXR before ALL biologic DMARDs; Isoniazid prophylaxis for 6–9 months if LTBI positive BEFORE starting biologic

TNF inhibitors in active TB = potentially fatal dissemination

GENERIC BIOLOGICS

Indian biologic manufacturers (Cipla, Sun Pharma, Zydus) produce biosimilars of Adalimumab, Etanercept, Rituximab at 20–30% of innovator price — significantly improves access

PUBLIC SECTOR ACCESS

Methotrexate and HCQ nationally available at Jan Aushadhi; biologic DMARDs not yet in national procurement

AYURVEDIC MISMANAGEMENT

Many RA patients in India delay effective DMARDs by 1–5 years using only Ayurvedic treatment; joint destruction occurs during this delay

COMORBIDITIES

RA + hypothyroidism — common co-occurrence in India; both autoimmune

Anaemia of chronic disease — very common; affects functional capacity
diseases.important_notes:
EARLY AGGRESSIVE TREATMENT IS JOINT-SAVING: Joint damage in RA begins within weeks of onset and is irreversible. The 'window of opportunity' for treatment response is the first 3–6 months. Every month of diagnostic delay = permanent joint damage. Any patient in India with symmetric small joint arthritis lasting >6 weeks MUST be referred to a rheumatologist immediately — NOT managed with only NSAIDs and analgesics while awaiting spontaneous improvement. Treat to target (T2T) with Methotrexate escalation or combination csDMARDs is standard of care.

METHOTREXATE MONITORING IS MANDATORY: MTX hepatotoxicity, pulmonary toxicity, and bone marrow suppression require CBC and LFT checking every 4–6 weeks when stable. Common Indian pitfall — prescribing MTX without any monitoring, patients continue for years without blood tests. MTX + alcohol is DANGEROUS — absolute contraindication. MTX + co-trimoxazole (Bactrim — widely used antibiotic in India) = folate antagonism + increased MTX toxicity — must counsel about avoiding this combination.

TB SCREEN BEFORE BIOLOGICS IS NON-NEGOTIABLE: TNF inhibitors (Etanercept, Adalimumab) significantly reactivate latent TB. India has the highest TB burden globally. Biologics in India without mandatory prior TB screening, IGRA testing, and prophylaxis have caused numerous documented TB deaths. This applies equally to IGRA-negative patients where false negatives occur in immunosuppressed individuals. When in doubt — give Isoniazid prophylaxis. Annual chest X-ray and clinical TB vigilance must continue throughout biologic therapy.

diseases.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
diseases.tests:
ASAS CLASSIFICATION CRITERIA (axial SpA)

Age <45 + chronic back pain ≥3 months + EITHER: (sacroiliitis on imaging + ≥1 SpA feature) OR (HLA-B27 positive + ≥2 SpA features)

ASSESSMET TOOLS

BASDAI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index) — patient-reported; 0–10; ≥4 = active disease

BASFI (Bath AS Functional Index) — functional impairment

Modified Schober test — lumbar flexion assessment

Chest expansion measurement — costovertebral joint involvement

IMAGING

X-ray pelvis (SIJ views) — sacroiliitis Grading I–IV; grade ≥II bilateral or ≥III unilateral = radiographic criterion; normal X-ray does NOT exclude early AS

MRI sacroiliac joints (STIR sequence) — bone marrow oedema = active inflammation; detects AS years before X-ray changes; crucial for nr-axSpA diagnosis

X-ray lumbar spine (lateral) — syndesmophytes (shiny corner sign, Anderson lesion), squaring of vertebrae, bamboo spine (late)

LABORATORY

HLA-B27 — positive 75–90% of AS; NOT diagnostic alone (8% general population positive; most positive never develop AS); but supports diagnosis

ESR, CRP — elevated in active disease (but normal in 30–50% despite active inflammation; especially men)

RF, anti-CCP — NEGATIVE (distinguishes AS from RA)

ANA — usually negative

CBC, RFT, LFT — baseline and monitoring

UVEITIS WORKUP

Slit lamp examination (anterior segment) — cells and flare in AAU

Rheumatology + Ophthalmology co-management
diseases.generic_medicines:
NSAIDs — FIRST-LINE (for pain and inflammation)

Naproxen 250–500 mg BD, Ibuprofen 400–800 mg TDS, Diclofenac 75 mg BD — continuous use (not just PRN) reduces bone formation/progression in AS

Aceclofenac 100 mg BD (popular in India)

Celecoxib 200 mg OD-BD (COX-2 inhibitor; better GI safety; evidence for slowing radiographic progression in AS)

ALL NSAIDs: Gastroprotection with PPI mandatory; assess CV and renal risk

BIOLOGICAL DMARDs — TNF INHIBITORS (after failure of ≥2 NSAIDs at adequate dose)

Adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks (widely used; Indian biosimilar available)

Etanercept 25 mg SC twice/week or 50 mg SC once/week

Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 6–8 weeks

Golimumab 50 mg SC monthly

Certolizumab pegol 200 mg SC every 2 weeks

IL-17A INHIBITORS (better for skin psoriasis; comparable spine efficacy to TNF-i; effective in TNF-failure)

Secukinumab 150–300 mg SC every 4 weeks (after loading)

Ixekizumab 80 mg SC every 4 weeks (after loading)

JAK INHIBITORS

Upadacitinib 15 mg/day, Tofacitinib 5 mg BD — oral; approved for AS

csDMARDs — LIMITED ROLE IN AXIAL AS

Sulfasalazine 500 mg–3 g/day — ONLY for peripheral arthritis; NOT effective for axial disease

Methotrexate — NOT effective for axial AS; used if peripheral involvement

PHYSIOTHERAPY

Daily extension exercises + pool hydrotherapy + posture training = Essential adjunct (as important as medication)

ACUTE UVEITIS

Topical steroids (Prednisolone eye drops) + Cyclopentolate (mydriatic) — ophthalmologist-led; urgent

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — strong evidence for ankylosing spondylitis pain and stiffness; 5-LOX anti-inflammatory; 400–800 mg BD; Shallaki tablets widely available

Guggulu (Commiphora wightii) — anti-inflammatory for Amavata and Vata-Rakta; joint conditions; Yogaraj Guggulu / Mahayograja Guggulu

Eranda (Castor oil / Ricinus communis) — Eranda Paka or castor oil basti specifically for lumbosacral Vata disorders including sacral back pain; 10–20 mL castor oil in warm milk at night

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — anti-inflammatory; adaptogenic; reduces morning stiffness; 300–600 mg/day

Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) — anti-inflammatory; leaf poultice externally for spine and SIJ pain; Nirgundi oil for Abhyanga

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Yogaraj Guggulu — standard preparation for all arthritis; Trikatu (digestive) + Guggulu combination

Simhanada Guggulu — Ama pachana; inflammatory arthritis

Rasnasaptaka Kwatha — decoction formulation; anti-inflammatory; lumbosacral disease classical use

Brihat Vata Chintamani Ras — classical metallic preparation; severe Vatavyadhi neurological/musculoskeletal; specialist supervision

PANCHAKARMA

Kati Basti — localised warm medicated oil retention on lumbosacral area; significantly reduces stiffness and pain in AS; practical outpatient procedure

Greeva Basti — cervical basti for cervical spine AS involvement

Prishtha Basti — full spinal oil treatment

Basti (Kshara Basti + Anuvasana Basti alternating) — classical Vatahara (Vata-pacifying) treatment; sacroiliac and lumbar AS

Shashtika Shali Pinda Sweda — warm rice-milk bolus massage; reduces stiffness and pain; classical for spine disorders

IMPORTANT NOTE

Shallaki (Boswellia) has the strongest evidence in spondyloarthropathy. Castor oil (Eranda) and Kati Basti (lumbosacral oil pooling) are practical and reasonably evidenced for low back and sacroiliac pain in AS. Exercise is crucially more important than any herb formula in AS outcome.
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

AS prevalence India approximately 0.1–0.5%; predominantly men (3:1); onset 20–30 years

HLA-B27 frequency: 6–8% in Indian general population vs 8–10% in white Europeans

HLA-B27 in AS patients in India: 75–88%

DIAGNOSIS DELAY

Average delay from symptom onset to AS diagnosis in India: 8–10 years

Chronic back pain extremely common in India; almost always attributed to muscular or disc disease; IBP criteria almost never applied in primary care

Radiographic AS (X-ray sacroiliitis) takes 7–10 years to develop — X-ray normal in early disease (nr-axSpA)

BIOLOGIC ACCESS — INDIA

TNF inhibitors (Adalimumab, Etanercept, Infliximab) — highly effective; dramatically reduce pain, stiffness, prevent progression

Indian biosimilars available (ZRC-3197 Etanercept, biosimilar Adalimumab) — 25–40% of innovator cost

TB screening mandatory before all biologics (same as RA — India-specific concern)

IL-17 inhibitors (Secukinumab, Ixekizumab) — very effective but expensive; limited government coverage

EXERCISE — ESSENTIAL

Physiotherapy and exercise are cornerstones of AS management — NOT optional; stretching, posture correction, spine extension exercises daily; swimming optimal

Patients in India often not referred to physiotherapy; exercise compliance poor without structured programme
diseases.important_notes:
EXERCISE IS NOT OPTIONAL — IT IS TREATMENT: In AS, physical therapy and daily spinal exercises are as evidence-based as NSAIDs for maintaining spinal mobility and preventing fusion. Unlike RA where rest during flares is appropriate, in AS patients must exercise even through flares. Morning hot shower + stretching routine + supervised physiotherapy program is standard of care. Swimming (hydrotherapy) is optimal — removes gravitational load while exercising. In India, patients frequently told only to rest — this accelerates spinal fusion. Every AS patient needs a written daily exercise programme.

NSAIDs GIVEN CONTINUOUSLY — NOT AS NEEDED: Unlike mechanical back pain where NSAIDs are given PRN (as needed), in AS the evidence suggests that continuous NSAID use (not just when painful) may retard radiographic progression (syndesmophyte formation) by reducing inflammation before bone remodelling occurs. Celecoxib 200 mg daily for 2 years was shown in RCTs to significantly reduce X-ray progression in AS. Gastroprotection with PPI is mandatory for long-term continuous NSAID therapy.

UVEITIS IS AN OPHTHALMIC EMERGENCY: Acute Anterior Uveitis occurs in 30–40% of AS patients, is often the first extra-articular complication, and can cause permanent visual impairment, synechiae, glaucoma, and cataracts if untreated. Patients must be counselled proactively: sudden unilateral painful red eye + photophobia = immediate ophthalmologic emergency — present to eye casualty same day, not the next day, not with self-purchased eye drops from a chemist. HLA-B27 positive uveitis recurs frequently and requires rheumatology-ophthalmology shared care.

diseases.name: Sjogrens Syndrome
diseases.tests:
ACR/EULAR 2016 CRITERIA — CLASSIFICATION

Entry criteria: dry eye or dry mouth symptoms

Lip biopsy (minor salivary gland biopsy) focal lymphocytic sialadenitis, focus score ≥1 = 3 points (weight 3)

Anti-Ro/SSA positive = 3 points

Ocular Staining Score ≥5 = 1 point

Schirmer test ≤5 mm/5 min = 1 point

Unstimulated salivary flow ≤0.1 mL/min = 1 point

Score ≥4 = classifiable as primary Sjogren's

AUTOANTIBODIES

Anti-Ro/SSA — 60–70% positive (major Sjogren's autoantibody)

Anti-La/SSB — 30–40% positive (more specific with anti-Ro)

ANA (speckled pattern in 80%), RF positive 50–60%

Anti-dsDNA — low titre positive in some; if high = consider SLE overlap

OCULAR TESTS

Schirmer test — tear wetting < 5 mm/5 min = abnormal

Ocular surface staining (Rose Bengal or Lissamine Green) — corneal and conjunctival staining score

Tear break-up time (TBUT) — < 10 seconds = abnormal

SALIVARY GLAND

Unstimulated whole salivary flow (UWSF) < 0.1 mL/min = xerostomia

Minor salivary gland biopsy (lip biopsy) — gold standard for focal lymphocytic sialadenitis

Parotid gland ultrasound — inhomogeneity, cysts (structural gland architecture damage)

SEROLOGY AND MONITORING

CBC (anaemia, leucopenia), ESR/CRP

Immunoglobulins (IgG elevated — polyclonal hypergammaglobulinaemia), cryoglobulins

C3/C4 (low C4 = cryoglobulinaemia risk = lymphoma risk)

Beta-2 microglobulin (elevated = lymphoma risk marker)

Serum protein electrophoresis (monoclonal band = lymphoma)

LDH (elevated = lymphoma activity)
diseases.generic_medicines:
SICCA MANAGEMENT (LOCAL)

Artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose 0.5–1%, hydroxypropyl methylcellulose) — multiple times daily; preservative-free preferred for frequent use

Sodium Hyaluronate eye drops — superior viscosity and retention; preferred for severe dry eye

Punctal plugs — ophthalmological procedure; blocks tear drainage to retain moisture

Lubricant eye ointment (Paraffin base) — night-time use

Saliva substitutes / Oral gels — xylitol-based mouth-wash, carboxymethylcellulose oral gel

Pilocarpine 5 mg TDS — muscarinic agonist; stimulates residual salivary and lacrimal function; SE=sweating, bradycardia, nausea; contraindicated in asthma, narrow angle glaucoma

Cevimeline — alternative muscarinic agonist; better tolerated; not widely available India

SYSTEMIC DISEASE

Hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day — reduces arthralgias, fatigue, constitutional symptoms; may reduce lymphoma risk; background Sjogren's therapy

Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day — acute extraglandular flares (ILD, vasculitis, glomerulonephritis)

Azathioprine 1.5–2.5 mg/kg/day — steroid-sparing; ILD, systemic disease maintenance

Mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–2 g/day — ILD, renal tubular disease

Rituximab — refractory extraglandular Sjogren's (severe ILD, vasculitis, neuropathy, MALT lymphoma); improving evidence

ORAL CARE

Daily fluoride toothpaste + fluoride gel/varnish — dental caries prevention (mandatory)

Antifungal: Fluconazole 150 mg single dose — oral candidiasis

Nystatin oral suspension — recurrent oral candidiasis

NEUROPATHY

Pregabalin 75–150 mg BD, Duloxetine 60 mg/day — painful peripheral neuropathy

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
EXTERNAL EYE CARE

Rose water (Gulab Jal) — organic, preservative-free; eye wash and drops; immediate symptomatic relief for dry eyes in India; widely available and culturally accepted

Triphala Ghrita eye drops / Triphala wash — reduces ocular surface inflammation; Triphala decoction as eye wash — traditional Akshi-tarpana base

Akshi Tarpana (medicated ghee eye pooling) — Panchakarma procedure; ghee (clarified butter = base fat for Ayurvedic eye preparation) pooled around orbit using dough ring; classical treatment for dry eyes, refractory to drops

Saindhavadi Taila for nasal and oral cavity lubrication

INTERNAL

Triphala churna 1–3 g at night — Tridosha balancing; particularly Pitta-Kapha in Srotas (channels); traditional for eye health and mucous membrane health

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — mucosal tonic; reduces dryness of all mucous membranes (Rasayana property); 500 mg–1 g BD; particularly indicated in women

Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra / Licorice) — mucosal moisture; anti-inflammatory; throat, GI, and oral mucosa; 500 mg BD; CAUTION: avoid in hypertension (licorice increases BP)

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulatory; anti-inflammatory; useful adjunct at physiological doses

Amalaki (Emblica officinalis / Amla) — highest food source of Vitamin C; antioxidant; mucous membrane support; 500 mg–3 g/day

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Drakshadi Kashaya — Pitta-Kapha dosha; mucous membrane conditions

Itrifal Shahtara (Unani) — eye and mucous membrane health

IMPORTANT NOTE

Rose water and Triphala eye washes are the most practically useful Ayurvedic approaches for Sjogren's dry eye in India — safe, accessible, inexpensive. Akshi Tarpana (Panchakarma) has small clinical evidence for dry eye improvement. Shatavari for mucosal dryness is well-tolerated and evidence-supported in animal models. None of these address the systemic autoimmune disease — HCQ and sicca-specific pharmacotherapy must continue.
diseases.india_specific:
DIAGNOSIS GAP

Sjogren's is significantly under-diagnosed in India; symptoms attributed to ageing, vitamin deficiency, or menopause; delayed diagnosis 5–10 years typical

Dry eye extremely common complaint in Indian practice — most attributed to screen use, environment, allergy; Sjogren's not consistently excluded

HEAT AND ENVIRONMENT

Indian climate (heat, dust, pollution, ceiling fans) dramatically worsens xerophthalmia and xerostomia; patients need extra lubricant drops in hot-dry months

Air conditioning in offices + ceiling fan at night = major Sjogren's symptom trigger

ORAL HEALTH

Progressive dental caries from absent saliva — Indian public dental health access is limited; early specialist dentistry + fluoride prevention must be part of Sjogren's care plan

Oral candidiasis (thrush) common in Sjogren's dry mouth + India dental hygiene access limitations

SECONDARY SJOGREN'S

Secondary Sjogren's in India often overlooked in RA, SLE, and SSc patients — routinely screen all these patients for dry eyes/mouth

ANTI-RO PREGNANCY

Congenital heart block due to anti-Ro in pregnancy is a Sjogren's-specific India-relevant concern — all pregnant women with Sjogren's or anti-Ro/La need foetal echocardiogram serial monitoring from 16 weeks
diseases.important_notes:
LYMPHOMA SURVEILLANCE IS ESSENTIAL: Sjogren's carries a 5–10x elevated lifetime risk of MALT lymphoma compared to the general population. High-risk features that must trigger urgent haematological workup: rapidly enlarging salivary gland (especially unilateral), generalised lymphadenopathy, persistent fever, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, recurrent epistaxis, cryoglobulinaemia, persistently low C4, rising beta-2 microglobulin, or monoclonal band on serum protein electrophoresis. All Sjogren's patients need annual examination of salivary glands and lymph nodes, and low threshold for CT/PET and biopsy when suspicious.

DENTAL CARE IS MEDICAL CARE: Loss of saliva (xerostomia) in Sjogren's creates continuous acid demineralisation of teeth — leading to rapid, diffuse dental caries within 24 months of onset if unmanaged. This is NOT a cosmetic issue. Daily fluoride gel application (tray or brush-on), chlorhexidine rinse, strict twice-daily brushing, 3-monthly dental review, and sugar restriction are mandatory. In India, where dental care access and health insurance are limited, patients need very specific and actionable dental prevention education from their rheumatologist at diagnosis.

ANTI-RO PREGNANCY MONITORING: Every Sjogren's patient (and any patient with anti-Ro/SSA antibody, even without formal Sjogren's diagnosis) who becomes pregnant requires serial foetal echocardiograms from 16–26 weeks gestation, because maternal anti-Ro antibodies cross the placenta and can cause congenital third-degree heart block (congenital heart block — CHB) in the foetus. CHB once complete is not reversible and may require foetal pacing. The risk per anti-Ro positive pregnancy is 2–3% (15–17% in subsequent pregnancies post-affected child). This counselling must happen BEFORE pregnancy.
confirmation_tests_curated: Entry criteria: dry eye or dry mouth symptoms
Lip biopsy (minor salivary gland biopsy) focal lymphocytic sialadenitis, focus score ≥1 = 3 points (weight 3)

diseases.name: Systemic Sclerosis
diseases.tests:
ACR/EULAR 2013 CRITERIA — Total score ≥9 = SSc

Skin thickening of fingers of both hands extending proximal to MCPs (sufficient alone — score 9)

Skin thickening of fingers (sclerodactyly, puffy fingers), digital tip lesions (ulcers, pitting scars), telangiectasia, abnormal nailfold capillaries, PAH/ILD, Raynaud's, SSc-related autoantibody

AUTOANTIBODIES (highly specific SSc antibodies)

Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) — diffuse SSc; ILD risk; poor prognosis

Anti-centromere antibody (ACA) — limited SSc (CREST); PAH risk; better skin prognosis

Anti-RNA polymerase III — diffuse SSc; SRC risk; malignancy association

ANA — 95%+ positive (speckled or centromere pattern)

NAILFOLD CAPILLAROSCOPY

SSc pattern: dilated, avascular, haemorrhagic capillaries — early, active, late SSc patterns

ORGAN ASSESSMENT

LUNGS

HRCT thorax — ILD: ground-glass opacity + reticulation ± honeycombing (NSIP pattern)

PFT (spirometry + DLCO) — FVC, DLCO decline = ILD or PAH; monitoring tool

Six-minute walk test (6MWT) — functional capacity in PAH/ILD

HEART

Echocardiography — RVSP (right ventricular systolic pressure) elevation = PAH screen; pericardial effusion; LV function

Right heart catheterisation — definitive PAH diagnosis (mPAP >20 mmHg with PAWP ≤15 mmHg and PVR >2 WU)

RENAL

BP monitoring (daily home BP in dcSSc), serum creatinine, urine protein — SRC surveillance

GI

Barium swallow / Upper GI endoscopy — oesophageal dysmotility, Barrett's

Gastric emptying study — gastroparesis
diseases.generic_medicines:
RAYNAUD'S AND DIGITAL ULCERS

Nifedipine LA 30–60 mg/day (calcium channel blocker) — first-line vasodilator for Raynaud's; reduces frequency and severity of attacks

Amlodipine 5–10 mg/day — alternative CCB

Sildenafil 25–50 mg BD-TDS — PDE-5 inhibitor; vasodilation; digital ulcer healing; also PAH

Iloprost IV infusion — prostacyclin analogue; severe Raynaud's and digital ulcers; hospitalisation required

Bosentan (endothelin receptor antagonist) — digital ulcer prevention in severe SSc; PAH; teratogenic; monthly LFT required

ILD

Mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–3 g/day — first-line SSc-ILD (SLS II trial equivalent to CYC with better tolerance)

Nintedanib 150 mg BD — antifibrotic; slows ILD progression in SSc-ILD (SENSCIS trial); nausea, diarrhoea; hepatotoxicity

Cyclophosphamide IV 600 mg/m2 monthly ×6 — alternative/resource-limited option

PAH

Sildenafil 20–50 mg TDS (WHO Group I PAH)

Bosentan 62.5–125 mg BD (endothelin antagonist)

Macitentan 10 mg/day, Riociguat 1–2.5 mg TDS — more potent options

Ambrisentan 5–10 mg/day

GERD AND GI

High-dose PPI (Omeprazole 40 mg BD, Pantoprazole 40 mg BD) — oesophageal disease; continuous long-term

Metoclopramide, Domperidone — gastroparesis; dysmotility

Probiotic + Rifaximin — small intestinal bacterial overgrowth

SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS — EMERGENCY

Captopril 6.25–25 mg every 8 hours (ACE inhibitor of choice; titrate to control BP rapidly; use EVEN in AKI)

SKIN

Regular emollient (petroleum jelly, Vaseline, thick creams) — skin moisturising; fibrosis = extreme dryness

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
RAYNAUD'S AND SKIN — VASCULAR

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — circulatory enhancement; vasodilatory properties at clinical doses; 300 mg/day

Ginkgo biloba — vasodilatory; improves peripheral circulation; small evidence in Raynaud's; 120 mg/day

Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna) — cardiovascular tonic; Hrit Dharaniya (heart-supporting); 500 mg BD

SKIN AND FIBROSIS

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Rakta-shodhana (blood purification); skin health; anti-fibrotic properties in animal models; 500 mg BD

Kanchanar Guggulu — classically indicated for Gandamala (glandular/nodular conditions), fibrotic conditions, thyroid; anti-fibrotic in traditional use; widely used in India for scleroderma-like skin conditions

Guggulu (Commiphora wightii) — anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic; Triphala Guggulu; reduces fibrotic tissue formation

Shilajit (Asphaltum punjabianum) — Rasayana (rejuvenative); mitochondrial support; connective tissue conditions; 250–500 mg/day

INTERNAL MUCOSAL AND GI

Yashtimadhu (Licorice) — mucosal protection; oesophageal symptoms; anti-ulcer; 250 mg BD (caution hypertension)

Avipattikar Churna — acidity, reflux, GERD symptoms; Pitta-reducing

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — mucosal nourishment; GI health; 500 mg–1 g BD

PANCHAKARMA

Abhyanga (full body Sesame oil massage) — skin nourishment, subcutaneous fibrosis softening; daily practice clinically recommended

Shirodhara — autonomic system calming; Raynaud's-related anxiety and vasospasm

Basti (medicated enema) — Vatahara; GI dysmotility and intestinal health

IMPORTANT NOTE

Kanchanar Guggulu is the most specifically indicated Ayurvedic compound for SSc in classical and modern Indian practice. Regular Sesame oil Abhyanga for skin management is practical and beneficial. GI symptoms respond to Yashtimadhu and Shatavari. No Ayurvedic intervention can reverse established fibrosis or pulmonary hypertension — biomedical management of ILD, PAH, and SRC is critical and life-saving.
diseases.india_specific:
HEAT AND RAYNAUD'S

Indian climate generally protective for severe cold-induced Raynaud's episodes — however air conditioning, cold food from refrigerators, ice water, cold bathing → triggers in urban India

Digital ulcers still occur in Indian SSc despite ambient warmth — suggest more prominent vasculopathy component

DIAGNOSIS

SSc is diagnosed late in India — skin thickening attributed to 'tight skin' or traditional explanations; specialised Rheumatology care for SSc is limited to major cities

Nailfold capillaroscopy — specialised investigation; available in major rheumatology centres; not available at district level

ILD MANAGEMENT IN INDIA

SSc-ILD is the most fatal complication in India — Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) and Nintedanib are the standard treatments

Nintedanib (antifibrotic) — approved for SSc-ILD; expensive; not in Jan Aushadhi

Cyclophosphamide IV pulse — still used in India for SSc-ILD in resource-limited settings

PAH IN INDIA

Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH-SSc) — Sildenafil widely available India; Bosentan (endothelin antagonist) available in India at subsidised rates for PAH; ECHO screening critical

SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS

Captopril or Ramipril — must be immediately started in acute hypertension in SSc; most Indian district hospitals do not recognise SRC; delay causes AKI → dialysis → death
diseases.important_notes:
SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS IS A LIFE-SAVING EMERGENCY: SRC occurs mostly in early diffuse SSc (first 4 years) and presents as sudden severe hypertension (often >200/120 mmHg) + acute kidney injury. Common misconception: steroid doses (>15 mg/day prednisolone) are a major trigger for SRC — high-dose steroids should NEVER be used freely in diffuse SSc. MANAGEMENT: Captopril is the drug of choice (life-saving ACE inhibitor — contraindicated for most other AKI but specifically indicated in SRC because the pathology is renin-mediated). Start immediately. Dialysis may be needed temporarily but kidneys can recover months later if ACE inhibitor was started promptly.

ORC PAH SCREENING IN LIMITED SSc: Pulmonary arterial hypertension is the major mortality cause in limited SSc (CREST). It often develops insidiously and is not clinically apparent until far advanced. Annual echocardiogram for all limited SSc patients is mandatory, with Doppler assessment of RVSP. DETECT algorithm (DLCO < 60%, FVC/DLCO ratio, ECG changes, NTproBNP, uric acid) allows risk stratification. Early PAH detected and treated with Sildenafil + Bosentan dramatically improves survival. Late PAH (RHC mPAP >45 mmHg) has very poor prognosis.

HAND CARE IS FUNCTIONAL PRESERVATION: Hand function in SSc determines daily living independence. Digital ulcers → infection → osteomyelitis → autoamputation. Indian patients must be educated: keep hands warm (gloves in cold, avoid refrigerators without protection), daily oiling/moisturising (Vaseline or coconut oil on hands), early wound care at ANY digital ulcer (no waiting), and immediate Nifedipine or Sildenafil escalation at first ulcer formation. Infected digital ulcers in SSc need IV antibiotics promptly — delayed treatment = digital loss.
confirmation_tests_curated: Skin thickening of fingers of both hands extending proximal to MCPs (sufficient alone — score 9)
Skin thickening of fingers (sclerodactyly, puffy fingers), digital tip lesions (ulcers, pitting scars), telangiectasia, abnormal nailfold capillaries, PAH/ILD, Raynaud's, SSc-related autoantibody
Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) — diffuse SSc; ILD risk; poor prognosis

diseases.name: Polymyositis Dermatomyositis
diseases.tests:
BOHAN AND PETER CRITERIA (classic; still used)

1. Symmetric proximal muscle weakness

2. Elevated muscle enzymes (CK, LDH, AST, ALT, aldolase)

3. EMG myopathic pattern

4. Muscle biopsy — necrosis, regeneration, inflammatory infiltrate

5. Dermatological features of DM (Gottron's, heliotrope, other rashes)

Definite: 3–4 criteria (+ rash for DM); Probable: 2 criteria (+ rash for DM)

MUSCLE ENZYMES

CK (creatine kinase) — most sensitive; 5–50x normal in active PM; may be normal in DM (especially amyopathic DM)

Aldolase, LDH, AST, ALT — all can be elevated

AUTOANTIBODIES — MYOSITIS-SPECIFIC

Anti-Jo-1 (anti-Histidyl-tRNA synthetase) — antisynthetase syndrome; ILD, arthritis, mechanic's hands

Other antisynthetase antibodies: anti-PL-7, anti-PL-12, anti-EJ, anti-OJ

Anti-Mi-2 — classic DM; good prognosis

Anti-MDA5 — amyopathic DM + rapidly progressive ILD; high mortality without treatment

Anti-TIF1-gamma (anti-p155/140) — malignancy-associated DM; mandates intensive cancer screen

Anti-NXP2 — calcinosis + malignancy in adult DM

Anti-SRP — necrotising autoimmune myopathy; severe CK elevation; resistant to standard therapies

Anti-HMGCR — statin-induced necrotising autoimmune myopathy

IMAGING

MRI muscle (thigh, pelvis) — STIR sequence; muscle oedema, inflammation, fascial involvement; guides biopsy site

ELECTROMYOGRAPHY (EMG)

Myopathic pattern: short duration, small amplitude, polyphasic potentials; fibrillations; positive sharp waves

Excludes neurogenic cause of weakness

MUSCLE BIOPSY

PM: endomysial CD8+ T-cell infiltrate, MHC-I overexpression on muscle fibres

DM: perifascicular atrophy, perivascular CD4+ T-cell and B-cell infiltrate

RESPIRATORY ASSESSMENT

HRCT thorax — ILD pattern

PFT (FVC, DLCO) — ILD monitoring

Pharyngeal / swallowing assessment — videofluoroscopy if dysphagia
diseases.generic_medicines:
FIRST-LINE

Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (up to 80 mg/day) — major induction; taper over months as CK normalises and strength improves

Methotrexate 15–25 mg/week (+Folic acid 5 mg/week) — steroid-sparing; skin and muscle

Azathioprine 1.5–2.5 mg/kg/day — steroid-sparing; equally effective to MTX; preferred if ILD present

SEVERE / REFRACTORY PM/DM

IV Methylprednisolone pulse 1 g/day ×3 days — severe acute presentations, rapid respiratory compromise

Mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–2 g/day — ILD-associated DM; refractory muscle disease; better GI tolerance

IV Cyclophosphamide — anti-MDA5 DM-ILD; calcinosis; rapidly progressive ILD

IV Immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg over 5 days — effective for muscle and skin; JDM; resistant PM; ProDERM trial data

Rituximab (anti-CD20) — refractory PM/DM; anti-SRP necrotising myopathy; anti-HMGCR statin myopathy; RIM trial

Tacrolimus 2–4 mg/day — particularly for DM-ILD; Japan/India data

ILD TREATMENT (rapidly progressive — especially anti-MDA5)

High-dose steroids + Cyclophosphamide + Tacrolimus triple therapy — anti-MDA5 DM-ILD standard

Tofacitinib — emerging evidence for anti-MDA5 refractory rapidly progressive ILD

CALCINOSIS (limited options)

Diltiazem 120–480 mg/day — reduces calcinosis formation; limited evidence

Colchicine 0.5–1 mg/day — anti-inflammatory for calcinosis-related inflammation

Rituximab, Abatacept — emerging for refractory calcinosis

ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA PREVENTION

Head of bed elevation, PPI, Metoclopramide for dysmotility; speech therapy assessment

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
MUSCLE STRENGTHENING (RASAYANA)

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — Balya (strength-promoting); 'Mamsa dhatu' (muscle tissue) strengthener; anti-inflammatory and anti-fatigue; 300–600 mg/day; multiple clinical trials for muscle mass and strength

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — Balya, Rasayana; muscle strength and endurance; 500 mg–1 g BD

Bala (Sida cordifolia) — Balya for muscular weakness; 500 mg BD

Vidarikanda (Pueraria tuberosa) — Rasayana herb; muscle mass; 3–6 g/day

Kapikacchu / Mucuna pruriens — natural source of L-DOPA precursor; muscle and neuromuscular function; 5 g/day

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY

Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — anti-inflammatory; muscle and joint pain in inflammatory myopathy adjunctive; 400–750 mg BD

Turmeric/Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; NF-kB inhibition; 500–1500 mg/day

Guggulu (Commiphora wightii) — Mamsa-vaha srotas (muscle channel) anti-inflammatory; Yogaraj Guggulu; Mahayograja Guggulu

SKIN (DERMATOMYOSITIS)

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — skin purification; anti-inflammatory; 500 mg BD

Neem (Azadirachta indica) — topical for inflammatory rash; antibacterial for skin fissures

PANCHAKARMA

Abhyanga (Mahanarayan oil, Bala Taila) — muscle strengthening and anti-inflammatory massage; practically indicated for proximal muscle recovery

Pinda Sweda (Shashtika Shali Pinda Sweda) — warm rice-milk-herb bolus fomentation; stimulates muscle repair and circulation

Basti (Balya Basti — Ashwagandha/Shatavari-based) — muscle strengthening through deep tissue absorption

IMPORTANT NOTE

Ashwagandha is the most evidence-based Ayurvedic herb for muscle strength and anti-inflammatory action in inflammatory myopathies. It should be considered as a useful adjunct but cannot substitute for immunosuppression in PM/DM. Malignancy screening MUST not be delayed in favour of herbal treatment.
diseases.india_specific:
TUBERCULOSIS

DM/PM treatment requires high-dose steroids + steroid-sparing immunosuppressants → TB reactivation extremely common in India

Mandatory baseline TB workup (IGRA/Mantoux + CXR) before initiating immunosuppression

On cyclophosphamide/azathioprine — annual review for TB signs; prophylaxis if IGRA positive

MALIGNANCY SCREENING IN INDIA

Cancer screening resources limited in India; cost of CT + PET limits comprehensive screening

Priority India-specific cancers to screen: nasopharyngeal CA (EBV-associated; higher risk in Indian subcontinent), gastric cancer (H. pylori-associated), cervical cancer, lung cancer

DIAGNOSIS

PM/DM often misdiagnosed as muscular dystrophy, myasthenia gravis, or viral myositis in India

Elevated CK + proximal weakness + skin rash = refer to rheumatologist; not primarily a neurology diagnosis

Anti-Jo-1 and antisynthetase panel testing becoming more available in India but still limited

JUVENILE DM

JDM (Juvenile Dermatomyositis) — includes calcinosis as major complication; more treatable than adult DM; early treatment essential

OVERLAP MYOSITIS

Anti-MDA5 DM — rapidly progressive ILD; life-threatening; emerging entity; more frequent in East Asian (including some Indian) populations; early aggressive immunosuppression critical
diseases.important_notes:
MALIGNANCY SCREENING IS MANDATORY IN DERMATOMYOSITIS: DM is a paraneoplastic condition in approximately 25% of adult cases. Cancer may precede, coincide with, or follow DM diagnosis. Anti-TIF1-gamma (anti-p155) and anti-NXP2 antibodies are particularly associated with malignancy. ALL adult DM patients need comprehensive cancer screening: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (minimum), PET-CT in high-risk, upper and lower GI endoscopy, tumour markers (CEA, CA-125, AFP, CA 19-9, PSA depending on age/sex). Indian context: nasopharyngeal carcinoma (EBV-associated — ENT examination + EBV VCA antibodies), gastric cancer, and cervical cancer are India-relevant priorities. Failing to diagnose an occult malignancy in DM = potentially fatal.

STEROID MUSCLE WASTING VERSUS DISEASE ACTIVITY: A major clinical challenge in PM/DM management is distinguishing 'steroid-induced myopathy' (from ongoing prednisolone treatment) from 'residual or recurrent PM/DM muscle inflammation'. Steroid myopathy: CK is normal; EMG is myopathic but different; occurs after prolonged steroid. PM/DM relapse: CK elevated; MRI shows active inflammation. This distinction determines whether to increase or decrease steroids — wrong direction causes deterioration. Regular CK monitoring + MRI muscle helps determine treatment direction. Transition from muscle disease to predominantly steroid side-effects is common without monitoring.

TB IN IMMUNOSUPPRESSED PM/DM PATIENTS IN INDIA: The combination of high-dose prednisolone (1 mg/kg) + Methotrexate or Azathioprine in a country with 25% TB prevalence creates severe TB reactivation risk. Standard India protocol: IGRA/Mantoux + CXR at diagnosis before any immunosuppression. If IGRA positive: Isoniazid 300 mg/day for 6 months concurrently or before starting immunosuppresants. PM/DM patients on long-term steroids also have a risk of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) — consider Co-trimoxazole prophylaxis (480 mg/day) when on prolonged high-dose steroids or cyclophosphamide.
confirmation_tests_curated: BOHAN AND PETER CRITERIA (classic; still used)
2. Elevated muscle enzymes (CK, LDH, AST, ALT, aldolase)
4. Muscle biopsy — necrosis, regeneration, inflammatory infiltrate

diseases.name: Vasculitis Anca
diseases.tests:
ANCA TESTING

cANCA (cytoplasmic ANCA) pattern IF → anti-PR3 (anti-proteinase-3) by ELISA — GPA (85% sensitivity)

pANCA (perinuclear ANCA) pattern IF → anti-MPO (anti-myeloperoxidase) by ELISA — MPA (60–75% sensitivity); EGPA (40–50%)

ANCA IF + specific ELISA (PR3, MPO) required together — IF alone insufficient

Sensitivity: GPA 85–90%, MPA 70–80%, EGPA 40–50%

RENAL

Urine dipstick, spot PCR, urine microscopy (RBC casts = nephritis)

Serum creatinine, eGFR — trajectory monitoring

Renal biopsy — crescentic GN with pauci-immune pattern (negative/scanty immunofluorescence) = strongly supports AAV; distinguishes from lupus (full-house IF) and anti-GBM

RESPIRATORY

HRCT thorax — nodules (GPA), ground-glass (haemorrhage), infiltrates

Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) — haemosiderin-laden macrophages = alveolar haemorrhage

Pulmonary function tests

ENT

Nasal endoscopy, biopsy (nasal septum, sinus mucosa) — granulomatous vasculitis

CBCT/CT sinuses — mucosal disease, bone destruction

BLOOD

FBC — leucocytosis, eosinophilia (EGPA > 1000/mm3, often > 5000)

ESR, CRP — markedly elevated

Serum creatinine, electrolytes, LFT

Complement C3/C4 — normal (unlike lupus nephritis) in AAV

Anti-GBM antibody — exclude anti-GBM disease; pulmonary-renal syndrome

HIV, HBsAg, HCV — exclude infection-associated vasculitis; before immunosuppression
diseases.generic_medicines:
INDUCTION OF REMISSION

Rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly ×4 or 1 g ×2 (2 weeks apart) — equivalent to CYC; preferred in relapsing AAV, young women (fertility), anti-PR3 GPA; biosimilar available in India

IV Cyclophosphamide 15 mg/kg every 3 weeks ×6 pulses (Euro-Lupus-like) — standard induction; AAV without severe renal (creatinine < 500 micromol/L)

High-dose Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day + IV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day ×3 (severe/RPGN/diffuse alveolar haemorrhage)

Plasma exchange — DAH (diffuse alveolar haemorrhage) or anti-GBM overlap (controversial for pure RPGN-AAV: PEXIVAS trial negative for plasma exchange in AAV-RPGN)

MAINTENANCE OF REMISSION (12–24+ months)

Rituximab 500 mg every 6 months — superior to Azathioprine for maintenance in GPA/MPA (MAINRITSAN study)

Azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day — standard maintenance alternative; less expensive

Low-dose Prednisolone 5 mg/day — taper over 12–18 months

EGPA-SPECIFIC

Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5 monoclonal) 300 mg SC monthly — approved for EGPA; reduces eosinophil-driven disease; expensive but may permit dose reduction of steroids

RESISTANT/REFRACTORY

IV Immunoglobulin — refractory GPA without nephritis; adjunctive

Mycophenolate mofetil — maintenance alternative (less evidence)

PNEUMOCYSTIS PROPHYLAXIS

Cotrimoxazole 480–960 mg alternate days — PCP prophylaxis mandatory on cyclophosphamide

NB: Cotrimoxazole double-strength daily also used as maintenance therapy for upper respiratory GPA (reduces relapse from Staph. aureus colonisation)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND VASCULAR

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator; anti-inflammatory; particularly useful in reducing systemic inflammation; 500 mg–1 g/day (at physiological dose — not large immunostimulant doses in active vasculitis)

Turmeric / Curcumin — potent NF-kB inhibitor; anti-inflammatory; 500–1500 mg/day with piperine

Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) — anti-inflammatory; renal protective; diuretic; extensively studied for renal conditions; 500 mg–1 g BD

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — renal supportive; anti-inflammatory for Mutraghata (urinary tract disorders); 500 mg BD

Varuna (Crataeva nurvala) — renal and urinary tract conditions; 500 mg BD

Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — anti-inflammatory; reduces systemic vasculitis-related inflammation; 400–750 mg BD

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Punarnavadi Kwatha / Punarnavadi Mandura — renal anti-inflammatory decoction; iron for anaemia of vasculitis; classical for renal conditions (Mutrakrichra)

Gokshuradi Guggulu — renal and urinary anti-inflammatory

Triphala — antioxidant; mucosal health; reduces systemic oxidative stress in vasculitis

PANCHAKARMA — ADJUNCTIVE

Virechana — Pitta-Kapha toxin elimination; systemic vasculitis

Basti (Tikta Basti — bitter herb enema) — Pitta reduction; systemic anti-inflammatory

IMPORTANT NOTE

ANCA vasculitis is a life-threatening medical emergency. Herbal therapy is strictly adjunctive for constitution support, renal protection, and anti-inflammatory support during maintenance. Punarnava and Gokshura have animal and small clinical evidence for renal protection and anti-inflammatory activity that may complement care during maintenance phases. Induction MUST be immediate biomedical therapy — delay causes permanent renal loss.
diseases.india_specific:
INFECTIONS MIMICKING AAV

TB granulomatosis — pulmonary and ENT TB can mimic GPA perfectly (granulomatous lung disease + sinusitis + haematuria from renal TB); rigorous TB exclusion before diagnosing AAV and starting immunosuppression is critical

ANCA may be positive in active TB (false positive AAV serology)

Treatment of TB as AAV = missed TB + potentially fatal immunosuppression of active infection

Treatment of AAV as TB = untreated vasculitis → renal failure + organ loss

DIAGNOSIS

AAV rare but not uncommon in India; large tertiary centres (AIIMS Delhi, CMC Vellore, PGI Chandigarh) have significant caseloads

RPGN (rapidly progressive GN) in India — must always include AAV in differential (alongside anti-GBM disease, post-streptococcal MPGN, lupus nephritis)

Renal biopsy is essential in RPGN — pauci-immune crescentic GN = high probability AAV

TREATMENT ACCESS

Rituximab — first-line AAV induction alongside CYC; Indian biosimilar available; cost = major barrier

Cyclophosphamide pulse — widely available; lower cost; still standard in many Indian centres

TB PROPHYLAXIS

IGRA/Mantoux + CXR mandatory before AAV induction immunosuppression; Isoniazid prophylaxis for LTBI
diseases.important_notes:
RPGN IS A NEPHROLOGICAL EMERGENCY — HOURS MATTER: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis from ANCA vasculitis can progress from normal creatinine to dialysis-requiring renal failure within 2–4 weeks. The key clinical indicator is a rising creatinine with active urine sediment (RBC casts, proteinuria). Any patient with unexplained rapidly rising creatinine + haematuria + proteinuria + systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, sinusitis, haemoptysis) MUST have URGENT ANCA testing and renal biopsy and be referred to nephrology/rheumatology SAME DAY. Do not wait for outpatient appointments. Induction immunosuppression (high-dose steroids + Cyclophosphamide or Rituximab) must start within 24–48 hours of biopsy-confirmed AAV to salvage renal function.

DIFFUSE ALVEOLAR HAEMORRHAGE IS IMMEDIATELY LIFE-THREATENING: DAH presents as massive haemoptysis + rapidly falling oxygen saturation + bilateral ground glass on CT. It is the most acute AAV emergency, with >50% mortality if untreated. Management: ICU, high-dose IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day ×3, immediate Cyclophosphamide (or Rituximab), plasma exchange considered. In India, DAH from AAV is sometimes mistaken for TB haemoptysis or infection — the distinguishing feature is systemic vasculitis clues (sinusitis, renal involvement, ANCA positive). Never give empirical anti-TB for presumed TB haemoptysis without investigations, especially if ANCA titres are pending.

TB MUST BE EXCLUDED BEFORE IMMUNOSUPPRESSION — THE INDIA-SPECIFIC CRITICAL RULE: ANCA vasculitis treatment requires powerful immunosuppression that will be fatal if unrecognised active TB is present. In India, GPA mimics TB clinically, radiologically, and histologically (granulomas in both). Strategy: (1) Simultaneous ANCA AND TB workup at time of presentation. (2) Biopsy showing necrotising granulomas = send tissue for AFB culture, TB PCR, AND vasculitis histopathology. (3) Start empirical anti-TB only if TB is more probable; start immunosuppression only if AAV more probable. (4) In diagnostic uncertainty, some experts start both — anti-TB + lower-dose steroids (1mg/kg) with close monitoring.

pANCA (perinuclear ANCA) pattern IF → anti-MPO (anti-myeloperoxidase) by ELISA — MPA (60–75% sensitivity); EGPA (40–50%)
ANCA IF + specific ELISA (PR3, MPO) required together — IF alone insufficient

diseases.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
diseases.tests:
UPDATED SAPPORO/SYDNEY CRITERIA (2006) — REVISED 2023 ACR/EULAR

Clinical: 1+ thrombosis or obstetric morbidity

Laboratory: 1+ of (Lupus Anticoagulant, aCL IgG/IgM medium-high titre, anti-beta2GP1 IgG/IgM) PRESENT ON 2 OCCASIONS ≥12 WEEKS APART

LABORATORY — ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID ANTIBODIES

Lupus Anticoagulant (LA) — most thrombogenic; paradoxically prolongs clotting tests (APTT) in vitro but causes thrombosis in vivo; tested by DRVVT (dilute Russell viper venom time) + APTT screen + mixing studies + confirm step

Anticardiolipin antibodies (aCL) — IgG/IgM; medium titre (>40 GPL/MPL) or high titre (>80) on ELISA

Anti-beta2-Glycoprotein I antibodies (anti-beta2GP1) — IgG/IgM; ELISA

TRIPLE POSITIVITY (positive for all three) = highest thrombosis risk; DOAC absolutely contraindicated; warfarin mandatory

THROMBOSIS WORKUP

Doppler ultrasound — DVT diagnosis (limbs, portal vein)

CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) — PE diagnosis

MRI brain (DWI) — acute stroke; older infarcts; white matter lesions

Echo — Libman-Sacks, intracardiac thrombus

COAGULATION

APTT (prolonged in LA — paradoxically)

Mixing study — failure to correct with normal plasma = inhibitor

PT/INR — baseline (warfarin monitoring)

THROMBOPHILIA PANEL (always check in young thrombosis)

Protein C, Protein S, Antithrombin III, Factor V Leiden, Prothrombin mutation, Homocysteine

Exclude secondary causes: SLE (ANA, anti-dsDNA), other CTD
diseases.generic_medicines:
ACUTE THROMBOSIS (venous or arterial)

IV Unfractionated Heparin (UFH) — loading 80 units/kg IV then infusion; monitor APTT 60–100 sec; acute treatment bridge to warfarin

LMWH (Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC BD or 1.5 mg/kg once daily) — DVT treatment; bridge to warfarin; preferred in pregnancy

LONG-TERM ANTICOAGULATION

Warfarin (Sodium Warfarin) — MAINSTAY of APS anticoagulation

Venous thrombosis APS: Target INR 2–3

Arterial thrombosis APS: Target INR 2.5–3.5 (or 2–3 with aspirin 100 mg/day)

Triple positive APS: INR 3–4 or INR 2–3 + Aspirin (debate; maintain strict monitoring)

Duration: Lifelong in confirmed APS with thrombosis (high recurrence risk if stopped)

DOAC NOTE: Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Dabigatran — DO NOT USE in high-risk (triple-positive) APS; inferior outcomes in TRAPS and other trials. May be used only in single-positive, low-risk, venous-only if warfarin not tolerated.

OBSTETRIC APS

Aspirin 75–100 mg/day from pre-conception or first trimester + LMWH (Enoxaparin 40 mg/day prophylactic dose) throughout pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum

Refractory obstetric APS: HCQ (hydroxychloroquine) add-on may improve outcomes; Prednisolone controversial

CATASTROPHIC APS (CAPS) — EMERGENCY

IV Heparin (immediate, full anticoagulation) + High-dose IV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day ×3 + Plasma exchange ×5 + IVIG 2 g/kg

Rituximab — refractory CAPS with microangiopathy

ANTIPLATELET for low-risk primary prevention

Aspirin 75–100 mg/day — asymptomatic aPL positive with SLE (not yet thrombosis)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTICOAGULANT-SUPPORTIVE HERBS

Ginkgo biloba — antiplatelet properties; improves peripheral circulation; evidence in vascular conditions; 120 mg/day; CAUTION: may increase bleeding if combined with anticoagulants — use under supervision

Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna) — cardioprotective; reduces platelet hyperreactivity in animal models; 500 mg BD of bark powder

Garlic (Allium sativum) — antiplatelet and anti-thrombotic properties; ajoene (component) inhibits platelet aggregation; 600–1200 mg standardised extract/day; CAUTION: anticoagulant interaction — increased bleeding risk with Warfarin

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY (for inflammatory component of APS)

Turmeric / Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; inhibits platelet aggregation at high doses; 500 mg/day; CAUTION: additive antiplatelet effect with anticoagulants

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulatory; anti-inflammatory; 500 mg/day

FOR LIVEDO RETICULARIS AND SKIN

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Rakta shodhana; vascular skin conditions; 500 mg BD

IMPORTANT CAUTIONS FOR APS

CRITICAL CAUTION: Several Ayurvedic herbs (Garlic, Ginkgo, Turmeric, Ginger, Fenugreek) have independent antiplatelet or anticoagulant properties. When combined with Warfarin or other anticoagulants (which are ESSENTIAL and lifelong in APS), these herbs significantly elevate BLEEDING RISK. INR can increase unpredictably. ANY herbal use in APS patients on anticoagulation must be disclosed to the treating physician and INR monitored more frequently.

OBSTETRIC APS: Herbal therapies are ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED as alternatives to Aspirin + LMWH in obstetric APS — the live birth rate difference with treatment vs no treatment is 80% vs 50%. No Ayurvedic intervention has evidence in obstetric APS.
diseases.india_specific:
MISDIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT GAP

Recurrent miscarriages and obstetric morbidity in India often attributed to nutritional deficiency, trauma, or divine fate — APS not routinely excluded

Young stroke in India — APS workup routinely done by tertiary neurologists but often missed in secondary care (only OCP/drug misuse searched for)

ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILL + APS

Combined OCP dramatically increases thrombosis risk in APS-positive women; OCP screening for APS antibodies recommended before prescription in recurrent miscarriage or prior DVT

Progestogen-only pill preferred if APS confirmed (lower thrombosis risk)

OBSTETRIC APS IN INDIA

Low-molecular weight heparin + Aspirin combination for obstetric APS — dramatically improves live birth rates (80% vs 50% without treatment)

Routine APS testing in recurrent miscarriage clinics in India has dramatically low rates — major treatment gap

WARFARIN MONITORING IN INDIA

INR monitoring services available nationally in India — regular INR critical for warfarin efficacy in APS; target INR 2–3 (venous), 2.5–3.5 (arterial)

Point-of-care INR machines increasingly available

DOACs (direct oral anticoagulants) — CONTRAINDICATED in high-risk APS (triple positive); inferior to Warfarin in APS — TRAPS trial showed Rivaroxaban significantly inferior to Warfarin in triple-positive APS
diseases.important_notes:
WARFARIN IS LIFELONG IN CONFIRMED APS THROMBOSIS: Unlike most DVTs where 3–6 months of anticoagulation suffices, confirmed APS with thrombosis requires LIFELONG anticoagulation because the underlying thrombotic risk does not resolve. The recurrence rate after stopping anticoagulation in APS is 20–67% at 2 years. The biggest India-relevant error: stopping Warfarin after 6 months 'because DVT has resolved'. This must be explicitly communicated to APS patients. Regular INR monitoring, awareness of drug-food interactions (Vitamin K in green vegetables modulates INR), alcohol interactions, and medication adherence education are essential.

DOACs ARE CONTRAINDICATED IN HIGH-RISK (TRIPLE-POSITIVE) APS: The TRAPS trial (2019) definitively showed Rivaroxaban was significantly inferior to Warfarin in triple-positive APS, with higher rates of arterial thrombosis, stroke, and major adverse events. Despite this, DOACs are sometimes prescribed in India for 'convenience' in APS. Triple-positive APS (Lupus Anticoagulant + aCL + anti-beta2GP1 all positive) and any patient with prior arterial thrombosis or stroke with APS MUST be on Warfarin with INR monitoring — not rivaroxaban/apixaban.

OBSTETRIC APS — TREATING RECURRENT MISCARRIAGE TRANSFORMS OUTCOMES: Untreated obstetric APS has a live birth rate of approximately 20–40% per pregnancy. Aspirin 75–100 mg/day + LMWH (Enoxaparin 40 mg/day) started as early as pre-conception or first trimester raises live birth rate to 70–80%. In India, recurrent miscarriage is often attributed to nutritional causes, hormonal issues, or cultural explanations. A simple blood test for Lupus Anticoagulant, aCL, and anti-beta2GP1 in any woman with ≥2 consecutive pregnancy losses can identify treatable APS. These tests should be standard in any recurrent miscarriage evaluation.
confirmation_tests_curated: Laboratory: 1+ of (Lupus Anticoagulant, aCL IgG/IgM medium-high titre, anti-beta2GP1 IgG/IgM) PRESENT ON 2 OCCASIONS ≥12 WEEKS APART

Lupus Anticoagulant (LA) — most thrombogenic; paradoxically prolongs clotting tests (APTT) in vitro but causes thrombosis in vivo; tested by DRVVT (dilute Russell viper venom time) + APTT screen + mixing studies + confirm step

diseases.name: Crohns Disease
diseases.tests:
ENDOSCOPY

Ileocolonoscopy (gold standard initial investigation) — visualisation + biopsies

Crohn's endoscopic features: cobblestone mucosa, aphthous ulcers, deep serpiginous ulcers, skip lesions, rectal sparing (unlike UC), perianal findings

Upper GI endoscopy (EGD) — oral, oesophageal, gastric, proximal small bowel Crohn's

HISTOLOGY

Non-caseating granulomas (40–60% of Crohn's biopsies) — supportive but not pathognomonic

Full-thickness transmural inflammation, lymphoid aggregates, normal or near-normal rectum in some

TB special stains (ZN stain, AFB culture, MTB PCR) — must be done on ALL biopsies in India

IMAGING

MRI Enterography (MRE) — gold standard for small bowel Crohn's extent, activity, complications (fistulae, abscesses, strictures); no radiation

CT Enterography — if MRI unavailable; good for complications; uses radiation

Small bowel follow-through (SBFT) — low-resource alternative; string sign (long stricture)

Transabdominal ultrasound — thickened bowel wall, vascularity

MRI pelvis — perianal fistula mapping (pre-surgical planning)

SEROLOGY AND STOOL

Faecal Calprotectin — sensitive mucosal inflammation marker; elevated in active IBD; used to monitor response and predict relapse; NOT elevated in IBS; distinguishes IBD from IBS/functional

CRP, ESR — systemic inflammation markers

ASCA (anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibody) IgG — positive in 60% Crohn's; negative in TB → helps differentiation

pANCA — more common in UC than Crohn's

CBC — anaemia (multifactorial), leucocytosis; Metabolic panel — albumin (nutrition), B12, iron studies, vitamin D

Stool: culture, O&P, C. difficile toxin PCR, faecal calprotectin — exclude infections before IBD treatment
diseases.generic_medicines:
MILD-MODERATE CROHN'S

Mesalazine (5-ASA) 3–4 g/day (limited efficacy in small bowel Crohn's; works better in colonic Crohn's; NOT as effective as in UC)

Budesonide 9 mg/day — controlled ileal release; first-line for ileocaecal Crohn's induction; less systemic SE than Prednisolone; taper 3–3 months

MODERATE-SEVERE CROHN'S

Prednisolone 40–60 mg/day — acute flares; taper over 8–12 weeks; NOT for maintenance

IV Methylprednisolone — severe hospitalised Crohn's

Azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day (Thiopurine) — maintenance; steroid-sparing; requires TPMT testing (enzyme — homozygous TPMT deficient = fatal toxicity)

Mercaptopurine 0.75–1.5 mg/kg/day — active metabolite of AZA; TPMT enzyme test mandatory

Methotrexate 25 mg SC/IM weekly — Crohn's maintenance (small bowel and steroid-dependent); folic acid mandatory

BIOLOGIC THERAPY (moderate-severe or steroid-dependent/refractory)

Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks — first-line biologic; fistulising Crohn's; Indian biosimilar available

Adalimumab 160/80/40 mg SC — first-line biologic; self-administered; Indian biosimilar available

Vedolizumab 300 mg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks — gut-selective; safer infection profile; less effective than TNF-i in extraintestinal manifestations

Ustekinumab 90 mg SC every 8–12 weeks (after IV loading) — IL-12/23 inhibitor; effective in TNF-failure

SURGICAL INDICATIONS

Perforation, obstruction, abscess (not amenable to drainage), refractory disease, cancer surveillance

NUTRITIONAL THERAPY

Exclusive Enteral Nutrition (EEN) — 6–8 weeks — first-line induction in paediatric Crohn's; effective as steroids + avoids growth suppression; elemental or polymeric formula

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
GUT ANTI-INFLAMMATORY

Boswellia serrata (Shallaki) — extensive RCTs in Crohn's disease showing clinical remission, reduced mucosal inflammation; H15 extract 3400 mg/day in clinical trials; anti-inflammatory via 5-LOX inhibition; gut specifically active; considered comparable to Mesalazine in some Crohn's studies

Turmeric / Curcumin — potent gut anti-inflammatory; inhibits NF-kB; clinical trials in IBD showing remission maintenance; 1–3 g/day with piperine

Kutaja (Holarrhena antidysenterica) — classical anti-dysenteric; intestinal anti-inflammatory; reduces diarrhoea and mucosal inflammation in IBD; 250–500 mg BD

Bilva (Aegle marmelos) — excellent anti-diarrhoeal; mucosal healing; astringent; reduces cramping; 500 mg–1 g of ripe fruit powder

Musta (Cyperus rotundus) — digestive; anti-inflammatory; carminative; reduces GI spasm; 250–500 mg BD

Aloe vera — mucosal healing; anti-inflammatory; trials in Crohn's; 100 mL juice BD; avoid in obstruction

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Kutajarishta — classical fermented Ayurvedic compound; Kutaja as main ingredient; diarrhoea and dysentery; 15–30 mL BD

Bilvadi Churna — anti-diarrhoeal combination

Dadimashtak Churna — digestive compound; reduces bloating and cramping

PROBIOTICS

Fermented buttermilk (Takra) — Ayurvedic probiotic; Lactobacillus; reduces intestinal dysbiosis

VSL#3 type probiotic combinations — IBD-specific trials showing benefit in ulcerative colitis; some evidence in Crohn's

PANCHAKARMA

Basti (Matra Basti — small volume oil enema; Anuvasana Basti) — colonic anti-inflammatory and mucosal healing in Crohn's colitis

Virechana — Pitta reduction; inflammatory bowel conditions

IMPORTANT NOTE

Boswellia serrata has the strongest Ayurvedic evidence for Crohn's — Phase II/III RCTs showing mesalazine-comparable efficacy for inducing remission in Crohn's colitis. This is one of the few Ayurvedic herbs with credible IBD-specific clinical trial data. TB exclusion is mandatory in Indian IBD management — any Ayurvedic practitioner managing apparent Crohn's must also check for TB.
diseases.india_specific:
DIAGNOSIS DELAY

IBD diagnosis delay in India: 3–7 years typical; Crohn's misdiagnosed as: intestinal TB (most common confusion), IBS, chronic amoebic colitis, enteric fever sequelae

INTESTINAL TB vs CROHN'S — THE INDIA DIAGNOSTIC DILEMMA

Intestinal TB (ITB) and Crohn's disease can be clinically, endoscopically, radiologically, and histologically virtually identical

Both: ileocaecal location, skip lesions, granulomas on biopsy, obstruction, fistulae

Key distinguishing features: ITB — TB focus elsewhere, Mantoux/IGRA positive, caseating granulomas, response to ATT; Crohn's — perianal disease, serpiginous ulcers, ASCA positive, response to anti-IBD therapy

Standard India approach: therapeutic trial of anti-TB (4-drug HRZE) for 8–12 weeks in ambiguous cases; lack of clinical and endoscopic response = diagnostic Crohn's; start anti-IBD therapy

Giving steroids or biologics for undiagnosed TB = potentially fatal dissemination

IBD PREVALENCE IN INDIA

India IBD prevalence rising rapidly — industrialisation, urban lifestyle, dietary change (hygiene hypothesis); now estimated 1.4–2 million IBD patients in India (ICMR data)

BIOLOGICS ACCESS

Infliximab, Adalimumab biosimilars available in India at significantly reduced cost; Indian Crohn's registries showing increasing biologic use
diseases.important_notes:
TB vs CROHN'S — THE SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT INDIA-SPECIFIC ISSUE IN IBD: Intestinal tuberculosis (ITB) and Crohn's disease are clinically, endoscopically, radiologically, and histologically indistinguishable in a significant proportion of patients. Both affect the ileocaecal region, cause skip lesions, granulomas, and complications like strictures. The consequences of misdiagnosis are severe: treating TB as Crohn's with steroids or biologics causes TB dissemination and death; treating Crohn's as TB delays effective therapy and causes progressive bowel damage. All IBD biopsies in India must be sent for AFB stain, AFB culture, and MTB PCR. IGRA/Mantoux is part of every Indian IBD evaluation. When genuinely ambiguous, a 2–3 month therapeutic ATT trial with clinical and endoscopic reassessment is the standard approach.

PERIANAL CROHN'S IS OFTEN THE KEY TO DIAGNOSIS: Perianal disease (complex fistulae, multiple fistula tracks, recurrent abscesses with non-healing) is pathognomonic of Crohn's disease and does NOT occur in intestinal TB. In any young Indian patient with chronic perianal fistulae/abscess repeatedly failing surgical treatment, Crohn's disease must be actively excluded with ileocolonoscopy and MRI pelvis. Perianal Crohn's is treated with combination antibiotics (Metronidazole + Ciprofloxacin), immunosuppression (Azathioprine), and Infliximab (highest level evidence for perianal fistula closure — ACCENT II trial) — NOT surgery alone.

BIOLOGICS IN CROHN'S REQUIRE TB SCREENING — INDIA-SPECIFIC PROTOCOL: All patients receiving biologic therapy (Infliximab, Adalimumab, Vedolizumab, Ustekinumab) in India require pre-treatment TB screening with IGRA/Mantoux and CXR. Positive IGRA = latent TB treatment with Isoniazid 300 mg/day for 6–9 months; biologic can start after 4–8 weeks of Isoniazid. Note: TNF inhibitors (Infliximab, Adalimumab) have the highest TB reactivation risk. Vedolizumab (gut-selective) and Ustekinumab have lower systemic immunosuppression and relatively lower TB risk — relevant consideration for Indian patients with higher baseline TB risk.

diseases.name: Ulcerative Colitis
diseases.tests:
COLONOSCOPY WITH BIOPSIES (GOLD STANDARD)

UC endoscopic features: continuous mucosal inflammation from rectum proximally; erythema, granularity, friability, ulceration, loss of vascular pattern, pseudopolyps (in chronic disease)

Mayo endoscopic subscore: 0 (normal) to 3 (severe)

HISTOLOGY

Crypt architectural distortion, crypt abscesses, mucin depletion, basal plasmacytosis (chronic mucosal damage features) — distinguish IBD from acute self-limiting colitis

STOOL TESTS

Stool culture (C/S) — Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli O157

Stool microscopy for O&P (ova and parasites) — amoeba cysts/trophozoites

C. difficile toxin PCR

Faecal calprotectin — elevated in active IBD; >250 mcg/g = mucosal inflammation; monitoring remission/relapse

CMV serology (IgG/IgM) + CMV PCR (blood + biopsy IHC) — in steroid-refractory UC

IMAGING

Abdominal X-ray — dilated transverse colon (>6 cm = toxic megacolon — emergency)

CT abdomen/pelvis — acute severe UC assessment; colitis extent, perforation, abscess

SEROLOGY

CRP, ESR, albumin, FBC — severity assessment and monitoring

pANCA — positive 60–70% UC vs 10% Crohn's — aids differentiation in indeterminate colitis

ASCA — negative in UC (positive in Crohn's)

ABDOMINAL ULTRASOUND (BOWEL ULTRASOUND)

Colonic wall thickening, vascularity — non-invasive activity assessment
diseases.generic_medicines:
MILD-MODERATE UC

Mesalazine (5-ASA) 2–4.8 g/day oral + Mesalazine 1 g enema/suppository (rectal) — cornerstone of UC induction and maintenance; oral and topical combination superior to either alone; lifelong maintenance in most UC

Sulfasalazine 3–4 g/day (older, cheaper alternative; more SE — sulfa-related; folate supplementation required)

Prednisolone 40 mg/day — moderate flares; taper over 8–12 weeks; NOT for maintenance

Budesonide MMX (Cortiment) 9 mg/day — mild-moderate extensive UC; limited systemic absorption

MODERATE-SEVERE UC

IV Methylprednisolone 60 mg/day (or Hydrocortisone 400 mg/day) — acute severe UC; hospitalisation

Azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day — steroid-sparing maintenance; onset 3–6 months

Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks — biologic induction; acute severe UC rescue (3 mg/kg accelerated dosing); Indian biosimilar available

Adalimumab 160/80/40 mg SC — induction and maintenance

Vedolizumab 300 mg IV — gut-selective; preferred if previous TB or infections; induction + maintenance

NEWER BIOLOGICS AND SMALL MOLECULES

Ustekinumab (IL-12/23i) — moderate-severe UC; TNF-failure

Tofacitinib 10 mg BD (induction) → 5 mg BD (maintenance) — JAK1/3 inhibitor; oral; rapid onset; HZV prophylaxis recommended

Ozanimod, Etrasimod (S1P modulators) — newer oral agents

ACUTE SEVERE UC — RESCUE (Failing IV steroids by Day 3)

Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV — rescue; reduces colectomy rate

Ciclosporin 2 mg/kg IV — bridge to AZA/biologic; specialist use

SURGERY — COLECTOMY

Refractory UC, toxic megacolon, dysplasia/cancer, steroid-dependence — restorative proctocolectomy + ileo-pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA)
