diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
MUCOSAL HEALING

Boswellia serrata (Shallaki) — RCTs comparing H15 Boswellia extract to Sulfasalazine in UC show comparable remission rates; anti-inflammatory via 5-LOX; mucosa healing; 1200 mg TDS of H15 extract

Turmeric / Curcumin — multiple RCTs in UC maintenance; curcumin 1 g BD + mesalazine significantly better than mesalazine alone for maintaining remission; gut-specific anti-inflammatory

Aloe vera gel — Phase I/II RCTs in mild-moderate UC; 100 mL BD for 4 weeks; anti-inflammatory, mucosal healing

Kutaja (Holarrhena antidysenterica) — classical Ayurvedic anti-dysenteric; direct mucosal anti-inflammatory; Kutajarishta fermented formulation; reduces bloody diarrhoea frequency

Bilva (Aegle marmelos) — anti-diarrhoeal; astringent; mucosal protective; 500 mg fruit powder

Amalaki (Emblica officinalis/Amla) — mucosal health; antioxidant; 500 mg–1 g/day

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Kutajarishta 15–30 mL BD — Kutaja-based classical formulation; anti-dysenteric and GI anti-inflammatory

Mustarishta — anti-inflammatory digestive; Musta base

Bilvadi Churna — astringent anti-diarrhoeal

Usheera (Vetiveria zizanioides) — cooling (Sita Virya); mucosal soothing; anti-inflammatory; UC-associated Pitta disturbance

PROBIOTICS (AYURVEDIC-ALIGNED)

Takra (Ayurvedic buttermilk) — classic Grahani treatment; probiotic; Lactobacillus cultures; consumed daily

VSL#3 type high-potency multi-strain probiotic — strongest evidence in UC maintenance (pouchitis and UC remission studies)

PANCHAKARMA

Matra Basti (small volume sesame oil enema) and Anuvasana Basti — anti-inflammatory and mucosal healing in colitis via direct rectal administration

Virechana — Pitta reduction; systemic anti-inflammatory

IMPORTANT NOTE

Turmeric (curcumin) and Boswellia have the most consistent RCT evidence for UC. Curcumin as adjunct to mesalazine for UC maintenance has multiple positive trials including meta-analyses. Kutajarishta is widely used in India as first-line IBD management — while evidence is less rigorous, it has a supportive safety record. TB exclusion BEFORE any steroids or immunosuppressants in IBD is mandatory in India.
diseases.india_specific:
PREVALENCE AND TREND

UC more common than Crohn's in India (unlike West where Crohn's ≈ UC); UC:CD ratio approximately 7:1 in India vs 1:1 in West

India IBD incidence rising — Chandigarh longitudinal data shows doubling over 20 years (hygiene hypothesis, dietary westernisation)

INFECTION vs IBD DISTINCTION

Acute bloody diarrhoea in India — must distinguish UC from: acute amoebic dysentery (specific treatment), Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella, CMV colitis (in immunosuppressed UC)

Amoebic dysentery can be superimposed on UC — treat amoeba before steroids to avoid fulminant amoebic colitis

Stool culture, O&P, C. difficile, serum amoebic CFT/serology essential before starting immunosuppression

ACUTE SEVERE UC IN INDIA

Management of acute severe UC requires intensive care-level monitoring; IV steroids, surgical backup on standby

Infliximab rescue therapy for steroid-failing acute severe UC increasingly available in India (Indian biosimilar) — reduces colectomy rates

CMV REACTIVATION

CMV colitis occurs in UC flares on steroids/immunosuppressants — India-specific concern given CMV seroprevalence >90% in adults; biopsy with IHC for CMV in steroid-refractory UC

COLORECTAL CANCER SURVEILLANCE

Pancolitis >8–10 years in India: surveillance colonoscopy should be offered but colonoscopy access and cost are significant barriers
diseases.important_notes:
AMOEBIASIS MUST BE EXCLUDED BEFORE TREATING UC WITH STEROIDS: Acute bloody diarrhoea in India can be amoebic dysentery, bacterial dysentery, or UC — they can appear clinically and endoscopically identical. Treating active amoebic colitis with steroids causes fulminant amoebic colitis with toxic megacolon and a high mortality rate. Standard India protocol: stool O&P examination, amoebic serology (indirect haemagglutination titre), and a colonoscopic biopsy (PAS-positive trophozoites) — mandatorily excluded BEFORE starting any steroid for presumed UC. Treatment: Metronidazole 800 mg TDS for 10 days, then Diloxanide furoate for luminal clearance — followed by UC evaluation if symptoms persist.

MESALAZINE IS LIFELONG IN UC — NOT JUST DURING FLARES: Unlike many conditions where treatment is only during acute disease, Mesalazine (5-ASA) in UC must be continued lifelong even during complete clinical remission. Multiple RCTs show Mesalazine maintenance therapy significantly reduces relapse rates (annual relapse: 30% on mesalazine vs 70–80% untreated) AND reduces colorectal cancer risk (30–40% risk reduction with regular 5-ASA use). Common Indian practice error: stopping mesalazine once stools normalise. This must be explicitly corrected at every consultation — this is the most important adherence message in UC.

TOXIC MEGACOLON IS A SURGICAL EMERGENCY: Toxic megacolon (transverse colon >6 cm on AXR + systemic toxicity: fever, tachycardia, leucocytosis, haemodynamic compromise) can occur in severe UC flares and acute amoebic or infective colitis. Management is ICU-level: NBM (nil by mouth), IV Methylprednisolone 60 mg/day, decompression (NGT, rectal tube), IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, surgical team involved from Day 1. If no clinical improvement within 24–72 hours on maximal medical therapy = emergency subtotal colectomy. Delaying surgery in progressive toxic megacolon = perforation + faecal peritonitis + death.

diseases.name: Primary Biliary Cholangitis
diseases.tests:
DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (2 of 3 required)

1. Elevated ALP (at least 1.5x ULN) persisting for >6 months

2. AMA titre ≥1:40 (anti-mitochondrial antibody) — sensitivity 95%, specificity 98%; near-pathognomonic

3. Liver biopsy showing non-suppurative destructive cholangitis and interlobular bile duct destruction

AUTOANTIBODIES

AMA (anti-mitochondrial antibody) — anti-M2 subtype (anti-PDC-E2 = pyruvate dehydrogenase complex E2 subunit); 90–95% sensitivity; near-pathognomonic for PBC

AMA-negative PBC (5–10%) — ANA with PBC-specific patterns: sp100 (nuclear dots), gp210 (nuclear rim); same prognosis

ANA — 35% PBC positive; sp100 and gp210 are PBC-specific ANA patterns

LIVER FUNCTION TESTS

ALP and GGT — cholestatic pattern; disproportionately elevated vs transaminases

Bilirubin — elevated only in advanced disease; rising bilirubin = prognostic (prognostic model: Mayo PBC score, UK-PBC score)

Albumin, PT/INR — synthetic function (late stage)

Transaminases (ALT, AST) — mildly elevated; disproportionate elevation = AIH overlap (Paris criteria)

Immunoglobulins — IgM elevated (characteristically elevated in PBC; not IgG as in AIH)

IMAGING

Abdominal ultrasound — exclude biliary obstruction (biliary dilatation = mechanical cause of ALP elevation); hepatic parenchyma (fibrosis, cirrhosis); portal hypertension signs (splenomegaly, ascites)

MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography) — exclude PSC (large duct disease vs PBC small duct)

Fibroscan (transient elastography) — liver stiffness = fibrosis staging; non-invasive

LIVER BIOPSY

Florid duct lesion — granulomatous destruction of bile duct, dense lymphocytic portal infiltrate; not required if AMA + cholestatic ALP

Required for: AMA-negative, overlap with AIH suspected, clinical uncertainty

TREATMENT RESPONSE MONITORING (UDCA response at 1 year)

Paris-I criteria, GLOBE score, UK-PBC score — assess UDCA adequacy; guide second-line treatment need
diseases.generic_medicines:
FIRST-LINE — ALL PBC

Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 13–15 mg/kg/day (in divided doses) — normalises ALP/GGT; slows progression; improves symptoms; reduces risk of cirrhosis and liver transplantation; start immediately at diagnosis; lifelong

INADEQUATE UDCA RESPONSE (at 1 year, assessed by Paris/GLOBE criteria)

Obeticholic acid (OCA) 5–10 mg/day — FXR (farnesoid X receptor) agonist; 45% achieve biochemical response with UDCA+OCA; SE: worsening pruritus, hypercholesterolaemia; CONTRAINDICATED in decompensated cirrhosis

Bezafibrate 400 mg/day — fibrate; potent ALP reducer in PBC; good data from BEZURSO trial; widely available India; cheap; reasonable alternative 2nd line

Fenofibrate — alternative fibrate

PRURITUS MANAGEMENT

Cholestyramine 4–16 g/day (bile acid sequestrant) — first-line for cholestatic pruritus; binds bile acids in gut; SE: constipation, steatorrhoea, reduced drug absorption (give 4 hours before UDCA); unpleasant taste

Rifampicin 150–300 mg/day — induces hepatic microsomal enzymes, reduces bile acid synthesis; highly effective for cholestatic pruritus; CAUTION: hepatotoxicity (monitor LFT); check for TB exposure (rifampicin is TB drug — resistance issues if TB present)

Naltrexone 12.5–50 mg/day — opioid antagonist; reduces centrally-mediated pruritus; opioid withdrawal-like SE at initiation

Sertraline 50–100 mg/day — SSRI; pruritus; colestyramine-refractory

OSTEOPOROSIS PREVENTION

Calcium 1000–1200 mg/day + Vitamin D3 800–2000 IU/day — fat-soluble vitamin deficiency prevention

Alendronate 70 mg/week — if T-score < -2.5 or fragility fracture

FAT-SOLUBLE VITAMIN SUPPLEMENTATION

Vitamins A, D, E, K — monitor levels; supplement if deficient; essential in cholestatic liver disease

PORTAL HYPERTENSION

Beta-blockers (Propranolol, Carvedilol) — primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding (in cirrhotic PBC)

PPI — oesophageal varices management

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
HEPATOPROTECTIVE HERBS

Bhumyamalaki (Phyllanthus niruri / Phyllanthus amarus) — potent hepatoprotective; anti-fibrotic properties in animal models; reduces ALP, transaminases; anti-cholestatic; 500 mg–1 g BD; multiple Indian clinical trials

Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata) — anti-inflammatory and hepatoprotective; classic Yakrit (liver) herb; reduces hepatic inflammation; 250–500 mg BD

Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) — hepatic and renal anti-inflammatory; reduces ascites and hepatic oedema; 500 mg BD

Katuki (Picrorhiza kurroa) — hepatoprotective; bile flow stimulant (cholagogue); anti-cholestatic historically used; CAUTION: can be hepatotoxic at high doses — use quality-assured standard preparations only

Kutki / Katuki is an endangered plant — use certified Himalayan sources only

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — liver protection during cholestasis; immunomodulatory; anti-inflammatory; 500 mg/day

Amalaki (Emblica officinalis / Amla) — antioxidant; hepatoprotective; Vitamin C source; reduces oxidative stress in cholestasis; 500 mg–1 g/day

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Arogyavardhini Vati — classical Ayurvedic liver tonic; hepatoprotective; anti-icteric (anti-jaundice); widely used in India for liver disorders; contains Guggulu, Kutaki, Chitrak, Shilajit components

Liv-52 (Himalaya) — proprietary formulation; Kasani (Chicory), Himsra (Caper bush), Kakamachi, Arjuna; hepatoprotective; extensively studied; widely prescribed by Indian practitioners

Punarnavasav — liver and spleen conditions; anti-ascitic

PRURITUS — AYURVEDIC

Neem (Azadirachta indica) — systemic anti-pruritic; external Neem oil for localised itch; anti-inflammatory skin; 250 mg/day extract

Haridra (Turmeric) bath water / external application — skin anti-pruritic

IMPORTANT NOTE

Bhumyamalaki (Phyllanthus) is the most evidence-supported plant for chronic liver disease in Indian traditional medicine. Arogya Vardhini Vati and Liv-52 are widely consumed in India. These are reasonable adjuncts for liver support BUT UDCA is the disease-modifying agent in PBC and must NOT be replaced by herbal therapy. CRITICAL WARNING: multiple Ayurvedic heavy metal preparations (Tamra Bhasma — copper, Loha Bhasma — iron, Navayasa Churna containing unsupported preparations) are HEPATOTOXIC and have been documented to CAUSE cholestatic and acute liver failure in India. These must be STRICTLY AVOIDED in any patient with existing liver disease.
diseases.india_specific:
EPIDEMIOLOGY

PBC incidence in India underestimated — largely under-diagnosed; predominantly women 40–60 years

Elevated ALP/GGT in young-middle aged woman with no obvious hepatic cause = AMA should be checked

OVERLAP WITH VIRAL HEPATITIS

PBC in the context of HBV or HCV — may complicate AMA interpretation

Elevated ALP in the presence of known HCV — dual pathology (viral hepatitis + PBC) possible; AMA testing important

HERBAL HEPATOTOXICITY

Ayurvedic and herbal remedies consumed widely in India — some (Ayurvedic metals-based preparations, certain TCM herbs, large doses of green tea extract) are hepatotoxic

Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI) must be excluded in any Indian patient with cholestatic jaundice before diagnosing PBC

URSODEOXYCHOLIC ACID AVAILABILITY

UDCA (Ursodeoxycholic acid 13–15 mg/kg/day) — first-line PBC treatment; widely available in India at reasonable cost; major generic availability

OBETICHOLIC ACID

Obeticholic acid (OCA) — second-line for UDCA inadequate response; expensive; limited India availability

TRANSPLANT

Liver transplantation — last resort for PBC cirrhosis; available at major Indian centres (AIIMS, CMC, Medanta, Fortis); long transplant waiting list
diseases.important_notes:
UDCA MUST BE TAKEN FOR LIFE — STOPPING IS DANGEROUS: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) is not a treatment for 'acute episodes' — it is a continuous disease-modifying therapy that must be taken lifelong at 13–15 mg/kg/day. UDCA works by replacing toxic hydrophobic bile acids, improving bile secretion, and providing anti-apoptotic hepatocyte protection. Multiple long-term studies (>10 years) show patients with good UDCA response have transplant-free survival equal to the general population. Stopping UDCA causes rapid biochemical relapse. Common India error: stopping UDCA when ALP normalises ('because it's better now'). This must be specifically corrected.

RIFAMPICIN FOR PRURITUS — TB STEWARDSHIP IN INDIA: Rifampicin at 150–300 mg/day is an effective second-line treatment for cholestatic pruritus in PBC when cholestyramine fails. However, in India — where Rifampicin is a CORE first-line anti-TB drug — using it for cholestatic pruritus requires careful consideration: (1) Document TB has been excluded before prescribing Rifampicin for pruritus. (2) Monitor LFT carefully (Rifampicin can cause hepatotoxicity — problematic in cholestatic liver disease). (3) The dose for pruritus (150–300 mg) is sub-therapeutic for TB and could theoretically select for Rifampicin-resistant TB — this is a theoretical concern, and in clinical practice pruritus doses are routinely used, but TB must be excluded first.

AYURVEDIC HEAVY METALS ARE A LEADING CAUSE OF LIVER FAILURE IN INDIA: Documented in multiple Indian hepatology publications, Ayurvedic herbo-mineral preparations — particularly those containing lead, arsenic, mercury, copper — are a significant cause of acute and chronic cholestatic liver failure in India. Iron-containing preparations (Navayasa Churna, Mandura-based preparations used without proper Shodhana — purification) can cause hepatic iron overload. Patients with PBC or any liver disease MUST be specifically asked about ALL herbal, Ayurvedic, and traditional remedies being consumed, and any heavy metal-containing preparation DISCONTINUED. This history must be taken at every consultation.
confirmation_tests_curated: 1. Elevated ALP (at least 1.5x ULN) persisting for >6 months
2. AMA titre ≥1:40 (anti-mitochondrial antibody) — sensitivity 95%, specificity 98%; near-pathognomonic
3. Liver biopsy showing non-suppurative destructive cholangitis and interlobular bile duct destruction

diseases.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis
diseases.tests:
REVISED IAIHG SCORE / SIMPLIFIED SCORE

Simplified score: ANA or SMA ≥1:40 = 1 pt; ANA/SMA ≥1:80 or anti-LKM1 ≥1:40 or anti-SLA = 2 pt; IgG ≥ULN = 1 pt; >1.1x ULN = 2 pt; compatible histology = 1 pt; typical histology = 2 pt; ANA/SMA titre ≥1:40 = 1 pt; Absence of viral hepatitis = 2 pt. Score ≥6 = probable AIH; ≥7 = definite AIH

AUTOANTIBODIES

ANA (antinuclear antibody) — positive in Type 1 AIH (up to 80%); homogeneous or speckled

Anti-SMA (anti-smooth muscle antibody) — F-actin anti-SMA most specific Type 1 AIH; 70–80%

Anti-LKM1 (anti-liver kidney microsomal type 1) — Type 2 AIH; specific; anti-CYP450 2D6

Anti-SLA (anti-soluble liver antigen) — specific for AIH; associated with poor prognosis; available in reference labs

SEROLOGY

IgG (immunoglobulin G) — elevated in AIH (often markedly >2x normal); distinguishes from PBC (IgM elevated)

LIVER FUNCTION TESTS

ALT, AST — markedly elevated (>5–10x ULN in acute; mildly elevated in chronic insidious)

ALP normal or mildly elevated (disproportionate ALP elevation = PBC-overlap or Drug-induced)

Bilirubin — elevated in acute; normal in indolent

Albumin, PT/INR — synthetic function; severe disease if abnormal

EXCLUSIONS

HBsAg, HCV RNA (anti-HCV is unreliable; need RNA) — viral hepatitis exclusion

Serum ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urinary copper — Wilson's disease exclusion (especially in children/young adults)

Drug history — complete medication + herbal + traditional medicine review

Alpha-1 antitrypsin level + phenotyping

LIVER BIOPSY

Interface hepatitis (piecemeal necrosis) — lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate at portal-parenchymal interface

Rosette formation, emperipolesis (hepatocyte engulfing lymphocytes) — characteristic but not pathognomonic

Fibrosis staging — critical for management planning
diseases.generic_medicines:
INDUCTION OF REMISSION

Prednisolone 40–60 mg/day (1 mg/kg) — start immediately; reduces ALT rapidly (50% within 4 weeks in responders); taper over 4–6 weeks to maintenance dose

IV Methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day — acute severe AIH (INR >1.5, or significant encephalopathy) in hospital; switch to oral when responding

MAINTENANCE AND STEROID-SPARING

Azathioprine 1–2 mg/kg/day — add after initial steroid induction; permits steroid dose reduction and eventual steroid withdrawal; check TPMT before starting; monitor CBC

Target: biochemical remission (ALT/AST normal + IgG normal) then sustained for ≥2 years before attempting withdrawal

DURATION

Total treatment 2–3 years minimum; 40–50% relapse on withdrawal — if relapse: restart and continue longer; many patients require lifelong low-dose therapy (AZA 50–75 mg/day + low-dose Prednisolone 2.5–5 mg/day)

STEROID-REFRACTORY OR STEROID-INTOLERANT

Mycophenolate mofetil 2 g/day — AZA intolerant or myelosuppression;  similarly effective

Tacrolimus 2–4 mg/day — refractory AIH; some evidence

Ciclosporin — refractory cases; specifically in acute severe AIH to avoid transplantation

Rituximab — emerging evidence in refractory AIH

PORTAL HYPERTENSION IF CIRRHOSIS

Propranolol — variceal prevention

Spironolactone + Furosemide — ascites management

Rifaximin 400 mg TDS — hepatic encephalopathy prevention

Lactulose 30 mL BD-TDS — hepatic encephalopathy

ACUTE LIVER FAILURE

Immediate liver transplantation evaluation if not responding to IV steroids within 7–14 days

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
HEPATOPROTECTIVE

Bhumyamalaki (Phyllanthus niruri) — most extensively studied Ayurvedic agent for hepatitis; anti-viral (HBV), hepatoprotective; anti-inflammatory action in liver; 500 mg–1 g BD; reduces transaminases in multiple clinical trials

Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata) — hepatoprotective; anti-inflammatory; stimulates immune regulation via NF-kB modulation; 250–500 mg BD

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — hepatoprotective and immunomodulatory; reduces hepatic inflammation; 500 mg/day

Amalaki (Emblica officinalis) — antioxidant; Vitamin C; hepatoprotective; 500 mg–1 g/day

Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra / Licorice) — anti-inflammatory; hepatoprotective; glycyrrhizin (IV preparation used in Japan for chronic hepatitis); oral Yashtimadhu 500 mg BD; SIGNIFICANT CAUTION: licorice raises blood pressure and causes pseudoaldosteronism at higher doses; avoid in hypertension and cirrhosis with sodium retention

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Arogyavardhini Vati — Ayurvedic classical liver tonic; compound anti-inflammatory; anti-cholestatic; widely used in India for chronic liver disease

Liv.52 (Himalaya Drug Company) — most widely used hepatoprotective supplement in India; proprietary blend; reasonable safety profile; may reduce DILI risk when on Isoniazid

Punarnavasava — liver and splenic disorders; anti-ascitic

Kumaryasava — liver health; Aloe vera based

IMPORTANT CAUTIONS

CRITICAL: The following Ayurvedic preparations are known hepatotoxins and are ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in autoimmune hepatitis or any liver disease: Kasondi (Cassia occidentalis — very hepatotoxic), raw/unprocessed Bhumi amla in large doses, copper-containing Tamra Bhasma without proper Shodhana, Navayasa Churna (iron compounds), Makaradhwaja (mercury-sulphide preparations without proper processing), Chinese herbal mixture preparations containing Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (found in some Indian herbs — Heliotropium, Crotalaria spp).

Moderate doses of Bhumyamalaki and Kalmegh appear safe even in autoimmune hepatitis and may complement corticosteroid management for hepatoprotection.
diseases.india_specific:
DIAGNOSIS AND EXCLUSION

Viral hepatitis (HBsAg, HCV RNA) must be excluded — HBV and HCV are common in India and can mimic AIH serology (false positive ANA/SMA in viral hepatitis; DILI from anti-viral treatment)

Wilson's disease — particularly in children and young adults; serum ceruloplasmin, urinary copper, slit-lamp — must exclude before steroids (steroids harmful in Wilson's crisis)

ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY

Isoniazid (cornerstone of TB treatment in India) causes drug-induced hepatitis in 1–5% of patients — clinical and biochemical presentation identical to acute viral or autoimmune hepatitis

Isoniazid-induced hepatotoxicity — transient ALP/ALT rise (harmless) vs overt hepatitis (stop INH) vs fulminant hepatic failure (rare)

ALL patients on anti-TB therapy must have baseline LFT and 4-6 weekly monitoring

All patients presenting with acute hepatitis in India — enquire about anti-TB medications

AYURVEDIC HEPATOTOXICITY

A significant proportion of 'AIH-like' presentations in Indian hepatology are actually Ayurvedic DILI

Systematic detailed herbal/traditional medicine history at presentation

Many patients conceal herbal use from biomedical doctors — specifically ask

STEROID SIDE EFFECTS IN INDIA

AIH requires long-term steroids (1–3+ years); in India — weight gain, diabetes, osteoporosis, TB reactivation, cushingoid features are major concerns on long-term prednisolone

IGRA/Mantoux before starting steroids; Isoniazid prophylaxis if positive
diseases.important_notes:
EARLY TREATMENT IS LIVER-SAVING — RESPONSE IS DRAMATIC: AIH is one of the few autoimmune conditions where the primary treatment (prednisolone) is universally available, cheap, and produces dramatic biochemical response (ALT often falls 50% within 2–4 weeks). The tragedy in India is patients presenting with established cirrhosis because AIH went undiagnosed for years (fatigue + elevated ALT attributed to fatty liver, viral hepatitis, or 'stress'). Any young-middle aged woman with persistently elevated transaminases (ALT > 2–3x normal for >3 months) with no obvious cause MUST have ANA, anti-SMA, IgG, and AIH revised score assessment before accepting other diagnoses.

WILSON'S DISEASE MUST BE EXCLUDED IN YOUNG PATIENTS BEFORE STEROIDS: Wilson's disease — autosomal recessive copper metabolism disorder — can present identically to AIH in children and young adults: elevated transaminases, autoantibodies, evidence of liver disease. The critical diff: giving steroids to acute Wilson's disease crisis is NOT beneficial and may accelerate copper-mediated liver destruction. Workup: serum ceruloplasmin (< 20 mg/dL), 24-hour urinary copper (> 100 mcg/day), slit-lamp Kayser-Fleischer rings (KF rings), liver copper content on biopsy. Wilson's disease is treatable: Penicillamine or Trientine + Zinc. FHF from Wilson's disease requires emergency transplantation.

RELAPSE IS COMMON — LONG-TERM FOLLOW UP IS ESSENTIAL: AIH relapse rate is 50% within 1 year of treatment withdrawal, and up to 80% at 5 years. Most patients require lifelong, low-dose immunosuppression (Azathioprine 50–75 mg/day only, or low-dose Prednisolone 5 mg/day + low-dose AZA). Monitoring ALT and IgG every 3–6 months indefinitely is required. In India, patients commonly stop medication when 'feeling better' — this must be specifically countered. Untreated relapse leads to progressive fibrosis, cirrhosis, and eventually transplant need. If a patient insists on stopping medication, serum IgG and ALT must be monitored very frequently (monthly) — rising IgG precedes clinical relapse.
confirmation_tests_curated: Simplified score: ANA or SMA ≥1:40 = 1 pt; ANA/SMA ≥1:80 or anti-LKM1 ≥1:40 or anti-SLA = 2 pt; IgG ≥ULN = 1 pt; >1.1x ULN = 2 pt; compatible histology = 1 pt; typical histology = 2 pt; ANA/SMA titre ≥1:40 = 1 pt; Absence of viral hepatitis = 2 pt. Score ≥6 = probable AIH; ≥7 = definite AIH
ANA (antinuclear antibody) — positive in Type 1 AIH (up to 80%); homogeneous or speckled
Anti-SMA (anti-smooth muscle antibody) — F-actin anti-SMA most specific Type 1 AIH; 70–80%

diseases.name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
diseases.tests:
DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (ADA/WHO)

Random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL + symptoms

Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL

2-hour OGTT ≥200 mg/dL

HbA1c ≥6.5% (48 mmol/mol)

Two results on separate days (except if unequivocal hyperglycaemia with symptoms)

DISTINGUISHING T1DM FROM T2DM

C-peptide — low/undetectable in T1DM; normal/elevated in T2DM; done when fasting glucose >4 mmol/L; stimulated C-peptide (glucagon or mixed meal test) more informative

GAD65 antibody (anti-GAD) — most useful and widely tested; positive in 70–80% T1DM; also positive in LADA

IA-2 antibody (anti-islet tyrosine phosphatase) — positive in 50–60% T1DM

ZnT8 antibody — adds 10% diagnostic yield when added to GAD + IA-2

Insulin autoantibodies (IAA) — useful in very young children (< 5 years) before insulin treatment

METABOLIC MONITORING

HbA1c — every 3 months; target <7% (53 mmol/mol); <7.5% in children with hypoglycaemia risk

Fasting glucose + post-prandial glucose self-monitoring; CGM time-in-range

COMPLICATION SCREENING (Annual in T1DM)

Urine ACR (albumin/creatinine ratio) — nephropathy screening; microalbuminuria >3 mg/mmol

eGFR (renal function)

Dilated fundoscopy — retinopathy; from 5 years after diagnosis (or puberty onset)

Foot examination — neuropathy; peripheral vascular disease

Blood pressure — target <130/80 mmHg

Lipids — LDL target <70 mg/dL (T1DM high CV risk)

TSH (annual) — thyroid autoimmune screening

Anti-TTG (anti-tissue transglutaminase) — Coeliac screening at diagnosis and periodically

ASSOCIATED AUTOIMMUNE

Thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPO Ab), thyroglobulin Ab

21-hydroxylase antibodies — Addison's if unexplained hypoglycaemia or falling insulin needs
diseases.generic_medicines:
INSULIN — LIFELONG AND MANDATORY

T1DM requires lifelong exogenous insulin — there is NO oral alternative; insulin is not optional

BASAL-BOLUS REGIMEN (Physiological replacement):

Insulin Glargine (Lantus / Toujeo) or Insulin Detemir — once or twice daily basal insulin; provides continuous background coverage; peak-free

Insulin Aspart (NovoRapid) / Insulin Lispro (Humalog) / Insulin Glulisine (Apidra) — rapid-acting analogue; given with each meal

CONVENTIONAL REGIMEN (Affordable — Jan Aushadhi)

NPH Insulin (Isophane / Humulin N / Wockhardt Insuman) — intermediate-acting basal; BD dosing

Regular Human Insulin (Actrapid / Humulin R) — short-acting bolus; give 30 minutes before meals

DOSE TITRATION

Total daily dose: ~ 0.5–0.7 units/kg/day; 50% basal + 50% bolus

Sick-day rules: never stop insulin even when not eating; increase monitoring frequency; check ketones

DKA TREATMENT (Emergency)

IV Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl) — aggressive fluid resuscitation

IV Regular Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour

Potassium replacement (critical — DKA = total body K+ depletion despite normal serum K+)

Bicarbonate — only if pH < 6.9 (controversial)

HYPOGLYCAEMIA TREATMENT

Conscious: 15–20 g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, fruit juice, sugary drink) — 15 rule

Unconscious: Glucagon kit 1 mg SC/IM; IV Dextrose 50% 25–50 mL

OTHER MEDICATIONS

ACE inhibitor (Ramipril, Lisinopril) — microalbuminuria or hypertension; nephroprotective

Statin — T1DM >40 years or established CVD

Low-dose aspirin — secondary prevention CVD

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
BLOOD GLUCOSE SUPPORTIVE

T1DM is an absolute insulin deficiency — no herbal preparation can replace insulin, and herbal therapy must NEVER be used as an alternative. However, certain herbs have adjunctive roles in glycaemic support.

Bitter Melon / Karela (Momordica charantia) — insulin-secretagogue and insulin sensitiser properties; multiple small RCTs; 50–100 mL fresh juice or 500 mg extract; modest FBS reduction; safe; does NOT replace insulin; can augment in T1DM as modest adjunct with close monitoring to avoid hypoglycaemia

Gymnema sylvestre (Gurmar) — 'sugar destroyer'; reduces glucose absorption; may support residual beta cell function in early LADA; 400 mg extract OD; published studies in T2DM

Fenugreek seeds (Methi) — soluble seed fibre reduces post-prandial glucose rise; 10–25 g soaked seeds before meals; good safety; common in Indian diet

Vijayasar (Pterocarpus marsupium) — beta-cell regeneration claims in animal models; clinical evidence limited; traditional use in India for diabetes

Turmeric / Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; may protect residual beta cells; reduces T2DM insulin resistance; 500 mg–1 g BD; safe adjunct

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Nishamalaki (Turmeric + Amla formulation) — glucose-modulating; anti-diabetic traditional combination

Vasant Kusumakar Rasa — classical Ayurvedic formulation for diabetes, debility, and urinary disorders; contains precious metals (gold, silver) — only quality-assured preparations from reputed Ayurvedic pharmacies

Chandraprabha Vati — UTI prevention (T1DM increased UTI risk); urogenital anti-inflammatory

Triphala — constipation and GI health; autonomic neuropathy-related gastroparesis support

NEUROPATHY

Bala (Sida cordifolia) — nerve tonic; anti-neuropathic; Vata-modifying; 500 mg BD

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — nerve tonic; anti-inflammatory; reduces oxidative neuropathy

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive support; autonomic and peripheral neuropathy

IMPORTANT NOTE

CRITICAL: Herbal medicines that lower blood glucose — Karela, Bitter gourd, Gurmar — ADD to the hypoglycaemia risk in patients already on insulin. Patients taking insulin AND Karela juice or Gymnema extract MUST monitor glucose more frequently to avoid additive hypoglycaemia. This interaction must be proactively anticipated. All reductions must be done with careful glucose monitoring.
diseases.india_specific:
YOUNG-ONSET T1DM IN INDIA

T1DM in India presents younger than global average; mean age of diagnosis 8–12 years in children; significant burden of childhood DKA as first presentation

Misdiagnosis of T1DM as T2DM common in India — obese adolescent with new-onset diabetes may have T1DM; check C-peptide, GAD antibodies; wrong diagnosis leads to inadequate insulin therapy and repeated DKA

INSULIN ACCESS AND AFFORDABILITY

Insulin access in India: significant urban-rural divide; interruption of insulin supply is the leading cause of DKA hospitalisation in India

Human insulins (Regular, NPH) available very cheaply and at Jan Aushadhi Kendra; Insulin analogues (Glargine, Detemir, Aspart, Lispro) are more expensive but superior in hypoglycaemia profile

LATENT AUTOIMMUNE DIABETES IN ADULTS (LADA)

LADA = Type 1.5 diabetes; slowly progressive autoimmune beta cell loss in adults (age >30); initially responds to OHA; eventually requires insulin; GAD antibodies positive

LADA misclassified as T2DM in India repeatedly — key: unexpected OHA failure, lean build, positive GAD antibody

FLAT GLUCOSE MONITORING CURVE / CGMS ACCESS

CGM technology increasingly affordable in India (Freestyle Libre); transforms T1DM management; especially helpful for nocturnal hypoglycaemia detection

FIBRO-CALCULOUS PANCREATIC DIABETES (FCPD)

India-specific; tropical chronic pancreatitis → pancreatic calcification → absolute insulin deficiency; clinically resembles T1DM; different aetiology; malnutrition + cassava toxin (cyanogen) historically implicated; South India, Kerala, Odisha; diagnosed on CT abdomen (pancreatic calculi)
diseases.important_notes:
NEVER STOP INSULIN IN T1DM — EVEN DURING ILLNESS OR FASTING: The commonest cause of preventable DKA hospitalisation in India is insulin omission during intercurrent illness, vomiting, or religious fasting. Patients mistakenly believe 'if I am not eating, I do not need insulin.' This is WRONG and dangerous. In T1DM, even with zero food intake, basal insulin requirements remain constant (50% of total daily dose). Stopping insulin triggers uncontrolled lipolysis → ketone production → metabolic acidosis → DKA. Sick day rule: NEVER stop insulin; reduce dose by 20–30% only if unable to eat but always maintain basal insulin; check glucose and urine/blood ketones every 4 hours; hospitalise if ketones rising and glucose >300 mg/dL.

LATENT AUTOIMMUNE DIABETES IN ADULTS (LADA) IS MISDIAGNOSED IN INDIA: A lean/normal-weight 30–50-year-old presenting with new-onset diabetes in India is frequently diagnosed as Type 2 and started on Metformin and Sulfonylureas. LADA (Type 1.5) is common — estimated 10% of all 'Type 2' diagnosed adults in India may have LADA. Clues: lean build, age 25–50, no strong family T2DM history, unexpected OHA failure within 6–12 months, coexisting autoimmune condition. GAD antibody testing is the key diagnostic test. LADA requires insulin earlier — Sulfonylureas accelerate beta cell exhaustion in LADA and should be avoided once LADA suspected. Misdiagnosis delays insulin treatment and increases DKA risk and long-term complication burden.

FIBRO-CALCULOUS PANCREATIC DIABETES (FCPD) IS AN INDIA-SPECIFIC FORM: FCPD results from tropical chronic pancreatitis destroying both exocrine and endocrine pancreatic function. It affects young adults (15–40 years) predominantly in South India, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and Odisha. Features: absolute insulin deficiency (like T1DM), pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (steatorrhoea), and large pancreatic ductal calculi on imaging. Unlike T1DM, these patients are negative for GAD and islet cell antibodies. Malnutrition is common. Management: insulin (like T1DM), pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon), nutritional support, pain management. CT abdomen showing pancreatic calcifications confirms diagnosis — this specific diagnosis must be considered in any insulin-requiring young patient from South India with malnutrition.

diseases.name: Graves Disease
diseases.tests:
THYROID FUNCTION TESTS (TFT)

TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) — suppressed (<0.01 mU/L) in hyperthyroidism; most sensitive test

Free T4 (FT4) — elevated in overt hyperthyroidism

Free T3 (FT3) — elevated; sometimes disproportionately high in T3 toxicosis; FT3 more useful than T4 in some cases

AUTOANTIBODIES

TRAb (TSH receptor antibody) — pathognomonic of Graves; sensitivity 95%+; anti-TSH-receptor stimulating antibodies; positive titre confirms Graves; use for: confirming diagnosis, assessing remission, pregnancy monitoring (neonatal Graves risk from transplacental TRAb)

Anti-TPO (anti-thyroid peroxidase) — elevated in Graves and Hashimoto's; less specific for Graves

THYROID ULTRASOUND

Diffuse enlargement, hypervascularity ('thyroid inferno' on colour Doppler) — Graves pattern

Nodular goitre — toxic adenoma or toxic multinodular goitre (different from Graves — no TRAb)

RADIOIODINE UPTAKE (RAIU) / I-123 SCAN

Graves: diffusely increased homogeneous uptake

Toxic nodule: focally increased; background suppressed

Thyroiditis (silent, subacute): DECREASED uptake (destructive thyrotoxicosis — NOT overproduction)

GRAVES OPHTHALMOPATHY ASSESSMENT

Clinical activity score (CAS) — inflammatory activity assessment; CAS ≥3/7 = active GO

CT orbits or MRI orbits — EOM enlargement; orbital fat; optic nerve compression

Proptosis measurement (exophthalmometer)

ANCILLARY

ECG — atrial fibrillation assessment

Glucose — thyrotoxicosis exacerbates diabetes

LFT — PTU (but not CBZ) can cause hepatotoxicity
diseases.generic_medicines:
ANTITHYROID DRUGS (ATD)

Carbimazole 15–40 mg/day (starting dose) reducing to 5–15 mg/day maintenance — Block-and-replace or titration regimen; 18 months treatment; 50% remission rate; preferred in UK/India

Methimazole 15–40 mg/day — equivalent to carbimazole (CBZ is prodrug → methimazole); USA preferred

Propylthiouracil (PTU) 300–600 mg/day divided TDS — preferred in FIRST TRIMESTER PREGNANCY (CBZ teratogenic); liver toxicity risk (fatal hepatotoxicity reported) — avoid long-term use preferring carbimazole

Block-and-replace: Carbimazole 40 mg/day + T4 100 mcg/day — simpler titration; more medication; preferred in severe disease or unstable patients

SYMPTOMATIC

Propranolol 40–120 mg/day — beta-blocker; controls tachycardia, tremor, anxiety, heat intolerance RAPIDLY (within hours); bridge until ATD takes effect (6–8 weeks); NOT anti-thyroid

Atenolol 50–100 mg/day — alternative cardioselective beta-blocker

RADIOIODINE (RAI) I-131

Administered as single oral dose in nuclear medicine; ablates thyroid; permanent hypothyroidism ensues (planned)

Pre-treatment preparation: ATD to control thyroid function (stop CBZ 3–5 days before RAI); RAI in euthyroid state reduces thyroid storm risk

Contraindications: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, active GO

THYROID STORM (Emergency)

PTU 500 mg stat (inhibits synthesis + peripheral conversion T4→T3); Lugol's iodine (reduces thyroid hormone release — after PTU!); IV Propranolol; Dexamethasone 8 mg/day; Supportive (cooling, fluids)

GRAVES OPHTHALMOPATHY

Selenium 200 mcg/day — mild GO; TRAb reduction

IV Methylprednisolone (EUGOGO protocol) — moderate-to-severe active GO; fortnightly pulses

Orbital radiotherapy — adjunct to steroids in active GO

Teprotumumab (anti-IGF-1R) — transformative for moderate-severe GO; approved FDA 2020; not widely available India yet

THYROID SURGERY — TOTAL THYROIDECTOMY

Definitive treatment; large goitre; ophthalmopathy (surgery preferred over RAI in active GO); children; patient preference

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
THYROID-MODULATING HERBS

Bugleweed (Lycopus virginicus / Lycopus europaeus) — European herbal medicine; mild antithyroid activity; reduces T3/T4 production; inhibits TSH binding; studied in mild hyperthyroidism; reduces palpitations and anxiety in thyrotoxicosis; 200–500 mg extract; NOT yet mainstream in Indian Ayurveda but available as supplement in India

Lemon Balm (Melissa officinalis) — anti-thyroid and anti-TSH receptor activity; reduces anxiety (thyrotoxicosis feature); available as standardised extract

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — central anxiolytic; reduces anxiety and insomnia secondary to thyrotoxicosis; well-studied in Indian Ayurveda; does not directly reduce thyroid hormones

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — IMPORTANT CAUTION: Ashwagandha can STIMULATE thyroid function and increase T3/T4 levels; HAS BEEN DOCUMENTED TO WORSEN HYPERTHYROIDISM; CONTRAINDICATED in Graves disease and hyperthyroidism; safe in HYPOTHYROIDISM (Hashimoto's)

Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina) — reduces tachycardia and anxiety in thyrotoxicosis; heart rate controlling Vata property; CAUTION: antihypertensive; sedative; psychosis association with Reserpine (active compound)

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — anxiolytic; reduces restlessness and insomnia of thyrotoxicosis; nerve tonic

Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna) — cardiac support; tachycardia and palpitations; reduces AF risk

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Sarpagandhaghana Vati — classical Ayurvedic anti-adrenergic; heart rate control in thyrotoxicosis

Brahmi Vati — anxiolytic and nervine tonic for thyrotoxicosis symptoms

Kanchnar Guggulu — classical formulation for thyroid enlargement (goitre); Kanchnar (Bauhinia variegata) is the key thyroid-modulating herb; widely prescribed in India for goitre reduction in BOTH hypo and hyperthyroid states; evidence limited

IMPORTANT NOTE

ABSOLUTE WARNING: Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — one of the most widely consumed Ayurvedic supplements in India — is directly contraindicated in Graves disease. It stimulates thyroid hormone production and multiple cases of hyperthyroidism exacerbation and thyroid storm precipitated by Ashwagandha supplementation are documented. Patients with Graves or any hyperthyroid state MUST be specifically warned. Bugleweed is a promising herbal option for mild thyrotoxicosis symptom support but does NOT replace ATD therapy in Graves disease.
diseases.india_specific:
IODINE EXCESS

Universal iodisation of salt in India — hypothyroidism has reduced; but Jod-Basedow effect: sudden iodine load (iodinated contrast) can precipitate thyrotoxicosis in nodular goitre (not Graves per se, but important differential)

HIGH SELENIUM DEFICIENCY

Selenium deficiency: suboptimal selenium in many Indian diets; selenium is cofactor for thyroid peroxidase and deiodinase enzymes; selenium supplementation (200 mcg/day) shown to reduce TRAb titres in mild Graves ophthalmopathy (EUGOGO et al; WHO recommendation now)

ANTITHYROID DRUG (ATD) AGRANULOCYTOSIS

Carbimazole/Methimazole or Propylthiouracil-induced agranulocytosis: life-threatening; fever + sore throat on ATD = STOP drug immediately, check FBC same day; educate all patients starting ATD

RADIOIODINE (RAI) THERAPY IN INDIA

RAI (I-131) widely used in India; principal long-term option in non-fertile adults and older patients; causes hypothyroidism in 50–90% at 1 year (planned outcome of treatment)

RAI CONTRAINDICATIONS: Pregnancy, active/sight-threatening Graves ophthalmopathy, breastfeeding, children

THYROID STORM IN INDIA

In India, thyroid storm occurs in: undiagnosed Graves + precipitant (infection, surgery, pregnancy); inadequate pre-operative preparation; Iodine during RAI in inadequately prepared patient

THYROID AND PREGNANCY

Uncontrolled Graves in pregnancy: fetal loss, stillbirth, neonatal Graves (TRAb crosses placenta); monitor TRAb in pregnancy; target euthyroid; PTU (not CBZ) in first trimester
diseases.important_notes:
AGRANULOCYTOSIS — STOP ATD IMMEDIATELY IF FEVER AND SORE THROAT: Carbimazole and Propylthiouracil can cause agranulocytosis (absolute neutrophil count < 500/microL) in 0.3–0.5% of patients. Agranulocytosis onset is unpredictable, can occur within days to months of starting treatment, and is potentially fatal. All patients starting antithyroid drugs MUST be educated: if fever, sore throat, or mouth ulcers develop — STOP the drug immediately and present to an emergency department for urgent FBC. Regular FBC monitoring does NOT reliably detect agranulocytosis before onset. Agranulocytosis recovery occurs with drug cessation; G-CSF may be used. Crucially: agranulocytosis with one ATD (carbimazole vs PTU) — cross-reactivity is 50% so switching is controversial.

GRAVES OPHTHALMOPATHY IS A SEPARATE DISEASE FROM HYPERTHYROIDISM: Graves ophthalmopathy (GO) does NOT always parallel hyperthyroidism severity. A patient can have mild thyrotoxicosis but severe sight-threatening GO — and conversely, severe hyperthyroidism with no eye involvement. Critically: Radioiodine (RAI) therapy can WORSEN active GO (30% risk of GO deterioration post-RAI) — therefore RAI is CONTRAINDICATED in moderate-to-severe active GO in preference to surgery or ATD. In India, this distinction is often missed — patients with active GO are referred for RAI without ophthalmology joint assessment. EUGOGO (European Group on Graves Orbitopathy) guidelines: active GO (CAS ≥3) → IV methylprednisolone; if moderate-to-severe, refer to multidisciplinary thyroid eye clinic; surgery preferred over RAI.

PREGNANCY AND GRAVES DISEASE — SERIOUS FETAL RISKS: Uncontrolled Graves disease in pregnancy causes miscarriage, preterm birth, and neonatal Graves disease. Carbimazole (CBZ) is associated with teratogenicity (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia — the CBZ embryopathy) in first trimester — PTU is preferred in first trimester. After first trimester, switch to CBZ is preferred (PTU hepatotoxicity). TRAb crosses the placenta: even euthyroid mothers with Graves remission can have TRAb-positive blood and deliver babies with neonatal hyperthyroidism (tachycardia, irritability, poor feeding in neonate). TRAb must be measured at 20–24 weeks and at 32–36 weeks in all pregnant women with present or past Graves disease to predict neonatal Graves.

diseases.name: Hashimotos Thyroiditis
diseases.tests:
THYROID FUNCTION TESTS

TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) — most sensitive test; elevated TSH = primary hypothyroidism; normal TSH range: 0.4–4.0 mU/L (lab-specific)

Subclinical hypothyroidism: TSH 4–10 mU/L + normal FT4 — treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic or anti-TPO positive or pregnant

Overt hypothyroidism: TSH >10 mU/L + reduced FT4

Free T4 (FT4) — reduced in overt hypothyroidism; reference range: 9–23 pmol/L

Free T3 (FT3) — low in severe hypothyroidism; T3 toxicosis screening

AUTOANTIBODIES

Anti-TPO (anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody) — positive in 90–95% Hashimoto's; confirms autoimmune aetiology; raised titres predict progression from subclinical to overt hypothyroidism; risk in pregnancy

Anti-thyroglobulin antibody (anti-TgAb) — positive in 60–70% Hashimoto's; often co-elevated with anti-TPO

TRAb (TSH receptor antibody) — needed to distinguish Hashimoto's from Graves in hashitoxicosis phase

THYROID ULTRASOUND

Heterogeneous echotexture, echogenic lines/bands (fibrosis), reduced vascularity (vs hypervascularity in Graves), small volume atrophic gland in long-standing disease

Not required for diagnosis if TSH + anti-TPO confirm AITD; useful for nodule assessment

COELIAC SCREENING

Anti-TTG IgA + total serum IgA — Coeliac disease co-association; Coeliac causes T4 malabsorption; mismanaged if missed

SCREENING FOR CO-ASSOCIATIONS

FBG / HbA1c — T1DM (annual in Hashimoto's)

Serum cortisol / Synacthen test — if unexplained fatigue despite adequate T4 (exclude Addison's)

Vitamin B12 — pernicious anaemia association

PREGNANCY

TSH at booking; anti-TPO; if anti-TPO positive monitor TSH monthly first trimester, every 2 months thereafter; target TSH <2.5 first trimester
diseases.generic_medicines:
LEVOTHYROXINE (T4) — FIRST-LINE AND STANDARD

Levothyroxine sodium 25–200 mcg/day — once daily; taken fasted 30–45 minutes before food; half-life 7 days; dose titrated to TSH and symptom resolution

Starting dose: elderly and cardiac disease patients start 25–50 mcg/day; younger healthy adults start 50–75 mcg/day; full replacement dose 1.6 mcg/kg/day estimated

Titration: repeat TSH 6–8 weeks after each dose change; titrate in 25 mcg increments

Target TSH: 0.5–2.5 mU/L for most adults; lower targeting logical for symptoms; during pregnancy TSH <2.5 first trimester, <3.0 second/third

INTERACTIONS (reducing T4 absorption — give 4 hours apart):

Calcium carbonate, Iron supplements, Antacids (aluminium, magnesium), PPIs/H2 blockers, Cholestyramine, Soya products

T4/T3 COMBINATION THERAPY

Liothyronine (T3) 5–10 mcg/day added to T4 in persistently symptomatic patients with normal TSH — evidence-limited; T3 has short half-life; unstable TSH; some evidence for improved mood/wellbeing; specialist initiation

SUBCLINICAL HYPOTHYROIDISM — TREATMENT INDICATIONS

TSH >10 mU/L — treat regardless

TSH 4–10 + symptomatic (fatigue, depression, weight gain)

TSH 4–10 + anti-TPO positive (high risk of progression)

TSH 4–10 + PREGNANCY (mandatory)

TSH 4–10 + dyslipidaemia (cholesterol improvement with T4)

MYXOEDEMA COMA (Emergency):

IV Levothyroxine 200–400 mcg loading + IV Hydrocortisone 100 mg TDS (protect against Addison's crisis); ICU

Levothyroxine sodium 25 mcg tablet

Levothyroxine sodium 50 mcg tablet

Levothyroxine sodium 100 mcg tablet

Selenium 200 mcg tablet

Iron tablet (Ferrous Sulfate 150 mg) — anaemia co-treatment

Vitamin B12 500 mcg tablet

Calcium + Vitamin D3 tablet

Levothyroxine 100 mcg (10 tabs): Rs.8

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
THYROID STIMULATING / SUPPORTING HERBS (for Hypothyroidism)

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — MOST EVIDENCE-BASED for hypothyroidism in Ayurveda; multiple clinical trials show Ashwagandha 600 mg/day increases T3/T4 levels in subclinical hypothyroidism; immunomodulatory; reduces anti-TPO antibody titres in some studies; adaptogenic — reduces cortisol (which suppresses thyroid function); 300–600 mg extract OD-BD

Guggul (Commiphora mukul) / Z-guggulsterone — guggulsterone stimulates thyroid hormone synthesis; multiple Indian studies showing reduced T4 supplementation requirement in hypothyroidism with Guggul; anti-inflammatory in AITD; 500 mg BD of standardised extract

Kanchnar Guggulu — classically indicated in Ayurveda for Gandamala (goitre/neck glandular swellings); contains Kanchnar (Bauhinia variegata), Triphala, Trikatu, Guggul; reduces goitre size and supports thyroid function; widely prescribed in India for all thyroid conditions

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive support; addresses brain fog and memory issues of hypothyroidism; well-tolerated; 300–450 mg standardised extract daily

Shankhpushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — cognitive and thyroid-supportive; reduces anxiety and depression component of hypothyroidism

Triphala — constipation (common hypothyroid symptom); reduces cholesterol; antioxidant; 5–10 g at bedtime

Metabolic Herbs

Trikatu (Ginger, Black pepper, Pippali) — thermogenic; metabolic stimulant; counteracts metabolic slowing of hypothyroidism; digestive stimulant; 500 mg with meals

Vrikshamla / Garcinia cambogia — metabolic booster; lipid-lowering; weight support in hypothyroid obesity

SELENIUM SUPPLEMENTATION

Selenium 200 mcg/day — reduces anti-TPO titres; reduces Hashimoto's inflammatory activity; important in selenium-deficient patients; evidence from multiple European trials (CATALYST trial N>500); safe at 200 mcg

IMPORTANT NOTE

Ashwagandha is the most evidence-supported Ayurvedic adjunct in subclinical hypothyroidism and Hashimoto's — it should be considered as an adjunct ALONGSIDE Levothyroxine, NOT as a replacement. Kanchnar Guggulu remains the most widely used and prescribed Ayurvedic formulation for thyroid conditions in India. Guggul's thyroid-stimulating effect is real (documented in Indian pharmacological literature) and can reduce levothyroxine dose requirement — close TSH monitoring required if starting Guggul in a thyroid patient already on T4. In contrast, Ashwagandha is CONTRAINDICATED in Graves disease (stimulates thyroid — worsens hyperthyroidism). This distinction is crucial.
diseases.india_specific:
IODINE DEFICIENCY AND HYPOTHYROIDISM COEXISTENCE

Iodine deficiency goitre (DDG — Dietary Deficiency Goitre) — historically a major cause of thyroid dysfunction in mountain and hilly areas of India (Himalayas, Vindhyas, Sub-Himalayan)

Universal salt iodisation improved this but iodine deficiency hypothyroidism still occurs alongside AITD in India

PREVALENT UNDERDIAGNOSIS

Hypothyroidism is the most under-diagnosed endocrine disorder in India — classic symptoms (fatigue, weight gain, lethargy, constipation) are attributed to anaemia, poor diet, menopause, 'aging' without thyroid function testing

National Family Health Survey data: only ~8% of eligible patients receive thyroid testing; community hypothyroidism prevalence estimates 10–15% in women >40 years in India

PREGNANCY AND MATERNAL HYPOTHYROIDISM

Hypothyroidism in pregnancy: critical for foetal brain development; pre-term birth, miscarriage, foetal cognitive impairment; TSH >2.5 mIU/L in first trimester now considered abnormal in many guidelines

India recommendation: Universal TSH screening in all pregnant women at booking (Endocrine Society India)

Anti-TPO positive women with normal TSH: monitor closely; higher miscarriage risk; consider T4 if TSH >2.5 in pregnancy

LEVOTHYROXINE CONSISTENCY

Significant brand-to-brand variability in India; generic Thyroxine formulations differ; switching brands causes TSH fluctuation; recommend sticking to one brand

Gastric acid requirement for T4 absorption: PPIs, antacids, calcium reduce T4 absorption; T4 should be taken on empty stomach 30–45 minutes before breakfast
diseases.important_notes:
TSH MUST BE NORMALISED IN PREGNANCY — FETAL BRAIN DEVELOPMENT IS AT STAKE: The fetal thyroid does not become functional until 12–14 weeks of gestation. During the first trimester, the developing fetal brain is entirely dependent on MATERNAL thyroid hormones crossing the placenta. Maternal hypothyroidism — even subclinical (TSH 4–10 mU/L with normal FT4) — during first trimester is associated with lower IQ scores, cognitive impairment, and poorer neurodevelopmental outcomes in offspring. India Endocrine Society and ATA guidelines: TSH must be maintained < 2.5 mU/L in first trimester, <3.0 mU/L in second/third trimester. Anti-TPO positive women even with borderline TSH 2.5–4.0 mU/L should have Levothyroxine started and TSH monitored monthly. Universal thyroid screening at first antenatal booking is recommended in India.

LEVOTHYROXINE ABSORPTION IS CRITICAL AND POORLY UNDERSTOOD BY PATIENTS: Levothyroxine is a narrow therapeutic index drug whose absorption is highly sensitive to timing, food, and concurrent medications. Standard instructions: take on empty stomach, 30–45 minutes before breakfast, with water only. Calcium supplements, iron tablets, antacids, PPIs, dairy products, soya milk, fibre — ALL significantly reduce T4 absorption when taken simultaneously (reduce absorption by 30–50%). Brand consistency: significant batch-to-batch variability between generic brands of Levothyroxine in India causes unexplained TSH fluctuations. The preferred practice is to maintain the same brand. If TSH unexpectedly changes on stable dose → first check timing of dose, concomitant medications, and brand switch before changing dose.

SELENIUM SUPPLEMENTATION IS EVIDENCE-BASED IN HASHIMOTO'S: Multiple randomised trials including the CATALYST trial (N=472, 12 months) and multiple meta-analyses confirm that Selenium 200 mcg/day significantly reduces anti-TPO antibody titres, reduces inflammatory markers, and improves quality of life and mood in Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Selenium is a cofactor for thyroid peroxidase and 5'-deiodinase enzymes essential for T4→T3 conversion. Selenium deficiency is common in India (soil selenium content low in many Indian states). Unlike many supplements, selenium supplementation in Hashimoto's has both mechanistic rationale and consistent clinical trial evidence. Dose: 200 mcg/day (NEVER exceed 400 mcg/day — selenium toxicity risk). Prescribe alongside Levothyroxine in all Hashimoto's patients.

diseases.name: Melasma
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Melasma diagnosed clinically by distribution, symmetry, trigger history, and pigment appearance

WOOD'S LAMP EXAMINATION (365 nm UV)

Epidermal melasma — sharply enhanced, darker borders under Wood's lamp

Dermal melasma — no significant enhancement; bluish-grey

Mixed — partial enhancement

Less reliable in very dark skin (Fitzpatrick V-VI) — melanin saturation reduces differentiation

DERMOSCOPY

Regular pigment network, dark brown dots, telangiectasias; distinguishes melasma from: Lichen planus pigmentosus (LPP), drug-induced pigmentation, PIH, Riehl's melanosis, solar lentigos

mMASI SCORING

Standardised score documentation at each visit for objective treatment monitoring

HORMONAL WORKUP (if suspected trigger or PCOS)

TSH — thyroid dysfunction

LH/FSH, testosterone, DHEAS — PCOS workup

Fasting glucose, HbA1c, Insulin — insulin resistance

REFLECTANCE CONFOCAL MICROSCOPY (RCM)

Non-invasive real-time melanin depth imaging; distinguishes epidermal vs dermal component; available at advanced dermatology centres

SKIN BIOPSY (rarely needed)

Confirms melanin location (melanocytes with increased melanin granules vs dermal melanophages); guides laser selection
diseases.generic_medicines:
SUNSCREEN — MANDATORY FOUNDATION OF ALL MELASMA TREATMENT

Broad-spectrum SPF50+ PA+++ sunscreen — daily application on all exposed skin; reapplication every 2 hours outdoors; TINTED sunscreen containing iron oxide or pigment mix = blocks visible HEV light — preferred in India; no topical agent works without strict photoprotection

TOPICAL DEPIGMENTING AGENTS

Hydroquinone (HQ) 2–4% cream — gold standard tyrosinase inhibitor; 8–12 week induction; nighttime use; NOT for >6 months continuous; monitor for ochronosis

Triple combination cream (Kligman's formula): HQ 4% + Tretinoin 0.05% + Fluocinolone 0.01% — most studied combination; 8–12 weeks induction; cannot be used long-term (steroid component)

Kojic acid 1–4% — tyrosinase inhibitor; safe alternative/adjunct to HQ; contact dermatitis risk in some patients

Azelaic acid 15–20% — mild depigmenting + anti-inflammatory; safe in PREGNANCY; well tolerated

Alpha-arbutin 1–4% — HQ precursor; milder; safer for long-term maintenance

Tretinoin 0.025–0.05% — epidermal turnover; melanin dispersal; nighttime maintenance; combine with SPF

Niacinamide 4–5% — inhibits melanosome transfer; anti-inflammatory; widely available in Indian skincare

ORAL AGENTS

Tranexamic acid (TXA) 250 mg BD — most important oral advance for melasma; competitively inhibits plasminogen activator → reduces UV-induced prostaglandin synthesis → reduces melanocyte stimulation; multiple Asian RCTs including India data; excellent response in Indian patients; combine with topicals

Glutathione 500 mg/day — antioxidant; inhibits pro-melanogenic eumelanin synthesis; popular in India; safety profile good at therapeutic doses

PROCEDURES

Chemical peels: Glycolic acid 35–70%, Salicylic acid 20–30%, Lactic acid — epidermal melanin dispersal; series of 4–6 peels every 2–3 weeks

Q-switched Nd:YAG 1064 nm laser — toning treatment for dermal melasma; multiple sessions

Microneedling with topical Tranexamic acid or Vitamin C serum delivery

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
TOPICAL VARNYA (SKIN BRIGHTENING)

Kumkumadi Tailam — classical Ayurvedic facial oil; contains Kesar (Saffron), Manjistha, Chandan, Yashtimadhu, Usheer; documented tyrosinase-inhibiting components (Glabridin from Yashtimadhu, curcumin from Haridra, saffron crocin); 5–10 drops as facial oil at night; widely used in India; clinically studied in multiple trials

Yashtimadhu (Liquorice / Glycyrrhiza glabra) — Glabridin content is a potent tyrosinase inhibitor; anti-inflammatory; inhibits UVB-induced melanogenesis; 1–2% Glabridin-standardised cream; highly effective in epidermal melasma

Chandan (Sandalwood) — alpha-santalol inhibits tyrosinase; skin brightening; cooling Pitta-reducing; sandalwood paste + rose water topical

Usheer (Vetiver / Vetiveria zizanioides) — cooling; anti-melanogenic; Usheer-based face packs

Amalaki (Amla) paste — natural Vitamin C; inhibits DOPA oxidation step in melanogenesis; widely used as topical brightening agent

Rakta Chandan (Red Sandalwood / Pterocarpus santalinus) — anti-melanogenic; anti-inflammatory; combine with milk

Masoor Dal Ubtan (Red Lentil scrub) — traditional Indian exfoliant; gentle NAD-based lightening; widely used home remedy

SYSTEMIC

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — premier Varnya (skin brightening) herb in Ayurveda; anti-melanogenic; reduces reactive melanocyte activity; 250–500 mg extract BD

Amalaki (Amla) oral — 500 mg–1 g/day; Vitamin C; potently inhibits DOPA oxidation; systemic antioxidant against melanogenesis

Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) — astringent; reduces Pitta hyperpigmentation; combined with Manjistha

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Kumkumadi Lepa — face paste formulation; saffron, sandalwood, turmeric blend

Sarivadyasava — systemic blood purifier; chronic hyperpigmentation

Chandra Prabha Vati — hormonal balance; melasma from hormonal (PCOS/OCP) causes

IMPORTANT NOTE

Kumkumadi Tailam is the most pharmacologically validated Ayurvedic formulation for melasma — it contains multiple genuine tyrosinase-inhibiting molecules. However, no Ayurvedic treatment replaces SPF50+ sunscreen as the foundation of melasma management. Mercury and lead-containing Ayurvedic skin preparations ('whitening bhasmas') MUST be avoided — documented to cause systemic toxicity and paradoxical irreversible hyperpigmentation.
diseases.india_specific:
FITZPATRICK III-V PHOTOTYPE PREVALENCE

Indian skin (predominantly Fitzpatrick III-V) has higher melanocyte density and more reactive melanocytes; more prone to relapse; more likely to have mixed/dermal component; more prone to exogenous ochronosis from HQ overuse

EXOGENOUS OCHRONOSIS — INDIA-SPECIFIC HQ COMPLICATION

Prolonged Hydroquinone use (>6 months, >4% concentration, under intense tropical UV) in dark skin can paradoxically cause Exogenous Ochronosis — irreversible bluish-black hyperpigmentation darker than original melasma at the treated sites; characteristic banana-shaped distribution; irreversible; documented in multiple Indian case series

Rule: Never use HQ for >6 months continuously; always take a 2–3 month break; switch to Kojic acid or Arbutin for maintenance

TOPICAL STEROID AND MERCURY FAIRNESS CREAM MISUSE

Billion-dollar Indian fairness cream market; many OTC products contain undisclosed HQ, mercury, lead, and steroids; mercury-based creams documented to cause systemic mercury toxicity (neurological, renal impairment) in Indian patients; rebound hyperpigmentation from steroid-containing fairness creams

OCP PRESCRIPTION WITHOUT DERMATOLOGICAL COUNSELLING

OCP is a major melasma trigger; in India OCP prescribed widely without specific sun protection counselling; SPF50+ and sun avoidance MUST be advised with every OCP prescription in Indian women

VISIBLE LIGHT CONCERN IN INDIA

Indian melasma patients significantly triggered by HEV (visible blue light) from indoor fluorescent lights and digital devices; standard chemical sunscreens (UV filters only) insufficient; TINTED SUNSCREEN WITH IRON OXIDE required for complete visible light protection
diseases.important_notes:
SUNSCREEN IS THE TREATMENT, NOT AN ADD-ON: Melasma is fundamentally UV and visible-light-driven. Without rigorous daily SPF50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen application (reapplied every 2 hours outdoors), no topical depigmenting agent — not HQ, not TXA, not retinoids — will produce lasting benefit. Melanocytes are re-stimulated by UV every morning, nullifying the overnight action of any treatment. Indian context: tinted sunscreen containing iron oxide is preferred because it uniquely blocks HEV (visible blue light and violet light), which drives melasma even indoors through windows and from digital screens. This is not well understood by most prescribers and patients.

EXOGENOUS OCHRONOSIS IS IRREVERSIBLE — HYDROQUINONE MUST BE USED WITH A STOP-RULE: HQ is highly effective but carries the specific risk of Exogenous Ochronosis in dark-skinned patients who use it long-term (>6 months, esp. >4% concentration) under tropical UV conditions — producing irreversible bluish-black hyperpigmentation worse than the original melasma. Multiple Indian case series document this. The safe HQ prescribing rule: maximum 6 months continuous use, then stop; switch to Kojic acid, Arbutin, or Azelaic acid for maintenance holiday; resume HQ after 2–3 month break if needed. Never dispense HQ without explicit duration and stop counselling.

TRANEXAMIC ACID IS THE ORAL ADVANCE THAT CHANGED MELASMA MANAGEMENT IN INDIA: Oral Tranexamic Acid 250 mg BD has multiple high-quality Asian RCTs demonstrating significant mMASI score reduction (30–50% improvement), excellent tolerability, and unique mechanism of action distinct from topical agents. In Indian practice, TXA oral + tinted SPF50+ sunscreen + topical Kojic acid/Azelaic acid is now considered the most effective combination for moderate-severe melasma. Take for 3–6 months; contraindicated in history of thrombosis, coagulation disorder, or pregnancy. Check baseline coagulation workup in patients with prior clotting history.
confirmation_tests_curated: Melasma diagnosed clinically by distribution, symmetry, trigger history, and pigment appearance
Epidermal melasma — sharply enhanced, darker borders under Wood's lamp
Dermal melasma — no significant enhancement; bluish-grey

diseases.name: Androgenetic Alopecia
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

AGA diagnosed clinically using Hamilton-Norwood (male) or Ludwig/Savin (female) classification and distribution pattern

TRICHOSCOPY (DERMOSCOPY)

Hair diameter diversity >20% — miniaturisation criterion (>20% of hairs on dermoscopy have reduced diameter relative to terminal hairs)

Peripilar sign — perifollicular brown discolouration (sebaceous gland hypertrophy)

Yellow dots, empty follicles — late AGA

HAIR PULL TEST

Pull 40–60 hairs gently; >10% anagen:telogen ratio change or >6 telogen hairs = active effluvium (TE component)

HORMONAL WORKUP (MANDATORY IN FEMALE AGA)

Serum testosterone (total and free) — elevated in hyperandrogenism

DHEAS — adrenal androgen source

LH/FSH ratio — PCOS

Prolactin — hyperprolactinaemia

TSH — hypothyroidism causes diffuse hair loss; hypothyroid + AGA coexist frequently

Serum ferritin — iron deficiency accelerates hair loss; target ferritin >70 ng/mL in women

Serum zinc — deficiency worsen loss

Serum Vitamin D — deficiency linked to hair loss

FBG, insulin, HbA1c — insulin resistance (PCOS/metabolic syndrome)

SCALP BIOPSY (if diagnostic uncertainty)

Horizontal sections: miniaturised follicle:terminal follicle ratio >1:1 confirms AGA; rules out scarring alopecia
diseases.generic_medicines:
TOPICAL — BOTH GENDERS

Minoxidil 2% solution (females) / 5% solution or foam (males) — vasodilator; prolongs anagen; promotes follicular vascularisation; apply OD-BD to dry scalp; results in 3–6 months; must continue lifelong — hair relost within 4–6 months of stopping; FDA approved for AGA since 1988

Minoxidil 0.25 mg oral (low-dose oral Minoxidil, LDOM) — increasingly used; 1 tablet OD; as effective as topical; better compliance; SE: hypertrichosis (body hair); very affordable in India; off-label but widely used

MALE AGA — SYSTEMIC

Finasteride 1 mg/day — 5-alpha reductase type II inhibitor; reduces scalp DHT 60–70%; approved for male AGA; 80% halt progression; partial regrowth in 65% at 2 years; take daily for life; SE: sexual side effects (2–3%) — erectile dysfunction, reduced libido, ejaculatory dysfunction (mostly reversible on stopping)

Dutasteride 0.5 mg/day — inhibits both 5-AR type I and II; more potent DHT reduction (>90%); approved for AGA in South Korea/Japan; off-label in India and West; more effective than Finasteride in some trials

FEMALE AGA — HORMONAL

Spironolactone 50–200 mg/day — anti-androgen; used off-label for female AGA; monitor potassium, BP; contraception needed (anti-androgenic effect on male fetus)

Combined OCP with anti-androgenic progestogen (Cyproterone acetate, Drospirenone) — reduces androgen-driven AGA in PCOS

Minoxidil 2% + Tretinoin 0.025% topical combination — enhances Minoxidil penetration and efficacy

PROCEDURES

Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) injection — autologous growth factor injection into scalp; 3–6 sessions monthly; evidence for halting progression and increasing density; widely available in India

Low-Level Laser Therapy (LLLT) — helmet/cap devices; FDA approved for AGA; photobiomodulation; 25 min 3x/week

Hair transplant (FUT/FUE) — permanent surgical solution; only for stable AGA; requires co-medical therapy

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
HAIR GROWTH PROMOTERS

Bhringaraja (Eclipta alba) — most evidence-based Ayurvedic herb for AGA; inhibits 5-alpha reductase enzyme (same mechanism as Finasteride) in animal studies; promotes anagen extension; darkens and thickens hair; topical Bhringaraja oil scalp massage 3x/week; oral Bhringaraja churna 3–5 g/day with milk

Methi (Fenugreek / Trigonella foenum-graecum) — contains DHT-blocking compounds; steroidal saponins (diosgenin); traditional hair loss treatment; Methi seed paste scalp pack; oral Methi seeds 10 g/day; multiple Indian studies

Amla (Emblica officinalis) — antioxidant; Vitamin C; inhibits 5-AR; promotes follicular health; Amla oil scalp; oral Amla 500 mg–1 g/day

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces cortisol (stress-driven AGA); hair follicle nourishing; scalp massage oil

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces cortisol; reduces stress-driven hair loss; androgen-modulating properties; 300–600 mg extract BD

DIETARY AND NUTRITIONAL

Sesame seeds (Til) — high in zinc, copper, selenium, Vitamin E; direct hair nutrition; 2 tablespoons daily; traditional Indian hair diet

Flaxseed oil (omega-3) — anti-inflammatory; scalp health; reduces inflammatory-driven loss

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Mahabhringaraja Oil — compound Bhringaraja-based oil; most prescribed Ayurvedic hair oil combination; daily scalp massage

Bhringarajarishta — internal systemic Bhringaraja formulation; supports from within

Keshargarbha / Neeli Bhringaraja Taila — Bhringaraja + Indigofera compound oil; hair blackening and growth

Neem + Amla Shampoo — reduces scalp inflammation (seborrhoeic components exacerbating AGA)

Panchakarma — Shirodhara (warm oil flow on forehead); Shirobasti (oil pooling on scalp); reduces Vata and nourishes scalp — popular in Ayurvedic trichology practice in India

IMPORTANT NOTE

Bhringaraja (Eclipta alba) shows genuine 5-alpha reductase inhibitory activity in pharmacological studies and has multiple Indian clinical trials comparing favourably to Minoxidil for hair growth promotion. It does not have the sexual side effects of Finasteride. However, for progressive AGA — particularly in males with significant Hamilton stage III+ — Finasteride + Minoxidil combination remains the evidence gold standard. Bhringaraja is best used as an adjunct and preventive, not a sole treatment for established AGA.
diseases.india_specific:
EARLY ONSET AGA IN INDIA

AGA increasingly presenting at younger ages (20–30s) in India; genetic predisposition in certain communities; premature AGA (<25 years) associated with higher insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome in Indian males

HAIR OIL CULTURE AND MISCONCEPTIONS

Widespread belief in India that hair oiling prevents or reverses AGA — oils do not address the androgen-driven follicular miniaturisation mechanism; oils maintain scalp moisture and reduce breakage but do not prevent AGA progression; heavy oil + infrequent washing may worsen dandruff which can accelerate loss

FINASTERIDE PRESCRIPTION PATTERNS

Finasteride 1 mg/day — first-line systemic for male AGA; widely prescribed in India; post-finasteride syndrome concerns discussed variedly; generic finasteride very affordable in India

Finasteride in FEMALES: absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic — male foetal genital deformity); used off-label in post-menopausal women only at specialist discretion

PCOS IN FEMALE AGA

PCOS affects 15–20% reproductive-age Indian women; accelerates female AGA; addressing PCOS insulin resistance (Metformin), reducing androgens (OCP with anti-androgenic progestogen), and photobiomodulation significantly improves female AGA outcomes

HAIR TRANSPLANT

Hair transplant (FUE — follicular unit extraction) industry booming in India; affordable relative to Western markets; however AGA must be stabilised before transplant (otherwise donor hair survives but native hair continues miniaturising); Finasteride + Minoxidil for ≥1 year before transplant surgical planning
diseases.important_notes:
FINASTERIDE SEXUAL SIDE EFFECTS — POST-FINASTERIDE SYNDROME: Finasteride 1 mg/day causes sexual adverse effects (reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, ejaculatory dysfunction) in 1.5–2.5% of males on prescribing trials. In most cases, side effects resolve within 3–12 months of stopping. Post-Finasteride Syndrome (PFS) — persistent sexual, cognitive, and neurological effects allegedly continuing after drug cessation — is reported anecdotally and in some case series, though its prevalence and mechanism are disputed. Indian prescribers: all male patients must be counselled specifically about sexual side effects before initiating Finasteride; document this counselling. Alternative: Dutasteride or Low-dose Oral Minoxidil for patients unwilling to take Finasteride. Contraindicated: crushed Finasteride tablets must not be handled by pregnant women (reabsorbed through skin; male foetal genital deformity risk; teratogenic).

MINOXIDIL MUST BE CONTINUED LIFELONG — STOPPING CAUSES FULL RELAPSE: A critical patient education point almost universally misunderstood in India: Minoxidil (both topical and oral) does not cure AGA — it suppresses DHT-driven miniaturisation while being taken. Within 4–6 months of stopping Minoxidil, patients return to where they would have naturally progressed. Patients frequently stop when they see good results, believing they are 'cured' — they then experience dramatic shedding, interpret this as the drug harming their hair, and do not restart. This 'discontinuation shed' is the natural AGA progression resuming, not drug-induced damage. Patients must be told: 'This is like blood pressure medicine — it works only while you take it.'

HAIR TRANSPLANT REQUIRES STABLE MEDICAL THERAPY FIRST — AND CONTINUES AFTER: Hair transplant (FUT/FUE) is a permanent surgical solution for AGA — transplanted follicular units from DHT-resistant occipital donor areas survive permanently. However, untreated native hair will continue miniaturising around transplanted grafts, creating an unnatural-looking outcome ('doll's hair' appearance) over years. The correct protocol: stabilise AGA medically (Finasteride + Minoxidil for minimum 12 months) to demonstrate stable or improving baseline; then plan transplant accordingly; continue Finasteride + Minoxidil after surgery to preserve native hair around grafts. Indian patients seeking transplant without establishing medical therapy first risk poor long-term outcomes.

Hair diameter diversity >20% — miniaturisation criterion (>20% of hairs on dermoscopy have reduced diameter relative to terminal hairs)
Peripilar sign — perifollicular brown discolouration (sebaceous gland hypertrophy)

diseases.name: Rosacea
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Rosacea diagnosed clinically: persistent central facial erythema/flushing, telangiectasias, inflammatory papules/pustules WITHOUT comedones, phymatous changes, ocular involvement

DERMOSCOPY

Telangiectasias (linear, branching vessels on nose, cheeks), polygonal vascular network, follicular plugging; confirms vascular nature; distinguishes from acne (BPO, salicylic acid would be wrong treatment)

REFLECTANCE CONFOCAL MICROSCOPY

Dilated vessels, perifollicular inflammation, Demodex mites in high density — research tool

DEMODEX DENSITY

Skin surface biopsy/tape stripping for Demodex density; >5 mites/cm² = elevated; Demodex-triggered rosacea responds to Ivermectin/Metronidazole

DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS INVESTIGATIONS

ANA, anti-Ro/La — SLE (malar rash of SLE vs rosacea erythema; SLE photosensitive)

TSH — thyroid flush

24-hour urinary 5-HIAA — carcinoid syndrome (episodic flushing + diarrhoea)

Serum tryptase — mastocytosis

OPHTHALMOLOGY REFERRAL

Slit-lamp examination for ocular rosacea; chalazion, blepharitis, meibomian gland dysfunction
diseases.generic_medicines:
TOPICAL THERAPY

Metronidazole 0.75–1% gel/cream — first-line topical; anti-inflammatory; anti-Demodex; BD application; reduces papules/pustules in PPR subtype

Azelaic acid 15–20% gel/cream — anti-inflammatory; reduces erythema and papules; safe and effective; calcineurin-independent mechanism

Ivermectin 1% cream — most effective topical; anti-Demodex + anti-inflammatory; OD application; superior to Metronidazole in head-to-head trials; approved specifically for rosacea

Brimonidine 0.33% gel — alpha2-adrenergic agonist; reduces redness/flushing within 30 minutes; temporary effect (4–8 hours); rebound erythema with overuse

Oxymetazoline 1% cream — topical alpha-1 agonist; persistent erythema of ETR; approved USA 2017

TOPICAL CALCINEURIN INHIBITORS

Tacrolimus 0.1% — anti-inflammatory; useful when topical steroids cannot be used; post-steroid rosacea management

ORAL THERAPY

Doxycycline 40 mg MR (modified release) ONCE daily — anti-inflammatory dose (sub-antimicrobial); FDA approved specifically for rosacea PPR; reduces papules/pustules; does NOT cause antibiotic resistance at this sub-MIC dose; safest long-term oral treatment; first-line systemic

Doxycycline 100 mg BD (short-course 4–6 weeks) — if 40 mg MR not available; then tapering to maintenance

FLUSHING — SPECIFIC TREATMENT

Propranolol 20–40 mg BD — reduces vasomotor flushing; particularly if anxiety-triggered

Carvedilol 6.25 mg BD — alpha+beta blocker; flushing

Clonidine 50–75 mcg BD — central vasomotor inhibitor; flushing (also used for perimenopause flushes)

PHYMATOUS ROSACEA

Isotretinoin 10–20 mg/day — reduces sebaceous gland hypertrophy; prevents rhinophyma progression

CO2 laser, surgical dermabrasion — established rhinophyma
