diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND ANTI-ERYTHEMA TOPICALS

Aloe vera gel — cooling; anti-inflammatory; reduces erythema and burning sensation of rosacea; soothing for sensitive reactive skin; apply fresh gel BD; widely available India

Chandan (Sandalwood) — anti-inflammatory; cooling (Sita Virya = cool energy); reduces Pitta-type erythema and burning; topical sandalwood paste in rose water

Usheer (Vetiver) — intensely cooling; reduces heat-triggered flushing; face mist with Usheer-infused water

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — blood purifier; reduces capillary hyperreactivity; anti-inflammatory; reduces Rakta (blood) Pitta driving rosacea

Green tea extract — topical and oral; EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate) is anti-inflammatory; multiple rosacea RCTs; reduces papules, erythema, and UV-triggered flushing

Neem — anti-Demodex; anti-inflammatory; antimicrobial; reduces Demodex-driven rosacea component; neem leaf extract topical

SYSTEMIC

Haridra (Turmeric / Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces NF-kB activation; oral 500 mg BD curcumin; rosacea as Pitta-dominant skin disorder in Ayurveda; turmeric supports anti-inflammatory treatment

Triphala — internal cleanser; Pitta balancing; systemic anti-inflammatory; 5 g at bedtime

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — cooling; Pitta-reducing; female hormonal rosacea (perimenopausal rosacea onset linked to oestrogen decline); 500 mg BD

DIET AND LIFESTYLE (Ayurvedic)

Pitta-pacifying diet — reduce spicy food, alcohol, fermented foods, sour foods, hot beverages; increase cooling foods (coconut water, coriander seeds, rose water, cucumber)

Avoid triggers specific to Indian lifestyle: chillies, masala chai, hot oil exposure in cooking

IMPORTANT NOTE

Rosacea in Ayurveda maps to Rakta Pitta Dushti (vitiated blood Pitta) causing facial erythema and inflammation. Cooling (Sita Virya) herbs — Chandan, Usheer, Shatavari — align beautifully with the pathophysiology of vascular heat-driven rosacea. Green tea (topical GTP cream) has the strongest non-prescription evidence for rosacea. However, Doxycycline 40 mg modified release (or standard 100 mg) is the oral gold standard for papulopustular rosacea and should be used in moderate-severe cases.
diseases.india_specific:
UNDER-RECOGNITION IN DARKER SKIN

Rosacea classically described in fair-skinned Celtic/Northern European populations (Fitzpatrick I-II); in Fitzpatrick III-V Indian skin, erythema is less visible and may be masked by baseline melanin pigmentation; telangiectasias less apparent; most Indian rosacea presents as papulopustular type (Subtype 2)

MISDIAGNOSIS AS ACNE

Rosacea papulopustular type frequently misdiagnosed as acne in India; key difference: NO comedones in rosacea, central face distribution, age >25, absence of response to anti-acne treatments, flushing history

Treating rosacea with aggressive acne therapies (BPO, salicylic acid, adapalene) WORSENS rosacea — skin barrier disruption and irritation aggravate neurogenic flushing

TOPICAL STEROID-INDUCED ROSACEA

Most common cause of secondary (iatrogenic) rosacea in India — long-term application of topical steroids on facial skin (from fairness creams, Betnovate GM misuse, etc.) causes Steroid-Induced Rosacea-Rosaceaiform Dermatitis; difficult to treat; requires gradual steroid withdrawal with bridging anti-rosacea therapy

DIET AND CLIMATE BARRIERS IN INDIA

Spice avoidance is an enormous cultural and practical challenge in Indian households; sun avoidance in tropical India; practical trigger management counselling is essential

ISOTRETINOIN FOR PHYMATOUS ROSACEA

Low-dose Isotretinoin 10–20 mg/day for rhinophyma — reduces glandular hyperplasia; increasingly used in India; surgical dermabrasion/CO2 laser for established rhinophyma
diseases.important_notes:
STEROID-INDUCED ROSACEA IS EPIDEMIC IN INDIA — AND REQUIRES A SPECIFIC WITHDRAWAL PROTOCOL: The most common cause of de novo rosacea-like dermatitis in India is prolonged application of topical steroids to the face — from OTC fairness creams (containing Clobetasol, Betamethasone) or deliberate misuse of Betnovate GM, Quadriderm, and similar combination creams. Features: perioral and perinasal acneiform papules, telangiectasias, severe rebound flare on stopping steroid, skin atrophy and shine, steroid dependence. Management: (1) Gradual steroid withdrawal — do NOT abruptly stop; taper from potent to mild steroid, then discontinue; (2) Bridge with Tacrolimus 0.1% or Pimecrolimus 1% during withdrawal; (3) Add Metronidazole gel or Ivermectin cream; (4) Expect 4–6 weeks of worsening rebound erythema — counsel patients specifically; (5) Doxycycline 40–100 mg orally for 4–6 weeks.

ROSACEA MUST BE DISTINGUISHED FROM ACNE — WRONG TREATMENT WORSENS ROSACEA: Both present with facial papules and pustules in adults, leading to frequent misdiagnosis in India. Critical differentiators: (1) Rosacea has NO comedones (no blackheads/whiteheads); acne does; (2) Rosacea is always central face, spares periorbital; (3) Rosacea patients exhibit flushing and burning after triggers (alcohol, spicy food, heat); (4) Age of onset: rosacea typically >25 years; acne peak 15–25. Consequence of misdiagnosis: Applying benzoyl peroxide, salicylic acid, retinoids, or isotretinoin to rosacea skin worsens the barrier dysfunction, stinging, and erythema. Mild-moderate rosacea responds to Metronidazole gel or Ivermectin cream; NOT to BPO.

SUNSCREEN IS THE SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT DAILY LIFESTYLE MEASURE FOR ROSACEA: UV radiation is the most consistent and universal rosacea trigger. SPF50+ sunscreen with mineral filters (Zinc oxide, Titanium dioxide) is preferred over chemical filters in rosacea because mineral sunscreens have no photochemical reaction and are non-irritating on sensitive rosacea skin. Apply every morning as foundation of treatment. Matte-finish tinted mineral sunscreen simultaneously reduces the visual appearance of erythema. India rosacea patients face the challenge of year-round intense UV — strict sun avoidance including UPF protective clothing and seeking shade is part of the management plan, not optional.
confirmation_tests_curated: Rosacea diagnosed clinically: persistent central facial erythema/flushing, telangiectasias, inflammatory papules/pustules WITHOUT comedones, phymatous changes, ocular involvement

Telangiectasias (linear, branching vessels on nose, cheeks), polygonal vascular network, follicular plugging; confirms vascular nature; distinguishes from acne (BPO, salicylic acid would be wrong treatment)

diseases.name: Seborrhoeic Dermatitis
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

SD diagnosed clinically; characteristic site distribution, greasy scale, sebaceous area involvement

DERMOSCOPY (TRICHOSCOPY)

Yellowish or white structureless scaling areas; superficial blood vessels on scalp (contrasting with dry scale of psoriasis); 'branch-like dandruff vessels'

KOH PREPARATION

Skin scraping in 10–20% KOH — Malassezia globosa and restricta visible as short curved hyphae with round yeast forms ('meatballs and spaghetti' like tinea versicolor pattern); confirms fungal component

SKIN BIOPSY (if diagnostic uncertainty)

Spongiosis (epidermal oedema), perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate, serum in follicular infundibula with Malassezia; differentiates from psoriasis (parakeratosis, Munro's microabscesses)

DIFFERENTIAL INVESTIGATIONS

Skin swab — bacterial superinfection in infected SD

HIV test — if severe, extensive, or recalcitrant SD, especially in high-risk patients

FBG/HbA1c — diabetes association

Serum zinc — deficiency SD association

ANA/anti-dsDNA — SLE can mimic facial SD (but SLE spares nasolabial folds)
diseases.generic_medicines:
SCALP SD

Ketoconazole 2% shampoo — first-line anti-Malassezia; leave on 3–5 minutes; 3x/week induction for 4 weeks; then 1–2x/week maintenance

Zinc pyrithione 1% shampoo — anti-Malassezia + anti-inflammatory; OTC; daily-use safe

Selenium sulfide 1–2.5% shampoo — anti-Malassezia; 2x/week; may discolour bleached or light hair

Ciclopirox 1% shampoo — antifungal with anti-inflammatory properties; 2–3x/week

Coal tar 2–5% shampoo — antiproliferative + antifungal; very effective; cosmetically less acceptable (smell); carcinogen warning with long-term overuse

Salicylic acid 2–3% shampoo — keratolytic; removes adherent scale; combine with antifungal shampoo

Topical corticosteroids for scalp — Betamethasone valerate 0.05–0.1% scalp lotion; Clobetasol propionate 0.05% scalp solution; short-course for acute flares

FACIAL SD

Ketoconazole 2% cream — apply to nasolabial folds, eyebrows, hairline BD for 2–4 weeks

Ciclopirox 0.77% gel/cream — very effective facial SD; anti-inflammatory + antifungal

Tacrolimus 0.03–0.1% — calcineurin inhibitor; anti-inflammatory without steroid atrophy risk; face-safe long-term; excellent facial SD maintenance

Low-potency topical steroids (Hydrocortisone 1%) — short-burst for acute flares on face; NOT potent steroids on face

SYSTEMIC FOR SEVERE/RECALCITRANT

Oral Fluconazole 150 mg once weekly × 4 weeks; then monthly maintenance — for severe scalp or facial SD

Oral Itraconazole 200 mg/day × 1 week each month (pulse) — severe resistant SD

Isotretinoin low-dose (10 mg/day) — persistent seborrhoeic component associated with severe oily skin; reduces sebum production

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
TOPICAL ANTI-MALASSEZIA

Neem Oil (Azadirachta indica) — most clinically relevant Ayurvedic antifungal; azadirachtin, nimbidin — documented anti-Malassezia activity; neem oil scalp, diluted 1:5 in coconut oil; apply before wash 30 minutes; replaces commercial mineral oil for Indian oiling ritual

Tea Tree Oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) — terpinen-4-ol; potent anti-Malassezia (MIC studies); 5% tea tree oil shampoo comparable to 2% Ketoconazole in RCT; dilute 1–2% in carrier oil for scalp

Bhringaraja (Eclipta alba) — antifungal + anti-inflammatory scalp; Bhringaraja + Neem oil blend for dandruff; widely used Indian home remedy and Ayurvedic recommendation

Nimba Taila (Neem oil) scalp application — pure or blended; leave 30 min, wash with anti-Malassezia shampoo

INTERNAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY

Neem (oral) — Nimbarishta or Neem leaf extract tablets 500 mg BD; systemic antifungal + blood purifier

Haridra (Turmeric) — anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta-driven sebaceous inflammation; 500 mg curcumin BD

Gandhak Rasayana — purified sulphur; antimicrobial; anti-inflammatory; classical for Kushtha (skin inflammation) including dandruff; 500 mg BD with ghee

Triphala — liver purifier; reduces Kapha-Pitta skin inflammation; 5 g at bedtime

AYURVEDIC SCALP PREPARATION

Jatyadi Taila — multi-herb anti-inflammatory oil; infected/weeping SD scalp lesions

Kaishore Guggulu — anti-inflammatory; reduces Tridosha imbalance underlying SD

DIET

Reduce Kapha-aggravating diet (excess dairy, fried food, sugar) — Ayurvedic seborrhoeic excess correlates with Kapha-Pitta vitiation

IMPORTANT NOTE

Neem and Tea tree oil have the strongest botanical evidence against Malassezia among Ayurvedic and natural preparations. Key practice point: patients who use heavy coconut/mineral oil should transition to diluted Neem oil for scalp application rather than stopping oiling entirely (which they find socially and cosmetically difficult in India). SD is a chronic relapsing condition — maintenance anti-Malassezia shampoo 1–2x/week is lifelong management, not treatment course.
diseases.india_specific:
HOT HUMID CLIMATE — YEAR-ROUND MALASSEZIA SUPPORT

Malassezia (Pityrosporum ovale/globosa) thrives in warm, humid, lipid-rich environments; India's tropical climate provides year-round ideal conditions; SD prevalence higher and more severe year-round in India vs temperate climates

HAIR OIL MISUSE

Heavy daily oiling of scalp (coconut oil, parachute oil, mineral oil) — deeply entrenched in Indian hair care practice; however, excess scalp oil creates lipid substrate for Malassezia; exacerbates dandruff and SD; patients with SD should limit oiling to 1–2x/week with anti-Malassezia oils (Neem oil, Tea tree oil) rather than plain mineral/coconut

HIV SCREENING

Severe SD is a marker of HIV in India; SD is present in 40–80% of HIV-positive individuals; sudden worsening of previously mild dandruff that becomes extensive, facial, or recalcitrant to standard treatment should prompt confidential HIV screening

DIABETES ASSOCIATION

Poorly controlled diabetes — fungal skin infections including Malassezia overgrowth; SD worsening in diabetics; blood sugar control improves SD outcomes

ANTI-DANDRUFF SHAMPOO RESISTANCE

Commonplace in India — patients use prescription-strength anti-dandruff shampoos for brief periods then stop; incomplete treatment leads to rapid relapse; SD is not curable — maintenance therapy is required albeit at reduced frequency
diseases.important_notes:
SD IS CHRONIC AND REQUIRES MAINTENANCE THERAPY — NOT COURSES: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is not cured by any available treatment. The Malassezia yeast is part of normal human skin flora and cannot be permanently eliminated. Treatment controls the abnormal inflammatory response and reduces yeast density. The correct management framework: (1) Induction — Ketoconazole 2% shampoo 3x/week for 4 weeks; facial Ciclopirox or Ketoconazole cream BD for 2 weeks; (2) Maintenance — Ketoconazole shampoo 1–2x/week INDEFINITELY; facial antifungal/calcineurin inhibitor intermittently. Patients who complete a 'course' and stop commonly relapse within 4–8 weeks. Communication: 'This is like asthma — we manage it, we do not cure it. You will need a maintenance shampoo permanently.'

FACIAL SD MUST NOT BE TREATED WITH POTENT TOPICAL STEROIDS: A common India error is prescribing Betamethasone, Clobetasol, or combination triple creams to the face for SD sebaceous erythema. Facial skin is thin and exposed; long-term potent TCS use on the face causes skin atrophy, telangiectasias, steroid rosacea, perioral dermatitis, and rebound flare on stopping. The correct agents for facial SD: Ketoconazole 2% cream, Ciclopirox 0.77% gel, or Tacrolimus 0.1% for maintenance. If a short steroid burst is absolutely needed during acute flare (maximum 7–10 days), use only Hydrocortisone 1% (mildest) on the face and taper quickly to calcineurin inhibitor.

SEVERE RECALCITRANT SD IS A RED FLAG FOR HIV AND DIABETES IN INDIA: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is documented in 40–80% of HIV-positive patients. Features that should prompt HIV screening: SD onset in a young adult without prior skin history, unusually severe or extensive SD (involving trunk, genitalia), SD unresponsive to standard Ketoconazole therapy, SD associated with oral candidiasis or other opportunistic infections. Similarly, poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c >8%) exacerbates SD through immune changes and skin glucose substrate for Malassezia. Standard checklist for new severe SD: rule out HIV (confidential rapid test), check FBG/HbA1c, and screen for Parkinson's in older patients.
confirmation_tests_curated: SD diagnosed clinically; characteristic site distribution, greasy scale, sebaceous area involvement
Yellowish or white structureless scaling areas; superficial blood vessels on scalp (contrasting with dry scale of psoriasis); 'branch-like dandruff vessels'

diseases.name: Post-Inflam Hyperpigmentation
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

PIH diagnosed clinically — match between lesion location/shape and history of preceding inflammation

WOOD'S LAMP

Epidermal PIH — enhanced (brighter) under 365 nm Wood's lamp = melanin in epidermis; more responsive to depigmenting treatment

Dermal PIH — no enhancement or bluish hue = melanin in dermis (melanophages); treatment-resistant; very slow natural resolution

DERMOSCOPY

Brown regular structureless areas (epidermal); grey pseudonetwork or dots (dermal); helps characterise treatment prognosis

REFLECTANCE CONFOCAL MICROSCOPY

Precise localisation of melanin to epidermis vs dermis; research tool for treatment planning

SKIN BIOPSY (rarely needed for diagnosis)

Melanin in basal keratinocytes (epidermal) vs melanophages in superficial dermis (dermal); confirms PIH type; rules out lichen planus pigmentosus

CAUSE IDENTIFICATION

History-taking: preceding inflammation, insect bites, procedures, medication

Identify and treat underlying acne if present

Phytophotodermatitis? — lime/lemon, celery, fig leaf history
diseases.generic_medicines:
PHOTOPROTECTION — FOUNDATION OF PIH TREATMENT (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

SPF50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen with PA+++ — daily application; tinted sunscreen with iron oxide blocks visible light (HEV); reapplication every 2 hours outdoors; without photoprotection, no topical depigmenting agent will produce lasting results

TOPICAL DEPIGMENTING FIRST-LINE

Topical Retinoids — Tretinoin 0.025–0.05% or Adapalene 0.1–0.3% — accelerates epidermal turnover; disperses melanin granules; key component of PIH treatment; nighttime application; mild purging initially

Hydroquinone 2–4% — gold standard depigmenting; inhibits tyrosinase; use maximum 6 months continuous; avoid >2 years (ochronosis risk in dark skin)

Azelaic Acid 15–20% — anti-tyrosinase + anti-inflammatory; safe long-term; dual action ideal for post-acne PIH (treats PIH + residual acne inflammation)

Kojic Acid 1–4% — tyrosinase inhibitor; alternative to or combined with HQ; contact sensitisation risk

Arbutin 2–4% — stable HQ precursor; milder; safer for extended use

Niacinamide 4–10% — inhibits melanosome transfer from melanocyte to keratinocyte; anti-inflammatory; barrier-repairing; extremely well tolerated; cosmetically elegant; excellent India option

COMBINATIONS (most effective)

Modified Kligman's Triple combination: Tretinoin 0.025% + HQ 4% + mild TCS (Fluocinolone 0.01%) — highly effective for PIH; use 8–12 weeks then switch to maintenance

Tranexamic acid topical 2–5% + Niacinamide 10% + SPF50 — excellent combination for Indian PIH; well tolerated

ORAL ADJUNCTS

Tranexamic acid oral 250 mg BD — particularly effective for PIH from acne and hormone-driven causes

Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) oral 500 mg BD — antioxidant; reduces post-UV melanogenesis; adjunct

PROCEDURES

Chemical peels: Glycolic acid 30–70%, Salicylic acid 20–30%, Lactic acid 30–40% — monthly sessions; exfoliate hyperpigmented epidermis

Q-switched Nd:YAG 1064 nm laser toning — dermal PIH and refractory epidermal PIH

Microneedling — mechanical delivery of depigmenting agents into epidermis

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
TOPICAL VARNYA (COMPLEXION-BRIGHTENING)

Licorice root extract (Yashtimadhu / Glycyrrhiza glabra) — Glabridin inhibits tyrosinase; multiple in vitro and clinical studies for PIH; 1–2% glabridin topical formulation; very suitable Indian botanical for PIH

Kumkumadi Tailam — saffron, Manjistha, Sandalwood, Licorice, Vetiver — multiple tyrosinase-inhibiting compounds; classical face oil for complexion; especially suited for post-acne PIH; nighttime application

Masoor Dal (Red Lentil) ubtan — traditional Indian scrub; gentle exfoliant; removes superficial hyperpigmented layer; popular home remedy with genuine mild efficacy for epidermal PIH

Chandan (Sandalwood) + Rose water paste — anti-melanogenic; cooling; anti-inflammatory; suitable for reactive PIH

Niacinamide (Vitamin B3) — mechanistically blocks melanosome transfer; best evidence-base among skincare ingredients for PIH; 10% niacinamide serum; not traditional Ayurvedic but naturopathic; widely available India

SYSTEMIC

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Raktashodhaka; reduces hyper-reactive melanocyte response; 250–500 mg extract BD or Sarivadyasava; most consistently recommended systemic Ayurvedic herb for hyperpigmentation and PIH

Amalaki (Amla) — Vitamin C; antioxidant; anti-melanogenic; 500 mg–1 g/day significantly reduces post-UV pigmentation in skin cells; oral Amla + copper water synergy in Ayurvedic practice

Haridra (Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces UV-induced melanogenesis; 500 mg BD with piperine; reduces PIH formation from inflammatory lesions

Lodhra bark powder — astringent; systemic Pitta reducer; used in combination with Manjistha

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Sarivadyasava — classical blood purifier; especially for chronic PIH in acne-prone patients

Kumkumadi Lepa — saffron-based topical paste for PIH treatment

IMPORTANT NOTE

The most important PIH intervention is PREVENTION — treating acne early with Azelaic acid or Adapalene (which simultaneously treat acne AND prevent PIH), and applying SPF50+ daily. Licorice (Glabridin) has the strongest pharmacological evidence among botanical tyrosinase inhibitors. Niacinamide 10% serum is the most accessible and evidence-based OTC ingredient specifically for PIH in Indian skin.
diseases.india_specific:
HIGH UV INDEX YEAR-ROUND

India's tropical location means high UV year-round; UV radiation is (1) the primary trigger that darkens PIH by stimulating melanocyte re-activation in affected areas, and (2) inhibits PIH resolution; patients not using SPF50+ sunscreen after acne treatment will develop PIH on virtually every healed lesion; PIH prevention = UV prevention

FAIRNESS CREAM CULTURE — TOXIC REMEDIES FOR PIH

India's billion-dollar fairness cream industry targets PIH as a primary commercial concern; documented toxic ingredient content in unregulated creams: Mercury (neurotoxic, nephrotoxic — causes systemic toxicity from skin absorption), Lead, Hydroquinone in unsafe concentrations, Topical steroids; patients apply these for months-years; Indian Consumer Court and CDSCO have recorded numerous cases of mercury toxicity from fairness creams

ACNE + PIH DOUBLE BURDEN

In Indian skin (Fitzpatrick III-V), virtually every resolved acne lesion leaves PIH — unlike fair skin where lesions fade without discolouration; treating acne without simultaneously preventing and treating PIH leads to poor patient satisfaction and premature treatment discontinuation

LIME/LEMON PHYTOPHOTODERMATITIS

Very common India-specific cause — lime juice applied directly to skin as home remedy (widely recommended in beauty blogs and traditional practice) + sunlight exposure = phytophotodermatitis; psoralen compounds in lime juice are UV-activated; extensive streaky PIH results; needs months of depigmenting treatment + strict photoprotection
diseases.important_notes:
PIH IN DARK SKIN IS NOT COSMETIC — IT IS A MAJOR DERMATOLOGICAL BURDEN: Dermatology training in India often under-emphasises PIH as a clinical priority because it is not 'active disease.' However, for patients with Fitzpatrick III-V skin (the majority of India's population), PIH from acne, insect bites, and minor trauma represents the PRIMARY cosmetic and psychological dermatology burden — often more distressing than acne itself. Patients spend enormous money, time, and emotional energy on PIH. Integrating active PIH prevention and treatment into every acne management plan (Adapalene or Azelaic acid addresses both), and ensuring SPF50+ is prescribed as part of the standard treatment protocol, significantly improves patient satisfaction and adherence.

BEWARE OF TOXIC FAIRNESS CREAMS FOR PIH IN INDIA — MERCURY, LEAD, UNREGISTERED HQ: FSSAI and CDSCO investigations (2020–2023) confirmed widespread presence of Mercury compounds, Lead acetate, unregistered Hydroquinone concentrations, and Topical steroids in OTC Indian fairness creams targeting PIH. Mercury is particularly dangerous — absorbed through the skin; accumulates in kidneys, brain, and peripheral nerves; World Health Organization has identified skin-lightening mercury cream use as a public health concern in South Asia specifically. Clinical indicators of mercury cream toxicity: neurological symptoms (tremor, memory changes, irritability), nephrotic syndrome, dark gum discolouration, wrist/foot drop. Query any patient using OTC 'fairness cream' about brand names and duration — refer toxic ingredient cases to internal medicine.

LIME/LEMON HOME REMEDIES CAUSE PHYTOPHOTODERMATITIS — PIH FROM TRADITIONAL BEAUTY PRACTICES: Applying lime juice directly to the face as a 'natural lightener' — widely promoted on social media and traditional beauty channels in India — causes psoralea compound-mediated phytophotodermatitis when exposed to sunlight. The resulting PIH is often streaky, covering large areas, and lasting 6–18 months despite treatment. Counsel patients to never apply citrus juice directly to skin before sun exposure. If phytophotodermatitis PIH is present, intensive treatment with Tretinoin 0.025% + HQ 4% at night + SPF50+ PA+++ morning for 12–16 weeks and a chemical peel series may be required.
confirmation_tests_curated: PIH diagnosed clinically — match between lesion location/shape and history of preceding inflammation
Epidermal PIH — enhanced (brighter) under 365 nm Wood's lamp = melanin in epidermis; more responsive to depigmenting treatment
Dermal PIH — no enhancement or bluish hue = melanin in dermis (melanophages); treatment-resistant; very slow natural resolution

diseases.name: Chronic Urticaria
diseases.tests:
DIAGNOSIS IS CLINICAL — INVESTIGATIONS ARE TARGETED

Chronic urticaria diagnosis: wheals lasting <24 hours (each), pruritic, >6 weeks duration; history-taking for physical triggers (cold, exercise, pressure, dermographism)

UAS7 — Urticaria Activity Score 7 daily diary assessment — severity documentation

INITIAL BASIC SCREEN (Low yield; done for baseline & to identify reversible causes)

CBC + ESR — eosinophilia (parasitic trigger?); elevated CRP (infection, abscess, occult malignancy — rarely)

Thyroid function + anti-TPO antibodies — Hashimoto's association; 20% CU; subclinical hypothyroidism coexisting

Total IgE — elevated in atopic; does not change CSU treatment unless specific allergy suspected

H. pylori stool antigen test / urea breath test — eradication may resolve CU in positive patients

Stool microscopy — Blastocystis, Ascaris, hookworm — parasitic triggers

ANA, complement C3/C4 — if SLE suspected

PROVOCATION TESTS FOR INDUCIBLE URTICARIA

Ice cube test (5 min) — cold urticaria: wheal at cold contact site after warming

FricTest or dermographometer — pressure/dermographism

Exercise + hot shower — cholinergic urticaria

Light testing — solar urticaria (minimal erythema dose with UVA/UVB)

AUTOIMMUNE CSU TESTS

Basophil activation test (BAT) — detects functional anti-IgER auto-antibodies; specialist centres

Autologous serum skin test (ASST) — intradermal injection of patient's own serum; wheal formation indicates autoimmune CSU; positive in 35–40% CSU; technical variability limits use
diseases.generic_medicines:
STEP 1 — SECOND-GENERATION NON-SEDATING H1-ANTIHISTAMINES (FIRST-LINE ALWAYS)

Cetirizine 10 mg OD — widely available; mild sedation; excellent for urticaria; well tolerated

Loratadine 10 mg OD — least sedating; good for daytime/working patients

Fexofenadine 120–180 mg OD — least CNS penetration; virtually non-sedating; preferred for driving/operating machinery

Levocetirizine 5 mg OD (evening) — highly potent H1 blocker; more sedating than Fexo or Loratadine

Bilastine 20 mg OD — newer; non-sedating; P-glycoprotein substrate (not taken with grapefruit juice)

STEP 2 — UPDOSING (4× STANDARD DOSE — LICENSED EAACI RECOMMENDATION)

If standard dose failed: increase to 4× standard dose (e.g., Cetirizine 10 mg TDS, Loratadine 10 mg BD–TDS, Fexofenadine 180 mg TDS) — proven in multiple RCTs to increase response rate in CSU without proportional increase in side effects; do this before escalating to biologics

STEP 3 — ADD-ON THERAPY IF HIGH-DOSE ANTIHISTAMINE INADEQUATE

Montelukast 10 mg OD — leukotriene receptor antagonist; add to H1-antihistamine; particularly useful in NSAID-sensitive CU

H2 blocker (Ranitidine 150 mg BD or Famotidine 20 mg BD) — add to H1; complementary antihistamine effect; modest benefit

Cyclosporin 2.5–5 mg/kg/day — immunosuppressive; severe refractory CSU; effective but toxicity monitoring (BP, renal function, CBC); short-medium term bridge

STEP 4 — OMALIZUMAB (THIRD-LINE — SEVERE REFRACTORY CSU)

Omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks — anti-IgE monoclonal antibody; FDA and European MA approved specifically for CU not controlled by antihistamines; 65–85% complete control; given 6–12 cycles then reassess; available India at specialist centres; expensive

ANGIOEDEMA/ACUTE URTICARIA RESCUE

Oral Prednisolone 20–40 mg/day for 3–5 days — acute exacerbation; NOT for daily long-term use

IM/IV Adrenaline 0.5 mg 1:1000 — laryngeal angioedema/anaphylaxis emergency

ANTI-HISTAMINIC AND ANTI-PRURITIC

Haridra (Turmeric / Curcumin) — anti-histaminergic; inhibits mast cell degranulation; reduces histamine release; 500 mg curcumin BD is most evidence-based; Haridrakhand — classical Ayurvedic compound for Sheetapitta (Urticaria = Sheetapitta in Ayurveda; cold-triggered, pitta-vitiated blood condition)

Kaishore Guggulu — anti-inflammatory; anti-allergic; Guggul-based; reduces Tridosha in chronic recurrent urticaria

Arogyavardhini Vati — liver and blood purifier; systemic anti-inflammatory; H. pylori / parasitic-trigger CU benefit through GI normalisation

Mustakarishta / Khadirarishta — Ayurvedic tonic formulations for chronic skin allergic conditions

LIFESTYLE

Sheetapitta diet — avoid cold food/drink, cold water exposure, sour (amla) and acidic foods, fish combinations, incompatible foods (Viruddha Ahara) — all increase Pitta-Vata vitiation; warm, cooked food

Stress management — Yoga, Pranayama (Nadi shodhana, Sheetali Pranayama); Sheetapitta has major Manasa (psychological) Nidana (cause)

IMPORTANT NOTE

Haridra (Turmeric) and Haridrakhand have the strongest direct Ayurvedic reference and some pharmacological evidence for urticaria (anti-histaminic, mast cell stabilising). H. pylori eradication (prescription antibiotics) and addressing gut parasites (antiparasitic drugs) remain critical disease-modifying interventions for CU in India. Chronic urticaria responding incompletely to antihistamines needs Omalizumab evaluation — natural medicines cannot replace biologics for severe refractory CSU.
diseases.india_specific:
HELICOBACTER PYLORI AND BLASTOCYSTIS

H. pylori eradication shown to improve or resolve CU in several Indian studies and global meta-analyses; H. pylori prevalence India 50–70% (among highest globally) — H. pylori testing (UBT or stool antigen) and treatment in CU patients with GI symptoms or as empiric screening may significantly improve CU control

Blastocystis hominis — common gut parasite in India; tropical prevalence; documented association with CSU; stool microscopy/PCR warranted in Indian CU patients with any bowel symptoms

NSAID OVERUSE

NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Diclofenac) — widely self-prescribed and OTC-accessible in India; NSAIDs worsen CSU in 30% of patients via COX-1 inhibition and leukotriene pathway augmentation; important counselling point for Indian CU patients: avoid all NSAIDs, use Paracetamol for pain/fever

FOOD ADDITIVES

Process food colours, preservatives, benzoates — common in Indian packaged snacks and drinks; pseudoallergic mechanism aggravates CU; food diary assessment useful

OMALIZUMAB ACCESS

Omalizumab (anti-IgE monoclonal antibody) — the gold standard third-line treatment for antihistamine-refractory CSU; available in India but expensive; used at AIIMS and major dermatology/allergy centres; significantly changes outcome for severe CU
diseases.important_notes:
UPDOSING ANTIHISTAMINES TO 4× IS THE MOST UNDERUSED GUIDELINE IN INDIA: EAACI (European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology), BSACI, and WAO guidelines all recommend increasing second-generation H1-antihistamines to 4× standard dose before escalating to biologics or immunosuppressants — this is an approved, licensed recommendation. In Indian dermatology practice, it is common to escalate to steroids (prednisolone courses) after failure of standard-dose antihistamine, rather than first trying 4× dose. Oral steroid courses for CU are NOT recommended as maintenance therapy — steroids suppress CU briefly but it rebounds; long-term oral steroids cause Cushing's syndrome, diabetes, osteoporosis, and infections. Correct pathway: Standard dose antihistamine (Step 1) → 4× updose (Step 2) → Omalizumab (Step 3) if refractory. Reserve steroids for acute angioedema rescue (3–5 day short course only).

H. PYLORI AND BLASTOCYSTIS TESTING IS CLINICALLY IMPORTANT IN INDIA BUT NEGLECTED: Multiple meta-analyses and Indian studies demonstrate that H. pylori eradication leads to complete resolution of CU in 25–60% of H. pylori-positive CU patients. Blastocystis hominis, a gastrointestinal protozoan highly prevalent in tropical India, has been specifically implicated in CU in multiple South Asian studies. Yet in routine Indian dermatology practice, CU patients are managed predominantly with antihistamines without any gut investigation. Recommended: all CU patients should receive H. pylori stool antigen test; those with any GI symptoms (bloating, altered bowel, dyspepsia) should have stool microscopy for parasites. Treating these infections is disease-modifying — not symptomatic.

OMALIZUMAB HAS TRANSFORMED REFRACTORY CHRONIC URTICARIA — AVAILABLE IN INDIA: Omalizumab (Xolair) — anti-IgE monoclonal antibody — represents the most significant therapeutic advance in urticaria pharmacology in decades. 300 mg SC every 4 weeks produces complete urticaria control (UAS7=0) in 50–65% and significant control in 80%+ of antihistamine-refractory CSU patients. It is specifically approved for CSU and has a favourable safety profile (no immunosuppressive toxicities). In India, it is available through the manufacturer (Novartis), at AIIMS and major dermatology/allergy centres. The main barrier is cost — approximately Rs.25,000–35,000 per injection; 6 monthly injections as initial course. For patients with severe, debilitating, quality-of-life-destroying CU who have failed high-dose antihistamines, Omalizumab should be discussed as goal of treatment.
confirmation_tests_curated: DIAGNOSIS IS CLINICAL — INVESTIGATIONS ARE TARGETED
Chronic urticaria diagnosis: wheals lasting <24 hours (each), pruritic, >6 weeks duration; history-taking for physical triggers (cold, exercise, pressure, dermographism)
UAS7 — Urticaria Activity Score 7 daily diary assessment — severity documentation
INITIAL BASIC SCREEN (Low yield; done for baseline & to identify reversible causes)
CBC + ESR — eosinophilia (parasitic trigger?); elevated CRP (infection, abscess, occult malignancy — rarely)

diseases.name: Keloid And Hypertrophic Scars
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Diagnosis is clinical — visual appearance, extension beyond wound margin (keloid), site, patient history; no specific blood test

CLINICAL CRITERIA FOR KELOID vs HYPERTROPHIC SCAR

Keloid: extends BEYOND original wound; does NOT regress spontaneously; recurs after excision without adjuvant therapy; pruritic and painful

Hypertrophic: confined WITHIN wound margin; may spontaneously improve within 12–24 months; lower recurrence after excision

DERMOSCOPY

White/pink structureless areas; loss of normal skin markings; branching vessels; hyperpigmented margins in Indian skin; helps characterise activity

HISTOPATHOLOGY (rarely needed — only for diagnostic uncertainty or atypical features)

Keloid: thick hyalinised glassy collagen bundles (pathognomonic); fibroblast proliferation; myofibroblasts

HS: wavy, more ordered collagen fibres; less hyalinisation; distinguishes from keloid histologically

PATIENT AND FAMILY HISTORY

Always ask: family history of keloids? prior keloid/HS in patient? BCG scar behaviour? ear piercing history?

POSAS SCORE

Patient and Observer Scar Assessment Scale (POSAS) — patient and physician scoring of scar pigmentation, vascularisation, stiffness, thickness, relief, itch, pain; standardised outcome measurement for treatment trials
diseases.generic_medicines:
FIRST-LINE — INTRALESIONAL CORTICOSTEROID

Intralesional Triamcinolone Acetonide (ILK) 10–40 mg/mL — gold standard single-modality treatment; injected directly into keloid/HS; every 4–6 weeks; reduces collagen synthesis and fibroblast proliferation; maximum clinical effect at 40 mg/mL; effective but 50% recurrence; multiple sessions (5–10) needed

Side effects of ILK: skin atrophy, depigmentation, telangiectasia if injected superficially or in excess

COMBINATION TREATMENT — MORE EFFECTIVE THAN MONOTHERAPY

ILK + Silicone gel sheet (SGS) — most evidence-based non-surgical combination; silicone sheet worn 12+ hours/day; begins immediately after wound healing; compresses scar, reduces water loss, alters cytokine environment

Post-excision + Immediate radiotherapy — most effective for large keloids; surgery excises bulk then RT prevents recurrence; RT within 24–48 hours of surgery; RT dose 12–15 Gy in 3–5 fractions

Cryotherapy — liquid nitrogen cryospray directly on keloid; 1–3 freeze-thaw cycles monthly; reduces bulk; combine with ILK

Pressure therapy — customised compression garments for post-burn and extensive HS; 20–25 mmHg continuous pressure; 6–12+ months

TOPICAL AGENTS

Silicone gel (topical) — alternative to sheets for small/facial scars; apply BD; improves hydration, flattens early HS/keloid

Onion extract gel (Mederma) — anti-inflammatory; anti-fibrotic; modest evidence; well marketed in India

Imiquimod 5% cream — TLR stimulant; anti-fibrotic; applied BD after excision to prevent recurrence; some RCT evidence

SYSTEMIC

Low-dose Isotretinoin (10–20 mg/day) + ILK — synergistic for acne keloid; reduces sebum and new acne lesion formation that would otherwise trigger new keloids

EMERGING

5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) 50 mg/mL intralesional — anti-fibrotic; combines with ILK as 4:1 ratio; more effective than ILK alone in some studies; reduces itch better

Bleomycin intralesional — anti-neoplastic; anti-fibrotic; second-line intralesional in resistant keloids

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTI-FIBROTIC TOPICAL

Aloe vera gel — anti-fibrotic; anti-inflammatory; MMP (matrix metalloproteinase) enhancing; reduces collagen over-deposition; topical fresh aloe gel BD; widely used India; well tolerated

Haldi (Turmeric/Curcumin) paste — anti-inflammatory; inhibits NF-kB; reduces TGF-beta1 fibrosis signalling; turmeric paste applied topically; oral curcumin 500 mg BD reduces systemic inflammation that drives keloid recurrence

Onion extract (Pyaz) — flavonoids quercetin and kaempferol; anti-fibrotic; reduces keloid fibroblast activity in vitro; topical application of onion extract BD; Mederma is commercially processed form

Castor oil (Arandi) — ricinoleic acid; anti-inflammatory; penetrating; traditional Indian scar massage oil; promote collagen remodelling

Neem leaf paste — anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta-driven inflammatory scar formation; healing

SYSTEMIC ANTI-FIBROTIC

Kanchanar Guggulu — most relevant Ayurvedic formulation for fibrotic/hypertrophic conditions; Kanchanar bark with Guggul; anti-fibrotic; reduces excessive tissue growth (Granthi — tumor/growth in Ayurveda); 2 tablets BD with warm water; classical prescription for keloid and fibrotic lumps

Guggul (Commiphora mukul) — guggulipids; anti-inflammatory; anti-fibrotic; reduces TGF-beta activity

Varuna (Crataeva nurvala) — reduces Granthi; fibrotic tissue reduction

Gotu Kola (Brahmi / Manduka-parni / Centella asiatica) — Centella asiatica specific compounds (asiaticoside, madecassoside) are among the most extensively evidenced botanical anti-fibrotic molecules; inhibit fibroblast proliferation and collagen production in human keloid cells (multiple in vitro studies); oral or topical; standardised Centella extract 60–120 mg BD; widely used in scar management globally

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Arogyavardhini Vati — liver and blood purifier; systemic anti-inflammatory adjunct

IMPORTANT NOTE

Centella asiatica (Gotu Kola / Manduka-parni / Brahmi in Ayurvedic text) deserves special mention: its active triterpenes (asiaticoside, madecassoside) are clinically validated anti-fibrotic molecules that reduce keloid fibroblast activity. This is used in commercial advanced scar creams globally. Kanchanar Guggulu is the classical Ayurvedic prescription for any fibrotic growth (Granthi) and aligns pharmacologically with anti-fibrotic activity. For established large keloids, Intralesional Triamcinolone + Silicone sheet remains the standard of care; Ayurvedic therapies serve as adjuncts especially in the prevention/maintenance phase.
diseases.india_specific:
FITZPATRICK III-V SKIN — HIGHEST KELOID RISK

Dark-skinned populations including Indians have 3–15x higher keloid incidence compared to Fitzpatrick I-II skin; genetic basis; higher TGF-beta1 activity and reduced matrix metalloproteinase activity in darker skin fibroblasts; BRCA pathway variants associated

BCG VACCINATION KELOID

India's universal BCG vaccination at birth creates deltoid scar; in keloid-prone individuals (especially those with positive family history), BCG scar develops into a raised keloid by age 2–5 years; often first presentation of keloid tendency; predictive for future keloid risk; awareness allows prophylactic silicone gel sheet application early

EAR PIERCING KELOID

Earlobe piercing-related keloid in Indian women — one of the most common keloid presentations in Indian dermatology practice; multiple ear piercings (traditional multiple ear piercings common) multiply risk; management: pressure earring + Triamcinolone injection; medical advice to avoid re-piercing in known keloid-prone individuals

SKIN TEAR AVOIDANCE

Cosmetic procedures (chemical peel, laser, waxing/threading) on keloid-prone Indian skin must be performed cautiously — any skin trauma in susceptible individuals can trigger keloid formation
diseases.important_notes:
KELOID EXCISION WITHOUT ADJUVANT THERAPY HAS 50–100% RECURRENCE RATE: Simple surgical excision of a keloid without post-operative adjuvant treatment is not a treatment — it is the beginning of a new cycle. The very trauma of surgery stimulates further fibroproliferation, and recurrent keloids are frequently larger and more symptomatic than the original. The evidence-based approach: (1) Small keloids: ILK injections monthly over 6–12 months as primary; (2) Large bulk keloids: surgical excision with IMMEDIATE post-operative measures — intralesional steroids at closure, silicone gel daily, pressure therapy; (3) Best recurrence prevention: Surgical excision + Post-operative radiotherapy (12–15 Gy in 3–5 fractions starting within 24 hours), which reduces keloid recurrence from 70%+ to <20%. Radiotherapy for keloid is specifically low-dose and safe; the carcinogenesis risk is extremely low at these doses for a benign condition. Inform patients that even with best treatment, keloid tendency is permanent.

BCG KELOID IN INDIAN CHILDREN — ADVICE FOR KELOID-PRONE FAMILIES: BCG vaccination at birth and deltoid keloid developing within 2–5 years of life is India-specific. Families with strong keloid history (both parents, siblings with keloids) can be counselled to start silicone gel application to the BCG scar site from 6–8 weeks after the wound heals (once epithelialised), worn continuously. This early prophylactic silicone therapy significantly reduces keloid development risk. If a BCG keloid does form, early intervention (ILK at small size) is far more successful than treating a large established keloid. Avoid aggressive scar manipulation, pressure earrings in young keloid-prone children, and any elective skin-breaking procedures.

SILICONE GEL SHEETS ARE THE MOST EVIDENCE-BASED PREVENTIVE MEASURE FOR POSTOPERATIVE SCARS: Silicone gel sheets (SGS) are supported by Level-1 RCT evidence for reduction of hypertrophic scar development after surgery and burns. The mechanism: SGS creates hydration, occlusion, and static electric field that inhibits fibroblast proliferation and reduces pro-fibrotic cytokines. Key prescription: apply for minimum 12 hours/day; start only after wound is fully epithelialised (not on open wounds); wear for minimum 3 months and preferably up to 6 months for optimal effect; wash daily with mild soap. For Indian patients undergoing elective surgery (C-sections, abdominal surgery, breast surgery) with any personal or family keloid history, prescribing SGS prophylactically from 2–3 weeks postoperatively is the standard of care in specialist plastic surgery practice.
confirmation_tests_curated: Diagnosis is clinical — visual appearance, extension beyond wound margin (keloid), site, patient history; no specific blood test
CLINICAL CRITERIA FOR KELOID vs HYPERTROPHIC SCAR
Keloid: extends BEYOND original wound; does NOT regress spontaneously; recurs after excision without adjuvant therapy; pruritic and painful
Hypertrophic: confined WITHIN wound margin; may spontaneously improve within 12–24 months; lower recurrence after excision

diseases.name: Hirsutism
diseases.tests:
SERUM ANDROGENS

Total testosterone — elevated (>2 nmol/L or >0.6 ng/mL) indicates hyperandrogenism; normal in idiopathic hirsutism

Free testosterone — more sensitive; protein-bound portion doesn't explain symptoms

DHEAS (Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate) — adrenal androgen; elevated suggests adrenal source (NC-CAH, adrenal tumour)

SHBG (Sex hormone binding globulin) — decreased in insulin resistance → more free testosterone available

Free Androgen Index (FAI) = Total T × 100 / SHBG — higher sensitivity for functional hyperandrogenism

PCOS WORKUP

LH/FSH ratio — LH:FSH >2:1 in PCOS (though not diagnostic criterion anymore)

Pelvic ultrasound — polycystic ovarian morphology (PCOM): ≥20 follicles 2–9 mm per ovary on modern US

Fasting glucose + 2-hr OGTT + fasting insulin — insulin resistance

HOMA-IR (fasting glucose × fasting insulin / 22.5) — insulin resistance index

Lipid profile — dyslipidaemia in metabolic syndrome

NC-CAH SCREENING

17-Hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP) early morning fasting — elevated suggests 21-hydroxylase deficiency (NC-CAH)

CUSHING'S SCREENING

24-hour urinary free cortisol; overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test; late-night salivary cortisol

IMAGING

Pelvic ultrasound — ovarian morphology, adrenal masses

CT/MRI adrenals and ovaries — if androgen-secreting tumour suspected (testosterone >5 nmol/L or very rapid virilisation)
diseases.generic_medicines:
NON-PHARMACOLOGICAL (FIRST-LINE FOR COSMETIC CONCERN)

Physical hair removal: threading, waxing, shaving, bleaching — temporary; do NOT cause permanent increase in hair growth

Laser hair removal: Nd:YAG 1064 nm or Diode 810 nm — safe for dark Indian skin; 6–8 sessions monthly; permanent hair reduction; gold standard cosmetic treatment for Indian hirsutism

Eflornithine 13.9% cream — topically applied enzyme inhibitor (ODC ornithine decarboxylase); slows facial hair regrowth; not permanent; combined with laser; BD application

PHARMACOLOGICAL ANTI-ANDROGEN THERAPY

Combined oral contraceptive (COC) — first-line pharmacological; reduces LH → less ovarian androgen + increases SHBG → less free testosterone; use anti-androgenic progestogen: Cyproterone acetate (Diane-35/Dianette), Drospirenone (Yasmin/Yaz), Desogestrel

Spironolactone 50–200 mg/day — aldosterone antagonist/antiandrogen; directly blocks androgen receptor in hair follicle; very effective for hirsutism; requires contraception (anti-androgenic risk to male fetus); monitor potassium and BP; improves over 6–12 months

Flutamide 125–500 mg/day — pure androgen receptor antagonist; very effective; hepatotoxicity risk requires LFT monitoring; not widely used

Cyproterone acetate 10–25 mg/day (reversed sequential regimen) — powerful antiandrogen; with ethinyl oestradiol; available India

INSULIN SENSITISING (ESSENTIAL IN PCOS HIRSUTISM)

Metformin 500–1500 mg BD — improves insulin resistance → reduces ovarian androgen production; slow improvement in hirsutism; excellent for menstrual regulation and metabolic PCOS

Inositol (Myo-inositol 2 g + D-chiro-inositol 50 mg BD) — insulin sensitiser; emerging PCOS evidence; safe; improves hormone profile

ADRENAL NC-CAH

Prednisolone 2.5–5 mg at night — suppresses ACTH-driven adrenal androgen in NC-CAH

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTI-ANDROGENIC / HAIR-REDUCING

Spearmint Tea (Mentha spicata) — most clinically documented natural anti-androgen; two RCTs showed spearmint tea 240 mL BD for 30 days significantly reduced total testosterone and free testosterone in PCOS women; anti-androgenic (reduces LH-stimulated testosterone); grows naturally in India; available as herbal tea; excellent adjunct

Methi (Fenugreek / Trigonella foenum-graecum) — furostanolic saponins; anti-androgenic; improves insulin sensitivity; reduces testosterone in PCOS women (FenuSmart™ trials); 500 mg–1 g/day

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — phytoestrogen; female hormonal balancer; reduces androgen dominance; regulates menstrual cycle; 500 mg BD

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces cortisol-driven adrenal androgen excess; reduces testosterone in some trials; regulates PCOS hormonal axis; adrenal hirsutism adjunct

PCOS / INSULIN RESISTANCE

Cinnamon (Dalchini / Cinnamomum zeylanicum) — insulin sensitiser; lowers HOMA-IR and testosterone in PCOS trials; 1–3 g/day in warm water or as supplement

Vijaysar (Pterocarpus marsupium) — Ayurvedic hypoglycaemic; insulin sensitising; reduces androgen-driving hyperinsulinaemia; Vijaysar water from wooden tumbler — traditional India diabetic remedy

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immune modulator; anti-inflammatory; reduces adrenal stress-driven androgen

TOPICAL HAIR GROWTH REDUCTION

Papaya + Turmeric paste — folk remedy; papain enzyme may weaken hair follicle when applied regularly; turmeric anti-inflammatory; popular India home remedy for facial hair; limited scientific evidence but widely practised

Thread + Turmeric combo — threading removes hair mechanically; turmeric paste may reduce regrowth rate; traditional wedding pre-beauty ritual

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Shatavari Kalpa / Shatavari Ghrita — female tonic; anti-androgenic; hormonal balance

Varunadi Kwatha — PCOS-specific formulation; reduces ovarian cystic changes

Phala Ghrita — classical Ayurvedic gynaecological formulation; hormonal regulation

IMPORTANT NOTE

Spearmint tea (Mentha spicata) has the strongest clinical trial evidence among natural anti-androgens — two published RCTs confirm testosterone reduction. Metformin remains more effective than any natural product for insulin-resistance-driven PCOS hirsutism and should be first-line pharmacological in India. Laser hair removal (Nd:YAG 1064 nm) is the most effective cosmetic solution for permanent hair reduction in Indian skin — pharmacological anti-androgens slow new growth but do not remove existing terminal hairs.
diseases.india_specific:
PCOS EPIDEMIC IN INDIA

PCOS affects 10–22% of reproductive-age Indian women — among the highest global estimates; hirsutism is the most visible and distressing manifestation; PCOS in India has higher metabolic severity (greater insulin resistance, higher BMI, metabolic syndrome risk) compared to Western cohorts

RACIAL HAIR DENSITY VARIATION

Indian women having darker, thicker terminal hair and higher baseline follicular sensitivity to androgens; even mildly elevated or upper-normal serum androgens produce clinically significant hirsutism in Indian women; the Ferriman-Gallwey cutoff may need India-specific lower threshold

INSULIN RESISTANCE DRIVEN HYPERANDROGENISM

Insulin resistance — prevalent in Indian PCOS (higher rates than white European PCOS); insulin stimulates ovarian theca cell androgen production directly; addressing insulin resistance with Metformin or weight loss produces proportional improvement in androgen levels and hirsutism

HAIR REMOVAL INDUSTRY

Laser hair removal industry in India — growing rapidly; suitable for Fitzpatrick III-V with modern Nd:YAG (1064 nm) and Diode (810 nm) lasers which are safe on dark Indian skin; Ruby (694 nm) and intense pulsed light (IPL) associated with post-laser hyperpigmentation in dark skin — avoid

ADRENAL TUMOUR SUSPICION

Rapid onset virilisation (deep voice, clitoromegaly, male-pattern alopecia, sudden hirsutism worsening) in Indian women should immediately prompt tumour investigation; ovarian and adrenal androgen-secreting tumours rare but present in India
diseases.important_notes:
HIRSUTISM IS A SYMPTOM REQUIRING CAUSE IDENTIFICATION — DO NOT JUMP TO HAIR REMOVAL ONLY: A woman presenting with hirsutism in India often wants 'treatment' meaning laser or anti-androgen cream. While addressing the cosmetic concern is valid, hirsutism is a clinical sign of androgen excess requiring systematic cause identification. The critical steps: (1) Complete reproductive history — menstrual regularity, family history; (2) Serum androgens — Total T, DHEAS, SHBG; (3) Pelvic ultrasound; (4) If rapid onset virilisation — immediate adrenal/ovarian tumour exclusion. PCOS must be identified and treated systemically (insulin resistance, metabolic risk, fertility planning, endometrial protection) — treating only the hirsutism cosmetically ignores a significant metabolic and reproductive condition that increases long-term cardiovascular, endometrial cancer, and type 2 diabetes risk.

LASER HAIR REMOVAL IN INDIAN SKIN — DEVICE SELECTION IS CRITICAL FOR SAFETY: India has the world's largest population with Fitzpatrick III-V skin type. Laser hair removal in this skin type requires careful device selection to avoid post-laser hyperpigmentation and burns. Safe devices for Indian skin: Nd:YAG 1064 nm laser (deepest penetration; melanin less absorbed; safest for dark skin; gold standard), Diode 810 nm with cooling (acceptable with proper settings). UNSAFE for dark Indian skin: Ruby 694 nm (high melanin absorption → burns), Alexandrite 755 nm alone (risk), IPL (Intense Pulsed Light) without careful settings (hyperpigmentation risk very high in Fitzpatrick IV-V Indian skin). Require 6–8 monthly sessions; hormonal causes must be controlled otherwise new hair continues growing. Laser destroys existing hair follicles — pharmacological anti-androgens prevent new ones from forming.

SPIRONOLACTONE IS HIGHLY EFFECTIVE FOR HIRSUTISM BUT REQUIRES CONTRACEPTION: Spironolactone 100–200 mg/day significantly reduces hirsutism score (mFG) in 70–75% of women over 6–12 months. However, its anti-androgenic mechanism means it can interfere with male fetus genital development — ambiguous genitalia have been reported. Therefore: all women of childbearing age on Spironolactone MUST use reliable contraception. The combination of Spironolactone + OCP (drospirenone-containing) provides both anti-androgenic effect and contraceptive cover simultaneously, and is a practical 'one medicine for two goals' approach in Indian practice. Monitor: serum potassium (first 3 months) — Spironolactone is potassium-sparing; avoid in women with renal impairment or hyperkalemia.
confirmation_tests_curated: Total testosterone — elevated (>2 nmol/L or >0.6 ng/mL) indicates hyperandrogenism; normal in idiopathic hirsutism
Free testosterone — more sensitive; protein-bound portion doesn't explain symptoms
DHEAS (Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate) — adrenal androgen; elevated suggests adrenal source (NC-CAH, adrenal tumour)
SHBG (Sex hormone binding globulin) — decreased in insulin resistance → more free testosterone available

diseases.name: Hyperhidrosis
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Primary focal hyperhidrosis: bilateral, focal, symmetrical sweating; onset <25 years; at least 1 episode/week; no night sweating; family history; cessation during sleep; interference with daily activities

MINOR'S IODINE-STARCH TEST

Iodine solution painted on affected area; allowed to dry; starch powder applied; sweating areas turn dark blue/black (starch + iodine + sweat reaction); standard test for quantifying affected area for Botox injection planning

GRAVIMETRIC SWEAT MEASUREMENT

Filter paper weighed before and after 5-minute application to axilla/palm; >50 mg/5 min = significant hyperhidrosis; research and Botox planning

SKIN CONDUCTANCE TESTING

Sudometer; electrodermal activity; correlates with sweating rate

SECONDARY HYPERHIDROSIS INVESTIGATIONS (when generalised/nocturnal sweating)

FBC, ESR, CRP — infection, lymphoma

Chest X-ray + Sputum for AFB / CBNAAT — TB (India first)

IGRA (QuantiFERON Gold) / Mantoux — TB latent/active

HIV test — AIDS night sweats

TSH — thyrotoxicosis

Serum glucose, HbA1c — diabetes autonomic neuropathy

24-hour urinary catecholamines / metanephrines — phaeochromocytoma

FSH, LH, oestradiol — perimenopause in women
diseases.generic_medicines:
TOPICAL ANTIPERSPIRANT (FIRST-LINE ALL TYPES)

Aluminium chloride hexahydrate 20–25% solution (Driclor) — first-line topical; apply before sleep on dry skin; occlusion optional; mechanism: aluminium salts plug eccrine duct; weekly maintenance after control; axillary and palmar; may irritate if applied to moist or recently shaved skin

Aluminium chloride 15% lotion — milder; useful when 20% causes irritation

IONTOPHORESIS (PALMAR/PLANTAR)

Direct galvanic current (15–20 mA) applied to hands/feet in water bath; mechanism: current + ions block eccrine sweat delivery; 3x/week for 3–4 weeks induction then maintenance; dedicated home devices available (Idromed, Hidrex); very effective for palmar/plantar (70–80%); no systemic side effects

BOTULINUM TOXIN (AXILLARY/PALMAR GOLD STANDARD)

Botulinum toxin A (Botox 50–100 units / axilla; Dysport equivalent) — subcutaneous injection at 1–2 cm intervals across affected area (Minor's starch test guides injection sites); axillary effect 4–6 months; palmar effect 3–4 months; palmar needs nerve block anaesthesia (painful); highly effective: 80–90% HDSS improvement

ORAL SYSTEMIC

Glycopyrrolate 1–2 mg BD — anticholinergic; reduces all eccrine sweating; helpful for craniofacial and generalised; SE: dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation, heat intolerance; use caution in hot Indian climate

Propranolol 10–40 mg BD — beta-blocker; reduces emotional/anxiety-triggered sweating; helps anxiety-associated hyperhidrosis; also reduces social performance anxiety component

Oxybutynin 2.5–5 mg BD — antimuscarinic; tolerable alternative to glycopyrrolate; similar side effect profile

SURGICAL

Endoscopic thoracic sympathectomy (ETS) — T2–T3 sympathetic chain ablation; permanent cure for palmar/axillary hyperhidrosis; RISK: compensatory hyperhidrosis (severe trunk/back sweating) occurs in 50–80%; Gustatory sweating; available at thoracic surgery centres India

Microwaving (MiraDry) — non-invasive microwave energy to axilla; destroys eccrine glands permanently; single/two sessions; available select India cities

Laser (diode 810 nm, 1064 nm) — subdermal eccrine ablation; axillary

Propranolol 20 mg (10 tabs): Rs.5

Clonidine 100 mcg (10 tabs): Rs.7
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
TOPICAL

Alum powder (Phitkari / aluminium potassium sulphate) — traditional Indian antiperspirant; applied to damp axilla/feet; aluminium salts reduce eccrine secretion; same mechanism as commercial aluminium chloride antiperspirants; widely available India; natural, economical; effective for mild-moderate axillary hyperhidrosis

Sandalwood powder (Chandan) — astringent; cooling; reduces sweat and odour; traditional Indian dusting powder for heat and sweat; chandan-based underarm powder

Neem powder — antibacterial; reduces osmidrosis (sweat odour); reduces bacterial decomposition of sweat

Rose water spray — cooling; astringent; temporary reduction of palmar and axillary sweat; traditional Indian cooling remedy

Multani Mitti (Fuller's earth) paste — absorbent; draws sweat; cooling; face and scalp pack for craniofacial hyperhidrosis

SYSTEMIC

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces autonomic stress response; reduces emotional-trigger sweating; 300–600 mg extract BD

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — anxiolytic; reduces anxiety-driven hyperhidrosis; GABA modulation

Shatavari — cooling; reduces Pitta excess; summer hyperhidrosis; women's hormonal sweating (perimenopausal)

Usheer (Vetiver / Vetiveria zizanioides) — intensely cooling; reduces Pitta; Usheer sharbat (Vetiver drink); Usheer-infused face mist

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Chandanadi Taila — cooling oil; scalp and body massage; reduces heat-driven sweating

Triphala — systemic heat reducer; anti-Pitta

IMPORTANT NOTE

Alum (Phitkari) is the most underutilised but effective first-line India-specific intervention for mild axillary hyperhidrosis — it is essentially commercial-grade aluminium compound antiperspirant in traditional form, widely available at Rs.20–30 and safe. Ashwagandha's cortisol-reducing property directly addresses stress/anxiety-triggered hyperhidrosis — a very appropriate adjunct. Medical-grade Botulinum toxin and Iontophoresis remain the evidence gold standards for significant palmar and axillary hyperhidrosis when topical measures fail.
diseases.india_specific:
TROPICAL CLIMATE

India's hot humid climate means primary hyperhidrosis is exacerbated physiologically; patients with primary hyperhidrosis in India may sweat proportionally more than equivalent patients in cooler climates; clothing culture (synthetics, saris — less ventilating) compounds axillary hyperhidrosis

SOCIAL AND PROFESSIONAL IMPACT IN INDIA

Palmar hyperhidrosis in software engineers (keyboard/mouse slipping), teachers/lecturers, surgeons, musicians — high-impact professions in India; affecting performance and career opportunity

Namaskar cultural greeting (folded hands) vs handshake — Indian patients with palmar hyperhidrosis prefer Namaskar but face pressure in formal/corporate settings to shake hands

TB NIGHT SWEATS — MUST EXCLUDE BEFORE TREATING AS HYPERHIDROSIS

India has the world's highest TB burden (2.5 million new cases annually); generalised night sweats in Indian context = TB until proven otherwise; chest X-ray, Mantoux/IGRA, sputum AFB/CBNAAT must be done for any generalised sweating, especially with constitutional symptoms

BOTULINUM TOXIN AVAILABILITY

Botulinum toxin injections (Botox/Dysport) — gold standard for axillary and palmar hyperhidrosis; available in India at dermatology centres; effect lasts 4–6 months (axillary) and 3–4 months (palmar); widely used at metro city centres; high cost but significant QOL improvement

ANTICHOLINERGIC SIDE EFFECTS IN HEAT

India's heat makes oral anticholinergic treatment (glycopyrrolate) more risky — anticholinergics reduce all sweating including physiological thermoregulatory sweating → overheating risk in summer; needs careful counselling
diseases.important_notes:
ALWAYS EXCLUDE SECONDARY HYPERHIDROSIS BEFORE TREATING AS PRIMARY — ESPECIALLY TB IN INDIA: Generalised night sweats in an Indian patient must be investigated as TB until proven otherwise. India bears 25% of the world's annual TB burden — 2.5 million new cases per year. TB classically causes drenching night sweats with fever, weight loss, and cough. Treating this as idiopathic hyperhidrosis with aluminium antiperspirant or Botox is a dangerous misdiagnosis. Standard panel for any new patient with significant sweating: (1) Clarify focal bilateral (primary) vs generalised/night sweats (secondary); (2) Constitutional symptoms screening (weight loss, fever, night sweats + cough = TB till ruled out); (3) Chest X-ray; (4) Mantoux/IGRA; (5) For women >45 years: rule out perimenopause (FSH, LH); (6) TSH screen; (7) If episodic hypertension + headache + palpitations: urine catecholamines (phaeochromocytoma). Phaeochromocytoma is rare but catastrophic if missed.

IONTOPHORESIS IS THE MOST COST-EFFECTIVE FIRST-LINE TREATMENT FOR PALMAR HYPERHIDROSIS: Palmar hyperhidrosis is the most functionally disabling form — affecting writing, device use, handshaking, professional performance, and human contact. Iontophoresis (direct galvanic current via water bath) is 70–80% effective for palmar hyperhidrosis, has no systemic side effects, and devices are available in India for home use (Idromed, models ~Rs.8,000–25,000). Botox in the palms requires digital nerve block anaesthesia (painful), lasts only 3–4 months, and is expensive for repeated sessions. The treatment hierarchy for palmar hyperhidrosis: (1) Aluminium chloride 20% solution topically (first-line, cheapest); (2) Iontophoresis 3x/week (evidence-based, durable); (3) Botulinum toxin palmar injections with anaesthesia (effective but short-acting and expensive); (4) ETS surgery (permanent but 50–80% compensatory hyperhidrosis risk).

ENDOSCOPIC THORACIC SYMPATHECTOMY (ETS) REQUIRES THOROUGH INFORMED CONSENT FOR COMPENSATORY HYPERHIDROSIS RISK: ETS is the only permanent cure for palmar or axillary hyperhidrosis, but at a high price — compensatory hyperhidrosis (sweating shifting to the trunk, back, buttocks, and thighs) occurs in 50–80% of patients and can be severe and more debilitating than the original palmar problem. Indian patients are heavily marketed ETS as 'cure' at private thoracic surgery centres. The informed consent conversation MUST include: (1) 50–80% risk of significant compensatory sweating; (2) Compensatory sweating is generally irreversible; (3) Gustatory sweating (sweating triggered by eating) risk; (4) Horner's syndrome risk; (5) Cardiology effects (reduced heart rate response). For most palmar hyperhidrosis patients, Botox + Iontophoresis should be exhausted before considering ETS.
confirmation_tests_curated: Primary focal hyperhidrosis: bilateral, focal, symmetrical sweating; onset <25 years; at least 1 episode/week; no night sweating; family history; cessation during sleep; interference with daily activities

Iodine solution painted on affected area; allowed to dry; starch powder applied; sweating areas turn dark blue/black (starch + iodine + sweat reaction); standard test for quantifying affected area for Botox injection planning

diseases.name: Perioral Dermatitis
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

POD diagnosed clinically by: perioral/periocular/perinasal papulopustular eruption; characteristic lip-sparing clear zone; history of topical steroid use; absence of comedones

DERMOSCOPY

Erythema, fine scales, superficial papules; no comedones; distinguishes from acne; hair follicle involvement pattern

SKIN BIOPSY (rarely needed)

Perifollicular lymphocytic and histiocytic infiltrate; spongiosis; granulomatous variant: sarcoidal or tuberculoid granulomas (without caseation); PAS-negative (no fungal hyphae)

KOH PREPARATION

Rule out tinea faciei (dermatophyte facial infection) as mimic

PATCH TEST

If contact allergen-driven POD suspected (toothpaste, cosmetic ingredient); fragrance mix, balsam of Peru, nickel — common facial contact allergens

DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

Rosacea — lacks clear sparing zone; usually older patients; erythema more prominent; triggers include heat, alcohol

Acne — comedones present; distribution different; hormonal pattern

Demodex folliculitis — similar papular perioral eruption; responds to Ivermectin/Metronidazole

Seborrhoeic dermatitis — greasy scale; sebaceous distribution; nasolabial folds but different characteristics
diseases.generic_medicines:
ZERO THERAPY — THE FIRST STEP

ZERO THERAPY: Stop ALL topical steroids, ALL heavy moisturisers, occlusive make-up, fluoride toothpaste on perioral area; stop any OTC fairness creams; this alone causes 30–40% clearance but with initial rebound worsening — patients must be explicitly warned

TOPICAL TREATMENT

Metronidazole 0.75–1% gel/cream — first-line topical; BD for 6–8 weeks; anti-inflammatory, anti-microbial; effective perioral dermatitis standard treatment

Azelaic acid 15–20% gel — anti-inflammatory; anti-microbial; good tolerability; safe alternative to metronidazole

Pimecrolimus 1% cream — calcineurin inhibitor; useful during topical steroid withdrawal phase; anti-inflammatory without steroid; prevents rebound; apply BD during the first 4–6 weeks of steroid cessation

Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment — similar calcineurin inhibitor; alternative to pimecrolimus

Clindamycin 1% solution/gel — topical antibiotic; second-line if metronidazole insufficient; always combine with BPO-free regimen

Ivermectin 1% cream — anti-Demodex; useful if Demodex-associated POD or concurrent rosacea features

SYSTEMIC FOR MODERATE-SEVERE

Doxycycline 50–100 mg OD for 6–8 weeks — anti-inflammatory dose; very effective for moderate-severe POD; India standard of care for persistent POD

Azithromycin 500 mg 3 days/week for 4–6 weeks — alternative to doxycycline; good India data

Tetracycline 250–500 mg OD–BD — alternative; older generation tetracycline; effective

MAINTAIN

After clearance, continue Metronidazole or Azelaic acid 3x/week for 3 months as maintenance

ABSOLUTE RULE: Never reintroduce topical steroids to face — POD recurs within weeks of any facial TCS

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
TOPICAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY

Aloe vera gel — cooling; anti-inflammatory; reduces perioral erythema and burning sensation; apply BD to perioral area; well tolerated; gentle on perioral sensitive skin during steroid withdrawal period

Multani Mitti (Fuller's earth) face pack — absorbs excess sebum; anti-inflammatory; gentle drying of micropustules; apply as thin paste to perioral area; rinse after 10–15 minutes

Neem (Azadirachta indica) — anti-bacterial; anti-inflammatory; diluted neem water rinse on perioral skin; reduces bacterial component of POD

Chandan (Sandalwood) + Rose water paste — cooling; anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta-driven erythema and burning sensation

SYSTEMIC PITTA-REDUCING

Haridra (Turmeric/Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta; 500 mg BD curcumin; reduces systemic inflammatory drivers

Triphala — systemic cleanser; Pitta balancing; gut-skin axis improvement

Manjistha — blood purifier; reduces facial inflammation

LIFESTYLE

Discontinue all heavy oil-based face creams (Kapha-aggravating); simplify skincare to minimal gentle products; avoid fluoride toothpaste during active disease (switch to neem toothpaste)

IMPORTANT NOTE

The most important 'Ayurvedic' intervention for POD in India is stopping all topical steroid-containing facial products — this directly aligns with Ayurvedic Nidana-parivarjana (elimination of causative factors). Aloe vera is the most appropriate soothing botanical during the steroid-withdrawal rebound phase. The Metronidazole gel + oral Doxycycline combination remains the pharmacological gold standard for established POD. Medical treatment with topical calcineurin inhibitors (Pimecrolimus/Tacrolimus) as bridge during steroid withdrawal is essential to prevent unbearable rebound flare.
diseases.india_specific:
TOPICAL STEROID MISUSE — PRIMARY INDIA CAUSE

POD is epidemic in India because of the near-universal misuse of facial topical corticosteroids in OTC products, prescribed for non-dermatological purposes by GPs, and self-prescribed. Clobetasol propionate (Class I steroid — most potent) is the most common causative agent in Indian POD because it is present in fairness cream formulations and Quadriderm-type combination creams widely available OTC. Any woman using any topical steroid-containing product on her face presenting with perioral or perinasal papulopustular eruption = steroid-induced POD until proven otherwise

REBOUND WORSENING ON STOPPING STEROID

When topical steroid is discontinued, patients experience a rebound flare of POD (and underlying skin condition) that appears worse than the original problem — this causes patients to reapply the steroid; the 'zero therapy' principle means absolutely stopping all topical steroids despite initial rebound; bridging with Metronidazole or Pimecrolimus reduces withdrawal severity

MISDIAGNOSIS AS ACNE

POD frequently misdiagnosed as acne in India; key difference: POD lacks comedones, has clear perioral sparing zone at lip margin, responds to Metronidazole not BPO; treating POD with BPO or benzoyl peroxide worsens irritation

PAEDIATRIC POD

POD increasingly recognised in Indian children from topical steroid applied to face by parents for eczema, insect bites, or 'skin rashes'; granulomatous POD common in children; management same principles — steroid withdrawal, topical antibiotics
diseases.important_notes:
STOP ALL TOPICAL STEROIDS — THE REBOUND IS TEMPORARY BUT THE RECOVERY IS PERMANENT: The most critical management step for steroid-induced perioral dermatitis is complete and permanent cessation of all facial topical corticosteroids — no exceptions, no tapering slowly with the same steroid (this only prolongs dependency). The rebound flare that occurs 5–10 days after stopping is real and often alarming to patients — eruption worsens, erythema increases, patients feel their skin 'needs' the cream. This is steroid-induced tachyphylaxis and dependence, not a new disease. The treatment: (1) Stop ALL facial TCS immediately and permanently; (2) Replace with Pimecrolimus 1% or Tacrolimus 0.1% as anti-inflammatory bridge; (3) Add Metronidazole gel BD; (4) Oral Doxycycline 50–100 mg OD for 6–8 weeks for significant POD; (5) Set expectation: skin may appear worse weeks 2–4 before improving weeks 6–12. Communication is the treatment.

TOPICAL STEROID FACE EPIDEMIC IN INDIA — POD IS THE VISIBLE TIP: Perioral dermatitis from facial topical steroid misuse represents the visible clinical tip of a massive public health problem in India. The underlying issue: millions of Indian women are applying potent fluorinated corticosteroids (Clobetasol, Betamethasone) to their faces daily — for months to years — from unregulated OTC fairness creams, self-prescribed steroid-antibiotic-antifungal combination creams, or well-meaning but undertrained prescribers. Consequences include: POD, steroid rosacea, skin atrophy, telangiectasias, steroid acne, delayed wound healing, systemic corticosteroid absorption leading to Cushing's stigmata in extreme cases, and HPA axis suppression in children. Every dermatology consultation is an opportunity to educate patients about never applying any unlabelled or OTC cream to the face — read ingredients, check for steroids.

DISTINGUISH POD FROM ACNE — TREATMENT IS OPPOSITE: Perioral dermatitis is frequently misdiagnosed as acne in Indian non-specialist settings, leading to treatment with benzoyl peroxide and adapalene — which worsen POD through skin barrier disruption and irritation. Critical differentiators: (1) POD has the PATHOGNOMONIC clear zone of spared normal skin immediately adjacent to the lip vermilion (3–5 mm); acne does NOT; (2) No comedones in POD; comedones are hallmark of acne; (3) POD distribution is strictly perioral/periocular/perinasal — not T-zone centred; (4) POD burning > itching in symptoms; (5) History of topical steroid use; (6) POD responds to Metronidazole/Doxycycline — NOT BPO or topical retinoids. The treatment dichotomy: acne = BPO + retinoid; POD = zero therapy + Metronidazole + Doxycycline.
confirmation_tests_curated: POD diagnosed clinically by: perioral/periocular/perinasal papulopustular eruption; characteristic lip-sparing clear zone; history of topical steroid use; absence of comedones
Erythema, fine scales, superficial papules; no comedones; distinguishes from acne; hair follicle involvement pattern

diseases.name: Striae Distensae
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Striae diagnosed clinically and require no specific investigation in typical presentations; history confirms trigger (pregnancy, growth spurt, steroid use, weight change)

WOOD'S LAMP

Striae albae (white) enhanced under Wood's lamp — delineates extent on light skin

DERMOSCOPY

Striae rubrae: linear vascular structures, erythema; striae albae: white linear areas, loss of normal skin markings, collagen fibres visible

SKIN BIOPSY (rarely needed)

Thinned epidermis; flattened rete ridges; collagen fibres horizontally oriented, parallel; decreased elastin fibres; confirm Cushing's if suspected

CUSHING'S SCREENING (if wide violaceous horizontal abdominal striae)

24-hour urinary free cortisol

Overnight dexamethasone suppression test (1 mg at midnight, cortisol at 9 am; <50 nmol/L = normal)

Late-night salivary cortisol

MRI pituitary / chest / adrenal — Cushing's source

EXCLUDE SYSTEMIC STEROID CAUSE

Oral steroid history; inhaled steroid use (very high-dose); topical steroid history on body
diseases.generic_medicines:
STRIAE RUBRAE — MOST RESPONSIVE PHASE

Tretinoin 0.025–0.05–0.1% cream — topical retinoid; stimulates collagen synthesis; best evidence for striae rubrae; apply at night; apply only on affected striae (photosensitising); avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding

Glycolic acid 10–20% lotion/cream — alpha-hydroxy acid; exfoliates; stimulates new collagen; safe in pregnancy when used topically; combine with Tretinoin alternating (not together — irritation)

Hyaluronic acid + Centella asiatica combination — stimulates collagen; reduces striae width; some RCT evidence in striae gravidarum prevention

STRIAE ALBAE — PROCEDURAL TREATMENTS MOST EFFECTIVE

Fractional CO2 laser — ablative fractional photothermolysis; gold standard for established striae albae; stimulates collagen remodelling; each session 30–50% improvement; 3–5 sessions monthly required; safe on Indian skin with Fitzpatrick settings

Fractional RF microneedling (Morpheus8, Vivace) — radiofrequency + mechanical micro-injury; excellent for striae albae; stimulates collagen and elastin; darker skin-safe

Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) injection into striae — growth factors stimulate collagen synthesis; India widely available; 3–6 monthly sessions

Non-ablative fractional laser (1550 nm Fraxel, 1540 nm) — stimulates dermal collagen; lighter treatment; fewer sessions for mild striae

Microneedling (dermaroller / Dermapen) — multiple collagen induction sessions; affordable in India; 4–6 monthly sessions

PREVENTION (During pregnancy — striae gravidarum)

Moisturiser massage daily — daily abdominal massage with any emollient appears to reduce striae gravidarum incidence slightly; Trofolastin cream (Centella asiatica + hydrolyzed elastin) has RCT evidence for prevention

Adequate hydration + Vitamin E + C supplementation orally

Silicone oil application — experimental; emerging evidence
