Glycolic Acid 12% lotion

Vitamin E oil / Vitamin E 400 IU capsule

Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid) 500 mg tablet

Collagen supplement (hydrolysed collagen peptides)

Hyaluronic acid serum

Centella asiatica (Gotu Kola) 500 mg tablet

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Tretinoin 0.025% cream (20g): Rs.35

Vitamin E 400 IU (10 caps): Rs.22

Vitamin C 500 mg (10 tabs): Rs.8

Centella asiatica extract (10 tabs): Rs.18
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
TOPICAL AND MASSAGE OILS

Coconut oil (Nariyal tail) — traditional Indian pregnancy massage oil; moisturises; prevents dehydration-related skin fragility; massage reduces skin tension; widely used India; safe in pregnancy

Almond oil (Badam tail) — rich in Vitamin E; emollient; anti-oxidant; traditional striae prevention during pregnancy; gentle massage increases local blood flow

Sesame oil (Til tail) — traditional Abhyanga base oil; Vitamin E and antioxidants; Ayurvedic recommendation for skin flexibility maintenance

Kumkumadi Tailam — saffron-based oil; contains multiple anti-inflammatory and collagen-supporting botanicals; post-delivery striae rubrae; nighttime application

Castor oil — ricinoleic acid; anti-inflammatory; penetrating; promotes collagen turnover; traditionally used for body skin regeneration

CENTELLA ASIATICA — STRONGEST EVIDENCE

Gotu Kola (Manduka-parni / Brahmi / Centella asiatica) — asiaticoside and madecassoside; specifically documented to stimulate Type I and III collagen synthesis in human fibroblasts; published RCTs for striae gravidarum prevention; oral Centella 60–120 mg BD or as topical cream; 'Trofolastin' cream contains Centella asiatica + hydrolysed elastin — this is an Ayurvedic plant used in conventional dermatology trials

Amla (Emblica officinalis) oral — Vitamin C; cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase in collagen synthesis; essential for collagen integrity; 500 mg–1 g/day

Haridra (Turmeric) oral — anti-inflammatory; reduces striae-associated skin inflammation; Curcumin 500 mg BD

Shatavari — female reproductive tonic; hormonal support; skin hydration; pregnancy adjunct

PREVENTION DURING PREGNANCY

Daily Snehana (oil massage) — traditional India: warm sesame or coconut oil massage to pregnant abdomen from 12 weeks; Garbhini Paricharya (pregnancy care) in Ayurveda recommends Abhyanga for skin health; aligns with clinical evidence that daily massage reduces striae formation

IMPORTANT NOTE

Centella asiatica (Manduka-parni / Gotu Kola) is the single botanical with strongest clinical and mechanistic evidence for both striae prevention and treatment — it directly stimulates collagen synthesis via fibroblast activation. Indian pregnancy tradition of daily abdominal massage with oil has clinical evidence supporting it: multiple RCTs show that daily massage (regardless of specific oil agent used) significantly reduces striae gravidarum compared to no intervention. This traditional Ayurvedic practice is validated by modern dermoscopy trial data.
diseases.india_specific:
MULTIPLE PREGNANCIES

Indian social and cultural factors (including joint family pressure, limited family planning uptake in rural areas) mean multiple pregnancies are common; each pregnancy adds new striae gravidarum; progressive abdominal striae accumulate

TOPICAL STEROID STRIAE

Long-term application of potent topical steroids to body skin (groin, trunk, thighs) — from misuse of Betnovate, Quadriderm, or OTC fairness creams on body — causes steroid-induced striae; these are particularly wide and violaceous; commonly lateral abdomen, groin, upper thighs; iatrogenic in India from TCS misuse

AYURVEDIC PREVENTIVE TRADITION

Application of oils during pregnancy — traditional Indian practice of massaging coconut oil, almond oil, or specific Ayurvedic oils on the pregnant abdomen is historically practised; clinical evidence for oil massage preventing striae is mixed (some RCTs positive for daily massage regardless of agent vs no treatment), but the practice has cultural validity and safety

LASER THERAPY AVAILABILITY

Fractional CO2 laser and fractional RF microneedling — most effective treatments for striae albae; available at dermatology centres in India across metro cities; sessions Rs.3,000–15,000 each; multiple sessions needed

RAPID WEIGHT CHANGES

Yo-yo dieting — crash diets followed by regain; repeated rapid skin distension → extension of existing striae + new formation; common in India influenced by rapid weight-loss promises
diseases.important_notes:
STRIAE TREATMENT IS MOST EFFECTIVE DURING THE RUBRAE (RED) PHASE — ACT EARLY: Striae rubrae (red/pink/purple) are actively forming lesions with ongoing vascular changes and relatively preserved tissue architecture — they are significantly more responsive to all treatments (topical Tretinoin, Glycolic acid, laser, PRP, microneedling) than mature striae albae (white/silvery). The pathophysiology: during rubrae phase, fibroblasts are still active and collagen remodelling is amenable to stimulation; in albae phase, the collagen is permanently disrupted and avascular, with no active cellular response. In Indian practice, patients often accept striae for months-years before seeking treatment, arriving at the albae stage where results are modest. The clinical message: any patient with new pregnancy-associated, growth-spurt, or weight-change striae should be counselled to begin treatment while lesions are still red — Tretinoin 0.025% at night + SPF30 in morning (Tretinoin + sun = hyperpigmentation on Indian skin) + microneedling monthly if access available.

PREGNANCY STRIAE PREVENTION — DAILY MASSAGE WITH ANY EMOLLIENT IS EVIDENCE-BASED: A meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (2015, Journal of European Academy Dermatology) confirmed that daily massage of the abdomen significantly reduces striae gravidarum incidence compared to no treatment. The specific product used (oil vs cream vs gel) mattered less than the act of daily massage itself — the mechanical effect of massage stimulates blood flow and fibroblast collagen synthesis in the dermis. In India, the traditional Ayurvedic Garbhini Snehana (pregnancy anointment) practice of daily oil massage on the growing abdomen from first trimester is entirely consistent with this evidence. Practically: any pregnant woman should be advised to apply and gently massage coconut oil, almond oil, or Centella asiatica cream to abdomen, breasts, and thighs daily from 2nd trimester.

FRACTIONAL LASER AND MICRONEEDLING ARE SAFE ON INDIAN SKIN FOR STRIAE ALBAE BUT REQUIRE INDIA-SPECIFIC SETTINGS: Striae albae on Indian skin (Fitzpatrick III-V) require careful laser parameter selection to avoid post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation — the skin tanning response to laser energy in dark Indian skin. Fractional CO2 laser at lower densities with adequate cooling, or fractional RF microneedling (which uses radiofrequency rather than UV/visible light energy), is safer for Indian skin tones. The dermatologist must: (1) Test patch first; (2) Use conservative settings; (3) Ensure SPF50+ daily from 2 weeks before to 3+ months after treatment; (4) Space sessions 4–6 weeks apart. RF microneedling (using radiofrequency current, independent of skin colour) has become the preferred modality for striae albae in Indian skin because colour-blindness reduces PIH risk compared to laser.
confirmation_tests_curated: Striae diagnosed clinically and require no specific investigation in typical presentations; history confirms trigger (pregnancy, growth spurt, steroid use, weight change)
Striae albae (white) enhanced under Wood's lamp — delineates extent on light skin

diseases.name: Erectile Dysfunction
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL HISTORY AND IIEF-5 QUESTIONNAIRE

IIEF-5 (International Index of Erectile Function — 5 item) — validated; 5 questions; scores 5–25; primary diagnostic tool; distinguishes severity; also documents treatment response

NOCTURNAL PENILE TUMESCENCE (NPT) TEST

Rigiscan device — home-based; measures frequency, duration, rigidity of overnight penile tumescence; PRESERVED NPT = psychogenic ED; ABSENT NPT = organic ED; confirms diagnosis direction before investgation

BLOOD TESTS

Fasting plasma glucose OR HbA1c — diabetes

Fasting lipid profile (TC, LDL, HDL, TG) — dyslipidaemia

Serum testosterone (Total testosterone, early morning fasting) — hypogonadism; confirm LOW <300 ng/dL

SHBG + Albumin (calculate Free testosterone if total borderline)

FSH, LH, Prolactin — if low testosterone found; pituitary axis assessment

PSA (Prostate Specific Antigen) — if testosterone replacement planned; ≥40 years

TSH — thyroid dysfunction

FBC, Creatinine, LFT — general medical

PENILE DUPLEX DOPPLER ULTRASOUND

After intracavernosal papaverine/PGE1 injection — assesses cavernosal artery peak systolic velocity (PSV >25 cm/s = normal); end diastolic velocity (EDV <5 = normal venous competence); gold standard for vascular ED diagnosis; identifies arterial insufficiency vs venous leak

CARDIOVASCULAR RISK ASSESSMENT

ECG — ischaemia; stress test (ETT) if intermediate cardiovascular risk before starting PDE5 inhibitors
diseases.generic_medicines:
PDE5 INHIBITORS — CORNERSTONE TREATMENT

Sildenafil (Viagra) 25–50–100 mg — on-demand oral; taken 45–60 min before; food (fatty meal) delays absorption; avoid with nitrates; first-line; 6–8 hours duration

Tadalafil 10–20 mg on-demand — 2.5–5 mg daily (continuously) — longer duration (36 hours on-demand); daily low dose maintains constant penile tissue oxygenation and allows spontaneous intercourse without timing; PREFERRED for diabetic ED and post-prostatectomy ED due to 24-hour cavernosal oxygenation benefit

Vardenafil 10–20 mg — meal-independent; on-demand

Avanafil 100–200 mg — fastest onset (15 min); newest; less interaction with CYP3A4

CONTRAINDICATION: ALL PDE5 inhibitors + nitrates (GTN, isosorbide) = ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION → fatal hypotension; also caution with alpha-blockers (doxazosin) — take 4-hour gap minimum

HORMONAL TREATMENT

Testosterone replacement (if hypogonadism confirmed): Testosterone undecanoate injection 1000 mg/12 weeks; testosterone gel 25–50 mg/day; improves libido + ED in hypogonadal men; must screen prostate and haematocrit before starting

Cabergoline — if hyperprolactinaemia confirmed cause

PSYCHOSEXUAL THERAPY

Masters and Johnson sensate focus; CBT; couples therapy — for psychogenic ED; often combined with PDE5 inhibitor to break performance anxiety cycle

VACUUM ERECTION DEVICE (VED)

Mechanical; no drug; useful when PDE5 contraindicated; good for elderly, post-prostatectomy men; creates passive erection via negative pressure

SECOND/THIRD LINE

Intracavernosal Alprostadil (PGE1 injection) — 2nd line; self-injection; very effective any cause; 80–90% response

Intraurethral Alprostadil (MUSE) — less effective than injection

Penile implant — inflatable 3-piece prosthesis; definitive surgical option for refractory ED; gold standard after all else fails

Sildenafil 100 mg (4 tabs): Rs.45

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
VAJIKARANA RASAYANA

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces cortisol; increases testosterone in stressed men; improves sexual performance in clinical trials (IIEF score improvement in RCT); doses 300–600 mg root extract BD; reduces anxiety component of psychogenic ED; widely used and validated

Shilajit (Asphaltum bituminous) — fulvic acid mineral complex; increases testosterone (RCT data); improves mitochondrial energy metabolism; traditional vajikarana; 250–500 mg BD; evidence for testosterone elevation and sperm quality in infertile men

Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — root extract; traditionally used Vajikarana; adaptogen; some animal and human studies for testicular testosterone production and sexual performance

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — increases LH-like activity; testosterone elevation in some but not all studies; marketed as testosterone booster; evidence weaker than Ashwagandha/Shilajit; traditionally used

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA precursor → dopaminergic enhancement → libido and psychogenic ED; also testosterone elevation; has RCT evidence in infertile men; dopamine pathway modulation

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Vajikarana Ghrita — classical Ayurvedic compound; milk + ghee base with vajikarana herbs; traditionally prescribed for sexual debility

Ashwagandha Lehyam — Ashwagandha-based confection; compliance-friendly

Chyawanprash — antioxidant rasayana; improves vitality; Amla-based Vitamin C; overall rejuvenation

Muskar Vati — classical formulaton; uses disputed

LIFESTYLE

Cow's milk with saffron and ghee — traditional Indian vajikarana; saffron (Crocus sativus) has clinical evidence for psychogenic ED improvement (4 RCTs positive); anti-anxiety, antidepressant effects that reduce performance anxiety component

IMPORTANT NOTE

Among Ayurvedic herbs, Ashwagandha and Shilajit have the strongest published clinical evidence for ED improvement — both with RCTs demonstrating IIEF score improvement and testosterone elevation. Saffron has specific RCT evidence for SSRI-induced ED (adding saffron 30 mg/day partially reverses SSRI sexual dysfunction). However, these should complement — not replace — diagnosis-specific treatment. A man with undiagnosed diabetes or severe vascular ED will not benefit from Ashwagandha alone. The diagnosis must be established first, cardiovascular risk addressed, PDE5 inhibitors used when appropriate, and Ayurvedic adaptogens as adjuncts for overall vitality and hormonal support.
diseases.india_specific:
DIABETES EPIDEMIC

India has 77+ million diabetics (2nd highest globally); diabetic ED occurs in 35–90% of male diabetics; multifactorial: (1) autonomic neuropathy → impaired NO release; (2) endothelial dysfunction → vascular; (3) low testosterone — obesity-related; (4) psychological burden of chronic illness; ED is often first presenting complaint that leads to diabetes diagnosis in Indian men

TOBACCO USE

India: 28% of adults use tobacco (smoking + smokeless); smoking is dose-dependent endothelial toxin; nicotine causes vasoconstriction and accelerated atherosclerosis of cavernosal arteries; independent risk factor for ED; young Indian male smokers develop vasculogenic ED a decade earlier than non-smokers; smokeless tobacco (gutkha, khaini) also associated with ED through oxidative stress

ALCOHOL

Chronic alcohol use — common India; directly toxic to Leydig cells → low testosterone; peripheral neuropathy; psychogenic component; 'whisky dick' = acute alcohol-induced ED; chronic alcoholic ED partially reversible with abstinence

ANTIHYPERTENSIVE MEDICINES COMMON IN INDIA

Atenolol — widely prescribed India first-line antihypertensive; STRONG ED association; beta-blockers reduce sympathetic tone, reduce LH pulsatility; switch to Nebivolol (beta-3 agonist → NO release → less ED) or amlodipine (calcium channel blocker — neutral or positive for ED) wherever possible; do NOT assume all antihypertensives cause ED equally — Nebivolol/Telmisartan are better choices

QUACK REMEDIES

India: massive market of unregulated 'sex tonics', Ayurvedic vajikarana preparations, and herbal products — many adulterated with undisclosed PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil); dangerous in men with cardiovascular disease taking nitrates — can cause fatal hypotension; PMOA India has issued warnings; patients must disclose all supplements
diseases.important_notes:
ERECTILE DYSFUNCTION IS A CARDIOVASCULAR WARNING SIGNAL — MUST NOT BE TREATED IN ISOLATION: ED in men under 60 is a cardiovascular sentinel event. The pathophysiology is shared: endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerosis affect small-diameter penile arteries (1–2 mm) earlier than larger-diameter coronary arteries (4 mm) because flow reduction becomes haemodynamically significant at a smaller plaque burden in smaller vessels. ED therefore precedes coronary artery disease by 2–5 years in studies. The Princeton III Consensus guidelines state: Every man with new-onset ED under 60 years without obvious psychogenic cause must be evaluated for cardiovascular risk factors (blood pressure, lipid profile, glucose/HbA1c, smoking, family history) BEFORE prescribing PDE5 inhibitors. This is also important for PDE5 inhibitor safety — a man with unknown severe CAD on nitrates would face fatal hypotension with sildenafil. The correct clinical sequence: (1) ED diagnosis; (2) Cardiovascular risk stratification; (3) Address modifiable CVD risk; (4) Prescribe PDE5 inhibitor safely.

PDE5 INHIBITOR + NITRATE ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION: This is the most safety-critical drug interaction in the treatment of ED. ALL PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil, avanafil) are absolutely contraindicated with ALL nitrates (glyceryl trinitrate sublingual/patches, isosorbide mononitrate oral) — the combination causes profound irreversible vasodilatation and can cause fatal hypotensive collapse. In India, this interaction is particularly dangerous because (1) ischaemic heart disease is extremely prevalent and undertreated; (2) patients often carry GTN spray/tablets for acute angina; (3) men with ED may not disclose IHD; (4) quack herbal preparations adulterated with sildenafil/tadalafil are taken unknowingly alongside nitrates. A careful drug history specifically asking about 'heart tablets, angina spray' is mandatory before prescribing PDE5 inhibitors.

DIABETES IS THE MOST COMMON ORGANIC CAUSE OF ED IN INDIA — SCREEN ALL MEN WITH ED: ED in Indian men has diabetes as its most common treatable organic cause, given India's diabetic epidemic. Diabetic ED is multifactorial: (1) autonomic peripheral neuropathy impairs NO release from cavernosal nerves; (2) endothelial dysfunction impairs cavernosal artery smooth muscle relaxation; (3) testosterone deficiency (adiposity, metabolic syndrome); (4) psychological burden. The practical implication: every man presenting with ED should have HbA1c or fasting glucose done as part of the initial workup — this is inexpensive, immediately actionable, and may reveal a new diabetes diagnosis that changes the entire management approach. Good glycaemic control (HbA1c <7%) partially reverses diabetic ED — medication alone (even Tadalafil daily) will have much better outcomes when glycaemia is controlled simultaneously.

diseases.name: Premature Ejaculation
diseases.tests:
STOPWATCH IELT MEASUREMENT

Patient and partner measure actual time from penetration to ejaculation with stopwatch; 3–5 representative encounters; mean IELT <1 min = lifelong PE; <3 min = acquired PE; this is the gold standard diagnostic measurement

PREMATURE EJACULATION DIAGNOSTIC TOOL (PEDT)

5-item validated questionnaire; scores 0–20; ≥11 = PE; 9–10 = probable PE; standard diagnostic and outcome tool

TSH

Thyroid stimulating hormone — to exclude hyperthyroidism as acquired PE cause (thyroid hormones reduce ejaculatory latency threshold)

PROSTATE ASSESSMENT

Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA); Urine microscopy/culture; Expressed Prostatic Secretion (EPS); Transrectal Ultrasound (TRUS) — if prostatitis suspected as cause of acquired PE

TESTOSTERONE

Serum testosterone — low testosterone associated with lower ejaculatory control; check if libido also reduced

NEUROLOGICAL ASSESSMENT

Bulbocavernosus reflex latency; pudendal nerve conduction — rarely needed; for neurogenic acquired PE

DIFFERENTIATE PE FROM ED

Key clinical question: is ejaculation premature because of anxiety about losing erection? Treat ED primarily, and reassess PE — often PE resolves when ED resolved (loss of erection anxiety drives rapid ejaculation)
diseases.generic_medicines:
ON-DEMAND ORAL

Dapoxetine 30 mg (up-titrate to 60 mg if needed) — short-acting SSRI; taken 1–3 hours before intercourse; only licensed on-demand pharmacological treatment for PE; increases ejaculatory latency 2–4x over baseline; does not require daily dosing; safe for intermittent use; SE: nausea, dizziness, headache — mostly mild; caution with syncope history; contraindication with MAOIs and thioridazine

DAILY SSRI (CONTINUOUS)

Paroxetine 10–40 mg OD — most potent SSRI for PE; highest ejaculatory delay among SSRIs; daily use; takes 2 weeks to reach effect; SE: sexual side effects (ED, anorgasmia), weight gain, discontinuation syndrome if stopped abruptly

Sertraline 25–200 mg OD — effective; better tolerability than paroxetine; daily

Fluoxetine 20–40 mg OD — PE treatment; longer half-life; good option

Citalopram 20–40 mg OD — alternative

TOPICAL ANAESTHETICS

Lidocaine 2.5% + Prilocaine 2.5% (EMLA cream) — apply 20 min before sex; remove before penetration; reduces glans sensitivity; caution — don't leave on too long (risk of over-numbing; loss of erection)

Lidocaine 4% spray (Fortacin spray) — EU-licensed; ON-demand; 3 metered puffs to glans; very effective; minimal systemic absorption

Topical Benzocaine wipes/gel — available OTC in India; similar mechanism

BEHAVIOURAL TECHNIQUES

Stop-Start technique (Semans) — partner stimulates to near-ejaculation, stops stimulation, resumes after 30–45 seconds; 3 cycles per session; builds ejaculatory control gradually

Squeeze technique (Masters & Johnson) — squeeze the glans corona at near-ejaculation point; breaks ejaculatory reflex; partner or self applied

Pelvic floor exercises — Kegel exercises for men; bulbocavernosus and ischiocavernosus muscle strengthening; recent RCT: 12 weeks pelvic floor exercises → 82% PE patients gained normal control

Tramadol 50 mg tablet (as last resort — analgesic with 5-HT3 effect)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
NERVINE TONICS — REDUCING HYPERSENSITIVITY AND ANXIETY

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces sympathetic nervous system hyperactivation that drives PE; reduces cortisol and anxiety; clinical trial data shows improvement in sex latency and satisfaction in men with PE alongside anxiety; 300–600 mg BD

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA precursor; dopaminergic modulation; helps psychogenic PE; also serotonergic indirect effects; increases ejaculatory threshold; 500 mg BD

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — anxiolytic; GABAergic modulation; reduces performance anxiety; nervous system calming; reduces fear of failure that perpetuates PE

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — nervine rejuvenator; reproductive tonic; reduces sexual debility; traditionally used for all male sexual disorders

Jatiphal (Nutmeg / Myristica fragrans) — traditional Ayurvedic-Unani remedy for premature ejaculation; myristic acid content; CNS sedative at therapeutic doses; reduces ejaculatory reflex hypersensitivity; traditional formulation: small amounts in warm milk before intercourse; not for chronic daily use (hepatotoxic in excess)

TOPICAL TRADITIONAL REMEDIES

Clove oil (diluted) — traditional Indian topical for PE; eugenol = mild topical local anaesthetic; apply to glans (diluted in carrier oil) before intercourse — mechanism closely mirrors Lidocaine/Benzocaine topicals; evidence for eugenol as topical anaesthetic is established in dentistry

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Kokilaksha (Hygrophila spinosa) with Vajikarana — traditional PE formula

Vrishya Vati — Ayurvedic compound tablet for sexual debility; various vajikarana herbs

Chandraprabha Vati — multi-herb genitourinary tonic

IMPORTANT NOTE

Among Ayurvedic approaches, the most evidence-supported are: (1) Ashwagandha for anxiety reduction + cortisol lowering — directly addresses psychogenic PE mechanism; (2) Brahmi for anxiolytic effect; (3) Diluted clove oil topically — the eugenol local anaesthetic mechanism is identical to medical-grade lidocaine. Behavioural therapy (stop-start, squeeze technique, pelvic floor exercises) is the first-line non-pharmacological treatment with the strongest evidence. Dapoxetine remains the pharmacological gold standard for on-demand PE treatment. For acquired PE, always address the underlying cause (ED, prostatitis, hyperthyroidism) first.
diseases.india_specific:
HIGH CONSULTATION DELAY

Indian men with PE often delay consulting doctors for years due to cultural shame; self-treat with Ayurvedic preparations, local remedies, alcohol (reduces sensitivity), multiple masturbations before sex (refractory period strategy), or topical numbing agents; many come with acquired secondary relationship dysfunction by the time they consult

MISINFORMATION

Widespread misinformation in India: PE attributed to 'semen weakness', 'nerve weakness', 'masturbation in youth', 'heat from spicy food' — none are scientifically valid; patients arrive with guilt about masturbation history which is not a PE aetiological factor

DAPOXETINE AVAILABILITY

Dapoxetine (Priligy) — first and only registered on-demand oral SSRI for PE in India; widely available; known brand Duralast, Dapox; has been available in India since 2012; effective in the Indian market; 30 mg or 60 mg taken 1–3 hours before sex; discontinuable on-demand (short half-life 1.5 hours unlike other SSRIs) — this is India-specific clinical opportunity

TOPICAL ANESTHETICS

Lidocaine/Prilocaine (EMLA) cream — apply to glans 20–30 min before sex, wash off before intercourse (to avoid partner vaginal numbness); available and effective; reduces glans hypersensitivity; often preferred by Indian couples who won't take oral medication

RELIGIOUS AND CULTURAL PRESSURES

Male performance expectations in Indian marriages (conjugal duty); wife's fertility-focused sex (pressure-driven intercourse without emotional foreplay) worsens psychogenic PE; psychosexual counselling must address cultural marital dynamics
diseases.important_notes:
PREMATURE EJACULATION IS THE MOST COMMON MALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION AND IS HIGHLY TREATABLE — BUT INDIA'S CONSULTATION RATES REMAIN LOW: PE affects an estimated 20–30% of sexually active men globally, making it the single most prevalent male sexual complaint — more common than erectile dysfunction. In India, the prevalence is consistent with global data but consultation rates are estimated to be <10% of affected men due to shame, partner non-involvement in treatment, and cultural silence about male sexual difficulty. The treatment is highly effective: pelvic floor exercises alone converted 82% of patients in a recent Italian RCT; Dapoxetine reliably doubles-quadruples IELT; SSRIs are effective; topical anaesthetics work for most men. The practical barrier is consultation, not treatment. Primary care physicians should routinely screen for PE in men who mention sexual or marital difficulties — a single question: 'Do you feel you ejaculate too quickly during sex and does this bother you?' is sufficient to open the consultation.

DAPOXETINE IS SUPERIOR TO CONTINUOUS SSRIs FOR ON-DEMAND USE — CHOOSE BASED ON PE PATTERN AND PREFERENCE: Dapoxetine has a unique pharmacokinetic profile among SSRIs: 77% first-pass extraction ensures its half-life is only 1–1.5 hours, making it safe to discontinue on-demand without withdrawal syndrome. This makes Dapoxetine ideal for men who need PE treatment only for specific sexual encounters and prefer not to take daily medication. Continuous daily SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline) are preferred when: (1) Man also needs antidepressant for comorbid anxiety/depression; (2) Man wants continuous sexual readiness without timing a dose; (3) Dapoxetine cost is prohibitive (Jan Aushadhi dapoxetine vs generic sertraline — significant cost difference). Practically: for most young Indian men with pure PE, Dapoxetine 30 mg on-demand is the least intrusive, well-tolerated first-line pharmacotherapy.

PELVIC FLOOR REHABILITATION IS THE MOST OVERLOOKED HIGH-EVIDENCE TREATMENT FOR PE: A landmark RCT (Pastore et al., 2014) demonstrated that 12 weeks of pelvic floor muscle rehabilitation exercises (target: bulbocavernosus and ischiocavernosus muscles) resulted in 82.5% of men with lifelong PE achieving normal IELT (mean improvement from 31.7 seconds to 146.2 seconds). Pelvic floor exercises are free, have no side effects, are permanent, and require no partner involvement if needed. The mechanism: strengthening voluntary perineal muscles allows men to identify and delay the ejaculatory reflex through conscious pelvic floor contraction. In clinical practice, particularly in Indian primary care settings where medication costs, stigma, and prescribing hesitation are barriers, pelvic floor exercises should be the universal first-line recommendation. They can be combined with stop-start/squeeze technique (Masters and Johnson behavioural methods).

5-item validated questionnaire; scores 0–20; ≥11 = PE; 9–10 = probable PE; standard diagnostic and outcome tool
Thyroid stimulating hormone — to exclude hyperthyroidism as acquired PE cause (thyroid hormones reduce ejaculatory latency threshold)

diseases.name: Delayed Ejaculation
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL ASSESSMENT

Detailed sexual history: onset (lifelong/acquired), situational pattern, masturbation vs intercourse pattern, relationship context, psychological factors; drug history (SSRIs, antipsychotics, alpha-blockers, opioids); surgical history (pelvic, retroperitoneal)

POST-ORGASM URINE MICROSCOPY (RETROGRADE EJACULATION)

Collect urine immediately after attempted ejaculation or orgasm; centrifuge; sperm in urine post-orgasm = retrograde ejaculation (absence of antegrade ejaculate; sperm in bladder); distinguishes retrograde from true anejaculation or DE

SEMEN ANALYSIS

Confirm absent or markedly reduced ejaculate volume; azoospermia vs sperm present

HORMONAL PANEL

Serum testosterone (low T from HPG suppression — opioids, hyperprolactinaemia)

Prolactin — haloperidol/antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia

FSH, LH — gonadotrophin axis

TSH — hypothyroidism association with DE

NEUROPHYSIOLOGICAL

Bulbocavernosus reflex testing; evoked pudendal potentials — spinal cord or peripheral nerve lesion assessment

DRUG HISTORY/PSYCHIATRIC MEDICATION REVIEW

SSRIs — confirm as DE cause; consider dose reduction or switch; SSRI-specific ejaculatory latency effect is well-established

PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT

Masturbatory pattern assessment — idiosyncratic masturbatory conditioning (rapid grip, specific stimulation incompatible with intercourse texture) — important psychogenic DE cause; CBT-based restructuring
diseases.generic_medicines:
ADDRESS UNDERLYING CAUSE FIRST

Review and reduce SSRI dose — if SSRI-induced DE; switch from Paroxetine to lower-risk SSRI (Escitalopram) or change to Mirtazapine/Bupropion; if psychiatric indication requires persistent SSRI, add Sildenafil or Bupropion augmentation

Review alpha-blocker necessity — Tamsulosin-induced retrograde ejaculation often resolves on stopping; switch to Silodosin (more selective; higher retrograde ejaculation rate but some men prefer) or Alfuzosin/Doxazosin (less retrograde ejaculation)

Antipsychotic review — switch to Quetiapine or Aripiprazole (lower prolactin elevation; less sexual dysfunction) from Haloperidol/Risperidone

PHARMACOLOGICAL THERAPY

Bupropion 150–300 mg SR — dopaminergic/noradrenergic; PRO-ejaculatory; used for SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction augmentation; improves DE and all SSRI sexual side effects including anorgasmia

Cyproheptadine 4–8 mg — serotonin antagonist (5-HT2); taken before intercourse; specifically reverses SSRI-induced ejaculatory delay; antihistamine with serotonin antagonist property; can be taken on-demand 30–60 min before sex

Cabergoline 0.25–0.5 mg twice weekly — dopamine agonist; pro-ejaculatory; reduces prolactin; useful for hyperprolactinaemia-induced DE or antipsychotic-induced DE

Testosterone replacement — if confirmed hypogonadism contributing

Amantadine 100 mg OD — dopaminergic; augments SSRI-treated DE

Oxytocin — intranasal; experimental; facilitates orgasmic reflex; not standard therapy

VIBRATORY STIMULATION

Penile vibratory stimulation — medical-grade vibrators (Ferticare, SatoVibe) at shaft/glans; particularly for neurogenic DE (spinal injury, MS, post-surgical); first-line for neurogenic anejaculation in spinal cord injury for fertility

Electroejaculation — rectal probe electrostimulation; for complete anejaculation (SCI patients); sperm harvested for IVF/ICSI; available at andrological fertility centres India

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
VAJIKARANA — EJACULATORY STIMULATION

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA rich; dopaminergic activation; directly relevant to DE as dopamine facilitates ejaculatory reflex; dopamine D2 receptor stimulation lowers ejaculatory threshold; 500 mg BD; has RCT data for improving male sexual function

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces cortisol; improves testosterone; reduces performance anxiety that inhibits ejaculation; for psychogenic DE component; 300–600 mg BD

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — LH-stimulating; testosterone support; reduces DE in hypogonadal context; 500 mg BD

Shilajit — testosterone elevation; mitochondrial energy; traditional androgenic rasayana; fulvic acid minerals; reduces sexual debility including DE

Shatavari — nervine rejuvenator; adaptogen; genitourinary tonic

PSYCHOGENIC DE — MIND-BODY

Brahmi — anxiolytic; helps psychogenic DE through performance anxiety reduction; GABA-A modulation

Jyotishmati (Celastrus paniculata) — cognitive enhancer; nervine; reduces mental blockade inhibiting ejaculatory reflex

WARMING SPICES (INCREASING VAJANA STRENGTH)

Long pepper (Pippali) + Dry ginger (Shunti) + Black pepper (Maricha) — Trikatu formula; increases digestive and metabolic fire (Agni); traditionally considered to increase vitality and prāna; historically used for sexual debility; possible pharmacological basis through piperine bioavailability enhancement of co-administered herbs

IMPORTANT NOTE

For drug-induced DE (SSRI, antipsychotic, alpha-blocker), the primary therapeutic step is medication review and switching — no Ayurvedic preparation addresses the pharmacological receptor blockade of SSRIs or antipsychotics. Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) through its L-DOPA content directly stimulates dopaminergic pathways that mediate ejaculation — this makes it pharmacologically the most relevant botanical for DE. Psychogenic DE requires CBT-based restructuring of masturbatory conditioning (directed toward intercourse-mimicking stimulation) alongside anxiolytic adaptogens. Cabergoline (conventional) and Kapikacchu (Ayurvedic) share a dopaminergic pro-ejaculatory mechanism that is directly relevant to most cases of DE.
diseases.india_specific:
SSRI-INDUCED DE — INDIA CONTEXT

Paroxetine is widely prescribed in India for social anxiety disorder and OCD — both highly prevalent in India; paroxetine has the highest ejaculatory delay among commonly used SSRIs (used therapeutically for PE but causes problematic DE at full doses); non-disclosure of sexual side effects by patients due to cultural shame leads to treatment non-adherence (silent discontinuation of antidepressants); psychiatrists and GPs must proactively educate about sexual side effects and offer switching to Mirtazapine (no ejaculatory side effects), Bupropion (possibly positive for sexual function), or adding Sildenafil/Tadalafil to manage SSRI-related sexual dysfunction

RETROGRADE EJACULATION FROM PROSTATE TREATMENTS

India: BPH (benign prostatic hyperplasia) prevalence rises sharply >50 years; Tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) widely prescribed for LUTS; causes retrograde ejaculation (dry orgasm/absent orgasm) in 4–28% users; patients unaware that dry orgasm is drug effect; cessation → fertility concern (retrograde ejaculation does not impair erectile or orgasmic function but infertility results); clinician should counsel about retrograde ejaculation before starting Tamsulosin, particularly in men of reproductive age

ANEJACULATION POST-PELVIC SURGERY

Retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) for testicular cancer; radical prostatectomy; Miles' procedure (abdomino-perineal resection) — all can damage sympathetic plexus (hypogastric plexus) → dry orgasm/anejaculation; relevant for India's rising oncological surgical volume

DIABETIC AUTONOMIC NEUROPATHY

10–30% of male diabetics develop ejaculatory dysfunction from autonomic neuropathy; retrograde ejaculation or DE; compounding India's diabetes burden with male sexual dysfunction
diseases.important_notes:
DELAYED EJACULATION IS MOST COMMONLY DRUG-INDUCED IN CLINICAL PRACTICE — ALWAYS TAKE A FULL MEDICATION HISTORY: In clinical practice, the single most common cause of acquired DE is iatrogenic — from SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline, fluoxetine) prescribed for depression, anxiety, OCD, or premature ejaculation; from antipsychotics (haloperidol, risperidone, olanzapine) via dopamine blockade and prolactin elevation; and from alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) via sympathetic ejaculatory reflex blockade (leading to retrograde ejaculation). The practical consequence: ANY man presenting with new-onset DE must have a complete medication list reviewed before proceeding with hormonal or neurological investigation. The most efficient and effective intervention is often a medication switch — from paroxetine to escitalopram or mirtazapine; from haloperidol to quetiapine or aripiprazole; from tamsulosin to alfuzosin. This avoids unnecessary investigation cascades.

RETROGRADE EJACULATION MUST BE DISTINGUISHED FROM TRUE DE BY POST-ORGASM URINE TEST: Retrograde ejaculation (semen travels backward into bladder during orgasm) is the most common cause of 'dry' orgasm in clinical practice and is frequently misidentified as anejaculation or DE. Clinical clue: man has orgasmic sensation but produces no (or markedly reduced) ejaculate volume. Confirming test: collect urine immediately after orgasm, centrifuge, look for sperm — the presence of sperm confirms retrograde flow. This is important because retrograde ejaculation has specific treatment implications: (1) Alpha-adrenergic drugs (pseudoephedrine, imipramine, midodrine) can restore antegrade ejaculation by increasing bladder neck coaption; (2) For fertility, sperm retrieval from post-orgasm urine for IUI/IVF is used; (3) Tamsulosin cessation or switching often restores antegrade ejaculation. This simple, inexpensive, non-invasive urine test should be routine in all anejaculation presentations.

IDIOSYNCRATIC MASTURBATORY CONDITIONING IS THE MOST IMPORTANT PSYCHOGENIC CAUSE OF SITUATIONAL DE — REQUIRES CBT RESTRUCTURING: A significant proportion of men with situational DE (can ejaculate with masturbation but not intravaginally) have developed a highly specific, idiosyncratic masturbatory pattern from adolescence — often very rapid stroking, very firm grip, very specific fantasy, or specific body position — that creates an ejaculatory threshold that intercourse simply cannot match. This is classified as Idiosyncratic Masturbatory Conditioning. The psychological treatment involves graduated progressive restructuring: (1) Masturbating in positions and with stimulation intensity that more closely mimics intercourse; (2) Gradual reduction in grip firmness and speed; (3) Sensate focus exercises with partner; (4) Progressive transition from masturbation to intercourse stimulation; (5) Partner involvement to create ejaculatory success experience intravaginally. This requires skilled psychosexual therapist involvement and patience — but is curative. Pharmacological approaches (bupropion, cyproheptadine) complement but do not replace this behavioural approach.
confirmation_tests_curated: Detailed sexual history: onset (lifelong/acquired), situational pattern, masturbation vs intercourse pattern, relationship context, psychological factors; drug history (SSRIs, antipsychotics, alpha-blockers, opioids); surgical history (pelvic, retroperitoneal)

Collect urine immediately after attempted ejaculation or orgasm; centrifuge; sperm in urine post-orgasm = retrograde ejaculation (absence of antegrade ejaculate; sperm in bladder); distinguishes retrograde from true anejaculation or DE

diseases.name: Male Hypogonadism
diseases.tests:
TESTOSTERONE MEASUREMENT

Total testosterone — early morning (7–11am) fasting sample (diurnal variation: testosterone is highest in morning); two separate readings required for diagnosis; LOW = <300 ng/dL (10.4 nmol/L) by endocrine society; many labs use lower cut-off; important: Fitzpatrick population norms may differ

Free testosterone — calculated from total T + SHBG + albumin (Vermeulen formula); important when SHBG altered (obesity → low SHBG → normal total T but adequate free T; liver cirrhosis → high SHBG → low free T despite borderline total T)

GONADOTROPHIN PANEL

FSH, LH — distinguish primary (HIGH FSH/LH) from secondary (LOW/NORMAL FSH/LH)

PROLACTIN — elevated in pituitary adenoma/antipsychotic use; suppresses LH

OESTRADIOL — elevated oestradiol in obesity / gynaecomastia evaluation

ADDITIONAL HORMONES

TSH — hypothyroidism mimics hypogonadism symptoms

SHBG — sex hormone-binding globulin; altered by obesity (reduces), liver disease, ageing

Cortisol morning — Cushing's causes secondary hypogonadism

GENERAL PANEL

HbA1c, Fasting lipids, Liver function — metabolic syndrome associated with hypogonadism

Full Blood Count — haematocrit baseline before testosterone replacement (TRT raises haematocrit — polycythaemia risk)

PSA — prostate specific antigen — before TRT in men ≥40 years; TRT contraindicated in prostate cancer

SEMEN ANALYSIS — if infertility coexists

GENETIC KARYOTYPE

47,XXY Klinefelter — if small testes + high FSH + azoospermia

DXA BONE MINERAL DENSITY

After diagnosis — long-term hypogonadism leads to osteoporosis

PITUITARY MRI

If secondary hypogonadism confirmed (low/normal LH/FSH + low T) → pituitary adenoma, Kallmann bulb hypoplasia
diseases.generic_medicines:
TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY

Testosterone undecanoate injectable 1000 mg IM (Nebido) — 12-weekly injection after loading dose; most convenient; standard of care for long-term male hypogonadism TRT in India

Testosterone enanthate 250 mg IM injection fortnightly — widely available India; effective; more frequent injection than Nebido

Testosterone gel 1% (50 mg/day sachet) — daily application to shoulders/upper arms/abdomen (not genitals); physiological delivery; no injection discomfort; risk: transfer to female partner/children — wash hands, keep clothing on; adjustable dose

Testosterone undecanoate 40 mg capsule (oral, Andriol) — poor bioavailability; least used; option for mild supplementation

CONTRAINDICATIONS TO TRT

Active prostate cancer; breast cancer; haematocrit >54%; untreated severe BPH with significant LUTS; desire for fertility in near future (TRT will suppress spermatogenesis)

FERTILITY PRESERVATION

Clomiphene citrate 25–50 mg EOD (anti-oestrogen; increases GnRH pulsatility → LH/FSH → endogenous testosterone + spermatogenesis) — used in secondary hypogonadism wanting fertility; does NOT suppress spermatogenesis unlike TRT

hCG (human Chorionic Gonadotrophin) injection — acts as LH; maintains intra-testicular testosterone + spermatogenesis; used when TRT causes azoospermia; or first-line secondary hypogonadism with infertility

FSH injections (Recombinant FSH — Puregon, Gonal-F) — alongside hCG for complete spermatogenesis induction in secondary hypogonadism

MONITORING TRT

After 3 months: testosterone (trough), haematocrit, PSA; then 6–12 monthly; aim testosterone 400–700 ng/dL (mid-normal range); haematocrit <54% threshold — if exceeded, venesect or reduce dose

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
TESTOSTERONE-SUPPORTING HERBS

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — gold-standard adaptogen with published RCT data for testosterone elevation; increases testosterone 15–17% in stressed men vs placebo; reduces cortisol (cortisol competitively inhibits testosterone synthesis); 300–600 mg root extract BD

Shilajit (Asphaltum) — fulvic acid mineral complex; RCT published in Andrologia 2015 shows Shilajit 250 mg BD for 90 days increased total testosterone by 23.5%, FSH by 9.4%, LH by 17.7% in healthy men vs placebo; direct action on Leydig cells and HPG axis stimulation

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — steroidal saponins; LH-like activity; small studies show testosterone elevation; body of evidence weaker than Ashwagandha/Shilajit; widely marketed but nuanced evidence

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-Dopa precursor; dopamine → inhibits prolactin → removes prolactin-mediated testosterone suppression; published RCT in infertile oligozoospermic men showing testosterone recovery and improved seminal parameters

Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — adaptogen; androgenic; traditional vajikarana; animal studies show testicular testosterone production improvement; human RCT data limited

SPERMATOGENESIS SUPPORT

Mucuna Pruriens (Kapikacchu) — RCT: improved spermatogenesis in men with idiopathic infertility

Ashwagandha — published data: 30 mg root extract daily for 12 weeks improved sperm count 167%, motility 57%, semen volume 53% (Ambiye et al., 2013)

Triphala — antioxidant; reduces ROS damage to spermatozoa

Amla (Vitamin C) — ascorbic acid cofactor for steroidogenesis; antioxidant; testicular support

IMPORTANT NOTE

Among all Ayurvedic herbs studied for male hypogonadism and fertility, Ashwagandha and Shilajit have the highest quality published evidence (both have peer-reviewed RCTs in reputable andrological journals). Kapikacchu has specific actionable evidence for spermatogenic improvement. Obesity-related secondary hypogonadism should be addressed with weight loss first — aromatase activity in fat tissue converting testosterone to oestradiol is the primary driver; weight loss alone raises testosterone 200–300 ng/dL and may normalise testosterone without any treatment.
diseases.india_specific:
MUMPS ORCHITIS

Mumps orchitis causes primary testicular failure — bilateral orchitis in post-pubertal males causes permanent azoospermia and low testosterone; MMR vaccination (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) — India's MMR uptake is improving but incomplete, particularly in rural areas; mumps continues to cause orchitis-related hypogonadism and infertility in India; any male presenting with infertility + small firm testes + history of childhood/adolescent parotid swelling should have mumps orchitis considered in India

ANABOLIC STEROID EPIDEMIC INDIA

Gym culture across metro and tier-2 India cities has driven dramatic uptake of anabolic steroids (testosterone, stanozolol, nandrolone); mostly unregulated counterfeit products; young men (18–35) using for aesthetics without medical supervision; risks: HPG axis suppression (takes 12–24 months recovery; some never recover fertility), testicular atrophy, cardiovascular risk (LVH, atherosclerosis), hepatotoxicity (oral AAS), psychiatric effects ('roid rage'); addressing this requires counselling by physicians encountering young males with hypogonadism

UNDISCLOSED OPIOID USE

India has growing prescription opioid misuse (tramadol, codeine, tapentadol) and illicit opioid use; opioids suppress GnRH pulsatility → low LH/FSH → secondary hypogonadism; patient may not disclose; opioid-induced hypogonadism presents identically to pituitary disease; always ask about pain killers and cough syrups (codeine)

TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT AVAILABILITY

Testosterone undecanoate injection 1000 mg (Nebido) — available India; 12-weekly injection; most compliant regimen; Testosterone enanthate injection 250 mg fortnightly — available; Testosterone gel 1% sachets — available
diseases.important_notes:
TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY SUPPRESSES SPERMATOGENESIS — CRITICAL FOR INDIAN MEN WANTING FERTILITY: Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT), while effectively treating symptoms of androgen deficiency, shuts down the pituitary-testicular axis through negative feedback on LH secretion. Without LH, intra-testicular testosterone production collapses and spermatogenesis ceases — often producing azoospermia within 3–6 months of TRT. In India, where family continuity is deeply important and men may be treating hypogonadism in the 25–40 age group, this is a critical counselling point. If hypogonadal men want to father children now or in the foreseeable future: (1) Do NOT start TRT; (2) Use HCG injections instead (acts as LH surrogate → maintains intra-testicular testosterone + spermatogenesis); (3) Or use clomiphene citrate (stimulates endogenous LH/FSH → stimulates endogenous testosterone + sperm). After fertility achieved, TRT can be reconsidered. All hypogonadal men under 45 must be explicitly asked about fertility desires before TRT begins.

ANABOLIC STEROID-INDUCED HYPOGONADISM IN INDIA'S GYM CULTURE — A GROWING SILENT EPIDEMIC: Young Indian men (18–35 years) attending commercial gyms in metro cities are increasingly using anabolic-androgenic steroids (testosterone, nandrolone, stanozolol, boldenone) for aesthetic muscle gain — often purchased through gym networks or online without medical supervision. The result: profound suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, testicular atrophy, azoospermia, and long-term secondary hypogonadism. Recovery of spermatogenesis and natural testosterone production can take 12–24 months after cessation and may never fully recover after prolonged high-dose use. These men present to andrologists or urologists complaining of infertility, low libido, or 'feeling flat' post-cycle. Management: (1) Complete cessation of exogenous steroids; (2) hCG 3x/week + FSH injection for spermatogenesis restoration; (3) Clomiphene or Tamoxifen for HPG axis restart; (4) 12–24 months patience; (5) Testosterone replacement only if HPG axis confirmed permanently failed.

LATE-ONSET HYPOGONADISM IN INDIA — DISTINGUISHED FROM AGEING AND DEPRESSION BEFORE TREATMENT: As Indian men age (>45–50 years), symptoms of testosterone decline (fatigue, low libido, depressed mood, reduced vitality, muscle loss) can be attributed to normal ageing, worsening diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or depression — all of which can co-exist. The clinical diagnosis of Late-Onset Hypogonadism (LOH) requires: (1) Biochemical confirmation: total testosterone <300 ng/dL on two separate early morning samples; AND (2) Clinical symptoms of androgen deficiency. The AMS (Aging Males' Symptoms) scale is a validated questionnaire useful in Indian practice. Critically, testosterone should NOT be started empirically in men with borderline testosterone without symptoms, or in men whose symptoms are explained by depression, diabetes, or obesity alone. The sequence: confirm biochemistry first; address metabolic causes (obesity, diabetes); trial testosterone only in confirmed deficiency with persistent symptoms after metabolic optimisation.

diseases.name: Female Sexual Interest Arousal
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL ASSESSMENT — DSFS / FSFI QUESTIONNAIRE

Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) — 19-item validated questionnaire; 6 domains: desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, satisfaction, pain; total score <26.55 = Female Sexual Dysfunction; standard research and clinical tool

Derogatis Interview for Sexual Function (DISF) — alternative structured interview

HORMONAL PANEL

Total and free testosterone — low free testosterone → FSIAD in peri/post-menopausal women; < 25 ng/dL total testosterone = low FSIAD territory

SHBG — elevated in OCP users; reduces free testosterone

FSH, LH, Estradiol — menopausal transition assessment

Prolactin — elevated: breastfeeding, pituitary adenoma, antipsychotics → HPG suppression

TSH — hypothyroidism causes reduced libido

Fasting glucose, HbA1c, Vitamin D

GYNAECOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT

Pelvic examination — atrophic vaginitis; evidence of GSM (genito-urinary syndrome of menopause); clitoral phimosis; vulvar dystrophy

SMEAR (Cervical cancer screening) — opportunistic if due

Vaginal pH — post-menopausal > 5.0 (atrophic) vs premenopausal <4.5

PSYCHOLOGICAL SCREENING

PHQ-9 depression score; GAD-7 anxiety; Trauma screening (PTSD Checklist)

PARTNER ASSESSMENT / RELATIONSHIP MEASURE

Dyadic Adjustment Scale / Relationship satisfaction assessment — partner factor
diseases.generic_medicines:
ADDRESS MODIFIABLE CAUSE FIRST

Switch SSRI to Bupropion or Mirtazapine if clinically appropriate — significantly improves FSIAD caused by SSRIs; Addyi (Flibanserin) addresses SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction specifically but not licensed India yet

Reconsider OCP formulation — switch to progestogen-only pill or non-hormonal contraception if OCP-induced FSIAD suspected; testosterone suppression from SHBG may reverse

HORMONAL

Testosterone therapy (off-label) — low-dose transdermal testosterone 150–300 mcg/day; approved in Australia and UK (Intrinsa patch — discontinued; off-label systemic testosterone gel at 1/10th male dose); best evidence for post-menopausal FSIAD; improves desire, arousal, orgasm, sexual wellbeing; monitoring: testosterone level + safety screen (virilisation, lipids, liver); Indian practice: off-label use of male testosterone preparation at low dose

Oestrogen therapy — for GSM (Genitourinary Syndrome of Menopause) vulvovaginal atrophy component; vaginal oestrogen cream (cream or pessary — Premarin, Ovestin) — local; minimal systemic absorption; safe even post-ER+ breast cancer (ASCO updated guidelines 2023); significantly improves lubrication, dyspareunia, FSIAD

CENTRAL ACTING

Flibanserin (Addyi) 100 mg HS — FDA-approved for premenopausal FSIAD in USA; 5-HT1A agonist + 5-HT2A antagonist + D4 antagonist; modulates dopamine/norepinephrine balance in PFC; modest effect (1.5 additional satisfying sexual events/month); not currently licensed in India

Bremelanotide (Vyleesi) — melanocortin receptor agonist; injected subcutaneously before sex; approved USA for premenopausal FSIAD; not India-licensed

PSYCHOSEXUAL THERAPY

CBT-based sexual therapy; sensate focus; desire-building exercises; partner communication; addressing spectatoring; trauma therapy if applicable — FIRST-LINE alongside or before pharmacotherapy

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
FEMALE VAJIKARANA / RASAYANA

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — pre-eminent female reproductive tonic in Ayurveda; phytoestrogens (steroidal saponins); adaptogen; reduces oestrogen deficiency symptoms (vaginal dryness, reduced arousal) in perimenopausal women; stimulates reproductive health; published evidence for menopausal symptom improvement; 500 mg–2 g powder/day or root extract BD

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — female FSIAD RCT (Dongre et al., 2015 BioMedResearch International): 300 mg root extract BD for 8 weeks in 50 women with FSIAD → significant improvement in all FSFI domains (desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, satisfaction) vs placebo; p<0.001; directly validated for female FSIAD; reduces cortisol and anxiety (psychogenic inhibitory mechanisms)

Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — female reproductive adaptogen; improves moisture and arousal in ayurvedic practice; traditionally prescribed for female sexual weakness ('kshaya') similar to FSIAD

Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) — female reproductive tonic; anti-inflammatory; genitourinary

SHATAVARI GHRITA / COMBINATIONS

Shatavari Ghrita — clarified butter with Shatavari; traditional female reproductive preparation; lubricating; oestrogenic; vaginal application possible for dryness

Phalaghrita — classical compound for female reproductive health in Ashtanga Hridaya

Chandrprabha Vati — genitourinary tonic; multiple herbs

TOPICAL CARE

Sesame oil (Til tail) — clitoral and vulval daily massage; moisturising; anti-inflammatory; traditional Abhyanga for perineal health

Rose water — cooling vaginal wash; anti-inflammatory; maintains pH

IMPORTANT NOTE

Ashwagandha is the highest-evidence Ayurvedic herb for female FSIAD with a published double-blind RCT specifically in the FSIAD population showing significant FSFI improvement. Shatavari has the strongest historical and pharmacological basis for female sexual health across reproductive life stages. However, the most important factors in FSIAD treatment are addressing relationship quality, psychological safety, trauma history, and communication with partner — no herb replaces psychosexual therapy as the primary intervention.
diseases.india_specific:
SILENCE AND STIGMA

Female sexual dysfunction in India is vastly under-reported due to cultural silence around female sexuality; women rarely approach doctors voluntarily with FSIAD; typically presents within broader marital conflict complaints, 'not interested in husband', 'feeling cold'; only recently discussed in Indian medical education; GPs rarely ask about sexual function in female consultations

ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILL (OCP) — SHBG MECHANISM

Ethinylestradiol-containing combined OCPs increase hepatic synthesis of SHBG (sex hormone-binding globulin); elevated SHBG binds free testosterone → reduces biologically active testosterone → impairs sexual desire and genital arousal in susceptible women; Androgen-index suppression can persist even after stopping OCP (post-pill FSIAD reported); Progestogen-only pill has less SHBG effect; India: OCP use rising in urban women; FSIAD from OCP under-recognised

PCOS AND HYPERANDROGENISM PARADOX

Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (extremely prevalent in India — 20–25% of reproductive women) — high androgens might be expected to protect libido but insulin resistance, overweight stigma, irregular cycles, fertility anxiety, anti-androgen treatment (Spironolactone suppresses testosterone) → net reduced sexual function; PCOS and FSIAD co-occurrence in India is clinically significant

POST-PARTUM FSIAD — UNADDRESSED IN INDIA

Post-partum physiological libido suppression: prolactin from breastfeeding suppresses LH/FSH → oestrogen deficiency + low testosterone → vaginal dryness + FSIAD for duration of breastfeeding; 12–24 months; Indian culture celebrates extended breastfeeding but rarely addresses post-partum sexual health; FSIAD post-partum affects reportedly 40–80% of Indian women
diseases.important_notes:
FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION IN INDIA IS A SILENT EPIDEMIC REQUIRING ACTIVE CLINICAL ENQUIRY: Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder affects an estimated 30–40% of women globally at some point in their lives, with India likely having comparable or higher rates given the additional psychological burden of arranged marriage, post-partum silence, trauma, and cultural shame. In Indian clinical practice, FSIAD is almost never voluntarily disclosed by patients — GPs, gynaecologists, and psychiatrists must actively create a safe space by routinely asking about sexual wellbeing during consultations, particularly during post-natal visits, OCP prescriptions, SSRI initiation, and menopause clinic consultations. A simple validated single-item screen: 'Over the past month, have you felt satisfied with your sex life?' opens the consultation without being threatening.

ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILLS CAN CAUSE FSIAD THROUGH SHBG ELEVATION — COUNSEL EVERY WOMAN STARTING OCP: Combined oral contraceptive pills containing ethinylestradiol elevate hepatic SHBG synthesis dramatically — SHBG binds testosterone, reducing biologically active free testosterone. Free testosterone is the primary driver of sexual desire in women of all ages. Multiple studies show OCP users have significantly lower FSFI desire subdomain scores vs non-OCP users. The clinical message: every woman starting a combined OCP should be counselled that reduced sexual desire can occur as a side effect; that it is reversible with OCP discontinuation; and that alternative contraception (progestogen-only pill, levonorgestrel IUD, copper IUD, barrier methods) should be offered if FSIAD develops. The copper IUD provides effective non-hormonal contraception for women with OCP-associated FSIAD — very relevant in India where copper-T is widely available in JSY schemes.

RESPONSIVE DESIRE IN WOMEN IS NORMAL AND SHOULD NOT BE LABELLED AS DISORDER WITHOUT DISTRESS: Basson's non-linear female sexual response model (2001) established that many women with satisfying sexual lives primarily experience responsive desire (arousal triggered by stimulation, context, and intimacy) rather than spontaneous desire (arising without external trigger). This is not a dysfunction — it is a different but normal desire architecture. FSIAD diagnosis requires PERSONAL DISTRESS as a mandatory criterion. A woman who has low spontaneous desire but becomes aroused and satisfied with intercourse once initiated, and is not distressed by this pattern, does NOT have FSIAD. This distinction matters enormously in India where marital pressure to 'want' sex (spontaneous desire model) may lead over-pathologising of responsive-type women who are actually functional. Clinical role: normalise responsive desire as equally valid; diagnose FSIAD only when the woman herself is distressed.

diseases.name: Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disord
diseases.tests:
SEXUAL DESIRE INVENTORY (SDI-2)

Validated 14-item questionnaire assessing dyadic and solitary sexual desire; used for research and monitoring treatment response in HSDD

IIEF-5 (FOR MALES)

Specifically the desire domain (Q7 and Q8 of full IIEF-15); scores 1–5 per question; low scores confirm desire deficiency

HORMONAL PANEL — MALE

Serum testosterone (early morning, two separate readings) — the most important; total T <300 ng/dL = low

Prolactin — hyperprolactinaemia (pituitary adenoma, antipsychotics, dopamine antagonist drugs, hypothyroidism)

FSH, LH — distinguish primary vs secondary hypogonadism

TSH — hypothyroidism

HORMONAL PANEL — FEMALE

Testosterone (total and free), SHBG

FSH, LH, Estradiol — menopausal status

Prolactin

TSH

PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT

PHQ-9 (depression) — major depression causes HSDD

GAD-7 (anxiety)

Relationship assessment — Dyadic Adjustment Scale

DRUG HISTORY REVIEW

Complete review for SSRI, antipsychotic, opioid, OCP, anti-androgen medications

PITUITARY MRI

If prolactin elevated (>100 mcg/L) or FSH/LH unexpectedly low → pituitary adenoma
diseases.generic_medicines:
MALE HSDD

Testosterone replacement (if confirmed hypogonadism) — same as male hypogonadism sheet: testosterone gel/injection brings testosterone to mid-normal range → improves libido within 3–6 weeks

Cabergoline 0.25–1 mg twice weekly — for hyperprolactinaemia; dopamine agonist; reduces prolactin → restores LH/FSH → testosterone → libido; highly effective for prolactinoma-associated HSDD; also increases sexual drive directly via D2 receptors in limbic system

Bupropion 150–300 mg OD — dopaminergic/noradrenergic; pro-libido; used for SSRI-induced HSDD augmentation; independent mild aphrodisiac effect through norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibition

Switch from SSRI to Wellbutrin (Bupropion) if SSRI-induced HSDD — treats depression without sexual dysfunction

FEMALE HSDD

Low-dose testosterone — transdermal 150–300 mcg/day (off-label India); best evidence in post-menopausal FSIAD/HSDD; Intrinsa patch (300 mcg/day) has 5 RCTs positive; current EMA/NICE guidelines support off-label use; apply 1/10th male testosterone gel dose

Vaginal oestrogen — if atrophic vaginitis contributing to painful intercourse → desire avoidance; local vaginal oestrogen cream/pessary breaks pain-avoidance cycle

Flibanserin 100 mg HS — FDA-approved for premenopausal female HSDD; central 5-HT1A agonist + 5-HT2A antagonist; modestly effective; not India-licensed

Treat depression — antidepressant selection: switch to Bupropion or Mirtazapine if depression driving HSDD; these are less HSDD-inducing than SSRIs

PSYCHOSEXUAL THERAPY

Couples therapy — relationship quality is most powerful predictor of desire in women; emotional intimacy restoration; communication counselling

Mindfulness-based sex therapy — Lori Brotto RCTs; 4-session mindfulness program; highly effective for HSDD in women; available via online programmes

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
VAJIKARANA FOR DESIRE

Ashwagandha — reduces HPA axis cortisol hypersecretion; enhances dopaminergic (desire) vs serotonergic (inhibition) balance; clinical evidence for both male and female sexual desire improvement; 300–600 mg BD

Shatavari — female reproductive tonic; phytoestrogenic; reduces post-menopausal HSDD symptoms; lubricating; 1–2 g powder day or extract 500 mg BD

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA → dopamine → directly mediates sexual motivation in mesolimbic system; both male and female desire enhancement; 500 mg BD

Safed Musli — adaptogenic; androgenic support; both sexes; traditional vajikarana

SAFFRON (KESAR, CROCUS SATIVUS)

Saffron 30 mg/day — well-conducted RCTs demonstrating SSRI-related sexual dysfunction reversal including desire improvement; saffron modulates dopamine/serotonin in prefrontal cortex; specific India-relevant as saffron is culturally familiar, widely available, easily supplemented as 'kesar milk' (saffron in warm milk — traditional Indian preparation that coincidentally delivers the 30 mg daily dose); safe; well-tolerated

APHRODISIAC SPICES

Cardamom (Elachi) + Clove (Laung) + Nutmeg (Jaiphal) — traditional Indian Unani aphrodisiacs; essential oils; CNS excitatory; limited formal evidence but traditional use basis; pleasant sensory adjunct to intimacy context

COMPOUND FORMULATIONS

Majun Muqawwi (Unani compound) — traditional aphrodisiac combination; used in Unani practice India for HSDD in both sexes

Vrishya Vati — vajikarana multi-herb tablet; increasing desire in both sexes

IMPORTANT NOTE

Saffron (Kesar) is emerging as the most evidence-supported botanical for SSRI-related sexual desire dysfunction — the RCT data is solid and culturally speaking in India, Kesar milk (saffron 2–4 threads in warm milk) is a traditional vajikarana preparation that delivers approximately the therapeutic dose shown effective in clinical trials. Ashwagandha for cortisol reduction directly addresses stress-driven HSDD. Cabergoline (conventional) and Kapikacchu (Ayurvedic) share dopaminergic mechanism for desire enhancement.
diseases.india_specific:
CULTURAL SEXUAL DESIRE NORMS

India's culture around male sexuality includes an implicit expectation of constant male sexual desire and availability; HSDD in men is profoundly culturally discordant → extreme distress, shame, and delay in consultation; men may instead attribute the problem to 'weakness', 'physical exhaustion', 'heat in the body', or 'masturbation as youth' before accepting a medical cause

POST-MENOPAUSAL WOMEN'S SEXUALITY INDIA

Indian post-menopausal women (~200 million menopausal women in India by 2025) are rarely offered sexual health counselling because cultural assumptions treat post-menopausal sexuality as irrelevant or inappropriate; gynaecologists focus on vasomotor symptoms and bone health; sexual health discussion is largely absent; this leaves potentially millions of post-menopausal Indian women with untreated HSDD, GSM, and dyspareunia without any medical support

ANTIDEPRESSANT BURDEN

India has among the highest untreated depression/anxiety rates globally; SSRI prescribing has risen dramatically; SSRIs (paroxetine, escitalopram, sertraline) are all potent causes of HSDD in both sexes through serotonin-mediated dopamine suppression; sexual side effects from SSRIs are under-disclosed by Indian patients due to cultural shame and must be actively elicited at every SSRI review

VEDIC CULTURAL SUPPRESSION

Some Indian spiritual/religious traditions (brahmacharya, vairagya) cultivate deliberate celibacy and sexual desire suppression; this is culturally valid and should not be pathologised; HSDD is only a disorder when the individual or their partner experiences distress from it
diseases.important_notes:
PROLACTINOMA IS AN IMPORTANT CORRECTABLE CAUSE OF HSDD IN BOTH SEXES — PROLACTIN SHOULD BE ROUTINELY MEASURED: Hyperprolactinaemia (serum prolactin >25 mcg/L in women; >20 mcg/L in men) causes HSDD in both sexes through GnRH suppression → reduced LH/FSH → testosterone deficiency → impaired libido. Common causes: prolactinoma (benign pituitary adenoma — most common pituitary tumour), antipsychotic medications (especially haloperidol, risperidone — D2 blockade → prolactin disinhibition), dopamine antagonist anti-emetics (metoclopramide, domperidone widely used in India), hypothyroidism. In India: metoclopramide (Perinorm) and domperidone are among the most prescribed gastroenterological drugs — long-term use can cause prolactin elevation and HSDD. Correcting hyperprolactinaemia with cabergoline dopamine agonist reliably restores sexual desire and testosterone within weeks — this is one of the most cleanly reversible causes of HSDD.

ANTIDEPRESSANT-INDUCED HSDD REQUIRES PROACTIVE COUNSELLING AT PRESCRIPTION — NOT JUST AT COMPLAINT: SSRIs cause HSDD in 30–70% of both male and female users depending on the specific SSRI and dose. The serotonergic inhibition of dopaminergic mesolimbic pathways (which mediate sexual motivation and reward) is the mechanism. In India, where sexual side effect disclosure is culturally difficult, many patients silently discontinue SSRIs because of intolerable HSDD — leading to undertreated depression and SSRI discontinuation syndrome. The correct approach: (1) Counsel every patient before starting SSRI that sexual desire changes may occur; (2) Review sexual function at every SSRI follow-up visit; (3) If HSDD develops: reduce dose; add bupropion augmentation (dopaminergic pro-libido); switch to bupropion or mirtazapine; or add Sildenafil for arousal deficit component. The correct time to address this is at prescription, not after years of silent suffering.

DESIRE IS THE MEETING POINT OF NEUROBIOLOGY AND RELATIONSHIP — TREAT BOTH DIMENSIONS: HSDD is rarely explained by a single biological aetiology alone. In most real-world presentations, there is a biopsychosocial intersection: low testosterone combined with relationship conflict and depression combined with medication side effect. Effective treatment addresses all three domains simultaneously: (1) Biological — hormonal correction (testosterone, cabergoline for prolactin), medication optimisation; (2) Psychological — depression and anxiety treatment, mindfulness, CBT addressing anhedonia and asexual cognitions; (3) Relational — couples therapy to restore emotional intimacy, communication, conflict resolution, and partner sensitivity. Single-domain treatments (testosterone only, or CBT only) are substantially less effective than integrated biopsychosocial treatment. This requires either a well-trained sexual medicine physician or coordinated care between psychiatrist/endocrinologist + couples therapist — a referral pathway that most Indian healthcare settings do not yet have formalised.

diseases.name: Female Orgasmic Disorder
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL ASSESSMENT — FSFI ORGASM SUBSCALE

Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) orgasm domain: 3 questions (Q10, Q11, Q12); score out of 6; <3.6 = orgasmic dysfunction; part of full FSFI assessment

SEXUAL HISTORY

Lifetime orgasmic history: has she ever experienced orgasm by any means?; Current pattern: masturbation vs partner; clitoral vs vaginal; with/without vibrator; medication history (SSRIs, antipsychotics); trauma history sensitively; relationship satisfaction

NEUROLOGICAL

Sensory testing of perineum and clitoris: vibration threshold (biothesiometer); bulbocavernosus reflex; pudendal nerve conduction — if neurogenic FOD suspected (SCI, MS, post-pelvic surgery, diabetes)

HORMONAL

Serum testosterone, SHBG, free testosterone — post-menopausal women with reduced clitoral sensitivity

Oestradiol — post-menopausal atrophy; clitoral changes with oestrogen deficiency

Prolactin — elevated prolactin → desire and arousal deficit contributing to orgasmic difficulty

TSH

DOPPLER CLITORAL BLOOD FLOW

Duplex Doppler of clitoral artery — reduced clitoral blood flow in vasculogenic FOD; similar to penile Doppler for ED; available at specialist centres; post-menopausal oestrogen deficiency reduces clitoral vascularity

PELVIC FLOOR ASSESSMENT

Weak pelvic floor (cannot contract bulbocavernosus/ischiocavernosus) → weak orgasmic contractions; assess via perineometry or pelvic floor physiotherapy assessment
diseases.generic_medicines:
DIRECTED MASTURBATION — GOLD STANDARD PSYCHOSEXUAL INTERVENTION

Directed masturbation program (Heiman & LoPiccolo): Step 1 — genital self-examination with mirror; Step 2 — exploration of genital sensations without pressure for orgasm; Step 3 — manual clitoral stimulation with or without personal lubricant; Step 4 — introduce vibrator if insufficient stimulation; Step 5 — orgasm transfer to partner setting; completion rate: 80–90% orgasm in previously anorgasmic women with lifelong FOD; this is the most evidence-based treatment for FOD

VIBRATORS

Clitoral vibrators — provide higher intensity direct clitoral stimulation than manual stimulation alone; 80–95% of anorgasmic women can achieve orgasm with vibrator; normalised tool in sex therapy; available in India online; recommended in evidence-based FOD treatment protocols

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Bupropion 150–300 mg OD — reverses SSRI-induced anorgasmia; pro-orgasmic dopaminergic mechanism; also direct effect on sexual arousal independently of SSRI use

Cyproheptadine 4 mg before intercourse — on-demand serotonin antagonist; reverses SSRI-induced anorgasmia acutely

Switch from Paroxetine (highest anorgasmia) → Escitalopram or Bupropion

Low-dose testosterone (off-label) — for post-menopausal reduced clitoral sensitivity and anorgasmia; increases clitoral sensitivity and orgasm intensity

LOCAL TOPICAL

Alprostadil (PGE1) topical clitoral cream — vasodilator; increases clitoral blood flow and sensitivity; available globally (FEMPROX); limited India availability

Personal lubricant (silicon-based or water-based hyaluronic acid) — reduces friction, increases comfort, enhances sensation; first step for situational FOD with inadequate vaginal lubrication

PELVIC FLOOR STRENGTHENING

Kegel exercises (PFE) — strengthens pubococcygeus and bulbocavernosus muscles; increases orgasmic intensity and frequency in women with weak pelvic floor; 100 contractions/day × 6–8 weeks
