diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
AROUSAL-ENHANCING HERBS

Ashwagandha — reduces cortisol; clinical RCT (Dongre 2015): 8-week Ashwagandha in women with FSIAD showed significant improvement in FSFI orgasm domain scores; also reduces spectatoring anxiety through cortisol reduction allowing PFC suppression necessary for orgasm; 300 mg BD

Shatavari — oestrogenic; increases clitoral sensitivity via oestrogen receptor activity; post-menopausal orgasm improvement; lubricating (reduces dyspareunia that inhibits orgasm); 500 mg BD

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — dopaminergic via L-DOPA; dopamine disinhibits orgasmic reflex; both desire and orgasm improved

TOPICAL AROUSAL ENHANCEMENT

Clary Sage essential oil (diluted in carrier) — topical application lower abdomen/clitoral area; contains phytoestrogens; vasodilatory; traditionally used as female arousal enhancer; safe externally

Sesame oil (Til tail) — daily vulval massage; traditional Abhyanga for perineal area; increases local blood flow; maintains tissue health

Rose (Gulab) — rose essential oil in carrier oil; traditional erotic arousal enhancer; aromatherapy component to sexual context

RELAXATION AND ANTI-SPECTATORING

Brahmi — anxiolytic; reduces spectatoring (the principal psychological block to orgasm); GABA modulation

Jatiphala (Nutmeg) — CNS relaxant; reduces performance anxiety; traditionally used to enhance responsiveness; small dose in warm milk

IMPORTANT NOTE

The most important clinical intervention for FOD remains psychoeducation (clitoral anatomy, normal female sexual response, directed masturbation instruction) + directed masturbation exercises — achieves orgasm in 80–90% of previously anorgasmic women. In India, this requires the clinician to actively provide frank sexual anatomy education and normalise self-exploration as a therapeutic technique -- cultural desensitisation is part of the treatment. Ashwagandha has the highest-quality Ayurvedic evidence for female orgasm improvement. Bupropion remains the pharmacological gold standard for SSRI-induced anorgasmia. Vibrator-assisted stimulation is evidence-based and should be recommended without shame.
diseases.india_specific:
CULTURAL SILENCE AROUND FEMALE ORGASM

Female sexual pleasure — particularly orgasm — is culturally not acknowledged in Indian mainstream culture; marriage guides (Grihastha dharma texts) focus on procreation, not female pleasure; many Indian women have never received information about their own sexual anatomy (clitoris location, function) or about the fact that direct clitoral stimulation is required for orgasm in most women; this fundamental education deficit is the largest modifiable barrier to FOD treatment in India

FEMALE GENITAL MUTILATION/CUTTING (FGC)

India: FGC (specifically female circumcision = khatna) practiced in Bohra Muslim community (2–3 million women); Type I (clitoral hood removal/partial clitoridectomy); significantly reduces clitoral sensitivity and orgasmic capacity; illegal in India (POCSO applies to minors); affects adult women who underwent FGC as children; India-specific clinical consideration that must be sensitively addressed in Bohra patients presenting with FOD

TRAUMA HISTORY

India: childhood sexual abuse 24% prevalence women (LANCET 2017); sexual trauma is a major psychogenic cause of FOD; requires trauma-informed therapy before standard FOD behavioural interventions; CBT-based trauma processing + targeted sex therapy

SPECTATORING — MODERN INDIA CONTEXT

Young Indian women exposed to pornographic standards through smartphones; unrealistic expectations of female sexual response from pornography (instant, performative orgasms); leads to spectatoring (monitoring oneself instead of experiencing); reduces orgasmic likelihood; specific modern India context requires psychoeducation about authentic (not performative) female sexual response
diseases.important_notes:
MOST WOMEN REQUIRE DIRECT CLITORAL STIMULATION FOR ORGASM — THIS IS PHYSIOLOGY, NOT DYSFUNCTION: A landmark global survey (Archives of Sexual Behaviour, 2017, n=52,000) found that only 18% of women reliably achieve orgasm from penetrative intercourse alone; 74% require direct clitoral stimulation (manual, oral, or vibrator) concurrent with or independent of intercourse. This is not dysfunction — it is normal female anatomy. The clitoris is a large internal and external organ whose external component (glans clitoris) requires direct stimulation to reach orgasm threshold. In India, the vast majority of heterosexual sexual encounters are penetration-focused without adequate clitoral stimulation; partners are not educated about anatomical reality; women are not educated about self-stimulation. The consequence: situational FOD is epidemic not because women are dysfunctional, but because the sexual technique used is anatomically insufficient. The clinical intervention: psychoeducation about clitoral anatomy; encouraging communicating stimulation preferences to partner; normalising concurrent clitoral stimulation during intercourse.

SSRI-INDUCED ANORGASMIA IS EXTREMELY COMMON AND MUST BE ADDRESSED AT PRESCRIPTION: SSRIs cause inability to orgasm (anorgasmia) or markedly delayed orgasm in 30–60% of users. This occurs because serotonin inhibits dopamine release in the mesolimbic reward pathway — dopamine is required for the final release of the orgasmic reflex. Paroxetine has the highest rate; escitalopram and sertraline intermediate risk. In India, SSRI-induced anorgasmia goes undisclosed because asking a doctor about inability to orgasm is culturally extremely difficult. The prescriber must: (1) Mention orgasmic side effects at SSRI prescription; (2) Ask about them at every review; (3) If present: reduce dose; add bupropion 150 mg OD (dopaminergic reversal); switch to mirtazapine (neutral) or bupropion; use cyproheptadine 4 mg on-demand as serotonin antagonist before intercourse. Untreated SSRI anorgasmia leads to SSRI discontinuation by patients who prefer orgasm to antidepressant treatment — a potentially medically dangerous trade-off.

DIRECTED MASTURBATION IS THE EVIDENCE-BASED TREATMENT FOR LIFELONG ANORGASMIA — REQUIRES CULTURALLY SENSITIVE DELIVERY IN INDIA: Directed masturbation programs (Heiman & LoPiccolo protocol, 1970s; since validated in multiple international RCTs) achieve orgasm in 80–90% of previously anorgasmic women through a graded programme of genital self-exploration and self-stimulation. In India, this treatment encounters two specific challenges: (1) Cultural prohibition strongly discourages female masturbation as shameful; many Indian women have never touched their own genitals; (2) Clinical delivery in a conservative primary care or gynaecology setting is extremely challenging — the clinician must create a private, non-judgmental space, use anatomically accurate diagrams, and frame self-exploration as therapeutic — not shameful. Specialist sex therapists and clinical psychologists with sexual medicine training are scarce in India. However, the psychoeducation component (clitoral anatomy, directed masturbation rationale) can be delivered by a sensitive trained GP or gynaecologist as a therapeutic conversation supported by appropriate educational materials. This is one of the most effective, side-effect-free, permanent treatments in sexual medicine.
confirmation_tests_curated: Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) orgasm domain: 3 questions (Q10, Q11, Q12); score out of 6; <3.6 = orgasmic dysfunction; part of full FSFI assessment

Lifetime orgasmic history: has she ever experienced orgasm by any means?; Current pattern: masturbation vs partner; clitoral vs vaginal; with/without vibrator; medication history (SSRIs, antipsychotics); trauma history sensitively; relationship satisfaction

diseases.name: Vaginismus
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Vaginismus is diagnosed clinically by: history of penetration failure + examination findings

ATTEMPTED GYNAECOLOGICAL EXAMINATION

Pelvic exam — involuntary levator ani contraction visible/palpable on attempted speculum or digital insertion; diagnostic; examiner notes involuntary vaginal closure; must be performed sensitively with patient control at all times; a normal exam finding except for the muscle contraction

PAIN MAPPING

Cotton tip mapping of vestibule and vaginal opening: Q-tip test — touch at different clock positions around the vestibule; identifies vestibulodynia if localised tenderness (especially 5-7 o'clock) which may coexist with or cause secondary vaginismus

PELVIC FLOOR PHYSIOTHERAPY ASSESSMENT

Perineometry — measures resting pelvic floor tone and voluntary contraction/relaxation; demonstrates hypertonicity; guides physiotherapy

PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT

PTSD Checklist (PCL-5) — sexual trauma screening; PHQ-9 (depression); GAD-7 (anxiety); relationship satisfaction; sexual guilt inventory

ANATOMICAL EXCLUSION

Examination for: intact hymen (rare imperforate hymen); vaginal septa; dermatological conditions (lichen sclerosus — causing introital fibrosis); infections (vulvovaginal candidiasis contributing)

MRI PELVIS (RARELY)

If structural cause suspected (Mullerian anomaly, vaginal agenesis, large hymenal remnant)
diseases.generic_medicines:
VAGINAL DILATOR THERAPY — CORNERSTONE TREATMENT

Graduated vaginal dilators (plastic or silicone; set of 4–6 progressively sized dilators, smallest = finger-sized); patient-directed insertion practice starting smallest → progressive increase; combined with pelvic floor relaxation exercises; success rate 80–90% with guided programme; available in India online; key: patient is in full control of insertion — not partner or examiner

Step programme: (1) Pelvic floor relaxation exercises (daily Kegel − contract AND RELEASE); (2) Mirror genital self-examination and desensitisation; (3) Insert smallest dilator with personal lubricant in relaxed position; (4) Progress size over weeks-months; (5) Transition to partner digital, then penile

PELVIC FLOOR PHYSIOTHERAPY

Pelvic floor physiotherapist (specialist) — biofeedback-assisted pelvic floor rehabilitation; manual internal myofascial release; trigger point therapy for levator ani hypertonicity; most effective when available; increasingly available in metro India

Pelvic floor biofeedback (electromyography) — real-time visual of pelvic floor muscle tension; helps women learn to relax pelvic floor on demand

BOTULINUM TOXIN INJECTION

Botulinum toxin A (10–100 units) injected into levator ani / pubococcygeus under anaesthesia; relaxes muscle spasm for 3–4 months; allows dilator use; particularly for severe or treatment-resistant vaginismus; performed under local or general anaesthesia; under-utilised in India; available at specialist centres

TOPICAL

Lidocaine 2–4% jelly — topical anaesthetic; applied to introitus before dilator insertion during treatment programme; reduces sensation during initial desensitisation; NOT chronic treatment

Topical oestrogen — if post-menopausal atrophic vaginal changes contributing (secondary vaginismus in older women)

PSYCHOLOGICAL

CBT-based sex therapy — addressing sexual guilt, anticipatory anxiety, fear conditioning; MUST accompany physical dilator therapy; particularly important for culturally-conditioned vaginismus India

EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing) — for trauma-associated vaginismus; recommended when sexual trauma identified

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
RELAXATION AND ANTI-ANXIETY

Ashwagandha — reduces cortisol and adrenergic hyperarousal; reduces the anticipatory anxiety that triggers levator ani spasm; 300–600 mg BD; adaptogen; helps normalise HPA axis that drives the pelvic floor guarding reflex

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — anxiolytic; GABAergic; directly reduces neurological anxiety that underlies vaginismus reflex; 300 mg BD

Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — Indian valerian; GABA modulation; potent anxiolytic; traditional nervine relaxant; useful for anticipatory anxiety

Valerian root — GABA-A receptor potentiation; mild muscle relaxant; sedative; helps with pelvic floor hypertonicity through GABAergic nervous system calming

TOPICAL RELAXANTS

Sesame oil (Til tail) — warm sesame oil gentle perineal massage; traditional Abhyanga (oil treatment) for perineum; reduces tissue hypertonicity; maintains mucosal hydration; Ayurvedic snehana for perineum

Triphala oil application — combined with warm oil massage; anti-inflammatory; traditional vaginal health

MIND-BODY

Yoga (Supta Baddha Konasana — reclined bound angle; Viparita Karani — legs up wall) — pelvic floor release poses; inner thigh and hip adductor stretching; reduces adductor muscle co-contraction that accompanies vaginismus; indirect pelvic floor relaxation

Pranayama (diaphragmatic breathing) — activates parasympathetic response (PNS); directly antagonises the sympathetic reflex arc causing pelvic floor guarding; 4-7-8 breathing during dilator insertion

IMPORTANT NOTE

Vaginismus is uniformly treatable — published clinical data shows 80–95% success with graduated vaginal dilator therapy combined with psychosexual support. The treatment requires: (1) Patient education (vaginal capacity reassurance, anatomy diagram); (2) Psychosexual therapy for anxiety and guilt; (3) Graduated dilator programme; (4) Partner inclusion in treatment programme. In India, the biggest obstacles are: patient shame, lack of pelvic floor physiotherapists, partner pressure for immediate penetration rather than graduated programme. Botulinum toxin injection is an effective adjunct for severe cases. Ashwagandha and Brahmi address the anxiety-physiological reflex nexus that maintains vaginismus — appropriate as adjuncts alongside the physical programme.
diseases.india_specific:
UNCONSUMMATED MARRIAGE — INDIA EPIDEMIC

Vaginismus is the most common cause of unconsummated marriage in India. Indian sexual medicine clinicians report unconsummated marriage couples as among the most common referrals — many presenting months to years after marriage. Contributing India-specific factors: (1) No premarital sexual experience → no opportunity to identify vaginismus before marriage; (2) Intense cultural pressure for consummation (family asking about pregnancy, first marital night expectations); (3) No sex education in schools; (4) Wedding night performances under extreme anxiety; (5) Husband's attempts at forceful penetration worsen vaginismus through pain conditioning

SEXUAL TRAUMA

India's high prevalence of childhood sexual abuse (24% women) and marital rape (legally not criminalised until 2024 in India) creates trauma-associated vaginismus; sexual trauma must be assessed in all vaginismus presentations; treatment requires trauma processing before or alongside physical rehabilitation

CULTURAL/RELIGIOUS SEXUAL GUILT

Many Indian women from conservative families carry deep sexual guilt — sex is 'dirty', female sexual desire is impure; this psychological conditioning creates the anxiety response that drives vaginismus; religious sex negativity is a major aetiological factor in India

LACK OF SEX EDUCATION

India: no formal sex education in most schools or families; women not knowing what normal penetrative intercourse involves; fear of unknown; anatomical ignorance of vaginal capacity; believing vagina is too small (it is a potential space of significant capacity); psychoeducation as primary treatment tool
diseases.important_notes:
VAGINISMUS IS UNIFORMLY TREATABLE — COUPLES MUST BE HELPED TO UNDERSTAND THIS IS NOT PERMANENT: Vaginismus — primary, secondary, or situational — is one of the most treatable sexual dysfunctions. Clinical data consistently shows 80–90%+ success rates with graduated dilator programs. The critical message for Indian couples presenting with unconsummated marriage due to vaginismus: (1) The vagina is anatomically normal; nothing is wrong with the vaginal structure; (2) The muscle contraction is involuntary — this is not deliberate refusal or resistance; (3) It is NOT the wife's fault; (4) It requires a graduated, patient, partner-supportive desensitisation programme — not force; forced penetration causes pain conditioning that worsens vaginismus and can cause severe secondary psychological trauma; (5) Full penetrative intercourse is typically achievable within weeks to months of consistent dilator use and psychotherapy. Husband education and engagement in the treatment programme is essential — without partner understanding, women cannot practice dilator therapy in the home environment.

BOTULINUM TOXIN INJECTION FOR REFRACTORY VAGINISMUS IS UNDER-UTILISED IN INDIA: Cases of vaginismus that do not respond to dilator therapy, CBT, and pelvic floor physiotherapy after 6–12 weeks of consistent effort can be treated with botulinum toxin A injection into the levator ani and/or pubococcygeus under anaesthesia. Toxin effect lasts 3–4 months and provides a window of reduced involuntary spasm during which dilator progression can be accelerated. Multiple case series show excellent outcomes when botulinum toxin is combined with dilator therapy — some consider it first-line rather than last-resort for severe cases. In India, gynaecologists with experience in pelvic floor botox are rare outside major academic centres, but the procedure is technically straightforward. For unconsummated marriages, where every month of non-consummation increases psychological and social pressure, botulinum toxin can be a transformative accelerant to treatment.

SEXUAL TRAUMA ASSESSMENT IS MANDATORY IN VAGINISMUS — EMDR IS THE TREATMENT OF CHOICE FOR TRAUMA-ASSOCIATED VAGINISMUS: India's high prevalence of childhood sexual abuse (estimated 24% of women — Lancet 2017) means that in any vaginismus population, a substantial proportion will have underlying sexual trauma that is aetiologically relevant. Failure to identify and address trauma will result in failed dilator therapy — because the muscle guarding is a conditioned trauma response (the body protecting against anticipated violation), not simply a habit to be desensitised. Trauma screening using validated tools (PCL-5, Childhood Trauma Questionnaire) should be part of every vaginismus workup. When trauma is identified: EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing) has the strongest evidence for PTSD and trauma-associated somatisation; typically 6–12 sessions; after trauma processing, the somatic vaginismus often resolves substantially. Combined EMDR + dilator programme is the most effective approach for trauma-associated vaginismus.

diseases.name: Dyspareunia
diseases.tests:
GYNAECOLOGICAL EXAMINATION

Speculum exam: atrophic changes, vaginal pH, discharge, cervical lesion/ectropion, candidiasis

Digital bimanual exam: uterine tenderness, cervical motion tenderness (PID), adnexal tenderness, uterine retroversion/fixed uterus (endometriosis), ovarian mass palpation

VULVAR ASSESSMENT

Q-tip test / vestibulometry — 10-N probe mapping of vestibular/vulvar pain; identifies vulvodynia and vestibulodynia location and severity

Dermoscopy of vulva — lichen sclerosus (white plaques, loss of architecture), lichen planus, contact dermatitis

Skin biopsy — if lichen sclerosus or suspicious vulvar lesion

VAGINAL PH + MICROSCOPY

pH>5 = atrophic or BV; KOH prep (hyphae = candida); wet mount (trichomonas motile, BV clue cells)

STI SCREEN

Chlamydia + Gonorrhoea NAAT (endocervical swab or urine) — PID evaluation

HIV, HSV serology (recurrent herpes causing introital pain/dyspareunia)

PELVIC ULTRASOUND

Transvaginal ultrasound: ovarian endometrioma (chocolate cysts — ground-glass appearance), ovarian cysts, fibroid location, free fluid, retroverted uterus

LAPAROSCOPY

Gold standard for endometriosis diagnosis; visualises peritoneal implants, adhesions, rectovaginal nodules; surgical treatment at same sitting; indicated when pelvic pain + dyspareunia + investigation negative or positive features suggesting endometriosis

CYSTOSCOPY

If interstitial cystitis suspected — urinary frequency, bladder pain, pelvic pain, dyspareunia complex
diseases.generic_medicines:
TREAT UNDERLYING CAUSE — PRINCIPLE

Dyspareunia is a symptom; treatment targets identified aetiology

ATROPHIC VAGINITIS / GSM

Vaginal oestrogen — Cream (Premarin, Ovestin) 0.5–1 g 2–3x/week; Pessary (Vagifem 10 mcg daily × 2 weeks then twice weekly); Ring (Estring); extremely effective; local action with minimal systemic absorption; safe even recent breast cancer (ASCO 2023); restores epithelial thickness, lubrication, elasticity, pH; first-line post-menopausal superficial dyspareunia

Vaginal hyaluronic acid moisturiser (Replens, Hyalofemme) — non-hormonal; twice weekly; good for women declining oestrogen

Ospemifene 60 mg OD (selective oestrogen receptor modulator — SERM) — oral systemic; treats GSM without vaginal oestrogen; approved US/EU; India availability limited

VESTIBULODYNIA

Lidocaine 5% ointment — topical anaesthetic at vestibule; reduces pain for intercourse; nightly application also desensitises

Gabapentin topical 2–6% cream — neuropathic pain at vestibule

Amitriptyline 10–25 mg OD oral — low-dose TCA; neuropathic component vestibulodynia

Duloxetine 30–60 mg — SNRI; neuropathic pain modulation

Vestibulectomy — surgical removal of painful vestibular tissue; effective for provoked vestibulodynia; last-line surgical option

ENDOMETRIOSIS-ASSOCIATED DEEP DYSPAREUNIA

GnRH agonist (Leuprolide Depot 3.75 mg monthly) — reduces oestrogen → suppresses endometriosis; for medical management alongside surgery

NSAIDs (Naproxen, Ibuprofen) — symptomatic dysmenorrhoea and dyspareunia pain reduction

Dienogest 2 mg OD — progestogen with specific anti-endometriosis action; highly effective for endometriosis-associated dyspareunia and dysmenorrhoea; available India

Laparoscopic surgery — excision/ablation of endometriosis implants; adhesiolysis; endometrioma cystectomy

PID

Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose + Doxycycline 100 mg BD 2 weeks + Metronidazole 400 mg BD 2 weeks (CDC 2021 regimen)

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND HEALING (ENDOMETRIOSIS/PID COMPONENT)

Haridra (Curcumin/Turmeric) — anti-inflammatory; COX-2 inhibitor; NF-κB inhibitor; most relevant for endometriosis-associated dyspareunia; multiple RCTs of curcumin in gynaecological inflammation; 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD

Ashoka (Saraca asoca bark) — uterine tonic; anti-inflammatory; specifically indicated in Ayurveda for all menstrual and uterine pain conditions including dysmenorrhoea and dyspareunia; analgesic component

Shatavari — anti-inflammatory genitourinary tonic; oestrogen-like effect on vaginal epithelium (reduces atrophic dryness); particularly useful in GSM-associated dyspareunia and post-partum dyspareunia (breastfeeding-related oestrogen deficiency)

Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) — uterine and vaginal anti-inflammatory; traditional treatment for pradara (vaginal discharge/inflammation complaints)

TOPICAL

Sesame oil (til tail) perineal massage — traditional Yoni Abhyanga (vaginal oil treatment); warm sesame oil; reduces introital dryness; heals episiotomy scar area; anti-inflammatory; maintains tissue elasticity; promotes epithelial healing

Aloe vera gel — lubricating; cooling; anti-inflammatory; safe topical for introital dyspareunia; readily available India

Triphala decoction wash — gentle astringent; anti-microbial; vaginal wash with diluted Triphala kashaya; reduces infection-component dyspareunia; traditional India practice

PAIN MODULATION

Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) — anti-inflammatory; analgesic; deep pelvic pain applied as tail or abdominal massage for endometriosis pain

IMPORTANT NOTE

Dyspareunia is a symptom requiring diagnosis — not empirical treatment. A young Indian woman with deep dyspareunia and dysmenorrhoea MUST be investigated for endometriosis with pelvic ultrasound + appropriate laparoscopic referral. Treating dyspareunia with Shatavari or analgesics without identifying endometriosis delays definitive treatment and risks progressive fertility damage. Vaginal oestrogen (post-menopausal atrophic dyspareunia) is the most effective and most under-used treatment in Indian practice — simple, local, safe, and transformative in quality of life. Curcumin represents the most evidence-supported Ayurvedic anti-inflammatory for pelvic pain and endometriosis-associated dyspareunia.
diseases.india_specific:
ENDOMETRIOSIS DIAGNOSTIC DELAY

India endometriosis is diagnosed on average 7–10 years after onset of symptoms — one of the longest diagnostic delays globally. Contributing factors: (1) Menstrual pain (dysmenorrhoea) culturally normalised ('it is normal to have painful periods'); (2) Dyspareunia not disclosed due to shame; (3) Low awareness among GPs and family physicians; (4) Transvaginal ultrasound used primarily to look for fibroids/PCOS — misses peritoneal endometriosis; (5) Definitive diagnosis requires laparoscopy which is not offered until years of failed treatments. Endometriosis causing infertility in India goes undiagnosed while couples undergo empirical fertility treatment without addressing the root cause.

EPISIOTOMY DYSPAREUNIA

India: episiotomy rate 60–95% in most Indian hospitals (compared to WHO recommendation ≤10%); iatrogenic perineal scar formation; episiotomy scar dyspareunia is extremely common and extremely under-reported by Indian women post-delivery; treatment: perineal scar massage; pelvic floor physiotherapy; topical oestrogen; in severe neuroma cases: surgical revision. This is a preventable iatrogenic cause of dyspareunia that Indian obstetric practice is only beginning to address

PID AND CHLAMYDIA DIAGNOSIS

India: STI screening rates very low; chlamydia and gonorrhoea estimated highly prevalent but vastly under-tested; silent PID (subclinical or treated inadequately) is common; leads to pelvic adhesions and deep dyspareunia years later; fallopian tube damage → infertility; any young woman with deep dyspareunia and fever/vaginal discharge must have PID excluded and urethral/cervical chlamydia NAAT test

GENITOURINARY SYNDROME OF MENOPAUSE (GSM)

Indian post-menopausal women (150+ million menopausal women India) are not routinely prescribed vaginal oestrogen despite it being safe, effective, and rapidly acting; cultural silence around post-menopausal sexuality means dyspareunia in this age group goes untreated; GPs must offer vaginal oestrogen cream routine at menopause consultations
diseases.important_notes:
DEEP DYSPAREUNIA IN YOUNG INDIAN WOMEN MUST BE INVESTIGATED FOR ENDOMETRIOSIS — DIAGNOSTIC DELAY IS CAUSING INFERTILITY: Endometriosis affects an estimated 10–15% of reproductive-age women globally (25–30 million women in India). It is the most common cause of deep dyspareunia in reproductive-age women, and it causes infertility through tubal damage, endometrioma, and pelvic adhesions. India's average diagnostic delay for endometriosis is 7–10 years — meaning women spend a decade with undertreated pain and progressive fertility damage while being told their periods are 'normal' or treated with symptomatic analgesia alone. The clinical diagnostic shortcut: any reproductive-age woman with BOTH dysmenorrhoea (painful periods) AND dyspareunia deserves pelvic ultrasound and endometriosis consideration regardless of age or parity. Negative ultrasound does not exclude endometriosis (only laparoscopy is definitive for peritoneal implants). Early referral to gynaecologist with laparoscopy when symptoms cluster is the correct path. Medical management (Dienogest, GnRH agonist) can be offered while awaiting surgery.

EPISIOTOMY IS A PREVENTABLE CAUSE OF DYSPAREUNIA — INDIA'S OBSTETRIC OVERCORRECTION NEEDS REFORM: India's episiotomy rate (60–95% of vaginal deliveries in most hospitals) is among the highest in the world — vastly exceeding the WHO recommendation of ≤10% and the current evidence showing episiotomy should be restrictive (only for specific foetal distress indications). Routine episiotomy causes: (1) Perineal scar tissue that can cause persistent introital dyspareunia; (2) Episiotomy wound dehiscence; (3) Dyspareunia for 3–12 months post-partum; (4) In some cases, permanent pain from scar neuroma. Post-episiotomy dyspareunia is common, treatable (perineal massage, pelvic floor physiotherapy, topical oestrogen, scar revision), but rarely addressed because Indian postpartum care does not routinely include sexual health assessment after delivery. The solution is upstream: advocate restrictive episiotomy practice AND ensure postpartum sexual health review at 6–12 weeks after delivery.

VAGINAL OESTROGEN FOR POST-MENOPAUSAL DYSPAREUNIA IS SAFE AND DRAMATICALLY EFFECTIVE — INDIA'S GYNAECOLOGISTS MUST PRESCRIBE IT MORE: Genitourinary Syndrome of Menopause (GSM) — vaginal atrophy, dryness, reduced elasticity, reduced lubrication — affects 40–60% of post-menopausal women and is the most common cause of dyspareunia in women over 50. Vaginal oestrogen (cream, pessary, ring) is highly effective for GSM-associated dyspareunia: 74% of women report significant dyspareunia improvement within 12 weeks. It is LOCAL treatment with minimal systemic absorption. Critically, updated ASCO 2023 guidelines confirm that vaginal oestrogen is safe even in women with a history of hormone receptor-positive breast cancer on aromatase inhibitor therapy, when systemic therapy is contraindicated. In India, most post-menopausal women with dyspareunia are told to 'use lubricant' or told nothing — vaginal oestrogen, which is inexpensive, available, and transformative, is dramatically under-prescribed.

diseases.name: Vulvodynia
diseases.tests:
Q-TIP TEST (VESTIBULOMETRY)

Cotton-tipped swab pressed gently at 10–12 equidistant points of the vestibule (clock face): 3, 5, 6, 7, 9 o'clock are most sensitive; patient rates pain 0–10; >3/10 at ≥2 sites = consistent with vulvar vestibulitis; this is the PRIMARY diagnostic test for localised provoked vulvodynia; simple, cheap, available in any gynaecology office

ALGESIOMETRY / VULVALGESIOMETER

Quantitative vulval sensory testing — standardised pressure probes; more precise than Q-tip; research settings

MICROBIOLOGICAL EXCLUSION

Vaginal swab microscopy + culture — MUST exclude candida, BV, trichomonas before vulvodynia diagnosis; pH, KOH, wet mount; culture for non-albicans candida

STI SCREEN

HSV serology / PCR — herpes simplex causing recurrent vulvar pain episodes; HSV-2 particularly

HPV status (colposcopy if lesions) — HPV-related VIN

DERMATOLOGICAL EXAMINATION

Lichen sclerosus exclusion: classic white, crinkled skin; loss of labia minora architecture; fusion; biopsy confirms

Lichen planus exclusion: Wickham's striae; erosive LP; biopsy

Biopsy — only if visible lesion preventing clinical diagnosis

NEUROLOGICAL

Pudendal nerve sensory testing: biothesiometry (vibration threshold at vulva); pudendal nerve terminal motor latency (PNTML) — if pudendal neuralgia suspected

PSYCHOLOGICAL

PHQ-9 (depression); GAD-7 (anxiety); impact of vulvodynia on daily life VAS scale

URINE

24-hour urinary oxalate — if dietary modification strategy considered

Urine microscopy culture — interstitial cystitis co-morbidity
diseases.generic_medicines:
TOPICAL TREATMENTS

Lidocaine 5% ointment — nightly application to vestibule; 6–8 weeks; desensitises nociceptors; reduces provoked pain; commonly used during dilator/physical therapy programme for LPV; also applied before intercourse for symptomatic relief

Topical gabapentin 2–6% compounded cream — GABA-analogue applied directly to vestibule; reduces neurogenic pain at vestibular nociceptors; compounded formulation; superiority to systemic gabapentin for localised disease

Topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05%) — limited role; only if inflammatory component identified on exam; NOT for chronic LPV without inflammation

Topical oestrogen / testosterone — reduced local hormones from OCP-related androgen suppression or menopause; restores vestibular integrity; particularly relevant for OCP-associated vulvodynia onset

ORAL TREATMENTS

Amitriptyline 10–75 mg nocte — TCA; neuropathic pain; starting 10 mg, titrate up; most commonly used systemic agent for vulvodynia; benefit at 4–6 weeks

Gabapentin 300 mg BD→TDS — neuropathic pain modulation; particularly for generalised vulvodynia and central sensitisation cases

Duloxetine 30–60 mg OD — SNRI; ascending pain modulation via noradrenergic pathway; evidence for neuropathic pelvic pain

Venlafaxine 37.5–150 mg — alternative SNRI

PHYSICAL THERAPY

Pelvic floor physiotherapy — biofeedback + manual therapy; addresses pelvic floor hypertonicity co-existing with vulvodynia; reduces secondary vaginismus; essential component of LPV management

INTERVENTIONAL

Vestibulectomy — surgical excision of painful vestibular tissue (posterior vestibulectomy with vaginal advancement); 60–80% success rate for severe treatment-resistant LPV; last line after 6+ months failed conservative

Botulinum toxin injection — injection into pelvic floor musculature / vulvar vestibule; reduces muscle hypertonicity and local nociception

Low-level laser therapy (LLLT) — emerging evidence; reduces vestibular pain via neural photobiomodulation

LIFESTYLE

Avoid irritants: soap, douches, fabric softener, synthetic underwear; 100% cotton underwear; sitz baths with plain water; cold compress for flares; avoid bicycle/horseback riding during acute phase

Clonazepam 0.5 mg tablet (off-label: vestibular neuropathic pain)

Vaginal oestrogen cream

Calcium citrate 500 mg (10 tabs): Rs.18

Vaginal oestrogen cream (30g): Rs.85
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND NEUROPATHIC PAIN

Haridra (Curcumin) — TRPV1 modulator; curcumin directly downregulates TRPV1 receptor expression and activity; TRPV1 is the key nociceptor in vulvodynia pathophysiology; anti-inflammatory via COX-2 and NF-κB inhibition; 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD; constitutes one of the most biologically rationale Ayurvedic interventions for vulvodynia given the TRPV1 mechanism

Ashwagandha — reduces central sensitisation and HPA axis dysregulation; useful for generalised vulvodynia with central sensitisation features; adapatogen; also addresses co-morbid depression/anxiety

Brahmi — anti-anxiety; reduces central pain amplification; useful adjunct in vulvodynia with significant psychological distress

TOPICAL SOOTHING

Aloe vera gel (fresh / cold) — cooling; anti-inflammatory; TRPV1 antagonism via aloe compounds; readily available; applied to vulva for symptom relief; safe mucous membrane application

Sesame oil (raw cold-pressed) — anti-inflammatory linoleic and oleic fatty acids; traditional Yoni snehana; gentle vulval massage to maintain tissue health; reduce contact friction

Coconut oil (virgin unrefined) — TRPV1 modulating properties (phenols); anti-inflammatory; natural lubricant; often better tolerated than synthetic lubricants in sensitive vulvodynia patients

Chandan (Sandalwood) paste — cooling; anti-inflammatory; traditionally applied for burning vulval conditions; diluted in carrier oil

DIETARY

Low-oxalate diet supplementation: avoid high-oxalate foods (spinach, tomatoes, beetroot, nuts); increase calcium citrate intake to bind oxalate in gut; reduces urinary oxalate; Indian diet often high oxalate — relevant practical recommendation

IMPORTANT NOTE

Vulvodynia management requires a multidisciplinary approach: Q-tip diagnosis → topical neuropathic agents (lidocaine, gabapentin cream) → systemic neuropathic treatment (amitriptyline, gabapentin) → pelvic floor physiotherapy → psychological support (CBT, treatment of depression) → surgical (vestibulectomy) for refractory cases. Curcumin is the most biologically plausible Ayurvedic agent given the TRPV1-mediated mechanism. In India, the overwhelming priority is correct diagnosis — stopping repeated antifungal misdiagnosis and performing the simple Q-tip test that takes 2 minutes and confirms the diagnosis.
diseases.india_specific:
REPEATED ANTIFUNGAL MISDIAGNOSIS

In India, vulvodynia is routinely misdiagnosed as recurrent vaginal candidiasis. Burning vulval symptoms → GP prescribes Fluconazole → temporary symptom reduction (or none) → symptoms return → repeat Fluconazole → this cycle repeats for years. The patient is labelled a 'recurrent candidiasis patient' and treated with prolonged antifungal courses, sometimes including oral itraconazole maintenance, without candida ever being confirmed on microscopy. The correct approach: if burning vulvar pain does not have candida confirmed on microscopy or culture AND does not respond to antifungal treatment → vulvodynia must be considered → Q-tip vestibule testing → diagnosis and appropriate management.

SPICE/CHILLI DIET AND VULVODYNIA

Indian diet: extremely high in chilli, capsaicin, spices in most regions; capsaicin is a TRPV1 (transient receptor potential vanilloid-1) channel activator — the same nociceptor involved in vulvodynia pain sensitisation; correlation between high-capsaicin diet and TRPV1 sensitisation is biologically plausible and clinically observed; many Indian vulvodynia patients report dietary triggers; a low-oxalate, low-capsaicin dietary assessment is appropriate (though high-oxalate diet as causative is controversial)

CALCIUM OXALATE URINE

A historical theory that high urinary calcium oxalate crystals cause vestibular irritation leading to vulvodynia; low-oxalate diet and calcium citrate supplementation was promoted; current evidence is inconsistent but remains relevant for a subset; India: high-oxalate diet common (spinach, tomatoes — Indian staples); dietary counselling for oxalate in vulvodynia workup remains clinical practice

TIGHT SYNTHETIC CLOTHING

India: synthetic fabric salwar suits, tight fits — continuous perineal pressure and moisture; triggers provoked vulvodynia; cotton underwear and loose clothing recommendation is a practical India-specific management step
diseases.important_notes:
VULVODYNIA IS THE MOST MISDIAGNOSED FEMALE GENITAL PAIN CONDITION IN INDIA — ENDING THE ANTIFUNGAL MISDIAGNOSIS CYCLE: The most common diagnostic error in India for vulvodynia is repeated misdiagnosis as recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis. Signs of this misdiagnosis: (1) Chronic vulvar burning NOT improving with antifungal treatment; (2) Multiple antifungal prescriptions over months to years; (3) No candida confirmed on wet mount microscopy or culture; (4) Symptoms persist between 'treatment' courses. The clinician must: STOP presumptive antifungal prescribing; perform vaginal microscopy to confirm or exclude candida; if negative or repeated non-response to antifungals → perform Q-tip vestibule mapping (2-minute test); diagnose vulvodynia when Q-tip confirms vestibular hypersensitivity. The 2-minute Q-tip test can end years of misdiagnosis and initiate effective treatment. This failure of diagnosis is causing widespread suffering among Indian women who are cycling through antifungal prescriptions without benefit.

CENTRAL SENSITISATION IN VULVODYNIA — UNDERSTANDING COMORBID CHRONIC PAIN SYNDROMES: A subset of women with generalised vulvodynia (whole-vulva burning, spontaneous, non-provoked) have a central sensitisation mechanism — the brain amplifies pain signals centrally, similar to fibromyalgia, interstitial cystitis, temporomandibular disorder, and irritable bowel syndrome. These patients: (1) Have multiple chronic pain conditions simultaneously; (2) Do NOT respond to local treatments alone; (3) Require central pain processing agents: amitriptyline, duloxetine, gabapentin, pregabalin; (4) Also benefit from CBT-based pain management and mindfulness; (5) Are frequently told by multiple specialists that 'there is nothing wrong' because individual organ investigations are negative. Recognising the central sensitisation phenotype in vulvodynia changes the treatment approach from local to systemic neurological management.

THE IMPACT OF ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILLS ON VULVODYNIA — AND HORMONAL MANAGEMENT IN INDIA: Combined oral contraceptive pills (COCPs — containing ethinyl oestradiol) cause a dose-dependent reduction in sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) and free testosterone. Low vestibular testosterone → vestibular gland atrophy → loss of vestibular mucosa integrity → increased pain sensitivity (testosterone receptors are present in the vestibular stroma). A substantial proportion of women with OCP-associated LPV onset: pain began within weeks to months of starting COCP; pain remits after stopping; this is a recognised clinical association. In India, young married women are often started on COCPs without being warned that a minority may develop vestibular pain. The management: switch to progestogen-only pill or non-hormonal contraception + topical testosterone 0.1% or oestrogen to the vestibule to restore hormonal milieu. Clinicians must take OCP start date relative to symptom onset in vulvodynia history.
confirmation_tests_curated: Cotton-tipped swab pressed gently at 10–12 equidistant points of the vestibule (clock face): 3, 5, 6, 7, 9 o'clock are most sensitive; patient rates pain 0–10; >3/10 at ≥2 sites = consistent with vulvar vestibulitis; this is the PRIMARY diagnostic test for localised provoked vulvodynia; simple, cheap, available in any gynaecology office
Quantitative vulval sensory testing — standardised pressure probes; more precise than Q-tip; research settings

diseases.name: Peyronies Disease
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL EXAMINATION

Palpation of penile shaft — identify plaque (fibrous nodule, usually dorsal); note size, consistency, calcification

Patient self-photograph or office photo of erect penis — documents degree and direction of curvature; baseline for treatment monitoring; Peyronie's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ) measurement

PENILE DOPPLER ULTRASOUND

High-frequency duplex Doppler of penile vasculature (limp + dynamic with intracavernosal PGE1 injection) — identifies: plaque location + echogenicity + calcification; cavernosal artery peak systolic velocity (PSV); end-diastolic velocity (EDV); resistive index: assesses co-existing vasculogenic ED; essential pre-surgical planning

PEYRONIE'S DISEASE QUESTIONNAIRE (PDQ)

Validated: symptom bother, pain, psychological distress; 15-item; measures impact and severity; Phase assessment (active vs stable)

ERECT PENILE PHOTOGRAPH

Curved angle measurement from photograph; or office gonometer measurement during pharmacological erection (PGE1-induced); curvature >30° = clinically significant; >60–90° = surgical correction often needed

MRI PENIS

If US inconclusive; better soft tissue characterisation; usually research / specialist setting
diseases.generic_medicines:
ACUTE PHASE (FIRST 12-18 MONTHS)

Colchicine 0.5 mg BD — anti-fibrotic; reduces TGF-β-mediated collagen synthesis; most widely used oral agent for early Peyronie's; some evidence for pain reduction and plaque stabilisation in acute phase; inexpensive

Pentoxifylline 400 mg TDS — phosphodiesterase inhibitor; anti-fibrotic; anti-inflammatory; reduces TGF-β expression; some evidence for plaque reduction and curvature improvement in early disease; also treats co-existing ED component

Tamoxifen 20 mg BD — anti-oestrogen; inhibits fibroblast activity; older evidence; still used in acute phase; some pain reduction benefit

Vitamin E 400 IU OD — antioxidant; reduces reactive oxygen species in fibrotic tissue; minimal evidence at high quality but commonly used; safe

L-arginine + L-citrulline — vasodilation; reduces hypoxic injury; adjunct

INTRALESIONAL INJECTION (SPECIALIST)

Collagenase clostridium histolyticum (CCH / Xiaflex) — enzymatic degradation of collagen in plaque; ONLY FDA-approved intralesional treatment; 0.58 mg injection into plaque + modelling procedure; 2 injections per cycle × 4 cycles; 17–20 degree curvature improvement; significant improvement in severe curvature; NOT available in India (very expensive; not registered)

Verapamil 10 mg intralesional — calcium channel blocker; reduces fibroblast collagen synthesis; locally injected into plaque; available India; reasonable evidence for pain reduction and partial curvature improvement

Interferon α-2b intralesional — anti-fibrotic cytokine; evidence for pain and curvature improvement

PENILE TRACTION DEVICE

Vacuum erection device + penile traction therapy (PTT) — mechanical elongation of contracted tunica; evidence for 1–5 cm length recovery and partial curvature reduction; used 2–4 hours/day; recommended particularly for penile shortening

SURGICAL — CHRONIC STABLE PHASE

Tunical plication (Nesbit procedure) — shortens longer tunica side to correct curvature; for mild-moderate curvature adequate penile length remaining

Plaque incision/excision + grafting — for severe curvature; longer procedure; risk of ED post-op

Penile prosthesis implant — for Peyronie's with severe co-existing ED + severe curvature; correction of both simultaneously

Tamoxifen 20 mg tablet

Sildenafil 25–50 mg (for co-existing ED; also reduces fibrosis via PDE5 pathway)

Vitamin E 400 IU (10 caps): Rs.18

Sildenafil 50 mg (4 tabs): Rs.32
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANTI-FIBROTIC

Haridra (Curcumin) — TGF-β inhibitor; anti-fibrotic; reduces collagen crosslinking; highly relevant mechanistic rationale for Peyronie's fibrous plaque (TGF-β1 mediated); 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD

Guggulu (Commiphora mukul) — anti-inflammatory; anti-fibrotic; reduces connective tissue proliferation; anti-lipid; traditional Ayurvedic agent for nodular fibrous conditions; Kanchanara Guggulu specifically indicated for fibrous growths/nodules in Ayurveda; 2 tablets BD

Kanchanara (Bauhinia variegata) — Ayurvedic anti-fibrotic; used for glandular enlargements and fibrous masses (gandamaala); Kanchanara Guggulu = classical formulation for fibrous/glandular conditions

Shilajit — mineral resin; mitochondrial coenzyme Q10 activator; anti-fibrotic in connective tissue; reduces ROS-mediated fibrosis; traditionally indicated for male reproductive system conditions

ERECTILE SUPPORT

Ashwagandha — testosterone support; reduces anxiety from Peyronie's deformity; improves erectile quality

Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — PDE5 modulating properties; erectile support; libido enhancement accompanying Peyronie's psychological inhibition

TOPICAL

Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) oil massage — anti-inflammatory; traditionally used for pain and swelling in musculoskeletal conditions; warm application to penile shaft (topical, external only); reduces acute phase inflammation; caution: gentle application only; no aggressive massage in acute phase

IMPORTANT NOTE

Peyronie's disease is progressive and irreversible in chronic phase without treatment. The biological window for anti-fibrotic medication is the acute phase (first 12–18 months). In India, the majority of men present in chronic stable phase because the acute phase was not recognised or disclosed. Curcumin and Guggulu have the strongest anti-fibrotic mechanism rationale as Ayurvedic adjuncts. Colchicine and Pentoxifylline are affordable (Jan Aushadhi available) oral options appropriate for Indian primary care use in acute phase. Severe symptomatic chronic Peyronie's requires urological surgical referral.
diseases.india_specific:
COMPLETE NON-DISCLOSURE AND LATE PRESENTATION

Peyronie's disease is almost entirely invisible in India's healthcare system — not because it is rare (3–9% prevalence implies millions of Indian men affected) but because no Indian man will voluntarily ask a doctor about the shape of his penis. The social script: a man with Peyronie's will avoid the problem, may reduce or stop sexual activity, and will not seek care until the condition is severe. Wives or partners are often also confused and distressed. Primary care physicians must ASK about penile curvature as part of any sexual history — it will not be volunteered.

ERECTILE DYSFUNCTION MISATTRIBUTION

Indian men with Peyronie's often present with 'erectile dysfunction' as the chief complaint — because penetration difficulty (from curvature) and anxiety from deformity causes secondary ED. The Peyronie's plaque investigation is not performed in many Indian settings because: (1) No palpation of the penis performed; (2) No penile ultrasound done; (3) Patient only mentions 'weak erection' not deformity. Correct practice: ALL ED patients should have penile palpation and history of curvature, painful erections, or penile deformity as part of evaluation.

RISK FACTORS IN INDIA

Vigorous intercourse penile trauma (microtrauma delamination injury to tunica albuginea): most cases result from repeated microtrauma or single significant blunt trauma to erected penis during intercourse; some Indian positions/practices; history of trauma during intercourse should be explored

Diabetes mellitus: strong association (DM → connective tissue abnormality, collagen crosslinking, fibrosis); India's massive DM burden (90+ million) creates a large at-risk pool

Hypertension + cardiovascular disease: same vascular risk factors; collagen dysregulation
diseases.important_notes:
PEYRONIE'S DISEASE IS UNDER-DIAGNOSED IN INDIA — ACTIVE INQUIRY IS ESSENTIAL: With a global prevalence of 3–9% of men (studies suggest higher with better methodology), India may have 15–30 million men with Peyronie's disease. Yet it is almost invisible in Indian medical practice. The reason is singular: extreme shame prevents any man from discussing penile shape with a doctor unless specifically asked. Clinical practice implication: every GP and urologist in India should include 'any change in penile shape or bend during erection? Any penile pain or nodule?' in the sexual history for any male patient presenting with ED or sexual difficulty. This single question will identify cases that would never be volunteered — allowing early-phase intervention with anti-fibrotic medications during the limited treatment window. Partner distress (female partner's dyspareunia from abnormal curvature) is often the presentation trigger that brings couples to consultation.

DIABETES AND PEYRONIE'S DISEASE — INDIA'S DOUBLE BURDEN: India has 90+ million people with type 2 diabetes — the world's largest diabetic population. Diabetes is strongly associated with Peyronie's disease through multiple mechanisms: (1) Advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) → aberrant collagen crosslinking → fibrosis; (2) Decreased MMP (matrix metalloproteinase) activity → impaired fibrous plaque degradation; (3) Comorbid ED from vascular and neuropathic damage → performance anxiety → trauma risk. Men with diabetes presenting with sexual dysfunction are higher-risk Peyronie's candidates and should have penile palpation as part of their urological evaluation. The combination of Diabetes + Peyronie's + ED is a triple sexual dysfunction complex requiring coordinated management of glycaemia (metabolic control), ED treatment (PDE5i), and Peyronie's-specific intervention.

THE LIMITED TREATMENT WINDOW IN PEYRONIE'S — IMPORTANCE OF ACUTE PHASE RECOGNITION AND TREATMENT: The acute phase of Peyronie's disease (first 6–18 months) is the biological window during which: (1) Plaque has not yet calcified and is potentially modifiable; (2) Anti-fibrotic interventions (colchicine, pentoxifylline, intralesional verapamil) have their best chance of altering natural history; (3) Curvature is still changing and not yet fixed. Once the chronic phase is reached (curvature stable, plaque calcified), medical management is largely ineffective — surgical correction or prosthesis implantation becomes the only effective intervention for severe functional impairment. In India, this window is almost always missed: men present years after onset when chronic phase is established. Raising awareness among GPs to identify Peyronie's in the acute phase through active inquiry — particularly in diabetic men — could allow the cost-effective oral anti-fibrotic treatment to modify disease progression.
confirmation_tests_curated: Palpation of penile shaft — identify plaque (fibrous nodule, usually dorsal); note size, consistency, calcification
Patient self-photograph or office photo of erect penis — documents degree and direction of curvature; baseline for treatment monitoring; Peyronie's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ) measurement

High-frequency duplex Doppler of penile vasculature (limp + dynamic with intracavernosal PGE1 injection) — identifies: plaque location + echogenicity + calcification; cavernosal artery peak systolic velocity (PSV); end-diastolic velocity (EDV); resistive index: assesses co-existing vasculogenic ED; essential pre-surgical planning

diseases.name: Priapism
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — IMMEDIATE ASSESSMENT

Clinical history: duration (> 4 hours = emergency treatment regardless); association with sexual stimulation; medications; substance use; sickle cell or haematological history

Penile examination: fully rigid, painful corpora (ischaemic) vs partial rigidity, painless (high-flow non-ischaemic)

CAVERNOUS BLOOD GAS ASPIRATION

Diagnostic and therapeutic aspiration: 21-gauge needle into lateral corpus cavernosum; aspirate 5–10 mL blood; send for: pH (<7.25 = acidosis = ischaemic); PO2 (<30 mmHg = ischaemia); PCO2 (>60 mmHg = ischaemia); appearance: dark blood (ischaemic/venous) vs bright red (well-oxygenated = non-ischaemic/high-flow); this test IS the immediate diagnostic AND therapeutic first step

PENILE COLOUR DUPLEX DOPPLER

If high-flow priapism suspected: Doppler of cavernosal arteries; turbulent or absent cavernosal arterial flow = ischaemic; high-velocity flow with fistula = non-ischaemic; identifies arteriovenous fistula in non-ischaemic for embolisation planning

HAEMATOLOGICAL

Full blood count (FBC) — sickle cell, leukaemia, thalassaemia evaluation

Sickle cell preparation / haemoglobin electrophoresis — if SCD suspected; especially tribal India patient

Peripheral blood smear — leukaemia, polycythaemia

URINE

Urine toxicology — cocaine, other vasoactive substance

Urine dipstick — haemoglobinuria (SCD crisis)
diseases.generic_medicines:
ISCHAEMIC PRIAPISM — EMERGENCY TREATMENT

Step 1 — Aspiration: Aspirate 20–50 mL blood from corpus cavernosum via lateral approach until drainage blood becomes bright red; usually reduces erection; repeat aspiration PRN; FIRST AND IMMEDIATE STEP

Step 2 — Intracavernosal sympathomimetic injection

Phenylephrine 100–500 mcg intracavernosal every 3–5 minutes × up to 1 hour — PREFERRED agent; α1-adrenergic agonist; minimal cardiovascular effect; recommended AUA 2021; titrated injection into corpus cavernosum; BP and cardiac monitoring mandatory

Epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.01 mg diluted — if phenylephrine unavailable (India: may not be available as IC formulation); significant cardiac risk

Etilefrine (available in India as Effortil) — α-agonist; used for priapism particularly in SCD-associated stuttering priapism; 50 mg self-injection programme; oral tablet for prophylaxis

Step 3 — If aspiration + sympathomimetic fails → Surgical shunting (T-shaped shunt, Al-Ghorab, or Quackels shunt) — urological surgical procedure; corporal-spongiosal shunt; last resort

NON-ISCHAEMIC PRIAPISM

Selective pudendal artery embolisation — gel foam or coil embolisation of AVF; interventional radiology; elective; NOT emergency

SICKLE CELL ASSOCIATED PRIAPISM

Simultaneous SCD crisis management: IV fluids; analgesia (morphine); oxygen; haematology referral for exchange transfusion if indicated

Long-term prophylaxis: Hydroxyurea 500–1500 mg OD — reduces HbS, reduces SCD crisis frequency, reduces stuttering priapism recurrence

PROPHYLAXIS FOR RECURRENT/STUTTERING PRIAPISM

PDE5 inhibitor (paradoxical maintenance therapy): Low-dose Sildenafil 25 mg OD or Tadalafil 5 mg OD daily — paradoxically prevents recurrent ischaemic priapism via upregulation of PDE5 and increased cGMP in smooth muscle; reducing hypersensitivity to nocturnal erections; counter-intuitive but evidence-based

GnRH agonist (Leuprolide) — reduces testosterone → reduces nocturnal erections → reduces stuttering priapism frequency (for refractory SCD-associated cases)

Baclofen 10–20 mg nocte — GABA-B agonist; reduces spinal erection reflex; reduces nocturnal tumescence frequency

Phenylephrine injection (may need specialist pharmacy or hospital procurement — ICU available)

Etilefrine 25 mg tablet (Effortil brand equivalent; α-agonist for prophylaxis / stuttering priapism)

Hydroxyurea 500 mg capsule (for SCD-associated priapism prophylaxis)

Sildenafil 25 mg tablet (paradoxical prophylactic therapy for stuttering priapism)

Tadalafil 5 mg tablet (daily; paradoxical prophylaxis)

Morphine / pethedine injection (inpatient SCD crisis analgesia)

Jan Aushadhi Prices
