Sildenafil 50 mg (4 tabs): Rs.32

Tadalafil 5 mg (4 tabs): Rs.28

Etilefrine 25 mg (10 tabs): Rs.42
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
SICKLE CELL DISEASE — ANTI-SICKLING HERBS

Haridra (Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces HbS polymerisation; anti-SCD cell adhesion to endothelium; reduces vascular inflammation in SCD crisis; 500–1000 mg curcumin BD; limited but growing evidence base for SCD management

Nilavembu (Andrographis paniculata) — anti-inflammatory; Tamil Ayurveda tradition for blood disorders; possible anti-sickling properties via haematological anti-inflammatory effect

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immuno-modulator; anti-inflammatory; reduces vascular endothelial inflammation in SCD-related vascular events

TONIFYING (POST-PRIAPISM ERECTILE RECOVERY)

Ashwagandha — testosterone support; corpus cavernosum smooth muscle health; reduces post-priapism anxiety and secondary psychogenic ED; 300 mg BD after acute episode resolves

Shilajit — mitochondrial support; penile smooth muscle recovery; anti-fibrotic; of theoretical benefit post-priapism for reducing corporal fibrosis from ischaemic insult

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — LH-stimulating; testosterone support; erectile recovery post-priapism

IMPORTANT NOTE

Ischaemic priapism is an emergency — Ayurvedic medicines play NO ROLE in acute management. In India, the greatest risk is patient delay due to shame and the consequent irreversible ED from prolonged ischaemia. Emergency education: any erection > 4 hours that persists without sexual stimulation and becomes painful = EMERGENCY; must go to hospital IMMEDIATELY. Post-successful treatment: Ayurvedic support for penile smooth muscle recovery (Ashwagandha, Shilajit) is appropriate. SCD-associated stuttering priapism: Curcumin as anti-inflammatory adjunct alongside Hydroxyurea. Sickle cell disease management in tribal India requires community awareness — priapism is often the presenting or recurrent crisis complication that goes untreated.
diseases.india_specific:
SICKLE CELL DISEASE — INDIA CONTEXT

Sickle cell disease (SCD) is disproportionately prevalent in India: 300,000+ SCD births/year in India (the world's second largest burden after sub-Saharan Africa); concentrated in tribal populations of Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Jharkhand, MP, Maharashtra (Vidarbha), and Gujarat; Sickle cell crisis → sludging of sickled erythrocytes in cavernous sinusoids → ischaemic priapism; SCD-associated priapism represents the most important haematological cause in the Indian context; management requires both priapism treatment (aspiration/adrenergic) AND simultaneously treating the underlying sickle cell vasoocclusive crisis (IV fluids, oxygen, analgesia, haematology input); Hydroxyurea prophylaxis reduces SCD-associated stuttering priapism frequency

ILLICIT APHRODISIAC USE

India: substantial market for 'herbal aphrodisiacs' claiming to enhance erection; many containing undisclosed PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil analogues) or other vasoactive compounds; adulterated products from pharmacies, online, or local markets; priapism caused by high-dose unregulated PDE5 inhibitor analogues in adulterated products; cases increasingly seen in Indian emergency departments; history must include traditional remedy, herbal supplement, or unregulated product use

TRAZODONE PRIAPISM

India: Trazodone is used as a night sedative / sleep aid at low doses (50–100 mg) — prescribed by psychiatrists, neurologists, general practitioners; Trazodone is the antidepressant most associated with priapism (α-adrenergic blockade in corpus cavernosum); risk typically at doses 100–150 mg; patients must be warned; given trazodone's common prescription in India for insomnia and depression, trazodone-associated priapism is among the most preventable drug causes India
diseases.important_notes:
PRIAPISM IS A UROLOGICAL EMERGENCY — EVERY INDIAN EMERGENCY PHYSICIAN AND GP MUST KNOW THE 4-HOUR WINDOW: Ischaemic priapism causes irreversible damage through progressive corporal hypoxia and acidosis. The timeline of damage: 4–6 hours: hypoxic stress begins; 6–24 hours: endothelial and smooth muscle injury; >24 hours: fibrosis with permanent ED >90% probability; >48 hours: necrosis, permanent severe ED near-universal. In India, the barriers to timely treatment are: (1) Extreme shame — man with prolonged erection will not go to casualty easily; (2) Emergency physicians may not recognise priapism or may perform inappropriate assessment; (3) Lack of phenylephrine in peripheral emergency settings. The Indian healthcare system response requires: Casualty doctors awareness; immediate aspiration (aspiration needle and syringe is the ONLY equipment needed for the initial life-saving step); intracavernosal phenylephrine or etilefrine procurement in district hospitals; patient explicit discharge education after recovery.

SICKLE CELL DISEASE AND PRIAPISM — INDIA'S TRIBAL HEALTH CRISIS: India has one of the world's largest sickle cell disease burdens (300,000+ births per year; 14 million carriers). Sickle cell-associated priapism is the most common haematological cause of priapism in India. SCD priapism: (1) Often presents as stuttering priapism — recurrent episodes each lasting minutes to hours, resolving spontaneously; (2) Progresses to prolonged ischaemic episodes; (3) Is undertreated in tribal regions where SCD is most prevalent (Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Jharkhand) due to lack of clinical awareness; (4) Causes ED cumulatively through repeated minor corporal damage even without single major episode. India's National Sickle Cell Disease Elimination Mission (2023) focuses on screening and neonatal diagnosis — but sexual health complications of SCD in adolescent and young adult males (priapism is the sexual presentation) are not part of standard SCD management education in India. Hydroxyurea prophylaxis significantly reduces SCD priapism frequency and should be offered to all SCD patients with stuttering priapism.

TRAZODONE, ANTIPSYCHOTICS, AND DRUG-INDUCED PRIAPISM IN INDIA — PRESCRIBER RESPONSIBILITY: Multiple commonly prescribed medications in India cause priapism as an adverse effect through α-adrenergic blockade in corporal smooth muscle. The most important India-relevant examples: (1) Trazodone — widely prescribed for insomnia and depression in India; α-adrenergic blockade mechanism; risk at 100–150 mg; prescribers must warn patients: any erection > 2 hours → come to emergency; (2) Chlorpromazine and other first-generation antipsychotics — extensive use in India's large psychiatric population; priapism is a recognised adverse effect; (3) Risperidone at high dose; (4) Recreational PDE5 inhibitor overdose / adulterated aphrodisiac products. The clinician prescribing these agents must: (a) Include priapism warning in patient counselling at prescription; (b) Ensure the patient knows the emergency instruction clearly: erection > 2–3 hours → Emergency Department immediately.
confirmation_tests_curated: Clinical history: duration (> 4 hours = emergency treatment regardless); association with sexual stimulation; medications; substance use; sickle cell or haematological history
Penile examination: fully rigid, painful corpora (ischaemic) vs partial rigidity, painless (high-flow non-ischaemic)

Diagnostic and therapeutic aspiration: 21-gauge needle into lateral corpus cavernosum; aspirate 5–10 mL blood; send for: pH (<7.25 = acidosis = ischaemic); PO2 (<30 mmHg = ischaemia); PCO2 (>60 mmHg = ischaemia); appearance: dark blood (ischaemic/venous) vs bright red (well-oxygenated = non-ischaemic/high-flow); this test IS the immediate diagnostic AND therapeutic first step

diseases.name: Retrograde Ejaculation
diseases.tests:
POST-ORGASM URINALYSIS — GOLD STANDARD

Patient masturbates to orgasm; provides urine sample IMMEDIATELY after (within 5 minutes); centrifuge urine; microscopy of sediment; presence of sperm (>10–15 sperm per high-power field) + fructose in post-orgasm urine = confirms RE; simple, cheap, decisive test

SEMEN ANALYSIS

Hypospermia (<1.5 mL) or anejaculation; low sperm count proportional to retrograde volume; low total sperm count despite normal testicular function

NEUROLOGICAL ASSESSMENT

Detailed history of surgery, diabetes, spinal cord injury, MS, medications; diabetic autonomic neuropathy testing: heart rate variability, postural BP drop, sweating assessment; EMG lumbar nerve root evaluation if spinal cause suspected

MEDICATION REVIEW

Current alpha-blockers, antihypertensives (guanethidine, methyldopa), antipsychotics (chlorpromazine — α-blockade), TCAs (imipramine — α1 agonist effects; paradoxically used to TREAT RE)

SPINAL MRI

If cord injury, MS, or spinal stenosis causing autonomic dysfunction suspected

HORMONAL

Testosterone, FSH, LH — distinguish testicular dysfunction from ejaculatory disease
diseases.generic_medicines:
PHARMACOLOGICAL — SYMPATHOMIMETIC AGENTS (RESTORE BLADDER NECK CLOSURE)

Imipramine 25–75 mg OD — TCA; the primary pharmacological treatment for RE; α1-adrenergic agonist + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition at bladder neck; achieves antegrade ejaculation in 30–60% of cases; particularly effective for sympathetically mediated RE (post-TURP less effective); start 25 mg OD; increase to 50–75 mg

Pseudoephedrine 30–60 mg before intercourse — sympathomimetic; α1-agonist; increases bladder neck tone; used particularly for intercourse/conception attempts; available OTC India (decongestant formulations); needs to be taken 30–60 min before sexual activity; India availability: common cold preparations contain it

Ephedrine 25–50 mg — similar to pseudoephedrine; older agent; less available in modern pharmacy

Brompheniramine with phenylpropanolamine — combination sympathomimetic (antihistamine + decongestant); older regimen for RE

SPERM RETRIEVAL FOR FERTILITY

Post-orgasm urine alkalinisation + sperm wash → IUI: patient alkalinises urine 12–24 hours before sperm collection (sodium bicarbonate 5g 4× daily); collects post-orgasm urine in sperm-friendly medium; lab washes sperm; uses for IUI (intrauterine insemination); achieves pregnancy in 20–30% of cycles; most common fertility approach for RE in India

IVF-ICSI with retrograde sperm: higher success rates; sperm from alkalinised post-orgasm urine injected into egg; for couples not achieving IUI success

Testicular sperm extraction (TESE) — if pharmacological + urine retrieval fails; surgical sperm aspiration from testis for ICSI

UNDERLYING CAUSE TREATMENT

Optimise diabetes: HbA1c target < 7%; reduces progressive autonomic neuropathy; Metformin, insulin optimisation

Stop/switch alpha-blocker: if RE from Tamsulosin/Silodosin → switch to Alfuzosin (lower RE rate) or 5-ARI (Finasteride/Dutasteride); RE reverses in majority after stopping alpha-blocker
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Imipramine 25 mg tablet

Pseudoephedrine-containing cold tablets (e.g., Actifed equivalent — combination formulations)

Sodium bicarbonate powder (for urine alkalinisation pre-sperm retrieval)

Metformin 500/850/1000 mg tablet (for diabetic RE — glycaemic control)

Alfuzosin 10 mg tablet (switch from silodosin/tamsulosin for drug-induced RE)

Finasteride 5 mg tablet (alternative BPH treatment avoiding RE)

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Imipramine 25 mg (10 tabs): Rs.12

Metformin 500 mg (10 tabs): Rs.5

Alfuzosin 10 mg (10 tabs): Rs.62

Finasteride 5 mg (10 tabs): Rs.28

Sodium bicarbonate powder (100g): Rs.15
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
AUTONOMIC NERVE TROPHIC SUPPORT

Ashwagandha — adaptogen; reduces cortisol; neuroprotective via BDNF upregulation; protects autonomic nervous system from diabetic neuropathic damage; of most value as preventive in diabetic patients at risk for autonomic neuropathy → RE; 300 mg BD

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neurotrophin enhancement; acetylcholinesterase inhibition; supports peripheral nerve health; neuroprotective; useful adjunct for autonomic neuropathy-associated ejaculatory dysfunction

Alpha Lipoic Acid + Benfotiamine (Biochemical Ayurveda interface) — antioxidant approach to diabetic neuropathy; reduces oxidative stress damaging autonomic nerve fibres; ALA has RCT evidence for diabetic neuropathy; though synthetic, clinically relevant for diabetic RE

SEMINAL VESICLE AND VAS TONIC

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA precursor; dopaminergic neurotransmission; may support ejaculatory reflex control at spinal cord level; traditional Vajikarana for ejaculatory health; 500 mg BD

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — LH stimulation; testosterone support; male reproductive tract tonic; traditional Vajikarana herb for ejaculatory disorders

Shilajit — mitochondrial support; reproductive system tonic; anti-diabetic and antioxidant; supports reproductive tract health and neuroprotection

IMPORTANT NOTE

RE management in fertility context in India requires a practical pathway: Post-orgasm urinalysis (confirm RE) → Imipramine trial (30–60% success for antegrade conversion) → if failed, urine alkalinisation + sperm wash for IUI → IVF-ICSI if needed. In India, the most common reversible cause is alpha-blocker medication — easily addressed by switching to alfuzosin or finasteride. Diabetic RE is the most common organic cause — glycaemic optimisation is both preventive and partially therapeutic. Ashwagandha and Brahmi support the neuroprotective dimension relevant to diabetic autonomic neuropathy underlying RE.
diseases.india_specific:
DIABETES-ASSOCIATED RETROGRADE EJACULATION

India has 90+ million people with type 2 diabetes — the world's largest diabetic population. Diabetic autonomic neuropathy is the most common cause of RE in India (along with TURP/prostate surgery). The sympathetic neural pathway controlling bladder neck closure (hypogastric nerve, L1–L3) is damaged by diabetic peripheral and autonomic neuropathy. RE from DM typically develops after 5–10 years of diabetes, particularly with poor glycaemic control. In India: (1) Many diabetic men are not aware that diabetes causes ejaculatory problems; (2) RE causes male infertility in diabetic men who may be presenting for fertility treatment — RE is a modifiable cause in some cases; (3) Glycaemic optimisation partially prevents or slows progression of autonomic neuropathy/RE; this is the primary preventive intervention

ALPHA-BLOCKER MEDICATIONS

India: alpha-blockers (Tamsulosin, Alfuzosin, Silodosin) are widely prescribed for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH); all α1-adrenergic blockers cause RE by blocking sympathetic α1A receptors at the bladder neck (same receptors that close the internal sphincter during ejaculation). Silodosin has the HIGHEST RE rate (28% in clinical trials) because of its high α1A selectivity. Tamsulosin has intermediate rate. Alfuzosin most bladder-specific with lower RE rate. In India, urology patients on tamsulosin/silodosin for BPH should be counselled that ejaculatory changes are expected; reversible on stopping the drug; fertility-seeking men of any age should use the lowest-RE-risk alpha-blocker (alfuzosin) or consider 5-alpha reductase inhibitor for BPH instead

RETROPERITONEAL SURGERY

India: retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) for testicular cancer; lumbar sympathectomy; retroperitoneal aortic aneurysm surgery; spinal surgery at lumbar level; all risk hypogastric nerve damage → RE. Testicular cancer is most common solid cancer in men 15–35 years old; post-RPLND ejaculatory dysfunction including RE has major quality of life and fertility impact in young survivors; nerve-sparing RPLND surgical technique reduces RE risk but is more technically demanding
diseases.important_notes:
RETROGRADE EJACULATION IS A TREATABLE AND OFTEN OVERLOOKED CAUSE OF MALE INFERTILITY IN INDIA: Male factor infertility is responsible for 40–50% of couples' inability to conceive. In India, male infertility workup is frequently incomplete — many men with azoospermia or severe oligospermia on semen analysis are not evaluated for the cause. Retrograde ejaculation causes male infertility by diverting sperm into the bladder instead of the ejaculate — but the testes and sperm are completely normal, and sperm are retrievable. The diagnostic test is a single post-orgasm urine microscopy (5-minute, inexpensive, definitive). In India: (1) Many infertility specialists do not routinely perform this test; (2) Men with 'no sperm on semen analysis' are labelled azoospermic without ruling out RE; (3) This matters because RE is often treatable (imipramine, stopping alpha-blocker) or sperm-retrievable — enabling successful fertility treatment. Every infertility assessment for male factor should include post-orgasm urinalysis as a standard step.

ALPHA-BLOCKERS FOR BPH ARE INDIA'S MOST COMMON DRUG CAUSE OF RETROGRADE EJACULATION — UROLOGISTS AND GPs MUST COUNSEL PATIENTS: BPH (benign prostatic hyperplasia) is extremely prevalent in Indian men over 50. Alpha-blockers (Tamsulosin, Silodosin) are the most commonly prescribed first-line pharmacological treatment. The failure rate of ejaculation (RE) is: Silodosin 28%, Tamsulosin 14%, Alfuzosin 3–5%. In India, urology outpatient consultations routinely prescribe tamsulosin or silodosin without counselling the patient that ejaculatory change is expected. The consequence: (1) Patients are alarmed by absent ejaculate; (2) Men who still wish to conceive (many Indian men over 50 have second marriages or late fatherhood) lose fertility capacity; (3) Patients discontinue BPH medication without telling their doctor, causing return of urinary symptoms. The solution: ALL patients being started on alpha-blockers for BPH must receive explicit counselling that ejaculation may change or disappear; that this is reversible if the drug is stopped; and that for fertility-seeking patients, alfuzosin or 5-ARI (finasteride/dutasteride) preferred.

DIABETES AND RETROGRADE EJACULATION — THE PREVENTABLE TIMELINE: Diabetic autonomic neuropathy progresses over years of chronic hyperglycaemia, typically becoming symptomatic in the sexual domain after 10–15 years of poorly controlled diabetes. The hypogastric sympathetic nerve controlling bladder neck closure is one of the autonomic nerves damaged in diabetic autonomic neuropathy — leading to RE. Once established diabetic autonomic neuropathy causes RE, reversal is limited and treatment is mainly pharmacological (imipramine) or assisted reproduction. The preventive window: tight glycaemic control (HbA1c <7%) from early diabetes significantly delays and reduces autonomic neuropathy development. Every diabetic man in India should be told: (1) Diabetes can cause ejaculatory problems; (2) Good BP and sugar control is the best prevention; (3) If ejaculatory volume decreases, report it — RE is diagnosable and fertility management is possible.
confirmation_tests_curated: Patient masturbates to orgasm; provides urine sample IMMEDIATELY after (within 5 minutes); centrifuge urine; microscopy of sediment; presence of sperm (>10–15 sperm per high-power field) + fructose in post-orgasm urine = confirms RE; simple, cheap, decisive test
Hypospermia (<1.5 mL) or anejaculation; low sperm count proportional to retrograde volume; low total sperm count despite normal testicular function

diseases.name: Genito-Pelvic Pain Penetration
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL ASSESSMENT OF ALL FOUR DIMENSIONS

Dimension 1: sexual history — penetration history; speculum/tampon difficulty; partner account

Dimension 2: gyn examination — vulvar pain mapping (Q-tip), speculum exam, bimanual; endometriosis signs, atrophy, infection

Dimension 3: FSFI fear domain; Female Sexual Distress Scale (FSDS); Pain Catastrophizing Scale (PCS); IES-R if trauma

Dimension 4: pelvic floor assessment — levator ani palpation; perineometry; biofeedback EMG

VALIDATED TOOLS

Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) — 6 domains; pain domain (Q17-Q19) specifically scores penetration pain; arousal, lubrication, orgasm domains also affected

Female Sexual Distress Scale (FSDS) — measures associated distress; required for diagnosis (clinically significant distress criterion)

Painful Intercourse Self-Efficacy Scale (PISES) — measures confidence to manage pain; relevant Dimension 3

PHYSICAL TESTS

Q-tip test — vestibulodynia identification (Dimension 2)

Pelvic ultrasound — endometriosis, adnexal pathology (Dimension 2 organic cause)

Pelvic floor EMG biofeedback — muscle hypertonicity quantification (Dimension 4)

LAPAROSCOPY

If deep dyspareunia + endometriosis suspicion — definitive for Dimension 2 organic cause
diseases.generic_medicines:
TREATMENT IS DIMENSION-SPECIFIC AND MULTIMODAL

Dimension 1 Treatment

Graduated vaginal dilator programme — cornerstone for penetration difficulty; patient-directed; sizes XS → XL progressively; 80–90% success rate with guidance

Partner engagement — inclusion in dilator programme; non-demand touch exercises (sensate focus, Masters and Johnson protocol)

Dimension 2 Treatment — depends on organic cause

Vaginal oestrogen — GSM/atrophic dyspareunia; highly effective

Dienogest 2 mg OD / GnRH agonist — endometriosis-associated deep dyspareunia

Amitriptyline 10–50 mg nocte or Gabapentin 300 mg BD — vestibulodynia neuropathic pain

Lidocaine 5% ointment topical — before dilator use or intercourse

Antibiotics/antifungals — if infection confirmed on culture

Dimension 3 Treatment

Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT) — pain catastrophising, fear conditioning; core psychotherapy for Dimension 3; 8–12 sessions evidence-based

Mindfulness-based CBT — reduces hypervigilance toward genital sensations; interoceptive awareness without threat appraisal

EMDR — for trauma-associated fear/anxiety (Dimension 3 with trauma history)

Dimension 4 Treatment

Pelvic floor physiotherapy — biofeedback + manual therapy; levator ani hypertonicity release; trigger point therapy

Botulinum toxin injection into levator ani — refractory pelvic floor hypertonicity

Diazepam vaginal suppository 2 mg — short-term pelvic floor muscle relaxant adjunct during dilator programme
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Vaginal dilator set (therapy equipment)

Amitriptyline 10 mg tablet

Gabapentin 300 mg capsule

Lidocaine 5% ointment

Diazepam 2 mg tablet (short term; pelvic floor relaxation adjunct)

Vaginal oestrogen cream

Dienogest 2 mg tablet

Metronidazole 400 mg tablet (if BV confirmed)

Fluconazole 150 mg tablet (if Candida confirmed)

Jan Aushadhi Prices

Amitriptyline 10 mg (10 tabs): Rs.9

Gabapentin 300 mg (10 caps): Rs.35

Diazepam 2 mg (10 tabs): Rs.8

Vaginal oestrogen cream (30g): Rs.85

Dienogest 2 mg (10 tabs): Rs.145

Fluconazole 150 mg (1 cap): Rs.12
diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
ANXIETY AND FEAR DIMENSION (DIMENSION 3)

Ashwagandha — cortisol reduction; anticipatory anxiety reduction; HPA axis normalisation; most evidence-based Ayurvedic anxiolytic; particularly relevant for the fear/anxiety dimension (Dimension 3) of GPPPD; 300–600 mg BD

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — GABAergic; anxiolytic; reduces hypervigilance and catastrophising; 300 mg BD

Sarpagandha (Rauwolfia serpentina) — CNS sedative; reserpine depletes monoamines; traditional anti-anxiety; short-term use; supervise for hypotension

PAIN AND INFLAMMATION DIMENSION (DIMENSION 2)

Haridra (Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; COX-2 inhibitor; anti-endometriosis; TRPV1 modulation for vestibulodynia component; 1000 mg phytosome BD

Ashoka bark — uterine anti-inflammatory; dysmenorrhoea and dyspareunia in endometriosis context

Shatavari — phytooestrogenic; restores vaginal mucosal health in atrophic dyspareunia; reduces dryness; lubricating properties

PELVIC FLOOR AND TOPICAL

Sesame oil warm perineal massage (Yoni Abhyanga) — relaxes pelvic floor superficially; anti-inflammatory; traditional India practice; reduces tissue hypertonicity; maintains tissue health for Dimension 4 indirect support

Aloe vera gel topical — cooling; soothing; anti-inflammatory; reduces introital pain on dilator insertion

MIND-BODY (ALL DIMENSIONS)

Yoga: Supta Baddha Konasana (Reclined Bound Angle) and Viparita Karani (Legs Up Wall) — internal hip rotation, adductor release; pelvic floor relaxation yoga postures; directly address Dimension 4 through parasympathetic activation and muscle release

Pranayama diaphragmatic breathing — parasympathetic activation during dilator practice; reduces Dimension 3 fear response; 4-7-8 breathing combined with dilator insertion

IMPORTANT NOTE

GPPPD management in India requires the GP or gynaecologist to be the de facto sex therapist in most cases given absence of specialists. Practical India pathway: (1) Diagnose all four dimensions; (2) Provide frank anatomical psychoeducation (diagrams, vaginal capacity reassurance); (3) Prescribe dilator set and graduated programme with written instructions; (4) Address organic pain cause pharmacologically; (5) Address anxiety dimension (Ashwagandha + referral for CBT if available); (6) Partner inclusion education. Ashwagandha addresses Dimension 3 anxiety; Curcumin addresses Dimension 2 pain-inflammation; Sesame oil + yoga address Dimension 4 pelvic floor relaxation — a complementary multi-dimensional Ayurvedic approach.
diseases.india_specific:
BIOPSYCHOSOCIAL INDIA PROFILE

In India, GPPPD arises from the intersection of: (1) Dimension 1 — No sex education → anatomical ignorance → penetration attempts without knowledge; no pre-marital exploration; first attempt on wedding night under performance pressure → traumatic penetration failure; (2) Dimension 2 — Organic causes: endometriosis (delayed diagnosis 7–10 years), episiotomy scar, atrophic vaginitis (post-menopausal), infections (Candida, STIs undertested); (3) Dimension 3 — India-specific anxiety: intense social pressure to consummate marriage + failure = catastrophic shame; fear of vaginal damage (anatomical ignorance); religious guilt; trauma history (24% India sexual abuse prevalence); (4) Dimension 4 — No pelvic floor physiotherapy availability outside metro India; partners attempt forceful penetration against guarding → worsened conditioning

UNCONSUMMATED MARRIAGE

Unconsummated marriage from GPPPD is the most extreme expression of the disorder in India; affects couples from all socioeconomic groups; presentation often years after marriage; the DSM-5 GPPPD framing helps Indian clinicians because it: names all four dimensions that the couple is experiencing; validates their combined psychosexual experience; guides a structured multidisciplinary intervention; replaces the shame of 'vaginismus' or 'dysfunction' with a clinical treatment target

ACCESS TO CARE

India: only a handful of certified sex therapists and pelvic floor physiotherapists in metro areas; vast majority of GPPPD cases never receive specialist input; GPs and gynaecologists are the de facto first (and only) professionals managing GPPPD; psychoeducation and dilator programme guided by GP/gynaecologist is the practical India pathway
diseases.important_notes:
GPPPD UNIFIES THE BIOPSYCHOSOCIAL MODEL OF SEXUAL PAIN — INDIA'S CLINICIANS NEED TO ADOPT THIS FRAMEWORK: The DSM-5 GPPPD diagnostic framework is important for Indian clinical practice because: (1) It moves away from a single symptom label (vaginismus = muscle problem; dyspareunia = pain problem) toward a four-dimension assessment framework that captures the full biopsychosocial complexity; (2) It mandates assessment of anatomy AND psychology AND pelvic floor AND fear — meaning no single-modality treatment (dilators alone, or antifungals alone, or psychotherapy alone) can be complete; (3) It provides a treatment roadmap: each dimension has specific evidence-based interventions; (4) In India, where single-modality management of sexual pain is common (gynaecologist treats physical; no one addresses psychological or pelvic floor dimensions), the GPPPD framework is a catalyst for comprehensive care. Training Indian GPs and gynaecologists to assess all four GPPPD dimensions in the first consultation — rather than treating only the presenting symptom — would transform outcomes for the large population of Indian women with this condition.

THE COUPLE IS THE PATIENT IN GPPPD — PARTNER INCLUSION IS NON-NEGOTIABLE: GPPPD is rarely a single-person condition — the couple's dynamic maintains and perpetuates the four dimensions. Partner behaviours that worsen GPPPD: (1) Pressure to attempt penetration before readiness (worsens Dimension 3 fear and Dimension 4 guarding); (2) Frustration, withdrawal, or guilt-inducing behaviours (amplifies Dimension 3 anticipatory anxiety and shame); (3) Attempts at forceful penetration (pain conditioning worsens Dimension 2 and 4); (4) Lack of knowledge about female anatomy and sexual response (leads to inadequate stimulation and inappropriate penetration technique). Partner inclusion in treatment means: both partners receive psychoeducation together; partner understands the dilator programme and their role (supportive, non-pressuring); non-demand sensate focus exercises; graduated partner participation in dilator progression. In India, including the husband in the consultation and therapy programme is culturally achievable but requires the clinician to create a non-judgmental, medically framing environment. Without partner inclusion, dilator therapy in the home environment fails.

SENSATE FOCUS — THE CORNERSTONE BEHAVIOURAL TECHNIQUE FOR GPPPD THAT INDIA'S NON-SPECIALIST CLINICIANS CAN PRESCRIBE: Sensate Focus (Masters and Johnson, 1970) is a structured couple behavioural exercise programme that directly addresses GPPPD Dimensions 1, 3, and 4: Stage 1 — Non-genital touching: face, neck, arms, torso, excluding breasts and genitals; no intercourse permitted; reduces performance anxiety; Stage 2 — Include breasts and genitals in touch exploration; no intercourse; Stage 3 — Non-demand genital stimulation; no pressure for orgasm or penetration; Stage 4 — Graduate to penetration at woman's initiation and control only. This structured programme: (1) Reduces anticipatory fear (Dimension 3) through non-demand desensitisation; (2) Teaches clitoral stimulation and arousal (critical for adequate lubrication that reduces Dimension 2 pain); (3) Breaks the fear-guarding-pain cycle (Dimensions 3 and 4); (4) Can be explained and prescribed in a 20-minute GP or gynaecologist consultation. Sensate focus prescription, combined with dilator instruction and Ashwagandha for anxiety, provides a practical minimum intervention for GPPPD in any Indian primary care setting.
confirmation_tests_curated: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT OF ALL FOUR DIMENSIONS
Dimension 1: sexual history — penetration history; speculum/tampon difficulty; partner account
Dimension 2: gyn examination — vulvar pain mapping (Q-tip), speculum exam, bimanual; endometriosis signs, atrophy, infection
Dimension 3: FSFI fear domain; Female Sexual Distress Scale (FSDS); Pain Catastrophizing Scale (PCS); IES-R if trauma
Dimension 4: pelvic floor assessment — levator ani palpation; perineometry; biofeedback EMG

diseases.name: Post-Orgasmic Illness Syndrome
diseases.tests:
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — WALDINGER CRITERIA

Diagnosis is primarily clinical: Waldinger 2011 criteria assessment (see symptom section); symptom diary: patient records every sexual/orgasmic episode and correlates with symptom onset, duration, character; retrospective review of past 'illness episodes' often reveals POIS pattern

INTRADERMAL SKIN TEST WITH AUTOLOGOUS SEMEN

Waldinger 2011: intradermal injection of autologous semen (diluted 1:40,000); positive wheal-and-flare reaction = type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to own semen; confirms allergy-mediated POIS; approximately 30-50% of tested POIS patients have positive skin test (type I); others have type II (delayed-type, cell-mediated) or cytokine mechanism — negative skin test does NOT exclude POIS

MAST CELL ACTIVATION MARKERS

Serum tryptase (within 1–4 hours of symptom onset) — mast cell activation marker; elevated in mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS); MCAS may underlie POIS in some patients

24-hour urine prostaglandin D2 metabolite — mast cell mediator

CYTOKINE PANEL

IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ during symptomatic episode — elevated in POIS; suggests cytokine storm mechanism; research context

STANDARD EXCLUSIONS

Blood count, CRP, ESR during symptom episode — rule out actual infection; renal function; TFT; viral screen; prostate-specific antigen (if prostatitis suspected)

TESTOSTERONE AND PROLACTIN

Ensure no hypogonadism (low T) contributing to post-orgasm dysphoria component
diseases.generic_medicines:
PHARMACOLOGICAL — LIMITED EVIDENCE (POIS IS ULTRA-RARE; RCT EVIDENCE ABSENT)

ANTIHISTAMINES — MOST USED EMPIRICAL TREATMENT

H1-antihistamines before or at first symptom: Fexofenadine 180 mg; Cetirizine 10 mg; Loratadine 10 mg — reduce allergic/mast cell mediated symptom component; first-line empirical treatment given safety profile; taken 30–60 min before anticipated orgasm for prophylaxis

H2-antihistamine: Ranitidine 150 mg / Famotidine 40 mg — combined H1+H2 antihistamine blockade for stronger mast cell suppression

HYPOSENSITISATION / DESENSITISATION PROTOCOL

Autologous semen desensitisation: intradermal escalating dose injections of own semen over weeks → tolerance development; reported by Waldinger to markedly reduce POIS severity; requires specialist allergist; very rarely performed; limited to specialist centres

NSAIDs

Ibuprofen 400–600 mg / Naproxen 500 mg at onset — reduces prostaglandin-mediated systemic inflammation; particularly for muscle aches, fever, malaise component

BENZODIAZEPINES — SHORT-TERM

Lorazepam 0.5–1 mg or Clonazepam 0.5 mg — reduces post-orgasm anxiety and CNS inflammatory response; helps cognitive symptoms and mood component; short-term use only; evidence from case reports

TESTOSTERONE

Testosterone replacement (if low testosterone confirmed) — POIS in hypogonadal men may improve with testosterone normalisation; case reports

SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS

SSRI (Paroxetine 20 mg, Citalopram 20 mg) — cytoprotective and anti-inflammatory; anti-serotonin-sensitisation; may reduce post-orgasm immunological response; benefit reported in case series

MAST CELL STABILISER

Cromolyn sodium (Sodium cromoglicate) — mast cell membrane stabiliser; reduces mast cell degranulation; oral formulation; logical for MCAS-associated POIS

SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT DURING EPISODE

Rest; adequate hydration; NSAIDs for myalgia; antihistamines for mucosal symptoms; acetaminophen for fever; cognitive symptoms managed with rest (no demanding tasks during episodes)
diseases.janaushadhi_medicines:
Cetirizine 10 mg tablet

Fexofenadine 180 mg tablet

diseases.ayurvedic_medicines:
IMMUNE MODULATION AND ANTI-ALLERGIC

Haridra (Curcumin) — mast cell stability; inhibits mast cell degranulation and histamine release; COX-2 inhibitor reducing prostaglandin mediators; NF-κB inhibitor reducing cytokine storm; TNF-α and IL-6 reduction; mechanistically one of the most plausible Ayurvedic agents for POIS given the immune-mediated pathophysiology; 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD; taken prophylactically

Tulsi (Holy Basil / Ocimum sanctum) — adaptogen; anti-allergic; mast cell stabilising; COX-1 and COX-2 inhibition; traditional anti-inflammatory; reduces hypersensitivity reactions; 300–500 mg extract BD

Shirisha (Albizia lebbeck) — classical Ayurvedic anti-allergic herb; mast cell stabilisation; used in allergic conditions; Shirisha arishtam for respiratory allergy; anti-histaminic properties from saponins; appropriate for the allergic/mucosal component of POIS

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immuno-modulator; reduces excessive immune activation; adaptogen; anti-inflammatory; reduces cytokine-mediated sickness behaviour

NEURO-INFLAMMATORY COMPONENT

Brahmi — neuroinflammation reduction; reduces post-orgasmic neurological symptoms (brain fog, cognitive impairment, mood disturbance) via anti-neuroinflammatory mechanisms; BDNF support

Shankhpushpi — anxiolytic; cerebral tonic; reduces post-orgasmic psychological distress; supports cognitive recovery

RASAYANA / VITALITY

Ashwagandha — adaptogen; anti-inflammatory; reduces HPA axis dysregulation; supports recovery from each POIS episode; reduces post-orgasm fatigue and weakness; anti-inflammatory via NF-κB inhibition; perhaps most relevant comprehensive Ayurvedic agent for POIS

Chyawanprash — classical rasayana; immuno-modulator; anti-oxidant; anti-inflammatory; multi-herb formula; broad regenerative and anti-exhaustion properties; daily 1 tsp BD prophylaxis

IMPORTANT NOTE

POIS is a genuine medical condition — not somatisation, anxiety, Dhat syndrome, or psychosomatic illness. It is a valid reason for orgasm avoidance. In India, the first clinical challenge is DIAGNOSIS — establishing POIS recognition among clinicians who have never heard of it. The Waldinger criteria are simple to apply. H1+H2 antihistamine trial is the safest and first pharmacological step. Curcumin, Tulsi, and Shirisha have Ayurvedic anti-allergic and mast cell stabilisation properties that align with the POIS immune mechanism — appropriate as prophylactic adjuncts. Ashwagandha addresses the systemic inflammatory and fatigue dimensions. The couple should understand that POIS is the cause of post-coital illness — removing enormous guilt, confusion, and relationship damage generated by unexplained recurrent illness after sex.
diseases.india_specific:
COMPLETE CLINICAL INVISIBILITY IN INDIA

POIS has not been reported in Indian literature and is not recognised in Indian clinical practice. Indian patients with POIS will typically have encountered: (1) Ayurvedic physicians who attribute their symptoms to 'shukra dhatu depletion' or 'veerya kshaya' (semen depletion theory) — which happens to capture the symptomatic pattern from a different mechanistic lens; (2) GPs who diagnose recurrent upper respiratory infections; (3) Psychiatrists who diagnose health anxiety or somatisation; (4) Urologists who diagnose prostatitis. None recognise POIS. The result: years of mismanagement. India POIS patients are almost certainly presenting in all these specialties without identification.

SHUKRA DHATU AND DHAT SYNDROME OVERLAP

India: 'Dhat Syndrome' — culturally-bound syndrome of semen loss anxiety — causes somatic and psychological symptoms attributed to semen loss (masturbation, nocturnal emission). POIS and Dhat Syndrome are distinct: Dhat = psychological semen-depletion belief with psychosomatic symptoms; POIS = objective immunological/physiological reaction causing systemic illness after orgasm regardless of ejaculation (POIS occurs even with dry orgasm in some patients). However, in India, POIS may frequently present as and be treated as Dhat Syndrome — missing the immunological diagnosis and appropriate management (antihistamines, HSD protocol). Cultural sensitivity is required: the patient's Dhat framework should be acknowledged while appropriate medical workup is pursued.

YOGA AND BRAHMACHARYA CULTURE

India: traditional belief in Brahmacharya (sexual abstinence) as a health-promoting and spiritually elevating practice; traditional texts attribute various physical and mental health benefits to semen retention; POIS patients may find cultural resonance with their lived experience (orgasm → illness) in this framework; this cultural context may facilitate or delay medical consultation; integration of the medical diagnosis with respect for the patient's cultural framework is important in India
diseases.important_notes:
POIS IS REAL AND INDIA'S MEDICAL COMMUNITY MUST LEARN TO RECOGNISE IT — BREAKING THE CYCLE OF MISDIAGNOSIS: Post-Orgasmic Illness Syndrome likely affects many more people than the handful of reported cases globally — because it is massively under-reported and under-recognised. In India specifically: (1) Patients are culturally unable to report illness related to sexual activity; (2) Physicians who hear 'I feel sick after sex' initially attribute it to anxiety, exertion, or psychosomatic response; (3) The episodic nature (days of illness after each orgasm) is dismissed as coincidence or 'viral illness'; (4) Patients eventually stop reporting the association to doctors out of shame. The result: patients adopt complete sexual abstinence — sometimes for years or decades — as the only means of avoiding incapacitation. The clinical ask is simple: include POIS in the differential of unexplained recurrent flu-like illness in reproductively active men; ask explicitly 'do you notice any pattern with sexual activity?'; administer the Waldinger criteria if pattern suggests POIS.

THE IMMUNOLOGICAL MECHANISMS OF POIS — WHAT WE KNOW AND THE AYURVEDIC INTERFACE: POIS pathophysiology involves multiple proposed mechanisms: (1) Type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to autologous semen components — supported by positive intradermal semen skin tests in ~50% of POIS patients; (2) Delayed type IV cell-mediated hypersensitivity — supported by negative skin tests with delayed-onset POIS; (3) Mast cell activation — mast cell degranulation releasing histamine, tryptase, prostaglandin D2 explaining flu-like systemic response; (4) Cytokine storm: IL-6, TNF-α surge at orgasm causing sickness behaviour (fever, myalgia, cognitive impairment) — this mechanism is identical to what governs post-infectious sickness behaviour; (5) CNS neuroimmune dysregulation: orgasm-triggered HPA axis and autonomic nervous system dysregulation causing hypothalamic sickness response. The Ayurvedic agents most mechanistically aligned: Curcumin (mast cell stabilisation, TNF-α/IL-6 inhibition, NF-κB blockade), Shirisha (mast cell stabilisation), Tulsi (prostaglandin inhibition), Guduchi (cytokine modulation), Ashwagandha (HPA axis normalisation). These are rational prophylactic adjuncts alongside antihistamine pharmacological management.

MANAGING POIS IN INDIA — THE PRACTICAL CLINICAL APPROACH: Given POIS rarity and India's lack of specialist infrastructure, a practical stepwise approach: Step 1 — Diagnose using Waldinger criteria; validate the patient's experience explicitly ('Yes, this is a real condition; it is called POIS; you are not imagining it; it is not psychological weakness'); this alone has enormous therapeutic value; Step 2 — Symptom diary: patient records orgasmic events with symptom onset, duration, severity over 4–8 weeks; Step 3 — H1 antihistamine (Cetirizine 10 mg or Fexofenadine 180 mg) 30–60 min before anticipated sexual activity + H2 antihistamine (Famotidine 40 mg) — 4-week empirical trial; Step 4 — If partial response: add Ibuprofen 400 mg for myalgia; Clonazepam 0.5 mg for CNS symptoms; Ayurvedic: Curcumin + Ashwagandha + Tulsi prophylactically; Step 5 — Relationship and sexual counselling for couple regarding POIS impact and management of sexual frequency; referral to allergist for intradermal semen testing and possible desensitisation if available.
confirmation_tests_curated: Diagnosis is primarily clinical: Waldinger 2011 criteria assessment (see symptom section); symptom diary: patient records every sexual/orgasmic episode and correlates with symptom onset, duration, character; retrospective review of past 'illness episodes' often reveals POIS pattern
INTRADERMAL SKIN TEST WITH AUTOLOGOUS SEMEN

Waldinger 2011: intradermal injection of autologous semen (diluted 1:40,000); positive wheal-and-flare reaction = type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to own semen; confirms allergy-mediated POIS; approximately 30-50% of tested POIS patients have positive skin test (type I); others have type II (delayed-type, cell-mediated) or cytokine mechanism — negative skin test does NOT exclude POIS
